title: Roasted Nectarine Mess With Pistachio Dukkah Recipe
author: Yotam Ottolenghi

comments.commentBody: Oh yes, Mr. Ottolenghi, there is a paean to nectarines--from Mel Brooks as the 2000-year-old man: Fruit kept me going for 140 years once when I was on a very strict diet. Mainly nectarines. I love that fruit. It's half a peach, half a plum. It's a helluva fruit, it's not too cold, not too hot. Just nice. Even a rotten one is good. That's how much I love 'em. I'd rather eat a rotten nectarine than a fine plum, what do you think of that? https://www.nytimes.com/1975/03/30/archives/oooooooooboy.
Marcia, I was just going to say the same thing about the 2000 year old man routine about nectarines. A classic!!

This is the most delicious dessert I have ever made. My dinner guests raved. I found lovely organic nectarines at the grocery and the roasted fennel seeds and tarragon added so much flavor and were creative additions. Love this dessert!!!!
He said to purchase them. I personally would make them, there are lots of recipes. @Kelly
Use mascarpone instead of ricotta!
Could not agree more. The texture of ricotta is icky and the flavor isn't much better.
For those who may not know – this dish seems to be a riff on Eton Mess, a lovely mixture of whipped cream, crumbled meringues, and strawberries.
I loved every step of making this and my guests really loved eating it. A beautiful, really delicious end to a summer dinner party.
Is it just me or does he mention adding meringues but doesn't provide direction in the recipe to make them?
He says to use store-bought meringue cookies.
The recipe uses store-bought meringues.
Recipe says store-bought.
Can I substitute almonds? I am sensitive to pistachios.
Made with store-bought dukkah (added my own fennel and dried tarragon) plus a little jar of vanilla bean powder. Excellent dessert!

This was sooooo good.  
A straight-up adult dessert - "wine-soaked" (without any wine) - rosewater's elusive-ness (without any rosewater) --- and kinda savory (I omitted the cookies)

Fussy?   NOT THE LEAST. 
Lots of Ingredients?   Not really.
Hard to cook?  NO.   is basically toss fruit with sugar and bake.  blitz pistachios with fennel

Expensive?   well --- nothing seems inexpensive any more -- this just adds the cost of pistachio above the cost of fruit and cream.   reasonable for what it is
What does " less fussed-over than peaches" mean?
As a horticulturist of treefruit and an avid cook I need to reiterate that there is but one recessive gene, lack of fuzz, separating peaches and nectarines. Otherwise, the same fruit.
Sounds amazing and love the savory hint. Any suggestions for substitution for tarragon? Basil? Not a huge tarragon fan sadly.

Go ahead and try the basil. It's from the same family of herbs as tarragon. I often make this substitution when I'm cooking meat and vegetable dishes since I don't care much for tarragon either. Usually it works out fine.
Finding store bought meringues where I live can be a challenge. Sometimes Trader Joe’s. Suggestions welcome. Online? That seems a bit much.
I mixed nectarines and apricots. Upped few ingredients but cut down on sugar. I love his recipes and this one didn’t disappoint. It was an absolute hit!
Superb and, while a little time consuming, a delight to eat. My guests for dinner just wanted more.
How much of this can you make in advance, the day before?

made this to the book — but used anise seeds instead of fennel and my grandmother’s garden basil instead of tarragon (because the tarragon at whole foods was hideously rotted). absolutely hit with everyone from my 87 year old grandmother to my 1 year old baby cousin (having a teeny tiny portion of course) to the family dogs who licked it off the baby’s fingers.
I enjoyed this! A very nice dessert for the summer, I did take some of the suggestions and make it with mascarpone, it was very very silky
Suggestions for dairy free “mess”?
A nectarine-based dessert? Had to try it. YUM. I fully omitted both the meringue and the ricotta (just made standard whipped cream). Still wonderfully layered & interesting. And it was a hit.

love nectarines, they're the stone fruit of the summer; followed recipe to the 't', used a high-grade basket-drained ricotta which was decidedly worth it texturally; layers upon layers of flavor & visually stunning; truly a show-off summer dessert.

My 1st comment here bc this was THAT delicious--and unusual. So, first off, I did not make the whole ricotta/merengue combo. The first night, we ate the roasted nectarines and dukkah over vanilla ice cream. After that I had some with plain greek yogurt and, surprisingly, I liked that even better--probably because the tang of the yogurt played well with the lime. And I used the last bit this blistering hot morning to top off my cold overnight steel-cut oats. Swoonable, all the ways!

title: Chickpea Salad With Cucumber and Roast Lemon Salsa Recipe
author: Yotam Ottolenghi

comments.commentBody:
Would you include metric measurement for the recipes? The NYT is supposedly global, but continues to put imperial measurements in the recipes. I'm not going to spend my time converting just to be able to make them.
Thanks in advance.
@AK all recipes have metric !  Click on the ruler to the right of the word “ingredients”.
I believe the metric option is only available on the phone app. At least for me there is no metric option for the web version.
I wonder if the ruler icon only appears in the app.  I don't see it on the desktop version.

Just as an aside to the author regarding the accompanying NYT article dated 5/13/26, oysters are not eaten in months without an R because they spawn between May and July; they are thinner and busy reproducing during that time. They were traditionally harvested only between September and March. I grew up within walking distance of an oyster house on the Rappahannock River.

KRH The availability of triploid oysters at market also means that they can be eaten in summer now. As for the safety, Vibrio bacteria makes oysters unsafe for raw consumption in many areas during summer months.

I make a similar salad , to substitute for tuna salad.  However, some of the ingredients are the same.  I basically use the ingredients of a classic tuna salad, celery, onions, chopped pickles, a little mayo to hold it together, and pickle brine to give it some accent.  I have found that chopping the chick peas manually provides the best texture.
Made as written except I reduced the mayo to 1 tbsp. It seemed like a strange mix of flavors but in fact it was delicious.

I made this almost as written, I cut way back on the yogurt and mayonnaise for the chickpeas.  Also - my lemons kind of disintegrated in the oven, so it was kind of lemony mush that was put into the salsa.  I didn't think those flavors would really meld together, but it tasted pretty good.
I would think preserved lemons would do nicely to add acidity and brine and could be substituted for the lemons + gherkins

Just as another aside to the author regarding the accompanying NYT article dated 5/13/26, one function chickpeas do not fulfill on a summer picnic plate is their ability to just sit in place on the plate. Chickpeas roll, potatoes do not.

@Debbie

They don’t roll when encased in yogurt and mayo

Loved the Cucumber and Roasted Lemon Salsa-  We actually grilled the lemons and next time we would add less Mayo and Yogurt to the chickpeas. But overall, LOVE this a "veggie" potato salad alternative! Kudos team NYT
A note that chickpeas provide much-desired protein, whereas potatoes (as beloved as they are) do not.
I’m a vegetarian and I make a similar salad about twice a month. Eager to try this variation. Love the author’s cookbooks.
Should we remove the skins from the chickpeas?

Liz, while I don't think it's *necessary*, when I use canned chickpeas I do remove most of the skins. 

The method I learned is to place the chickpeas in very cold water, and swish them around. The skins float to the top, so they're easy to remove.

That said, I wonder whether this also removes beneficial fiber or other nutrients?

Winner winner summer dinner! Our family loved the taste and texture, and it’s easy to put together. One suggestion: reduce the yogurt and mayo to 1/3 cup each. There was a lot of dressing left once the chickpeas were finished. Or maybe add more chickpeas.
Fabulous combination of flavors. Reduced the yogurt and mayo significantly. Used organic dill pickles with a touch of organic sweet relish. Perfect.
The lemons were very bitter, and I followed the directions!

I made this today as written.The lemon, thinly sliced, charred, and very finely chopped was way too bitter/sour. There is also too much mayo yogurt dressing. I’m not a sweet taste person, but had to add an additional teaspoon of maple syrup and some additional salt to compensate for the lemon. I love chickpeas, so I was anxious to make this, but it was disappointing. I’m serving it tomorrow and may slice and add some potatoes.
I didn’t have any cucumbers on hand but some watermelon.  I used it in place and was very delicious!

Reading the ingredients, this is a very unusual recipe, but WOW!! What an amazing dish!! I made it as instructed with no substitutions, but I only used half of the jalapeño sliced, then added half of the remainder after it was seeded to tame the heat a bit. The flavors hit on so many levels - tangy, sweet, spicy…and it plates beautifully.

As far as the roasted lemons, Don’t fret if they fall apart when chopping them up. Just scrape those bits up and put them in the bowl with the rest of the relish ingredients. I promise, it will turn out just fine.

This will be my go-to for any summer BBQs, and it’s earned a permanent spot in my recipe box. It was a hit at dinner tonight with roasted salmon sprinkled with a little Thai chili seasoning and a light basting of maple.
Has anyone made this without the jalapeno? Did you substitute or just leave it out?
@Donna I made it without the jalapeño and it was delicious!
@Donna I omitted the jalapeño and mustard and subbed capers for pickles, English cukes for Persian and dried dill for fresh. It's a delicious salad! I served it on a bed of arugula - yum!

I cook and bake by weight in grams.  None of your website recipes include these weight measurements.  And no, I do not use a phone app for anything.  No nutritional value per serving, either.

This information is common in website recipes.  Please, NYT, get with the times (no pun intended) with your recipes.

@Mary Rae Fouts

You’re wrong. Recipes for baked goods include measurements in grams and most recipes have nutritional information.
I wasn’t so sure about this but came out great! I used Famous Dave’s Devils Spit pickle brine instead of jalapeño. I used kosher pickles but would probably sub for the Devils Spit next time.
You  can speed up the chickpeas chopping by using a pastry blender.

Balthasar, is that like a mezzaluna with multiple blades?

I just looked it up, and it looks like a very useful kitchen tool!
Delicious! Added a small spicy yellow pepper for some more heat. Presented it on   butter lettuce leaves. Excellent lunch the next day. New fav!
This chickpea recipe was such a Big Hit at a neighborhood gathering!  Will certainly make it again!  It was a bit too “wet” for my liking so will ease up on the mayo next time.
This seemed like an interesting idea but ended up being too soupy and too acidic for my taste

I made the recipe (almost) as written, but used spicier pickles instead of the sweet ones, which I did not have.  Roasting the lemon slices made a terrific difference, as they caramelized and lent wonderful zest to the salad. 
I'm making again for a Memorial Day cookout, but will use less mayo and yogurt, and less oil in the salsa.

Good but bean salad is a completely different dish than potato salad a traditional side dish in bbq of some type of protein. A good side but not a substitute.
But then I’d never wear white pants in winter it’s what makes seasons special certain foods and traditions.
Delicious! I don’t like dill and it was fine without. Celery seed is also great in it. I used less mayo and (coconut) yogurt based on comments. I could eat this every day!
Curious if anyone has made this with dill pickles, vinegar or fermented?  I'm not a fan of sweet pickles, but not sure if the flavors would meld the same way.
Next time I will use half as much yogurt and mayo. I poured off about half of it and used it separately as salad dressing.

This salad was a big success; easy to make, particularly, if you simplify things a bit. I didn't roast the lemon; instead I used one preserved lemon and brine for 3 chickpeas cans. I also didn't use the olive oil as it didn't seem necessary. Delicious, nutritious and easy!

Lovely! Bitterness of the lemons, the crunchiness of the cucumbers, together with the creamy chickpeas.

Next time I will add dried apricots to the cucumber salsa to give it a bit more sweetness!

Have made it twice and adjusted it both times taste wise and caloric intake wise. It’s a little sweet for me following the recipe exactly, cutting back on pickle brine and maple syrup I’m going to omit. I may substitute the mayonnaise with a little sour cream.  It’s a wonderful recipe and will become a staple in our house.

This one is a keeper!
Of course I adjusted: I don’t like scallions, and skipped celery salt and maple syrup. 
I added 5 big celery stalks diced. It needed more vegetable. 
Otherwise it’s great and I will make it again.

Loved by my husband and I. My 20something didn't like the sweet pickles in it (he says "old people food"always pairs mayonnaise with something sweet"). Next time I'll use dill pickles. So anyway, there were leftovers, and because a lot of moisture comes out of the cucumbers when they sit, the next day I folded in diced cooked potatoes to absorb the liquids—and my husband liked this recipe even more.
Such an unusual combo of ingredients - cucumber salsa was great.  No fresh dill - used dried. Served on bed of mixed greens.

title: Jammy Eggs With Asparagus and Chipotle Recipe
author: Yotam Ottolenghi

comments.commentBody:
What a beautifully rendered thought, and so true: 
“Tempting as it may be, I don’t find the idea of a perfect anything very useful. It makes you seek something that isn’t about you — your taste buds, your mood, your likes and dislikes — but rather about a static platonic ideal, a silver bullet to all culinary dilemmas. It eliminates wonder, discovery and subjectivity.”
As a musician and a school teacher, and someone who loves to cook, those words resonated so perfectly (!) with me. 
Thank you Chef.
Thank you for saying what I wanted to better than I could. This whole post was wonderful writing and the recipe? Looking forward when asparagus comes from closer than Mexico...NJ comes to mind.
I wonder how this would be with green beans when asparagus is not in season?
@Arglebarglor a handful of spinach would work too.
@Arglebarglor use Kale. It can be cooked in the same way as its robust enough to handle it. Spinach would wilt. Although raw spinach would be okay.
I boiled my eggs straight from the fridge and then put them in an ice water bath for 2 min. They were perfect. I loved this dish!! Delicious.

@Annie in Berkeley Thank you for posting your results with fridge-cold eggs. I was wondering about this, as I live in a country where our eggs are kept at room temperature. I’ll be sure to adjust the boiling time.

I boiled my eggs for 7 minutes - they were not jammy when cut, more on the soft end of the hard boiled spectrum, solid throughout. And the eggs in the photo are clearly cut, not torn open. Tearing eggs is not an appealing preparation for me in any case. The flavors were great.
The doneness of your eggs sounds far more appealing than the recipe. I admit I find the term "jammy" eggs very off-putting even though I am rarely squeamish about any food.

@Judith We've been doing soft boiled eggs this way for years and while this sounds a bit OCD, 6:20 - 6:25 is the window followed by 15-20 seconds in an ice bath...until they can be handled.  Tap liberally and peel...  JAMMY!
did you start with a cold egg or room temp?@Robb

When I make this, I will save the woody ends in the freezer for making stock. It tastes great with asparagus ends plus the usual onions, celery, peppers, etc. I just save it all in a bag until I have enough for a stock. I place a steamer tray in the stock pot and add the veggies on top, which makes it very easy to remove the scraps afterward.

This was superb. Love Ottolenghi but rarely have all the ingredients he requires (and am not inclined to go out and buy a jar of something I will only use once...) However, I had everything needed and it is April and asparagus season in Europe so this was perfect. Rather than cooking eggs first I cooked them while the asparags was macerating in the dressing. It came out perfectly. Will definitely make again. Yum!
Alternative:  Works nicely with horseradish, honey and tomato paste, adding a spoonful of tahini to thicken.

With all those varied flavors  (which I like individually but cannot imagine as a blend - would have to try it) can you even taste the asparagus? My asparagus in the garden has an ephemral sweet green flavor that would be overrun by that sauce, not enhanced. Of course our tastes may vary.

Looks fabulous, but in my part of the world basil isn't harvestable until long after asparagus has gone to frond. How about some spring herbs, like garlic chives, regular chives, mint or baby cilantro?
My choice would be tarragon- I live at 7500' and it comes up even under the snow, early in the spring- and it's great with asparagus.

@Ken In addition to not mentioning room temp or refrigerated, recipe authors almost never warn readers that the timing varies radically with altitude. I'm over 5000 feet and a 7-minute egg would be half-raw. Always bugs me that there's no warning that these timings can vary! (Unfortunately I think you really have to dial in egg timings for yourself and your kitchen, mostly through trial and error. It's worth it to get a good jammy texture though!)

I guess most recipe writers give people who live at altitude credit for knowing that timing is different up there on your mountains and don't want to seem patronizing. I have always lived at sea level but even I know to make those adjustments.
He mentions this in the featured article as one of the reasons there is no perfect, only perfect for yourself.

I made a quarter portion for just myself as a Saturday brunch. Although I didn't execute it well (had trouble peeling the eggs!) I felt the flavor profile was superb. I will make again, a half recipe, and serve it for two people.
@Jonathan Steam your eggs per Kenji. Recipe on this app. Perfect peeling every time!
@LC not true for me. Tried steaming for 7 minutes and did not easily peel
Looks fire, but very clearly not an alternative to deviled eggs

As usual, a wonderful recipe. I’m not a big fan of ATK, but their egg technique is absolutely foolproof. Boil a couple of inches of water. Put as many eggs as you want straight from the fridge into a steamer insert, and steam the eggs covered. 10.5 minutes and perfect eggs with jammy yolks. Never ever fail.
I agree!  I always steam my eggs then an ice bath with great success every time.
When will we ever get rid of the Words Jammy and cheesy In recipe titles? They sound like kindergarten menus.
And “melty,” too.
Those eggs were so NOT torn "in half by hand".

@Robert
I find myself wondering exactly how one would tear an egg and get anything that looks halfway presentable.
7 Minutes is too long - they won´t be soft any more
Most disappointing NYT recipe I’ve ever experienced. A bad use of my garden-fresh asparagus.
@CR would appreciate what could improve it as the concept interests me

@Teresa Davis visually, the sauce and eggs combine into an ugly color. Frying asparagus to “deeply golden spots” isn’t the best way to cook it, either. It’s always tastier roasted. I’m not predisposed to “jammy” eggs either, but I was giving it a try. Nope. I did not have basil leaves, but I don’t think that would have saved it.

I'm not quite understanding the use of the words "torn open" with reference to the eggs.  I've always tapped the boiled egg with a spoon to create cracks and then peel.  Is "tear open" another term for "peeling"?  ???
The recipe now says "sliced or torn in half.  Torn open meant to literally tear the eggs roughly in half, after peeling them.

will definitely cook this I love the ingredients and the flavor profile.
I always very fresh eggs that are difficult to peel with methods other than pressure cooking them. For jammy eggs I have found that  large or extra large eggs straight out of the fridge put on a rack over water in a pressure cooker, high, for 3 minutes and 45 seconds or a bit less depending on how cold your eggs are and immediately quick released and plunged into ice water are reliably easy to peel cleanly.
Chipotles in adobo are whole roasted jalapenos in adobo sauce. Is it just the sauce we are to use? The peppers include seeds. Might be more than "a hit" of heat.
I like my eggs to be warm and they’re too cold after in the ice.

If the eggs go into boiling water when they are cold, it makes them much easier to peel (there's science readily available on the inter webs for this). I haven't tested the seven minute time, but you could always take one out a minute early, drop it in the ice bath, and test.

A far easier (and more predictable option) is a Dash Egg Cooker which is a tiny, electric cooker that takes up almost no room, is VERY inexpensive,  ridiculously easy to clean, and is available on Amazon. I speak highly of this little device for perfectly poached, as well as soft and hard boiled eggs. A frittata option is another option although I can't comment on that.

When I make jammy eggs, I follow Step 1's method to begin. After 7 minutes (8 for extra large) I carefully lift the eggs into a bowl of iced water with my spider so they can cool. When they're cool, I gently roll them (starting at the large end) on the counter to crack the shells. Peeling them submerged in the same cold water (starting at the large end where the air pocket usually is) really helps my fingers get under the membrane and easily lift off pieces of the shell. This works every time.
Wonderful recipe. I made it as written, but only used 4 eggs. Perfect. Don’t think there would be enough asparagus and sauce for 8 eggs.

Great recipe.  The thick and slightly runny eggs mixed with chili dressing to coat everything nicely when eaten. 

One note: Cook your asparagus appropriately depending on its thickness. Even after pealing some of the thicker woody stalks I had to sauté these a good bit longer than  2-3 minutes.  A splash of water and a cover helped speed it up at the end. 

But otherwise a solid springtime dish and not nearly as spicy as I would have though that many chipotles would be (the runny egg and olive oil tames it). Will definitely make again.

This was delicious. I used a slight variation on the cooking of the garlic and asparagus: put a  tablespoons of olive oil on a parchment lined half sheet pan. Put the pan into a 400 degree oven until sizzling hot. Add the sliced garlic and cook until just beginning to brown than add the asparagus. Roast 7 to 8 minutes and then follow the recipe as written. I didn't have fresh basil so used parsley instead.

Elle Kaye, how can you say that your asparagus "would be overrun by that sauce" without even making the recipe and trying it out? It is not very helpful reading an opinion about the taste of a dish you never ate.

Brigitte, I know what my garden asparagus tastes like, it is delicate and fresh-vegetable sweet. We harvest it an hour before cooking when we can. I know what chipotle in adobo tastes like, I use it in other dishes - it might be good on shredded cabbage, which while mild flavored, doesn't have the ephemeral floral flavor of fresh asparagus.

Have you made this recipe as written?
How do we feel this would go meal prepped to be eaten like up to 3 days later?
I feel it would last 3-days and still be delicious. Even out of the fridge cold.
maybe with okra?
This preparation has us peeling the eggs at step 1 and then again at step 4. What exactly are we peeling at step 4? ;)
I can’t handle the heat of the chipotle and adobo sauce. How can I tame down the heat n this recipe?
@Linda Use just the adobo sauce without the chipotle. If the sauce is still too spicy, cut it with a little crema, sour cream, creme fraiche etc.
As Salvador Dali once said, "Have no fear of perfection...you'll never find it!"
The eggs are peeled in Step 1, and again in Step 4?
The recipe doesn't mention if the eggs should be room temperature first or straight from the fridge.
No, but they should be straight from the fridge. Boil based on your preference or altitude and then in the ice bath with them for 2-3 minutes.
Does anyone know what the timings would be for the eggs in an Instant Pot? I do my hb eggs there as they are a cinch to peel when they are steamed.
I struggled when placing my eggs in boiling water; the roiling water causes the eggs to bounce and inevitably crack.  Any tips?
Turn the temperature down to a less rapid boiling.
Put eggs in cold water and bring to a boil.  Reduce cooking time by about a minute, dropping to a simmer after full boil.

I like the ideas of this recipe but ... I really hate asparagus ... it is vile on my tongue, alas (I've got that gene that cannot properly taste "bitter" - OTOH - I -love- brussels sprouts!).

Is one going for color (green), shape (pointy tubes), flavor, ability-to-absorb flavor ... or what? What can I substitute?
Broccoli.  Baby Broccoli.
@Jeff 1 tablespoon chopped chile, 1 1/2 tablespoons sauce
What a delightful recipe ☺️
Easy weeknight dinner. Sauce was super tasty!  For more jammy eggs boil 6 minutes. I doubled asparagus.

Per @Emily (and @Ken), I'm at 7,200 feet and did 7-7 1/2 minutes* followed by ice bath, and they were perfectly jammy.
I love this flavor profile, though I wish that the particular jar of chipotles en adobo I used had a little more heat.
Recipe is easy and quick. I did the eggs before putting the kid to bed, so when I came back, finishing it was a snap.
*Since we don't refrigerate eggs here, I suppose that cooking them from room temp rather than cold could have made a small difference in timing.
I rarely make soft-boiled eggs. But I do make hard-boiled eggs and I imagine this advice applies equally: make them in a pressure cooker and they will peel smoothly and easily.
Any advice for jammy eggs with an induction cooktop would be appreciated.  I’ve had no success boiling eggs on induction.

Accidentally overcooked the eggs b/c I'm in Denver and the recipe is for sea level.  Just a little overdone so I will reduce cooking time.

Didn't have chipotle in adobo so I mixed morita (ground black and red chipotle) with adobo seasoning, cider vinegar, and tomato paste.  Worked beautifully.

Super easy and tasty!

I’ve found a lot of success with steaming eggs in a simple vegetable steamer. Not sure why but the shells peal more cleanly for me anyway.  And while the Times makes a deal about “tearing the eggs by hand” it sure is not how they look in the photo!  Just saying..
Asparagus with sauce gribiche is an old French classic and perhaps a nicer way to combine hard cooked eggs and asparagus.
I am not a big fan of Adobo... suggestions anyone?
Not a huge adobo fan either.  We made it with chili crisp and substituted honey for the maple syrup.  We loved it.

I used broccoli rabe. I think probably it’s better with asparagus, but it was good, served with crusty bread. My egg yolks were more liquid than jammy, but I like that anyway. When I put my eggs directly from the refrigerator into the boiling water, it took quite a while for them to boil again— pretty much the full seven minutes.
7 minutes is too long. 6:30 to 6:45 is jammier better tastier. Otherwise good salad.
Like so many Ottolenghi recipes, this was beautiful and delicious, but unusually was also easy to make.  Perfect for a light lunch.
This was absolutely delicious. I did not have asparagus or basil but it was perfect nonetheless. I will make the recipe in its entirety for a first course dinner.
Question for everyone - In Step 2, do you transfer all the oil along with the asparagus to the bowl?  Or use a slotted spoon to transfer just the asparagus?  Thanks.
This was really tasty, but a very light meal. I added farro with olive oil, pistachios and grated parm as the side.

title: Smoky Tomato Pasta With Shrimp and Almonds Recipe
author: Yotam Ottolenghi

comments.commentBody: Made as written with the addition of a pinch of chili flakes in the sauce prior to blending. Really really tasty. I think I would use a little less liquid next time as the pasta was done before enough liquid had been absorbed. I was dubious before I put it all together as the pasta sauce seemed quite plain, but once the dish was complete I had no qualms at all. The juxtaposition of crunchy almonds, sweet shrimp and creamy pasta is really interesting and has a great mouthfeel. The flavours all work together too. It was not too oily as the oil emulsifies into the sauce. Not sure what is so confusing about the instructions, all was perfectly clear to me, especially after reading the article which explained much of the logic of the recipe. Can't go wrong with Ottolenghi, the man is a genius.

Re earlier comments - it is confusingly written, but I think he means to use dried long pasta and the “shimmying” is just poking at it as it cooks so that less cooked parts sticking up are submerged in the sauce.

I need clarification on this: "Add the pasta, stirring once the pasta has started to soften, then shimmy the uncooked noodles into the sauce and cook, stirring occasionally, until al dente, about 6 minutes." Anyone?
@Daphne NYC. I agree that it reads leaving you to say, “Uh?”. After reading Jeff’s comment below, it makes sense.  “shimming it an odd word choice for this action.  Maybe someone will change it.
I think this would be better (shrimpier!) if it used a stock made from the shrimp heads and shells than with chicken stock.

@MER Not everyone has the time and many who do have the time likely do it that way. However, the chix stock is a great ingredient for this or anytime you need stock if you don’t have a home made one ready.

This is an absolutely delicious, restaurant-quality dish. I live in coastal GA where shrimp are delicious, plentiful, and always come with their shells on. I added the shells, a bay leaf, some pepper corns, and salt to the 2 C of water called for in the recipe. This amped up the sauce's flavor. A good shake of red pepper flakes did more of the same. Yes, there was extra sauce left after the pasta was cooked, but it went into the freezer to be used another day. Perhaps on a pizza with clams...
We also want to know if the pasta is cooked separately or in the blenderized sauce.
@David. Your answer is in the head note and the recipe.
@Gale You are correct, but the instructions should stand on their own.

I was also confused at step 3, but after seeing Deb's take that reading the article explained what author was doing, I went back and sure enough that clarified it. I still believe the instructions should be re-written.
Too much time. Too much oil.

I’m confused by step 3. Does the “remaining chicken stock plus 2-1/2 cups water” go into a separate pot for the initial cooking of the pasta, after which the partially cooked pasta is transferred to a separate pot containing the sauce?
@Margaret I’m confused about this too!

No you just add to the prior ( blended) sauce to increase the amount of liquid, then add the dry pasta to cook in the resulting combined liquid. In other words you are cooking the dry pasta directly in the sauce.

I’ve been cooking for about 75% of my 75 years, always  looking for something unique with interesting flavors and willing to work to get there. This recipe looked unusual but odd and I should have trusted my experience. No pasta will cook fully in that amount of sauce or that amount of time. There’s not enough lime to taste its brightness, not enough garlic for punch. But the method itself is absurd, going to silly lengths for a mediocre dish. Oh, and leaving the almonds with the shrimp will overcook the almonds. Just silly.
@Melissa The angel hair pasta does indeed cook that quickly. If you use a thicker pasta it will take longer.
@jbhstats helpful, I had forgotten it was Angel hair pasta, not a thicker variety.thanks!
yes i think you cook the pasta in the water n broth  but just to barely soften it and then drain in and add the el dente pasta to the sauce to finish cooking it

This may be the first Y.O. recipe I've run across that's totally flawed.  I mean, it's shrimp, garlic, and tomatoes, its not all bad.  But it's not good, either.  And it's messy, and not a "one pot" dish (your bowl is going to be covered with shrimpy oil).  There's a reason for all the rules of pasta that this recipe breaks.  

Would not make again.

@David
Agree with you, this was really nothing special
That’s a lot of oil! Sounds good but not sure it’s healthy for someone with heart disease. Did anyone use less?
@Jeanine M i used way less. Not a problem. Just enough to lightly cover the pan.
This looks good. However, step 3 is confusing. I assume it should read "In a new pot, combine the remaining chicken stock with 2½ cups of water and bring to a boil."

I really really enjoyed this. Fried almonds and shrimp separately in the oil and fished them out as I did not want the almonds to burn. It looks like a basic tomato sauce but the taste is much more exciting with the paprika, cilantro, lime and toasty almond flavor. Will definitely make again, possibly adding some broccoli or asparagus for the last couple minutes of cooking to up the vegetable content. (This dish would also be great with lightly charred roasted veggies as a side.)

Sounds like an impressive dish, although I'd forgive a busy person for wanting to prepare this for Saturday lunch rather than a weeknight. 

Previous Ottolenghi recipes call for the shrimp shells to be fried in oil (occasionally with a splash of Pernod). I wonder if he encourages this flavor enhancement, especially since he's asking for the pasta to be cooked in diluted chicken stock.

This is very good, but honestly, the shrimp mixture completed in step 1 is exceptional and by far the best component of this dish while the pasta and sauce are just okay. I subbed pepitas for almonds, arugula for cilantro, and spaghetti for cappellini because they were what I had, and added some grated parm at the end. If you want to save yourself a significant amount of time and effort but still end up with something delicious, make step 1 and serve it over much simpler pasta with a white wine and olive oil based sauce, maybe with some burst cherry tomatoes.
Has anyone made this using less olive oil?
Nice flavor, but too oily and heavy for the shrimp and the bright flavor of the tomatoes is lost. I'd

This was very good! I cut down half of the olive oil, didn’t have a lime so used lemon instead, had no cilantro so added a few dried parsley flakes at the end. It still turned out very tasty, and will definitely make again!

I haven’t yet made this but it appears that in Step 3 you add the pasta to the sauce, stir it once when it begins to soften (angel hair pasta is thin, delicate, and cooks quickly, THEN when it says to “shimmy the pasta into the sauce”, they mean to gently shake the skillet (while holding the handle) until the pasta settles down into the sauce to continue cooking.

We love Ottolenghi. We buy his cookbooks. We guffaw at the title “Simple,” for he is anything but. But when I saw a “weeknight” Ottolenghi dinner, I forwarded to my wife — great! Here’s a great way to use the shrimp in our freezer. Originally, somewhere in this article it said it comes together in 30 mins — this was wisely edited out. While we are eating this on a weeknight, this is no weeknight meal, at least not in our house. Be forewarned, this was 90 mins, and we cut corners.

I mostly followed the directions but used spaghetti (rather than cappelli), regular  fish (rather than chicken) broth, and two cans of crushed tomato.     The taste was great, esp the aroma of the almonds and the smoked paprika— very, very tasty.  Next time I would sub fish broth for at least some of the water at the end, since I like a seafood flavor throughout.   Also, I’d delete one onion (my wife and I thought two was too much).   Note that the spaghetti takes about 5minutes longer to cook in sauce than in water.    But SO TASTY!!!
What does “the oil has started to split” mean?
zach, I think it means when you see that the oil has separated from the tomato sauce. It will "un-emulsify," and you'll re-emulsify with a hand blender.
For those questioning the instructions, read the accompanying article and it will make more sense.

Article mentions coriander then the recipe calls for fennel seeds?
Any explanation?

Coriander in the UK, where Ottolenghi lives and works, in leaf form is the same as cilantro in the US, which is in the recipe.  (Coriander seeds are the same in the UK and US.) I think it's just a miss by the editors...
It's referring to the cilantro stirred through the shrimp. In Europe we call cilantro coriander.
fresh cilantro is referred to as coriander in some countries as it is in fact the same plant, just different parts (seeds vs leaves)

heat the olive oil in a medium saucepan at high heat!?  Olive oil has a relatively low burn temperature, compared to say peanut oil.  Just what should the temperature be before adding the almonds and the shrimp?

i added the almonds before the oil was either smoking or shimmering, i think that’s most important. (add one almond to test the temp if needed, it should bubble a bit.) once it has something substantial to transfer heat to i don’t think there’s any remaining risk of the oil burning
Can it work with what you actually keep on hand -which is frozen shrimp?
@VG all shrimp is frozen in the boats when harvested so yes.
20 min prep and 40min cook for a Tuesday dinner, 60min for a weekday dinner, get real!  It does sound good though.
too much salt! too much oil

i was at the step of reducing the tomatoes before i realized i was gonna be adding angel hair to a medium saucepan… glad to report is works as written. i used barilla protein+ which maybe can’t be al denté (first time using it) it was done around the 4 min mark. to me, “weeknight” as a descriptor is aspirational in the + and - sense. perfect middle ground of the familiarity of “pasta w/ tomato sauce” and something unexpected and refreshing with the lime/cilantro.
I’m allergic to nuts. Any suggestions on a substitute?
Toasted fresh breadcrumbs (search for pangrattato) is quite common and provides a similar effect.

Re comment by "David," "I don't understand the method in step 3." It just means put the stiff dry noodles in the pot with the sauce: "shimmy" just means put them in and press them down into the sauce quickly as they soften, e.g, by pressing down on the top ends with a wooden spoon. Another point: for health reasons, I would reduce the quantity of olive oil by about half. I do that with all the fat in non-baking recipes--I like to maintain my 30/31 inch waist size (male).
I don't understand this....add the pasta and when it softens, shimmy the uncooked noodles. What uncooked pasta.  I'm assuming once the pasta softens, it's cooked. So what is the uncooked pasta?

Dish is delish when done. The starch from the pasta makes the sauce extra rich. Made with Jovial GF rotini shaped pasta. 

The directions ARE confusingly written. Biggest suggestion: Cook the almonds separately in the oil after cooking shrimp in the oil. Otherwise, the almonds will get burned.

I followed the directions (once you get to the point at which you’re directed to “shimmy” the pasta in the saucepan it really makes sense! Haha.) and was heavily complimented by everyone who tried the dish (four of us on a Tuesday night)!! In all honesty, I did substitute the chicken stock and water for store-bought fumet (fish bones broth), which in Spain is readily available everywhere. I also added a couple of hot pepperoncini and ground them coarsely with the two teaspoons of fennel seeds, which I first thought would be overwhelming but no, the amount is correct. I found the “good” capellini, which took exactly the time the recipe says to be done to perfection. Had I read Ottolenghi’s recommendation to fry the shrimp heads and tails in the olive oil before anything else, I would have done it… I can totally see what it would do for the flavor. Total yum!

The creaminess of this dish checks the box for comfort food.  I really did marvel how all of this came together.  I used 2 cups of water and it was not soupy at all.  I omitted the fennel as I am not a fan.  I felt the lime was a bit of a strong flavor and am inclined to use lemon next time.

This was delicious! It tastes like a rich cream sauce at the end. The lime is essential. I doubled the garlic and like Deb also added half teaspoon of chili flakes in the sauce before blending. Be careful of burning the almonds in the first step on high heat. No angel hair on hand last night but spaghetti worked just as well.

I made this on a Tuesday night, followed (almost) all directions, and got heavy praise from the friends who tried it! In all honesty, I subbed the chicken stock and water for "fumet" (fish bone stock), which is readily available everywhere in Spain. I also added two pepperoncini that made it spicy, but not hot. Used the "good" capellini (Nº2 semola di grano), which was done to perfection in four minutes. I wish I'd fried the shrimp heads before in the olive oil, as per the article, though.

This is outstanding! The only issue is keeping the shrimp and almond mixture hot while you cook the rest of the dish. I ended up adding a few spoonfuls of the hot tomato sauce in and then setting the dish over the boiling pasta for the last minute or so.

Super confusing - I made the same mistake. It should read in step 3 - IN A SEPARATE PAN - boil the water and chicken broth. Boil noodles. Then after they are cooked add them to the sauce. Otherwise you do what I did and dilute the sauce with 2.5 cups water and 1 cup chicken stock.

Made as written after reading the instructions multiple times during the process. During the preparation I kept thinking the dish wouldn’t be worth all the effort. Just a little too weird. But in the end it was really delicious. Would I make it again, probably not.

Followed recipe but sub'd 1 tsp powdered fennel for seeds. Moved ingredients to large saucepan before blending. Step 3 is too confusing & others' comments add to confusion. Decided to go with my initial guess and added remaining broth & H2O to blended tomato/onion mixture. Added dry capeilini to sauce mixture; after 3 minutes, used tongs to separate pasta and mix/shimmy it in the sauce while cooking. Doing it all: about 2 hrs total. Very tasty.

Loved this! I pre-treated shrimp by velveting in baking soda so they were firmer and plumper! His timing suggestions re: cooking time are spot on (as usual). Best to prep everything and treat it like a stir-fry. Loved the crunch from the almonds.
Good, not great. And not exactly quick or easy even if it was one pan. I followed the advice of a commenter to reduce the water but it was a bit dry, should have followed the recipe.

Angel hair is one of the most difficult pasta shapes to cook as quickly overcooks, dries up and form a blob of strands difficult to untangle and mix with the sauce, especially a sauce with large pieces of fish in it. Better choose spaghettini or thin spaghetti, which are a bit thicker than angel hair and more manageable. Always keep a cup of cooking water to help at mixing...
There is a massive difference in the flavor profiles of sweet and smoked paprika.  Which kind was used by Ottolenghi when he created this recipe?

IMO, a bit too complicated.  
- Cook the pasta and sauce separately then combine
- Sauce had too much chicken stock and onion for our taste
- Flavoring the oil with the almonds is fine, but do it first, then remove almonds with slotted spoon, THEN cook the shrimp.  Per recipe the almonds will end up saturated with oil, dark and with burnt taste.
- We used TOASTED sliced almonds (buy toasted or do it yourself) and add then to the shrimp mixture just before serving to preserve their crunchiness.

This is an excellent recipe and a restaurant quality dish. I made it using the directions for half the recipe and made very few changes to it. I saw the comments about the amount of liquid added to the sauce in step 3 and decided to cut it back by about half. Other than that I added a dash of pepper flakes and a quarter cup of vermouth to the sauce. I add these things to every pasta dish so that’s just me. I do think the instructions for step 3 could be a bit clearer but after reading a couple of times I did get it. Finally, have to say that even with a well stocked spice cabinet did not have the smoked sweet or hot paprika. Used sweet Hungarian paprika but wondered where the Smokey flavour was. That’s on me.

Absolutely delicious. I followed the recipe exactly, with the exception of the pasta. I used spaghetti instead of angel hair, and I wouldn't do that next time as it took a long time to cook and I had to add water to the sauce. However, the starch from the pasta thickened the sauce and the result was amazing.
A friend made this last night and it was soooo good! I highly recommend it.

Completely delicious and yes, confusing directions!

Everyone at our dinner loved this entree!

Suggest that you cook the almonds at the END of cooking the shrimp. I tried per the recipe and burned the almonds. You could also just toast the almonds in a pan.

You are making the pasta in the same pot that you're making the rest of the recipe in.

You are correct:  Step 3 is confusing.  Ottolenghi's note is clarifying. Cooking the pasta in the sauce is good, but there's too much liquid at the end.  I would prefer a thicker sauce.

I left the tails on the shrimp, which may have improved the taste of the sauce, but then the shrimp are a nuisance to eat.

In Step 3, add remaining stock and 2 1/2 C water to pan and bring to boil; add pasta, and when it starts to soften, stir it into the liquid (some may be sticking out above the surface of the liquid). Slide (shimmy) the pan around on the burner to jostle and submerge exposed pasta so it cooks evenly (you could certainly aid in that by pushing it down into the liquid), then stir occasionally until al dente.

Made as written, and would do so again.  Next time, I’ll add the shrimp as soon as the almonds start to turn blond — they were *this* close to being burned.  I’d also add about twice as much cilantro (blessed with the I Love Cilantro Gene) , and keep it out until the end.  Mixing it with the acidic lime juice turned it an unattractive khaki color.  A very good recipe, with excellent flavor.
Disappointing recipe. Not quick, unnecessarily complicated, odd flavors.
This was fantastic! Made as described but substituted orecchiete for the angel hair pasta. The combo of fennel and paprika is wonderful.

title: Stewed Greens and Chickpeas With Toasted Garlic and Lemon Recipe
author: Yotam Ottolenghi

comments.commentBody: Something that goes very well with chickpeas, greens and chicken broth and already has the fennel is Italian sausage. I'll be making this, but will add some in for a little fatty-meat goodness (and I prefer cutting the cased examples into 1/2" slices instead of using the loose stuff).
I tend to skip the stories or explanations that usually accompany recipes, but I read Mr Ottolenghi's with great pleasure - a chef with a poetical heart.
I haven't made this yet, but to those who have, one Cup of fresh Oregano??? Isn't that excessive? It sounds crazy!
No, it's very good. I'm not sure how the 1 oz. oregano I used compares to what he intended, but I'm very pleased with the result.
@Jax a container of fresh oregano at my store is 19g so this covers it.
@Jax I planted an oregano seedling last year and it’s turned into a bush despite neglect.  I welcome any chance to prune

Super fresh and tasty soup! I used orzo as it was all I had on hand, but it worked perfectly. I also skipped the second teaspoon of salt, I think two would be a little too much. I agree with others that good quality stock is a must - but unlike others I didn’t have too little liquid, it was just right. Perhaps because I used orzo instead of a larger pasta. I added more garlic, and more zest, which turned out well. Sour cream and lemon at the end was a great touch!

I made this last night, and my diners and I declared it "excellent."  Because my regular grocery store didn't have any fresh oregano :O, I had to make an on-the-spot substitution as dinner was in 2 hours... so I bought thyme.  I share the question of "a whole cup of loose oregano leaves."  Is that really true?  To err on the safe side,I settled for 2 T of the thyme.  But I'd love to know if anyone actually used that much, because perhaps it could be even more delicious.

I liked the whole cup of oregano. Only half goes in the soup. The other half sautéed with garlic and drizzled on top. It did pretty much wipe out my patio pot of oregano. Used veg stock because I wanted it parve. For sure, chicken stock would have been tastier. Still we liked it.

I made this with 2 .5 oz. packages of discounted oregano (1 oz. total), a mix of greens and pastina. Very good flavor and a strong comfort food sensibility. The 1/2 t. red pepper flakes and 2 t. salt were perfect, to my taste. Important note: it almost overflowed my 3.5 quart Le Creuset. Definitely needs a 4-5 quart pot. I'm still pondering a future instant pot version and may very well use quinoa instead of pasta next time, for more nutritional heft. About 75 min. to make.

@Anna Broadway
I used my 3.5 quart Le Creuset.  I put the greens in first, put the cover for a couple minutes, and let them wilt. Had plenty of room for the rest of ingredients after that.

I am sitting at the table eating this as
I write and think it’s delicious. My family loved it. I used orzo. And the greens I had on hand. It’s a great way to use up remainders of greens. I didn’t notice the quantity of fresh oregano the recipe called for and that sounds a bit overwhelming. I just used what I had and threw some dried oregano in with the sautéing onions. Plus a spot of turmeric which gave it a lovely color. I think I went a little heavy on the olive oil and would moderate that. I threw the greens in at the end and served right away. We skipped the sour cream because the soup was so simply delicious without. A good, warm baguette would have been nice. A perfect winter soup. Very easy for a weeknight. I’ll be making again.

I have an awful cold and limited items in the house. I threw a can of chickpeas, some orzo, a spoon of Better Than Bullion veggie broth in a pot. Added some dried parsley, lemon juice, and Parmesan at the end. I don't know why this was so delicious but for such little effort it is a keeper! When I am feeling better I will make it as instructed but just know you can go real basic and still enjoy it.
One cup fresh oregano leaves and one and half cups fresh parsley? Are you kidding? It's mid-winter.
@Ed Easy to get at a Whole Foods or Trader Joe’s.  I’m in California, maybe it depends upon where you live?
Even in Anchorage, I was able to find these. ;) Definitely glad I used the full amounts. Gave it lots of flavor and nutrition.
On day 3 I chopped up some hot Italian sausage  to put in for lunch. Made a great recipe even better. Will be making this again.

Made this when winter returned to Maine after two warm days.Truly delightful. Made as directed, used cannellini beans, rainbow chard and acini de pepe for the pasta. Used a whole bunch of parsley and three packs of oregano totalling a cup. Don’t be afraid of all the oregano as the fresh is milder than the dried. Strip it off the woody stalks…the smaller stems attaching to the stalks are ok to use. Watch the garlic for the topping as it cooks quickly and too brown means bitterness. Not a weeknight meal if you work. A lot of prep! But so worth it and especially if you haven’t been eating your greens. Don’t skip the lemon and sour cream. I used creme fraiche. Nutritional powerhouse. Will definitely make again! Thanks, Mr. Ottolenghi.
Is it me, or is this soup high in fat and salt?
@Metsfan41  Yes, the nutritional info lists 31 grams of fat and 1035 mg sodium! I plan try making this with low-sodium chicken broth, less olive oil and no sour cream.
@Metsfan41 yes it’s comfort food.  Of course adapt as needed but you don’t question a macaroni and cheese recipe that is high fat.

I believe the fats are all pretty healthy (and hello with satiation). I used chickpeas I'd cooked from dry and found the salting perfect. I usually have to add more to recipes. Saltiness does vary by type, I believe, so that may be part of it.
I like the idea of this recipe but cannot eat oregano. Wondering about substitutions...basil? Tarragon? Sorrel if I can find it?

I thought there was too much pasta in the recipe and not enough heat. Next time I'll definitely only do a half a cup of pasta at most and try to bump up the red chili flakes. I also wish it had more protein in there besides the chickpeas and bet that a spicy Italian sausage of sorts would play nice in the recipe. It was good though and I would definitely make it again.

I followed Independent Observer's lead because I had some Italian sausage meat in the fridge that needed to be used up. Spinach because no chard at the grocer's today. Thai basil because ditto for oregano and I didn't want to used dried oregano. Orzo because that's what's in my pantry. Generous (although not excessive) with the lemon zest and added lemon juice to taste in the pot at the end (I'm guessing 1/4 -1/3 cup). Wowzers. Don't skip the garlic oil, into which I dipped warmed naan.

This was truly a delight! It sounded so simple yet the layering of ingredients was sublime. A flavor powerhouse. I also questioned the amount of fresh oregano but it makes the dish. I couldn't find the smaller pasta so used orzo and it was lovely. I used Greek yogurt vs sour cream. Delicious and also not to be missed. Will make again & again.
Wow.  Highly recommended.  The herbs seem excessive, but it results in one of the most delicious, intense, and healthful soups you could imagine.

This requires a very flavourful stock and top quality chickpeas . For me the 10 minute cooking time for the pasta was too long . There was also too little liquid at the end . The sour cream didn’t add anything extra . 
Not a recipe I’ll be adding to my box .
Very tasty, but took a very long time to make - over an hour. I added spinach to my bowl of soup instead of chard in the pot. Sprinkled my serving with grated parm.
My family doesn't like fennel. Any good substitutes? Just read about dill as a possibility.
@Intuitive cook celery will work.

@Intuitive cook
I used anise seed. I’m not a fennel fad, but I do love anise.
Made the recipe exactly as it was written and it was excellent!! Greater than the sum of its parts.

I really wanted to try this recipe but I did not have all the ingredients available.  So, I used dried oregano (in a smaller amount) instead of fresh, cannelini beans instead of chickpeas, black kale instead of Italian kale, orzo,  and powdered (ugh!) chicken stock. I know right? That was a lot of substitutions. But it was all in the same category...sorta.

You know what? It was delicious!

I am not an bean fan but cannelini beans were meant to be in this dish. I will not make this dish any other way!
Delicious - will go into my favorites folder. I also questioned the amount of oregano but it was fantastic with the fennel. Perfect for a grey, cold spring evening.
I forgot to get tiny pasta so used bigger ones (some penne and some gemelli) but that doesn’t even matter because this soup is a total flavor bomb!! Wow. Amazing. Incredible.

I found this to be fantastic! I only had one bunch of rainbow chard, but with all the other greens it seemed sufficient. It was a bit thick/short on broth  and I might back off the pasta to 3/4 of a cup next time. Used al the oregano. I think the lemon and the sour cream really added depth and brightness. So good.
This was awesome! I only had dried oregano and didn't go overboard with it, but we still loved it.

Delicious! Broth is so flavorful with all of the herbs and spices! Skipped the oil on top and subbed Parmesan cheese for the sour cream. Used macaroni noodles which worked great too. Was fast to make and would definitely recommend/will make again!

This is shockingly more delicious than the sum of its parts. I did use all the oregano (its not too much at all) and this is a great way to use up all other early spring greens too: instead of just chard I used a mixture of the mustard greens, arugula and chard that were coming up in my garden. Don't skip the oil at the end, in fact make extra and dip bread into it. Delicious!

This was fabulous!  I added small turkey/hot pork sausage meatballs balls to it. I grow oregano, so getting a cup was no problem. It is best the first day, but that has not stopped me from eating it as leftovers.

I made this last night--I'm glad he had weight for the oregano as I would have used far less. I used the cooking liquid from the chickpeas and some water. My garlic garnish burnt a bit, which I suspected it would.  Next time, I will use lots of thin slices instead. Also, I'll make that first, and put it aside so I don't need to pull out a second pan.

Delicious variation of the usual “beans and greens” stew. Fennel seeds added an unexpected perfect licorice element; drained the beans and added appropriate extra water to total; 1 tsp salt; used chard but next time will use the mature spinach I’m getting from our CSA; finally, the key is to add all that golden garlic at the end, along with the oil (I smash garlic cloves instead of slicing so they don’t burn but get sweet and soft); tiny whisper of sour cream; maldon salt finish.

So good! I actually forgot to purchase oregano- there was a small one at the store and instead of picking it up I looked for more then forgot! So I had to use dried instead. I also added cooked ground Italian sausage at the very end. For greens, red chard and grown spinach (not baby). It was less soupy than I expected, which is an easy fix for leftovers- more broth/stock! The squeeze of lemon and a dollop of Daisy was excellent.

title: Spiced Potato Bites With Yogurt Recipe
author: Yotam Ottolenghi

comments.commentBody: Curious about sunchokes, I clicked on the instacart for this recipe. Sunchokes are unavailable, and alternatives are similarly unavailable. What is this ingredient, where can I find it, what is an alternative?
@June root of a sunflower plant. Roast deliciously. Excellent pre-biotic.
@June also called Jerusalem artichokes
@June I think they are called Jerusalem artichokes in the USA.

This recipe is unnecessarily complex.  I have been roasting very tasty potatoes for 30 years and these directions make it a bit of an ordeal. As an aside, for a snack or as a side course, if you have an air fryer the process becomes much faster.

What's complex? You roast the potatoes, covered with foil, then partway through you add in the sunchokes. Otherwise, it's about the spicy yogurt sauce. So many roasted veggies require adjusted times, so that is hardly fussy or time-consuming.

@Paul Marc this is an Ottolenghi recipe. You could reasonably call all of his stuff “complex”. Lots of steps, interrupted steps, small ingredient amounts etc. 

Also, please dont do this recipe in an air fryer. This recipe is the opposite of that. And no air fryer can support 2.5lbs of potatoes anyway.

I routinely pre-cook potatoes in the microwave before roasting them with seasonings.  This serves the same function as roasting them covered (i.e. steaming).  Saves time & foil & also allows them to be added to the same pan as other vegetables that don’t require as much roasting time.

@YiaYia Agreed.  The best roasted potatoes I have ever had which were beautifully crisped on the outside and pillowy soft and still moist on the inside were steamed first then finished in the oven.   I usually steam on the stove but steaming with a lid on in the microwave would be faster.

Just so you know, the nickname for sunchokes is fartichokes
If you can actually find them, beware, they taste great but don't plan on being around anyone for awhile.
It's way worse than beans.
@KOGNYC so very true. I made sunchoke soup and it had a nuclear impact on my GI. They taste great but not for me!
@KOGNYC Exactly. I’m not sure I’d want to sit next to someone on the sofa, or anywhere, after ingesting sunchokes!!

@KOGNYC - I was curious about this vegetable and learned that they have inulin - a carb that humans can’t digest - must be broken down by gut bacteria.   Warning to not overdo it if not a regular part of diet or can cause stomach pain from gas

Here's the conversion to metric for other interested non-US readers:
400°F is 205°C
1.15 kg Yukon Gold potatoes, peeled and cut into 4 cm to 5 cm pieces
80 ml olive oil, plus 15 ml to serve
Flaked sea salt
285 g sunchokes, washed and sliced into half-centimeter pieces (or equal weight potatoes)
10G oregano leaves 
6g white sesame seeds 
2g coriander seeds 
1.5 g cumin seeds 
10 g finely chopped dill 
180 ml plain yogurt
@Kevin The NYT Cooking section now allows readers to switch to metric and the conversions, in my experience, have been excellent.

@Paul Marc

Yes, to the right of the Ingredients heading, there is a small ruler icon. Click on that to select metric.

Thanks Kevin for the conversions- they are helpful as the browser only version of NYT cooking does not display the conversion tool and seems specific to the app version. I am sure this is much appreciated by those fatigued with yet another app download and related privacy headaches.
For those interested in culinary history, this is an Arab Palestinian recipe that has been adapted to be a bit more gourmet.
@Elliot, I really don’t think that a recipe needs to become political

@Yaël A. @Elliot said “for those interested in culinary history.” I for one am interested in understanding the origins of foods from different cultures and regions. Being interested in that is not political, it’s a sign of respect in wanting to understand other ethnicities which can include culinary delights. You are the one turning something scholarly into something political. It’s food- lighten up.
just tried this using mini baby potatoes bc I didnt have sunchokes  - PHENOMENAL!!!
Would smallish turnips be a good substitute for sunchokes? I think they're lower in carbs.
They'd be tasty, and yes, lower carb, but wouldn't crisp up as well due to higher water content.
@Vivianparsnip might be a better choice.
I’m guessing it’s fresh oregano?
Why peel the potatoes?
I called my local Boise Co-op and they just had local Sunchokes delivered yesterday.  I'm excited to try these in this recipe.
I agree about the potatoes , the seed mixture is good in small doses, I would cut the amount way down. With yogurt it is delicious.
Sunchokes are also called topinambur.
@kevin, thanks, NYT could so easily have a widget to convert the measurements automatically, it’s not difficult but is a bit of a pain doing individually.
@steveeb they do. The ruler icon does the calculation.

I've never eaten a sunchoke, but tried growing them much to my regret as they took over the garden quickly and I had to dig them up to get rid of them.  I like roasting parsnips with potatoes.  They are much more easily found in US stores and I do add them after the potatoes have a head start since they cook more quickly. Parsnips give a nice caramely slightly sweet taste that enhances the more bland potato taste.

This past summer I planted my first sunchoke (aka Jerusalem artichoke) plant. After studying up on how to prepare the tubers I bumped into a very danged funny reddit discussing the - ahem - after-effects of eating them (https://www.reddit.com/r/Cooking/comments/q0g40t/jerusalem_artichokessunchokes_how_to_avoid_farts/). Inulin is the enemy, but apparently low-and-slow baking beats it back. But they really are delicious!

Sunchokes are still available this time of year, however, probably not at your regular big shop grocer.  More like weekend farmers markets. In some places they are plenty available and in others, not so much.  Depends on location and demand in your area.
I suspect this recipe would be good with potato and rutabaga or celery root.
Sunchokes invaded my front yard (in MN) a few years ago. They are BEAUTIFUL sunflowers that spread like wildfire. I love the fartchokes comment!
In step 2, when you return them to the oven, do you cover them with foil again?
@Richard no. Uncovered so they crisp.
IMHO depends on how much they're browning.
Foil on or off for the second (45 min) go in the oven?
Stir halfway through; doesn't sound like the foil is on.
Sunchokes are the tuberous roots of sunflowers.

Not completely accurate. Sunchokes (Helianthus tuberosus) are also known as Jerusalem artichokes. They are in the sunflower family and are known as a root vegetable. The tubers of all sunflowers are NOT edible, but you can eat the sunchokes without worry (other than intestinal upset).
@steveeb the app has a toggle which switches between US and metric
why would you wanna peel the potatoes though? i do similar dished with well washed (brush) potatoes

The first time I had sun chokes I went all in with a big bowl full. They were delicious but If it is your first time I suggest going smaller.  Digestive upset is an understatement. I thought I was going to die. They are no longer in my cooking repertoire.
They contain Inulin which is a Fructan which causes digestive upsets in many people.

Has anyone tried this? I would think that roasting cubed potatoes for over an hour would make them exceptionally hard on the outside. As for Sunchoke substitutes ( they have been known to cause extreme after effects not worthy of their flavor). What about carrots or parsnips? They would pair well with the potatoes and the yogurt dip.

What does “sliced into 1/4 inch pieces” mean (re the Jerusalem artichokes)?  There are no slices in the photo and 1/4 inch cubes would not be finger food fit for dipping. More precise language please!

@Charli

I don’t think this is finger food. I use a spoon when I’m eating vegetable hash on the couch. Also, a bowl. And I drizzle mine with steak sauce, but I’m going to try this yogurt. Also, tzatziki, hummus, babaganoush . . .
Sounds great! When returning the potatoes to the oven with the sunchokes, should the tray again be covered with foil?
Stir halfway and cook until golden: neither would work if the foil were on.
Wondering if anyone has tried this substituting yams for the sunchokes? I assume you would probably roast the yams at the same time as the potatoes instead of later.
@June: I would substitute peeled parsnips (small to medium, firm, and smooth roots) for a change of pace.
@June Sunchokes taste a lot like artichokes...maybe you could sub artichoke hearts.  I've seen them frozen, canned and jarred but the canned and jarred versions can have an oil or vinegar base.
I would not suggest using canned/jarred/frozen artichokes as a sub because they do not roast well in the way that the potatoes would.

@TheBlackSquirrel
I love roasting jarred artichokes! Put them in later as they only take 15 mins or so.
June: just add a like amount of extra potatoes.
I found sunchokes at a Chicago suburban Whole Foods this week.
You can order them on Amazon, 2.59 a pound and up. My go to source for unusual, hard to find ingredients.
when sunchokes aka Jerusalem artichokes are in season, Fresh Direct often carries them

What’s with the 2/12 ponds of potato’s and 10 oz of sunchokes  ?
Not everyone has, wants or needs a kitchen scale. How about a universal cooking language like: two or three mid sized potatoes or 4 sunchokes depending on how many people are served upping quantity as needed.
Cooking is not a lab science as baking is,

Here in South Texas, one 'mid-sized' potato can weigh in at around 2 pounds. Back in DC, a 'mid-sized' weighed much less. 'Mid-sized' is way too vague a description to be universally useful. Pounds and ounces (or their metric equivalents) are much more accurate and very useful. Scales are usually found in the produce isle, where you bought your vegetables. Measure them when you buy them.

@2cents - I didn't realize there was a toggle until I switched from the browser version to the app version. I was tired of  seeing recipes not refreshing despite clearing the cache - so I reluctantly switched to the app.

I too had to search sunchokes....we call them Jerusalem Artichokes in the UK!!
The arrive in my organic veg box in the early spring and make delicious gratins and soups.  They are neither artichokes nor from Jerusalem...this is thought to come from the French Girasole (sunflower) being the family to which they belong.
I never comment but I made these as described but halved for just two people… omg….so good! Thank you for both the new way to make roast potatoes and the spices on this recipe. Chefs kiss!

I made these with all potatoes as I haven’t seen sunchokes in my neck of the woods for awhile. Otherwise followed the recipe exactly and it was beyond delicious. The potatoes were soft and creamy on the inside and perfectly crispy on the outside, and the crunchy spice blend on the yogurt, with a splash of quality olive oil, made the whole dish next-level. This is definitely going in the repertoire.
Am I to use fresh oregano since it is listed as “oregano leaves?”  I’m a veteran cook, but I find the term confusing.  I know the dill is fresh.
Any guidance how to prepare the yoghurt sauce?
@Noel I think it’s just step 4 - add the oil and leftover spices to the yoghurt?
I think you drizzle the extra tablespoon of oil over the yogurt (I'd stir it in) and then sprinkle it with the reserved spice mixture.
Any suggestions on a substitute for the sunchokes?
@NY Guy  In the list of ingredients, sunchokes are listed with an equal amount of potatoes in parentheses as an optional substitution.
Of course, as everything Ottolenghi, delicious. But swap the spuds for all sunchokes. Guilt-free, keto, fab.

It’s a bit confusing what you’re meant to do if you opt to make this without the Jerusalem artichokes (which are not yet in season here in NZ). I assume you’d just add the additional potatoes to the beginning of the recipe rather than adding them when you’d add the sunchokes?
If I can't find Jerusalem artichokes, I will try with parsnips. Also a sweet tasting vegetable especially when roasted.

To June, sunchokes, also known as Jerusalem artichokes, are part of the root of a type of sunflower. They are a winter crop and have a pleasant crunch and sweet flavor; and may be eaten raw or cooked. Perhaps Mr. Ottolenghi or another reader would have a substitute in mind for a cooked vegetable; I can think only of jicama and water chestnuts, which are normally eaten raw.

I love Yotam, I have all of his books. However, I ate at his restaurant in London this fall and was really disappointed at how under-seasoned every single thing that we ordered was (and we pretty much ordered the entire menu). This recipe tells me that I am correct in thinking that seeing that he calls for only 1/2 teaspoon of salt for over 4 pounds of vegetables (potatoe/sunchokes). As someone who has been cooking professionally for 30 years, I promise you that 1/2 teaspoon salt is not enough.
@Marie Gerard: The spice mix also includes 1-1/4 teas salt, and half of the mix is also added to the roasted vegs. Perhaps that still isn't enough salt, but it's a lot more than 1/2 teas in total!

Delicious.  Used 1 can artichoke hearts, halved.  For spices, used what we had.  Ground coriander, ground cumin, roasted sesame seeds, basil (didn’t have dill).  Mixed spices together, added some to cooked vegetables and the rest to the yogurt.  Potatoes were cooked perfectly.  Served with baked chicken wings (cooked in oven with potatoes).  Great dinner for a freezing winter’s night.
@Athena  Maybe next time use sunchokes, aka Jerusalem artichokes, as the recipe specifies, rather than artichokes, which are an unrelated vegetable?

I found the yogurt too salty and next time would add less. I liked the recipe. I used fresh oregano but wish the recipe specified fresh or dried. We ate these at the dining table over plates; I don't want to vacuum fresh dill from my couch.

I made this dish as written (except without the sunchokes because they're not available where I live so I subbed in 10 oz. more of the Yukon gold potatoes as the recipe notes) and it was pretty darn delicious. Just make it with all potatoes, people. No big whoop. It's good and you'll like it!
An outstanding healthy roast potato variation that is fun to make. You can play with the herbs and spice ratio after making it once.

We are lucky in Southern California to have access to sunchokes. This was delicious and I didn't find it unnecessarily complicated--unlike some Ottolenghi recipes:-/ Even a vegetable averse teenager had seconds.

My CSA had delivered a bunch of lovely sunchokes, and I really appreciated this elevated but simple approach to preparing them. the spice mix/yogurt sauce is delicious -- on both roasted sunchokes and on the roasted potatoes. Will make this again for sure!

title: Cheesy Cauliflower Nests Recipe
author: Yotam Ottolenghi

comments.commentBody: This recipe could use a video please

@Liz
Agreed, Liz. I’m having difficulty picturing this on so many levels.

@Liz
A 30 sec video has been added
Intrigued but utterly hung up on how to cut 2 cauliflowers through the core into 3 even wedges. And the pic isn’t helping.

@Jen moody Agree thirds are a bit messy. I’d instead cut the heads into quarters. And either make four nests from each head. Or, to finish with a similar quantity as the recipe, I’d divide that ‘extra’ (fourth) section into thirds and distribute to the other three sections. Depends on the size of the cauliflower…
@Jen moody. Two cauliflower, 3 pieces each, serves six

I had some trouble with the scrunching the phyllo part, so I put each nest in a two-cup ramekin and proceeded with the recipe as written. It was fabulous!  I had worried that the bottom might be soggy but it was nice and crispy. We just ate from the ramekins but they would unmold just fine.  We’ll be having this again ourselves snd keeping it handy for the next time we have vegetarian guests.
@Elizabeth so nice to see a comment about actually making the recipe!

I can’t make the words match the photograph. A wedge of cauliflower cut through the middle would be, surely, 8 to 10 inches tall. The phyllo “purses” pictured fit snugly in an 8x10 dish! Couldn’t the cauliflower just be cut into reasonably sized chunks—the heck with the core??!!
I’m uncertain about using just one phyllo sheet per nest, seems too fragile…

Thin k of this as only one possible blueprint and make the recipe your own if your concerned about one sheet of phyllo use two, if you can't figure out how to assemble line a dish with phyllo overhanging the dish layer in the cauliflower scrunch the overhanging phyllo into the dish and over the top leaving a few slits to pour in the custard if the slits disappear put the Custard in a turkey baster and add the custard though the phyllo.Make it an adventure, practice ahead for a reg. dinner night.
I’m equally stuck on wrapping the phyllo up around the cauliflower but leaving a finger width of space all around for the custard???
@Jenn E you don’t wrap the phyllo around the cauliflower, you just kind of scrunch it up like almost like a nest around the cauliflower.
This recipe is intriguing but a YouTube tutorial would help with managing the whole cauliflower cutting thing. I got totally lost on "scrunching" the phyllo...
@Freida. My thoughts exactly
Hard agree on the need for a video before I work up the courage to try this.

Agree with the comments on confusion on the assembly of this recipe. However it did spark a memory of a past Yotam Ottolenghi NYT Cooking recipe for a phyllo butternut and cheesy custard pie (https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/1020507-butternut-squash-and-fondue-pie-with-pickled-red-chiles?smid=ck-recipe-iOS-share). Think I’ll try that recipe using cauliflower inspired by this recipe…a mash up of the two.
@Lisa K that recipe is amazing

Some questions about the phyllo: Scrunch it up like a sheet of newspaper going into a fire? How does it become a nest? If you do it a day ahead, how does it stay flaky? And, finally, if each sheet is estimated to weigh more than an ounce (in the ingredient list), maybe it's a different kind of dough?

Calm down folks!  This looks intimidating, but actually easy to do and so delicious.  Living in hot humid Florida, I have been traumatized by phyllo and typically avoid it.  But I had a previously open package (for NYT Hetty Lui McKinnon's Chickpea, Spinach & Feta Pie also easy and excellent), craved the ingredients and textures of this and so decided to try it.  I'm so glad I did and will definitely make again.  Many thanks to Yotam Ottolenghi for sharing so many fabulous and creative recipes!
Has anyone tried this in a muffin tin with smaller chunks of cauliflower?  I noticed a mention of ramekins- but it seems like a muffin tin might be even better.
I’ve been cooking for over 50 years. And of used phyllo dough many times. This recipe is not written well. It feels like there’s some steps missing

Having used a quarter piece of cauliflower for each section I think it would be better (and easier to make) if the cauliflower is cut into florets and a small handful is used for each individual pie. The filo can just be scrunched around it for baking, this leaves enough of a gap for the custard to be added later, unless you like it with loads of sauce. The flavours were spot-on.
I find most of Yotam Ottolenghi’s recipes are unnecessarily fussy, albeit delicious.

Very confusing recipe. I agree with the confusion of others (assembly, how to cut wedges, etc.) but step 4 does not say how many sheets of phyllo sheets to use, or how many times to repeat. It came out okay, but not great and not at all like the photo.

The dish is fine. The recipe is just poorly written and it makes a number of assumptions, not least of which is that the reader knows how to handle phyllo pastry. Nothin especially “cheesy” about it. It’s essentially quiche batter poured in. The seasoning is important as the recipe doesn’t indicate when to season and if you don’t, you’ll end up with a very bland dish. All in all, many better things to do with cauliflower.
Don’t overthink the phyllo nests, it’s forgiving for sloppy execution, and comes out great!

I made this today. It’s very forgiving. I halved the recipe using one head of cauliflower split into four sections. I used 2 sheets of phyllo for each cauliflower section. Otherwise made the recipe as written. Absolutely delicious and a big hit.
Me too. I used two eggs and one yolk for the custard, and the rest I used the amounts listed when using “cook mode” x0.5. The custard filled the four nests about right.
