Thankfully, NYT Cooling has finally added the ability to tap on the picture in order to zoom in and see it more clearly. The wedge shapes show up in 3-dimensions and it appears there are several layers of Philo under each wedge. Now, I will give the recipe a try.
Not a fan, skip the nutmeg and cloves, use more cheese and add some Dijon, steer it down the cheese sauce, savory path.

I had no trouble assembling this dish, but we were disappointed with the results.  Despite the sharp cheddar and interesting spices, we thought it lacked flavor.  Maybe some fresh rosemary or an assortment of assertively flavored cheeses would help?
@Leigh totally agree.

I made this and many of the readers comments/concerns occurred with me. One sheet of phyllo was very fragile, went dark brown within 20 mins. The custard leaked out of the fragile nests but confirmed to the shape of the sheet pan, so looked fine. And tasted pretty good

@Elizabeth thank you for the suggestion to use ramekins! I used a sheet of phyllo and a chunk of cauliflower that fit snugly inside for each, and they came out really cute.

Next time I think I’ll add more flavourings to the custard - or skip it entirely and pour some kind of thick sauce into the pockets. All in all, I found the flavours a bit boring. I can hardly fault Ottolenghi, given how much I freestyled this recipe, though.

It's an yummy recipe but I'll tweak it next time. I cut the cauliflower into segments starting with scoring the bottom stem. I would cook the custard first to thicken it so it's not in the oven forever setting. I would also consider making another half recipe of custard for even more creaminess. Needs more salt and I would saute the garlic a little to mellow it.
Seems like too much fat - cheese, heavy cream, and butter. Nevermind 3 eggs plus 3 yolks.

Perhaps it was my oven, but these were browned and the cauliflower was tender by 20 minutes. Based on the other comments and the delicate nature of the phyllo dough, used 2 sheets per nest (which I cut in half). Use your instinct!
If you follow Instagram there is a video for this recipe today. Much easier when you see it!
I am puzzled. How do you cut a cauliflower into THREE even wedges? My limited abilities don't seem up to this. Wouldn't 4 even wedges be easier and make more sense - halving it and halving each half?
@Jen Moody-Serves 6. So you cut each cauliflower into 3 pieces. You end up with 6.
How many phyllo sheets per nest? I’ve made other things with phyllo before but the instructions give the number of sheets to be used. Thanks!
I suggest forgetting the phyllo nest and just making custards as described and baking in a water bath. However, I have not tried it yet myself.
How is this vegetarian with all these eggs?  Wouldn't it be possible to just use the cauliflower and spices in the phyllo cups?  And perhaps cheese for those lacto-vegetarians.
Vegetarian does not preclude eggs and dairy.
@Emily_tc maybe you’re confusing vegetarian with vegan? One eats eggs/cheese, the other doesn’t…
@DJ vegan is no dairy or eggs. Lacto-vegetarian is no eggs, dairy ok. If not specified, vegetarian generally means no meat or fish, eggs and dairy ok

Chef-y but fun to make and much easier than it sounds. I totally messed up handling the phyllo and it still worked fine - just huddle the buttered-up phyllo around the cauliflower and it all works because the custard is very thick. The textures are truly beautiful, but it’s a bit bland to my taste. Maybe more salt and cheese? My favorite cauliflower prep is still boiling the whole head, cooling, smearing thickly withe butter and grated parm and heating until bubbling and golden. If there were a way to add custard and shattering phyllo to that - super!

This looked amazing but I found it a bit lacking in flavor.  The cooking times were off and the phyllo almost burned during the first phase of the baking and the cauliflower was over done by the end.  The wrapping step was a bit weird and I ended up just pouring the custard over the cauliflower.  The flavor would be aided by salt and pepper.  Not sure that this is something I would make again

A question, not a comment: where would I locate the pan in the oven if the heat comes from the bottom. My stove is a gas model, as old as I am (69) and has nice even heat in the oven, but the heat comes from a gas burner under the oven.
@Gina I think top third is still fine as the heat rises and I imagine one would not want the bottom of the pan to burn.
I am also intrigued by this recipe… I think a video would make all the difference!

Oooh! This looks great, but I’m currently eating vegan.  I’m going to try swapping out the cream, eggs & cheese with silken tofu cashew nutritional yeast (maybe garlic & a bit of mustard too) blended to make a the custard.

Let's complicate this even more.  I'm thinking of using those round Vietnamese rice papers instead of the phyllo.  I've watched a lot of videos using it and even tried it once myself and it was fantastic.  I would brush one or two rounds with the butter, (maybe a spritz of water first to soften enough to cup) form a cup for the cauliflower and then follow the directions as written.  When I try it, I'll write about it and let you know how it worked.

directions were confusing, but my daughter and i figured it out using context clues. mind you, we found this recipe in the magazine sans detailed photo. comments: we doubled the phyllo sheets since they weighed about 1/2 the version in the recipe. this was necessary for structural integrity, however overpowered the cauliflower and definitely needed more custard. mostly enjoyed the book exchange & hot cocoa mentioned in the article, could do without the nests. good luck folks!
Wonderful recipe - my first time working with Phyllo dough which I found a bit fiddly- but results were well worth it - might try Guyere next time
Added a few dashes of balsamic vinegar at the end - perfetto!
@J Bishop thanks. We felt it a bit bland. That’d added to it!

Anyone else confused on how 6 sheets of phyllo pastry end up weighing 8oz total? Just don’t see how that maths… will check out the other ottolenghi recipe someone else mentioned for method. Or just wing it.
Where does one buy fresh sage leaves? Is there a good substitute for the sage?
@BJ any fresh savory herb would do… thyme, oregano, chervil, parsley, marjoram, tarragon. I actually think it would be fun to vary the direction of the dish playing with the herb. Good luck!
@BJ I used fresh sage- but truly think it can be omitted. Maybe another herb would be better.
Has anyone ever tried this with gluten-free pasta pastry instead of Filo?
@Chaya I also would like a Gluten Free option. What is “Pasta Pastry” and where do you get it? Thanks!
Used more phyllo than recommended for extra body and crunch. Cut cauliflower into chunks instead of wedges (made for easier eating with a kid). Delicious and lovely to behold. Oh! And easy!
I added mustard and instead of cream I used Greek yoghurt. Was good!
I am going to try to make this tonight. Has anyone made it and was it successful? Was it as delicious as the photo looks? Any tips?

@Carolits fine.  A bit fiddley .  A bit bland. But cute idea. Make it once( see video on the right side) and then maybe tweak for yourself. It needs more spices or herbs. Someone suggested balsamic at the end - that’d help I’ll probably makeit again and tweak- they are cute little  nests. Maybe a broccoli and cauliflower combo     Good luck.
Setting 425 F (220 C) in the upper third of the oven burns the phyllo. What a shame...

I loved this dish.  I made a few changes, some of them accidental, but they turned out to be great.  I doubled the custard and doubled the cheese in the custard. I poured as much as I could into the 9x13 pan and then poured the rest of the custard into a small 8x8 baking dish.  Both turned out to be fantastic.  I also roasted the cauliflower for 15 minutes instead of blanching. The phyllo was a massive pain, so I basically created pockets with it.  This is going on permanent rotation

Don’t forget to thaw the phyllo dough. My poor nests were a patchwork of broken half-frozen phyllo chards. I didn’t have much hope that they would hold any custard but they did, hooray! I used x 0.5 recipe and cut 1 cabbage into 4 pieces. The whole family liked it. I will make it again (with a more satisfying phyllo experience next time).

title: Baked Kale Rice With Halloumi Recipe
author: Yotam Ottolenghi

comments.commentBody: I consider myself a proficient cook but thought this recipe was a bit overly complicated. It tasted good! I think in the future I would still make the kale puree as described but then just use it to make a regular stove-top risotto which should only take about 20-25 minutes instead of the almost hour this required. I love halloumi but I feel it fell a little flat here..as I mentioned I'll likely use this to make a risotto and then sub parmesan for the halloumi.
@MJ Well, then that’s a different dish or recipe, isn’t it?
@Molly that’s my point.
@MJ I think you’re right, will do on the stovetop next time
Fantastic.  Works with Spinach just as well.

This had to cook for a LOT longer than the recipe directed, for my oven. Also, in this day of ginormous vegetables, 2 yellow onions was way too many onions - a cup measure would be more helpful  (I know, I should have used common sense)
Completely agree.

@sybil seconding cooking longer than it says.  Even at altitude (Denver, CO) I increased the temperature and time to cooks off excess liquid.  I will also say I typically expect more complex flavors from Ottolenghi and was a little let down by the lack of dimensions of flavor in comparison to most of his recipes.  Fun to make, but probably won’t do again.
@sybil I know everything’s bigger across the pond, but here in the UK the hot and dry summer means our onions are tiny this year. I’d probably need to use three!

This part is confusing: "1 lemon, skin peeled into 8 strips then finely chopped, plus 1 tablespoon juice." As far as I can tell, just the lemon peel and 1 T juice are used. So it should presumably be: Peel of one lemon, finely chopped, plus 1 tablespoon of the lemon's juice. Right?
I followed the directions to the letter, but the stock was n where near to being absorbed by the rice— hmmmmmmm
can brown rice be substituted? Might need to change liquid amounts?
Yes, but it will take longer to cook. Gonna try this with some short grain brown rice this evening
I made this with short grain brown rice and found the result tasty and with a satisfying chew.

This used a few too many dishes and was somewhat more complicated than I’d like for a weeknight meal in my tiny kitchen, but it was yummy! I wanted some extra protein so I also added some paneer to the mix at the same time as the halloumi and it turned out great.

Folks, I want to emphasize the weight/quantity issue. As we are often reminded of the accuracy of using a scale for baking measurements and due to the worldwide access to these recipes and confusion as to size and weight of foodstuffs, I would like to see ingredients listed by weight rather than ‘large’ , ‘medium’ ‘cup of’. Thoughts?

Hi @Brian I'm in a metric country and I simply select the metric option at the top of the recipe which gives weight equivalents where appropriate eg: in this recipe it said "a large bunch of kale (approx 285g)". 
I appreciate the US isn't metric, but most digital scales these days will easily swap between US and metric, so you could try that?
Just a suggestion :)

This tasted very good although It came out quite soupy. But the biggest change If I make this again is to cook the halloumi in a sauté pan so it’s gets some color and crisps up. As written the cheese turns soft and melty, I like halloumi for the nice texture you get by sautéing in hot, dry pan. 
I also agree that this is overly complicated and time consuming. Making a risotto on the stove with the kale purée and then adding the crispy kale and cheese would be a superior result IMO.
Going to make this and serve with West Indian Lamb Curry, too much you think?
@Beckythat sounds really nice!

Delicious, hearty, meatless meal. I ended up cooking the rice for a solid 10-15 minutes longer than the recipe calls for, think that one needs to go with their gut when it comes to their personal ovens.

This sounds good and I will make it--lacinato kale is my favorite for its dark dark greenness and good flavor.  But why discard the stems??  I always save the stems, cut them into little pieces and incorporate them into soup, my latest favorite involving sausage, shitake mushrooms and gigante beans
Just reading the cooking directions for this dish exhausts me (let alone actually making it).

@Tom of Poland funny - yes it was pretty exhausting- I think you can skip the puree part and just add the garlic into the onion mixture….. the puree and the ‘toasted’ greens were sort of uninspired flavor- wise

Overly complicated recipe that used too many dishes. Flavor was fine, but definitely not worth the time or mess. Halloumi would have been better grilled and then added. I would also just use lemon zest instead of chopping the peels

OMG, this is a fantastic recipe! I cut it in half for  my mother's 10" cast iron frying pan. Otherwise, I followed it to the letter. (Well, bomba rice instead of arborio, and vegetable stock instead of chicken stock-- it still came out perfectly seasoned and delicious.) This is definitely a repeat recipe!

This recipe needs some work . 1/2 c oil is twice the amount needed. Even with turning oven up to 400 halfway through cooking time  the rice still took a lot longer to cook than called for. 1 tsp salt is plenty especially with salty cheese. 
I will make a stovetop version next time reducing time , dishes and propane

Makes a wicked riff if you go the typical risotto route (I.e. stovetop, deglaze with white wine) then add the greens whizzed with the stock later. Finish it in the oven with the halloumi until they're tanned.
@Lucy did this and added a couple scoops of ricotta and the lemon juice with a bit of honey to the kale sauce and it was great — different recipe, no doubt, but tasty!
@Margaret I did think it was bitter. I’m glad to hear honey fixed it.

This took closer to 2 hours total with all the prep, and the payoff just wasn't worth it. It turned out good, but not something I'll jump to make again. If I were to, I'd do it on the stove instead of in the oven, and I'd make sure someone else was home to help with the dishes after because WOW I've never had so many after such a seemingly simple dish.

I enjoyed this, but it was a fair amount of time and effort plus a huge amount of dishes. I just don’t know that the result was worth it. If I made it again, I would def use some modifications to simplify
Made as written. Too much oil — it looked wrong and I should have scaled back. Otherwise okay but not worth the effort.

As others have said, this recipe can be made more easily on the stove. I suggest leaving the broth in a pot and ladling some of it into the rice (risotto method), then add the puree and then more broth if necessary. The crispy leaves are nice but unnecessary (and they soften quickly).

A suggestion of what type of lemon to use (often his recipes use preserved lemons) would be helpful. To me, the addition of the lemon peel made the dish too bitter. Although others really liked it. Adding yogurt helped make it less bitter. I used a “normal” US lemon. Next time, I’ll try a Meyer lemon maybe. Or—try scraping more of the pith off the peel.
@Jonathan I used preserved lemon, some of its juice and reduced the salt a little. Excellent.

My advice: Reduce the salt dramatically. Easy to add salt, hard to remove.  it was way too salty with 2 teaspoons of salt plus broth (which has salt in it), and halloumi. 
One could argue that I did not make this recipe at all: I did not have kale, but I had spinach, so I used that. I did not have an hour, so I made a risotto on the stovetop instead of baked rice in the oven. Nonetheless I enjoyed the lemon and greens, and halloumi -- I fried it on the stove and put it on top of my risotto. :-)
I think it was around step 5, maybe 6, that I ordered takeout.

Why not zest the lemons?

Those little bits of actual "finely chopped" lemon peel turned into unavoidable astringent bombs in my mouth.  

Maybe I didn't chop the peel finely enough, but also maybe I'm not a world class chef with a staff who knows what "finely chopped" means.

I know how to zest.

Fig I try this again, I'll zest the lemon peel.  I know, gauche, but hopefully more edible...

I adapted this recipe to the Instant Pot and simplified considerably.  Puréed the raw kale with 1/2 c water in the cuisinart. Sautéed the onion and garlic in olive oil with herbs in the IP. Added the rice. Then 1/2 white wine. Then added chicken stock and pureed kale. Cooked for 6 minutes at high. Let sit for 9 minutes then did a manual release. Added grated lemon and 1/2 cup grated Parmesan. Cooked halloumi in olive oil in skillet til brown. And served on top the risotto.

I thought this recipe was over-complicated and time consuming. I would cook the risotto separately if I made this again. The flavors didn’t meld as I had expected and the result was not worth the effort.
Sure, quibble about the blender, the baking sheet, all those dishes. This was delicious--I didn't deviate from the recipe.
One day I will find a recipe for kale purée that I actually enjoy. Today was not the day.
Man, this was insanely salty.  Half the salt and it would have been delicious.

@Lauren did you use Better-than-bouillon broth or another one? I find that when using b-t-b I need to refrain from adding other salt until tasting at the end, but I’d like to know what you used before making this dish
Way too much liquid for the amount if rice: 1 cup to 4 cups. Very confusing. I had to boil off all the extra liquid to make it palatable.

Made this just this evening. One, takes way longer than the time indicated. Two, had to cook longer, at least in my oven, for the rice to absorb the liquid. Used vegetarian stock and cut the halloumi in much smaller pieces. Could have gone all the way down to half inch blocks, so One does not have to mess around with cutting them to bite size while eating . thought it was pretty good, but maybe not worth all the effort and time required. If I make it again, I will definitely add some cherry tomatoes on top when adding the halloumi. I felt like it needed a little pop of something acidic.

This was absolutely delicious.  I didn't feel like dragging the food processor out and having to wash all those parts, so I just used used a stick blender, which really didn't result in a puree.  I'm not sure I would even bother with the stick blender next time. 

Also, I only have a 10-inch skillet, so I cut the recipe down (3/4 cup of rice, 3 cups of stock, 1/3 cup water, 8 oz. kale, etc.). I recommend tasting the rice for doneness before pulling it out of the oven,

This looks delicious and I'm eager to try. Might be a dumb question but I'm used to carmelizing onions and toasting rice on the stovetop so I'm not sure whether the instructions to bake them means....put them in the oven?
Yes, you make the whole dish in the oven. For aach step you add the ingredients and then put it in the oven. I found it to be sort of slow and unnecessary. I would have rather done it on the stove.

Made as is (added lemon juice and salt to taste) and really enjoyed it! Reminds me of the filling for dolmas I’ve made and will lean into that next time by adding a bunch of fresh mint, dill, and parsley. Could probably cut the oil in half if you keep an eye on the onions in the first step. Also recommend turning the halloumi as our nearly burned.

Due to oven constraints, I had to start this as a classic, stove top risotto...stirring, stirring, stirring...and at the end, I added the Halloumi and, then I baked that in oven to melt and finish. I also added parmesan for fuller flavor. It was delicious.

I cooked the rice on the stove until the step where you mix the kale mixture in, then layed the mozzarella slices on top and baked for 15 min. Turned out great! Just keep on eye on the rice on the stove top until it's nearly done (just a bit crunchy on the inside) before putting it in the oven. The lemon on top is key!

Changes:
- Bomba rice 
- Stovetop 
- Queso de Freir 

Delicious! A lot of work, but my family told me I absolutely had to make it again. Just make sure you have a Saturday or Sunday evening set aside because this takes a while.

I liked it! As did two other family members. One didn’t love it but she’s a kid and this is more of an “adult recipe”, she said. It tastes like spanakopita rice. I used just one onion, used dried thyme, 
 and skipped the kale chips on top part because the recipe was already somewhat involved. It didn’t need them. There are a lot of steps and I almost got scared off but worth it but I’m glad I made it and would make again. Also enjoyed two days of leftovers for lunch!
Would make this with tofu, as I'm dairy intolerant, but not allergic.

This was delicious, but a LOT of work and a LOT of mess. It took me 2 hours because each step took so much longer than estimated, even though my oven temp was just calibrated.

If you do embark on the journey, I concur with what others have said re: onion (I am not against onion, but one is plenty) and oil (I love olive oil, but suggest using 1/4c - 1/3 c. instead of 1/2 c. The change in liquid will not be an issue.). Salt was okay with me because I used unsalted veggie broth.

As others have stated, it’s a bit fussy for what you end up with.  However, the chopped lemon peel does add a compelling bitter pith note, which builds nicely on the earthy kale. 

If I do make this again, I will absolutely fry up the halloumi separately, cube, and add as a topping once dished out.

I agree with Spark Chef.  This uses a lot of items in the kitchen and it's quite easy to get too much pith and make the lemon peel pieces bitter.  Better just zest a lemon.  I'm sure a more professional cook will pull it off fine, but then they usually have someone to wash the pots too.

This was tasty but even with two of us in the kitchen, the prep time was way longer than the suggested 15 minutes. It’s definitely a recipe for an afternoon when you have time and not a weeknight. I wish the suggested times were a little more honest. I take them with a grain of salt, but they’re clearly written by and for professional cooks in professional kitchens.

A bright and comforting dish that reminded me of a saag paneer minus the spices. The lemon came through beautifully and enlivened the kale. But I agree with the other commenters that this is an overly complicated recipe that needs at least 20 minutes more cooking time. It’s a showstopper coming out of the oven, but next time, I’ll make a risotto, add the kale purée to it, and cook the halloumi separately. It can all be assembled into a shallow roasting pan or on a platter to give the same wow visual, minus the too-long cooking time.
Wonderful flavor payoff for a moderately complex recipe. Don’t skip the crisped kale!
This is so good! Couldn’t find halloumi in my supermarket so I used feta and it was fabulous
This was delicious but I needed up with way too much liquid. Would reduce to 2 cups next time.
Very yummy and surprisingly rich. When I make this next I won’t make the crispy kale it really didn’t feel like it did much for the dish.

This dish is less complicated than it appears (although I did skip the crispy kale on top). You touch it three times, letting the oven do the work rather than constantly stirring on stovetop.
1.) I used the peel of one preserved lemon, unrinsed and finely chopped
2.) My oven took five minutes longer to absorb the stock into the rice
3.) I cut the halloumi into squares rather than leaving in big rectangles as pictured.
4.) I gave it 30 seconds under the broiler so it came out sizzling.

really liked this and enjoyed making it. i washed the dishes as i went. it took longer to cook and i would use less oil next time, but the flavors were so so good. I've never made risotto or cooked with arborio rice before so it was an opportunity to try it. A very rich dish. i really liked the crunchy kale on top--would make that as a snack!

I loved the convenience of oven cooking but, unfortunately, the rice needed more broth and time, and then became mushy, overcooked, and olive-colored. Also, 1c of arborio easily feeds 6 as a main---it was excessive. I'm going to try to salvage by making arancini or rice soup, but it's not palatable on its own. Next time I will make the risotto in Instapot at 6 min high, then brown the halloumi on stovetop, for guaranteed results.
I hate kale so I'd use chard instead
That's exactly the opposite of my relationship to chard. Ick.
"...the extra kale has crisped up"? We're the "extra kale" in the oven waiting and crisping? Or just in single layer with oil on "the table"?
See step seven.
Step 6 "Drizzle 1 tablespoon of oil over the halloumi and return to the oven, along with the separate baking sheet of kale."

George, the kale is crisped in step 6: "Drizzle 1 tablespoon of oil over the halloumi and return to the oven, along with the separate baking sheet of kale. Cook for 12 minutes until the halloumi is softened and the extra kale has crisped up, then remove from the oven."
Is there a substitute for Halloumi as I can't find any near where I reside.
I use bread cheese as substitute for Halloumi. I think you could use paneer or queso blanco. If you have an Aldi near you, they usually have Halloumi and bread cheese.
@JessieOnBI same question here. Maybe feta?
Looks like the Indian recipe for Palak Paneer and rice. Substitute spinach with kale and Halloumi for Indian cottage cheese and you have the dish.

Made with Better than Bouillon veggie, started on the stove top to caramelize the onions, then added a splash of dry white wine when putting it in the oven. It definitely took longer than planned time, but the results were delicious with slow cooked salmon. Leftovers for days!!
I wonder if it would work with riced cauliflower? I guess I need to try it and see. . .
I halved this and it came out fine! I also skipped the whole crispy kale bit and just pureed all the kale together. It's a weeknight, ain't nobody got time to make some crispy kale stopping.

The flavor of this dish is incredible. After cooking the rice and broth for the prescribed 15 minutes, I cooked it more  to absorb the liquid  before adding the kale bc it was so soupy. The "al dente" instruction should have clued me in that it would all work out, but it didn't, so I overcooked the rice. But it was still delicious.

This recipe needed a sous chef and a clean-up squad and overall wasn’t worth the number of pot twists. I subbed brown rice for arborio but it was more than al dente and never made a sauce. Lemon skin cutting was tiresome. Simplify simplify simplify.
Thank you @Claudio.  I prefer spinach and will try it with that.

I wanted to love this recipe, I love Ottolenghi’s cookbooks, but the kale had a bitter taste to the dish. I used tender kale, maybe if I had added salt to the cooking water? Also I wish it specified if the dish should be covered or uncovered when in the oven.

Still in the process of making this and I think it will be delicious, it’s interesting and different but I will never make this again. It’s taken over two hours and I’ve had the rice in the oven with the stock that is supposedly supposed to be mostly absorbed after 15 minutes and it’s still swimming in it. I look forward to the day when NYT actually gives accurate timing descriptions for how long these recipes take.

Although mine didn't turn out as expected either (took longer to cook than indicated, then rice not al dente), I thought it was so delicious I will definitely make it again with tweaks that have been already suggested here.  I'd cut up halloumi into pieces because it was impossible to cut with a fork.  I would also cook over stove like a traditional risotto, adding liquid a portion at a time for more control over the texture of the rice.  Baking in the oven would seem more convenient, but since you have to keep adjusting this every 10-15 minutes or so anyway, why not hover over the pan?  I probably cut too much pith from the lemon but didn't mind the bitter taste.  Still, I would zest instead next time to better distribute the flavor.  The lemon flavor from the peel and juice complimented the dish beautifully!
I added a can of beans to make this a bit more hearty.
I'm confused- the recipe says at step 3 to put in the stock .... and yet at step 5 it says 1/2 cup stock- add to the kale and puree. Did I miss something? I already used my stock .... ahhhh help

In step 3, you put the kale and garlic in the stock.  You don't put the stock in anything (other than a separate pot/sauce pan). At the end of step 3 it says to drain the kale, RESERVING both the stock and the kale.  Later you take out 1/2 cup of stock (keeping the remainder in reserve) and puree it with the kale and garlic.  Still later, you add the remainder of the stock to the rice.

This is tasty. I made it exactly as directed except for subbing veg stock for the chicken stock. I would reduce the oil by half and the stock somewhat. Even after cooking 10 min. beyond the recipe's recommendation, it was soupy.

From comments: I adapted this recipe to the Instant Pot and simplified considerably.  Puréed the raw kale with 1/2 c water in the cuisinart. Sautéed the onion and garlic in olive oil with herbs in the IP. Added the rice. Then 1/2 white wine. Then added chicken stock and pureed kale. Cooked for 6 minutes at high. Let sit for 9 minutes then did a manual release. Added grated lemon and 1/2 cup grated Parmesan. Cooked halloumi in olive oil in skillet til brown. And served on top the risotto.
I used an Instant Pot as well. I did everything in it except for steps 3 and 5. I used vegetable stock in lieu of chicken as my wife is a Pescatarian. It came out great.

I cooked this during a lazy late morning - didn't want to be under the gun. I cooked it in the bottom of a tagine set in the cold oven and added onions once preheated. The onion and broth-absorption steps took longer than quoted in the recipe, but otherwise timing was pretty close. Followed the recipe exactly otherwise. Tastes great, beautiful presentation, and you have lots of down time to do a little clean-as-you-go. Cook this!
Fantastic dish, wouldn't change a thing! Timings, quantities, flavours are all perfect, thanks Yotam!
Curious if anyone tried this without the step of cooking the kale? Seems unnecessary given the fact that its also going into the oven.

I have not.  I don't think it would cook enough in 12 minutes without much liquid (the stock has been absorbed by the rice by the time you add the cooked kale).  Before I cooked it, I thought there were an awful lot of steps.  I did skip pureeing the kale and garlic and thought it was really good, so if you're looking to skip something, my recommendation woul be to skip pureeing.
Based on the comments about too many steps, I decided to make a stovetop risotto. I added chopped kale and lemon juice in at the end and topped with fried halloumi. It was delicious.

Made exactly as is and it was fantastic. Ottonlenghi recipie can be a bit extra, but there is a method to his madness. You could do this stove top but oven makes it more hands-off. And if it’s too soupy just let it ride in oven a little longer!

I used the half-recipe, for 2 of us.
Found this delicious, but it took way, way longer using short grain brown rice, maybe an extra 15-20 minutes. I used more halloumi - about 4.5 ounces, and wound up adding a small portion of leftover cooked lentils.  I finally used my baster to remove some of the liquid.

Second time I’ve used an Ottolenghi recipe, and again I think if I make it again it’d be easier.  I agree with the comments about the directions for the lemon peel being a bit confusing, glad I read lots of comments first. Thanks to all of you!
a bit too greasy, will add less oil and salt next time around. otherwise, 10/10

I should have listened to all of you. Too many steps, too many dishes! But also: delicious.

My single most practical tip: Use tongs to fish out the kale that you want to use for the crispy bits. That's two fewer dirty big items (colander, second pot) and one more small item (tongs, which are useful for getting the crispy bits off the sheet pan).

I'll skip the crispy bits, though, and spend some time browning the halloumi on stovetop. Also: halve the salt!
Adapted this and made it on the stove instead as a risotto. Fried up the halloumi to put on top.
I had no rice so used barley instead, it was delicious ! Same for the thyme, I used rosemary, it was lovely :)
Rice just free-balling in the oven?! I covered in tinfoil and still had to beg the oven gods for the rice to toast/marinate/cook. Also, 1/2 an onion was enough. Only two cloves of garlic is criminal.

Made this exactly as the recipe directed! Felt there were two many onions, could have done one large instead of 2. And there was a lot of liquid and oil still in the dish even though the rice was finished cooking. Maybe after blanching the kale could have squeezed out some water?? It was too wet for my taste, but had great flavor nonetheless. The halloumi is key!

Surprised at all the complaints about this recipe—glad it didn’t stop me! I used some lobster stock because I had it, and took the advice to cook a little longer and a little broil at end to brown the cheese, otherwise followed directions and was happily surprised how good it was. It’s less starchy than a risotto.

title: Parsnips With Miso and Parmesan Recipe
author: Yotam Ottolenghi

comments.commentBody: I made this tonight and it was really delicious and easy. Followed the recipe exactly. For my taste, the cheese wasn't necessary and didn't fit in well with the other ingredients. Next time I'll try it with some toasted nuts or pepitas instead!
I accidentally put the lemon zest in the oil and it was great! I recommend doing it that way. We also skipped the Parmesan.

Excited for a new way to incorporate parsnips into our meals. Already enjoy using them in roasted mixed root vegetable mélanges, and added to chicken soup, but this dish kicks it up a notch and makes parsnips—the liquorish-carroty flavor—the star of the show.
I ate a whole recipe of this in a single night. I felt very ashamed and a bit nauseous, but it was totally worth it!
@Jan My stomach aches as I write this, having eaten the entire half recipe I made, minus a single piece tasted by my accommodating partner. I’m so glad for your company!

Yum! Haven’t tried this yet, but the parsnips in my garden are ready for harvest, and the fall arugula has just grown enough to be harvested as baby arugula. Miso, on hand, in the fridge. I am tingling.
Followed the recipe as written. Paired it with salmon. Was delicious. Will make again!

This was our first time out with parsnips and it was a wow. Wish I were quicker with prep, but it came together easily with delicious flavoring and was a hit with everyone at the table. Didn't change a thing about it, since we know that you can't go wrong with Mr. Ottolenghi as your guide. This one goes onto the menu rotation for sure.

Mixed parsnips and carrots, halfed the garlic, skipped the Parmesan. Otherwise followed the recipe exactly. Restaurant quality with a piece of salmon. Can’t believe I did an Ottelenghi project on a school night!
My parsnips in arugula miso lemon garlic were tough as nails.

@mary street I wonder if US cooks of 1955 were familiar enough with parsnips to know which to buy, or how to deal with old woody ones, or NOT to deal with old woody ones…or a dozen other negatives which could come along with a kilo of parsnips from a farmer, or a co-op.

I’ve roasted them before, cut up like carrot sticks along with actual carrot sticks, tossed w olive oil and salt. Excellent! But I may have lucked out with my batch of parsnips.

Parsnips are delicious; makes any soup more delicious and is the most delicious roasted root vegetable.  ..the picture of the dish in  NYT magazine is so much prettier. agree ; skip the parmesan ..let the parsnips shine .
I think 450 is too high.  My parsnips are well and truly burnt.
@John agreed. 450* for a home oven will scorch your food and set off your smoke alarms. 425* a few minutes longer does just fine. We don’t cook in restaurant kitchens!
Good recipe but next time I would double or triple the amount of arugula.  Didn’t need the Parmesan.
Thank you your substitution ideas for parsnips. Can’t wait to try carrots. Also try sweet potatoes!

First time cooking with parsnips and I enjoyed it! The flavours work quite well, just that the thyme took some time to pick off the stems… we don’t like rocket so had skipped it and paired with other veg. Will deffo make again

@J turn the thyme stalk upside down, grip the top with two fingers. With your other hand, pinch the stalk with two or three fingers just below where you are holding it and move your fingers down sharply along the stalk.  The leaves should all come off at once. Your scissors may also have holes in the blades that help with this. Or you can look for ‘herb stripper’.
A hack I've seen is to use a colander - just put the stem in one of the holes and pull it through.
I was really disappointed in this. Parsnips were tough, dressing was not very interesting.  I ended up pitching it out and just serving an arugula salad instead.

Delicious! I’ve made this many times now. Have also done this salad with carrots (or both carrots and parsnips), and substituted fresh oregano when I didn’t have thyme. The dressing is versatile, having also used the it with crispy roasted Brussels sprouts. Highly recommended.
Meh.  Just okay.  The miso dressing is nice.  But otherwise nothing too exciting to say.

I don't understand the love for this one. I like every single ingredient, and made it almost exactly per the recipe (using a bit less Parmesan than called for, because I ran low), and the flavors just didn't add up for me or my family. It tasted muddy and strange. I normally really trust Ottolenghi on flavors, and was surprised by this one.
@Gila totally agree. Plus all that work for nothing.

Christmas Eve for the clan. Liked by all! Used a combo of parsnips and carrots for color. Forgot the parm and didn’t miss it. Might add toasted peppitas next time. Will also make with other root veggies.

Great recipe, wonderful depth of flavor and texture. The high temp for roasting with oil made the parsnips crunchy like air fried french fries(which I really liked) but am wondering if this is what was intended from original recipe.

Excellent dish and a reminder to eat parsnips more often. It doesn’t always have to be carrots! We actually ate the parsnips and arugula separate so we felt like we had three dishes instead of two for dinner.
Can this be prepared on the grill instead of the oven?
@Mary Sure, I think grilled parsnips would work just as well!

its good to eat . You can choose  vegetables
you just have and it works. Very good when you have different ones in small volumes.
One of my simple truths: I don't like the taste of miso. But I do love parsnips. Any recommendations on a substitute for miso or should I just omit it?

@Ann maybe just your favorite umami seasoning (I sometimes use anchovy paste if out of miso- it’s saltier and more flavorful, but can enhance a dish if use right amt).   Hard to suggest sub,  as not sure what you don’t like about miso (salt, texture, soy?)
Vegemite!

We loved it. Switched the parmesan out for Pepitas (One of us is lactose intolerant and the other has vegan sensibilities) and then spent the whole meal chasing pepitas around our plates. So delicious. This is an awesome recipe. 10/10
The flavors of sweet and tart really shine. The dressing is perfect for not only the arugula but on the sweet parsnips. I didn’t change or add anything. This recipe is a keeper!

I love roasted parsnips, and this recipe really lightens them up.  The lemon/garlic/miso dressing is scrumptious, and I liked the Parmesan. This would be a wonderful side to a heavy main - such as a roast (or even the Thanksgiving turkey).
I made this in order to try to like parsnips and my goodness it was delicious and easy and the parsnips take on some otherworldly flavor. I will make this again.

I don't know why I made this recipe tbh when all the ingredients so fundamentally had nothing to do with each other. Guess I was just distracted during Thanksgiving prep and just went for it. Nothing here really elevated parsnips in anyway and for the most part detracted from a lovely root vegetable that shines so well on its own.
Made this yesterday with rainbow carrots/parsnips and our plethora of arugula ! Is a new fav !

Made recipe “as is” with parsnips- excellent. Then used same method/sauce for Brussel sprouts. 

Two wins! Both vegetables I don’t much like but really enjoyed with this recipe.
Evoo should never be used in high heat situations because it degrades the healthful benefits and can create harmful byproducts. Best used t in this recipe only for the dressing.
I was so surprised it's not been mentioned! And it's a waste of money - the taste is also degraded by high heat which is why true extra virgin olive oil is cold pressed

The dressing is fantastic, and this is a very nice and simple dish. I left the Parmesan out because the idea of it didn't grab me. I cooked it at 425 degrees. I think it would work with other root vegetables and could certainly be served at room temperature.

The miso garlic dressing is a keeper that ill recycle for other veg! Supplemented some carrots because we didn't have quite enough parsnips in our winter farm share and it was still divine. Agree with others that the parmesan is unnecessary. I left it out and this was still a giant hit.
This is great. Made for a large group and ran out of parsnips so used half carrots. No Parmesan. Everyone loved it.
Could parsnips be substituted?  Don’t know if the have them here in Rome. Recipe sounds wonderful!
I would think carrots could be substituted

cathy, I cook and roast with root vegetables a lot and find them very interchangeable.  I suggest trying another root vegetable of a combination of root vegetables:  rutabaga, carrot, turnip, celeriac, taro root, white potato, sweet potato.  (Parsnips are one of my favorite vegetables!  They are also yummy raw and finely grated.)
Can I use red miso paste instead? It's what I have in the fridge!
@Tanya red miso has a stronger flavor than white but people often sub one for the other. You may want to adjust the quantity down a bit.
Would turnip be good? I have a giant one.
I love parsnips and this is a delicious way to enjoy! Made as is and quite easy for an Ottolenghi recipe including no hard to find ingredients. Will be using this a lot this fall and winter.

I enjoyed this dish, but it cooked much faster than the recipe indicated.  I cooked the parsnips for 15 minutes on the first side and 5 minutes on the second side and they were certainly done. They would have been burnt to a crisp if I had them at 450 degrees for 30 minutes.
Do you think sauce and parsnips can be made morning of Thanksgiving and then reheated to serve?
Delicious.  Made as written with a fried egg on top for dinner.  Added carrots as well as the parsnips, also used Parmesan cheese.  Really nice meal.

Turned out great aside from terrible smoke from the burnt parchment paper! Second time around and same issue. Any thoughts?? I even added some ghee to the EVOO this time. Seems the 450 temp is just too high.
I just made this as a side for a Sunday roast. What an incredible flavour blend. I’ll use this vinaigrette on pasta, rice, carrots etc but parsnips is genius.
I like the parm with the arugula.  I think it adds depth the dish would not have otherwise.

I bet this is delicious as written, but I made with some substitutes to use some stuff in the fridge and it came out great! I did a mix of carrots, parsnips, and red cabbage, subbed rosemary instead of thyme, and massaged kale instead of arugula. I do appreciate ottolenghi’s precision but this one seems easy to riff on
I loved the paring of arugula, miso and garlic with the carmelized turnips.  I would omit the salt next time as the Miso has quite a bit.  I also doubled the lemon zest and juice.
Obviously, vegetarians know that Parmesan isn’t suitable for vegetarians. Swap this out for an alternative that is.
@Andy Taylorthat would be vegans, wouldn’t it?  Is there another animal product in parm?
@Linda: Traditional Parm isn't vegetarian because it's made with animal rennet from calf stomachs - if microbial or plant-based rennet is used, it is vegetarian (never vegan).
Cooking with parchment paper at 450 degrees is a little too exciting for me. Maybe do this at 425 or use aluminum foil instead.

This will be a new go to recipe. Was very impressed. Packed with flavor. The arugula was the cherry on top.  Love, love, love. Also agree that the cheese was not necessary. And I added a little bit arugula because I love it so much.

Wow, I made this last night according to the recipe and it was delicious. Great way to give parsnips their moment in the spotlight.  Plus it looked so bright and inviting.  This would be great for a winter dinner party.
Best thing that I ate this week....and I have eaten many good things.
What a delight. The miso lemon combination is outstanding. Will make this again and again throughout winter

This was so good! I included Parmesan. I also through in a bit or rosemary and sage with the thyme. If I was introducing someone to parsnips for the first time, this would be the recipe I chose to highlight them.
Yum! Make extra dressing because it’s soooo good!

title: Crispy Fish Salad With Parsley and Ciabatta Croutons Recipe
author: Yotam Ottolenghi

comments.commentBody: This is a really nice concept and collection of flavors if you lay it all on a bed of hearty romaine or tender spinach for veg and balance. And while my mouth loves the idea of sea bass, my wallet won't tolerate it, but I find that either crispy chickpeas or strips cut from a tender pork roast work beautifully for the protein.

I used the frozen branzino fillets from Trader Joe's, thin enough to cook in 4 minutes, and their white gluten-free sandwich bread.  Also used currants instead of raisins, which are used in the Zuni chicken recipe on which this is based.  Absolutely delicious recipe!

@susan

I agree - TJ frozen branzino is excellent and inexpensive.

@susan
Currants sound like the right choice, having made the Zuni bread salad many times.  They are the same size as the capers and it would distribute the sweetness more evenly.
Sounds great, but here in Nashville, sea bass is $40/pound wholesale. Branzino is fishy. Any other fish suggestions?
@Susan in Nashville Shrimp, perhaps?

Branzino is not fishy. It’s one of the mildest white fishes available. Bluefish and mackerel could  properly called fishy, but not branzino.

Don’t use shrimp as per the other suggestion. The whole point of this recipe is the crispy fish skin.
Halibut, snapper or cod. A firm, white fish is closest.
Wonderful combination of flavors that all work together beautifully. Loved this dish.

A 1 lb bag of frozen branzino fillets worked great for me--not fishy at all.  Used gluten free sandwich bread for the croutons and currants instead of raisins.  Truly a delicious dish.  I might add a little sweetener next time to offset the acid of the vinegar--a great company dish.  separate ingredients could be prepared ahead of time and warmed as needed before combining.
Am I correct in assuming it would be okay to fry all the fish at one time, rather than in two batches, assuming you can do so without crowding them in the skillet?
We substituted cubed and crispy roasted sweet potato for the bread. It works well and is easier on the blood sugar. Also used 1/2 parsley & 1/2 arugula.
Any thoughts about using dried cranberries instead of raisins?
@Mo I have done that with the original Zuni chicken recipe! It was great!
Only make this if you love vinegar. It was way too acid-forward for me.
Or…shocker!…adjust the vinegar level to your liking.
If your fish is fishy, it's not fresh.

Loved this dish. Came together quick in a single pan and was an elegant, quick lunch. Used homemade sourdough bread for the croutons and served over a bed of arugula. The Trader Joe’s frozen branzino fillets that another reviewer mentioned crisped up great and were the perfect thinness.
The point is to get crispy skin. So, you need fish with skin. Score the skin and it won’t curl.
Substituted grouper!! Maybe best fish recipe of all time!  Fabulous flavors!!
It sounds as though a nice, fat flounder or fluke would work well, although not as firm as halibut or even branzino. It would fry up crispy as can be, though.

Use Maryland rockfish. Very tasty, though I didn’t have any raisins on hand. I had the leftovers cold for lunch today, but of course the skin won’t stay crispy, even though I tried semi-successfully to rewarm it in the toaster oven.
The meal was delicious, but the stovetop was a mess!  Any suggestions for turning this into a sheet pan recipe?
Why so much oil?
I abhor raisins but loved the spirit of this recipe. I substituted golden berries - they work brilliantly and pretty too!

Ottolenghi's recipes are always a project-- long ingredient list, usually a little bit of lots of things, combined in many steps. But they always turn out so satisfying! The crunchy, juicy and leafy textures with the savory, salty, sweet, and acid flavors work on every possible level. Every forkful is an adventure. I cooked it on the burner of my outdoor grill so it wasn't too messy. I won't make this often, but it was something completely different than anything I would come up with on my own.

title: Tomato Salad With Dates Recipe
author: Yotam Ottolenghi

comments.commentBody:
I get it, it's all about the fish sauce. But what's an appropriate umami sub suitable for vegetarians or fish allergic people? 
(This recipe is a good reminder to those and others that vegetable dishes don't always mean vegetarian. The number of vegetable soups that start with chicken stock is astounding.)
There is vegan fish sauce available in stores. You could also likely use soy, tamari, miso, or something similar.  Personally, I might sprinkle in some of Trader Joe's mushroom powder.

Not only veg soups---chicken stock is hidden in soooo much (cheap flavor).   
The problem is some of us are allergic to chicken (and all poultry).  Try sussing that out from servers/menus.  
And, for extra credit, try doing it a non-english speaking country (hello Portugal in September!).    Sooo frustrating.   Which is to say, I feel your pain.
@Handsome John I am allergic to the entire allium family: I feel your pain!
Does the recipe call for fresh dates? If so, are dried dates suitable substitutes?
Believe the recipe means *dried* dates -- don't think fresh dates are typically eaten, or even readily available?

@LL readily available at the right time of year if you know where to look (Arabic, Turkish and Mediterranean stores) and absolutely delicious - but yes, I think this needs dried dates. I'll be trying it tonight.
Recipe looks great but which is it:  thickly sliced tomatoes or thinly sliced?  The description says thickly and the recipe says thinly.

@Barbara
And they saw you were right and changed it so the directions just read "sliced", so it’s thinly. I'm making them medium slices because I want to toss it a bit and thin slices will break up too much.
Re: fish sauce brands - Red Boat is my go-to!
There are different fish sauces — which one should I buy?  (I sneak a bit of white miso paste into so many dishes for the savory flavor and feel.)
@Nan I’d say Red Boat fish sauce would do the trick.

The secret to a great tomato salad is buying tomatoes that have ripened fully on the vine. Supermarket tomatoes are tasteless (even heirloom varieties) because they are picked before they are ripe. I pick ripe tomatoes from my garden and slice them while they are still warm from the sunshine. A sprinkle of good quality balsamic vinegar, a dash of my favorite olive oil, and a handful of torn basil is all they need. Yum.
@Captain O I would try using some Miso in place of the fish sauce. If that does not taste right I would try nutritional yeast.

I was excited to try this, with the salty-sour-sweet mix of the sauce. Sadly, it did not turn out very exciting. I followed the recipe, although at the end I added more lime juice. I couldn't taste the dates at all and to be honest  I felt like there were better tomato salads to show off the great flavor of my local tomatoes. Maybe it was my fish sauce, I don't know. 

Also, the cherry tomatoes are a bit dangerous, make sure to eat them whole as they splatter everywhere.

Very excited to see this as my home grown cherry tomatoes began to come in.  Instead of dates I used a fresh local peach (about 1/2), Squeezed in lime juice, but let half a lime "stew with the 'sauce".  Served over local yellow and red tomatoes, not quite ready for heirlooms in Western MA!  Best dish on the table (served with grilled corn, green beans and Tuna....)
@Booker Bush The peach is a FANTASTIC idea!
@Booker Bush Way to capture the spirit of the dish versus dithering over the details.  We should all cook this way I think.

Ottolenghi’s writing here is a reminder of what a lucky thing it is to have taste buds. This recipe is simple, but there’s serious alchemy at work. Made it exactly as written—let the dates break down, sliced the tomatoes thinly, and napped them with the magical sauce. Wow.
Am I slicing the tomato’s thinly or having them mirror the photo which looks like thick slices?

The Sunday magazine article alongside this recipe brings this creation to a whole new appreciation level. We made it with smaller heirlooms from our garden and it was beautiful! We enjoyed the salad cold, alongside some grilled chicken for lunch the following day and it was actually better, having melded into a chutney of sorts.

Incredible. We eat a lot of tomato salad all summer, but this took it to another level. I used maple syrup instead of brown sugar—that worked really well— but otherwise made as written. So, so good. I literally drank the leftover dressing from the platter.
Made this salad last night for family dinner and it was delicious! My partner declared it “suitable for company.” Thank you.
This is a game changer tomato salad. It is soo good we were drinking the delicious juices on the platter. Do try it with your best summer tomatoes, and get the best dates you can find.
Maggi sauce is a great sub for fish sauce.
@Inks which one? There are so many flavors.

Fortunately, not allergic to anything in here - made as written with farmers market big toms and Wholefoods cherry toma (flavor bombs...expensive but worth it), and old dates warmed in oven to be able to slice  So delicious. Will make as much as possible before summer's end...
Best thing for a gardener’s tomato surplus.  I used a beefy yellow heirloom for the slices and small Brown Torch tomatoes for the dressing.  Fantastic.
Maybe it's sinful, but I forgot to get dates so used a couple of raisins instead and it was still good.

I bought a bottle of fish sauce once and immediately threw it out! Just for the reasons you say here. But now I am willing to try again. What brand do you recommend?
Thanks for a great article.

Not liking my  eggplant, tomato, mushroom ragu esp.  and not having kalamata or Moroccan olives at hand I added a couple anchovies lost in the fridge  -- and boom.    Amazing what the fish does for the tomato. Sprinkle of fresh lemon at the end also.
Just made this today with Sungold cherry tomatoes and 1st of the season heirlooms for the slicers.  Brown sugar not needed in the tomato topping .  OMG good!
A great recipe!  Serve with fresh mozzarella!
I feel like the date mixture minus the cherry tomatoes served over fresh heirlooms would be better- the salty sweetness was delicious but I didn't love the combo of cooked and raw tomatoes.
Loved it. It gave tomatoes a new look!! We had to sub dates for prunes. Added a bit of balsamic and some tiny mozerells balls. Company dish worthy
Works just fine to make the sauce ahead, and let it cool on the counter for several hours, slicing the rest of the tomatoes and assembling just before serving, if prepping other dishes.

I used dried dates sliced but they clumped together when cooked, though still tasted good. My husband loved this salad but I will reduce the amount of fish sauce next time. There was too much for my taste.

I made this and felt that the sautéed tomato combo was addictively delicious straight from the pan. I did make the entire salad as written and used it atop some lovely roasted fish. But I could see how a reviewer might find it meh. I think the addition of the fresh tomato here just dilutes the intense caramelly umami of the cooked tomatoes. For me, this is a keeper but next time I will just make the cooked tomato combo and use it as a topping/sauce. Nothing against beautiful fresh tomatoes in the summer — I just think this particular recipe is better without them.
I made this today and I must say it's one of the most disgusting things I've ever cooked--so bad that I pawned it off on a neighbor with instructions to toss it if she, too, cannot abide it.

This tastes great but there's too many skins ( a lot of sungolds)
I'm wondering if anyone has tried a bigger tomato
put the tomatoes in a food mill if you don't care for the skins. I love the sweet flavor and color of a sungold tomato, so fleeting at the end of the Summer

This is fine, but not worth the trouble.  Summer tomatoes taste wonderful with just salt and pepper and this sauce doesn’t improve on that.  Also it’s weird that the recipe never suggests salt.  I salted the slices before adding the dressing.  By by way I followed the recipe pretty exactly.
Can I make the sauce day ahead and keep it in the fridge befoer pouring it over the sliced tomatoes?
I used dried apricots, cut into small pieces, instead od dates, and only a 1/4 tsp of minced garlic, and soy sauce since I didn't have any fish sauce.
Terrible thing to do to good garden tomatoes. Even a surplus. The fish sauce assassinated the tomatoes.
@Mary Beth i thought it was delicious

Step 2 implies that you continue adding ingredients to the skillet still containing tomatoes.  Nope! Read carefully the entire recipe noting that the tomatoes are removed from the pan and set aside before you continue with the HOT caramel dressing.

@Ellie Cat I respectfully disagree. I haven’t made this yet, but the directions don’t indicate that the cook remove the cherry tomatoes/dates before caramelizing the sugar. You first make the caramel sauce with the cherry tomatoes and then pour that (cooled) sauce over the freshly sliced (larger) tomatoes.
There is a vegetarian version of fish sauce - also works for kosher kitchens
I used garlic scape and basil pesto to cook tomatoes for a more subtle garlic flavor, honey instead of brown sugar. Lots of fresh basil on top with a sprinkle of coarse salt. So yummy!
Echoing another reader….please suggest a brand of fish sauce that you can get in nyc.
Made it. Loved it. Had no basil. Piled into ciabatta.
@Nan best fish sauce: Nettuno - Collatura de Alici / cheaper but not bad at all: Red Boat - 40 degree N

Colatura is not the fish sauce that is being called for here. Yes it’s deeply delicious, but it’s Italian, akin to the garum of ancient Rome. Red Boat or another Vietnamese fish sauce would be appropriate (and yes, Red Boat is the best brand).

Yotam does NOT specify which brand, or even which type of fish sauce to use, so I’d just go with whatever I already have (we have Red Boat). If you have Nettuno colatura di alici, go ahead and use that. Use what you like.
Red Boat is fantastic fish sauce.

The recipe into talks about “thickly sliced tomatoes, but the recipe calls for thinly sliced.  I don’t believe the distinction is unimportant.  I’d personally be inclined to slice on the thick side, but d like know which one is intended?

This is fantastic.  If you are vegetarian / vegan, maybe this recipe isn't for you, and that's OK.  There are other ways to enjoy ripe tomatoes. I used fresh dates, because I had them, and they work great. But quality dried ones would probably work fine also. I prefer Medjool because of their rich caramel flavor.  If dried, the less processed the better.

When dad was sick we were helped by a Spanish woman who made delicious tomato salad. Her recipe is very simple. Slice the tomatoes. Add a little sugar, salt (both in the fish sauce), vinegar and stir. Wait for a few minutes. Add olive oil. With time, we have removed both sugar and salt :
1- mix sliced tomatoes with organic cider vinegar. After 2-4 min,olive oil
2- refrigerate a little (tomatoes quickly loose their fragrance in the fridge). 
3- Eat. Dip fresh bread in sauce to delight.
Superb!! Absolutely delicious!
Add more dates and be sure sauce is caramelized.  I topped with shrimp to make a full meal with a nice Rose wine.
Truly outstanding. Just don’t slice the dates too thin or you’ll end up with a sticky purée rather than discrete pieces.
What about the garlic? Is it cooked? In the sauce or separately?
@Rich it’s cooked.
So delicious! My family would not have eaten it if they knew it had fish sauce, so I did not tell them until afterwards. They were happy I didn't tell them because it was so yummy! Spot on.
When do you add the sliced garlic? The directions don’t say…

This sauce is amazing. I skipped the sliced tomatoes and use the sauce with everything -- over pasta, as a side with roast chicken, as the base for this roasted fish recipe https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/1020454-one-pan-roasted-fish-with-cherry, etc. I double the dates, because I love them, but it is not necessary. For vegans, replace the fish sauce with equal parts soy sauce and either rice vinegar or lime juice.
Awesome! I doubled the sauce and the dates and added the zest from the lime. Will mqke qgqin and again.

title: Caramelized Peaches With Rum and Cream Recipe
author: Yotam Ottolenghi

comments.commentBody: Let’s say I don’t live in a metropolitan area and am not keen on giving Amazon $10 for 4 Tbs of muscovado sugar. Any recommendations on substitutes?
@Gonzaga Fan You could use dark brown sugar, perhaps with a bit of molasses mixed in.
I think dark brown sugar would work just as well
Brown sugar is what I will use.

If you can’t get dark muscovado sugar, consider dark brown sugar plus a very small amount of molasses as  the best substitute for dark muscovado sugar (1 part molasses to 16 parts dark brown sugar). For this recipe I would make a batch with 1 teaspoon molasses & 5 tablespoons plus a teaspoon (16 teaspoons) of dark brown sugar. You’ll have a little leftover for your coffee or tea in the morning. Plain dark brown sugar will do in a pinch, if you can’t get molasses

Made precisely as written and it was crazy delicious!  Oh, the flavors!  Yotam, the story about your youth and the joys of messy eating brought back such wonderful memories of being a kid and truly being in the moment and savoring each bite.  Thank you.

Cane sugar is a decent substitute for muscovado, but not quite as complex. It’s available at most grocery stores. If you have a Latino grocery store nearby, you could get a block of piloncillo and grate the sugar. My other suggestion for this recipe, if you want to add spice, would be either Chinese 5 Spice or Ras al Hanout. Both of these go well with stone fruits, such as peaches, plums or apricots.
We grilled the peaches halves then placed them in the caramelized sauce face down at the end. Delicious! Also used whipped cream with a few dollops of yogurt, a lighter alternative to the marscapone.

Oh, yes, ripe, sweet peaches?
One of the very few things I miss about the US South, some others being grits & boiled peanuts.
Used cognac instead of rum. Worked great. Substituting crushed ginger snaps for sugar and sesame seed topping
@Gonzaga Fan - Use dark brown sugar as a substitute for muscovado sugar.  (I personally do not care for the strong flavor of muscovado sugar.)
Wonderful flavors, but the next time I make it, I will not cook the peaches for 10 minutes.  I think I'll go for 2-1/2 minutes a side.  First time the peaches came out way too soft.
I think this would be amazing with Smith & Cross rum, incredible stuff produced in Mr. Ottolenghi's adopted home town of London.
Yeah, made it tonight with S&C. Awesome.
This was wonderful!  I’m thinking that it would be very good on Chez Panisse almond cake!  Maybe with some sliced almonds sprinkled on it all.
@Kriste Yea to almond cake with this!  A good almond cake (David Leibovitz) goes great with anything — fruits  of all kinds, chocolate ganache, you name it.
I tried this over the 4th holiday weekend and it was fantastic, and very easy. Loved it so much i made it again using bourbon (very good bourbon) and liked it a little better.
Can't use alcohol, best rum substitution?
McCormick Imitation Rum has the flavor without the alcohol. I also saw some recipes where you use almond and vanilla extract as a substitute.

Question: If I want to double this can I make it in a large cast iron skillet. Love Ottolenghi, but he is a bit vague on the specifications regarding cooking pots "large skillet or casserole dish". What have you used if you doubled the recipe. Thanks!

Made this as written (with a brown sugar/molasses blend as suggested by the comments since I don't have muscovado sugar) through step 2. Then portioned into bowls, added a scoop of vanilla ice cream, and devoured. Absolutely delicious!! Would it be better with the yogurt/marcarpone/sesame finishes? You'll need to tell me, because I'll always stop at step 2.
Will canned peaches do?
@H in T  -don’t think canned would work, but frozen might.

For cooks looking for alcohol free option and substitute for rum, when cooking for friends who do not use alcohol, to replace the 1/4C rum I used a big squeeze of lemon juice and 1 tsp pure vanilla extract then added peach nectar (the kind in a can like Jumex or Kerns seen in many markets) to make 1/4C. Used this in step 2.   Not the same flavor as rum, of course, but  good.  Then I skipped step 4.
My family doesn’t drink alcohol. What can we use instead, or just omit it?
@ Gonzaga Fan I plan on substituting muscovado sugar with dark brown Demerara style sugar. Or you can add molasses to white sugar.
Pile that whole thing on a meringue pavlova shell -- even better if made with some almond flour.
Let the mascarpone come to room temp, so you can mix it better with the yogurt.

Great recipe as written. Only issue I had was the tsp. rum mixed with Tbsp. muscovado created a slurry vs. something grainy I could sprinkle. Still tasted great! This dessert is not too sweet either, really nicely balanced.
I would use my homemade crème fraiche to cut the sweetness
You can substitute brown sugar plus a little molasses for a taste similar to muscovado sugar.

Question...I would like to make this delicious dessert, but without rum. Is there a alcoholfree aroma available to get the good taste of rum. I have no problem with alcohol but I would like to make it for our family which includes children also..
Have you tried apple cider or apple or orange juices? They'll change the taste, but that may be more to the children's preferences.
Chevre instead of mascarpone would work, too.
Easy-Peasy... and a real crowd pleaser. A great way to finish off the bumper crop of peaches I bought at the farmers market to make jam and pie filling.
I bought some marscapone and otherwise used what was on hand—dark brown sugar, regular yogurt, and light rum—and I was 100% satisfied with the results!  Delicious.

Instead of the yogurt/mascarpone mixture, I made whipped yogurt using a little bit of heavy cream and Greek yogurt, adding a big glug of rum and a little brown sugar and molasses.  Absolutely delicious!
Absolutely fabulous and so easy!  The yogurt mascarpone mix drizzled with rum sugar really makes the dish special.  Highly recommend for summer peaches.

This was rated a five star desert by all my guests! Even my usual critical self was duly impressed and it is well worth hunting for the sugar. Here in Miami I found it at Plum Market.
Only drawback is that it is a desert that needs to be prepared before serving…but O so worth it!
Anne

I used very good farmer's market peaches, dark brown sugar, 8 yr old rum, and was surprised it was meh. It also requests ingredients I don't usually have on hand (marscarpone, Greek yogurt), so I won't make this again. I've had much tastier results simply roasting fruit with brown sugar and a little lemon juice.
I'm not seeing where the rum and sugar in step 4 end up in the final presentation?
See Step 5. ...sprinkle with the sugar mixture, the sesame seeds, and a pinch of salt.

This is amazing. I didn't have muscovado sugar, so I used Turbano sugar that I did have on hand. Additionally, I didn't have mascarpone, so I used whipped cream cheese instead. So yummy. I will definely use the actual ingredients someday.
@CedarSuz Definitely worth trying with the muscovado, which makes a gorgeous dark caramel sauce without the usual work that goes into a caramel.
What would you eat this with? Ice cream? Looks so good!
I really dislike rum. Do people have thoughts about using a fortified wine likePort, Madeira or Marsala instead?

A luscious to eat, slightly indulgent but easy to make dish that is a celebration of summer peaches.  It works with bourbon too if you find yourself out of dark rum.  You could sub in dark brown sugar for the muscovado, not quite the same intensity for flavor but it will work.  This is so good; try it as written the first time.  And if you're looking for a lighter and also perfect summer stone fruit dessert, try David Tanis' Nectarines in Lime Syrup on NYT Cooking site.

Sesame seeds don't seem to fit. Hand-crumble a few shortbread cookies/biscuits--even a few sugar cookies work. Also animal crackers or English "biscuits." This is also nice served over a slice of pound cake.
I thought I had all the ingredients but didn't. I subbed demerra sugar for the moscavado and cognac for the rum and it was delicious. The sesame seeds are a must (for the crunch).

A very nice adult dessert.I made half the recipe for two people. It was easy and quick but did necessitate a number of dishes and utensils. The greek yogurt and mascarpone blend bring it together. I think the next time I make it I will omit the sesame seeds and use pistachios and will use Demerara sugar in the rum and sugar topping.
Am I right in thinking (hoping) that the peaches are not to be peeled?

Charlotte Thacker - Like you, I was uncertain about whether peaches needed peeling.  Since recipe didn't specify, like you I assumed unpeeled.  But I tried it both ways, that is I peeled 2 of the peaches and left 2 unpeeled.  Both worked but the unpeeled pieces held their shape better.  Unpeeled is my choice next time.

Hi Gonzaga Fan, you could use regular dark brown sugar. It just wouldn't have such a deep intense taste. I ordered the muscovado sugar together with other items from a shop where I usually buy hard to find items. I never use Amazon, just direct orders from shops and shipping with regular DHL post. I intend to use the muscovado sugar for chocolate chip cookies as well.

Fresh peaches on hand but not wanting to run to the grocery store, I made several modifications. 1. loved the tip about using brown sugar with molasses. 2. didn't have dark rum but had golden rum so I used that. I don't know rum so not sure what I missed but it worked just fine. 3. also did not have mascarpone so I just put greek yogurt, the rum, and a touch of cascara vanilla cocktail mixer in the blender. 4. didn't top with sugar but used toasted sesame seeds and sea salt. It was delicious!
I’m dying to make this, and it’s surprisingly hard to find peaches in late fall. Might it work with pears?
Do we think apples would good here, too, when peaches aren’t in season?
Can you make this recipe with frozen peaches?
Hear me out: spoon this over fresh, warm Belgian waffles.

Frankly, I was disappointed in this recipe.  My gorgeous peaches would've been better eaten as is.  I did make a mistake and swirled the yoghurt mixture into the peaches, causing an ugly beige color.  Even with the rum, this dessert did not knock my socks off.  Not worth buying the muscovado sugar.  However, I also made Ottolenghi's blistered cherry tomatoes with yoghurt base, and it was a fabulous appetizer.

title: Spiced Couscous With Fragrant Steamed Fish Recipe
author: Yotam Ottolenghi

comments.commentBody: I found this recipe tasty but fussy and overly complicated. Next time I will streamline: blend all the tomatoes (wedges didn't make much of a contribution); use ground cumin; flip the fish so it finishes before the couscous is almost irretrievably stuck to the bottom; skip the oil preparation and just add the garlic and all cumin to the couscous mixture.
@EileenOt Ottolenghi is notorious for fussy recipes. I tend to avoid them unless I have time and patience on my side.

That couscous looks righteous. Might use some cod we have on hand. I bet this would be nice with other seafood - shrimp, scallops etc. - either steamed as in the recipe or grilled and served on top. Grilled lamb anyone?

You could turn this into a hamburger casserole, but the deliciousness of the flaky white fish over tomato-flavored cous-cous would be lost. Other seafood would be a poor substitute; grilled lamb something different altogether.

@Thom
Did you try this recipe Thom?  If so your comments/suggestions would be appreciated.

Make it as written and found this to be perfect at every level! Smoky, earthy tangy couscous with delicate fish and the oil bath of garlic was heaven. Only tweak was to increas the allspice and garlic a tad bit to my families liking. So, so, so good! 
@Eileen i beg to differ with some of your proposed tweaks. The whole tomatoes added an after cooking the crushed tomatoes adds a subtle bite with the couscous that adds an earthy zing. The fried garlic and ground cumin is critical at the end. This is a very common technique used in middle eastern and Pakistani/Indian cooking. The bite if the garlic is perfect and the oil finishes cooking the fish almost like it was poached.
Smoky, earthy, tangy couscous... My couscous was rather bland. What brand of couscous did you use?

I’m usually all in when it comes to using fresh ingredients, but sometimes the world gives you fish you need to cook and a convenient can of crushed tomatoes. Voila, just as good. I spiced this more like a Spanish dish and served with corn and a cabbage salad. Very satisfying and easy.

Very nice. I chopped the tomatoes and onion finely rather than get the blender dirty. Also had to use milled couscous rather than pearl as I couldn’t find it in the supermarket. This was fine, but steaming took longer as it absorbs more water I think. Will definitely try again with the right ingredient!
@Tony pearl couscous is pasta—NOT a grain. The two are not interchangeable.

@Tony
Pearl couscous is a pasta—not a grain. The two are not interchangeable.
Do not skip the lemon! Changes the whole character of the dish. Very nice!

Delicious, and a refreshing summery departure from the usual arrabbiata or shakshuka direction this dish often takes. Canned tomatoes and an electric skillet made easy work of it.  A hit at our table, with a request for crusty bread next time.

Please, reward us with the forever comment that says, "I used canned tuna in place of fresh fish, egg noodles instead of couscous, and didn't have those exact spices on hand so used a can of cream of mushroom soup -- great recipe, now on my rotation!"
Great with tilapia instead if on a budget. Don't skip the garlic oil. A pinch or two of ground cinnamon can be substituted for the cinnamon stick.
@Belle if you look up tilapia and what they eat, you’ll never eat tilapia again. I can no longer even see the fish without envisioning the photos I saw. Ugh.
My daughter is a marine ecologist who worked with tilapia farming during her training and she refuses to eat it because of the practices and the food they are fed.

Like many Ottolenghi recipes this one has a lot of steps that could be optional, but for me they make the dish special. Reading the recipe in the Sunday magazine I realized I already had everything I needed to cook this: Frozen Branzino fillets from Trader Joes, garlic, tomatoes, cilantro, lemon, etc. The fillets were scaled, so I didn't bother removing the skin. Very tasty.

Made this with Bob’s Red Mill tri-color pearled couscous, which was delicious - had some decent cod filets that needed to be cooked, but next time I’ll look for branzino. I also need to replace my rather geriatric allspice for next time. And I’ll also try canned San Marzanos, because the Roma tomatoes in the grocery store were uninspiring. But still, this is really a wonderful recipe!

Don’t be afraid of salt on this one it needs it! I a bit under-seasoned but it was still good. I did 1.5 x the recipe and it needed a bit more water than advertised. I also used a non stick and I think it would be better without one as the tomato sauce can properly caramelise and catch which I think would up the flavour profile. I’d probably swap in smoked paprika next time instead of regular to add more depth. V tasty!
Absolutely delicious! I made this as described, though I used tilapia. It was fantastic, great flavors, unlike anything I've ever cooked before. Will definitely make it again!
We wereciut of couscous so I used quinoa instead. Excellent dinner!
Simplify this and use canned diced tomatoes. No need for wedges.

Upped the spices, otherwise very good.  The steps oulined are essential, including the lemon.  I did use half canned whole tomatoes  and half fresh (for the second half) as I did not have enough fresh on hand.   I don't undertsand the comments here about couscous - milled or not, it is not a grain. Milled is just the process.
This was an easy dish to prepare but the I found the flavor unremarkable and would not do again. I  love pearl couscous with tomatoes and fish...just not the flavor here.

I never got to the "couscous stuck to the bottom stage" - I must not have given it enough time. The recipe doesn't really give you much instruction on what state the liquid should be in when you add the fish. I also found that 1 tsp salt wasn't quite enough - but didn't taste the couscous before adding the fish, so it was too late to adjust once it all started steaming. BUT... this dish was a hit with the fam. Will try it again!

I looked at this recipe and saw "2 cups of couscous." The box I bought didn't even have 2 cups and when I did some research, it would have cooked up to 6 cups. That seemed like a lot for a very small amount of fish (not even a pound as the recipe is written). I used about 4 oz of couscous (1/2 of my box) and that was plenty. I added seafood broth and white wine for cooking. I also added a cubed eggplant and simmered that for about 20 minutes before adding the couscous and fish. Yummy!
@Leslie Sounds like you may have been using standard couscous? Pearl couscous is different.
