I just loved this recipe.  The only change I made was using fresh oregano instead of coriandre, because Ottlenghi's recipe for pearl barley risotto which is so similar used oregano.  This is not a fussy recipe at all as some comments have said.
This was restaurant quality. I was worried about doneness for the fish but it cooked perfectly.  One note, I forgot the oil at the end and frankly it didn’t need it.
Followed the recipe except reduced the amount of couscous.  Honestly I didn’t find the recipe fussy at all.  Used ground spices and frozen cod filets.  It really was full of flavour.

Mea culpa, did not follow the menu as written. I did stick to the spices and garlic and lemon, which is critical, used cod which worked great, but for the couscous I substituted tricolor quinoa cooked til only chewy. Magnificent. The sauce is superb! I can imagine marinating extra firm drained tofu in the sauce and subbing it for the fish, perhaps serving with a strong dark green. Lots of steps but relatively quick and easy.

Enjoyed this recipe with cod steamed on top.  Minor modification -I used canned sweet marzanos for the early cooked sauce, and added halved cherry tomatoes later for flavor and texture.  I also toasted the cous cous with a pinch of cumin seeds before adding boiling water and cooking to al dente.  Really flavorful and easy.  Don't skip the garlic oil at the end - it is an amazing finishing touch.
Eileen's comment was spot on. another streamlining suggestion: I used a 12 oz can of san Marzano tomatoes, plus one tomato. Delicious.

Made the recipe as written (bar using ground cumin instead of seeds). Absolutely delicious, ate it for a weeknight meal but could easily be served at a dinner party. 
In future getting a head start on prep before the first step will likely help a lot with timing and speed. Also using nonstick would help with serving.
I love Ottolenghi’s recipes and this one is a winner.
Wouldn’t change a thing. A memorable meal exactly as written. Prep is easy & fast. Removing the skin takes a few seconds. Flavors are complex and wonderful.

Very much agree with Eileen. Three pots, the food processor, way too fussy. Too much couscous. The fish was lost. There’s got to be another way to preserve the lovely tastes of cumin, cinnamon bark, tomato and garlic with fish and couscous.

Personally, and no offense to Yotam, I’d use regular couscous and a fish such as orata, which holds flavors well. Also like many of his recipes, which I adore and keep but usually change the way they’re prepared, is overly complicated when it shouldn’t be. It reminds me of a dish I had the other day on Symi, an island in the Dodecanese. It was pickled fish, an orata, sliced with local hot peppers which have complex flavors but don’t burn, they just linger on one’s tongue for a few minutes. The couscous was flavored with lemon zest, parsley, salt and black pepper along with a tomato jam and cherry tomatoes. It couldn’t have been simpler but the flavor was amazing. Everything was local, except for the fish which came from 30 kilometers out in the Aegean Sea.

Well I played it fast and loose with this recipe and paid the price. Didn’t feel like cleaning the food processor so I just cooked the onion, tomatoes, garlic and spices down into a sauce. The fish I used (pacific cod and pollock) didn’t exactly get the flaky texture but that might have been because our power went out as I was cooking :/ Needed more salt than is called for here and might have benefitted from some capers, too.

This was so delicious, simple to make, and the branzino was perfect! I used smoked paprika and canned whole peeled tomatoes, blitzing them all as previous reviewers suggested. I would make it again with fire roasted diced tomatoes which would add to the smokiness of the paprika. Also, don't bother removing the fish skin, which becomes soft enough that it melts into the flesh when forked and adds a pleasant buttery flavor.

I made as written except with half as much couscous.  Yes, you can take shortcuts, but the touches such as the sliced garlic in spiced oil to finish are what elevate this dish (the garlic is so sweet and wonderful this way - not lost in the sauce as the usual minced garlic would be).  I also liked the tomato wedges added later for textural contrast.  Now that I've made it once as written, I think I will add preserved lemon paste to the sauce next time for an interesting flavor enhancement.
don’t change a thing - it’s perfect!

Ottoleghi's recipes are always very fiddly, so I simplified.
I used canned plum tomatoes, rather than fresh, and didn't bother with the food processor. Simply mashed up the canned tomatoes, slosh the can with water to get all the juices, chopped up the onion and cooked them in the pan, along with the tomato paste. Also, rather than a mix of whole and ground spices, used a tablespoon of ras el hanout, since it has the same major components. It was tasty, though.

Next time, I will also throw all the tomatoes into the blender with the onion - the wedges broke down in the pot, anyway. 
I'll also taste the couscous before adding the fish. My family wanted more salt in the couscous, but once you lay the fish on, it's hard to season the couscous without disturbing the presentation.
I skipped the the oil at the end and simply threw the garlic into the sauce, but then the fish could probably use more S&P for more flavor.
Unfortunately found it bland and the steamed fish to be an odd texture mixture with the couscous

This recipe is not the simplest, but it is so delicious that I am going to add it to my rotation. 
This earned a rare "banger" rating from me, and everyone who ate it. I would cook this on a weeknight, and I would gladly serve this at a party. 
My only note is that if your fish is scaled (which it really should be), no need to remove the skin. I found the quartered tomatoes really added to this and personally wouldn't skip that step. Would like to try adding in chickpeas.

I enjoyed it. I only had ground cumin so used that and next time I'll find cumin seeds and use those instead. 
I halved the recipe for 2 of us and it was plenty
we doubled the amount of fish. and I ended up freezing half of the couscous tomato mixture. I think next time I would serve with a side of vegetables also. very tasty dish and pretty easy too!

Delicious sauce. We didn't have any couscous so used orzo, which was fine. Also we didn't have enough fish so used half prawns. The combination was perfect. Used fresh tomatoes. No cilantro so we used basil. My son, who was visiting, cooked it for us, and we all agreed we would make it again.

This had a lot of interesting flavors for an easy weekday dish, but it needed intensity. Next time, I will use 50% more spices and another tomato. Using a coffee/spice grinder for the tomatoes and the same pot for the pasta and then the sauce/fish made cleanup easy, as well.
I thought that GF orzo would be the closest substitute for couscous, and it was good. But even though it was al dente and I did not add it until the fish, it was still too soft. I will make sure it is even firmer next time.
I scaled this up for twelve people and there was probably twice as much couscous as needed.  Since

Scaled this up for twelve people and there was twice as much couscous as needed.  Since there was no fresh sea bass to be found, I used halibut. It was an excellent substitute.  I agree with others who noted that some steps could be combined.  It certainly would have made it easier when cooking for a crowd.  It got rave reviews, even though the fish was a bit overcooked.

Yummy but I echo others that it's not salty enough, nor enough spices in the couscous. Would probably toast the garlic   seeds for a bit longer. Lemon is vital. Fish turned out perfectly cooked, and quite a simple weeknight meal for it being a Yotam.

I have now made and served this delicious dish 5-6 times - always a hit with guests  - and find that 1.5 cups pearled couscous yields a much better balance of fish to starch (don’t change the other measurements). I think branzino or cod are ideal, but it has also worked well with salmon fillets. The only suggested “shortcuts”  I would not recommend are eliminating the tomato wedges and skipping the garlic oil and lemon. They provide really good texture and flavor layers that elevate the dish.

I made as written with branzino and sorry - but I don't have patience on this day to peel a thin of piece of fish of its skin after prepping and cooking this dish for an hour.  Skin stays on and my husband can wrestle it off.  Agree it needs more salt than recipe calls for.  It is tasty but not one I'll make again.  Too fussy.

Pretty much made as written, but didn't have cinnamon stick or garlic (!). After tasting, I thought it would benefit from saffron, which it did. Will definitely make again. I'll add a chopped chili for some extra kick (or use in garlic oil). It would be good with farro subbed in for the couscous  (which I know is not a grain) for extra protein. Also a big handful of chopped parsley or arugula.

Rather bland after all the hype about Yotam and his cinnamon and allspice dishes.  The flavor was in the charred tomato and onion on the bottom of the pan.  I would add more allspice, cumin seeds and use some cayenne with the paprika.  My guests asked for more lemon wedges and some hot sauce.   I would also use parsley over cilantro on the garnish.

Will try this again but I think that - as written - it's too bland. Def double the seasonings in the couscous - next time I'll use smoked paprika. I put the fish in as soon as I stirred in the couscous, otherwise it would have been super overcooked and mushy. 
I agree not to skip to garlic oil but strain the garlics out before serving and I think you could use whole, smashed garlics. I think this method is adaptable to many proteins, incl. sliced tofu, chicken tenders or meatballs.

This is FABULOUS!  Made as written with one change - subbed grape tomatoes for the plum.  It also presents beautifully and worthy of a few dinner guests.  I was contemplating skipping the last step - glad I didn't - fantastic!  Thank you Ottolenghi!

I made 1/4 of the recipe for the two of us. Still too much couscous. I would reduce it to 1/3 cup next time but keep everything else the same. Up the spices too. I found it rather bland - and my spices are fresh! Nice to have another interesting recipe for whitefish - I used rock cod.

Had to sub in cod loins, the closest the market had to sea bass. No skin. Also had to use ground cinnamon and ground cumin. All worked fine. For all the spices, I found it slightly underseasoned, and wished I'd used broth instead of water for the couscous, and/or salted everything more heavily during cooking. But still good.

Sadly, it was not terrific and a lot of effort. I used wild sockeye salmon , I won’t make it again.
I am a great fan of Yotam, but everything can not be terrific, and perhaps it was my mistake.

Similar to two others, I substituted quinoa for the Cous Cous. I also used Chilean sea bass. Was overall delicious and mild and I agree with whoever said the oil was an important final step! I made the oil in the same pan I originally used for the quinoa which saves you a dish when cleaning up. Served over mixed greens for more veggies. 4/5 overall. Not spectacular but worth repeating!

Slightly fussy prep, but worth it.  I made this as directed, except used tilapia -- my budget does not extend to sea bass when I'm just feeding myself.  Everything was very good, and I think this recipe would work well with cod, haddock, or any "everyday" white fish.  And why not use something skinless, rather than add one more fussy step at the end?  
Pace, those who want to skip the hot garlicy oil at the end, but like oil tarkas in a lot of Indian cooking, this adds something special.

BF loved this (and he is hard to please), with a few modifications:  Sauteed 1/2 chopped onion and 1/2 chopped fennel bulb, then added ~8 defrosted summer tomatoes (skins slip right off), tomato paste, spices, and calabrian chili oil for heat. Blended in the pan with immersion blender before following rest of directions. Yes - you get a little scorching on the bottom, which was like a delicious paella soccarat. Don't skip the oil/garlic/spice - it is distinct and delicious.

I’ve cooked this with haddock filets and cod loin, both great. Following some others’ tips, I skipped the complications and instead just small diced the onion and added it to the pan with one 15oz can crushed tomatoes after quickly sautéing the spices. I halved the cinnamon and upped the paprika (hot) slightly. I added a small amount of chopped green olives (I’m partial to castelvetranos) to top of the couscous before adding the fish and it added a nice additional note that brightened the dish a little. Especially helpful if you don’t have lemon on hand!
Made as written but honestly not that impressive. Something felt off with the spice profile. also too fussy for little return. I'll pass on this one.

title: Charred Asparagus Lettuce Cups Recipe
author: Yotam Ottolenghi

comments.commentBody: The sauce on this is unbelievable!!! Would put it on anything. So good with butter lettuce and endive alone and the roasted asparagus added a great sweetness.

Loved the idea and presentation of this. But I made it ahead of time and found that my beautifully charred asparagus got mushy by 2 hours later - so I'd recommend assembling all and broiling them soon before serving. The dressing was bland as is (even with more lemon juice and salt), so I added a lg clove of minced garlic which helped. Used fresh mint and chives from garden.

Made as directed except I used fresh mint (no dried on hand). Fast, simple, and delicious. The Belgian endive leaves in particular were fabulous with the sauce and asparagus bits, and the urfa sprinkles were inspired. Many possible variations are possible after asparagus season ends.

Fab Fab Fab,  only used little Gem lettuce.  Grilled my asparagus vs broil. This is my new easy / lux side salad and veggie combo. Great with just about anything. I served this recently with steak frites.

Made this with NF yogurt, chopped scallions since didn't have chives, a bit of fresh mint, a bit more sugar than stated, and lots of lemon juice. Put the asparagus whole on a hot gas grill to avoid heating up the house, then chopped into smaller pieces when it was done. Quick, delicious and summery.

Very nice. Different in a good way with interesting textures and flavors. Market didn't have endive or dried mint, so I used Mint Medley tea and left out the endive. I am going to order dried mint. I know that dried mint and fresh mint are not interchangeable, but I think fresh mint would not be out of place. I cooked the asparagus in the air fryer. I agree that the dressing would be good on lots of other things.

I made the sauce exactly to recipe except used fresh mint and we were all disappointed.  The sum of something with so many delicious ingredients was just logy and uninteresting.  I served the salad with some roasted salmon, potatoes and beets and the presentation was beautiful. I might try making a smaller portion of the sauce again since I have all the ingredients to see if I can get it right.  Next time use dried mint and maybe up the lemon to make it more acidic?  We wanted to like it but we didn’t.

I agree with the several other commenters who found the dressing disappointingly bland given the long list of interesting ingredients, and it was furthermore three times the volume required for the vegetables. The large amount of dairy is probably the culprit on both fronts. Reducing the yogurt and sour cream by 50% might bring back some zip.

I've not yet made this recipe and asparagus season is now over. It occurs to me that this recipe would work well in the summer with green beans (or better yet a combination of green and yellow beans) or hariots verts

Because I was to serve this the next day, I roasted the asparagus using the recipe found on this site. Worked so well! Played around with the herbs, loved it, and will make it again. It was simply fun to create lettuce cups with the endive!

title: Slow-Roasted Lamb With Grapes Recipe
author: Yotam Ottolenghi

comments.commentBody: Temp seems high for such a long roast. Can you reassure me?

@Pete Thulson 
I made it last night according to the recipe and it was great! Just as described.
Could this recipe be used for a leg of lamb?  What adjustments would be needed?
I think it would be too dry, and is a waste of much more expensive cut. Usually the leg is roasted to medium rare, not braised.

As noted by Elle, a leg of lamb is meant to be served rare and juicy, not falling apart and shredded. However, you could easily incorporate some of the same flavors—cumin, lemon, thyme, and grapes too. I think some lemon zest at the end would be lovely and spring-y.

@Equilibrist Here in France we often cook lamb like this. It’s absolutely delicious, is so tender it can be eaten with a spoon and tastes totally different to lamb cooked any other way. It’s just a completely different dish…and well worth a go!

Be sure you use Lamb SHOULDER (the front legs), not lamb LEG (the back legs). You braise shoulder until falling off the bones, as in this recipe. You roast leg of lamb until medium rare. Very "different animals"!
Can you interrupt the cooking? Roast for 3 hours...leave at room temp...and then return to oven for the last 25 minutes closer to the dinner party?
@Melinda Marshall Go through Step 3 and then leave it tented with the foil. Gently reheat and add the vinegar from step 4 when you’re ready to serve. Or do it all the way through and serve room temp!

Prepared exactly as written, except for cooking 3.5 hours under foil because I had five lbs of meat. Did not need to skim off juices. Hands-on time is minimal for a wonderfully delicious result.  Served for a Passover meal with brown rice mixed with sauteed apple, a smidge of honey, and toasted pecans (haroset ingredients) and NYT parsley/fennel salad. Even lamb-skeptics loved this.
@MarMar I plan to serve mine for Passover too and your rice/apple dish sounds super yummy!
1 3/4 cups of water is too much!! Had to reduce in the sauce pan for too long- n next time just one cup

@Berel L.Too much water

How did it taste?!
I once had a butcher tell me that the shoulder is the front leg,and leg of lamb is the rear leg. Hard to imagine they are that different

I plan to make this recipe but I'm wondering if starting the lamb at 375 is too high.
Has anyone made this recipe yet and could offer an opinion?
Planning to make this but will use the oven probe provided by my Wolf oven to auto cook medium rare.

@Cynthia That would be ok if you’re cooking leg of lamb. Shoulder of lamb is a different cut, tougher and fattier, and it wouldn’t be good cooked medium rare. Needs bruising/slow cooking to tenderize.

@Suchachef there’s an awful lot of give in a shoulder of lamb and while I normally opt for more like 300 over 5 hours or so, I reckon 3 @ 375 would be fine. Oven thermostats tend to be all over the place so I’d just check to see how it’s doing after a couple of hours if you’re worried.
In step 4 it says to mix in any extra gravy.  How can there be any if it was all used in step 3 to baste the lamb and grapes?
@J-Man 50. I think the directions for WHAT to do with the liquid in the pan are quite confusing!
In step 3 it says that if there's an excess of gravy, strain it into a saucepan. Then in step 4 if there's any extra gravy, you add it back.

@Drgonzo777
Agree the sauce instructions are confusing. Do you strain then return all of it after reserving the excess? Can this be done in a Dutch oven?
I’ve never seen a lamb shoulder anywhere. Where do you obtain this?
@Bonnie G Costco!

@Swapna
I know costco meat is very affordable, but very unethical
I had hoped to make this for my seder but when the butcher told me how much it would cost (insanely expensive),  I decided on a leg of lamb. He also needed a week's notice to order it.

Lots of debate about using a leg of lamb for this dish. I just searched online and Jaime Oliver has a recipe for slow cooked leg of lamb. He suggests 300° for 4-5 hours. I’m going to try it! I will use Ottolenghi’s recipe but sub in the leg of lamb (that I have) and the lower, slower cooking instructions. Life is too short to not take an adventure now and then. Will report back!

Made this last night according to directions. Delicious and tender, but needed carving. No falling off the bone at all, and the weight of the lamb was exactly as recipe called for. So I reviewed the instructions and found a discrepancy: the total Cook Time listed at the top of the recipe is 4 hours and 50 minutes.  The time described in the recipe is 3 hours at lower heat plus 25 minutes at higher heat, i.e., 3 hours 25 minutes total. Which is correct?  Thoughts?
@JD Three hours and then thirty or so minutes at the higher temperature. Delicious, but I would carve on a cutting board to reduce fat.

Used 1.5lb bone-in shoulder chops for Easter lunch. Kept the rub the same, but scaled down to 3 shallots, 1 head of garlic, 1 C cooking liquid, and roasted for 90 min on 375F before raising to 450F for 25 min. Unbelievably good! The lamb was so tender and flavorful- it's one of the best dishes I've ever made. Served with saffron rice, roasted carrots with whipped tahini (another NYT recipe) and a green salad with herbs, artichokes, and feta. Will be dreaming of this lamb until next time!
@Emily M: did you place the chops in a single layer in the roasting pan?

@Jennifer I used a round dutch oven which fit the largest chop on the bottom, and then two side by side on top of that. My braising liquid just barely came up past the first chop. I still covered with foil and then put the lid on the dutch oven, and all three chops were perfect. I got better color on the top ones during the uncovered roasting step but all were delicious.
Can you double the recipe and cook in a single roasting pan? How would I adjust the time?
This is super delicious. Made with a boneless lamb shoulder roast, roasted for a little under two hours at 375 and then finished as directed, adding some water to the pan for the final 25 minutes.

Made a "flaked sea salt" rookie error and used our usual fine sea salt without adjusting the amount.  Very good flavor over all, but with bracing salinity in the sauce.  If using fine sea salt, use about half the amount in the recipe.

This was fabulous.  Prep was the most difficult part, and even that wasn't hard.  Could not find a bone in shoulder, so went for a partial bone in leg.  Worked beautifully, as far as I'm concerned, but took less time. If only there were smell-a-comment.  Shortly after this began to cook the house smelled sooooo wonderful and still does (just ate).  Only variation I had to the recipe was leg for shoulder (so don't let them say 'no'), and shorter amount of time - added grapes after 2.5 hours.

For the people wondering, here is the adjustments I made to use this recipe with a lamb’s leg (1,7 kg/3,75lb with bone): cooked first covered with everything (already the grapes) at 160°C (325°F) for a big hour, then removed the foil and cooked at 230°C (430°F) for 35min, until the thermometer showed 55°C (130°F) internally. Took the meat out, reserved covered, and let the grapes and rest grill on high for a little more. Came out perfect, still pinkish in the center.
I have a lamb shoulder in the freezer, just waiting for Easter.  But it is boneless and 5+ pounds.  How should I plan to adjust the cooking times for this recipe?

Hi - I had a smidge over 1 lb boneless shoulder in the freezer, half a package bought for an earlier occasion. (The store didn’t have any bone-in, and in fact was giving away the bones if you asked for them.) I looked at the result the recipe described and waited for that point, rather than strictly timing. Maybe a couple of hours, though I stopped paying attention to time after some point.

Turned out quite well. I would like to try the bone-in version at some point.
I totally agree with the person who asked about the temp for such a long roast.  375f is 190c. That's a hot oven .
I’m not loving the idea of roasted grapes…does anyone have a replacement suggestion?  Thanks.
Just a thought replace grapes with prunes
Could this be adjusted to make with lamb shoulder chops instead? If so any advice on that?
@Lynniespring Dried apricots?

Can we make 2 days ahead of an event, stopping before the grapes are added, refrigerate and then finish the grape sauce reduction on the day of? Will this be a better way to achieve the desired sauce consistency? Or will finishing the whole recipe 1-2 days ahead allow for better flavor development in the fridge?

After reading the comments I decided to roast at 300. I had a 5 pound roast and I roasted it for 3.5 hours at 300 and then turned it down to 275 for another hour. Slowing up the roast does make it fall apart, which is great. Also, I only use one teaspoon salt for the rub. It was very difficult to find a shoulder roast, but after calling five butchers, I found one.
It is perhaps the best lamb I have ever cooked!
Tried this with a boneless leg, cooking to 135 with rosemary replacing tarragon.  Well-done, but still juicy.  Could not get the sauce to thicken.  Will certainly try again!

Incredible!!! I couldn't find a bone in shoulder, so I used a 4 lb. boneless.  First cooking for 2 1/2 hrs.  Got a temp of 195 F. The rest according to the recipe.  One of the best recipes I have ever made.
Looking forward to leftovers.

Caraway seeds instead of cumin.
Veal stock!!!
Give black garlic a try if you can find some.
And finish it off with a good xeres (sherry) vinegar.
A terrific recipe.

I couldn’t find a shoulder anywhere and despite other comments telling me not to, I got a boneless leg… and it was great! The only change I did to accommodate was cooking at 325 for 4 hours to start. Then everything else was pretty much the same. Super tender, moist, and fell apart nicely. It also got an incredible crust when I bumped up the heat. Sauce thickened and the lemons got so jammy and delicious. Served with roasted fingerling potatoes, a very springy green salad, and sourdough. A special meal for sure.

If you can find lamb shoulder (you will likely need to go to a butcher shop, we pre-ordered from ours) - its worth it. This recipe however has some problems, and we are experienced in the kitchen and made many NYT recipes over the years. 
If you use a 'large' roasting pan as it suggests you need to double the liquid. All of the vegetables charred and the pan a mess. Moved it to a much smaller one and put on top of the grapes. I will look for a different recipe next time though.
Great flavor but our meat was a bit dry. Would add more liquid next time.

Slow cooked per these instructions a 3.4 lb. lamb leg sirloin, and it was absolutely delicious!   The meat almost fell off the bone, and was fork tender.  The juice/marinade, while it did not thicken up very much, was a wonderful complement to the buttermilk mashed potatoes I served with it.  With maple glazed carrots, we had a memorable Thanksgiving dinner, without the hassle of cooking a turkey.
Has anyone made this with Australian lamb? I love American lamb but it is pricey. So lamb is not something I frequently eat though I love it.
Can this be made with a few lamb shanks and adjusted cooking times?
I’m sure it would work well with either lamb shanks or necks.
Simply do not like cooked grapes.  Alternatives, por favor?
Do you think a lamb rack would work?

absolutely not. Rack of lamb is meant to be eaten Rare/mr/med at most. Long cooking it will ruin the meat and everything that is special about it. This is meat for a cheaper cut that needs a longer roast.
Could this be adapted for a slow cooker ?

I was worried about temp so braised at 350. Finished at 400. Worked. Maybe didn’t get the gravy as reduced as possible, but that’s ok because was quite sweet. Added the vinegar right into pan at second baste because I could smell was sweet and figured the acid would mitigate. Served with roasted potatoes and green salad
In my freezer I have Split Lamb Breast Plate.  Would it work in this?  I have no idea how to use this cut of meat.

Hi all. I’ve been reading a lot of of confusion about lamb shoulder versus leg of lamb. Just had an excellent conversation with my butcher as I was buying shoulder. The shoulder is neither the front leg nor the back leg of the lamb. If you were to dis articulate the front leg of a lamb, the shoulder is literally the shoulder: it’s the confluence of the front ribs, and the back ribs, which converge on a small piece of neck. This combination of lamb rib and neck is your shoulder roast.
If making double the amount, how would that affect the roasting time required?

It's a higher temp than I normally use with slow-cooked lamb. Couldn't find bone-in so went for two small rolled boned shoulders. They shrank dramatically at that high heat and the broth is salty, despite my having cut the salt by a third. Planning to serve this tonight with fat white beans and garlicky broccoli rabe, so salt hopefully won't be a problem.
I have a boneless shoulder that is around 4.25 lbs. Do you have any tips or suggestions for me since you also used boneless?
Can I substitute with lamb shanks? My grocery store doesn’t carry shoulder and looking for substitute

@JD I assumed that the extra hour of "cook" time included the hour of marinating the lamb with the dry rub before it went into the oven. Also, the age of the lamb sold may vary and also affect roasting time needed for it to become tender.
I am reluctant to add 3 TABLESPOONS of salt. It seems excessive, even for a 4lb shoulder. I don't want to ruin my dinner, which was expensive enough. Has anyone done this?
@Jacqui 3 Tbsp of fine sea salt was too much.
What goes well with this
I’m making this today and considering adding carrots to the pan. Thoughts?
@Shari i agree. We added potatoes in ours, and they were great, augmenting the dish while reducing need to make another side dish
I found one at Whole Foods, but no bone-in.@Bonnie G
@Kerry our Whole Foods had without bone too but I thought the bone would impart more flavor
The recommended amount of salt is far too much. The pan juices were inedible. I had to swap pans and improvise on the gravy.

made this for Easter dinner,  came our great .. no changes in the recipe except cooked at 350 in dutch oven instead of roasting pan and used a 3 lb boneless lamb shoulder.. great balance of flavors on the gravy

Wondering if I should’ve halved the grapes or pierced them before they went into the pan.  Otherwise the result I got was great! Only difference is I used a boneless shoulder and roasted for 2.5 hours with the last 30 mins as instructed. The lamb was buttery soft either way.

Couldn’t find lamb shoulder so used lamb shanks, otherwise did more or less as written. Absolutely delicious, with comments of “best lamb I’ve ever had” and “I didn’t know lamb could taste like this.” Impactful presentation for relatively little work!

This was absolutely delicious and so very easy.  I poured off some of the liquid as the recipe directed, after skimming and reducing I added the vinegar and garlic to the au jus and combined well before pouring back over the lamb.  We will make this again and again.

This was delicious. I could only get a boneless shoulder (4.25 lbs) so I bumped down the initial temp to 350 degrees and the time to 2.5 hours. I removed the shoulder from the netting, seasoned both sides and then re-rolled and tied it and let it sit for 45 minutes. I didn't pierce with a knife since I was able to season inside and out. From there, I followed the rest of the recipe as written. The lemon pulp dissolved into the sauce and at the end I chopped the cooked peel into smaller pieces.
Made this with a leg of lamb. Was amazing!
This was superb!  "I love it!" was what I heard when I served it.  Thank you!!!

While the ingredients sound wonderful, the roasting turned into a fire place. For instance. How large is the large cooking pan?  Were the ingredients measured carefull only to be incinerated.  The cooking temps are wayyyy off to slowly (heavy on the slowly) cook the lamb. What a horrid waste of time and precious lamb.

Luckily I caught the shoulder/leg distinction before roasting this... but I loved the flavors despite using a different method for my boneless leg. I'll come back to this if I ever have a bone-in shoulder on hand!
I made this with lamb shanks and put it in a smaller roasting pan and it turned out perfectly. My guests were amazed (including my very apprehensive partner)! Would make again :)

I used shoulder. It was very tender and tasty and well browned at the end but too greasy, and the grapes/lemon/garlic turned to an unattractive mush. If I do it again, I’ll pour out all the liquid before the final roast (not leave 1/2 inch), and strain the fat off of all of it before basting with the drippings.

We had a fatty cut and removed about 1/2 fat after the initial cooking, but were fine not adding any additional water. 

Added potatoes to roast at the same time as the grapes. Works well, and could be done also with carrots, parsnips, brussel sprouts etc.

This is delicious.  I did manage to find lamb shoulder.  I followed the recipe pretty closely, except I reduced the temp to 350 (convection) and added 1TB cumin by accident.  After 3 hours the meat was tender but not coming off the bone.  There was a lot of fat after the grapes cooked, so I removed meat and grapes, removed some fat and made a gravy.  The grapes and roasted garlic made the sauce sweet and rich, no cumin flavor.  I cooked everything the day before and rewarmed, worked well.
Has anyone tried it using preserved lemons and pickled grape? I have both ingredients at home (Ottolenghi’s fault!) and I am very tempted to use them.

Made it exactly as written, except that I lowered the temp to 350° because it was a 3 pound piece of meat. Next time will add more water halfway through. Tender and delicious. Did not need 25 minutes at 450°.  More like 10.

I just did two of these today, each in a separate pan - and all the lemon & thyme that were in contact with the foil burnt & couldn't be added to the juices as the recipe instructs in the final segment. 
Anyone have an idea how to prevent that?
Also, the juices in the pan with 2" sides browned nicely; those in the pan with sides 3"+ stayed clear. 
After all steps were completed I combined everything into one pan before giving them to a friend; so I don't have any further feedback.

Ah, one further comment: would have been super helpful to have the quantity of salt and pepper included in the ingredient list, i.e, the norm and as all the other ingredients in this recipe do. I had the misfortune of moving too quickly & in seeing "3 tablespoons" right after the word "sugar," added 3 T of sugar. I did spot my error before adding the salt & pepper, but not without setting me back.
I used a leg of goat for this and it came out a bit too sweet for my taste.  I feel the more gamey flavor of lamb might be a better choice.  Also could perhaps have upped the lemon/acid.

The meat tasted incredible. The grapes....just tasted like grapes. I will try again with a different fruit. I got a bonus quart jar of lamb fat to roast potatoes in for the next couple years. This cut is exceptionally fatty. Don't serve at room temp or it will feel like wax candle drippings got mixed into the sauce.

I was disappointed with this recipe (and I had an excellent shoulder cut). I found it to be bland despite the great combo of flavors in the rub and sauce. The sauce was way too thin and greasy, and did not reduce down to a gravy-like consistency. The grapes and shallots were nice, but I think the grapes made the sauce too sweet (and I used more vinegar than the recipe called for).
Can I go up to step 2 then refrigerate and complete 3 and 4 the next day?? A lot to do for a 1:00 luncheon.
Could this be done in a go slow cooker? And how far in advance would you say it could be cooked before serving? Our oven makes things way too hot and we’re making chocolate eggs that need to cool!
Has anyone tried Moroccan salted lemons with this recipe?  Thinking to add slivers of well rinsed cured lemons to this dish.

Made this for Easter yesterday, and it was great. A couple of comments: I made it in a big Le Creuset lidded pot. I think this is more accurately a braise, not a roast. I used chicken stock instead of water. The shallots and lemons are perfect. I spun the cumin seeds and peppercorns in the spice grinder, since I never have much luck in a mortar. It very definitely needs to be carved in the kitchen, since the bone structures are so complicated. I returned the meat to the sauce (which never got very sticky or sweet) for serving.

This recipe needs a good editor. 
- The directions for WHAT to do with the liquid in the pan are VERY confusing! 
- Time req'd also confusing. Total: 5 hours. All that chopping! Then, 1 hour to "set". 3 hours to bake. Another 30 mins at 450. Rest for 15. Math?   
Leg v. Shoulder - Use Colorado lamb shoulder. Flavor is much better than lamb from Australia or NZ. It does pull away from the bone, not quite like pulled pork but similar. 
That said, I make it work and the flavors are amazing. Truly.

I reduced the heat to 325 and make this with a venison shoulder roast and it was absolutely delicious. Just be sure to add extra liquid (I added red wine and beef broth) and some extra fat (bacon is nice) because venison is quite lean.

I tried this on a smaller scale using 3 leg of lamb chops and reducing the ingredients proportionately. I cooked the lamb chops for 1and half hours on the slow and then added as the rest of the ingredients as per recipe. It was superb, the taste and texture.  I was only cooking for one guest and myself. I will do lamb this way in future using the recipe in full.

I just tried this following the instructions (reduced the amount of salt because that’s an outrageously unhealthy amount)!and timings. It’s still quite wet and the grapes haven’t really collapsed down. The lamb (hogget actually) isn’t falling off the bone. It’s fine but not special. Next time I will cook a little lower and for an hour or two longer and possibly add some carrots into the roasting tin along with the shallots.

leg of lamb does not need to be done medium-rare. lots of traditions around the world braise it to the same consistency as this recipe calls for. I used a leg: did one hour for 375 and then 3.5 hours at 325 and followed the recipe as is after. The recipe tells you to reduce the sauce if there is too much liquid! (I did use a mixture of bone broth and red wine to enhance umami). Flavor combinations here are great!

title: Spiced Pea Stew With Yogurt Recipe
author: Yotam Ottolenghi

comments.commentBody:
This is an East-meets-West variation of the humble "aloo-matar" or potato-peas curry that every Indian kitchen on every continent has on its everyday home-cooking rotation. Every family has its own tasty version. It can be as fussy or unfussy as time permits. We do not use chicken stock - this is a vegetarian curry for us.  Yogurt is served on the side or in a raita.
The comfortable cook will find that this recipe is very forgiving - no precise measuring necessary. It will always taste good!

I'm pretty sure Ottolenghi made it after asheh goje faringi, a persian stew that is very popular (As a persian myself I like regular ash better but oh well) I do agree this recipe is much like aloo-matar and wonders why he omitted the tomatoes because then it is essentially aloo matar
@AD what’s wrong w aloo-matar? Does it need to be Persian, somehow?
Well nothing is wrong with aloo matar but Ottolenghi said this dish was "Inspired by a warmly spiced Persian stew" in the description of it

This sounds wonderful!  I love all kinds of peas and have everything but the yogurt and ginger. 

It reminds me that I should make mushy peas before it gets too hot - I consider them a cold-weather dish, though I’m now rethinking that…
If you have a Trader Joe’s they have ginger frozen in little ice cube trays. It’s great to keep in the freezer.
@GardenRose thank you for reminding me I had bought some!!!
@GardenRose You can easily freeze chunks of ginger yourself. Cheaper and plastic free!
As to why Ottolenghi omitted the tomatoes, he gives his reason: “this version allows the sweetness of the peas and the warmth of onions, ginger, cumin and turmeric to lead.”

@LT made this recipe with about 3 tablespoons left over cherry tomatoes  & garlic that I'd cooked slow in olive oil , basically Marcia Hazan's old standby chestnut and switched the vegetable to chicken broth , oh and had no fresh ginger so used Diaspora Company dried . The yogurt i feel is essential ., the cilantro only for those who love it  just as Awesomely delicious without .
Any idea why Ottolenghi omitted the tomatoes ( which are in the Persian dish that inspired his version!  Among other things,seems like the red bit would brighten up the appearance.

I couldn’t face the prospect of overcooked peas(commissary canned peas anyone?) so I used the flavor profile with split pea soup which is “supposed to taste like that”, topped with frozen pea and yogurt garnish and it was yum!
As a potato lover, wish I’d used more potatoes! It felt like a lot of peas, and it was—not a bad thing, but I bet more potato would have helped it thicken and helped balance all the peas.
Two and a half teaspoons of salt seems an awful lot.
@Bruce Alter I agree!
@Bruce Alter definitely could use a little less salt, especially with the broth! Delicious recipe though
Looks like you need the zest and juice from half a lemon. Leave the other half unpeeled.
This was delicious! The only adjustment I made was reducing the amount of salt. Will definitely make it again.

Agree about using more potatoes next time. I’d also use slightly less broth—it was good, but more soup than stew. I used ground cumin and ginger because I had them. Would use more than 1 T of each next time. Overall, though, we liked it a lot and will definitely add it to the rotation!

This recipe is excellent. It's a keeper exactly as written. But I wish NYT recipes were a little more realistic about prep time. I can't get onions, potatoes, ginger, lemon zest, and cilantro finely chopped, plus sesame seeds roasted and dry ingredients measured, in 15 minutes. And not having a large (or even medium-sized) kitchen, I'm constantly having to clear and clean during prep.
@Roslyn i agree, i would also love them to clearly define how everything should be prepped so that im not manically slicing something last minute that i suddenly realize has to go in the pot
@Roslyn The potato, ginger, onion, and cilantro are listed as chopped in the ingredient list, so those tasks are not included in the time estimate.

@David B, point taken. Unfortunately, this approach makes the time estimates basically useless. It doesn’t take much time to make almost anything if you omit both the prep time of washing, peeling, and chopping, and the cooking time!! If only we all had sous chefs to do all that prep work for us.

I’m a tinkerer. I always read the notes and modify based upon my knowledge having cooked professionally. I used less water, added 1/2 a cauliflower instead of more potatoes. Cooked for one, so I made my topping with cilantro, lemon zest as well as peas and sesame seeds. I used 1# fresh peas. I ❤️ the results. I salt per serving. So good!  Combination of hearty and fresh is perfect!
Best ever
Just to be clear -- this is two pounds of frozen peas (two one-pound packages), yes?

@C
Same question, hoping someone can clarify?
The flavor is a little flat even though it is chock full of spices. Not sure how I would fix…
@Amy Ballard i added more spice and i actually think a goid dehydrated ginger gives more punch
Is there any way to successfully sub the milk yogurt with non-dairy?
@Tracy I would try using tahini! It would add a nice creaminess while mirroring the sesame flavor in the topping. You might have to thin it out with water or olive oil to make it drizzle nicely.
Inspired by your suggestion, I made a tahini drizzle with some kick (fresh garlic and lemon) and it MADE the dish. Thanks! @anna
@anna great tip! trying this tonight
Help me understand - is there a reason why i should not simply use a zester for the lemon rind.  Perhaps it will be more flavorful in slightly larger pieces?

@YiaYia Yes, the lemon flavor is more distinct than it would be if the rind were zested. I got little pops of lemon flavor along with bursts of flavor from the whole cumin. Personally, I love dishes where various flavors are distinct like this. Deelish!

So delicious! In Step 4 when it says bring heat to high and boil, then cook for another 20 minutes, that means boil for another 20 minutes. I was very skeptical, but luckily it was a success! I did turn the heat down a bit to the temp I'd use just to maintain a boil instead of full blast on it.
Terrible way to phrase the pea amount. I THINK it means 2 lbs. of peas, but my god they've written it in riddle form. This is why Ina asks 10 people to cook a recipe before publishing.
Question to those who've made this - wouldn't all that cook time reduce the peas to utter mush?
@Edith no

This is delicious! I love Ottolenghi’s food, but most of his recipes are so complicated. This is very do-able and excellent effort-for-outcome ratio. Following a comment here, I added some cauliflower and used just 1 lb of peas. Definitely a keeper!
Surprisingly vibrant and fresh with the lemon, cilantro, yogurt and sesame.  Don’t skip the toppings.
Simple. Delicious. The cilantro adds so much to the dish that I would recommend not leaving it out. Very spring. Will serve with radishes.

Wow, nothing prepared me for how good this is!  It sounded like it was going to be a little ho-him, but it’s full of flavor.  I made it exactly as written.  I will definitely be trotting this out to impress my vegan friends!
My husband hates cilantro so I generally sub with parsley or basil. Any recommendations for the best substitution? Thanks
Tarragon or basil might maintain the Persian flavor profile.
Lynette - You ask about a cilantro substitute in this recipe given your husband doesn't eat it.  For this recipe, given the other seasonings like ginger, cumin and tumeric, I would choose parsley.
I used dill and parsley and it was delicious @dimmerswitch
I knew halfway through cooking that this wouldn’t b good. Thin, bland and far from a tasty hearty spring soup.  I followed a lot of the suggestions but they didn’t help

Incredible! Made a couple of adjustments:
Was completely out of cumin so substituted curry powder, I cut up some preserved lemon instead of the lemon rind and used preserved lemon juice as well (and lots of it), didn’t do cilantro, and added some fennel seeds. Was to die for. Will be doing again very soon.

I love two things about this recipe: 1) It saved me from going to the grocery store when I thought I didn't have the ingredients to make a square meal and 2) It reminded me that well-cooked peas can be delicious. I was initially nervous about adding the peas before the potatoes, thinking the former would surely overcook before the latter cooked at all, but I was wrong.
This is one of those rare, well paced recipes where everything comes together as simply as it sounds, and so very tasty!
Wow!!! Made this pretty much exactly like the recipe. Only substitute was I had to use ground cumin it’s all I had. So flavorful!
Seriously good, and far less of a pain in the posterior than many of his other recipes.
@MG so agree, he can be a bit over the top on details and ingredients. This one was a basic version
I liked this, but it tasted a little bland to me, like it needed some heat or more spices. Still a nice hearty dish, great for winter.

It was a fairly tasty dish but nothing spectacular. The frozen peas were really, really sweet although the spices and the lemon helped with the sweetness. We skipped the separate reserved peas and sesame seeds. We also used less peas and more potatoes and still wished for more potatoes. I would recommend finding a real 'aloo-matar' recipe, rather than this version.

As others have stated, go ahead and add more potato. I added the equivalent of 3 more Russet potatoes. My wife loved it! The topping is what gives this 5 stars, IMO. Next time I might some garam masala.

This was great!  I added 1 teaspoon of red pepper flakes to give it some mild heat.  I also found it was too soupy after adding 4 cups of broth and boiling for 20 min, so I took one tablespoon of cornstarch and mixed it with a few tablespoons of the liquid an added it back to the pot an it thickened up nicely.  Next time I will make more of the pea/cilantro/sesame mixture, as it was delicious!

Hi
Is there a way to change the weights etc to metric? Would be nice for the rest of the world.

@Eejay

Find the tiny ruler at the top of the recipe. It converts to metric measures.
@Eejay there is a metric button at the top of the ingredients list in the app
@nancy Huh. I never realized that was there! How convenient.
I used three medium-size russets. Perfect ratio. @PJ
Simple and flavourful.  Not your normal Ottolenghi recipe as for as simplicity, but the flavours were all there.

Made a half recipe, although full potato. It made a huge amount, certainly enough for 4. I minced the garlic, although it doesn't say to. Served with fish, roasted vegetables. 

It might be nice as a soup.
This was unexpectedly delicious!  The lemon juice at the end really elevated the flavor.

I made this last night and it was delicious!  Omitting the tomato (as mentioned by others about a different recipe) does highlight the other ingredients. My husband does not like peas, but ate this dish up!
Delicious. Followed the recipe to the letter.
This was SO good! Followed the recipe exactly, but used the zest and juice of a whole lemon—and white truffle infused olive oil along with truffle salt. Will eat this again and again.

This is a delightful recipe. Far be if for me to suggest adjustments to the stunning Yotem, however I think you can use a bit more lemon. Zest the whole lemon, add a tiny bit more lemon juice and you don't need to cook the peas in the first batch that long. This is a simple and really satisfying dish where you can taste every ingredient and every ingredient justifies its place.
I recommend using pulsing an immersion blender just a few times as a last step to to add a little body to the strew. Excellent recipe! A real pleaser.

Fantastic recipe, delicious result. My recommendation is to read the recipe carefully before you start. There's a lot of mise en place in this project, and you need a mortar and pestle. Nothing difficult, just a lot to do before you start cooking. Definitely worth the effort. I served it with King Arthur's Naan bread recipe. Yum yum!

Yum!   This started out ok, until I was adding the cumin, when the top fell off into the pot and a pile of cumin wound up in the pot!  Then I read the directions incorrectly and added 2 teaspoons of turmeric. Went to the pantry but couldn’t find the potato!   I added a bit more lemon juice and added a bag of baby spinach cuz it was almost ready to be trashed.    I had crusty ciabatta, that I put in the bottom of the bowl and spooned the stew over the bread then Added non dairy plain yogurt.  It turned out delicious.   My dinner partner loved it too!   I’ll definitely make this again. Thanks Yotam!

I doubled the potatoes and dropped the peas to 22oz. My husband still thought there were too many peas.  I used only 1 tsp of salt, and it was flavored well.  Definitely add the lemon zest and 1 tbsp lemon juice at the end, otherwise this dish would have been bland.  I will be making this again as my teenagers and husband did not complain.  Served it with garlic naan.

Very nice soup. Personally, I didn't think the sesame-pea mixture was vital; the overall flavor was already great without it. So could be omitted to simplify the recipe (or just add the cilantro and some sesame oil). I regretted cooking with a lid, as the soup did not really thinken that way. Still really enjoyed the result. Finally, especially if you eat this with rice or pita, the recipe makes very generous servings.
This is a delicious recipe as written. I've also made it with chicken broth and 8-10 small roasting potatoes instead of the russet and it was great. Don't skimp on the ginger and cumin seeds.
A total flavour bomb absolutely amazing, had it with a side of roti
I am biased bc I love peas and every ottolenghi recipe ever. This was delicious. Oops didn’t have sesame seeds so threw in a splash of sesame oil instead. 10/10

Agree with some others that the flavor was a bit bland or "flat". Had high hopes after cooking the onions and spices! Not sure if this will make it in the rotation, but I'd probably double the spices and aromatics next time. I did 2 quick pulses with an immersion blender for texture.

Used two potatoes, as others recommended. One or two would have been fine. Zested a whole lemon instead of cutting slices of zest. Made with rice and tofu on the side. Really good! Next time I'd mix some lemon juice and salt with the yogurt before adding that. And could sub some of the peas for other veggies if desired. Really good as-is though.
Delicious, but next time I will blend half the soup so it’s thicker and more stew-like. I will also skip the “pea topping” and just finish the soup with sesame seeds and cilantro.

Really enjoyed this, I made it according to the recipe except used powdered ginger because it’s what I had. My teenage daughter liked it too. However, leftovers the next day had an overpowering lemony household cleaner taste due to all the lemon peel. I might try just zest next time, or only add juice per serving.
Despite the spices, this was quite bland. I feel like it needed more garlic and more potatoes.

Absolutely amazing! I doubled the potato and used chicken bone broth as I am not vegetarian ( though I am sure vegetable stock would be just as lovely!), really went heavy handed with the spices and added a couple chopped thai chilis for a little heat. I doubled down on cumin with both cumin seeds and a splash of cumin powder. I added the sesame seeds in with the pea and cilantro topping and whipped up a quick tahini garlic drizzle while the soup simmered and served with that and greek yogurt.

Many people here miss the tomatoes; I do not, nor does my 5 year old, who hates them. Made on a night when I had basically nothing in the fridge or pantry. But I had a couple old bags of peas and a potato! Made as written except I used water instead of stock and it was STILL delicious! Cut some pita in half and toasted in oven with olive oil and zaatar. Great early spring dinner, cheap, for me a very minimal ingredient list, healthy, satisfying. What’s not to like?

My favorite dish of frozen peas is mousseline of  peas, a recipe from a cookbook on French cooking that I’ve been making for years. I serve it with roast lamb and daupininois of potatoes. Cook the peas in plenty of water with a small carrot, peeled onion and a sprig or two of thyme. Get them real soft. Drain.  Discard the carrot, onion and thyme. Put in a food processor or food mill. Purée, adding generous bits of butter and heavy cream until the puree is smooth. Absolutely delicious
More potato
Elegant and delicious.

@Lynette my mother does too. I was thinking flat leaf parsley, lemon zest and juice as written  and 1/4-1/2tsp ground coriander (preferably from grinding toasted coriander seeds - a much better flavour intense, nuanced i.e. more flavours apparent) to provide fresh herbaceous and citrusy notes found in coriander/cilantro herb
Step 4:  "Stir through the lemon juice".  What kind of English is that? Does that mean add? Seems simpler to say stir in or add.

@Dan

this is what all the recipes these days say. “Stir through the lemon juice” to me means put the lemon juice in a bowl and stir whatever it is into that! I’ve learned what they mean though..

Yawn. This so-called ‘Persian’ spiced pea stew is really just a wet Indian sabzi with a fresh garnish. While the cumin, turmeric, and ginger are textbook Indian flavors, Persian stews tend to have more layered acidity—think dried limes, barberries, or pomegranate molasses. What’s presented here as innovative is actually a recycled dish with a trendy topping. It’s a an ok recipe, but let’s not blur culinary traditions just to give an old idea a new name.
I added ground coriander and swirled in coconut cream instead of yogurt. Served with fresh naan topped with a generous sprinkling of Dukkah. Great meal. Thanks for the inspiration.
Exceptionally bright - the lemon zest is a key element. The toasted ground sesame seeds with minced cilantro was also brilliant. A real keeper.
Delicious! I only had Japanese sweet potatoes on hand, so I used those and it was incredible. Followed the rest of the recipe exactly.
Weirdly good

Loved it!  I didn’t have vegetable broth so used chicken, some store bought with a hit of homemade.  My husband really wants meat with dinner so I paired it with some smoked pork chop, heated with mustard in the air fryer.  Nice combination.
good adding kale
Amazzzing!  I subbed a Japanese sweet potato for the russet potato.  Added a few sprigs of fresh fenugreek leaves from the farmers market, but not needed.  Will definitely make this again!
For something so simple this was delicious! Really pleased with how it turned out, especially as I was missing a lot of ingredients (sesame, lemon, ginger)
Followed Ottolenghi’s recipe exactly as written and it was wonderful.
Could someone tell me what to do with the garlic? Recipe says only to lightly crush, then peel. Putting it in whole seems wrong.

Have made this twice. Follow the directions re/ the garlic using five or six nice fat cloves. I think they dissolve in the stew. Also, if it's a bit thin at the end, pulse an immersion blender in there a few times to add body.

Absolutely delish—will be adding to the rotation!—however, I’d wait to season with salt after it has cooked, not in Step 1, since the stock may be more or less salty. Will be making this again soon, and look forward to trying the leftovers cold…
In Step 4, when it says turn heat to high, bring to a boil, and then cook for 20 minutes more, does that mean keep at high heat and continuous boil for 20 minutes?
@Edith surprisingly no! They still have texture and even some "burst"! But they are definitely cooked, just not to mush
So delicious! I like this on the first day exactly as it is, and the second day with a bit of baby spinach (and maybe egg or cheese next time around) stuffed into a roti.
When I cooked this, the color screams bland-but no this is packed with flavor! Super surprising and making my lunches for this week something to look forward to.
I had the same thought as you, but inadvertently made it with one pound. It was still delicious and made four 1.5 cup servings (enough, IMO) @C

This was a lovely easy meal to make for myself while feeling under the weather. As a solo business owner, I don't have as much time as I would like to cook, and this recipe was a breeze! most of the chopping happened in the first 10 minutes, and then after that I was able to leave it be. thank you!

It would definitely not be on my “easy” list; I found the steps time-consuming. I modified as I neared the end, wanting to be done. And it still was very tasty! Flavor is great. Next time I would definitely cut back on the amount of water; it came out more as soup. I cut down on amount of salt required and my husband thought it was still a bit salty. And it makes a HUGE amount; would cut recipe in half next time.
To increase the pantry-ness of this dish, I substituted the lemon and salt for a chunk of preserved lemon - everything except the lemon was already in the house.
After reading through the comments, reduced salt and broth amount, added two potatoes and half of a head of cauliflower. Delicious!
Excellent! And I agree, a very forgiving recipe.
I have made this twice and am about to do so again to satisfy my current craving. So yummy.
Absolutely Loved it! Very creative combo, esp the toppings, that made it really special. Agree w rec to use more potatoes next time to thicken it up.

I didn't have vegetable stock on hand so added sliced carrot and celery to the fry and a cup or so of frozen mixed vegetables to the boil, along with a few shakes of a spice mix. It tasted great, and the carrots and corn added some nice color to the stew.

Delicious recipe but that 2.5 tsp of salt was CRIMINAL!  this level of salt is the kind that will give you a night of fitful sleep riddled with the most violent nightmares you will ever see.  do NOT put in 2.5 tsp salt at any cost !!!!! my goodness!!! i feel like i had salt stew. !! and i love salt!!! i oversalt everything!! but that was just too much!!!
Easy to make and tasty. A little sweet, but easily balanced with salt and more lemon. Some siracha also helped this dish sing.

The flavour profile on this is fantastic. A few notes: I guess it depends on your stock, but 2 1/2 tsp fine salt seemed like way too much for me; I reduced to a heaping teaspoon and it was plenty salty. We prefer a slightly less brothy stew, so I reduced to about 3 cups of stock. I was in a hurry to finish dinner and only cooked the final stew for the recommended 20 mins, but if I had more time I think I'd simmer it all for longer.

The only thing ommitted was the salt and we subbed a Japanese sweet potato for the russet. It was so was DELICIOUS. Definitely does not need tomatoes as they would compromise the flavor profile as it is. I totally will make this again and would compliment the Coconut Kitchri from a few weeks ago in a Thali style meal well.
This soup is so good, and my husband who despises peas liked it.  Going into my favorites for sure.
I have fresh peas. Can anyone think of any reason why I would not use them instead of the frozen peas? I don’t want to embark on a recipe and ruin it.
I really wanted to like this recipe but was underwhelmed. The lemon rind was overpowering and the yogurt just didn’t go with such a watery soup. Maybe I didn’t thicken it up enough with the potatoes.
Weird flavor. I ate a bowl and threw the rest away
I enjoyed this one! Followed as written. That lemon zest packs great flavor.

Agreed that this came out watery. I would use at least one more potato, and possibly less broth. I siphoned off some of the liquid to make it more of a stew. The addition of red pepper flakes would be welcome. But overall a tasty vegetarian soup (not really a stew in my opinion). I don't have a mortar and pestle to smash the sesame seeds so I used the back of a wooden spoon
I really enjoy this recipe. I’ve already made it several times.
How much vegetable stock? It just says “cups”

title: Chicken and Chickpea Tray Bake Recipe
author: Yotam Ottolenghi

comments.commentBody:
Ras El hanout recipe: 

1 teaspoon salt

1 teaspoon ground cumin

1 teaspoon ground ginger

1 teaspoon ground turmeric

¾ teaspoon ground cinnamon

¾ teaspoon freshly ground black pepper

½ teaspoon ground white pepper

½ teaspoon ground coriander seed

½ teaspoon ground cayenne pepper

½ teaspoon ground allspice

½ teaspoon ground nutmeg

¼ teaspoon ground cloves

@Sid thank you so much! Came here from the print magazine which had a huge column of empty white space that could have been used for this. Surprised the recipe doesn’t have the oft-seen “tip.” Sure, there’s Google but that defeats the point. Thank you again for posting this!

@Sid thank you Sid. Recipe should have been in the Sunday Times article?He refers to it in the article. I was able to save your recipe with a screen shot of your comment!

Ras El hanout recipe: 

1 teaspoon salt

1 teaspoon ground cumin

1 teaspoon ground ginger

1 teaspoon ground turmeric

¾ teaspoon ground cinnamon

¾ teaspoon freshly ground black pepper

½ teaspoon ground white pepper

½ teaspoon ground coriander seed

½ teaspoon ground cayenne pepper

½ teaspoon ground allspice

½ teaspoon ground nutmeg

¼ teaspoon ground cloves
@Sid - thank you for this! This was a delicious spice mix. I halved the cayenne (to make it more kid friendly), but everything else was just right!
3/4 cup oil???  Used 1/4 cup and even that 4 tablespoons was too much.good combo of ingredients but I do t get the excessive oil in a “tray bake”

@Lynn
I agree. I used 3/4 of a cup and it was about 80% too much.
@Lynn concur wholeheartedly!!

Made my own ras el hanout mix and used boneless skinless chicken thighs. I was concerned that the thighs would be dry, so I put them in the bottom of the serving bowl and piled everything else on top. Squished the garlic over all and tossed it in. Delicious!

This was a winner. I went with half the oil and double the garlic and it came out perfectly. Great simple meal with lots of interesting flavors and textures. I'll definitely do it again, maybe add a few chili flakes for a touch of heat.

Soooo delicious. I made it for two, using only four thighs, but mostly everything else at full quantity with a good, crusty bread for sopping up. I agree that 1/4 cup or less oil is plenty, and I didn't bother with the extra garlic step, just put peeled cloves in with everything else and they were ready to smash and eat as part of the paste when everything else was done.

Banging flavour. The mash of tomato and garlic mixed through the other ingredients is just heavenly. Next time I’ll throw in a few extra pieces of tomato and garlic so I can have more mash on the side, just in case! I used less oil than called for.
Delicious blend of flavors and textures as one would expect from Ottolenghi. Ras al hanout was a star on the crispy chicken skin. Quick weeknight dinner.
And where would I get this spice?  As always his recipes feature hard to procure ingredients.
@Marti Mart you will find it at World Market, maybe Whole Foods, and of course Amazon
@Marti Mart easy to get on Amazon or Instacart
@Marti Mart I just got it from Whole Foods. It was the only store I could find it in.

Ras al hanout is a mix of cardamom, cumin, clove, cinnamon, nutmeg, mace, allspice, dry ginger, chili peppers, coriander seed, peppercorn, sweet and hot paprika, fenugreek, turmeric and sometimes rose petals. It has a kick, if you are sensitive to spice. Most markets are now carrying it and it's easy to order on-line.

NLS, thank you! That sounds like a delicious blend! 

While I don't have *all* of the spices listed, I can make up a blend that tastes great. Based on your description, I might use a combination of Garam Masala [my favorite spice blend] plus peppers, peppercorns, and paprika.
@NLS Rose petals! I'll look for those, sounds like an interesting addition. I appreciate your tip, and Sid's recipe, posted in another comment.

I am fortunate to be visiting my sister in Alameda, CA. That gave me relatively easy access to Oaktown Spice Shop on Grand Ave in Oakland to buy ras el hanout. They have sister shops in SFO and Albany.

Prepared as written. Had to work hard to find ras el hanout in my local market, but it was worth the search. I loved the new flavor profile and will continue using the moroccan spice mix in other dishes.  For my taste, the dish could use a bit more salt on the veg. I particularly liked the garlic and tomato mashup for sauce. Next time, I'll add a touch of green (green beans, asparagus, brussel sprouts or the like).
I was anxious that the potatoes would not cook, but all was well.  I do agree with the olive oil 3/4 cup, I would reduce the oil in future. Aside from that perfection to be repeated.

Sid's recipe is a great resource, but I find my ras el hanout at Fresh market and I prefer the mix made by NY Shuk.  I've  pretty much  filled my pantry with their spices and use them often  (e.g., sprinkle  some  ground Sumac  berries on Melissa Clarks Red Lentil Soup: OMG).  Ras el hanout literally means "head of the shop", appropriate since it includes all  the shop's best spices.  Interesting that my spell check keeps  converting the name to "gas el hangout", a version I'd prefer to avoid.

I’m a bit confused. The first step has the chicken marinate in the sheet pan. The second step says to add everything else, mix, and then put the chicken on top. Does that mean you mix chicken and everything together and then pick up the chicken pieces and put them back on top? Seems like it makes more sense to mix all but chicken together and then add chicken.

Yes, that's what it is saying to do.  I plan to let the chicken marinate, then toss it in a bowl with all the other ingredients ... and then put everything on the sheet pan.  Then I'll fish out the chicken pieces to put them on top, skin-side up.

@Ruth B

That’s what I plan on doing. NYT seems to have this thing about mixing things on a sheet pan instead of a bowl which IMO makes no sense-it just leads to a mess.
I was confused too.  Should be clear to mix all of it together in the end and then lay on sheet pan.

Followed the recipe with exception of using skinless, boneless thighs. Did not change anything else. Absolutely incredible. Yes, there was some excess oil, but that made cleaning the sheet pan very easy. Flavors were fantastic, looking forward to the leftovers tonight!
Used 4 tomatoes and 2 garlic head and few extra potatoes. Was a hit very very good. Used home made ras en hanout mix. ( Have made this mix before for Spoon broccoli ).  Yummy !!
I would add some extra potatoes, tomatoes and garlic. I also found that, at 375, this needed 10-15 more minutes in the oven than what was specified.
@Kate I threw on convection for the last ten minutes or so and was glad I did.

I think the author has it wrong about the meaning of "tray bake" in Britain. If you watch the Great British Bake Off, you know that a tray bake is a treat baked in a pan and cut into squares. Think brownies.

@Agatha Andrews I’m British - its true that the term can be used both ways, but much more commonly it means an all-in-one, oven tray meal (protein, veggies, starch). In America, this would be described as a sheet pan meal. Sheet pan = oven tray in the UK. I’ll admit it seems strange to argue about a domestic term as a non-native, non-British resident based on what you’ve seen on one television show about a different kind of cooking.

Great flavors. But some suggestions:
- Pre-bake the veggies/potatoes for 15-20 mins, and then add chicken and bake another 40 mins. 
- If you can find them, use Jimmy Nardello peppers. They have a wonderful fruity flavor. Not spicy at all. 
- Much less oil. 1/4 cup is more than enough, since chicken thighs are full of fat. After I finished the bake, I tipped one end of the tray and removed about 1.5 cups of fat/oil 
- Remove tomato skin before mashing. Only mash after getting rid of tray oil/fat

For the Ras el Hanout, Ottolenghi's blend, from a Guardian column: "My own home blend is as follows: a half-teaspoon of ground black pepper, a teaspoon each of ground cumin, ground coriander, ground ginger, ground allspice, ground nutmeg and ground cinnamon, a quarter-teaspoon of ground cloves and two teaspoons of ground cardamom; if you like, add a teaspoon of ground turmeric and a quarter-teaspoon of ground cayenne, too." No salt in his, which explains the salt on the chicken.
So where is all this "black pepper in it, cumin, cloves, allspice, coriander, nutmeg and cinnamon, and ras el hanout"???????
Anyone think you can substitute tofu for the chicken?  Thoughts?  Thx!
@CGnomeat. 100%

Try mixing scant olive oil with spice mix and then rub on both sides of chicken.
4 packages Trader Joe's chicken thighs (10 thighs)
2 sheet pans
Double the tomatoes and potatoes, keep garlic and chickpeas same.
Potatoes should be sliced in rounds about 1/4 inch thick to cook in time.
Peel garlic cloves and mix in with all.
Scant doubling of bell peppers (2 big bells).
If chickpeas and other beans mess with my digestion, is there a good substitution?  Might peanuts or cashews work?
@Chickpea substitution, you could increase the potatoes, perhaps try some sliced carrots. Moroccan is a tough cuisine to work with if you can’t have chickpeas or lentils.
I substituted parsnips and carrots for potatoes. Excellent.
Just curious. Is the baking pan lined with parchment paper?  Hard to tell from the picture.
@Cmglas There’s no parchment paper needed.
@Herta I use parchment paper. For this recipe, it makes cleanup easy!
All the fat of deep frying but none of the delight.

I always start to chuckle when I see Yotam Ottolenghi's name on a recipe, because he crafts recipes like the professional chef that he is. If his recipes don't have 15 ingredients or complicated techniques beyond the sophistication of most of us, he calls for exotic ingredients like romano peppers and ras el hanout --- uh, wozzat? I have been to some fancy produce sections, but what are romano peppers? And ras el hanout?

Wow, just wow! This dish was amazing and most definitely did not feed 4-8 people. Just three of us (one teenager included) finished off this entire dish. The flavours were so good together. My ras el hanout had rose petals in it and the depth of flavour the spice added to the chicken was great. Someone else mentioned adding more tomato and garlic, which I should have done, to get even more sauce. I used canned romas as I didn't have fresh and it was still awesome! Totally recommend this recipe!!
I used boneless skinless breasts because we had them: cut the veggies smaller for faster cooking and used an instant read meat thermometer. Fab

T’as el hanout substitute
1 part cumin
1/2 part coriander
1/2 part ginger powder
1/2 part paprika (or cayenne if you like it hotter)

This was so delicious, satisfying and easy to make. I used 12 mini bell peppers as the recommended substitute for romano peppers and otherwise followed the recipe exactly. Will definitely be making this again.

I added enough EVOO to coat the veggies in a mixing bowl before spreading out, then used just a touch to glaze the (thoroughly dried) chicken thighs. Added no more oil or water and there was still plenty of schmaltzy goodness/gravy in the sheet pan after an hour. I love EVOO, but 3/4 cup is really overkill here.

I was confused that the recipe called for 3/4 c. oil and then called it "sauce"! You can use 1/4 cup at most, in my opinion. I didn't have Ras El hanout on hand, but did have Garam Masala and it worked fine. I used Japanese sweet potatoes (which have white flesh) rather than the Yukons. It was delicious - definitely a dish I'll keep "in rotation."

Holy Camoly!  This was Good with a capitol (capital?) G!  Added an onion.  Forgot the cilantro and last spoonful of vinegar, but it didn't matter.  My sweetie and I scarfed it down, and both thought it fabulous!  Used a whole can of  canellini beans (good sub for garbanzos).  (I'm having trouble typing this because my cat is licking my fingers!).  Hubby says two cans of beans next time.  This was fabulous.  And easy.  And healthy. A+++++

I realize this is a major change but if anybody is wondering if this is still good without the chicken, I can confirm it is. I made this with just the chickpeas, potatoes, peppers, tomatoes, and garlic, and it was super delicious -- per the comments I didn't quite use 3/4 cup olive oil. But the tomatoes and garlic melt down into the most delicious sauce and the result is almost a stew-like medley.

I've made at least 300 NYT recipes and this is probably the best! Some complain about 3/4 cup olive oil, I probably used about 1/2 cup  but hey it's good for you!
I put a twist on the technique; after baking I put the tomatoes, the squeezed garlic, and the oil left on the pan into a container and them immersion blended it, leaving it slightly chunky, then spread that back out over chicken and veg. Didn't have Roma so used plum, didn't have Romano so used Cubanelle quartered lengthwise.

Used a few glugs of olive oil (nowhere near 3/4 cup). Didn’t have cilantro so added chopped preserved lemon and thinly sliced lemon on top for a punch of freshness. Blasted on the broiler for 1 minute for crispy sliced lemons and chickpeas. Grinded coriander seeds instead of black pepper. Perfect blend of spices, schmaltz, and lemon. DELISH! Will make a thousand times more.
@Michelle  I love the idea of the preserved lemon !  I will try that next time.

This is a great recipe! Easy to prepare and very flavorful. The last tablespoon of sherry vinegar really makes a difference. Others have commented about the oil being too much but I didn't think so. The juices that came out of the chicken and the peppers mixed well with the oil to produce an excellent sauce.

I wish I read the comments about excess oil ahead of time but otherwise, this dish has a lovely flavor profile and the tomato/garlic sauce is delicious. I would add more veggies next time to modify for vegetarians. Yum!
Have lived in the UK for 30 years. A traybake is anything baked in a tray. Savoury or sweet.

Much though I love Ottolenghi's recipes I found the chickpeas AND potatoes in this one was too much starch. I made it exactly as instructed including the 3/4 cup of oil. However next time I will substitute shelf stable gnocchi for the chickpeas and potatoes and reduce the oil and add in a bit of passatta. The ras al hanout spice was a brilliant addition to the chicken and is one I will use again.
And I agree: only need about 1/3 cup olive oil, split between veggies and chicken.

Ras al hanout is a worthy addition to the spice cabinet, for sure! But this recipe could accommodate all kinds of flavor profiles. I had shawarma seasoning on hand, which worked well. Or go with a combo of cumin, cinnamon, ground ginger, pepper, paprika and oregano … lots of pantry staple spice options would work. This recipe invites experimentation.

This is my favorite NYT recipe — I’ve made it at least 20 times, most after coming back from Morocco with bags of spice.  I agree with others on more tomatoes and garlic and I use fingerling potatoes cut lengthwise in half.  We don’t like chicken thighs (which is a huge problem with recipes these days since nearly all of them call for thighs).  So I buy those Volkswagen-sized bone in, skin on breasts and cut them in half with a scissor.  Not gorgeous, but it works.

I had never used ras el hanout.  Wow, what an explosion of flavor!  I halved this recipe for just the two of us, but used the full amount of chickpeas.  Used less than 1/4 cup of olive oil and it was plenty with the fat from the thighs.  Cooked the vegetables first for 15 minutes, then bumped the heat up to 400 and added the chicken for another 40.  Next time I will do the veggies for a good 20 minutes, the potatoes (large russets cut into 8 wedges, which was what I had) were a tad underdone.  Added some cauliflower florets a few minutes after the chicken to round out the meal. Easy meal and only one pan to wash.  I will be making this again and again!!

I agree with one of the commentators, potatoes should be pre-baked 15-20 minutes before the other ingredients, mine were WAY undercooked. I think I might even parboil next time.   Additionally, the peppers do not require 65 minutes of cooking time - mine were overcooked. Overall, a very nice recipe with great flavor profile. Yes, I know that all ovens differ.
Absolutely delicious! I was a little short on chicken thighs so added some thickly cut carrots. Used peeled garlic which worked perfectly. Would make a great vegan side or main without the chicken.
