I loved this. It is pretty forgiving too. I used 2 chicken breasts seasoned with a TB of the spices then buried under the veggies. TB spice added. I only had canned FR tomatoes- used the whole can. I used a half cup of roasted red peppers. Smashed 2 large garlic cloves and sprinkled on top. Oh, and canned white beans. Slice the potatoes very thin, or omit. I thought the recipe could stand alone. I cut my potatoes in 8ths or so and some still had a bite when the chicken was done. Thank you Sid!

Another winning recipe from Ottolenghi! Following the suggestions of others we used less oil (@1/3 cup total) and doubled the tomatoes. The chicken and the vegetables were both perfectly cooked in one hour. The combination of the roasted tomatoes and the roasted garlic was an outstanding feature of the dish. We will definitely make this again.
Used boneless thighs and 1/2 cup of oil. Otherwise prepared as directed—really delicious.

This recipe was at hit with my boys! I made my own ras el hanout spice blend (cardamon, cumin, cinnamon, ginger, chili powder, coriander, peppercorn, and turmeric) I kept the oil content as is, but after baking all ingredients per instructions, I removed the potatoes and the chicken, then blended the rest (chicken juices, oil, garlic, peppers, tomatoes, and chickpeas) to create a creamy sauce that I poured over chicken and potatoes.  Finger-licking good!  I served with rice.

Made this a bunch of times! It’s a slam dunk. I like to make two sheet pans worth so there are lots of leftovers and add onion. Repurposed the chicken/peppers/onions into tacos - corn tortilla with melted jack cheese, kimchi and avocado, sounds crazy but it was INCREDIBLE.
I can’t find Romano peppers anywhere!  I assume these are fresh but can’t find anything of the sort!
@Matt: I've never looked for Romano peppers, but I buy mini bell peppers all the time at CostCo, Trader Joe's, and other stores.
This is wonderful. Ras el hanout, what a discovery!!
Delightful and easy once the ras el hanout is made. Added leeks. Halved the oil. Deserves a better name than Tray Bake so have retitled it Ras el Hanout Electric Kiln Roast.

A restaurant chef friend of mine always used a couple spoons of ras el hanout in his bolognese ragu.  I’ve used it regularly since then and it adds a lovely, distinctive flavor. I live in the city so it’s easy to find, but it’s easily  available online.
Superb! Lots of comments saying to use less oil— I used 1/2 cup instead of 3/4 and that wasn’t too much, just enough. I also substituted garam masala for ras el hanout and that was perfect!

I used Ras el Hanout from Spice Leaf. I used two red bell peppers, and added some more potatoes. I also used only 1/2 cup of olive oil because I had a brain fart. I also forgot to put the cilantro on at the end. The result was unbelievably good. I'll certainly be making it again, and probably adding more raosted vegetables to the mix. Prep time for me was about thirty minutes and I'm an experienced cook (but not a virtuosos).
Has anyone tried this with chicken beast, with or without skin? I'm just not a fan of thighs. Philistine, I know!
Not a philistine. I dislike thigh meat too. Preferences are just that.

This is an absolute banger of a recipe. Probably my favorite in our family’s dinner rotation. My slight modifications- I use 1/4 cup oil on the veggies. The chicken is cooked right at the 65 minute mark but we like the potatoes really roasty so I put the chicken in a warming spot and return the veggies to the oven for another 10 minutes at 400. That extra heat on the end does well by the chickpeas and garlic as well. I can’t say enough about this recipe. It’s happiness on a baking sheet.

Excellent flavors and textures! I added a few small sliced carrots to the vegetables in the recipe. My teenage son, after cleaning his plate, said if he ever cooks for a girlfriend, he will make this dish because it tastes and looks great. I told him to make sure he trims the excess skin from the thighs, leaving it to cover just the tops. Make sure the thighs are dry then marinate in the spice mixture (I made my own per a reader’s recipe). After baking at 375, broil everything until the skins are sizzling but not burning. The thighs come out tender but not jiggly with an audibly crunchy skin. Amazing. Love this recipe!
Delicious! Vaut back oil to ½ cup and next time will reduce salt !

As suggested elsewhere I used less oil and basil instead of cilantro and it was DELICIOUS!!! Ordered the ras from
Amazon. So now I have it in my pantry to make this again!!

For the Ras El Hanout I used Sid's recipe (thank you!) and did everything like the recipe request, with the exception of the cooking method. My oven has an air fryer setting. I did air frying for the same time than baking. It turned out amaaaazing!! The chicken skin was delightful and the veggies packed with flavor. I've done it the regular way to, and I felt that the air frying made the recipe even better.
1/2 cup olive is plenty! Truly a crowd-pleaser. Elegant to serve, warming flavors and easy to prepare. Next time I will add a sweet element such as dried apricots or prunes and maybe some carrots.
Very Good Recipe! Less Oil. we maybe used a few Tablespoons.
Amazon has the ras el hanout.

We subbed in shrimp and used a dutch oven vs. sheet pan, left out the potatoes, increased the amount of Ras El Hanout. The latter is for us at least a terrific flavor discovery! Want something delicious and even simpler? Generously rub chicken with Ras El Honout, olive oil, salt and pepper. Cook it however you like. Eat. Amazing, dead simple, terrific.
Excellent recipe BUT there is way too much oil to my liking. Divide the oil from 3/4 of a cup to 1/2 or even 1/3 depending on the amount of veggies you add into your dish.

I made half this recipe with 2 Romas, 6 mini bells, the full can of chickpeas, and 4 boneless skinless chicken thighs (don't care for bones/skin).  I love the flavors of all the veggies, but I think I might almost have liked it better without the chicken!  Maybe next time I will try adding some cut up chicken breast in the last 10 minutes of cooking.  Or maybe just make it with a few more potatoes, and go vegetarian.

Amazing meal. I went with sweet potatoes instead of regular potatoes and it tasted amazing. (I just prefer sweet versus reg for most recipes) Also my local grocery store did not have ras el hanout so I made a substitute with the following ratios:

2 cumin
2 coriander
1 cinnamon
1  paprika

I recently prepared this easy dish for a group meal on a Sierra Club service trip and everyone went back for seconds, even the folks in the group who don't like 'spicy' dishes, raved about this one.  I used chicken breasts cut into chunks for my recipe and it turned out well.  I did find that the potatoes take quite a bit longer to cook through than the other veggies, so, I may parboil next time before adding.  Also, there was a lot of discussion about the amount of olive oil, so, I erred on the side of caution and used the 1/4 cup amount suggested in notes.  Next time, I will use the full amount in the recipe, as it will assist the veggies to cook better and make that delightful sauce. 
Thank you to the contributor who wrote out the spices needed for the ras el hanout mixture🙏🏻.

Wow! Everyone in my family, including one very picky child, devoured this. It was delicious! I recommend adding the spices under the skin as well as all around (underside of chicken thigh) and on top of the skin. I was worried it might be too salty with spices over and under the skin but it was perfect - savory, juicy thigh meat and crispy, packed-with-flavor skin.
Reduce oil to 1/4 cup as folks suggest.
WW folks - 18 pts as 7 servings, 21 pts if 6 servings - high but worth it.

Summary Recommendations:
Reduce oil to ⅓–½ cup.

Add onion or fennel for aromatics.

Sear chicken before roasting for crisper skin.

Scatter extra garlic cloves for fuller flavor.

Quarter tomatoes for even cooking.

Finish with lemon zest or juice.

Pair with bread or couscous.

Absolutely delicious! I used Moroccan Spice instead of ras el hanout because that's all I had. Added some powdered ginger and nutmeg as well. Also substituted the chickpeas for butter beans because that was what I had in the cupboard. Like everyone else, I used less oil. I got so many compliments!

Ottolenghi is the king. I have ALL of his books, and probably 80% of what I cook comes from his recipes. But admittedly, I don't usually think "Ottolenghi" when I need to prep a super easy meal after a long day. So this one really surprised me. It was wonderfully flavorful, and probably the easiest meal I cooked in years.

Omg this was delicious! I used far less oil than the recipe asked for and it turned out amazing. I used chicken breast instead of thighs, and shortened the cooking time for the chicken and let the potatoes and chickpeas cook for longer.

Absolutely scrumptious.
Any benefit to marinading the chicken with the spices overnight?

Is this not one of the best recipes the Times has ever run? Here’s Ottolenghi at his finest—and at his easiest by a long shot: Major payoff for little effort, with a result as painterly as it is dazzlingly delicious. To state the obvious: This is why cooking can be such a thrill. Six stars! 
⭐️ ⭐️ ⭐️ ⭐️ ⭐️ ⭐️
Pre-bake the veggies/potatoes for 15-20 mins, and then add chicken and bake another 40 mins.
Excellent flavor!! Definitely cut the oil…I used 1/4c which was perfect for the tossing of the vegetables, and then another good drizzle at the end.  3/4c would just be too much.

Surprisingly flavorful! I seared the chicken thighs skin side down in the pan for 8-10 minutes first to get the skin extra crispy. Served the final dish with some harissa and pomegranate syrup. Don’t skip the vinegar.

I reduced the amount of olive oil to 1/4 c & added 2 more tomatoes and 4 more mini peppers. They added juice so after I took the chicken out of the oven I put the veges & chickpeas back in for 10 minutes at 400.

I recently had problems getting the potatoes cooked. So I read the comments here, and I think I now have the perfect adjustments at least for my oven:
-7 fingerling potatoes, halved lengthwise and placed cut side up onto the tray
-include some 20 minutes at the beginning where I bake the vegetables only
-then put the chicken thighs on top of the vegetables and bake for 65 minutes

I’m a huge fan of tray bakes and have to say this recipe is outstanding! It has an amazing array of flavors and the chicken comes out so 
moist and tender! Not to mention the tray’s colorful presentation when serving to guests. Made exactly per the recipe instructions and served over basmati rice. Thank you!!

Prepared as written with a little less olive oil & boneless skinless chicken breasts—reduced cooking time by 15 minutes.  It was amazing! My daughter suggested sweet potatoes instead of Yukons next time.

Added half a purple onion, a few white mushrooms and used cherry tomatoes in place of Roma. Also exchanged 2 tablespoons of blackberry-ginger infused dark balsamic vinegar for 1/4 cup of the oil (only used 1/2 cup of oil) and got a very satisfying flavor result.
WOW! Absolutely mind-blowing good! The house smells fantastic too! Family raved about the combination. Will definitely prepare for guests.
What is “ras el hanout” and how do we find it or make it?
@Susan, I ordered on Amazon
Would this work with a cut up fryer (legs and breasts in addition to thighs)?
I think it might ... but you would want to start the breasts later or they will seriously overcook.

For those asking about chicken breasts, the short answer is, I wouldn’t, not for this recipe. (Nyt has a whole section on excellent chicken breast recipes). But if you insist, use them at your own peril as follows: marinate with spices and oil. Proceed with tray bake, pull chicken and keep warm. Proceed, add and toss. Be aware you’ll be missing the unctuousness and richness of the chicken juices. Add a spoon or two of good chicken base/broth to tray.

Not my favorite Yotam recipe (and he is my favorite chef). Maybe my oven was off but it needed more time as a few others stated. I also added more tomatoes and garlic and cut the oil to 1/4 C. Made the Ras El hanout (thanks so much for the recipe Sid!!). My kid and I were trying to figure out what didn't thrill us (like the majority of Yotam's recipes). It didn't have the layers of flavor we are used to and I think this is the first recipe (out of dozens) I made that had chicken.

I did not use as much oil. Just enough to coat the veggies and the bottom of the pan. I used canned whole tomatoes instead as I did not have fresh. I thought the dish was wonderful. I cooked it in my Ninja and it took about 45 minutes.

Mind the notes about too much oil.  I used relatively large Yukon Gold potatoes and they could've used a head start relative to the peppers, tomatoes and chicken at the 375 temperature.  Also, I think the user note about adding some chili/red pepper flakes would've been a nice addition.

Agreed on all counts. I had a russet potato on hand so I used that, and i did steam the slices in a separate pan on the stove for a bit just to help them get a head start. I also felt it could benefit from a little freshness, I'd like to add a squeeze of lemon next time.

Don't believe the "prep time" to be 10 mins. More like 1/2 hour +.
Interesting that no one mentions how difficult it might be to find Romano peppers. I finally found some Anaheim peppers to use as a substitute...not as pretty, but a good substitute I suppose.

This was great! Like most people, I cut way back on the oil — between the chicken fat and the tomato juices, two tablespoons of oil was enough to make a savory sauce. Served with salad the first night and couscous as leftovers. Will definitely make again!

The Ras El hanout was a winner.  I found a recipe online and made it myself.  Lightly toasted some of the spices first. Def going to find other ways to use. It alone makes this recipe worth trying.  But somehow the rest of the dish didn't come together for me like others.  My sheet pan was piled way too high so like some others I had to cook veggies for an extra 20 mins.  Still tasty, just had higher expectations given other's comments.

I have three little boys who don’t like thighs, but love wings.  So I cut up  3.5 pounds of chicken wings (omitting the wing tips) and otherwise followed the recipe.  If I make it again, I will add another can of chickpeas.

Made this twice and each time was fantastic. Family loved it. My mods:

For veggies
-Eggplant, sliced (8-10 oz) (skin crisps nicely and develops smoky flavor)
-8 mini sweet peppers, halved
-12 cherry tomatoes, halved
-2 yukon potatoes, sliced
-1 can chickpeas, drained
-12 smashed garlic cloves (no need to mash afterwards)

Had about 1 tablespoon of ras el hanout left over from chicken rub, which was sprinkled over veggies on pan. Drizzled remaining 1 tablespoon sherry vinegar over chicken and veggies on pan as well. After taking out of oven, drizzle juice of half a lemon before serving.
This is an easy recipe, but I'll never make it again. Too much oil and the flavors weren't anything special. Other sheet pan chicken recipes have the same easy prep and a better flavor.

@André Gotta say, some of us (perhaps older &/or more experienced) cooks - def no shade intended! actually look for these types of recipes vs the simpler, less complicated ones. IMHO they're tastier, more nuanced flavors. IF I've got the time... To each their own!

Wonderful recipe. I added 8 small shallots cut in half lengthwise, and used about 1/3 of the recommended quantity of olive oil (1/4 cup made the dish plenty rich). I used the small peppers, which got very caramelized and delicious, and a head of pink garlic (ail rose), which is available in French markets in the Spring as I happened to be in France when trying this recipe. My French family loved the dish. The flavors were amazing, and the chicken was crisp on the outside, yet very tender and juicy inside. I will definitely make this again!

Delish recipe but if you’re on a budget it is not budget friendly! I spend several hours hunting ingredients down between stores, and still had to make substitutions. All in all spent about $60 which was crazy since I have a lot of the regular ingredients at home already like the garlic, tomatoes, and potatoes

Super recipe!  Agree with the comments that much less oil is needed.  Made a sauce at the end by pureeing the roasted garlic, tomatoes, 1/3 of the peppers, olive oil, splash of the sherry vinegars, and salt and pepper.

While I had to look up the spice blend and make my own, this was a great recipe.  Second time I made it, which is why I'm now just writing a review is because I really wasn't in the mood for chicken, so I subbed pork tenderloin and to be honest, preferred it over the chicken.  I also let the rub marinate for 30 minutes rather than the recommended 10 minutes.
Instead of thighs I made little chicken meatballs seasoned with a version of the ras el hanout spice mix. Turned out great!

I didn't have peppers so I doubled the tomatoes and put orange slices on the tray for color and sweetness. I was not disappointed and will do this again even if I have peppers!  My husband and I loved this recipe.

This was loved and worshiped by all, though I did adapt to my situation. I used zataar instead of ras el hanout because I had it to hand and liked the idea. I added v thin slices of lemon under the chicken halfway through, then nestled in 12 or so spears of asparagus cut to ~1.5 inch for more veg. Less chicken more chickpeas. If your family/guests aren't too fussy, consider cooking the garlic as cloves (skin on) rather than a whole head for easier serving and they can decide their own fate.

- Use half of everything for two servings
- Try without cayenne pepper next time for less heat
- Very delicious. 
- Worth the work.
- Ras El hanout recipe:   1 teaspoon salt  1 teaspoon ground cumin  1 teaspoon ground ginger  1 teaspoon ground turmeric  ¾ teaspoon ground cinnamon  ¾ teaspoon freshly ground black pepper  ½ teaspoon ground white pepper  ½ teaspoon ground coriander seed  ½ teaspoon ground cayenne pepper  ½ teaspoon ground allspice  ½ teaspoon ground nutmeg  ¼ teaspoon ground cloves
I will use a half cup of olive oil next time.  Maybe some chickpeas will have a chance of getting crispy then.  It’s a delicious dish!
I saw the recipe and thought it would be nice. It’s so flipping good. Too much oil so I cut that down. Recipe not as concise as I’d like but a must try.

Really different flavor profile that was delicious but didn’t hit you over the head with a club! Not too tomato-ey, and the peppers didn’t overpower the dish. Made as written except only used 1/4 C EVOO. I used the ras el hanout substitute that Google suggested: 2 tsp ground cumin, 2 tsp ground coriander, 1 tsp paprika, 1/4 tsp ground cinnamon. Next time? Oven at 400 degrees. Put the chicken on the edges of the sheet pan. Double the tomatoes and garlic. Add 2 C frozen green beans.

This was delicious. I couldn’t find the type of peppers it called for, so I didn’t use peppers and it was still so good. I also did not use as much oil as the recipe calls for. I just drizzled to make sure everything was coated. I would cut my potatoes smaller next time as they didn’t cook at the same speed as the rest so I had to throw them back into the oven another 10 mins after taking the chicken off the tray. Will def. Make again!

I tried making this with boneless, skinless chicken breasts and used way less oil. It didn't work. The chicken was dry even though I used a poultry thermometer and took it out as soon as it was cooked. Some recipes work if you want to lighten them up but this one should be followed as written.

I took reviewers' suggestions and halved the olive oil and at the end of the cooking time, there was no "sauce"...it was scorched away and rather dry. I think if I'd used the 3/4 cup, it would've been delicious! Otherwise the flavors were good.
I've turned this into an excellent vegan dish by eliminating the chicken. the chickpeas provide protein.

This meal is very oily.  As you're instructed to leave the skin on the chicken, there's no reason to drown the vegetables in an additional 3/4 cup of olive oil. May want to consider reducing the olive oil to 1/4 cup (or less.). If I make this again, I would remove the top skin from maybe half of the chicken thighs.  I don't care to eat the skin anyway, but BF does.
Also - consider salting/seasoning the vegetables too, as they're tossed in the olive oil.  This recipe came out a bit bland.
This is a beautiful set-and-mostly-forget recipe. Thank you to Sid for the spice blend recipe.
Reduced the oil. Only needed 40 min in the oven. Removed the skin on half the thighs (might try skinless boneless next time). Absolutely amazing dish! This is going into the rotation.
Great flavors but way too much oil. Would decrease significantly if I make again.
Delicious and easy meal.  I used chicken breasts, and they worked fine.

Even better as leftovers; make this a day in advance (add salt to chix) & let it settle into its flavors before serving. You can avoid the otherwise smart advice re halving the oil, by doubling the potatoes (so delish; why limit to just 3?) & letting them soak up all that yummy sauce. You won't need to up the ras el hanout; 2TBS is more than enough to flavor extra taters. Throw some sliced onion (I used yellow) on the tray, too... that roasted sweetness is a treat to sop up with good bread.

Love this recipe. Easy, tasty, and I can walk away and do other quick chores while it cooks. I have to say though... 3/4 cup EVOO for one recipe in THIS economy? ;D Also just feels unnecessary... I usually just generously glug over everything on the sheet pan and call it a day. But hey, you do you.

Used garam masala instead of ras el hanout because its what i had on hand. Barely used 1.4 cup of olive oil based on comments and still found it way too much. All of my veggies and chicken are just swimming in oil in my meal prep containers now. Would probably cut back on the salt as well
Ras al hanout recipe should be added as a note to the recipe so its easily accessible.
Please and thank you
@What is Ras El Hanout? North african spice mix. See https://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ras_el_hanout. Yum. Just yum.

@What is Ras El Hanout? Ras El Hanout is a Moroccan spice been with about a dozen spices all mixed together, including cinnamon, cumin and much more. It’s a nice mix that adds flavor but isn’t too spicy hot - can be used in dishes like this one or even in baking. You can buy it at specialty stores or online and you can also make your own - recipes available on the internet. Check it out!

This is very tasty and flavorful and super easy..
  BUT 3/4  cup OF OLIVE OIL?? ABSOLUTELY NO NEED
I put maybe 1/4 cup and it was plenty!!
thoughts on using boneless breasts instead?
Typically complicated.  The spice mixture counts in 14 or so spice ingredients. I have three of his cook books, gifts, that are seldom used. To much bother.
While tray bakes may be tasty, I think it’s overstating to call them recipes.
Has anyone experimented with a vegetarian version of this? How was it? Please share results. Thanks!
The Spice House has a great ras el hanout blend ( also others, like baharat….)

I agree, 3/4 C of oil is off the charts! I wondered why it seemed everything was swimming in oil...until I read the recipe.

Considering how much fat is on skin-on chicken thighs, the amount of vegs, and that the vegs are placed under the chicken, you should only need a couple tablespoons to toss with the vegs to give them a thin coating.
One can get Ras El hanout at a local Mediterranean store if you live in a large enough city. Amazon also sells it online.

LOVE this. Have already made this twice since last week. Mixed Aleppo pepper into the spices, and added a sliced zucchini to the veg. A few minutes before finishing, I twirled two handfuls of baby spinach into the sauce. Another brilliant recipe, YO!

This was amazing!  I ended up making my own mix of garam masala, turmeric and paprika.  I also used only 1/4 cup oil and even that could be reduced to maybe 2 TB as the chicken also contributed fat to the dish.   Very delicious, I'm looking forward to tomorrow's leftovers
Tossed all the ingredients (except chicken) with a reduced amount of oil before spreading on the sheet pan. Still in the oven.
Just wondering for us who don’t eat meat, might there be a good fish substitution?
I'd think any thick fish, also tofu.
Try half the oil, double the garlic, and peel it first. Maybe more tomatoes. Could use carrots & parsnips in place of potatoes.

This was outstanding! I did not have ras el hanout, but I do have mansaf spice (from Jordan), which is almost the same thing. Taking a cue from the "Oven-roasted shawarma" recipe on this site, I marinated the thighs in (1/3 cup) olive oil, salt, pepper, and two tbsp of mansaf spice for about two or three hours. I followed the rest as is, and it turned out beautifully. (I also made a quick tzatziki of plain Greek yogurt, chopped Persian cucumbers, minced garlic, salt, and lemon juice.)

Cooked it and loved it - i would say the second cook time can be closer to 15 minutes depending on your oven.

Big shout out to Sid for saving the day with the Ras El hanout mix.
Delicious and very easy. Followed the recipe and got excellent results and positive feedback from the family.

I made a half recipe of this as written, which was excellent, and then when I had family coming over I doubled it, except for the beans, and added broccoli and cauliflower florets.  What wonderful flavors!  I lost track of whether I doubled the oil or not, but I don't like to waste olive oil in this climate/economy, so I always add oil by sight and more only if needed.  So interesting that when we add these warm spices, the younger children and the teenagers all love it.

Huh. Made as written but with less oil. Not a winner for us. If making again, I would use skinless chicken, peel the garlic before roasting, toss everything in the seasoning together, and serve over orzo or rice or something that would absorb the flavors better than the potatoes. And, much less oil.

This is outstanding! It all melds together into deliciousness. The prep took me longer than 10 minutes, but it was still easy and then it's mostly hands off while it cooks. Thanks to Sid's very helpful comment, I made the ras el hanout. I used mini peppers since I couldn't find the romano peppers. We will definitely be making this again!

good recipe.  I rubbed the chicken w/olive oil.  The chicken was crisp but needed more Ras El hanout. I substituted chickpeas for hominy.  I added chopped onion sprinkled around the chicken when it baked.  It could be improved by mixing in shallots halved, they do great on sheet pans.  I didnt have sherry vinegar so I used white balsamic vinegar and sherry wine.   Substituted cilantro for dried parsley because that's what I had on hand.  Family enjoyed.
Great recipe made greater with handful of arugula for serving. Received the highest level of compliment from the hubs: “I would order this at a restaurant.” Success!

This was so good.  I get so bored with everyday dinner cooking and needed to cook something different. I was afraid that the spices would not be a good choice for my husband, but he loved it and said it was a keeper and he would love to have it again. Luckily I made enough of the Ras El hanout when I couldn't find it so I can make it again soon.  YUM!

This is a very flexible recipe. I didn't have chickpeas but lots of zucchini and potatoes. No problem, the melding of flavors still worked brilliantly. A quick time under the broiler at the end was very helpful. Was delicious with a Syrah from Morocco.

made this, it has the potential to be great if i tweak a few things. I used less olive oil, which was actually a mistake because the chickpeas and veg burnt (setting off my fire alarm). But the flavors of the chicken, the veg and the spices were really good together. I served it with farro and steamed asparagus. It was easy to make, pretty quick to pull together. I'll definitely make it again.

Too much liquid in the pan with the tomatoes the chicken and all the oil. Reduce the oil to a quarter cup. 
Pretty good, but potatoes are not roasted
Made exactly as recipe specified, except reduced oil to a scant half cup. Absolutely delicious! Found the ras el hanout at Whole Foods (Rumi brand). Love Yotam Ottolenghi!
Omg, this was idiot proof and very delicious! As others have said, you only need 1/4 cup of olive oil.

Looks like Romano Peppers are Jimmy Nardellos, sweet and summery, not available now, even here in California. Used red and orange bell peppers. Added carrots and an onion cut in eighths. Separated the garlic, left the papers on to mess with when we eat. Yum!

Solid 4 stars...Tracey has banished cinnamon from all future recipes....I thought it was okay but unsure how to improve.

Made our own Ras-al-Hanout from the recipe in the comments.   Next time leave out the cinnamon!

OFF THE CHAIN delicious, regardless of how quick it is to put together! I only made a couple basic changes as many people did, such as doubling of items but not chicken. No potatoes. Add some spice mix to the veggies as well why not? used butter beans and navy beans,put them in halfway because they are soft.  Made spice mix a la Sid, but also added some cardamom (not much!), paprika like other recipes state. I must be good at balancing the flavors, because absolutely fabulous combo of spices!

I prepared per the recipe. Okay — almost … forgot to add the chickpeas until about 10 minutes into the first roasting period. (Oops). Added them and tossed everything as best I could. Still a delicious result with lots of leftovers.
Will definitely make again.  Used boneless skinless chicken thighs and they were so tender.  Will add Moroccan black olives next time and may try a veggie version as well.

Marked as easy, but there's a lot of prep involved.  Cooking just for myself, so only did two chicken thighs, but I probably didn't cut down the potatoes, peppers, and chick peas enough.  Ended up with far too many veggies, and even after 70 minutes in the oven the potatoes were still a bit crunchy. Not going to bother to make again.

This was sooo amazing! Everyone loved it. It was a bit heavy on the olive oil but I didn't really mind, and we used 2 whole garlic bulbs. I will absolutely make this again. I used store-bought ras el hanout.

Wowzer!
Just delicious ~ not only quick, hands off, & very little prep, it seems open to substitutions & additions. Boneless, skinless? Just take the chicken out & let the potatoes keep roasting. No fresh peppers? Try roasted red peppers (bonus flavors from the brine.). Allium options abound.
Have at it and be prepared to enjoy
Made a vegetarian version by replacing the chicken with tofu blocks. Turned out great.

You guys! I did this with sweet potatoes. I used less oil— 1/3 cup. I also used skinless boneless thighs so I dribbled some duck fat over the pan. It is sooooo delicious. The sweet potatoes really meld so well with the ras el hanout. Give it a try.
Question: After the chicken marinates, do you toss it with the other ingredients? Or do you toss the veggies etc then place the chicken on top so that the spice mix stays on the chicken skin? Thanks!
The instructions are frequently unclear and 3/4 cup of oil went over the edge of a baking tray and onto the bottom of my oven. Messy, fussy and very bad for a week night.
Tastes way more complex than it is. Ridiculously easy for a weeknight.

Made this today. I used Sid's seasoning recipe from one of the comments but went a little light on the cayenne as we are not huge on heavy heat. The crunchy seasoned skin from the chicken adds such flavor and texture. I added a sweet potato cut in chunks,  I used about 3/8 cup olive oil - about half of what it called for. This recipe will become a standard for me. Would be wonderful for a casual supper with friends and I think would go equally well with wine or beer. Totally enjoyed!

This was good. I halved the vinegar and used less oil. I put a glug of oil, ras el hanout, and a little salt and pepper in a gallon bag and let the chicken marinate for about an hour. I also used a handful of peeled garlic cloves rather than whole heads of garlic. Next time I'd increase the amount of tomatoes by one or even two and maybe go with just diced bell pepper.

When using oven for other meals I bake several heads of garlic with cut off top, drizzle 1 Tbs EVOO, sprinkle salt.  For future use I freeze in vacuum sealed bags.  Recommended in several food sites.  

I made this like you, put whole, peeled garlic clove in the pan.  We’re Lebanese, love garlic.

I didn’t have any tomatoes, so I used a 14 oz can of whole tomatoes drained and quartered - worked fine. I also blasted the tomatoes, garlic and drippings with the immersion blender at the end and served sauce on the side. 

Agree this needs a bit longer in oven. I also put the whole tray under the broiler for a few minutes at the end to crisp up the skin and potatoes. And definitely more potatoes!
This could be my favourite NYT recipe of all time. I just love the flavours. Agreed, 1/4 cup of oil is enough.

Used boneless skinless thighs because I'm lazy. Didn't adjust the cooking time and they still tuned out very juicy. You could easily get away with far less oil. After cooking, I put the tomatoes and squeezed the garlic into a separate bowl and mashed it all up (a potato masher worked way better than a fork for me since there were so many tomatoes), and then took the juices from the pan and incorporated that into the mash. Spooned the mash over each serving and voila!

used 1/4 cup of oil after reading the comments- added carrots to the veggie mix and it was so delicious! The roasted garlic mixed in at the end was such a great taste- I will use that method more often on other recipes!  This is a keeper.

I was going to say i followed the recipe, but i guess I mostly did. Absolutely reduced the oil by more than half, and next time I'll use less again. 
Used verjuice, lemon juice and lemon zest in place of the vinegar; used a commercial Moroccan spice mix in place of the recommended blend because i live in Brisbane and our supermarkets are kinda vanilla, added carrots and pumpkin. Next time I'll roast the potatoes, carrots and pumpkin for 15-20 minutes before adding.
Used culantro as no coriander
Romano peppers?

I’m gonna dream about this recipe tonight. My future dinner party guests will be blessed with this delicacy every time they come over. I prepared ahead and put everything on the baking tray, wrapped it, and put it in the fridge until an hour before I had guests arrive, leaving plenty of time to lounge around in bed. I’m greedily anticipating midnight when I’ll sneak a cold chicken thigh from the fridge.
Without Ras El Hanout if you JUST use equal parts salt pepper turmeric cumin and a touch of nutmeg it’s still delicious.

Delicious! I used the New York Shuk Ras El Hanout and used cherry tomatoes and regular size bell peppers. I used chick peas from Rancho Gordo that I had made separately.  I added some garlic scapes from my garden. The rest was the same.  Really great recipe!

Delicious! Double the potatoes at least. First time we made it the chickpeas didn’t crisp up so the second time we roasted the potatoes and chickpeas separately and combined everything before serving. Perfect!
Delicious. Made my Ras El hanout from the recipe below.
I’m a lousy cook but I made this tonight and it was a hit! Easy to put together and delicious. Thank you
I hate thigh meat. I wish recipes like this would give tips on adapting for chicken breasts or a mix of pieces. If it works only with thighs, I’ll pass.
my god the blend of spices was so good in this. the chicken came out so juicy and the tomatoes and chickpeas just soaked up the rest of the flavor that the chicken cooked in. 100% will cook again

The raz el hanout disappeared, so I used berbere (not the same, I know). It was tasty. 
I reduced the oil to 1/4 cup but it was still too much. It needed an extra 25 minutes to get the chicken and potatoes fully cooked. Maybe my oven is faulty. I added a bunch of fresh corn since I had it. Came out well. Will cook it again.

I made this was 2 tbsp of oil instead of 3/4 cup and it was fantastic, the sherry vinegar really brightens the flavor and cuts throw any heaviness. Sometimes the potatoes come out a little al dente but if I cook the veg for 15-20 minutes first and up the heat to 375 it's perfect

Really delicious! I salted the potatoes, tomatoes mixture a bit after adding the oil and vinegar (I'm sure this was omitted accidentally to not season these ingredients at all). Make a bit more of the spice mixture to coat both sides of the chicken, and double the chickpeas. will make again!

I loved this!  Building on other feedback, I reduced the olive oil and peeled the garlic ahead of time (rather than after the cooking).  And, I used two garlic bulbs and additional tomatoes for additional mash - which was my favorite part.
Made the recipe as is.  Soooo delicious

This was stupendously good. Didn’t use Ras Al Hanout as I don’t love it and I’m sure I did myself a disservice there , but even so it was utterly delicious . Don’t forget the vinegar at the end - really pulls it all together .

Made as written. Dish was a bore which is surprising given the 5 stars. There are plenty of similar recipes that warrant their rating, but this one was lacking any kind of glaze, dipping sauce or other condiment to add some interesting enhancement to what is essentially a super healthy tray bake of veggies and chicken in their own juices.

Definitely too much oil. I made it as written, except I used mini bell peppers as suggested as an alternative. I think it would taste just as good with half of the oil. Probably better. 

If your potatoes aren’t cooked through, you may have sliced them too thick. 

That spice is my new favorite. I ordered it on Amazon. Came next day. 

The chicken was perfect. I rubbed the rub under the skin as well as on it. 

The veggies/etc. were soggy. Tasty, but I will make it with less oil next time.

I suspect the many people who had issues with the amount of oil did so because chicken thighs are quite fatty. I got a delicious result by using boneless, skinless thighs and removing the visible fat. I used the directed amount of olive oil, and increased the amount of ras el hanout and vinegar. I baked it for 50 minutes and removed the chicken to a plate. I continued to cook the vegetables for another 15 minutes and they were perfect. Will definitely make this again.
It’s really tasty I have had it several times !!
It is an amazing dish. So rich on spices and definitely, not an ordinary chicken recipe. It is a festival for your taste buds. It becomes so easier after first one, thank you.
I used leftover potatoes from this recipe to make breakfast: smashed and fried them, topped with eggs. Yum!

I understand why this needs more oil, especially if you’re eating it with rice or naan. I only used enough oil to coat the chicken and vegetables. After cooking, I wanted to drizzle the chicken and rice with the oil and drippings from the pan but there just wasn’t enough to add to the rice lol

Cut the oil to 1/2c per recommendations, and I'm happy I did. I used the seasoning recipe offered by Sid, and it turned out nicely. I was skeptical of how this would be received by my family with 4 younger kids, but they all enjoyed it!
Can you use chicken breasts? Half my family won't eat thighs. I have chicken breast skin on on bone.
Really good  used the recipe for Ras el hanout that you can find in the comments. Also made a vegetarian version with slices of celariac in stead of chicken.
@Katarina, I love celeriac! Thank you for the idea!

Delicious and easy! Added half sweet potatoes and used 1/2 c fire roasted tomatoes in place of the fresh ones. The potatoes needed and extra 20 min after the chicken was cooked. Was a hit with the teens as well as adults. Will make again!
Easy, rich and spicy. I reduced the number of Romano peppers and increased the red pepper, not knowing how spicy the end result would be and it turned out great.

Made for a birthday dinner and it was a big hit !
I am not a big chicken eater but this dish was absolutely delicious - will definitely make again

Star dish—officially going into rotation. I love an easy recipe that still delivers big flavor. I followed the oil measurement exactly as written and it was perfect, IMO. The pan juices were unreal—I poured every last drop into the garlic-tomato mash and over the dish itself. Absolute perfection.

I didn't have the peppers, so I omitted them. I used skinless boneless chicken thighs because that is what I had on hand. I also added soaked and chopped sun-dried tomatoes because we did not have fresh tomatoes, either.
The Ras al Hanout (sp?) is such an incredible flavor profile. I just really wanted to use the spice I had ordered so I found this recipe and used what I had. I think it is way too much oil, and I had a little bit of an issue with the sun-dried tomatoes burning because they were up above some of the ingredients in the oven. My sheet pan also warped slightly so the oil pulled in some areas.
I would add far less oil and cook for a little bit less time. The potatoes were really mushy. The thighs despite being boneless and skinless were perfect, tender and probably could've cooked a little less along with the potatoes. 
Definitely try this recipe the flavors are really wonderful.
Delicious and easy! On rotation

Strong dish. Agree that the amount of olive oil is a bit much. Also potatoes took a lot longer to cook for me than the other vegetables. I may not have cut them small enough. In any case, was excellent when I used about 1/2 cup of olive oil and ditched the potatoes
Prepared as directed - but the potatoes were bland. Was I supposed to mix Ras El on the chickpea / potatoes?
Outstanding!  I had a lot of shallots so I added them. I used boneless chicken thighs. Keeper

I add some Kinder’s Roasted Garlic powder to the tray before baking to bring this to the next level for my family. It’s a go-to for those nights when I am not wanting to think too much about what to make and when I don’t want to do a bunch of dishes after dinner.

This blew my mind. I had never heard of Ras al Hanout and now it is a staple in my cabinet. We prefer the New York Shuk brand, but you can always make your own. This dish can be a bit oily, but reducing it really changed the experience - making it drier (obviously) and 'different' than the original recipe. I tend to forget the sherry vinegar but it doesn't matter!  :)
Any suggestion for a chickpea sub? We have an allergy to legumes. Thanks!
@Lazy Cook Toronto same here! Can’t eat them. Was thinking white beans, but they might not crisp up the same

I made it exactly with two exceptions. After reading comments, I only used a healthy glug of olive oil — definitely not 3/4 cup! With a drizzle of olive oil and the fat the rendered from the chicken, it was plenty. Also, I added pickled onions at the end to continue the vinegar vibe. I’d make it again, but with just smoked paprika and cumin because I wasn’t a big fan of the spice mix.
@Agatha Andrews  this isn't true at all. I'm British and we definitely call this recipe a tray bake. I've rarely heard the term used for desserts.

This is a winner.  I don't agree with others about there being too much oil; if you're using good olive oil, why not?  Use a good bread to go along with the meal, and just pour the juice left in the pan into the tomato and garlic deliciousness.  I had a lot of chicken thighs so made essentially a double recipe.  It can use more veg: carrots, asparagus, long beans, whatever.  Just make sure you add them at the right time so they don't over or under cook.

I love sheet pan dishes!
What is ras el hanout, and could someone provide a substitute ingredient? Thanks
chickpea allergy here! any substitutions?
I used cannellini beans, which I had on hand.  Used a whole can for two of us.

Thank you NLS for your recipe for Ras Al Hanout!  Could you also post quantities please?  Sid's is also good and both of them remind me of our home made Garam Masala my MIL taught me to make - each household makes their own and is slightly different.  I know you can buy Ras Al Hanout from Amazon but the one I ordered seemed pretty bland/meh.  I can't wait to try these on this wonderful sheet pan/tray bake recipe.  I think it would be fabulous with warm pita bread or naan to sop up the gravy.
Anyone try with chicken breasts?
Has anyone tried this with chicken breasts?
Assume I can try this recipe with chicken breasts.  Thighs are not our thing.  By the way, I watched Ottolenghi’s Master Class episodes on a recent cross country flight.  Simply wonderful!
can I  used skinless boneless chicken breasts instead? I husband is not a fan of chicken thighs.
Suggestions for replacement for Cilantro?
I too made my own spice. I also doubled all the veg but I should have used more chickpeas; my wife ate them all. Chicken was absolutely delicious.
I made this with lamb chops. Seasoned about an hour before searing in a skillet, then followed recipe as written, except I used black beans. Oh my! A keeper!!
I made this dish tonight; didn’t have any potatoes and wondered if I should make rice but didn’t.  Turns out it is delicious and filling without the starches.  Will be making this again.
This recipe is fire. 🔥
substitute for the roma tomatoes?

Made my own gas el hanout...recipe from food. net.  Might not put as much cayenne in it next time.  Also cooked it with the micro wave and heat to cut the cooking time to half.  Very easy and tasty.   Highly recommend it.  Go sheet pan cooking!
@GGD: The recipe on Washington Post doesn't include cayenne. Perhaps you can skip the cayenne altogether.
This was just terrific! Needed less oil (1/3c) and some ground red pepper for a little punch, but it was easy and awesome served over jasmine rice. Will definitely make it again!
Made this recipe for the first time last night for a group of friends.  We ALL thought it was divine!  So easy and so yummy!  Luckily I tripled the recipe so we have some leftovers for today.
Delish! Maybe stir in ¼ to ½ cup water to create more sauce
Perfect balance of flavors! A new favorite.

Brilliant! But I propose the following modifications
1/4 - 1/3 cup of oil
Season veges
Reduce chicken cooking time

Doubled veg, chicken, and seasoning.  Kept olive oil same as single recipe.  35 minutes at 375F then had to boost to 400F for 30 minutes  to finish cooking and really crisp the thigh skins.  Excellent.
This was very easy and super tasty.  Served to a 5 person dinner party.  Everyone raved about it!

I couldn't find or make El Ras Hanout so I kept it simple with garlic and onion powder so as not to totally throw things off with other spices. I made it in a roasting pan and added oil by eye, Putting ALL that on a flat pan with ALL that oil is crazy and just asking for a spill in the oven. I loved it though! Definitely adding it to my repertoire, with just a few tweaks in mind for next time.
This is FANTASTIC! My entire family had seconds and my son asked that it go into the “regular” plan. I am so happy we had this!

Drawn to this recipe because I had plenty of bone in chicken thighs in the freezer and a partly used (but still potent) bag of ras el hanout in the pantry. Followed recipe as written. Would add more potatoes next time. Just one wrinkle - the thighs were red bird, smallish, so 3 1/4# added up to 15, too many for one sheet pan. So I split everything and used 2 sheet pans. Worked out fine - given small size the thighs only took 45 minutes.
@Marti Mart: You can purchase Ras El Hanout on the web at Spice + Leaf or on Amazon.

This is delicious and so easy and fast to make.  I tweaked a few things.  I used six (a package, 1.25 pounds) boneless thighs.  I switched to mash potatoes (same Yukon Gold as the recipe) as a side dish.  I used garlic cloves, cut in medium size pieces.  The Ras el Handout spice stole the show!  I was able to find it at our local Savory Spice store.  I learned that its use dates back to the Middle Ages in Morocco.  I am sure we'll be using it again.  Yum-yum.

Thanks. Sid, for the Ras el hanout recipe - used that, minus a couple of spices I didn't have on hand, and found it great! Cayenne seemed crucial, added a nice touch of heat but not overwhelming.  

Like others, I reduced the oil to 1/3 cup - wouldn't use less but that seemed a good amount.

Made this last week minus the peppers and the family loved it.  Next time I will add more potatoes and garlic and a little less oil.  The skin was super crispy and it was requested to become a member of the rotation
Would this be good without the chicken? Am pescatarian.

I think so! I love the veggie roasted mix so much I’m making a batch of just that tonight. But using a bit of the spice mix on that, of course. Unbelievably good. then, just put your fish or whatever to cook in much later in the game. Or just eat the veggies as a dish in and of itself.

This was quite delicious, but I have a comment on the use of ras al hanout.  I felt that given the amount of chicken, there was too much spice, and it felt harsh, even though I used a quality seasoning mix.  Many Moroccan recipes use ras al hanout, and you'll find that 2T. is normally used with a 3 1/2 or 3 pound chicken.  The delicious Moroccan dish of stewed lamb or lamb shanks with raisins and onions often uses ras al hanout, but barely 2 teaspoons.  Depends on your taste.  Cut the oil too.

I disagree. I used extra. It was bomb. Some directly on veggies. I couldn’t even find 2 teaspoons of that stuff on that much food. Perhaps your mix is a bit in balance. I made my own and adjusted it bit by bit until no one particular spice stood out.

I looked up a few recipes for lamb shanks. Moroccan style. The recipes I saw, for, 4 lamb, shanks, each had a minimum of between 4 teaspoons of ground. Spices up to 2 tablespoons combined ground spices. I’m not sure where you get the 2 teaspoons for 4 lamb shanks …that isn’t much at all for a combination of all the dry spices together. Maybe the recipe you are referring to also had additional dry spices.

I loved the flavors in this (ras el hanout for the win!) but found myself a little bit confused about the process of making the ‘sauce’ - does one remove the chicken, potatoes, and peppers from the tray and then mash the tomatoes, still in the pan, into a sauce before adding the garlic?  What about the chickpeas?  I think maybe I messed up by cutting the tomatoes too small and using pre-peeled garlic cloves - it was difficult to not include the chickpeas in the mash, too. 

I’m planning on making this for ten at a dinner party and am hoping to streamline that last step, so any advice would be welcomed.

@Debra to make the sauce part easier, I removed the chicken to a plate, then made a space in the middle of the sheet pan, where I mashed the garlic with a fork after removing the garlic from the skins.  Then I just stirred it all around with the addition of the vinegar.  The tomatoes fall apart and give juice that forms a sauce with the oil/garlic/vinegar.  If you aren’t serving on the sheet pan, you could also mash the garlic in a bowl and then pour in the oil and some of the tomatoes and the vinegar and stir, then dump in the potatoes and chickpeas.
Used quarter cup of oil otherwise made as written. Simple and delicious! Next time will add 2 heads of garlic. Will definitely make again!
I am only ranking this a 4 due to the difficulty finding ras el hanout. I wound up making my own. The recipe tasted great, and my family loved it.
Really delicious and easy to make, especially if you make the ras el hanout ahead and keep in a jar for future.

Absolutely delicious for a (fairly) easy one-pan meal! I made a few tweaks—reduced the olive oil to slightly <1/4 cup, used 2 garlic heads vs 1 and doubled the Roma tomatoes to 6 - to create more saucy goodness and for leftovers. I turned over the chicken to skin down midway during cooking to get some of the spice flavor and skin drippings on the veggies (which is also why I reduced the olive oil). I knew I was not going to eat the skin but wanted the flavor. Next time I will try with skinless chicken thighs. I cooked the veggies for another 7 min to get them more crispy and roasted flavor. Definitely a keeper!

Subbed in what I had on hand: red bell pepper instead of the romanos - it turned out great! I also had one gold potato and one sweet potato, and we all agreed that the sweet potato was way better! Very tasty, versatile dish.

The chickpeas start out crispy (good) but because it says to smash garlic and tomatoes on pan into sauce and stir around, they lose the crisp.  I baked it maybe 10 mins less than recommended time (total 55 mins) and chicken thighs were still overdone.  I should have been testing chicken temp  at 35-40mins.  I love eating leftovers and these were an exception—even though I kept chicken separate from sauce and veg/chickpeas in fridge.  Have made ras el hanout in past but for ease purchased Sadaf brand from local Persian grocery.  I don’t think I’d make again but if I did I think what another commenter said might help— it was overwhelmed by chickpeas and potatoes.  I’d reduce those and add carrots
Great easy fish. Served for company and it was a big hit. Any good spice blend you like will work. I could not source ras el hanout so used Penzey’s Rogan Josh seasoning which worked very well.
Used homemade chickpeas and served it over arugula--everyone loved it.
I doubled the garlic and also put a can of whole peeled San Marzano tomatoes on the sheet.

I made this last night (as written) and it was a hit. I have just a couple of tips. As with others, 1/4 of oil is more than enough if you are using thighs with skin on them. If you are using the tomatoes that you have (I did not have Romas but just tomatoes on the vine) you may find that there is a lot of liquid released - less browning.Next time I would add some sliced red onion and sprinkle extra Ras El Hanout on the  veggies as well.

Most delicious chicken dish ever!!
Restaurant quality!  The chicken skin was crispy, chicken tender. Flavor out of this world. Will be making this dish again and again!
Delicious! I've made it twice. I love the ras el hanout and it's flavor. ( I since sprinkle it on lots of things included eggs....

I made this tonight for Shabbat dinner! The grands, who are usually picky, loved the flavor! My garlic wasn’t cooked enough even though I added 30 minutes. Next time I’ll cook at 400 or 425!
I used a cut up chicken instead of only thighs.
Delicious! I agree about reducing the oil. The next time I make it I’m going to double the potato, tomato, chickpea, and peppers mixture.

The flavor profile is amazing.  So good.  I made my own Ras el hanout and it was also delicious.  Something I would use in other recipes.  Some have commented on the amount of oil as being too much.  I can understand the amount in the recipe, it did confit the veggies and potatoes and that much oil just emulsifies the flavor beautifully.  But I will experiment with less oil as I'm not used to cooking or eating that much in a home cooked meal.  I'll make this recipe again and again.
So tasty! Add more veggies - carrots, kumara, whatever you like!
Amazing. I homemade the Ras El hanout with the spices I had on hand and it was still great!
Is step 4 necessary? When I shake nothing changes layers. Could I just bake for 65 minutes straight and skip the shaking?
This was a hit. Used half of chicken and everything else as written. So flavorful!

Thank you, thank you, thank you Sid for the how-to instructions for the ras el hanout! I had seen this recipe and was dying to try it but couldn’t find that spice anywhere so when I read Sid’s comment I said let’s go - I didn’t have any potatoes and I tossed in broccoli rabe at the end of baking because I had it - DELICIOUS! Thank you Yotam for this wonderful recipe! Can’t wait to make it again but with potatoes!
Delicious meal!  I didn't have potatoes, so I substituted cauliflower florets after rubbing them in the oil and the seasoning.

Fantastic. Did not use salt and pepper as was in my spice blend. Used 3/4 c oil, but may cut to 1/2 c in future. Cook time was perfect. We used the mini peppers as that is what we have here. Stemmed and seeded them. Really easy dish.
This was amazing.  1/2 c oil was plenty; next time I'll try 1/3 c.
I’m not sure if I did something wrong but I found this dish to be bland and watery. I was quite disappointed.

This was a bit of a miss for me as written. I took others advice and made my own Ras el hanout and it was fantastic! The kitchen smelled great while it was cooking. I didn't reduce the oil enough and  the potatoes were too soft for me. I added extra tomatoes  which was good. The chickpeas were perfect here. In all fairness, I prefer breasts to thighs, and particularly boneless. Next time I'd add more tomatoes, use cutlets, put some of the spice on the potatoes but cook them separately.

For the first time ever, I met an Ottolenghi recipe I didn’t love. (Seriously, Yotam, I spent a whole year just cooking your food, like Julie & Julia.) I’m willing to try again, but I feel like I’d like to reduce the size of the potatoes - they came out undercooked - and add a few more spices, like smoked paprika & aleppo pepper. More tomatoes plus more garlic. I did have some very good ras al hanout and loved that on the chicken.
Delicious! I agree with the community's comments about decreasing the amount of olive oil. If I had done that, perhaps the potatoes would have been as crispy as the chicken. Overall so darn good.

So very good! Chicken came out moist with flavorful and crisp skin. I used boneless thighs, so it cooked about 10 minutes quicker than indicated. Also used all the olive oil and the sauce was delicious! Thinking it would be a good dish with pasta.
Cooked at 425. Veg in one sheet pan. Boneless thighs on another so things would crisp up rather than steam. Removed  seeds from tomatoes and diced. Diced peppers too.

Okay so I made this recipe and didn’t follow the ingredients exactly 🙈 but it still came out fantastic! 

I used baharat seasoning instead of ras al hanout, Japanese sweet potatoes instead of the Yukon gold, onions instead of peppers and rice wine vinegar instead of sherry (thanks poor planning) 
But following the recommended method (and chemistry) it came out great! I had it with some store bought tzatsiki!

This was convenient with minimal prep, but didn’t hit for us. I used gnocchi instead of chicken (added 20 min before taking out) to save some money, also added an extra can of drained white beans and used sweet potatoes as well as yellow potatoes. I had zero luck squishing the garlic and everything turned out dry, I’m sure it was missing the juices from the chicken. Luckily I had some extra chicken shawarma and that jazzed it up!

Easy-to-make and comes together quickly. I reduced the ingredients to make two servings and omitted the tomatoes, which my wife doesn't like. Otherwise followed the recipe as written. This was a nice dish that we both liked. I'll make it again.
I used baharat (a Levantine spice mix, not the same as ras el hanout but very tasty anyway) and it worked fine.
Having an allergy to Alliums, I made the recipe without the garlic and found a Ras El Hanout spice blend without onion powder. The recipe was very good, definitely a keeper.
Had to cook extra 20 minutes with half the amount of chicken

3rd time I've made this and my elderly mum, with very conservative tastes, absolutely loved it. reduced oil, added pumpkin and carrot to the potatoes which I parboiled and then rose with the chickpeas for 15 minutes before adding the rest of the ingredients. made my own ras el hanout, but had no cayenne pepper so used dried chilli flakes in its place. finished with culantro as no cilantro and finely sliced spring onions.
Great dish, simple and delicious! I added some cardommom seed (just to flavor, removed at end) used red bell pepper, used small multi-colored potatoes, only 1/4 cup olive oil (plenty). Wonderful dish
This might be one of the best things I’ve cooked from this site. The speedy prep time is fantastic. Unbelievably good. That spice blend is everything
Absolutely delicious! The family loved it. Ottolenghi is unmatched for delicious flavors and healthy ingredients. This one we’ll make again and again.
It sounds delicious, but ultimately I think you need to make this one 2-3 times to really get it right.  The first time it was OK, but not the lovely deliciousness I was hoping for.
Honestly, this was an oily mess even with 1/3 the oil called for.  My family did not care for it.
I made this dish exactly according to the recipe and it is absolutely delicious!
It was very dry in general. Chickpeas burned. Chicken overcooked.

This is an excellent sheet pan chicken dinner, and a healthly choice as well. Particularly if you choose, as I did, to include a couple of skin-on bone-in chicken breasts. The Ras El Hanout adds a savory and fragrant dash to what could have been a bland dish, and the peppers and tomatoes add some color to food that would otherwise be, well, beige. Next time I will stick with 1/2 cup of olive oil. Otherwise, this is absolutely superb, and next time, I might add a sturdy green such as kale.
Perfect. The whole family loved this.  It’s going into the rotation.
No keen on nutmeg, cloves and cinnamon in the Ras El hanout, so I'm going to try some Za'atar instead.

Do it.  The spice "ras el hanout" with the sherry vinegar is the key, to me.  Couldn't stop eating this dish.  I did use the 3/4 cup of XVOO as well.  It kept the chicken moist and the vegetables cooked.  I lined the baking pan with parchment paper.  My chicken was done before the potatoes and peppers (I used la rouge red peppers), so I did pull the chicken out before it got overly done.  This dish is a keeper!!  Whole Foods had the ras el hanout spice.  I did use 2x the chick peas.
Excellent! Not too much oil, cause I have bread for that.

The flavors were delicious, but I wasn't happy with how the vegetables cooked.  I felt that the chicken could be added to the other ingredients after a half hour of cooking and then another 65 minutes in the oven with the chicken.
Absolutely love this recipe. A family favorite and delicious enough to serve to company.

This is a great dish! I made a small amount with as written (with sweet mini peppers which I had on hand; but since my husband doesn't like peppers, but does like chick peas, I took the recipe in another direction using parsnips cut into sticks. That was a sweeter version of the original, and also delicious. (I had no cilantro on hand, so I substituted dried basil.)  I had to concoct a Ras el Hanout; mine was on the sweet side.

this was heavenly. i have to admit I didn't read the recipe too closely, and forgot the garlic in the first 20 minutes, and added onion, and had no energy to mash the canned diced tomatoes I substituted, and I only had Jamaican all spice. would put this in a weekly rotation.
Great recipe, and unlike most NYT recipes, this only took me 1 and a 1/2 hours to make, start to finish. This is truly an ideal "weeknight" formula.

I'm a huge Ottolenghi fan and have yet to be underwhelmed by one of his recipes, but this is a first. I feel like it was developed for American palates and just isn't up to scratch with his usual amalgamation of texture and flavour. It was ok. As others have said, vegetables are underseasoned, overall flavour is muted and it was decidedly underwhelming at best. Followed recipe to the letter. Disappointed. If you want true Ottolenghi, go for his smoky shrimp pasta, which was heaven on a plate, although the instructions seemed too complicated for many of the Americans reviewing it.
3-4 Tbsp of oil should suffice - with a drizzle more if needed - was there a top in the recipe?

Made last night for 8 adults after working a full day.  Easy to assemble and gorgeous in presentation.  Used skinless boneless thighs and it was absolutely delicious.   Doubled the amount of everything and very very little was leftover.  No need for a starch because of the potatoes.   Made a side salad.

This was quite good. I cut the oil to 1/2 cup and that was plenty; 3/4 cup would have been too greasy. I think next time I'll double the garlic and add more tomatoes and potatoes.

Tip: If you're having trouble finding the Ras el Hanout spice blend, you might check the food section at your local Home Goods store, which is where I found it ($5.99 for a 4.5 oz jar). You can't predict what they'll have in stock, but they often carry some specialty spice blends, as does Marshalls and TJMaxx.
This was amazing. Will definitely make again. I used 1/4 cup oil as per comments, would add another potato or two next time.

Very good! It was hard to find ras el hanout, so I had to procure and gather the ingredients to make myself. At first I thought the 3/4 oil was too much, but it might prove useful as a dipping sauce for some homemade bread. I would also season the potatoes, tomatoes, and peppers before adding next time as I thought they came out a little bland, but they got better as the food sat overnight. 

Would also recommend adding another can of chickpeas.

This recipe is amazing. I was able to order the ras el hanout through Amazon (Morton and Bassett brand). I made exactly as written, which meant I ended up using 10 bone-in, skin-on thighs, as that was the closest I could get to 3 1/4 pounds. Some are saying to reduce the olive oil to 1/4 cup. Why?? It works perfectly at 3/4 cup. The thighs just crisped up beautifully. Not sure if that would have happened at 1/4 cup.
How would you adjust this for chicken breasts?

title: Ultimate Veggie Meatballs Recipe
author: Yotam Ottolenghi

comments.commentBody:
Why the hatred for halloumi? It's being used here as a firm cheese that won't separate as you cook it. I'm sure paneer would work but halloumi isn't a pungent goat cheese & isn't always made from goat milk... 

And anyway, as he says in the article, "put the constraints of the recipe to one side, with some creativity and a bit of trial and error, you will find that more goes than you would think. " So just leave it out

I like to cook my beluga lentils in some veggie broth with a sprig of thyme.
@MCScott my only issue with halloumi is price. I can get two huge blocks of paneer for the same price as a small block of halloumi and have roughly the same outcome.
Halloumi isn’t goat cheese. It’s a very firm sheep’s milk cheese that can stand up to being grated—as in this recipe—and hold its own in a mixture (instead of disappearing into mushiness).

@all
I used canned lentils, but they were crumbly, and the meatballs barely held together. Next time I’ll mash them before adding to the recipe. These were delicious and the halloumi made a really nice crunchy crust when baked. I also used a about a quarter of a cup of olive oil instead of the half cup called  for in the recipe before baking the meatballs and it came out fine.
Is there a substitute for halloumi?
Paneer.
@Judy Queso Fresco (Walmart, Aldi)
@Judy Queso Fresco
Can’t stand goat cheese. Any substitute for the halloumi?
@PCranstonConley Paneer
If you don't like/have halloumi -- paneer, ricotta salata, feta or tofu would all be good substitutes. Or just leave it out.
@Mark L. Or Queso Fresco, readily availability in most big supermarkets
why Holaumi??? is there an alternative cheese?

@mary
Paneer cheese.
Because halloumi is delicious, unique texture and it is usually not goat cheese. Trader Joes sells cow milk halloumi!
Halloumi haters and agnostics, It's a recipe not a mandate. Add in grate any cheese you like, or skip it entirely. Consider it a suggestion like "salt to taste."
Well the use of cheese and eggs cuts out a lot of us vegetarians and vegans. Vegetarian means more than just cutting out animal tisssue than many of us.
@John L Ghertner, MD I’m going to try it with a pressed firm smoked tofu and Bob’s Red Mill egg replacer and will report back!

@Hande
Please do!  I cant take lactose!
@John L Ghertner, MD you can also use JustEgg for the egg replacement and tofu for the cheese. The site Nora Cooks has excellent tofu or cashew-based cheese replacement options.

Really delicious! Pleasantly surprised. 
Line your baking dish or meatballs will stick. 
I only slightly modified: tablespoon of dried Italian herbs, slightly less mint (don’t skip the mint- it adds body), slightly less cumin, added diced shallot. 
I hand squeezed the zucchini before adding salt, then squeezed again. Then I used the dishcloth. 
I let the mixture sit in the fridge for a bit, then poured off even more liquid. 
It still looked too loose so I added 1/4 cup breadcrumbs 
I cooked the meatballs for about 15 minutes longer. 
Used Rao’s jarred sauce. So so good!!!
Is the couscous cooked or uncooked?
@Mary Fran The author must have used fine couscous (not medium or course) which basically cooks with a drizzle of hot water and closed lid if you leave it for 5 minutes or so.

@Mary Fran the recipe says to add 3 tbsp of boiling water to the couscous. You don’t want to add too much water or it will mess with the ability to form them into balls. It doesn’t take much to cook fine couscous and it will cook fully in the oven.

As my wife has told me on multiple occasions, “ you really should confine yourself to recipes with no more than 3 ingredients.”
She may be right. But it won’t stop me from trying.
Last night I assembled all the ingredients in a bowl, mixed them in my hands and realized there was no way I was going to form meatballs from this mixture. Well they were all healthy ingredients, so I just pored it into a casserole dish and baked it for 30 minutes, added the sauce and voila, the best “meatloaf” ever.
@Art  Seelenfreund … I also had the “these will never work as meatballs” moment, so I formed them into patties.  Best veggie burgers ever!
thats a lot of oil to pour over the meatballs
@Oriana      I agree that was a lot of oil, so I kept some of it back and scrapped off the residue from the meatball pan and added it to the extra pasta . It was delicious.
The addition of halloumi is a great idea!  It doesn't melt and leave you a mess when the "meatballs" come out of the oven.
To those who’ve made this: did it really need to be drizzled with a half a cup of olive oil? That seems like a lot.
@cocomero the oil cooks them in a similar way that frying meatballs would
@cocomero definitely not! A tablespoon was plenty.
@Food with tweaks hardly, but tastes vary.

As a vegan substitute for halloumi cheese, pitted black olives would come close to the flavor profile but wouldn’t provide the binding quality of melted cheese. If dairy isn’t an issue, another cheese, like a mix of parm and mozz, would be tasty.
@Lewis I’m going to try it with a pressed smoked firm tofu and will report back!
Halloumi is a sheep’s milk cheese that grates easily. Very mild flavor. Holds up well to heat.

Halloumi is hard for me to find so I did a Google search. Paneer (a common cheese in south Asian/Indian cooking) is a good substitute if you have that more readily available. That's what I'm going to try.
@Julie C. Queso Fresco is also usually available in most big supermarket chains
I would use most any semi-hard cheese like Colby, cheddar, Monterey Jack, or even pepper Jack.
Queso fresco works in this recipe and is easily found at Aldi and Walmart.
Bread cheese has a similar consistency.
Responding to others:  Any soft cheese will do.  Do not get pre-shredded, as it is too dry.

Wow! I was amazed at the meat-like texture of these! An excellent recipe. And likely pretty versatile, too — I could see switching out the herbs and spices to a number of other combinations. I used black Beluga lentils and skipped the couscous part and used 1/2 cup cooked quinoa instead and it worked well! Served with Rao’s and spaghetti squash, making for a delicious and pretty virtuous dinner…except that I had some wine with it, which is now, apparently, officially lethal :(

To mary, PCranstonConley and Judy, in the spirit of the recipe, you can swap with another dairy cheese, vegan cheese or even extra-firm tofu! Paneer is similarly sturdy and keeps its shape; it's a lot less salty than halloumi (which I personally love).
has anyone tried these in an air fryer?

I think Queso Panela might be the easiest to substitute in terms of similar properties and availability (find it at Latino grocers). It's more firm and salty like halloumi and also doesn't melt much in high heat. However, I'm not sure why the recipe calls for halloumi to begin with. Is it only because it's saltier and doesn't melt? Or for other reasons? It would help to know why the recipe calls for halloumi so that we can make appropriate subs if it isn't available.

These were delicious, easy to make and very tasty. I made my own tomato sauce and transferred the meatballs  to that pan before putting them back in the oven., I made sure to drizzle some sauce over the meatballs to keep them soft. I served this with a side dish of orzo with spinach and feta cheese, perfect.

These veggie balls were delicious. I only had pearl couscous which I used but the mixture was so wet I then added a half cup of cooked quinoa. I didn’t have enough sauce so I will make more tomorrow for the leftover pasta. I was surprised that the veggie balls held together because the mix was so wet. Next time I will add some garlic to the mix. Thanks NY Times.
Vegan/dairy free sub for halloumi?
@alice extra firm tofu might work, squeezed dry a bit. Can partially freeze to grate easier. Squeeze again once grated and thawed.
Lightly sprayed pan and then reduced olive oil (drizzled over meatballs) to 1/4 cup.  Seemed like plenty. Next time I might add another cup of sauce (personal preference)

Takes time to cook the lentils separately, add the boiling water to the couscous, shred the halloumi, shred and drain the zucchini, and put it all together but absolutely worth it. It made 12 meatballs as the recipe states, but two of us snacked and ate them all in one sitting. Given the work that goes into the recipe, I would absolutely double or triple the quantity next time and freeze.

It seems to me that cumin and mint are going to make this a very Mediterranean recipe. To keep it Italian, I would leave the lentil/zucchini base, add some cooked bulgur or barley for texture, and otherwise proceed as with my regular Italian meatballs (bread crumbs, garlic, parsley, parmagiano-reggiano). Of course then it would be a completely different recipe!

To keep these veggie meatballs round, prior to cooking place in the freezer to firm them up and then dip into a thin flor water mix and deep fried. Crunchy when done, but not after re-frigeration afterwards
what is a green lentil? split peas ? what is a brown lentil?
These are legumes sold as dried along with the bags of dried beans at a grocery store. They cook faster than, say, dried pinto or navy beans and don't require soaking first.

This was excellent, as is everything I’ve made by Ottolenghi. My first time using canned lentils (did not know they came canned!). Made the tomato sauce and it will be my go-to from now on. 
There’s a lot of shade for halloumi (and dairy in general) in the comments. The recipe would be called “vegan meatballs” if that were the intent.

For people looking for a halloumi substitute - sometimes rinsed cottage cheese curds (salted to taste) will work.  I love halloumi but it's expensive, so I substitute in recipes like this where it's not a standalone ingredient.  Planning to try it.

I really was baffled how great these were. My only sub was cooked jasmine rice in replacement of the couscous b/c we are gf. They were a sloppy wet mess when I put them on the (lined) baking sheet. Miraculously, they came out great….I guess all this is to say have faith, if you follow through they’re pretty incredible. My newly vegetarian five year old / harshest critic I know had seconds.

It's not that there are halloumi haters, it's that it can be really impossible to find if you live in a rural area or it's wicked expensive. I do wish recipe writers would provide a couple ingredient substitute suggestions.
substituting queso panela will save you a lot of money here
These are so delicious and freeze really well. I like to serve them with marinara and spaghetti squash.

Tastes good as all Ottolenghi recipes do IMO but if you follow the tips, you double the cooking time length.  Not sure if it was worth all the effort to make homemade lentils / sauce.  Next time, store bought is fine!

Also, you use every kitchen pot / bowl / utensil you own with this dish.  Another strike for maybe not worth the effort..
Does anyone know how these hold up in the freezer ?

Does the Times not respond to notes? I had a question about a substitute for couscous, since I have celiac.

Also for those who say it's too wet, I think the recipe writer meant to add dry couscous to the mix. It would absorb liquid during the bake. Theey don't say anywhere, but that's the only way the recipe makes sense.
Maybe try quinoa?

Tasty recipe, but a lot of work for the result. These are definitely veggie balls, no pretense of tasting like meatballs. I have no idea how halloumi turned into WW3 in the comments, but when my grocery was out, using shredded mozzarella (tillamook for us, but whatever) worked well.

This was fantastic. I follow the recipe exactly, even weighing ingredients in grams. The veggie balls were quite wet and barely held together. At first, I was worried and disappointed in the recipe. But they firm up perfectly in the oven due to the egg content. I think you could keep some bread crumbs on hand in case you need them, but I would use them sparingly and aim for a fairly wet mix. The flavor is great— you taste the mint, parsley and halloumi. The lentils and egg give it the slight firmness of meat. Others said they taste like falafel, but I didn’t think so at all. Very good. Will definitely make again.

To reply to queries:  some halloumi is made from cow's milk, and  some is made from a mixture of goat's and sheep's milk. I've been buying it without paying attention to the type of milk and notice primarily the pleasant chewy texture after sauteeing or otherwise cooking, rather than the flavor, which seems neutral, especially when cooked with other ingredients. But Finnish bread cheese (cow's milk, mozzarella or perhaps queso fresco could be tried.

My husband and I loved it. Couldn't find fresh mint at the market. I substituted cilantro. Used feta instead of halloumi. Oops, I forgot to drizzle the oil over the meat balls. They were fine. The bottoms browned more nicely than the tops. But the tops of the meat balls got slightly brown. Still very tasty and has that Middle Eastern flavor palate.  Definitely will make again.

I’ve made this twice now and I am enamored with its genius. It really does hold together like the magic that it is.  The first time I made it as written, but doubled. It was delicious, but I made it on a metal sheet pan and it was hard to clean. The second time, I left out the mint and cumin and doubled down (quadrupled probably) on parsley for a more Italian taste.
