I also reduced to three eggs instead of four, because I suspected eggs were the source of most of my stubborn stuck on stuff. I packed them in tight in the glass dishes (as the recipe had told me to do!) and voila, no mess!

Also, as a person counting calories and upping my protein, I didn’t make the pasta, but loved them just covered in sauce. The couscous counted as my carb for the meal.

Better than expected! With no garlic or onion, I thought this would be bland, but the flavour was there. I added some chili flakes to the canned red sauce once out of the oven. Don't skip the halloumi, the rubbery texture helps hold them together. I found still liquid left when forming the balls and had to really squeeze to form them. I wouldn't call this an easy weeknight meal with many dishes and prep involved. I kept them in the fridge for 2 days before baking. Will make again for a crowd!

Ok yall. The robustness of the halloumi makes this dish so awesome and gives me that “meatball” taste! My blocks of halloumi come in 8 oz and I grate the whole thing rather than measure out 1/2 cup grated so I use a lot more cheese but too much cheese has never been a complaint of mine. I’ve made this twice and the second time, I smushed the whole mushy lot into a loaf pan and cooked it like a meatloaf! Cooked it about 30-40 min? My family (husband and 15 month old) enjoyed it with some simple white rice (definitely need a starch otherwise it’s too salty on its own) and ponzu sauce for some tang. Weird, I know, but delicious and pretty straightforward. If the baby likes it, I consider that an ultimate win!
These were fun and tasty and perfectly textured. But I will definitely cut the salt in half next time. Also used half the olive oil, and didn't miss that extra 1/4 cup.

I made these as directed and found them only OK and my wife and I agreed that they were not worth making again.  I agree that the texture is good, but in no way would this make me "forget its more traditional meaty predecessor." 

These veggie balls are best viewed as you would a bean burger, though the haloumi (which I like) contributed only texture, not flavor. We ended up eating them the way we would falafel: inside a pita, with tahini sauce and vegetables. But we'd rather have had falafels!

Third or fourth time making these.  Still love them.  I mixed basil, mint and parsley together for the herbs.  I like halloumi.  It's tasty.  The mixture is very wet so you have to be patient and do your best to form it into balls.  When it bakes they solidify.  It's a clever, great recipe.  One I'll keep making.

Great meatballs and very different from typical veg meatball recipes.   Not as firm, but formable for sure.   I enjoyed the olive oil level it added an unctuous crispy edge to the balls. 

Very riff able- subbed bulgar wheat for couscous.  Added a little fennel seed. 

If you use a substitute cheese instead of halloumi you’ll want to check your seasoning level for example paneer is a much much lower salt content.  

Make these meatballs!

The mixture fell apart, even after squeezing out more excess liquid. I added a tablespoon or two of coconut flour, but still no joy. Solution: grabbed a mini muffin tin, sprayed some mini muffin cases generously with olive oil, and used a mini ice cream scoop to add some mixture to them and baked them like that. Worked a treat!

@Natalie Great tip! Mine fell apart and don’t firm up as I had hoped they would - will try the muffin tin next time!

Loved the taste of these overall, and happy to find a recipe without mushrooms.

I think it has to do with how the egg is prepared. I found that by mixing the eggs really well and coding them liberally that it was able to bind well. I added the egg to the mixture after all the other ingredients were combined.
Is this cooked or raw couscous that's called for? Normal size or Israeli?

Halloumi is traditionally 100% sheeps milk(not goat) but there are some brands made with mixed milk (sheep, cow and goat). 
Aldi sells”halloumi like” cheese fries( strips of cheese)in the cheese case that is the best price around. I like to cube and fry it for ‘croutons’. Should be able to coarsely grate it.
Adding to the chorus--why halloumi? It's not easily available where I live and it's godawful expensive when it is. I'm willing to splurge on a winter zucchini but what to use instead of the halloumi?
Is the egg in there for binding? Any suggested substitutions for the egg or could we leave it out? We have an egg allergy in the house.
@Malone, maybe try Just Egg.  It’s a vegan egg substitute sold in the same section as regular eggs.
For those who don't care for haloumi I suggest feta or paneer. For a truly vegan version I suspect that TVP might work here.
I imagine any hard cheese that holds up to baking would do - paneer would be a solid choice.

For another take on meatless meatballs, check out chef John's recipe (food wishes) AND VIDEO. that recipe makes fabulous "meatballs". (Does include parmesan and eggs tho -- easy to substitute). Do watch the video. It's key.
Yes, Chef ohn's are the best!

I think all lentils should be referred to by some name, not just color - In most places, lentils are referred to by the plant they come from, which removes any confusion when there are so many lentils of the same color.

My meatballs, whether made with turkey, pork, beef, or Beyond “meat”, have lots of garlic. If I were to make these, I’d add some. Also, Halloumi is very firm; subbing any soft or crumbly thing (tofu, feta, etc.) would require several other adjustments to the recipe.
Thoroughly enjoy the NYT mag article that accompanies this recipe, a fun and inspiring read.  Why isn't there a link to it in the app? If there is, please advise!
@Moma The link is at the end of the recipe description. It was a fun read!
I made this as the recipe is written, including making the tomato sauce and lentils (as I had neither pre-made). It is absolutely delicious, and I highly recommend it!

I think the zucchini (even after squeezing) was still too watery. Really gotta triple squeeze! so we added panko and a little flour. Once done, these were great. We didnt cook the couscous, was that right? In any case the little bits of crunchy couscous were a great texture. Would make again and next time add chopped olives.

A great recipe that got high ratings from all family members. I was surprised to see my kids go back for seconds. This has been added to the standard rotation. I had my doubts about using a 1/2 cup of olive oil over the veggie balls but worked quite well especially when combined with the sauce. I used Melissa Clark's Simple Tomato Sauce with it -https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/1017173-simple-tomato-sauce An excellent meal.

Definitely too much moisture. Added breadcrumbs as a quick fix. Next time, I will start pressing the zucchini earlier and do several rounds... and have extra couscous available to dry it up if needed. Flavor is great.

This recipe intrigue me. My cous cous were too wet I think so they didn’t hold together well. I popped them in the freezer for two days ( I forgot about them). I thought of trying that cornstarch slurry but I didn’t want to complicate things. Heated the oven  super high. Let the balls sit on the stove top until it was really hot. I added the oil and waited. That oil started to boil and these came out nice and crispy. They did get stuck on the bottom of the pan, but they were delish. I will definitely try again to make for my vegetarian daughter. The mint makes them.

Liked them/made as written except used paneer as I did not have halloumi/didn't want to go out in the cold and drive to the store that carries it.  I don't think these were outstanding; if I made again, I'd put in some chopped pine nuts for texture.And I'd have to add grated parm for the vegetarian kidswho didh't like them much. Nor were they great for a "busy night"/a few too many steps for what I'd call quick and easy supper.    Maybe I'll make again, but they're not on the top of my list.

These taste so much like Falafel that you might as well make, well, falafel. (I did leave out the mint.) The mixture is very wet. Don‘t expect the consistency of ground meat, it‘s pretty crumbly stuff. But if you press it some, it will hold shape in the dish and then the egg will do the rest so they bake up quite firm. (I wonder if it would help to put the eggs in a sieve first to siphon off some of their liquid.)
@Helene I‘d like to add: If doused in tomato sauce, these do indeed have the mouthfeel of meatballs.

I made this dish for the second time tonight.  The first time I used quinoa and goat cheese and thought the "meatballs" were a bit loose.  This time, I used couscous and provolone cheese which was listed as a substitute in my online search ( My husband shopped and said our Trader Joe's did not have Halloumi--maybe he missed it)  The "meatballs" held together better.  The Olive Oil helps to brown them and from sticking to the pan!  This recipe though a bit time consuming is a winner!!

Delicious and really hits the meatball spot compared with other veggie ball recipes I've tried. Like others, though, my mixture was far too wet (even after squeezing the living daylights out of the zucchini) and would not hold a ball shape. I added 1/3 cup of panko, which firmed up the mixture perfectly for shaping. I also used only 1/4 cup of olive oil and I still found it a bit on the greasy side. With those mods, though, this was a great dinner that will be in frequent rotation.

This was a homerun! I could not find halloumi at my grocery store, so I put in feta cheese instead -- it worked great! My friend I cooked for wanted THIRDS! If anyone likes a kick, a hint of turmeric and cayenne complement the cumin well. Will be making again!

I've eaten these with spaghetti and marinara sauce, and liked it very much. But next time I want to take these more in a Greek direction and serve with rice or orzo, cucumbers, tomatoes, tatziki, etc. I think the spicing will translate well.
Now my go to! Amazing!!
What might be a good substitute for the cheese?
Is the couscous cooked? is it pearl couscous or finer couscous?
@Janis it cooks in the boiling water that's poured over it

Assuming this is very fine couscous, not the larger Israeli couscous?

What can replace zucchini?
You can get cow's milk halloumi.
How would you make this gluten free?
Try substituting quinoa for the couscous.  I'm not sure it would work, but should be close.
Is this pearl couscous or the finer one?
Quinoa instead of couscous? Use the same amount of water?
Interested in how well they might freeze if you make a big batch.
What would you substitute for couscous to make it gluten free? Not sure which grain would work best to hold it together.
I’m almost always able to find Halloumi at our small Trader Joe’s, and it’s not crazy expensive.
Didn’t make as many balls as I expected (volume quite low) - should they be made v small?
Can you simply omit the lentils and substitute an equal amount of beef or chicken?
Delicious.  Next time would add a little more sauce.
I really tried to follow the recipe and make it work. The meatballs were just too moist to do much with. The recipe was also labor intensive for little yield.

Worked well but I took the opportunity to tweak and eliminate some steps. I used corn instead of lentils and mozzarella instead of halloumi. The corn gave the dish more sweetness. I’d say cut back on the oil and the sauce.

Recipe was tasty, the fresh herbs provide nice flavor. Followed the recipe aside from the insane amount of oil, which would make an otherwise healthy meal into a calorie bomb. The balls firm up when cooking, but shaping them was challenging, especially since I had to move them to a new tray. Pretty fussy if you have to cook the lentils—and the end result has the flavor profile of a falafel. In short, I’ve made better veggie meatballs in terms of texture and flavor.
Great ideas for substitutions.

This was a semi bust. Lots of steps and nothing was like what the directions said. Lentils were too wet even after letting them strain. Zucchini was too wet and consequently the mix was just too wet. Like someone else we added panko bread crumbs. Also way too much oil for baking the meatballs. They didn’t stick but I should have turned them as they were pretty crispy on the one side. We made 1/2 lb of pasta and we still had way too much. I’m not sure how it would have needed a pound. They were tasty do sure.
Can you freeze these?
Yes, they freeze very well.

If making again use cooked quinoa but add some bread crumbs and line pan with parchment paper. Also use mozz or parm instead of halloumi; basil instead of mint; and oregano and garlic powder instead of cumin.

I would not make these again.  They were too watery to form into meatballs, even after adding bread crumbs.  When they were done baking the ones that held together weren’t really balls—more domes with a flat bottom.
do you really need 120 ml of oil? that seems like really a lot - from normal cooking practice I would assume that around 1/5 of that amount would suffice
These still weren’t firm after following all of the instructions. They were crumbly and fell apart even after baking for longer than the instructions called for.
This is a fantastic recipe. I used black lentils and it came out fine.

Made these three times now and I really love them. Hearty and flavorful, always a crowd pleaser. My critique is that they often fall apart when cooking. I add 1.5x the halloumi to help with the binding and give it more richness. I might try freezing it next time before baking to see if that helps. Maybe adding a small amount of breadcrumbs could help? I serve with a sweet sticky date and tomato sauce spiced with cinnamon. Would love a video of this recipe 💕

Really delicious. Less like meatballs than some other formulations but very tasty and holds up to pasta. I used feta only because I like the flavor and it worked well. Next time I may add some chopped walnuts to give it an even more nutty taste and texture.

An unusual combination of ingredients for no-meatballs that works out to be both tasty and versatile. In fact, I'm not so sure this flavorful ball is done justice on top of spaghetti and covered with tomato sauce.

Added about 1/4 cup breadcrumbs so they would hold their shape. Also used a lot less oil than recommended (just enough to lightly brush on top). Baked in air fryer at instructed temp, and they were ready in less than 20 min. Tasty, and I really liked the texture.

So good! 

I substituted extra firm tofu in place of the halloumi with excellent results. 

Great texture and flavour without trying to replicate the meatiness of meatballs.  A dish everyone enjoys, vegetarian or not.
Very accidentally used quinoa instead of couscous and they were still amazing!  Just a very slight difference in texture.
After all the complaints and problems in the comments 5* seems off and impossible...wouldn't even attempt it!

Bake at 375 and check at 10 minutes (someone noted 450 at 10 minutes worked but the outside was too crispy and they planned to try 375-400 next time) 
Par into 8” circle and cut into 6 wedges (like scones). 
Cut baked wedges in half to serve

title: Sticky Date and Pecan Pudding Recipe
author: Yotam Ottolenghi

comments.commentBody: Date molasses???  What’s that?  Could regular molasses be substituted?
No. Date molasses is very different. Middle eastern groceries have it. My regular store carries it in the baking aisle as well. Also called date syrup.
It's available in Jewish and Middle Eastern stores and called "Silan."  I think you could substitute honey or regular molasses or maple syrup.
Trader Joe's has date syrup! Would be just right in this recipe; leftover syrup would be great on anything delicious with dates: a peanut butter sandwich, an almond butter sandwich, oatmeal ...
I feel stupid asking, but do you mash the dates into the water once you have softened them ? Or, pour the water off and just mash the softened dates?
I'll join you in feeling uninformed, and hope someone answers this question!
Read Step One.
@Muffy Duffy no, do not drain.
"Date Molasses" sounds like a slow dating app.

@TJ
What about “date wine”. I drank once, it was too good.

Thank you for sharing this--my mother in law introduced me to this years ago, while she is long gone we have served it rarely.  This reminded me how much I loved it and the legacy it has in our family.  I will make this for Christmas dinner and share with our new daughters in law--  gluten and dairy free to boot
The all-purpose flour would not be gluten-free.
@Paula watch out - NOT gluten free, it uses flour!!
What  is "date molasses?"
What I really want to see is the recipe for the magical two-tiered lemon pudding!

@L Whitley same! But I wondered if it was maybe like the lemon pudding cakes Melissa Clark has a recipe for here. It’s a sort of magical batter that you bake in a water bath. The part above the water becomes a light spongy cake while the bottom (if you don’t over cook it) stays like a lemon curd pudding consistency. So delicious!
@L Whitley Search recipes here for “Lemon Pudding Cake”, I just made it last night!

Goodness people, such drama and exclaiming over date molasses, also called date syrup. It's available everywhere, and makes this dish less sweet and more interesting. One of the things i appreciate most about Yotam Ottolenghi's recipes.
@leslie  not available in rural areas period end of story
@Oregonian there is always the internet. Lots of resources available to those of us in the hinterland which I use frequently.
Pour the water over the cake? Can someone explain that? Sorry if its obvious. Also not a fan of pumpkin spice, is there something else we can substitue it with?
@Sharmistha. I am sure the boiling water goes over the uncooked cake batter. It would be added just before baking.
@Sharmistha skip the pumpkin spice. The sticky toffee pudding recipe (from the big yellow gourmet cookbook) that I’ve used for years doesn’t call for any additional spices and it’s lovely.

@Sharmistha if you don’t care for pumpkin spice, think about the spices you do like! For example, if you like cinnamon only, just sub that in. Or mix cinnamon and nutmeg. You can play a little bit and make the flavor just the way you like it!
I made this right away after seeing the recipe.  Awesome!  highly recommended!
No to pumpkin spice mix!!  What can I susbsittute?

If you Google diy pumpkin spice mix, you'll find that it's basically cinnamon, nutmeg, ginger, cloves and or allspice. Recipes vary so if you like any of those flavors, just use enough of those to get the amount of pumpkin spice the recipe calls for.
@BJR the Cooking section provides a recipe for Pumpkin Spice Mix. Take it from there to get the mix you want!

@BJR Here is a NYT recipe for pumpkin spice. But you can change it to suit yourself and your taste:

1T cinnamon, 
1t ground ginger, 
1/2t ground nutmeg, 
1/4t ground allspice, 
1/4 to 3/4t ground cloves

I made this as written except for the sour cream topping. Sticky toffee pudding is my favorite dessert but I am loath to make it due to its richness. This is an excellent, lighter alternative to the OG version, perhaps not quite as delicious but definitely worthwhile. I think you could even skip the date molasses though it is no more exotic an ingredient than, say, mirin (mine is from Trader Joe’s). Next time I will try sprinkling the pecans on the bottom of the baking dish and serving it upside down so the toffee sauce is on top. I might try omitting the butter on top as well. For those wondering, yes, you pour boiling water over the batter and as it bakes it forms a sauce. There is a whole category of these sort of puddings— weird but true (and tasty)!
I think since it's a measured amount of boiling water mash the dates in it to get to a paste/molasses consistency
Who has date molasses? This is another recipe where you hunt around to buy a unique ingredient that likely sits in your pantry till you make the recipe again in another year.
@Scott Date molasses is good on so many things, and easy to find.
Flakey salt?
@Judy Maldon salt

Hoping to hear from someone who has made this.  Sorry Google wasn't working for all those wondering about date syrup.

Yes, I am being snarky about the absence of cooking notes here in the space called "Cooking Notes."
Please confirm: Do I pour the 2 cups of boiling water directly over the raw cake batter? This is new to me.

Betty Crocker's old cookbook had a chocolate pudding cake that called for the same thing.  I think the water is needed to make the sauce.  It worked beautifully in  the chocolate pudding cake.  It was my go-to dessert when I was in my 20s.

@Christine
When I make the chocolate sauced version folks are referring to, I sometimes hold a TBS over the cake, pour the water into the TBS and let it run over the sides of the spoon and move around over different parts of the cake.  I have also just poured the water gently straight onto the cake, moving around so I’m not making a big hole anywhere.  I don’t think it matters how you do it - the water/sauce ends up on the bottom no matter what you do.  I’ve made the chocolate version a zillion times.  It really does work.
@Karen likewise, except mine is from my 40 year old Pilsbury cookbook. Since high school I’ve been making pudding cakes. Easy
Trader Joe’s has date syrup.

All kinds of options to buy it or make date molasses/syrup.  There are easy recipes online.  One I saw had a comment that used a slow cooker.  It's just dates, water and a pinch of salt.  One recipe had a pinch of cardamom optional.
Any substitutes for date molasses?  I have Lyle’s golden syrup (treacle) - could I use that?

@Coriander

Regular molasses is fine
If you click on the underlined words "pumpkin spice mix" in the ingredients list, it takes you to instructions on how to make pumpkin spice mix.

Everyone was excited to see sticky toffee pudding on the menu of my wedding lunch.  In Oban, Scotland.  SCOTLAND!  I'd have thought they got it all the time there.  I've made it here in SoCal- great bc I can have as much as I want.  However, they don't make enough ice cream for that.
@McGuava Juice sounds beautiful! We are celebrating my husband’s 69th birthday in Oban in May. Any restaurant suggestions? I think we’ll be at the Premier Inn.
Our wedding lunch was at the Manor House, but the last time we went, we just wandered around and found the best burger I've ever had!  Please, have a wonderful time!
Date molasses is also called silwan. It’s widely available at stores that sell middle eastern food. It’s much, much tastier than molasses, so I wouldn’t substitute

Nagi Maehashi from RecipeTin Eats has a great tip for her self saucing chocolate pudding where she pours the water slowly and carefully over the back of a dessert spoon and moves it all over the top of the batter for even coverage. I imagine this would come in handy here!
Please explain a little more about pouring boiling water over the batter in the cake pan before baking?  I have never run into a baking instruction like this.

The magical lemon pudding is usually called Lemon Sponge Pudding. It is not complicated to make, and while it bakes it separates itself into a top layer that's light and spongy and a bottom 
layer of lemon custard. Delightful flavor and texture contrast.
Easily found date syrup in baking section of local grocery store. Don’t get discouraged!
According to the instructions 2 cups of the remaining boiling water is poured over the cake (mixture) batter.

Absolute hit at Christmas dinner tonight - perfect amount of sweetness and the sour cream was a nice touch, although some folks at the table had theirs with vanilla ice cream and liked that as well. 

Slight changes were made: was more generous with the sprinkled pecans so don’t be afraid to add more than what the recipe calls for. I also melted the remaining butter to distribute it more evenly across the top of the batter before adding the boiling water. And instead of folding in the dates/flour/etc I used my electric mixer for those steps as well - between that and baking it in a 9.5x9.5 corningware dish it made for a tall, perfectly spongy cake with a luscious almost-toffee sauce below it. Will definitely be making it again!!!

Delicious! Everyone at our holiday table really loved it. My only issue was finishing it in the oven. The directions say when the caramel is bubbling on the sides and the center has “set“ however, the center never really said it was pretty wobbly jiggly and liquid like the whole time. I finally just said the hell with us took it out of the oven And thought well it’s supposed to be somewhat soft like a pudding. I’ll just serve it. That ended up being absolutely the right thing to do before the pecans burned. Not sure what else I would’ve done differently next time maybe scant less water? The date syrup was great. The pecans added such great nutty, savory tones kind of reminded me of a pecan tassy holiday treat
Wait, 1.5 t flaky salt?
Has anyone tried with gluten free flour?

It was ok, a little dry in parts, and the “pumpkin” spice mixture overwhelmed.  Maybe I’d make it again with just a smidge of cinnamon instead of all that, and in a slightly smaller dish.  The caramel was a bit watery.  We ate it all over a few days, but it was not all I had imagined it to be.  Definitely better with sour cream, crème fraîche or plain skyr as a muted accompaniment, than say vanilla ice cream.
What type of gluten free flour works best in lieu of the all-purpose flour?

Since the recipe was developed using regular all purpose flour, I doubt they experimented with any other versions.  But since you probably ? have experience using gluten free, try the brand you've used and are most comfortable with.
What sort of pan do you bake this in? Would a Bundt pan be ok?
Did the meatballs in an air fryer with a spray of neutral oil. Perfect and minimal cleanup.

Oh my - this brings back memories.  Every Christmas my mom made a version of this where everything was dumped into the Dutch oven and what came out of the oven and hour latewas ooey gooey deliciousness topped with a cakey layer. We called it Eileen Foulk's Date Pudding because Eileen Foulk wrote out the recipe for my mother. I no longer have the recipe (sort of glad) but I do recall it asked for 1 pint of white sugar. It was served straight out of the oven with a big bunge of vanilla ice cream.

Made this with gluten free flour, didn't have date molasses, used pomegranate molasses instead. Replaced sour cream with coconut whipped cream. 
Sooo good!

A wonderful Christmas dessert for our crew of 12!  We topped it with scoops of vanilla bean ice cream and everyone was happy.   (Date syrup was new to me, but was on the shelves of our local coop.)  Will certainly make this again!
Has anyone made this gluten free? Almond flour?
SO delicious and so easy! A big hit at Christmas dinner. I was a little hesitant about the sour cream with the date molasses swirled in, but wow it was delicious. I will definitely make this again

I was so disappointed with this.  I love sticky toffee pudding, and often make self-saucing cakes with the boiling water step.  This came out with far too much cake an almost no sauce - and the taste was half as rich as I expected with the date molasses and pecans.  Might try again but double the topping and increase the final water pour to 3 cups.

Not much caramel sauce was generated and I followed the recipe exactly.  This was more like a cake than a pudding.  Still tasty, but definitely needed the creme fraiche (I used this instead of sour cream) with the date molasses "marbled" in and dolloped on top or else it would have been dry.

Pretty good but nothing I’d make again. Messy to make and lots to wash up and tasted only like a rich spice cake. I’m not a fan of spice cakes.  It has a nice sticky bottom but not the thick caramel goo I was hoping for.  I’d like to experiment more with making real toffee and bread puddings which I suspect I’d like more. Not a centerpiece dish.

I made this last night. My partner, who is the sticky toffee pudding love, really enjoyed it. I thought is was unusually dry for a toffee pudding. Caramel did not show up. 
I found date molasses, and I think they are great and could be used for many purposes.
So much discussion about ingredients!  Not enough about how amazingly DELICIOUS this dessert is!  It's pretty easy to make and it is a stunning presentation with ice cream.
Is there a substitute for dates? Not my favorite… If I use apples it isn’t the same dessert and there would be liquid to contend with. Anyone have thoughts?
@Woodrose O I wonder if you use another dry fruit to replace the dates? Like cherries, plums, raisins?  Something squishy like dates. My kids think they don’t like dates.

@Carolyn

I promise your kids won’t even know there are dates in this dish. I loved sticky toffee pudding for YEARS before I knew the main ingredient was dates. I was floored when I found out.

@Carolyn. You won’t taste the dates at all. After my son fell in love with sticky toffee pudding in Britain, I searched for recipes to make it at home. I was shocked to see that the recipes included dates! I would never have guessed based on the taste. I was also afraid my son wouldn’t like dates, so searched for dateless recipes. They do exist, but are nearly as good as the ones with dates. And your kids will have no idea.
What is “date molasses”, and is there an acceptable substitute?
Where in the heck does one buy date molasses.  I never heard of it.
I did some poking around on the interweb tubes and date molasses is the juice from steeped dates.  I think I'll substitute with maple syrup.
Pretty sure the date molasses is the dates mushed in water at the beginning of the recipe.
A question. If you prep ahead and refrigerate, should it be brought to room temp before cooking?

Since I am not alone in asking: What is Date Molasses? Why don't the editors explain to us readers why they are using an ingredient we must buy in an Middle Eastern market to make a decidedly English pudding? Few of us have Middle Eastern markets.

@Kim Hurtle
It is only four TBS.  I assume Lyle’s syrup, molasses, maple syrup, ginger syrup or even chocolate syrup would all be fine.  The flavor will be a bit different with different syrups.  I will make with Lyle’s syrup the first time because I have some I need to use.  If I like this recipe as written I will definitely try subbing cherries for dates and using cherry syrup.
I just Googled Date Molasses and found they have it at Whole Foods and WalMart. Not so exotic.
Use one half cup sugar total.

I had such high hopes while putting this together -- love Ottolenghi! the sour cream, the date syrup, complex! -- but it came out like bland bread pudding with some caramel-ish mud on the bottom. Maybe I should have pulled it out sooner? The description of "self-saucing" and "bubbling" made me expect something saucy, but it was just overly sweetened okay cake with some nuts on top.
Any thoughts on halving this recipe?  Not sure if the 2 of us need a whole 9x13, and if it is best served out of the oven….
Good, but nowhere as good as sticky toffee pudding. I would make that instead. If you do make this, purée the date/water mixture.
And this is an easy recipe and will save me time???
Is it possible to use prepackaged date paste instead of the mashed date + water combo?
This recipe is way too complicated and time consuming for a holiday. I had Lebanese pomegranate molasses.
Used what we had which was walnuts instead of pecans and pomegranate molasses instead of date - I’m sure the date would have added a nice flavor but ours came out not too sweet and tasty

Super easy recipe—I found the date syrup at my very average grocery store and indeed just…poured the water over the raw batter. Decent result—my cake was gooey underneath (seemingly underdone, in fact) not on top, with good flavor. I’d sub regular salt for flaked—I had a few unpleasant, super salty bites. All around an ok dessert, but nothing to write home about. And the next day, my “seemingly underdone” cake was almost inedibly dry, so definitely plan to eat it [all] same day!

Delicious. Not as sweet as I thought it would be. It was a challenge to find date molasses in rural Alaska, but I did! Halved the recipe with no problems. Do use the sour cream. It adds just the right compliment.

This was absolutely amazing.  Followed the recipe exactly except that I couldn't find dark brown sugar in France, so I used a brown sugar that was a little lighter but very aromatic.  The result was a light moist texture with crunchy top and a pudding-like sauce on the bottom.  The salt gave it a caramel-like taste. Date syrup was a wonderful discovery.  Served with creme fraiche instead of sour cream, mixed with the date syrup.  One of the best desserts I've made--everyone raved.

Fantastic recipe.  I made this exactly as it is written.  Not too sweet and it has a custardy texture.  For the person asking if you have to pour off the water that you soak the dates in, no you do not.

Soak the dates in 3/4 cup of boiling water then mash together after 10 mins of soaking. Pour the other 2 cups of boiling water over everything before putting in the oven.  Don’t use blackstrap molasses… tastes too earthy. Maybe maple syrup would be a better sub!

Added some dried figs to the dates and “mashed” them up together in the Cuisine Art. Worked great, and tasted great! I made this at the last minute for a Christmas dessert and all the eaters at my house were so pleased!

Okay folks: this one is a winner! Absolute hit. Instead of just sour cream, I whipped up 1 cup heavy cream, 2 tablespoons of sour cream, 1/8 teaspoon of bourbon infused vanilla and 1/4 cup of brown sugar.  Whip everything but the sour cream first, to soft peaks, then add the sour cream to firm peaks. LAWWWD!
This is soooo good! I made it last night for Christmas dinner.  Everyone had seconds.  BTW, I added zest of 1 orange and upped the pecans from 3/4 cup to 1 cup.   Deeelish!
Has anyone tried to substitute cane sugar or grain flours for almond flour or coconut sugar and was it possible?
I was not really happy with this recipe. The cake was dry. I cooked it according to instructions. I will not make it again. I thought for sure it would be beautifully moist but it was not

Just a side note: anyone wondering if they should invest in a bottle of date syrup/molasses to try this pudding should know that it is delicious drizzled over Greek yogurt instead of honey. I like it better, in fact.
Interesting depth of flavor; delicious and luscious when served straight from the oven; definitely best eaten on day it is made. Huge quantity suitable for a crowd.

I made this a day ahead, as noted in the recipe, then baked it for Christmas Eve dinner.  My guests were amazed when I poured the hot water over the batter; "Magic!" they said.  
A few notes:  
Be very careful with the nutmeg and cloves; can get bitter if using too much.  
It was delicious hot, but even better the next day when the spices had melded.  
Sweetness level is high for our tastes; next time I will cut down the brown sugar to about 3/4 cup but keep date syrup and molasses the same.

One of the best things I have made from NYT! The family went back for thirds. I subbed in Bob's 1-1 gluten free flour and it worked perfectly. I added 1/2 c extra nuts because it looked skimpy and I love nuts. I was nervous about needing a high-sided 9x13 pan; I just used my normal-sided, pyrex 9x13 casserole pan and put a tray underneath in case of drips but it was fine. No drips. This was truly excellent and will be made again

I made this with the requisite date molasses yesterday. Not convinced it makes a huge difference. Given all the dates and brown sugar, I think leaving it out completely is fine. 
Otherwise, we really enjoyed it. Don't skip the sour cream. It adds a nice counterpoint to the cake's sweetness.
I made this exactly as written for Christmas dinner. It served 16 (generously) and was a huge hit. Tasty, festive, warm. Loved it!

I made this to follow Easter lunch for a group that had also tried STP from BBC Good Food (imho the perfect STP). This was very good but to me the other was better because it absorbed more of the caramel sauce and was more decadent however some of us preferred this version which had more distinct separation of light cake with pockets of sauce. It’s simpler and quicker to make and it was definitely a crowd pleaser. Cream swirled with date syrup on the side is a must for balance of flavours.
I added a little less sugar than in the recipe – came out amazing! Five stars, can't recommend this enough.
Another "what is a substitute for Date Molasses"?  I have pomegranate molasses, would that work???
pomegranate molasses will be too tart.  regular molasses tastes closer to date syrup/ date molasses.
Is there a nut-free alternative to the pecans or can they simply be omitted?
Can you suggest a different spice mix besides pumpkin?
Nothing, and I mean NOTHING, is better than Ina Garten’s Sticky Toffee Date Cake! To die for, especially over the last piece!!
I think it would benefit mightily from the addition of cloves, cinnamon, and nutmeg, which are staples in traditional STP.
@Stephen Martin just saw the spice recipe includes.
Can I substitute plain goat or sheep’s yogurt for the sour cream? ( I have to avoid cow’s milk) Is there a good nondairy substitute for the sour cream?

@Jenna
I had the same question and concluded anything tangy and creamy, sort of dairy-seque, should work.

@Jenna
I have used tofu sour cream in a similar recipe with good results and will try it for this recipe too.
Thank you thank you for listing weights! I hate measuring by volume when baking.
This for dessert tomorrow night but subbing maple syrup for date syrup
This looks amazing, can I substitute something for the dates?  I don't eat dates but everything else looks amazing

TY for the do-ahead tip! About the boiling water on top. I grew up with a "brownie pudding" like this & was pleased to find a yellow cake, berry version called "Ozark pudding". These are both surely much lighter than the toffee one.
To all those asking about pouring boiling water over cake batter, yes pour over top and don’t mix!! It will combine with the sugar and syrup to form a yummy sauce underneath the cake.
Ingredients call for brown sugar but when reading the recipe it calls for “sugar”, got me, but only for the amount that went into the bottom of the pudding.
Does it matter what kind of dates?  Deglet Noor  or Medjool?
I buy date syrup ( same as date molasses ) at Whole Foods. It is not a one recipe ingredient. I think cooks will find it has many uses
Trader Joe's sometimes has date syrup
Can eggs be replaced or eliminated?
I hate to say this, I haven’t tried this recipe yet but the silver palate cookbook has a date nut pudding recipe to die for, no date molasses though. (& I’m sure this recipe is delish also.)
I wonder if Chinese 5 spice would substitute for the pumpkin spice?

I decided to make this, partly based upon the ease of obtaining date syrup at Trader Joe’s. Alas, it has been discontinued. No worries, Date Lady date syrup is available at Walmart for $10, compared with $14 on Amazon.
Found date syrup at Whole Foods, BTW.
Cake part needs to be light, mine was a bit stodgy, so pay attention to your folding to get the air in!
How far in advance can do you think you can prep this prior to cooking?
I’m just wondering how high sided does the baking dish need to be.  Would a regular Pyrex baking dish be OK or does it require higher sides than that?
If you were to double the recipe in a 10 x 15 Pyrex baking pan, would you pour 4 cups of boiling water over the mix?  Seems like a lot!  My instinct says 3 cups.  Anyone want to weigh in?
I didn’t love this. Sticky Toffee Pudding is a more decadent dessert in the same vein as this but way better.

Broke with at least four generations of “date pudding” (with pecans) which I never liked as a child or as an adult. THIS is what I wanted the whole time. Not too sweet, no icky chopped dates. This is so delicious! I did skip the pumpkin spice and used two kinds of cinnamon, some nutmeg and cardamom. Also used lightly sweetened whipped cream. I had two helpings just now and would eat a third if it wasn’t embarrassing.

Went against at least four generations of “date pudding” (with pecans). I never liked the family recipe — too sweet and with icky date chunks. THIS is what I wanted all along. So delicious! I’d eat a third helping if it wasn’t embarrassing. Skipped the pumpkin spice and used two kinds of cinnamon, some cardamom and nutmeg. Used some lightly sweetened whipped cream to top. Sooooooo good! See you next year, new  Christmas dessert!

For the work that this took me on Christmas day, I found this dessert to be really disappointing. Too much pumpkin spice mix I think, and the sour cream topping was gross. I’m going to look for a traditional sticky toffee pudding recipe with vanilla ice cream next time I serve something like this to guests.

Enjoyed it a lot, also the refrigerated leftovers, both cold (cake like) and microwaved (soft and custardy).  Modifications:  Half recipe, ample for 4 adults.  No salt and Hain no sodium baking powder.  Less sugar on top (about half, the full amount looked way too much).   I used a round deep baking dish, about 8 inch diameter (7 on bottom, 8.5 at top) and the cake rose. Fine with or without the sour cream.  Date molasses is also called date syrup, found it at my Albertsons.
Loved this recipe .. I had already used every pan I had for Thanksgiving dishes so used a large cast iron pan --- it looked amazing.
Any tips for making this a day ahead?

Worked beautifully with maple syrup. I’ll try date molasses next time but no one complained! I added sliced dates to the top with the pecans for a more in your face datey flavor and added an extra teaspoon of the spice mix as we like bold flavors. Topped with ginger ice cream, but a good vanilla would have been fantastic too. Wonderful dessert that I can’t wait to make again.
would suspect this could be done easily with dried figs also

title: Cabbage and Mandarin Orange Winter Slaw Recipe
author: Yotam Ottolenghi

comments.commentBody:
Murkat lime leaves (aka lime leaf or kaffir lime) have no substitute.  Their flavor is so distinctive but is an acquired taste. It is easy to grow.  Order it on line and grow it in a pot so it can be brought 
in in the winter.  That way you will always have fresh.  I add it to most Thai recipes even if not called for.

@Alice Waters
Have you ever used dried makrut lime leaves? Do you think I could substitute them in this slaw recipe?
@Alice Waters This is the way!

Made this exactly as written in the recipe (lime leaves and all) and here are my take away: 
* I would leave the lime out of the slaw as it makes the slaw VERY lime-y. It almost over power it
* Definitely don't slice the lime (skin & pith) as it is a bit bitter
* A better substitute with the lime might be to add just the zest and juice of the lime to wilt the slaw
* Slaw is definitely much better the next day. It really needs time for the ingredients to meld together
How would one treat an extra lime to substitute it for Makrut lime leaves in the dressing as Ottolenghi suggests? Slice it up? Squeeze it? Zest it?
I would be inclined to zest it.
@Kelsey, since he didn’t say otherwise, I would treat the extra lime the same as the other limes in the recipe — it would go in the food processor with the other limes.
Boyajian lime oil is a better substitute for makrut lime leaves. A few drops is all that is needed.
Still waiting on the lime leaf substitution response...
Lime zest can replace
lime leaves give a special and different aroma and taste. Try to find them and put the unused ones in the freezer. Definitely worth the effort.

Yotam's salads have never, ever let me down.  I can't believe I've never thought of putting cumin in a  slaw, it's so obvious now that he's said it.  This all looks marvelous to my slaw-loving self, but I'm sorry, the pith between a lime's flesh and peel is just too bitter and unpleasant  for me to include it.  I'll be using only the lime zest, and following the rest of it exactly, especially the innovative dressing technique.

I'm confused about people asking about the extra lime substitute. It's pretty obvious if you're reading the entire recipe! Instead of slicing the 1 lime (skin and all) into the slaw, you're now slicing 2 limes (skin and all) into the slaw. You're doing this instead of the 8 lime leaves in the oil.

for context: "Makrut lime leaves add an extraordinary citrusy aroma with a slightly floral note that makes the slaw truly special. If you can’t find them, substitute with an extra fresh lime."
He never said to sub the lime for the lime leaves IN the dressing. The sentence is about the SLAW portion.

I agree with Kevin T that the lime should be prepped the same as the other lime. But I assume the additional lime slices should be used in Step 1 in a manner similar to the Makrut leaves (i.e., saute in oil with spices, and then strained to become part of the toasted spice mix).

I’ve carefully read the recipe and agree with SusanW that the substitute for the  makrut lime leaves, a very fresh lime, belongs to prepping the oil. Because the makrut lime leaves belong in the oil. A one-for-one substitution. I would finely chop a small lime (you could thinly slice, then chop) and add this to the oil. Be aware that it will cause the oil to splatter. I’d probably go for 45+ seconds to offset that effect. It will slightly crisp the lime bits, adding a nice textural element.
I added pomegranate seeds- made it prettier and tasty.
needed more of a dressing, so I added a bit of fig vinegar. That worked well with the fennel and orange, and made it a wonderful slaw.

It is important to source the round, thin skin limes with very little pith. The type with thick bumpy skins (and knobs at both ends) have way too much bitter pith for this dish. Both the fennel and cabbage, thinly sliced, are core flavor and textural elements. As others noted, it was strange to add only oil, but over the hour between prep and eating, the lime slices infused the salad and balanced it out. Pick those lime slices out if you have leftovers! It was way too strong by the next day.

This was absolutely fantastic - I made it the day before to let it marinate in the dressing, and everything got more tasty and crisp on day 2 and 3. I used lime leaves from the freezer, ground cumin instead of whole, skipped the coriander seeds (didn't want to buy more ingredients), and juiced and zested the lime instead of slicing into the slaw. I added a fifth mandarin since it looked a little sparse, but after it all wilted down I think four would have been the right number.

I agree with the comment about bitterness from the lime pith - if I make this again I'll zest the lime and then juice it, though that adds some extra steps!   Might also tweak the ration of fennel to cabbage, the fennel flavor was not very noticeable.  The cumin flavor was nice with the sweet and tart citrus flavors.
Substitute 1 tablespoon lime juice and 1/2 teaspoon grated lime peel for each lime leaf, so says the internet, and I have used this substitute and it works fine.
The dressing for this slaw is Indian tadka - oil infused with spices of your choice. Tadka is a very potent culinary technique & can be used to add oomph over soups, stews, pizza or even nachos
If you add both the oil and the spices to the salad, why do you need to separate them by draining?
@AnnaConda because otherwise would keep cooking in the hot oil off the stove and thus burn
I'm thinking lemongrass is the best substitute for kafir lime leaves.

I loved this recipe, but not as is.  The lime skin peel was hard to bite into in the salad.  I will zest the lime next time, and peel the lime before slicing.  Also, I did not want to deal with straining the oil, so used ground coriander instead of whole seeds, regular cumin seeds, no makrut lime leaves as I did not have them on hand, and poured all of that over the slaw.
Thought the sliced lime with skin was inedible. Maybe if shredded with the food processor versus slicing. Also thought the fennel pieces were too big. Would shred that also
This sounded like it would be delicious, but in actuality, it was a boring coleslaw, almost as if the flavors cancelled each other out.
Made it without the makrut leaves and no substitute (couldn't find them at two places I checked).  No problem!  The slaw is still super delicious.
I used key limes - little pith, mellower flavor
It was good. Lively mix of flavors. Question: are we eating those lime slices, along with the rest of the salad? One too many for us. :)

I did not have makrut lime leaves, so I used two fresh limes without skin for the total recipe.  However, the pith was still on and they made the salad much too bitter ( sugar came to the rescue.)  I suggest using only juice of lime, to taste.
I made it exactly as the recipe said, with fresh makrut leaves and all, but something seemed missing.  With  all those wonderful ingredients, we expected much more flavor.  Disappointing.
Where did you buy fresh makrut leaves? TY.

Loved this one. I added some yellow carrot which I cut into chunks and simmered in butter, water and the coriander and fennel seeds in lieu of making the spiced oil. I didn’t have scallions so I added crispy onions which I had in a jar, as well as some chopped roasted almonds for crunch. Dill and parsley work just as well as cilantro.

I didn’t have some of the ingredients so I used the recipe as a jumping off point for making a cabbage salad with what I had. No lime leaves, no fennel…my salad had the gorgeous oil and seeds on red cabbage and slivered kale, plus the scallions, one lime, the mandarins and lots of fresh dill. Different but so good!
Very nice.  Needs more acid, like orange vinegar at the end.

Unable to obtain the Makrut lime leaves, I simply thinly sliced the rind in sheets off a Makrut lime I was able to purchase. It seemed to work as an adequate substitute. The taste was superb, but very citrusy. I hope this helps.
Could I try this with shaved Brussels sprouts instead of the cabbage? Thinking Thanksgiving.

Yes, Brussels sprouts could be used in the place of cabbage in any salad. Also there is a Roasted And Raw Brussels Sprouts recipe by Susan Spungen here in NYT Cooking that is meant for Brussels sprouts and is amazing.

Good idea! Of course you can use Brussels sprouts--or red cabbage or Savoy cabbage or maybe even bok choy leaves. Make it the way you want, and, if you're worried, try a test run with a small batch before Thanksgiving.
How are the cilantro leaves used ?
@eChristine, See Step 2.

Could not find lime leaves …substitute: mostly fresh mint and a bit of fresh basil …made about 1/3 of the portions, for 2 …very delicious…thank
you…

Actively unpleasant, leaves a bad taste in the mouth.  Way too much cumin, only used half the sliced lime, a fresh lime with thin skin cut very thinly, still the salad is bitter, not in a good way. I found nothing to suggest it would be worth reworking.
Appreciate Waters' suggestion but how does this work for apartment dwellers? I can only seem to find dry leaves on line. How does that work here? Soften in water, then remove the central stem?

Used 3/4 tsp ground of each of the spices instead of seeds. This was a hit at a recent potluck. I had to barter with a neighbor for the leaves and was gifted thin skinned limes. Delicious. They have a bright glavor.
Whew!  Didn't have the lime leaves, so used the extra lime. Waaay too sour to eat. I'll try adding honey or maybe dried cherries, but I definitely won't be making this as suggested again.
Are lime leaves actually edible? My impression always was that they get fished out of curries, unless it’s just a very few slivers.

I was scared of skin-on limes, but it worked great as recommended:wedges then thin slices from a food processor. Though next time I’d do six or eight wedges because the slices were a little too big to distribute the flavor through the salad as I’d like.
A little bland, but still tasty. Toasted walnuts, pistachios, or pumpkin seeds might be good to add.

I made this salad for company last weekend. There are no makrut leaves to be found in our medium-sized city, so I thinly sliced two limes instead of one and put in salad, as the recipe suggests. Not many people, myself included, find raw lime peel tasty. So I scoured the salad and removed all the limes. My guests liked, but didn’t love, the salad. Because I think this salad has potential, I read the comments and ended up adding zest and the juice of two limes instead of the makrut and raw lime rind. And it is delicious! I did everything else as written except I was short green cabbage, so supplemented with red cabbage, which worked well.
Where does one find lime leaves?

I've made this twice; first time as it was written. The second time I used naval orange supremes instead of mandarins, I cut the supremes over the bowl of slaw to capture the orange juice and cut each segment roughly into smaller pieces. I also thought the lime was too harsh the first time, so this time I cut and sliced the limes and sprinkled them lightly with sweetener (I used allulose but regular sugar would be fine). The additional sweetness was welcome in the dish, in my opinion.
Is there a good substitute for fennel? Just leave a out?
apparently leek or celery. I would assume that if you can't get a fennel bulb, you can maybe add some fennel seeds to the toasted spice.
@Lo: My internet search turned up Bok Choy as the best sub for an uncooked recipe like this one. Another possibility is celery.

It's clear that some people are rating this recipe poorly because they don't have one particular ingredient and can't figure out a substitute. It's a shame because Amazon can deliver this ingredient to just about anywhere in the world within 2 days.
I was able to find dried makrut lime leaves. Any idea how they might work?

I used dried makrut lime leaves (that had been in my freezer so long they were totally dried out) and they worked great following the recipe as written (I fried on low heat a little longer than 30 seconds). Very fragrant and very pleasantly crisp.
Can this dish be prepared today (Tuesday) for Thanksgiving Day?  Thank you.
What do you think of using Napa cabbage. I still have a bunch from my CSA?

This is delicious. We have makrut lime leaves at the local grocery. I peeled and then rough- chopped the lime for no pith. I didn't add the toasted spice mix to the cabbage; even though it cooked for only 30 secs, it tasted burnt. Really love this recipe and will make again! I used a cheesecloth to separate the oil from the spices.
This looks good apart from leaving the skin on the lime slices.. no matter how thinly they’re sliced.
8 Kafir lime leaves are overpowering. ONE at the most unless you want to ruin the dish.
What kind of cabbage? Napa? Savoy? Fe like that matters hugely.

The recipe is excellent, exactly as is. It is worth the effort to source Market (Kaffir) lime leaves; once you find them, they freeze well and you can use the frozen defrosted leaves in this recipe. Don't worry about bitterness from the lime; if you slice it thinly enough, it's not an issue and adds depth.

If you anticipate leftovers, store the slaw mixture before adding the oil, crispy bits, or mandarins. Just add those in and mix when you're ready to eat.
The oil didn’t taste good so ditched the recipe and added yogurt. I ground coriander and cumin…I served it with oven roasted chicken and yellow beats and it was really good…

Followed recipe. Kaffir lime leaves are a huge component of the taste profile. I loved this salad. Slicing the lime super thin is important. 
I would not bother making g this salad if not using kaffir lime leaves.

Made this twice, mostly as written.  But I do recommend crushing the coriander seed vs leaving them whole.  And my family enjoyed the added citrus of a small sweet grapefruit.  Next time I will have procured the lime leaves and can re assess that addition.

Loved the slightly bitter tang of the lime skin.  Do not omit!  Added splash of rice wine vinegar (cuz I’m in love with the sassiness!) Used ruby red grapefruit, Napa cabbage, added dash of honey and a diced sweet red bell for color. Delicious and a keeper.  We had with quick stir fry of snap peas, asparagus and thin sliced leftover rib eye with toasted sesame seeds. Perfect summer dinner in this heat on our ‘low carb’ day.

This was basically inedible - the only item we didn’t give was the lime leaves, but I doubt that would have saved it. We had to add mayo and sugar to make it remotely palatable. Only then were there moments of flavor bursts, especially with the mandarin, which I found pleasant, but on balance the lime and coriander rendered it excessively bitter.

As others have commented, I found this slaw to have potential but to be a bit lacking in flavor. I found it was considerably improved by adding 2-3 tablespoons each of sesame oil and rice vinegar, as well as a dash more salt. Topped it with some sliced almonds for additional crunch and a rounder flavor profile. Next time I will try skipping the EVOO and cooking the herbs straight in the sesame oil.
And definitely let this marinate for as long as feasible. It is much better than the next day.
I made it with green but I think Napa or Savoy would be better. @virago
This was excellent! Very fresh! Used zest and juice of 2 limes instead of the leaves and slicing 1 lime into the salad.

title: Butternut, Tamarind and Coconut Stew Recipe
author: Tara Wigley

comments.commentBody:
I was drawn to this recipe because of many of the ingredients, but I was a little apprehensive because there were no reviews.  I’m thrilled to be the first one to give this five stars.  Processed the onions and the jalapeños in a food processor which might’ve meant it took longer for the onions to brown (30  mins) or maybe I didn’t have it hot enough or maybe it was because I triple the recipe), but you definitely need to wait until the onions. I opted to use coconut oil.  

The processed onions give the stew a whisper of a soup texture, which I loved.  Use my own chili powder from Guatemala, which is spicier than regular chili powder so I used about a third less of what the recipe called for and the spice level ended up perfect.  The only ingredient I couldn’t find was jaggery, so substituted it with piloncillo.  

Other than that, I followed the directions to a T and it’s a big big hit in my house!
Profoundly undelicious. Just a sour bath of tamarind with chunks of squash. A ton of time chopping and stewing resulting in a ziploc filled with next week’s trash. Literally make anything else.

It would seem that you either:
Left out the sweetening ingredient (jaggery or muscovado sugar), or more likely:
Used the wrong tamarind concentrate. See tip in the recipe.

It’s necessary to taste as you go.

I was uncertain of the tamarind product on hand, so mixed in a quarter of the amount called for into the hot water. Stirred well, and added about half of that to the stew. As it was heading in the right direction, I added the rest. Tasted and good. Cooked.

Fantastically delicious! Five *
I usually add tamarind at the end of the cooking process, I find things get bitter if you add the tamarind paste in too early.

Fresh curry leaves are going to be hard to find, for many in the US. It would have been nice if the recipe included substitutes. An online search came up with bay leaves, lime zest and leaves, basil, and lemon balm. Does anyone know what is best?
Indian and Asian markets often have fresh curry leaves e.g., subzi mandi, ranch 99, hmart.

Only 1 UK supermarket (not "Asian") sells them. But all supermarkets sell dried which is the best substitute for the real thing (allbeit not the same flavour but better than omitting them completely). I also read you can "create" a curry leaf tree easily enough if you have a garden (yard?) of course...

During pandemic the supply of fresh curry leaves disappeared from local markets. My workaround was dried curry leaves ordered online. I found that I had to roughly double the number of leaves compared to fresh or frozen ones, but when those were not available the dried ones were better than any other substitute.
You can find frozen curry leaves in an Indian store which actually retain good flavor.
This has all the makings of a south Indian curry - tamarind, curry leaves, mustard seeds. I fail to understand why you can’t use an Indian tamarind.
Adding tamarind before the squash is softened is not a good idea. Sourness prevents vegetables breaking down.

Like some people mentioned here, the type of tamarind you use is going to be the key. If you use something like Indian tamarind, it will be extremely sour. You will need to reduce the quantity drastically. So start with a few tablespoons first and balance it with jaggery or palm sugar or whatever sweetener you are using. If you like it spicy, a spicier chilli powder will balance it very nicely.
Curry leaves have a very distinct flavor that has no substitute. One can, of course,  use something else, but it will not be the same.

I haven't made it yet but this is the first recipe I've seen that calls for the weak-tea tamarind paste instead of soaking a lump of the pulp in hot water and then straining out the seeds and solids. Given that it's from the great Yotam Ottolenghi I'll go with it but...
Would coconut sugar work as a substitute for jaggery?

@Stephanie
I think coconut sugar IS Jaggeri.  Sri Lankan cooks call it that.

My family and guests had much the same reaction to this as I do to many of Mr. Ottolenghi's recipes. Many ingredients, much chopping and prepping but then the results are just - meh.
I love Middle-Eastern and Asian food and probably have every spice under the sun. I had all the ingredients to hand.  I'm a good cook - Cordon Bleu trained many, many moons ago - I just find his recipes and their results quite tedious.
However, his recipe for charred cherry tomatoes with yoghurt is just marvelous.

I couldn't agree more.  1000 steps using tough to find ingredients (and I have a huge pantry of indian and asian spices), lots of prep for dishes that are ok but not worth the effort.  It's almost as if he wants to make it as complicated as possible.
@CB.  Haven’t delved to much into OL recipes, but this recipe is very straightforward to prepare. And the ingredients are pantry staples…just not in the US.
Exotic spices, wrestling with the squash, a bit of cooking fussiness and it comes out way too sweet.  Won't bother with it again.

Followed the recipe to a T and it was truly 5 stars!  I used 5 peeled Mexican tamarind pods and we picked out the seeds as we ate.  Made the recipe again with tamarind pulp (we were warned not to) and it was meh — my portion went to the trash.  Lesson?  Follow the recipe!

This recipe is excellent and interesting. For the skeptics reading the negative reviews, here's what you need to do to ensure good results:

1. Make sure you give the onions the full 25 minutes in the pan
2. Like with many NYT recipes, trust your taste buds, rather than the recipe in terms of spices, garlic and salt. I doubled the amount of all spices (grind them fresh if you can), used a couple extra cloves garlic, and salted throughout the process, starting with the onions. This is KEY.

Second one to give this 5 stars! I halved the recipe since I had a small butternut squash, thrilled I have a portion leftover to enjoy tomorrow.
I used frozen curry leaves, Kashmiri chilli powder for the chilli powder, and tomato powder in lieu of paprika which I did not have.
This was utterly delicious exactly as written. I'm already looking forward to making it again soon.

Tamarind flavor was too overpowering (fyi, I used Neera’s tamarind concentrate). Needs something to stand up to the tang of the tamarind—maybe something smoky (dried chile)? If you figure it out, let me know!

Did not pay attention to the amount of coconut milk, and put the whole can in, A very nice thick soup of a much paler color than the picture resulted. Used delicata squash because that’s what I had, and substituted a teeny bit of brown sugar for jaggery. I have a curry plant on my porch as it’s not easy to come by around here, so was fortunate I had all the ingredients otherwise. We loved it. Agree with others that food processor cuts down on time, and don’t skip browning the onions. It does take time. One regret is that I wish I had saved this for the weekend, as squash takes a long time to cook —so not great for a weeknight dinner.

This is an interesting change of pace that is unusual for my western palette but still tasty.  If you are using Thai tamarind paste that comes in a dark block, you need to add the step of straining out the pulp with a metal sieve after mixing with boiling water. I used a little over half of the resulting paste and that was flavor enough. I used just 1 tablespoon of brown sugar instead of and it was still pretty sweet. I hit up the Indian market for fresh curry leaves.

i have cooked well over 200 Yotam recipes since 2008 and this recipe (followed via the Comfort cookbook as it is more accurate) is probably the most unsuccessful one yet. Do not use any tamarind paste like the note suggests. even the Thai paste makes this recipe bitter, tasteless, and very unattractive.

Made this tonight after reading all the comments. It is a lovely recipe. I did add a bit more coconut milk and cooked it longer. I do think it will be better the 2nd day as my butternut squash took way longer to cook and not even.

I followed the recipe, using the ingredients called for, and found it delicious and not at all soupy or soup-like. Much of the dish self-cooks, so not a lot of effort (beyond sourcing ingredients). I might add some pan toasted cashews (unsalted) to top it and add a zap of protein.

Made this pretty much true to the recipe except for the suggested substitution of dark sugar for jaggery and it tasted brilliant.

A possible problem would be the variation in tamarind pastes available (maybe they're less intense in the UK?) so I did end up tasting, re-tasting and re-tasting as I added the sugar to get the sweet-sour balance exactly as I wanted. In the end I needed slightly less sugar than recommended.
What brand did you use? I I’m UK too, and haven’t a good one

Wow this is fantastic.  I did have to cook on simmer for an hour after adding the coconut milk, as my squash pieces were probably too big.  The tamarind I used was Swad Concentrate - light brown in color and in a glass jar.  I have a heavy hand with spices - usually doubling everything, and I love, love curry leaves.  I did not add the jaggery, as the squash is at peak flavor this time of year.
It was absolutely delicious.
I used Trader Joe’s tamarind sauce and didn’t need the sugar. It turned out really well

I loved it.  I had everything on hand except the curry leaves - and my local market carries them.
(What can I say, I make a lot of Indian and Asian meals so my pantry is deep.  I will make this with other main ingredients - Paneer, chickpeas, chicken, shrimp.  If you do your mis en place ahead of time it’s not that much work.  Again - loved it!
The Tamarind "tip" is essential, not a tip.

I made this exactly according to the recipe and it was more soup than stew. Looked nothing like the picture. Tasted it and it and got nothing but tamarind. Simmered off the water to end up with a rather disappointing mess of grey, tamarind flavoured squash.
Not his best.

This recipe calls for far too much tamarind concentrate, it was overpowering (yes, I bought the correct kind). I balanced it out with a tbsp more of  jaggery, 1tbsp creamy peanut butter and 1/2c. more coconut milk and a 1/2 c vegetable broth. If I made this again, I'd use a scant 1/4 c, but there are better recipes in my NYT recipe box using tamarind correctly. Don't think I'd make this again.
I love all the ingredients here separately, but this just didn't work for me. It was ok- nothing special.
Stunning. I will make this again and again (as written).
Why not the Indian tamarind concentrate? Too sweet and not tangy enough? Can I add mango powder to add the tang?
I used grape leaves
I made it as the recipe called for (except substituting sugar for jaggery) and it was extremely delicious. Don’t pass it up.

The first time I made the recipe it was absolutely 5 stars!  I used 5 peeled Mexican tamarind pods and we picked out the seeds as we ate.  The second time I used tamarind pulp and it went straight to the trash.  Both times I skipped the curry leaves.

This was quite good.  It was a really unique, different way to use butternut squash.  I made this pretty much as instructed, except I used coconut sugar in lieu of jaggery and I didn't have curry leaves so I just omitted.  I don't think this is satisfying enough to stand as a main dish, but it was great as a side!

Ans for S: "What brand did you use? I’m UK too, and haven’t a good one"

I used TRS tamarind concentrated paste (an asian brand available on Amazon etc) which is easy enough to use and adjust to taste . 

Next time around, I'm going to use Natco tamarind pulp to make my own paste which is a bit of a faff but can be frozen and it's something that keeps cropping up in recipes

In order to make the recipe a bit more streamlined, I roasted the butternut squash alongside making the onions (I gave the onions 18 minutes on my stove.) 

As the squash was already mostly cooked, when I mixed the tamarind, water and squash, I only needed to cook that for 1-2 minutes. 

I used a Thai tamarind concentrate, which worked just fine. For curry leaves - I used dried ones. 
I didn’t need to add the sugar, it was tangy and not sour. 

Yes, i adapted the recipe, but it worked well, and tasted delicious!

Went to the store before I read the “Tip” about tamarind and came back with Tamicon, which (I think?) is the one that the chef warns about.  I think the one that I used to find at the store when making tamarind-raisin chutney was the southeast Asian kind that the chef prefers in this context.  Looking back at Yamuna Devi’s chutney recipe it looks she may have preferred Tamicon (she mentions it by name).  Whatever, I reduced the tamarind.  Meh.   I might make again with the right tamarind… or not

Definitely read the note on the tamarind - I almost bought the wrong kind and at least visually it was so different that I can’t believe it wouldn’t affect the taste. Now I’m glad I got a big jar (only size they had) so I can make it again! I skipped sugar at end (didn’t think it needed it) but otherwise followed closely and it was like eating at a restaurant - really happy I sought out ingredients that aren’t in my pantry normally.
I used Dragonfly tamarind concentrate - the importer is in California so available on west coast at least

I used an autumn frost squash, a slightly less sweet tasting butternut substitute, and a bit of sweet potato that was in the fridge -- fantastic! The coconut sugar I have as a brown sugar substitute was fine. I've never tasted jaggery or muscovado sugar (diabetic most of my life) so who knows, but the tamarind and sweet were nicely balanced. Crowd favourite.

title: Smoky Shrimp Saganaki Recipe
author: Yotam Ottolenghi

comments.commentBody:
This is a great dish and made even more special with the dried peppers that melt into the sauce. I followed the recipe closely, but did add more cooking time for my tomatoes. I also added some white beans in the first step because we were hungry! 
I was a skeptic, but there’s plenty of flavor and depth. Still confused about the garlic cloves for serving but we managed. 
I served a black lime focaccia from Ottolenghi Test kitchen cookbook which went surprisingly well. Delicious!
@Linda About the garlic: When it says skin on for the whole cloves, how does that work in the final dish?

@Linda & @Sidetrack 
Garlic instructions are so unclear.  The ingredients call for one clove minced, and the Prep calls for crushed garlic.

The garlic cloves cooked in the baking dish are left in their skins.  You squeeze out the garlic as you eat the dish -- delicious.  It's one of my favorite ways to eat garlic.  

The single garlic clove is minced or "crushed" in a press and tossed with raw shrimp before you add them.

We just cook this and it was spectacular. To chime in about the garlic, once the cloves were in our bowls, we just squeezed the whole cooked clothes right out of the skin and ate them with the bread and the rest of the recipe. Superb.

This is exceptional. My wife described this as the "best meal"... and I kept waiting for a qualifier that didn't come. I used real Greek feta and good tomatoes, and it came together beautifully. I agree with the previous sentiments about handling the skin-on confit garlic. It's ambiguous as written and a bit of a hassle to eat that way. Next time, I'll remove the skins and just make sure they stay submerged in the oil along with the dried chilies -- easier to eat with marginal effect on flavor.

I don’t know the answer either about the garlic with the skin on it. I’m guessing it’s like when you usually roast a head of garlic, and then squeeze the contents of the individual cloves onto bread. I suppose,  in this case, you could also just squeeze the clove contents into your individual servings and give it a stir.

I made this for dinner last night. Added pearl onions and 2 sweet red peppers. Delicious!!  Hubby asked if we could have it again tonight but with lamb meatballs instead of shrimp. Once again,outstanding meal. Seasoned ground lamb with thyme, rosemary, salt, pepper, garlic. Small meatballs (undercooked) which I baked separately during the first 10 min bake and added to the pot for the second baking. Same additions as before plus 1 sliced jalapeno.  Easy, company worthy meal.

We made this for dinner tonight and it is fantastic.  Had to sub the dried peppers with other varieties but otherwise followed exactly. Absolutely worthy of company and takes about 10 minutes to put together.  Five stars!
Could you use powdered ancho and chipotle chiles?

This was STELLAR. It tastes like something you would get in a fancy tapas restaurant, but it would be 1/2 the size and cost 2x as much. I am wondering if the quantity of oil is a typo? I used about 1/4-1/3 cup and it was PLENTY of oil. I peeled the garlic, doubled the peppers as we like spicy, and doubled the shrimp, because why so little? When I added the cheese, I smashed the tomatoes a bit. Served with crusty bread, and my husband and I DEMOLISHED this. It was SO delicious. And so easy!

Unpeeled garlic.  To me, the peel keeps the garlic whole with no "melting" into the juices. It keeps everything from becoming too garlicky.  Plus, biting into that intact clove is an unexpected delight. I'll add more cloves next time I make this fabulous dish!!
Wonderful Flavor. Toned down chilies with poblano peppers. Agree with some others:  keep garlic in skin, cut oil in half. Easy to experiment with. Crusty bread perfect addition.
Loved loved loved this dish. So flavorful! Had none of these chile options available so used half of a habanero. Delicious. Will cook again often!

Of all the NYT recipes I’ve cooked thus far, this was the most enjoyable in both the simple preparation and the amazing shared experience of eating it. My bread came out particularly well which made the meal sublime. I changed absolutely nothing. Thanks!

Sadly this was WAY too acidic, oily, rich, and on the tippiest edge of too spicy, for my otherwise tolerant stable gut. 

The flavor *was* wonderful though...
Perhaps, as-is, as a small appetizer? Chopped up shrimp would made a fabulous dip.

IF we try it again, in the interest of swinging this into some semblance of healthy for us, I'd need to: 
⅛ the oil
½ the dried chiles
½ the feta
x2 the shrimp.
And serve like a melanzane salad- warm over greens.

Yeah, I just totally talked myself into it.

Long red chilies?
Screaming hot or softly mild?
I think that's up to you. My market has tons of local options right now so I picked a medium heat and it was great.
Dried chili not included on the ingredients list?
@Duke I think the first two listed are the dried chilies.
@Duke the chipotle and the ancho are both dried. The long red chilis are fresh.
The recipe does not state if/when you cook the garlic before you crush it- or if you crush it raw etc.
Garlic should not be cooked prior to adding it to the pan.
Excellent!  I used a mix of Roma and cherry tomatoes because that's what I had on hand, just increased the initial cooking time to 15 minutes.  Next time, I'll use more smoky hot peppers.

This was excellent. VERY rich; I'd made a salad to go with it and nobody could even eat a bite of that. Could serve 6+ IMO.

Notes: Due to market limitations, I subbed "California peppers" for chipotle and a jalopeno for the red. The amount of oil is called for, I believe, to cover the peppers until the tomatoes soften enough to create a sauce. Somehow, though, the sauce is not overly oily. Medium shrimp is the right size; I did a pound and then cleaned them.

This is tremendous and super easy! We all agreed it was one of the best things we've ever eaten. Thanks to early reviewers I gathered the chipotle and ancho chilis were dried, and I peeled the whole garlic cloves and then we just ate them with everything else. My only change was to use 1 LB of shrimp because I opened the bag and love shrimp so why not. A perfect end of summer meal with ingredients easily sourced year round. Heated the bread in the oven during the last 5 minutes of cooking.
If I were to use chipotle powder instead of the pepper, how much would you add?

I'm not much of a cook so please forgive my ignorance.  I'm guessing the dried chilies referred to in the cooking instructions are the chipotle and ancho, though the ingredients list doesn't specify they should be dried, though I suppose it's implied. Is that right?  Also, other than splitting the chilies to remove the seeds, do the chilies need to be cut up before being put into the cast-iron pan or baking dish for cooking? Finally, when serving, do you mix in the dried chilies with the rest?

Made a half recipe. Came out well but will add more shrimp next time. I also plan to slice the  cherry tomatoes in half. They didn't explode and add their juices to the chilies and garlic. The ancho chilies could have been torn into smaller pieces as well.

Delicious!
I would make a few changes next time, however. 3/4 cups of oil is wayyyyy too much for me. I had to tip off the oil when I served it.
Also... Either I'm an over-eater, or this really serves two people, not four. Actually, I could have eaten almost the whole thing myself. 
My husband called it restaurant-worthy and keeps declaring "This is so good!"

Made this for the first time last night. Keeping the recipe! 
- Tomatoes - I added 5 minutes to the first and second steps. 
- The unpeeled garlic - The idea is to squeeze the garlic out of the skin after they roast. But who can wait! We ended up just eating the skins - they were mostly soft at that point.(We also eat unpeeled shrimp tails so ...)
- Speaking of shrimp, we left it out and added cubed eggplant in the first step. So glad we did!
Did this with only one ancho chili and sadly cooked shrimp. It was still delicious and would be even better starting with fresh shrimp.
