Yep this was great. Didn’t need to butterfly the shrimp. Used basil bc that’s what we had fresh. Simple, quick, and great for casual entertaining!
I halved the feta and doubled the prawns, so good, so easy.

Having made over 500 NYTC recipes, this one is top ten.  I made it as written.  Crusty bread is a must for squeezing out the roasted garlic (this is why you don't peel the garlic).  Happy to pay more for domestic wild caught shrimp.

For those who have made this: 3/4 cup of oil seems like a lot--does it come out oily? Do you press the garlic cloces with your fork as you eat it to get the garlic out? I have questions but it sounds delicious.
@Aren, we added even a little bit more than 3/4 cup and it worked perfectly. Mix well with the water from the tomatoes and made a rich broth!

It does not come out oily, everything mixes to a perfect broth to dip your bread in and gather up the other ingredients. I just peeled the whole cloves before adding to the pan and they came out perfect with no fuss. They didn't get too melty or burnt and were just a happy addition to the mix.
Made exactly as recipe dictated however had to substitute a poblano and Serrano chili for the ancho and jalapeño. Used dried red chili peppers also and was perfect

This was fabulous! I added the can of white beans as someone recommended and skipped the bread. A great way to use up the late summer garden cherry tomatoes. I also used 1 lb of shrimp instead of 1/2. I had a little left over which I used the next night over grilled swordfish - too good to waste!
Did I miss something about oil.  Where does it say how much oil or does this refer to oil in the chilis?  Also which are dried chilies/which fresh?
Chipotle and ancho chiles are, by definition, dried chiles. (Jalapeño and poblano, respectively.)  One of the ingredients in the list says "3/4 cup extra-virgin olive oil.
“a handful of sliced shrimp that blush bashfully as they cook. “. This sentence by Melissa just made me smile in the best way.  I look forward to making this dish…Blushing shrimp and all.
I SO want to make this but hubby has a shellfish allergy.  What say you all about adding chunks of a sturdy-fleshed fish at the end?
Why not? I would substitute monkfish rather than cod or haddock as the fish has a good solid texture and definitely holds up well in stews.

Used 2 fresh poblano peppers, 2 fresh serrano peppers, 3 small- mid chipotle chiles in adobo, 1 tsp ancho chile powder. Added 1/8 more olive oil. Individual garlic cloves. Cooked 3x longer on 425. Left under broiler for 6 minutes. Stirred everything before serving, then added cilantro. Served on baguette slices I’d panini pressed. Soft sprinkle of Maldon salt on top. Yay.
It turned out great—very soupy. 3/4 c. Olive oil? Really? I’m pretty sure 1/4 c would be fine.
Loved this! Minor pepper swaps, ate the garlic skin and all. Might throw in a bunch of fresh spinach next time
Really tasty—and simple! Will certainly make it again, possibly doubling the recipe (I wanted seconds.)
Great flavor. What i would expect from a better quality of restaurant.

Great recipe! I took the skins off the garlic, used extra dried Chiles (NM and chipotle) and reconstituted then blended them beforehand so that it was evenly distributed throughout the dish. Would make again!

This was delicious! Only added half of each chile to cut down on potential heat. When it came time to add the shrimp, I also tossed in some cubed tofu I had lightly browned in oil for the non-shellfish eater at our table. Everyone loved it!

This was so good, the smoky Mexican peppers really WOW! I wouldn't change a thing. I don't think it's too oily if you use 24oz of tomatoes as long as there's enough oil to coat and cover the dried peppers. The peppers meld to contribute a lasting warmth and heat that's very pleasing but not overly spicy. The combination of smoky Mexican flavors with a Greek recipe is amazing.

This was easy and tasty, though a bit oily.  I used some head-on shrimp, just because I saw them at the fish market.  The shrimp heads in this garlic oil were pretty delicious, but imo not necessary. If I make this again, I will cut down on oil and try to add some sweet peppers.
This is so good it made my partner and I almost tear up. Served over pasta and it was divine! The smoky, simple sauce is a perfect fall meal. I used precooked shrimp and they worked just fine.
Have served this twice now once with a green salad, once with faro salad. And baguette. It needs a bit more heft for company, maybe beans or a more filling side salad.
Can one substitute hot house tomatoes for cherry tomatoes?
@Mark yeas but it won’t have quite the sweet intensity of cherry tomatoes. Might need to sneak in a bit of tom paste….

This recipe was delicious!  I didn't follow the directions that well.  I didn't realize the Ancho and Chipotle chiles were DRIED.  SO...I chopped up two large poblano peppers for the FRESH chiles and mixed them with the tomatoes (I knew I would be spooning it over pasta, so I opted to chop them).  I had dried chipotle and Ancho chili powders and added some of each to the mixture.  It didn't get the full nuance of the chiles but it was still delicious!  Perfect spooned over spaghetti noodles!
Delish! I added sweet (long) bell peppers to up the veggie content. Why is no one talking about the 3/4 c of oil?? I'd cut that to a couple of tablespoons...

This was amazing! My whole family loved it and wanted me to make it again immediately. I followed the recipe pretty closely, except that I peeled the garlic, cut the cloves in half, and submerged them in the oil. They roasted and spread on the bread perfectly and then we didn't have the mess of peeling them after they were cooked. I also couldn't get fresh peppers today (store alwas out) so I doubled the dried chilis and they tasted great. Will make this delicious and easy meal again soon!

My husband and I cooked this as written. WE LOVED it! The presentation, the variety and richness of flavors. We had a little leftover that we’ll have for lunch today. Whatever oil is left after lunch we’ll keep for dipping bread. Yum!

I goofed and prepared rice to go with this sumptuous dish.  It doesn't need anything but the Shrimp Saganaki and sauce in a bowl with crusty bread to soak up the fabulous tomato sauce.  I used what I had in the pantry: dried guajillo and ancho chili peppers plus some fire roasted green chilies.  Otherwise followed the recipe. It was fantastic, except the rice soaked up too much sauce, but was excellent also.  Just a salad and bread makes this a five-star dinner.  Fun to make.

This was great and will go into regular rotation. I eyeballed quantities because I needed to feed 7 people, and served it with buttered orzo. Next time I will try to prioritize surface area and use a wider, shallower dish to maximize the grilled parts of the feta and shrimp, they were so good.

Brilliant recipe, absolutely delicious. 
That being said, we did make some adjustments; peeled the garlic cloves, used one red bell pepper and 3 fresno peppers, used the dried ancho, but substituted 1/2 tsp chipotle powder for the dried chipotle chile. Doubled the shrimp. Paired with a zucchini ribbon salad with garlic lemon dressing and homemade wheat bread. 
This will go on repeat.

One of my favorite shrimp recipes! I serve this on orzo, and double the shrimp because it's so delicious and there is plenty of sauce. Good quality feta makes all the difference too. Serve with a nice crisp white wine and you're all set!
Great flavor and very quick and easy.  Also good with rice.

Adapted for hot weather:
I didn't want to crank up the oven and broiler on this warm and humid day, so I cooked it in a big pot on the stovetop.
I did everything in the same order as the recipe and I nestled the cheese right in the middle of the tomatoes on the bottom of the pan.
I didn't measure the oil; I just used the amount that I would normally start with to sauté onions... definitely not 3/4 cup.
I peeled the garlics and threw them  in whole. They became soft and delicious and squishable.

title: Chopped Salad With Everything Bagel Croutons Recipe
author: Yotam Ottolenghi

comments.commentBody: The article didn't mention za'tar as a possible seasoning...a blend of sumac, oregano, sesame, thyme & more. While not a bagel seasoning, it is used in Israeli & Palestinian dishes and works beautifully in a salad.
It is also better for people who are allergic to sesame seeds and poppy seeds.

I really like the idea of tossing the almonds and pine nuts with the bagel bites, olive oil and seasoning before toasting in the oven, but I am going to put that on top of the salad instead of tossing ir in.  I roasted fish with everything seasoning just once, and it taught me that everything seasoning loses something wonderful when it absorbs significant moisture. It is meant to crunch.

Logically, an everything bagel would seem like the optimal choice over a plain bagel. But I guess one might argue that there is the potential for burnt onion and garlic flavors due to double baking. That said, I will take the risk since a plain bagel is not in my vocabulary. I am excited to make this. Yotam Ottolenghi is a genius and everything he comes up with is brilliant.

@JLT quick follow up. This is fantastic! 
Perfect use for the bagels in the freezer that lose a few too many seeds. I used et bagel in this condition and added more et topping as directed.
Delish, though I will double the nuts for the crouton part of the recipe next time.
My family loves Everything seasoning but we found it overpowering in the salad.

I recently made a “bagel salad” and wanted to share my tips.
- 1 cucumber
- 4 Roma tomatoes (any will do)
- ricotta 
- olive oil
- everything bagel seasoning 
- red wine vinegar

Chop the vegetables and put into a bowl. Add the olive oil and vinegar. Stir to mix. Add ricotta. Stir until the ricotta is well incorporated. Sprinkle with seasoning. 

It’s the same flavor profile as a tomato cucumber everything bagel with no bagel. Vinegar is probably optional, but helps bring everything together.
loved the bagel croutons!
Absolutely in love with this salad - perfect lunch salad for the work week! I added beans for some extra protein
I used sour dough bread instead of a bagel for croutons and also just put on top of salad with sliced avocados.

Rip bagel into 30 pieces 
Maybe a little more tahini a little less yogurt 
Might try Vidalia onions instead of scallions
A list of Everything Bagel seasoning ingredients would help for those us who have to make a safe substitute for allergies or digestive issues.
The Trader Joe's version, which I think is pretty standard, contains sesame seeds (white and black), poppy seeds, dried garlic & onion, and flaky salt.

Loved the nut topping with the bagel &
EBS. I topped the salad with it instead of
mixing it in. Also added poached shrimp to make it an entree. Wasn’t crazy about the dressing. Added salt and additional water as it was super thick.

I made this last night for my book club and everyone loved it. I have two types of Everything bagel seasoning in my cupboard and decided to use the Old Thompson one instead of the Trader Joe’s. The TJ one includes salt and this recipe already has salt. My croutons only took about 17-18 minutes to get crispy and brown. I kept a close eye on them because I was worried about the possibility of the pine nuts burning. I wouldn’t change a thing but I will try za’atar sometime for fun
I live in a place where bagels aren’t easily available, but I would love to try this salad. Any ideas what I could sub? Would regular stale bread work?
Loved this!   Skipped the bagel, though.
Delicious but prep time for us was at least an hour.
Delicious but took my husband and I over an hour prep time. Really loved the bagel crouton mix and will use that again for sure.

title: Smoky Confit Tomato and Lemon Pasta Recipe
author: Yotam Ottolenghi

comments.commentBody: I made this last night, and the dish was very flavorful.  However, it was also VERY oily.   If I made it again, I would use perhaps half the amount of olive oil indicated.
@Glenn I cooked this several times more and found 1/3 of what the recipe calls for was still plenty of oil so that’s what I am sticking with.

Lemon or Zest: The following quote is from the NYT article that accompanied this recipe (July 18, 2024): “Today’s recipe sticks to only that zest, because tomatoes normally have enough acidity in them to give my pasta a nice sharpness. But extra juice would definitely not hurt, particularly if your tomatoes are extra sweet.”
So, zest only. 
But the comments are right - the instructions in the recipe are unclear and the editor should revise the text regarding the lemon ingredient.
This looks fantastic, but I am a little confused: Should I add the whole lemon into the pan? Or just the strips?
wondering whether its supposed to read 2 lemons zest,  peeled into thin strips....which would mean only the lemon skin is inlcuded.  Any thoughts?
You add just the strips, as I read it.
I agree - as written it's a bit confusing. I would surmise he means use the zest only from two lemons.

In the recipe he says “Today’s recipe sticks to only that zest, because tomatoes normally have enough acidity in them to give my pasta a nice sharpness. But extra juice would definitely not hurt, particularly if your tomatoes are extra sweet.” So it sounds like just the zest.
Neither the recipe I see nor intro notes state what you say is in the recipe??

This quote appears only in the article "The Most Important Seasoning After Salt and Pepper," which is linked in the paragraph describing the recipe. It's too bad it was left out on the recipe page itself, because it is key!

It's in the article that discusses his cooking with lemons in general, and not cooking with the juice specifically with this recipe. The Most Important Seasoning After Salt and Pepper https://www.nytimes.com/2024/07/17/magazine/smoky-tomato-pasta-lemons-recipe.html?smid=nytcore-android-share
Cinnamon sticks and lemon… Ottolenghi’s Palestine-by-way-of-London touch is subtly distinct and tasty here, as usual.

I made this last night and it was excellent! So flavorful. Rather than turn on the oven on a hot day, I sauteed everything in the stove top. Once the pasta was done I mixed it in with the sauce until all the liquid was absorbed.
Simple to make and amazing flavor! I'll definetely keep this in the rotation.

I made this last night and will make it again.  The main flavor was the lemon.  Very nice.  The oil seemed like a lot but after mixing in the pasta seemed fine.  I might break the cinnamon sticks up a bit next time and go a bit easier on the chiles as it was kind of spicy for us.  I made it with GF pasta (Bionaturae) which seemed to absorb the sauce nicely so no need for the pasta water.  All in all a very tasty pasta dish worth trying again.
Make this dish a few hours before and reheat. You will be happy you waited. The tomatoes get jammier and the rest of the concoction melds perfectly!  Great recipe. Serve with claret

This was disgusting. Oily, not lemony, and not something I would make again. I really didn't get what this was supposed to be, I followed the recipe exactly. Chewing on lemon peels and giant cloves of garlic? Not appealing.

You probably didn't roast it for long enough. Garlic cloves cook down to a soft, sweet, sticky mush when cooked like this. I use a citrus zester tool https://www.procook.co.uk/product/procook-lemon-zester-stainless-steel1?cq_src=google_ads&cq_cmp={campaigned}&cq_net=x&cq_plt=gp&nbt=nb%3Aadwords%3Ax%3A21334087812%3A%3A&nb_adtype=pla_with_promotion&nb_mi=127414221&nb_pc=online&nb_pi=5782&nb_si={sourceid}&gad_source=1&gclid=CjwKCAjw4_K0BhBsEiwAfVVZ_z2bA3S6dsknbtnCG to zest lemons and limes.
It really sounds as if additional lemon juice would work if your tomatoes are extra sweet. He leaves it up to the cook. Taste your confit and decide for yourself!
I would just add grated lemon zest instead of strips. eating strips doesn't sound appealing but adding lemon zest to tomatoes sounds interesting.
@mally The strips of lemon should be discarded prior to serving, they impart flavor during cooking. You can add some lemon juice if you feel it needs more lemon flavor

Agree that there’s too much olive oil to add it all to the pasta. Suggest not dumping it all in but rather putting the pasta back into what it was cooked in then ladle in the confit tomato. Then use a spoon to add just the amount of oil that you like. I found that about half works. And much of the great flavor comes from the roasted garlic. Since it will be in hot oil pick it up with tongs and squeeze. All the cloves pop out at once - easy - and then can be mixed into the sauce.

This recipe is fantastic. It's easy and delicious. I have made it exactly as written several times, and it always comes out great. Today, I had an eggplant on hand, so I threw it in. You cannot go wrong!

I used only a half cup oil and it was bordering on too oily. I’ve learned from YO’s confit chickpea recipe to scale waaay back on the oil. Added fresh basil and pecorino before serving. Better the next day.

This works really well, with a bit less oil. You don't need to go through all that crazed chef tonging-and-peeling-with-upside-down-head-of-garlic rigmarole, just chop or grate in plenty garlic at the start. And roast it all for longer. Think the 1/3 cuppa pasta water is a mis-speak. You don't get "a few tablespoons at a time" out of 1/3 cup. Use a cup, spend a good 15 mins cooking it in. Up comes a silky, gooey mess and the heat emerges slowly as you eat. Fantastic.

My favorite solution to the "too much oil" problem: spoon the excess off into a bowl, salt it, and serve with bread or mozzarella on the side. The relative lightness of these ingredients (yes, at least in contract to this wonderfully rich pasta!) does a lot to round everything out.
1 cup EVOO and ancho powder

Before tempting any recipe, I always read through it several times. The first thing that jumped out at me was the amount of olive oil- one and a quarter cups?  that seems excessive So I probably won’t make it or if I did, I would cut the oil way down.

This was tasty, but my goodness the oil! Does Ottolenghi’s test kitchen actually test recipes? Because this is an experience I’ve had with his recipes before. In what world is 1  cup of oil appropriate for a pound of pasta. Halve the oil, at least, or double the quantity of pasta. Good flavor for the effort, at least.

This was FAB! Absolutely delicious. Will be even better tomorrow as leftovers because the flavors will have had a chance to coalesce.  

I tweaked it a little, given that I've made tomato sauce from scratch all my life (so easy, really, no need ever to buy pre-made sauce). But though I have added orange zest at times, which I learned in Sardegna, I'd never done it with lemon and cinammon. Also used more garlic and peeled it first, and eyeballed rather than measured the olive oil. Fantastic!

This recipe does not work!  Although the ingredient combination sounds good, the chili, at least the high quality dried ancho chili I used, overwhelms all the other ingredients, and not in a good way.  Also, as several other comments have noted, the recipe doesn’t clearly indicate what is to be done with the lemons.  Just the zest?  The zest and the juice?  The zest, the juice and the pulp?  This recipe needs to be reworked!

Perhaps it was because my tomatoes were very juicy but I ended up with a lot of oily liquid. Next time I would use half as much olive oil. I didn't let the liquid go to waste; we sopped it up with bread but still would prefer not to use and consume so much oil.
Surprisingly boring.  I ended up having to add additional spice and smokiness.  Too much oil.  It was a bland greasy mess.

This was so amazing! So rich, but so delicate at the same time. Amazing with parm at the end. Even cooked some long pole beans in it and they turned out amazing. Next time I’ll add anchovies to the oil and maybe some cod or other white fish. I think anything would be amazing poached in this oil. And I even though I liked the pasta a lot, it would be even better smother across a toasted crostini. So many ways to riff!!

So little lemon.
So MUCH olive oil.
The instructions for the lemon are in the accompanying article, not the recipe.
So do you remove the lemon strips before eating or are they small enough to eat? Why not just zest the lemons?

Followed recipe exactly, except cut in half. Enjoyed the flavors but thought there was far too much olive oil; the tomatoes were swimming in it. I ended up pouring some off to use later. Loved the lemon rind and I think a better instruction would be to cut the rind into long slivers for easier eating and better flavor distribution.  Also think it would be better to tear the ancho up for flavor distribution while cooking.
The article suggests, I think, cook only with zest. Taste when done, and add juice if it seems to be needed to offset sweetness.

I followed the instructions as listed, but found the garlic hadn't roasted sufficently to just melt into the sauce. After some consideration, I decided to reserve pasta liquid/drained the pasta and then put the "sauce" (minus the cinnamon sticks) into the pan and went over it with an immersion blender. Added the pasta/about a half cup of the reserved liquid and everyone loved it.  I think it would have been fine with half the oil but nobody complained!
I just made it as per the recipe. It has been a success with my family, given top ratings among discerning pasta lovers. Thank you!

Way too much oil. Reduce by a third or even half.
This was oddly bland. Even though  I tasted the ingredients both separately and together, as in a good cocktail.

I'm planning to make this for dinner this evening but was thinking about adding some Shrimp - either grilled alone with some smoked paprika or roasted (for a few minutes) with the tomato mixture).  Tomato. Garlic and Lemon, at least to me, play so well with Shrimp.  It seems like some caramelized Vidalia Onion would be a nice addition as well, but I might wait on that until next time.

Totally solid recipe. I cut the oil somewhat but the recipe has "confit" in the title--it's about the deliciously smoky, tomato-y, lemon-y oil! The recipe doesn't call for cheese, live a little. I tripled the recipe and used half for one pound of pasta which was the right ratio of pasta to sauce, and am saving the other half to enjoy in a week or so. Added tons of fresh basil because it was around. A sublime summer treat.

Excellent, flavorful!

The quality of the tomatoes will determine the outcome. If you have only average tomatoes, you can probably make adjustments, such as adding a few pinches of sugar, plus some slices of lemon in place of the zest called for.

If the oil seems excessive, adjust. But use more than you would ordinarily, it’s necessary for the proper roasting of the tomatoes and garlic.

It was too hot outside to heat up the oven, so I cooked this on the stovetop in a deep, wide skillet, and I added some cubed eggplant that I had on hand. The eggplant was a big plus. Everything slowly caramelized beautifully! If you decide to try the stovetop version, definitely lessen the amount of lemon peel. As others recommended, I halved the amount of olive oil. I served it over rigatoni, with chopped fresh basil. This was very different,  in a good way!
This was truly glorious, but I agree with the other posters who suggest using less oil. I used 1 cup, and it was a bit much. I'd suggest 3/4 cup. Otherwise, this was a fantastic vegetarian meal!
This looks wonderful. DO you think the sauce could be made a day ahead and refrigerated, and. perhaps, even frozen?
I enjoyed this recipe, but I would recommend using half or even a quarter of the recommended oil.
This was awful. Greasy, tasteless. So disappointed.  Totally failed recipe.

This was amazing. Roasting the tomatoes made my apartment smell wonderful. I had to make a few modifications and it still came out amazing: used 1 whole preserved lemon (ran out of regular lemons), accidentally used 2 dried chipotle peppers, reduced the olive oil to ~1 half cup, served with gnocchi and chickpeas. I also blended the sauce since my blender was already out (I blended in half of the preserved lemon and one chipotle).

There's definitely potential here flavor-wise and I look forward to experimenting with the concept, but the end result as written is an oily mess with leathery nubs of lemon peel that need to be picked out while eating. It doesn't really come together into a cohesive sauce at all.

I live in a Maine beach town where it would be dicey to find ancho chiles at the grocery store. But I shared the recipe with a coworker and she brought some dried anchos to our tiny art museum to give me. We both made the dish and were both impressed at how delicious it was! A keeper.
Definitely agree with other comments saying too much oil. This recipe does not need 1.25 cups of it. Loveddd the kick of the chili pepper and cinnamon!!

The first time I made this I used fresh thyme and it was extraordinary. The next time I used fresh oregano as called for and it didn’t have that same spark. Next time it’s thyme for the win. It’s a fabulous company dish, minimal prep and a big payoff.
I cut down on the oil and zested the lemon-- it was amazing. I also added sausage the second time I cooked it and was loved by all!
How many people does this serve?
The nutritional information claims 6 servings
It's not clear to me if the tomatoes go back in the oven to continue cooking at step 2.
Would love to add protein to this pasta dish, but if so, what is the best for it to be prepared?
chickpeas maybe?

Ok, two questions:
1. For those of us who live in boring food regions like the Netherlands, what can be swapped for Ancho chilies? They're impossible to get here. 
2. Can a protein be added to this so it's not just tomatoey pasta?

Sean, Assuming no other dried chile is available, you’re trying to replicate the fruity/chocolaty mildly spicy flavor of the ancho chile. So maybe a roasted & peeled sweet red pepper, a bit of smoked paprika and a dash of powdered cayenne for spiciness.
I'm confused about the use of the dried ancho chiles. Most recipes call for them to be rehydrated before use. But I don't see that here.

Any kind of smoked chili or smoked chili powder (chipotle, mulatto etc.).  You want the smokiness and the subtle heat. You can also try a spoonful of canned chipotle peppers if you can find them (these won't be dried....sort of like a paste).  
I don't think you reconstitute the chilies, otherwise they could break open and you don't want that

Step 2 of the recipe instructions state, “ Nestle the ancho chiles under the tomatoes, tearing in half if necessary to immerse them,”  which means the chile will rehydrate during the roasting process.
Where does the tomato paste go? In the roasting pan? Wont it burn?
What's an heirloom tomato?
"What Are Heirloom Tomatoes, Anyway? Those colorful, strangely-shaped tomatoes are cool, sure. But what makes them the way that they are?" https://www.bonappetit.com/story/what-are-heirloom-tomatoes

I'm confused, do you return the tomatoes to the oven after you've squeezed the garlic and chopped up the chiles? The way it's written ("... while the tomato confit roasts, ...")? For how long, just while the pasta cooks? That's the way I read the instructions but I want to be sure.
Did this happen to anyone else? My baking pan came out as a big watery mess. The sauce wouldn’t stick to the pasta before or after addition of pasta water. Help please. Really want this to work.
Possible solutions: 1-Use a bigger pan so you can spread mixture out. (Mine was a bit wet, too.) 2-Oven? Check accuracy of oven temperature, crank up the temp, use convection.

"Ingredients....2 lemons, zest peeled into strips, avoiding the white pith"
Please, what does this mean??

TJ - It means using a citrus zester, box grater, microplane, a vegetable peeler or a paring knife to simply remove only the yellow part of the lemon skin - taking as little of the white "pith" as possible. It's much like peeling a potato or a carrot - you just need to use a light touch and don't go too deep. Save the zested lemon or add the juice to the tomato mixture for a bit of extra "bite". More on technique: https://www.crazyforcrust.com/how-to-zest-a-lemon/

This was really good! We made it as the recipe stated, but I agree that it was confusing at times. I would make this again for sure though. Next time I’ll use about half the oil. It was tasty as is, but good grief I didn’t need that much! I would also double the garlic next time.

Great flavor, but there is no justification for all that oil! Hard to believe that this recipe was tested before publication. The confit dishes I know cook in oil but generally remove the dish from the oil before serving.
Delicious, worth every minute of preparation.
Great recipe but a bit oily. I used half the second tome and it was delicious. Love the Israeli inspired cinnamon-garlic-tomato combo.

I like this recipe for its warm, sweet, smoky flavors and generous use of tomato.

But there are problems: It specifies "2 lemons, zest peeled" instead of "2 lemons' zest, peeled" wrongly implying that the entire lemon is used (only use the zest). It says to remove the cinnamon sticks, but not the sprigs or zests (remove them, per photo). It calls for way too much oil (I poured off at least 1/2c at the end). And the flavor doesn't penetrate the pasta (fridge and reheat later --maybe bake mixed).
oh my my. Less oil. but so silky.

Great summer meal - BUT the lemon peel strips at least at 2-3" length by 1/2" width seemed a bit large to eat after baking, I reduced the oil by 1/4 cup, and I believe the oregano sprigs should be partially stripped then bulk of the stems and leaves removed along with the cinnamon. End result I placed the mix in a bowl and used my immersion blender for a light few pluses.  Then added the pasta for a great pasta dinner. Prepared oyster on half shell as a starter then served this pasta & a salad.

Wish I'd read the notes first. Definitely reduce the oil & I'd increase the tomatoes. They were delicious but the proportions were off. Too much oil, not enough confit to flavor all of the pasta. Also the garlic didn't roast enough - peel & smash then roast?  Disappointing.

I thought understood what to do with the lemon zest but after reading the comment about chewing on large strips of lemon peel I realized, needed to totally zest both lemons with my very sharp name brand micro-planer. So I’ll chop up the larger strips and put them back in. 
Way too much oil. I’m draining it off the top. 
I added 2, additional 15 minutes units of cook time and raised the rack more to the middle of my oven. 
I can’t find the tiny piece of cinnamon stick. Hahaha
I enjoyed the dish, but I will definitely cut down the oil--prob to 1 cup or maybe even by half, to 3/4 cup.  Tomatoes don't brown floating in oil!!
Roast at 350 degrees instead and less oil
350 degrees and less oil
Remove most of lemon zest before puréing
If I use dried ancho chili  pepper, instead of the whole ones, how much would you recommend?

I cooked it with small sweet grape tomatoes cut in half. Reduced a little the olive oil, too.
Absolutely delicious. Everybody, including kids 5 and 7, loved it.
The zest, garlic and cinnamon added a super nice kick. On the other hand, the oregano sprigs were superfluous. I used already less than the recommended amount, and next time, I will skip them completely. This dish made into our home's cook-it-again list!
The late, great Anthony Bourdain couldn't stand the sight of Yotam Ottolenghi. Just watch the Gaza episode of No Reservations. His disdain is evident. RIP to a great man.

Excellent and unusual recipe.  I halved it, and used 1 1/2 pounds of heirloom tomatoes.  Good tomatoes are key, and I could have left the tomato portion larger.  I served with some chicken Italian sausage.  The comments about just the lemon zest, and cutting down the oil a bit were key.  I used a half cup of oil.
I also cut down the garlic, and just used about two cloves.
I forgot to add oregano until the end.  I now see the recipe has you added most before cooking, then adding a few afterwards just for color.

I made this as a topping for a risotto rather than a pasta dish and the flavor was unique and delicious. I agree with other reviews that there is a lot of oil being used here (at the end it felt like the tomatoes were swimming in it) but it was very easy to separate, even as the tomatoes were becoming mushy and sauce-like. If I were using pasta I would add the confit to the pasta rather than the other way around so you can control the amount of oil you’re using. 

None of my grocery stores carried whole dried ancho chilies so I used ground ancho chili powder and it came out great. I was light handed with it (used 1tsp) but I could have used more and it would have providing a smokier flavor.
This was very tasty, and I would make it again. I omitted the chilli and added some smoked paprika, used half the oil and doubled the garlic.

To answer the lemon question, I peeled strips of zest off the entire lemon, then cut the actual fruit in half and placed one half cut-side down in the center of the baking dish. Having no ancho chiles, I doubled the tomato paste and used chili powder! Oregano? Dried from a bottle. Ditto with a sprinkling of cinnamon powder because I couldn’t find the sticks I swear I have somewhere. Instead of olive oil, I chopped up a good 10 ounces of raw bacon and sprinkled it over the tomatoes. Did it work? Yes! Delicious. Very forgiving recipe. You can wing it. Oh—and I didn’t save enough pasta water, so I used half-and-half to loosen the sauce. I know, I know—it’s hardly the original recipe, but it was great. I love recipes that are so forgiving with substitutions.

@Caroline – It's evident you know how to cook. OT's recipe was a mere inspiration, and a technique (as I assume you put your concoction in the oven!). Yours is very creative and worth a try...or a riff.

I like the sauce but for me it was soupy. Next time I’ll try halving the oil and replacing the remainder liquid with water, bringing it to a boil on the stove top and cooking it in a 300 degree oven for longer. I don’t know. We’ll see.

There is a good dish struggling to get free of these instructions.  I'm a long term devotee of Ottolenghi and am used to his recipes being spot on, but this is out of kilter: too much oil or too high a temperature?  My tomatoes were charred twenty minutes in and far too much oil left in the pan to properly emulsify with the pasta water.  Needs much tweaking...

Another creative, technique-building recipe from YO, making a quick pasta sauce by tossing ingredients into a roasting pan; a sheet-pan sauce, if you will. 
Cut it to 2 servings and roasted the garlic separately (so I could roast and preserve a lot). A bit too much olive oil? But it transmitted all the flavors to the pasta; whipping in the tomato paste at first and later the roasted garlic made a delish sauce. Lemon peel a bit fiddly but cooks flavor into sauce. May try zest at end next time.
I'm assuming after garlic head cut off...the remainder of bulb is left whole and roasted with skin; correct?
This sounds yummy and a great place to use Trader Joe’s Lemon Torchetti pasta (recently arrived in stores!) in place of “regular” pasta!

I omitted the lemon and cinnamon sticks, reduced the oil a bit, and added anchovies for extra umami and a little red cooking wine. I didn’t use a whole garlic bulb and opted rather to add whole cloves which smashed beautifully at the end just the same. I used two different tomatoes, quartered compari and whole constellation cherry tomatoes. I fried some Italian sausage, sliced, and tossed in at the end with some chiffonade fresh basil to garnish. One of my new favorites, by far.

title: Black Lime Salmon Recipe
author: Yotam Ottolenghi

comments.commentBody: Photo shown is deceptive. Recipe specifies cook with skin down and place toppings on salmon flesh. One would need to scrape the off to get at the flesh is the picture we’re followed.
I think that the black you're seeing in the photo is the black lime marinade and not the skin.
I thought that also but magnifying picture makes me think that's not skin, its the black lime coating.
@Sandra Kistner Just made this.  It’s the black lime that you’re seeing.  My salmon looked similar to the photo.
I would use hot lime pickle, sold in Indian food stores. Both Swad and Atak brands are good. The pickle contains all of the spices listed, plus mustardseed, which is always good with salmon.
From one of Yotam’s cookbooks, 1/2 oz / 15g dried black limes equals about 3 large ones.

Burlap & Barrel sells a ground black lime, which you can purchase on Amazon. Since it’s going to be ground up anyways I would think this would work. Anybody know how much ground to substitute for the whole limes?
Tip at the end of the recipe: Rather than pulverizing whole black limes, you could substitute 5 teaspoons black lime powder.

This is incredible!  Prepared exactly as written, cooking 12 minutes at 475, delicious.  Served w roasted zucchini spears.  Only change I would suggest is less oil in the marinade and also less salt.  SO GOOD.

Hi! Photo is pretty accurate. The black lime paste is definitely black. It does look like skin in the picture, but it’s the glaze.
Went to a Middle Eastern market to find black limes and couldn’t resist the most gorgeous flat bread-still warm! 
This one should be fun.
Burlap & Barrel sells ground black lime which might work in this dish. They’re an online spice and herb company. (Full disclosure: the company’s founder is my cousin.)

You can buy lime powder, thus eliminating the need to grind it.  Iranian épiceries have it and my Indian épicerie has it as well.  I'm sure it can be purchased online as well.  It also makes a wonderful ingredient in kebab marinades.
Dried limes can be easily ordered on Amazon and delivered within a day or two. Once you have them, Google recipes for them. Worthwhile to be adventuresome.
475 degrees for 12 minutes is too hot and overcooks the salmon.  400 degrees for 10 minutes worked perfectly for me.
I thought also but magnifying picture makes me think that's not skin, its the black lime coating.

I have made this recipe for guests several times and always to rave reviews. It is delicious and easy. I have used both ground whole black limes and preground black limes. I find no significant difference. In fact, I prefer the ground lime because my spice grinder tends to leave some larger pieces of the whole black limes. Also, I always have made this with four individually cut pieces of salmon instead of one large. The cooking time obviously is less. I prefer that each person has a stand alone piece of art on the plate. The presentation is very dramatic with the vibrant colors of the salmon, the sour cream, the black lime spice mix and the grated tomatoes. Wonderful!
Sandra, I thought the same thing but after looking closely at the photo, realized the black is the black lime marinade, not the skin.
Phoenician Lebanese market in North Brunseick, NJ. sells both dried black limes (Adonis spices) and dried lemons (Ziyad brand). The salmon is fabulous!
This was delicious and full of bold flavors. I think I would cut the salt in half next time and it would still be outstanding.
Made as is but had some extra marinade leftover and ended up pouring it all over the salmon and rice. Cooked, it was more smoky, while the uncooked was more tangy and (in our opinion) yummier.

The salmon was amazing with the dried black limes. I subbed greek yogurt for the sour cream and omitted sugar and salt. Baked in airfryer for 12 minutes. Easy and delicious. Loved the flavor profile that the black dried limes added to the dish. The limes pulverized easily in my simple blender with the other spices.
Baking the sauce at such high temperature turned it into a smoking sludge! Very complex array of flavors interesting, but almost made it not-salmon.
I left out the sugar. used greek yogurt.. and sliced compari tomato … was fine!
Super easy and yummy. Skin side down. The black on top in the picture is the ground black lime mixture.
loved the flavor of black lime. totally worth getting.  adds so much flavor to the salmon. I didn't have sour cream so i used greek yogurt, which was also very nice.
Tried this. Not my favorite taste. I thought it a bit too bitter. But, that's me.
Sadat Foods is your best source for Iranian packaged foods including limoo amani (dried limes), whole or powdered. https://www.sadaf.com/products/sadaf-ground-lemon-omani-09-1280
Disaster!  I am convinced it was operator error, but weight of lime powder would be useful.  Made a freshly caught Alaska salmon inedibly bitter.
was great without the sugar
Good.   I cooked the grated tomatoes for a few minutes because I wanted them saucier and hot.  The sour cream wasn’t necessary.

This was an amazing meal, thank you so much! I added steam to the oven and the salmon was really melting. WOW. I prepared the tomatoes in advance, putting them in halves on a little bit of olive oil, pinch of salt, cayenne and sugar 1 hr at 180° C , peeled them (very easy when warm), then let them sit overnight in the fridge. What a treat!
For black limes (and many other hard-to-find ingredients), Amazon is your friend.
I happen to already have dried lime powder.  Anyone that tries out this recipe want to give an estimate on how much powder 3 dried black limes creates when ground up?
Kalustyan’s( Lexington Ave/28th street in NYC or foodsofnations.com ) sells whole and ground black limes
I see the 5 star rating yet no comments on the success of the recipe. Only how to procure black limes. Is the oven at 475 degrees too much at 12min? Any additional recommendations?

Added maple syrup to the spice/oil mixture to correct bitterness. Everyone finished the salmon dish and really liked it, though it this is a strong flavor (similar to li hing mui if you grew up in Hawaii) so you may want to glaze the salmon sparingly the first time you try the recipe.  Made a salad dressing the next day with the remaining oil mixture by adding lemon juice and more olive oil and that was really good.

I made this recipe exactly as written using the ground black lime powder from Burlap & Barrel that others noted is available on Amazon.  Wow!  Loved the flavors, very complex. The cumin mixes nicely with the paprika and black lime powder.  It was fast and simple.  I served it with long grain wild rice and steamed broccoli and had it for a regular Sunday evening meal but this would be a great dinner party recipe.
Easy and delicious!subbed dried lemons, greek yogurt and omitted sugar. Baked in airfryer at 450F for 12 minutes, perfect and succulent.  Served with rice and chopped salad.
This recipe works best with a thick fillet. And shorten the cooking time IMO. I hate overcooked salmon.

Found it to be bland - couldn’t detect the black lime flavor with sour cream and grated tomato on top. 12 min at 475 deg was too long - salmon was dry. Now what else can I use the remaining black lime powder (Burlap & Barrel) for?

Made this just as directed and it was next level delicious! We used the black lime powder from Burlap and Barrel (Amazon) and it worked beautifully. Would not change a thing. Thanks for a great new way to make salmon!

Preparing the black lime concoction I wondered if I would be ruining a lovely salmon filet, but it turned out to be a flavorful, delicious recipe.  This definitely will be in my favorites folder. The only substitution I made was crème fraiche instead of the sour cream.
Use less black lime.
Has anybody tried to make their own black limes?
Perfectly spiced as written, did reduce cooking time slightly. Used creme fraiche. Tomato was delicious, and the creme fraiche melted deliciously over the salmon. Beautiful dish!
Any recommendations for a wine to serve with this? I’m thinking a lively riesling but would love for others to weigh in.

@CatB Riesling is great. Also try Sauv Blanc like a Sancerre, Chablis, Albariño, Etna Bianco (Carricante grape). Any white with some salty or mineral properties to highlight those notes in the black limes. Cheers!
This was a weird one for me. Too sweet. All together with sour cream and tomato in one bite was edible, but won't be making this again. Disappointed as it's such a gorgeous presentation.

I made this with boneless, skinless chicken thighs. Yum. I bought the Ground Omani Black Limes at Oakltown Spice in Albany, California. I didn’t have any fresh tomatoes so used some canned, diced tomatoes and some vegan sour cream. It was delicious.

Gave this a try on the cod filets I had on hand, so reduced the marinade portions to fit, and omitted the sugar. Quite nice. Next time, however, I'll make the full amount of marinade because I bet it will taste good on chicken, too!

This is gorgeous and delicious. I cooked it at 425 degrees, and it worked fine; 475 degrees almost seems like a typo. That would be silly hot. Using plum tomatoes is crucial for achieving a dense, rich red sauce. I used Burlap & Barrel's black lime, available directly from them or on Amazon.
I found pre ground dried black limes on Amazon. Can you provide the equivalent measures for three limes?
I have black lime powder (from Burlap and Barrel).  How many teaspoons/tablespoons is the average whole black lime?
For this recipe, you'd be safe with two tablespoons.  You can actually add more if you're not getting the desired citrus kick.
If you happen to have dried lime powder, how much would you use instead of grinding whole limes?
Is there a substitute for Black Limes? Maybe lime zest and a bit more brown sugar?
Not an exact flavor sub, but you can mix lime zest and garam masala.  This will provide the sharp citrus + smoky/eathy effect.
Would Kalyustan’s have the lines?
Súper interesting ingredient recommendation here, black lime! Can’t wait to find some!
In Hong Kong, Regency Spices sell dried limes, either whole or ground.

We love crispy salmon skin. What is the process for cooking it to get the gorgeous result pictured in the photo? Is the black lime powder responsible for the uniformly black color of the skin? In which case, one follows the recipe as is but starting with the fish skin-side up…?
I think the photo is showing a fillet either skinless or skin side down, with the black color on the flesh from the marinade.
Sorry, recipe does NOT say serve skin side down,
It says to cook skin side down.  With the oil mixture on the top, what would be the point of serving skin side up?

Made last night. I would cut the oil in half, as it was very thin - not like a paste, which is what you want if you are putting it on immediately before cooking. 

I used black limes from my Shaheen Roastery (Jordan) Kabsah Spice Blend (whole).

I bought dried Persian limes.  The smell is gorgeous and they are great to throw in while cooking rice.  But they are hard as a rock!  How the heck to you grind them? It would break the blades on any grinder!  I know you can buy it already ground (I have some) but I would really like to understand the trick here.
Try grating them.

Put the dried limes on a cutting board and bash them with a heavy pan. Then put the pieces in with the rest of the spice mix in a blender and you'll have a wonderfully fragrant spice mix easily. Fabulous recipe!

I would modify to: 1 SUSTAINABLY HARVESTED WILD salmon.

(Farmed salmon don’t make the journey referenced in the article, and those raised in open net pens in the ocean are far from sustainable for the environment and wild stocks.)

On my list to make again this summer. I smashed the limes using my meat pounder on a cutting board and scraped it into my spice grinder; doubled the cumin and cayenne. Did not have any labne or sour cream which would definitely have balanced the strong citrus flavors. I used wild salmon which could have used the richness as well.
Mmmmm! Delicious! I will make this again.
Heray Spice has black lime powder.
Added the juice from the tomatoes to the sour cream to loosen it up a bit along with salt and pepper. Minced jalapeño added to garlic tomato mixture. The more limes, the better.
Anyone try to serve this cold? Roasting anything just before serving in this weather is not a great idea Thanks for any comments on this

Hi, do you marinade the pink side of the salmon, or the skin, or both? And the ratio of black lime to the other spices seems kind of high - doesn't the black lime overpower the other spices? Does anyone use less of the black lime?
Has anyone substituted dried lime for the black lime?  Wondering if there’s a taste difference.
Any thoughts on what to serve with this? Just wondering if the flavors will go well with fall vegetables?
Correction: Photo in fact "shows" the salmon flesh covered in the roasted black-lime oil mixture, which happens to "look like" roasted salmon skin.
Loved meeting black lime. Told any one who would listen about it. This is going into rotation. Will lower temp and time a bit next time.

Simple recipe, came together quickly. The sour cream and tomato blend with the black lime into a beautiful creamy sauce. The taste is more complex than citrus - it's earthy and smoky. The acid of the the tomato and the punch of the garlic mixed with the sour cream was lovely. Served with rice and broccoli.

title: Butter Bean Dip With Frizzled Onions and Preserved Lemon Recipe
author: Yotam Ottolenghi

comments.commentBody: I modified this recipe after reading the other notes, and it was a big hit at my party. The real standout was the onions, which I caramelized for about 30 minutes. I cut back on the garlic cloves to 3 and put them in a food processor with the beans, juice and zest of one lemon, salt, pepper, and yogurt (kefir), then streamed in olive oil to taste. Other canned beans work. I used a mixture of canned chickpeas, fava, and borlotti, as that's what I had in my cabinet.

Good flavors but I also found the recipe method a little fussy. Decided to just caramelize onions and garlic, add preserved lemon and thyme, add beans and water directly and then pulse everything with an immersion blender.

Boy this was terrible. Am I the only one who thought it was a preposterous recipe? 12 whole barely cooked cloves of garlic looking at you from the bowl? Uncooked preserved lemon peel sprinkled on top? Over a half cup of oil? One and a half teaspoons of salt in addition to salty preserved lemons? The whole thing was a greasy salty mess.

After reading the notes, I modified the recipe and give it 5 stars. It was a huge hit and guests wanted the recipe. I cut the salt in Step 3 back by half and adjusted it later. I only used 4 garlic cloves and then mashed them along with the beans. They fell apart easily. If you follow the  recommend preserved lemon amount of 25 grams, you'll avoid over salting. I finely chopped the preserved lemon skin and sprinkled it on top at the end. Amazing umami.

This recipe is both too complicated for the mediocre result and, to my taste, far too salty. I would make it again, but I would fry the onions as directed and put everything else in a food processor. I would reduce the amount of preserved lemon peel. 
Also, I believe butter beans are the same as fava beans.
@Stephanie K. No, they’re closer to limas

If you have a World Market (formerly Cost Plus)in your area they carry the Casablanca Market brand of preserved lemons. They're $5.99 in my store. Also, Amazon has a variety of them in various sizes and at various prices. Amazon also carries the Casablanca Market brand but today are priced at several $$ more than the same ones at World Market.
I've been using the Moroccan Casablanca Market brand for years. It's a women owned company.  The lemons are consistently reliable in size and flavor.

I used 2 garlic cloves instead of 12, fresh lemon juice from 1 lemon, and chopped some parsley to fold through at the end. I added the garlic and spices toward the end of the onion cooking time. Next time I'll add the butter beans to the oil and onion instead of taking the onion out (I don't think it needs to be on top). I added the lemon and parsley when the dip cooled a little. It was very popular at the event!

I made this as directed, using canned beans and the fresh lemon as suggested in the recipe and it was delicious. I did make a mistake when trying to finely chop the pulp of the lemon. I cut out the whole flesh of the lemon and tried to chop it with the inner membranes still attached, and it wouldn’t chop. So I just squeezed out the juice and used that along with the zest. I might try supreming the lemon next time to get real pulp but it was still good this way.

I used white beans and followed the recipe, more or less. I wasn’t sure how the cumin seeds were supposed to stay or go. In the photo you can’t see them and they were definitely in the finished product. I also didn’t have preserved lemons so I thinly sliced a lemon and cooked it at a very low heat and put them on top. A few of us ate the slices. Not great, but bearable. I added a little lemon juice to the finished dip. The frizzled onions were the star.

TA-Da!!!  I found the preserved lemons at Whole Foods (on a shelf next to heirloom grains and near small jars of tuna fillets).  The lemons are small, and the label says Moroccan Preserved Lemons. The ingredients are: lemons, water, sea salt.   So excited to make this dip!
OMG! Has anyone actually made this dip? All I see are complaints re: Preserved Lemons!
Made it as written.  Delicious.  Making preserved lemons is easy, great in a variety of dishes, and keep well in the fridge.  Happy cooking!

I made this and would give it five stars. Used Rancho Gordo baby limas, had to substitute for the preserved lemon — 1 (microplaned) grated lemon peal, 1 1/2 juiced lemons, 1 teaspoon Diamond kosher salt. Everyone at the Memorial Day cookout asked for the recipe. On another note, scrolling through the comments, so few writers have actually made it. You really should. Using canned beans, I could imagine chickpeas or any white bean would work well.
yeah!  a great use for my baby limas!
What about substituting Toom for the yogurt? It would make it vegan, but would it be too much?
What a great idea - Toom is so yummy. Probably wouldn’t need as much garlic?

I have a subscription to Rancho Gordo so I’m always looking for bean recipes. Just started a folder and these are in it. Don’t be afraid of the preserved lemons. Ottolenghi wants you to use them and he’s even posted a “Quick Lemon Paste” on MasterClass.com if you don’t already have your own stash.
Can use canelli beans

Sorry, but I really hated this. The recipe also seems incomplete. 12 whole cloves of garlic, barely cooked and just sitting there looking up at me from the bowl?  One and a half teaspoons of salt for two cans of beans? And a half a cup of oil? Not to mention that it's a little odd to sprinkle raw preserved lemon rind on top. I made this, though the flavors had great potential, it was really nothing more than a salty, greasy mess.
Outstanding!

OK folks, this was really amazing– made as directed with the exception of using the cheaters preserved lemon paste instead of preserved lemon. 
It was not overly salty, but the trick is to use diamond crystal salt. If you don’t have diamond crystal, use regular salt, but only half the quantity. 
I also love caramelized onions, but the frizzled onions with the char gives a bitterness that is important in this dish I believe. And yes, you eat the garlic whole. It’s delicious!

I've had my onions and garlic cooking on high for 15 minutes and they aren't anywhere near crispy and cooked. I'm going to carmelize for at least another 15 minutes or more based on other people's comments.
I followed the directions with one exception and it was AMAZING!  I put all the lemon peel in with onions.  I'm kind of glad I did though!   It mellowed the lemon out a little.
Lots of effort for not that great a result. Won't make again, despite LOVING Ottolenghi in general.

After reading the story that accompanies this recipe, I thought I’d give it a go. Stopped in my tracks by the preserved lemon. No problem with the onion, it’s the lemon that’s the sticking point. I made preserved lemons years ago. They’re a pain and a home cook needs to come up with all kinds of recipes to use them up. I sincerely doubt a suburbanite can find preserved lemons at the grocery store. So, where is the tip section for preserved lemons? How might a regular lemon be used?
He says, "If you can’t get your hands on preserved lemons, fresh lemon pulp and finely sliced zest can be used; just be sure to add an extra pinch of salt, too."
From the description up top: "If you can’t get your hands on preserved lemons, fresh lemon pulp and finely sliced zest can be used; just be sure to add an extra pinch of salt, too."

For immediate use, you may be able to find a jar of preserved lemons in the catch-all "ethnic" aisle of supermarkets near harissa paste and other Mediterranean-adjacent packaged foods. They're more common now since more recipe writers use them. 
I am lucky enough to have access to homegrown meyer lemons and made a pickle jar full of preserved lemons well over a year ago with just lemons and salt and some dried chili peppers. Still have some in the 'fridge. Almost like jam now. Delicious.
I made my own preserved lemons. It’s easy and requires only a few ingredients. You can make just a few preserved lemons using a small jar.
Any Williams Sonoma location should have preserved lemons. As Yotam writes, if you can’t find them use a combo of lemon zest, lemon pulp and additional kosher salt.
Canned? Yuck. I love Limassol and butter beans. Buy and cook frozen then you will have something really great.
Sounds wonderful.  I'm wondering if cannelini beans would work as well -- what do you all think?
I suspect they would be fine. I used butter beans because I never had before, but I use cannellinis all the time and I don't imagine there would be any problem.

Preserved lemons aren’t difficult to make. Lemons, lemon juice, salt, and patience are all that’s required. Other uses: NYT Dirty Tonic, in salads, chopped on top of roasted red peppers as a mezze, mixed with goat cheese & herbs for a spread. I’m lucky to live in Southern California where I can get lemons 4 for $1 most of the year.

Meh.  Overall, the beans are a lacking in flavor and entirely dependent on the lemon onion mixture which is… just a lemon onion mixtures.  Time to taste ratio off for me, I don’t think I’ll make it again

Reading and making this one brought back memories of making "fasole batuta" or whipped beans back in in the old country in my childhood and adolescent years. Okay, I admit that I skipped the thyme, since no one in my house eats this. It's okay, since, after sampling this, the old-fashioned bean dip was declared superior, despite being less fussy. It's also vegan, but not by design. Let me know if you'd like the version of the old world recipe I grew up eating, a recipe which is very forgiving.
Yes please! Would love your version
Another enthusiastic request for your version.
Not to be cheeky, but - still waiting on that other recipe.  One is never enough...
This looks delicious but I'm watching for variations as I don't care for cumin. The substitute would have to carry a lot of flavor. Peppercorns?
Smoked Paprika or Chilli flakes

The photo and description look great.  Of all the things I can make in this kind of time, including dips, this simply isn't worth it.  Served at a dinner and the response was "meh" to a group of not-picky folks who also appreciate great food.

All the whining about preserved lemon. Buy them at Whole Foods or your nearest Middle Eastern grocery. Or make them...they'll keep forever.  I made a 1.5 qt jar of them several years ago. Crammed about 15 well-scrubbed, organic lemons into it, starrd and salted, and filled the jar with olive oil. Set in a cool dark place for 6 months. Once opened, they have kept fine in the fridge for when we need that " 2 square inch, minced" bit of preserved lemon.
Thank you! Just need to know what “starrd” was supposed to be - stirred?

This recipe fortuitously appeared just as I was trying to figure out what to do with the last few cups of cooked limas from our Rancho Gordo Bean Club subscription. Despite having no preserved lemons on hand at the moment, I served it to guests for lunch, with homemade sourdough bread, and it was a hit. The onions add a little pizzazz. I’ll definitely make this again, & maybe with try it other white beans.

I can recommend Lemon Goop to have handy (from Dorie Greenspan) for the preserved lemon. This condiment is SOOO useful!
Looking forward to this recipe!
What are butter beans
Also called "Baby Lima Beans"
Actually NOT the green 'baby' limas, rather they are the large beige ones

you tend not to find them in regular American grocery stores, you have to find them in a store that caters to former British colonies, including Indian and Caribbean groceries. Those are generally where I find them along with the other canned beans.

Did anyone mention adding minced preserved lemon to tuna salad?  Elevates it to the beyond.  
Also, this recipe is to work intensive! Just drain 2 cans of cannellini or Great Northern beans. Into food processor with a garlic clove, salt, couple of three glugs of olive oil and an optional bit of rosemary .  Pulse and  serve. Or … eat it off a spoon when no one is looking.

That's a different recipe.  You left out the main ingredient and the herbs.   Ottolenghi is a wonderful chef.  Why don't you try the actual recipe before midwesternizing it.   Why don't you just go to subway and get a tuna sandwich
Sounds delicious -- butter beans are less ordinary than a lot of recipes call for.  Am I the only one that noticed that there is 1/2 cup olive oil to 2 cans of drained dbeans?

This was very good. 

Don’t much like canned beans. But pressed for time. The beans lacked flavor but the liquid in the can tasted good. So I cooked the beans in the can liquid. Yield delicious beans for bean dip.
Do the whole garlic cloves get mashed up with the beans, or do they stay with the onions?
It was well received by my motley crew and leftovers gone in a day.  Downside is the preparation time; al hoping to shore it up a bit the next time I make it.
Where do I get preserved lemons, or does anyone have a recipe to make them?
I'm confused - I grew up in the south where a butter bean was a very small delicious green baby Lima. My aunt grew them in her garden and we helped shell them so she would cook a batch for us!
Does this use whole garlic cloves?? No mention of mincing...
Flavors are good, but way too much salt in the onions.  I used dried beans, which absorbed some of the excess salt, but you’ve been warned.
hi..can this be m,ade in advance or should be served warm ?
Instead of using whole preserved lemons, try NYShuk preserved-lemon paste.  It’s a game changer!

title: Grilled Asparagus With Miso and Olives Recipe
author: Yotam Ottolenghi

comments.commentBody: Made this with miso butter, pine nuts, lemon but no dill. I put the olives in my martini instead. And I grilled a steak. Good times.

The sauce seriously overpowers the flavor of the asparagus and is much too heavy for this delicate and wonderful vegetable.  The dill is optional, fortunately; dill does not complement asparagus at all.  Nothing is better than asparagus, butter, and a bit of salt.
@John dill is marvellous with asparagus.

@John not sure why you made this or considered making this if you have such specific tastes with respect to asparagus? IMHO dill and miso complement the subtle and unique flavor of asparagus beautifully.

I still love my Neapolitan grandmother's simple asparagus: boiled or steamed, dressed with olive oil, a crushed garlic clove, and a hint of vinegar or lemon, salt & pepper. Or she tucked it into a frittata!  My greatest pleasure was to visit my grandparents' farm, where they grew at least an acre of it.  It is a delicate vegetable, so less is more.

I disagree with many of the comments on this recipe. This was DIVINE. My boyfriend said he would literally drink the sauce it was that good. I did make slight alterations, as I added a bit extra olive brine and lemon to the dressing- just cause I personally like things a bit acidic. I also had garlic I’d already chopped on hand and included that. Out of this world delicious.

We had very thick asparagus vs thin stalks, so I think that helped it hold up to this sauce.  This is a new fave.

Tried it specifically because it was different from my usual approach, liked it. I will say I made the sauce first and kept it warm while quickly cooking the asparagus. I asked my hubby how he liked the sauce and he said "what sauce", so some won't find it overpowering as others have commented. 
I always get a laugh out of people who comment with their own recipes. If you prefer yours, well... easy decision, don't make this. :-) I find it fun to try something new.
Wow. The comments are so negative. Tried this tonight as an appetizer. It was delicious, my guests loved it and every last bit was lapped up. No changes to recipe.

Disagree with all the comments from people who did not make the recipe but felt the need to comment it would be bad. I really enjoyed this. Made as written but swapped the dill for parsley and served over orzo so it would be dinner.

Made this dish exactly as directed and it was wonderful. Castelvetrano olives are much less salty than the typical martini olive, perhaps that is the issue some people are having.  I thought this dish was a great way to showcase asparagus.  Served with a simple lobster salad and baby potatoes.

For those in the comments spewing vitriol, I certainly understand the purpose of simple preparations, however, I wouldn't look to Ottolenghi for that. That being said, this is a wonderful recipe, rich in flavors as one would come to expect from Ottolenghi. For those poo-pooing on the preparation, it is a fantastic recipe that will work in any season, especially in those winter months when we might be tempted to reach for an imported asparagus bunch.

I felt it was a wonderful tangy “vinaigrette” on top of last night’s asparagus.  So much better than just olive oil and a nice change of pace.  I’ll make it again for asparagus as well as other vegetables.
I cut them diagonally into 1 inch pieces, sautee them in a dab of butter and olive oil, crushed garlic, hot pepper flakes,  and salt in a hot pan for about 3 minutes. They are perfection!
I made this according to the recipe with fresh dill. I loved it. The two of us ate the full 1.5 pounds with a small grilled steak and a salad. Excellent!
I roasted the asparagus. Made the sauce minus the dill and was very pleased. When you have eaten so much spring asparagus either roasted or grilled this recipe is a great alternative!

At medium high my oil was vigorously frying. At seven minutes, my chopped cashews(What I had on hand) were perfectly golden brown. I turned off the heat and added the miso, olive brine, and lemon juice. But I think the oil was too hot practically exploded and the sauce broke. Should I have allowed it to cool for a while before I added the liquids?

Made the recipe exactly as is except I heated up the toppings before I grilled the asparagus.  Otherwise you’d definitely have cold vegetables.  This dish is bursting with umami and so delicious.  All the comments saying to just roast asparagus with oil and seasonings, DUH!  Do that on a weekday, this is a special occasion or weekend dish.  Yes it’s decadent and high in sodium, make a few times a year and enjoy.

This recipe is genius. I’ve made it repeatedly since asparagus came into season and it is the centerpiece of the meal. I do like my vegetables simple but did any of the naysayers actually make and eat this? The asparagus is not overpowered at all but richly and deliciously complemented. It doesn’t need the dill if you don’t have it but- yes- the dill goes. What’s up with all these food rules in other comments? Ottolenghi rules the taste buds- that’s for sure.
Toss the olive/pine nut sauce with pasta and the grilled asparagus on the side?
Delicious! I cooked the asparagus cut in 1 inch pieces in a hot cast iron skillet and served over orzo with fresh  grated Parmesan.  Absolutely fantastic

A typically esoteric Yotam Ottolenghi sauce, but one I could try because I had the ingredients on hand (save the optional dill). After starting step 3, I thought it might be better to brown the pine nuts before cooking with the olives, but I did it as written and it worked. The sauce is strong (even with mild Castelvetrano olives) and flavorful, so a little goes a long way. Use sparingly and you will enjoy it. Grilled asparagus is not as subtle as steamed, so this sauce works with it.

This was packed with flavor. Like many reviewers have said, usually asparagus is great with just a light touch, but for a change of pace this was a wonderful combination. Used the suggested olives, which are far less briny than many varieties so they don’t overwhelm the other flavors. No dill on hand. I made the dressing in advance but next time I’ll make sure I conbine these while everything is warm. Would be great with some shaved Parmesan on top, too.  Agree about using this with green beans.

I thought it was excellent.  I did make the sauce concurrently rather than after cooking the asparagus, but that is the only change.  I love love love the flavors and felt it elevated the entire plate!

The best asparagus I've ever had at home our out. I absolutely loved this, so did my kids. I went very heavy handed on the pine nuts, but why not..? I also skipped the dill & will definitely make again. Love it!

I made this last night - following the recipe to the letter. A total fail, and a terrible thing to do to fresh asparagus. The olives heated in oil produce an off flavor (yes they were the recommended Castlevetranos), and we had no idea what the miso was intended to do. Next time, as others agree,  butter, lemon and salt.
Made this tonight. Too much oil, and the sauce clumped in an unattractive way. Next time back to basics: roasted with salt, pepper, and a little olive oil.

I thought the recipe sounded strange but decided to try it anyway. My first impressions were correct. The flavors of the ingredients did not meld - instead of becoming the sum of it's parts, it just  tasted like parts, overly oily parts.

Made 1/2 recipe as written. Delicious.. It sounded very questionable in reading the ingredients. But, I was quite sure that Yotam Ottolenghi would prevail. And he did.  Interestingly, when the ingredients were combined it was difficult to detect each ingredient,  Served with a  very good, basic chicken recipe-Baked Chicken thighs{NYT}. A lovely Spring dinner

If like me you eat asparagus almost every day during its ever so brief season, it’s good to have some options for variety. I can easily imagine this in one of Ottolenghi’s wonderful restaurants or cafés in London.

Contrary to other commenters, we love this treatment of beloved asparagus. This sauce is marvelous, a really extraordinary set of tastes. And it works very well with nice, fresh, al dente asparagus. Sure, serve it more simply sometimes but why be doctrinaire? The season is long enough to enjoy it many ways.

Made this tonight sans dill. Sauce is delicious, and would go well on any green vegetable that needs jazzing up. Next time I’m going to try it on wilted kale. it is pretty salty and strong tasting though, so I’d save it to serve to people that like things like anchovies.

I prepared this last night, even making a special trip to the store for Castelvetrano olives.  I thought the sauce was mediocre, as it fell off the asparagus to the bottom of the plate as it was served.  I will not be making it again.
Tasty but much too oily and therefore too heavy for my taste. However, at least salty enough that my teenager ate it.
Too salty for me and the olive mixture overwhelms the flavor of the asparagus. I scrapped the topping off to use on a more robust vegetable.

I disagree with the asparagus purists.  This was incredible. The toasted pine nuts, and theCastelvetrano olives really take asparagus “to another level”.  One of my guest is not fond of asparagus, but will eat it when it is the only veg option.  She loved it so much, that she toook some leftovers!  I did not use dill, because I did not want to buy a whole bunch for just a teaspoon. This is replacing my plain steamed asparagus at Passover this year!

My family loved the sauce - on the asparagus, the accompanying roasted salmon, and on broccoli. Cooking the olives mellowed the assertive brine notes and deepened  the flavor. In our opinion, it actually enhanced the sweetness of the asparagus. The only thing I would change is using less olive oil next time. And there will be a next time - my family wants to try it on everything!
Uh no. Way too salty. Skip this one. Although grilling the asparagus in a cast iron skillet was a nice touch. So not all was lost.

Made this with pan-seared salmon and dressed everything in the sauce. Hubs and I absolutely devoured it. It's definitely on the salty/savory/umami side, so add a touch more lemon if you like. Overall, delicious and we are brainstorming other fun ways to use the dressing.

Hahaha, these comments are so funny. The NYT published a humor piece just a short while ago riffing on the comments made about recipes that people never made or totally changed. Well I loved this recipe, have made it several times and plan to take it to a garden party this weekend. So here goes -- I didn't have any miso and added a touch of garlic. LOL
But I will get the miso for the party coming up.
I love Ottolenghi recipes as a rule but this didn’t work for me. I found the sauce too heavy handed for asparagus.
I thought the sauce was really delicious! Would I use this for the most fresh picked asparagus from my garden? No. But, for the grocery store asparagus that can get a bit bitter, this is perfect!

Delicious.  I know a lot of people have commented, saying why deviate from a classic olive oil or butter and lemon dressing, but I have to say this was delicious.  My husband Raved about it, saying it is his new favorite way to eat asparagus.  I followed the ingredients-including the dill- and it all worked well together.  I roasted the asparagus in the oven for a few minutes, then finished them under the broiler.  The caramelized tips blended beautifully with the sauce. I'll try chives next!
i made as directed, then chopped up the asparagus and put it all over pasta. highly recommend for turning into a meal!
Not terrible, but definitely not greater than the sum of its parts. Unlike other ottolenghi recipes there didn’t seem to be any harmony of flavor here so I will skip next time!

Served this as a side to grilled salmon. The sauce went nicely with the salmon too. I omitted the dill. Very tasty, adds depth and texture to the asparagus. I might substitute butter for the oil next time or add a dab of butter at the end.
I found some white asparagus this week. Would this recipe overwhelm it?

I’ll be grilling some nice thick asparagus spears tonight. I marinate for an hour or so in some Good Seasons Italian dressing made with a decent EVOO and red wine vinegar. Grill on a hot cast iron plate set on my gas grill. Perfect every time.
Yes, green beans!

Like others, I found this a bit overpowering for grilled asparagus, but the sauce tasted great with the other things I had on my plate - potatoes, fresh tomatoes, grilled yellow squash. Would make again (and probably add lots more fresh herbs to brighten it up) with a simple grilled meal.
If made ahead, how to reheat?
High heat with olive oil?
After reading the comments I decided to substitute cauliflower for asparagus. Delicious!

I found miso clumped with the pine nuts and was difficult to whisk in… flavors worked but on the salty side.
It

If you first mix the miso with the lemon juice and olive brine this won't happen and it will all blend. And let the oil cool a bit before adding the liquid (which should go without saying but other comments have noted this was a problem for them).

I microwaved my slightly salted asparagus with a small amount of olive oil and miso, covered,  for a total time 4 minutes. Served with chopped olives with lemon juice and fresh dill. Yummy. (No nuts).

I think chives - some onion family member would be good and I’ll try that next time.
Fast and easy!  This is delicious.  Broiled the asparagus, not grilled.
Fantastic dressing. I served asparagus with swordfish and the dresser no was great on the fish too.

This was delicious, and I'm trying to think of additional things on which I can use that amazing sauce.

I did have some trouble emulsifying the miso (which I'd already thinned with the lemon juice and brine) with the rest of the sauce and I'm wondering if others were more successful. If so, did you add a little of the oil/olive/pine nut mixture to the miso/lemon juice/brine first (the way one does when adding miso to soups)? 

Any other suggestions for optimizing miso incorporation?
I didn't have any issues with this, perhaps it was the miso?
I always pick the thickest asparagus and I peel them.  Got the habit from my mother who came from France.  They are so tender, lemon butter and egg mimosa.  Best way to eat asparagus.
So delicious and yes, a very memorable way to prepare and serve asparagus, indeed.  Everyone loved, and even talked about next day!  Recommend trying and I know I'll be making this recipe again.
Great recipe! You can make the sauce while grilling/frying the asparagus, so you’ve got your food on the plate in no time. The flavors are spot-on.
I loved this recipe. I made the sauce while the asparagus was grilling. I served with grilled pork chops, and the asparagus and sauce were perfect with the pork.
I did not use the olives, replaced them with some crispy diced pancetta.  Delicious.  Would use this sauce on many things!

Well, we had crummy olives with bland brine, and I am allergic to tree nuts, but I made a version with pepitas.  It was tasty but very oily and I did not love the way the sauce did not emulsify.  We used broccolini based on the comments, and with our new stove it cooked on convection and the flowers got crispy. It was quite delicious all in all.  I would make it with a finer texture, less oil, and far more lemon in the future.  The added garlic we used was a good idea…

Help readers - I loved the taste of this, but when I carefully whisked in the miso/brine/lemon combination to the hot oil mix, it seized up and would not incorporate into the oil. The only change I made was to use heavy cream instead of EVOO. (Kidding. I used Italian lemon flavored EVOO) Advice?

Overall, good dish, worth making again but without dill or miso. I substituted walnuts for the pine nuts, which worked fine. No need to use a small saucepan to heat up the sauce. Just remove the asparagus when it's done and make the sauce in the large skillet. I may have had closer to 1 lb than 1.5 lbs, and all the asparagus fit nicely. No need for batches.
Great recipe. I reheated some of the asparagus and sauce for 5 minutes at 200F the next day. I used sliced pimento stuffed olives for a bit of colour.

I loved asparagus with butter and lemon. And yet, a change once in a while is needed. What a wonderful change this recipe was. Such a great blend of flavors and textures.
