I made it exactly as recipe was written with one exception: I quickly blanched the asparagus before stove grilling them. As my husband and I ate this for dinner last night, we were musing over all the possibilities for this sauce. A perfect topping for sword fish, green beans, or even pasta? Just great recipe.
If you have good-quality asparagus, this sauce is superfluous.

So glad I ignored the negative comments, this dish was amazingly good! I didn’t have Castelvetrano olives so substituted with 2 tablespoons chopped capers and 2 teaspoons caper brine. With less volume, I used half the oil. Cooked the asparagus in an air fryer at 400F for 7 minutes. I skipped the optional dill.
Bake the oiled and salted asparagus on a sheet pan at 400°F for 15 to 20 minutes for convenience and similarly delicious results.

title: Halloumi Chicken Parmesan With Za’atar Recipe
author: Yotam Ottolenghi

comments.commentBody:
1. Put the cutlets into an empty cereal bag and fold the opening under. Then pound them. This prevents "juice" from flying all over. 
2. After the breading step, let them rest on a rack for at least 15 minutes. This helps prevent the breading from falling off in the pan. 
3. If I make this, I'll freeze the haloumi for a few minutes, to make grating easier.

I haven’t made this recipe yet but I have fried tens of thousands of chicken, pork, veal and other cutlets and the best way to pound them is to layer hem between two sheets of wax paper and using the smooth end pound them until 1/4” thin. 1/2 is way too thick for a fried cutlet. Trust me when I say this. Also use panko with the za’atar and mix thoroughly. I find that halloumi melts best when broiled on low and then set it back under the broiler to finish it off.
I think I'll trust the professional chef, thanks.
I just love ottolenghi. This recipe is a great idea but before I make it I'm going to simplify the heck out of it and reduce the oil! And I like olive oil.

This recipe is fantastic. Dinner party-worthy. I did freeze the Halloumi for a bit before grating, great tip. It's full of flavor and tastes just as rich as chicken parm but more interesting. It's not complicated. Next time I'm going to serve along roasted carrots: https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/1020863-grilled-carrots-with-yogurt-carrot-top-oil-and-dukkah?algo=identity&fellback=true&imp_id=1687938863920965&req_id=6281296317718834&surface=cooking-search-web&variant=0_relevance_reranking

I didn’t have the cheese so I used Comté with a good result.  I made the tomato sauce but included a tablespoon of my harissa.  The quantity of EVOO is just right. I made a chopped salad from shredded radicchio, carrot peels, pickled onions and a thinly sliced bok choy tossed in Dijon vinaigrette.  My breasts were 9oz so I halved them before pounding them thin.  Recipe is fantastic, and deliciously satisfying.
Made as written. Super tasty.

This was lovely, but instead of melting, the cheese browned into a crust even though the broiler was further away from the food than the recipe called for.  It still tasted nice, but had lost the cheesy, melty texture.  The sauce was excellent.  I was a bit concerned about adding a whole teaspoon of allspice but found that it was really well seasoned.  Will make again but bake instead of broiling.

What a treat - cumin, allspice and za'atar replacing the cusomary italian seasoning. I used pork tenderloin, no cream, rye bread crumbs, and make it all on the stovetop. The warmth of the spice combo lingers.
This was incredibly delicious. I didn’t add the cream or zaatar oil at the end, but we loved it all the same. Served it with sautéed rapini.

I cooked this exactly as written, and prefer this version to the more traditional Italian version. I usually pound my chicken breasts very thin when I bread them, then they cook evenly.  I will definitely make this chicken again, it was fabulous.  I love Mr. Ottolenghi's recipes, he is so imaginative and his recipes are out of this world.   As another commenter suggested, the halloumi needs to be semi-frozen to make it easier to slice,  Thank you for the tip!
This is tasty but, man, so many steps and dishes and pans. Not sure outcome is quite worth the hassle.

@susan totally agree. Massive amount of work and cleaning up, I was exhausted after I’d finished cooking. One also needs to make a salad and a carb too to make it a complete meal. Not sure if’s worth it really at all

Excellent! Lighter tasting than the Italian version and the zaatar oil at the end gives it a salty nutty lovely finish. Chicken came out super moist and the halloumi melted nicely and toasted up perfectly. I’ll be making this again for sure

This was delicious but the recipe called for way too much panko - we had so much left over so wasted a lot of panko and precious za'atar. We weren't brave enough to put extra cream and oil on top!  I love the flavors of the breading and sauce and will make it again.
Made a tofu version that held up well. Drained a block of tofu, sliced horizontally into 3 sections, then prepared the same as the thin chicken cutlets.
Absolutely delicious and just as good with mozzarella if you don't have halloumi. Used yogurt instead of cream as someone suggested. Will make again!

This was good but I simplified it for weeknight cooking. Did tomato sauce as listed, seasoned and breaded the chicken breasts as directed but baked for 20 minutes instead of alternately broiling and frying. Sure maybe it lost a little flavor but still tasted great. Also used way less oil. Freezing the cheese was clutch and the flavors were amazing, like a warm hug but crispy on the outside. Best of both worlds.

It is not stated in the recipe, but I assume it’s boneless skinless breasts?
I prefer using thighs, but will definitely try this recipe.
You're welcome to try flattening a bone-in breast to half an inch thick, but I feel like it won't go well.
@Kentfield Barbara why not use boneless skinless thighs?

Made this last night with one change. I didn’t have cheese so slathered chicken with Vegemite. Sorry to say but it tasted like a dog bomb. I guess I don’t like Zaatar. I probably don’t have the courage to try this again.
Weird, who could have foreseen that changing out one of the most important ingredients for something completely different would make the recipe not turn out well?
@Dos Cinco well, Vegemite isn’t halloumi. At all. In fact, I could point to the source of the “dog bomb” effect, IMO, but I’m not a fan of Vegemite myself.

600g skinless chicken breast (2 cut into half)
2 x tins tomatos
Freeze haloumi b4 grating
Cooked whole lot in one go - oven at 190C for 20 mins
I wish there was a way to print the comments, is there and I don’t know how.
Not worth the effort, pots and pans. Lacks adequate spicing to achieve middle eastern taste level.

What a lovely twist on chicken parm.  The allspice and cumin were a nice combination with the tomatoes and halloumi.  As others have commented, you do not need the amount of olive oil called for.  2 T with the tomatoes and garlic and enough to coat the skillet when pan frying the chicken.  Then add enough to the za’atar to make a drizzle.

I'm lazy and also needed this as a meal prepped meal for six days so I made some modifications.

Step 1, do exactly as written but I used Greek yogurt instead of cream.
Step 2, do exactly as written but for three huge butterflied breasts I only needed 2 eggs.
Step 3, didn't smash, can't be asked.
Step 4, no broiler. Instead preheat normal oven to 400 and line a big metal baking sheet with aluminum foil.
Step 5, do as written, but I used avocado oil and it took a little longer than a minute per side for me. Chicken won't be cooked through yet and that's ok.
Step 6, don't do any of this, just put the chicken onto the baking sheet you prepped.
Step 7, put the chicken into the oven preferably with a meat thermometer. When it hits 155°, put a 2 oz slice of some salty white cheese on each piece of chicken. I bet halloumi is good or whatever but it's Colby jack for me. Put the chicken back into the oven until it hits 165°, then take it out and let it rest for 10 minutes. 
Make that oil stuff as recommended because it's magical.
Step 8, put a bed of the tomato sauce on a plate, slap a cheesy chicken on it, drizzle some za'atar oil on it and have at it.

Store the sauce and chicken separately in the fridge. Store the oil in a room temp jar or something. During the week take out a chicken breast and put it in the toaster oven or air fryer until it's warm, heat up the sauce separately, and plate it up as you did the first time.

title: Sweet and Sour Tofu With Barberries Recipe
author: Yotam Ottolenghi

comments.commentBody: Made this for Valentine’s Day dinner - husband loved it. There are a lot of things to do in this but it’s very tasty. It’s reminds me of a light Chinese orange chicken dish but healthier, not quite as crispy, made with tofu and seasoned with Persian flavors. So not that similar - lol.

On a quest to really enjoy tofu, I was delighted to see this recipe and followed it to the letter. What can I say. Disappointing. Took me close to an hour. For whatever reason the flavours were murky, no bright notes. BUT - used the microwave trick for draining tofu and the texture of that was perfect. Will not make this again but remain an Ottolenghi fan.

Delish! Will make again. Used 1/4 c olive oil in step 3, and 2 tbsp on a baking sheet, grilling the tofu instead of coating in corn starch and cooking in a pan. Added last ingredients into onion/etc. mixture with 3 tbsp cornstarch in 1 cup water (instead of 1.5 c). Brought to a quick boil, turned down to low, gently stirred in tofu, 1/2 tsp red pepper flakes, and simmered on low, 5 minutes. Was SO GOOD - thank you!
I was excited to try this recipe..until I got to the 2/3 cup of cornstarch requirement. I can barely stand to use a Tbsp.Do you think it would work with less cornstarch?
Looks like you need enough to coat the tofu cubes; I would treat it like a dredge, where you coat  each piece and shake off the excess. That should cut down on the amount in the final dish.

If you grill or roast the tofu, you will not be requiring any cornstach whilst reducing fat by not deep frying. You could if you wanted to brush the tofu and or the tray with a touch of oil to have them a little crispy. I do the same with Chinese Sesame Prawn Toast and a number of other dishes.

One of the rare times that all of what I call my esoteric pantry ingredients matched a recipe. The only thing I changed in the recipe was cooking the tofu in the oven with an olive oil cornstarch coating. But I’m rather underwhelmed by the final product. Seems like it’s missing something   Usually love Ottolenghi recipes but this needed some kind of something else to make it worthwhile.
Made this for my husband on Valentine’s Day because he loves Persian flavors and eating vegetarian. It was a hit. There’s a lot to do in this recipe but it was worth it.
The technique for salting the tofu was interesting and I would do that again.  The sauce was okay but not great, particularly given the price of some of the ingredients.
This was very good. But felt like a lot of complex effort for a fairly simple end result. Pine nuts and cilantro are key to making it both delicious and appetizing.

I love to cook with tofu, but this was a a very disappointing dish. Slimy tofu texture, unpleasant flavor brought by orange in combination with all spices, tofu and way to much salt. Most of all, this is a very labor intensive dish. Struggled for well over an hour. Was not worth the effort. For the cooks who like to try cooking this dish, I recommend to use ‘extra’ firm tofu and fry it to a crisp and to skip the corn starch.

This wasn’t bad, but wasn’t worth the time and effort it took to make. I made as described, with the one exception being the amount of oil. The recipe calls for WAY TOO MUCH oil. I halved the first amount for the onion and carrot mixture and made the tofu in the air fryer. Again, not bad, but it took over an hour to make and was just okay.
My sentiments exactly.
Where do the sliced oranges go?  I added them with the juice & it worked well. This is delicious & I’ll make it again
My thoughts exactly! Have not even eaten yet and looked at the orange slices and am scratching my head

There are no orange slices in the recipe. You slice the four large strips of zest and juice the orange. Since several people have asked about orange slices, though, I think the recipe writing could be clearer.

I followed the recipe, except I only used 1 pkg tofu. I liked the tofu consistency but cooked longer than suggested to get a nice crust. After tossing with sauce, it still had a nice texture. Since I only used 1 pkg tofu, it probably could have used a bit less olive oil with onions. Everyone liked it, I loved it. The orange with the onions and garlic - delish. Took a little longer than an hour. Served with rice. Perfect amount for dinner for 3 and no leftovers.

Great dish!  Having never eaten a barberry, I sampled a hydrated one and I could not tell the difference between it and a dried cranberry.  For convenience and to save money, next time I will use dried cranberries, substitute peanuts for pine nuts and skip the saffron.
Pretty good, maybe worth it when you have the time, but it's a lot of steps and a ton of cleanup. Not a weeknight project.
Just had a lot of fun making this, but do question the method. The cornstarch makes. Perfect crisp tofu.  Adding it into sauce. With water makes it soggy again. Did I get it wrong? Tips welcome

What a wonderful tofu recipe. I didn’t have any pine nuts so we substituted almonds (I think pistachios would have worked as well), otherwise the recipe was done as is. Nice bright flavors with a Persian note. Served with rice, a sliced orange/onion/oil cure olive salad, and a glass of Gruner Veltliner. We’ll be making this again.
Prep and cooking time considerably more than estimated in the recipe.

I found this recipe highly disappointing. He doesn’t say to take the tofu out of the bowl before adding the cornstarch, so a lot of the cornstarch just turned to mush. Hence, tofu texture not crisp. Never says what to do with orange slices. Doesn’t say to serve over a bed of rice, which is clearly necessary and which I had to make at the last minute. Too much work for a kind of mushy bland result.

The people freaking out about the cornstarch:  you only are using it to dredge.  What isn’t attached to the tofu is left behind.  You’re not eating 2/3 cup cornstarch. 

Also, if you let the dredged tofu sit a minute, the cornstarch on the surface hydrates a bit which I’ll stop it from falling off during cooking and getting into the finished dish
The photo looks like you end up with sauced tofu, but the recipe and headnote suggest something much wetter like a stew. What did people who’ve made it get?
Exactly. Cooked as directed yields an OILY stew. The "food stylist" created an appealing photo that does not look like the completed soupy dish.
I agree with the others who say it’s “murky” and fussy and the tofu just gets slimy. One of the few times Ottolenghi does not deliver for me.
I had the ingredients and wanted to use those barberries. It’s ok, but not wonderful and a lot of work. I doubt I’ll try it again.

TASTY but way too oily so will try the prudent suggestions to either grill or bake tofu next time (I wish I read these recommendations before cooking since preparation was more time consuming than indicated). I added peas & finely chopped mushrooms for a nutritionally complete vegan meal which also added depth of flavors.

This recipe is a nightmare. You will have a disaster to clean up and a horrible dinner. You need a minimum of two large frying pans -one to fry and the other for the sauted onion carrot and orange mixture. The recipe does not make clear to use separate pans for all the steps and not to mix the two until the end. This is in addition to bowls for the tofu berries, saffron, onions and the orange juice mixture. Oh and one more for the pine nut garnish. Save yourself a major headache and order out.

Bland and disappointing.  Followed the recipe carefully--which I rarely do--and found that even adding Korean hot sauce couldn’t  make it palatable. Lots of work for a colossal failure of a meal.  Or maybe  I just don't have a vegan palate?
For all the potential this recipe has, do not, do not waste your time. I doubled the spices. I doubled the garlic. I cooked the tofu with more care than Kobe beef. And it turned out bland as can be.
It’s the orange peel that gets thinly sliced, not the orange itself.

Gave this 5 stars.  I loved it.  My spouse disliked it.  She found orange flavor to be too strong and that it had excess sour/bitter flavor notes.  I thought the orange flavor blended well with the other flavors.  Given the family divide, won’t repeat.  

I combined salting the tofu, 2 minutes in the microwave to dry it after 20 minutes, then light cornstarch dredge, teaspoon of oil and oven roast for 25 minutes.  1/3 C oil for onions.  

Took some time but not excessive.
Didn’t love it, but I was having an off day and probably did something wrong

On a quest to really enjoy tofu, I was delighted to see this recipe and followed it to the letter. What can I say. Disappointing. Took me close to an hour. For whatever reason the flavours were murky, no bright notes. BUT - used the microwave trick for draining tofu and the texture of that was perfect.  Will not make this again but remain an Ottolenghi fan.

On a quest to really enjoy tofu, I was delighted to see this recipe and followed it to the letter. What can I say. Disappointing. Took me close to an hour. For whatever reason the flavours were murky, no bright notes. BUT - used the microwave trick for draining tofu and the texture of that was perfect.  Will not make this again but remain an Ottolenghi fan.

Sweet and Sour Tofu With Barberries offers a delightful fusion of flavors, combining the tanginess of the sauce with the unique tartness of barberries, creating a vibrant and satisfying dish. The tofu's versatility absorbs the flavors, resulting in a balanced and delicious vegetarian option that tantalizes the taste buds.

Just made the recipe....pretty satisfied, did not take me that long to prepare.
I did really enjoy the combination of flavors orange/cranberries/carrots/saffron and spices.
Also did not have pine nuts and cilantro but it did not bother me, there's plenty of flavors already.
Served it with sauteed kale.
Barberries? Can I pick them off the hedge that separates us from our neighbors? Sounds like they're full of good stuff.
If you live in NYC you can buy them at Kalustyan's.

Marietta, I'd love someone to answer your question.  Any gardener knows there are waaaay too many barberries (a non-native, invasive plant, notorious for harboring deer ticks)around.  But, I'd have to assume they are the same ones you can buy.
I've never foraged them myself though I'd like to (none around my area, unfortunately). I hope this helps, I trust this crowd https://www.wildfooduk.com/edible-wild-plants/barberry/

I have a barberry bush hedge, so I wondered if the berries could be foraged for this dish. Turns out they are edible. They are thorny little buggers, so watch out! https://britishlocalfood.com/barberry/
I don't like tofu so I made this with boneless chicken breast and served with Persian rice.  Wonderful.
I use wheatgerm instead of cornstarch.  It works beautifully on tofu.
Adding this to my recipes to try list!

Very time consuming and confusing instructions.  Read through first.  Elaine made, baking tofu with cornstarch and olive oil until fairly crispy.  Made half recipe - 3 servings.  Nice light flavor. Elaine wouldn’t make again - David yes. Used cranberries. Julienne peeler worked for carrots.

After reading the reviews, I was pleasantly surprised by how much I liked this recipe and how relatively simple it is.  A few things to point out:
The tofu is supposed to absorb the sauce so it is not supposed to be crunchy
The cornstarch is necessary to thicken the sauce so if you decide to fry without corn starch, would recommend adding some to the sauce via a slurry

Overall, I’d recommend this recipe, it’s very pantry friendly (besides the saffron) and it’s fun to try a new flavor combination with tofu.

Interesting that folks thought it was laborious - I thought it was a great, easy weeknight meal! Mine was not bland at all. I used cranberries and also added Szechuan peppercorns and broccoli that I had sautéed separately in a touch of coconut milk, a dash of water, leftover oil from the onion mixture, and some scallions. Made coconut rice to go with it. I think the only change I would make would be less oil. Oh I also cut the water in half, seemed like it would dilute too much and also be too watery.

This recipe is very good, but you don't need anywhere near this much oil. I use 1/4 to 1/3 cup in step 3 and 2-3 Tbsp to fry the tofu. I also have never used saffron, but didn't feel like I missed it, this recipe is delicious without it, too. It's very hard to find in my area and it's quite expensive, though I'm sure it makes the dish even better. We have made the recipe twice this way and will definitely be adding it to our rotation.

title: Winter Minestrone With Cabbage Pesto Recipe
author: Yotam Ottolenghi

comments.commentBody: Yes!! This is my go-to winter soup. The first step of caramelizing the onion/carrot/celery mix is key and one that takes a little patience!!  I love using fire roasted canned tomato’s for this recipe.  I also use chick peas or white beans instead of black beans.  And for the non-vegetarians in the household sometimes I will make some small meatballs on the side that can be added to the soup.  Top with Parmesan cheese and a nice crusty bread ….. YUM!!!!

1/2 cup of olive oil to sauté the veggies????  Really? 

A quarter cup was fine.  Particularly given the pesto topper.
@PatC the olive oil gives the soup richness. It’s common to use a lot in Portuguese cooking as well.

I recently made Marcella Hazan’s Minestrone from The Classic Italian Cookbook, and she does, in fact, begin with 1/2 cup olive oil (plus 3Tbsp. butter!) to sauté the vegetables.  She also adds potatoes, zucchini and green beans prior to the cabbage, and throws in a parmesan cheese crust, which I highly recommend. I deviated slightly by adding preserved lemon paste (2 Tbsp.) and a Tbsp. of harissa. All in all it was delicious.
Dang, this is good!  I used good old green cabbage.

OH MAN THIS IS GOOD. Don’t skip the olive oil. Don’t cut short the celery/carrot/onion cooking time. It basically turns into savory vegetable butter. (Legit thought the cooking time was a typo, but I’m never going back). 

For those wondering about the cabbage amount for the pesto, I used a cup. (I also used walnuts instead of pine nuts and added some lemon juice.) I’ll be making the pesto again for pesto’s sake, and this soup is gonna be on repeat. 

This recipe is heaven.
Made this as written. The carmelized onions and veggies really bring the flavor and complexity.
To bump up the protein in this vegetarian soup, I'm swapping in tiny cubes of high-protein extra firm tofu for the pasta.
I make a similar minestrone, using all the fresh veggies available, and serve it with traditional basil pesto. Minestrone alla Genovese!
I read this recipe over a number of times and in step 6, the instructions say to use “remaining sliced cabbage”, remaining from what? About how much is needed for the pesto?

Step 3 says "Next add 4 cups of sliced cabbage" so I guess the pesto gets the rest of the 1/2 cabbage. Since cabbages come in all sizes it would be more helpful if it said how much to use for the pesto.
@Patty I looked at the picture of the pesto and noticed that it was rather green. Hence I used the same volume of savoy cabbage as of parsley. It tasted really good.

This looks delicious, but for the black beans.  It reminded me of chili. Black beans have a very distinctive flavor. I will try the recipe but use my usual cannellini or cranberry beans.  They are creamier...

(Warning: substitutions ahead) This is delicious! I added some already cooked grains (rye berries) rather than pasta which made it thicker and chewy, and cooked chick peas because that's what I had. Added anchovies as someone suggested as well as lemon zest and lemon juice at the end to brighten it up. A little parmesan on top and pesto from a jar. Caramelizing the onions and celery is key to getting a tasty broth. And less water made it more like a stew. Yum.
Why drain the tomatoes? Can the liquid be used later with the stock and water? Are the beans really not cooked a bit, maybe before the orzo and spinach are added? Thanks.
@Steve I didn’t drain the tomatoes. Seems wasteful!

Had some issues with the recipe, and in that for us, half a savoy cabbage *was* 4 cups, so had to guess what “the remainder” might have been. Used white beans, and sautéed the 4 cups of cabbage along with the other veggies in 1/4 cup oil. And we love the results! Oh, so delicious!

A common emergency meal in my house is canned minestrone enlivened by whatever dabs are in the fridge ... cooked pasta, an anchovy filet, bit of leftover veggies. Some parsley flakes 'freshen' canned soups, and a dash of green Tabasco 'brightens' it. I let it sit together so the flavors can 'get married.'

This was excellent. I used Rancho Gordo black beans that I had previously cooked plus their broth. Used kale instead of spinach, used a bit less oil but didn't skimp. Regarding cabbage pesto, the savoy cabbage half is sliced into quarters per instructions. After 4 cups were added to soup, a quarter wedge was left and that went in the pesto. Used walnuts instead of pine nuts. Don't sleep on the pesto, adds a bright, fresh element to the soup. Cooked orzo separately (better for leftovers).
Add anchovies and lemon zest, juice

Maybe the best meal I have cooked from this site. Followed the recipe as is. Used about 1 1/2 cups of cabbage in the absolutely crazy delicious pesto, but 1 cup would have been ok.  Three more meals now in the freezer!  Winter dinner is solved.

yes, use the 1/2 cup of oil (or a third). it’s obviously a different technique to braise the vegetables for 25 minutes in it until caramelised - adds lots of depth to the dish instead of just frying the vegetables in a tablespoon.
The cabbage pesto almost didn’t make it to the soup… I made the poor decision to taste it on a tortilla chip. I somehow managed to stop shoveling it in my face after about a 1/4 a cup was gone.
Loved this! I used napa cabbage instead of savoy because that's what the store had. I guesstimated about two cups of cabbage to go into the pesto. Super flavorful soup

Recently, my cardiopulmonary rehab team explained that I should be consuming 4 Tablespoons of extra-virgin olive oil daily (and in place of other fats) to lower my LDL cholesterol. However, olive oil has a plethora of health benefits beyond lowering bad cholesterol including fighting inflammation. This recipe works out to be roughly 2 to 3 1/2 Tablespoons per serving. Considering that it is such a healthy fat, and that the rest of the ingredients are low in calories, I wouldn't skimp on it.

Delicious!  Made some substitutions because that’s what we had: great northern beans instead of black beans.  And for the very yummy pesto: cilantro instead of parsley, and toasted walnuts instead of pine nuts. Added grated Parmesan to the pesto, and more Parmesan to the soup when serving.

Incredible. Reduced oil to do the veggies. Excellent flavor, well balanced and complex.

And then I ruined it by putting in the whole box of orzo instead of the half cup. Don’t be me! Read the quantities! (It turned out ok with some added broth but I am sad.)

I'm not a big bean fan but I had a can of red beans I wanted to get rid of, so I thought I would try this recipe. I used rice instead of orzo (and so, put it in during the 20 minute simmer), and Napa cabbage, but other than that I followed the recipe as is. It was good.
skipped the pesto, added all the cabbage and extra beans, completely delicious

I liked this, but could you change the spinach portion from cups to ounces? I can’t imagine I’m the only one who doesn’t totally understand how many ounces of spinach equals five cups, especially when the recipe doesn’t specify loosely or tightly packed (I echo this for all recipes that use cups instead of ounces for greens)

Delicious! Followed exactly but with 1/4 less oil than directed. Pesto is a beautiful addition. Placed a piece of broiled cod with a lemon squeeze in the middle of a large soup bowl & it made a gorgeous , filling, and healthy winter meal.

I thought that the 25 min tires for the mirepoix was a typo, so I sautéed them for about 5 minutes instead. I get that it’s not now! I’d also caramelize some tomato paste with them towards the end of the process.

My half of savoy cabbage yielded 5 cups so I had one cup for the pesto, which was plenty, but I ended up adding two extra tablespoons of EVOO and I included the celery tops.  My pasta was toasted Fregola, a little more than a half cup, and I used my veggie scrap soup stock in the recipe.  The extra liquid from the can of fire roasted dice tomatoes came in handy because of all the slightly more amounts of each ingredient that I ended up using. Recommendation: follow Yotam’s directions and learn.

I wanted to make this soup ahead of time, so I reversed some of the steps.  Knowing what happens to pasta if it sits in soup for a while (it gets soft and mushy) I cooked the soup without it.   Added the beans and spinach at the end of the cooking so it wouldn't get too soft.  Cooked the orzo the day of serving, but I cooked it using some of the drained liquid from the tomatoes.  Assembled everything at mealtime.  It was delicious

Probably the best minestrone I've ever made.  Very light and healthy! 
Didn't have savoy so used reg cabbage.  Will try with savoy next time and maybe some lemon zest/juice as suggested!
Also used Great Northern beans and mini shell pasta.

We really liked this minestrone, in which I also added shiitake mushrooms, spinach and young chard, as well as Rio Zeppelin beans which I cooked from dried, and which I think made a big difference from canned beans.

I also used wheat berries instead of pasta, and enjoyed their slightly chewy texture.

The pesto was a hit, though I’d add some arugula for more zip.

Really easy and delicious. I had about 2 cups of cabbage for my pesto and it made quite a bit. Pesto is a tasty edition to the soup, but the soup in its own was already flavorful if you’re feeling lazy and don’t want to dirty the food processor.

This was great on Day One and even better on subsequent days. Like some others, I used white beans (Great Northern). I wanted parmesan with it, so decided to add it to the cabbage pesto, and I won't look back!

We loooooved this. The cabbage pesto was the perfect topping. I used Napa cabbage instead of Savoy because that’s what the store had. The only other change I made was doubling the beans using cannellini and kidney instead of black. As soon as we finished this I wanted to make it again.

Wow! This soup is incredible. I’ll admit I couldn’t quite grasp how all the ingredients would come together and how it could have much flavor. But everything marries together so well - it’s fresh, perfectly balanced and delicious. Like another reviewer, I used fire roasted tomatoes and they mellowed as the soup cooked. This is going into my favorites folder.
Ha! I basically made this same soup yesterday. But, I had frozen pesto I made and froze in 2 ounce containers. Delish!
Completely and utterly delicious. Perfect for a chilly February evening. Trust me.
I swapped out the rosemary for cilantro, replaced the cabbage with bok choy, and added crispy tortilla chips on top.  Voila - Mexican/Italian pesto with an Asian twist!  Everyone loves it!
I browned up about one lbs of beef chuck, cut into one inch pieces, started well before making the soup. When braised for about two hours, then shredded and added to the recipe, excellent addition.
I too was thinking soup needed beef. Was going to try w beef broth rather than chicken or veg stock

this is a tasty soup but has a really large amount of fat in it (approximately 1/4 cup olive oil per serving, plus whatever is in the pine nuts).  it can be made with much less fat but still with a good result.
I hope this is not sacreligious - but I lowered the olive oil with the veggies and added 4 ounces of pancetta. It was by far one of the best soups I have ever made.

Super good recipe. We did make a couple modifications for food allergies... we used toasted almonds instead of pine nuts and thyme instead of rosemary. The half cup of olive oil to simmer the veggies at the start is awesome and definitely contributes to this soup being pretty luxurious. Really awesome, homey, tasty and filling without being too much. The Pesto is simple and fresh on top. Added a little extra salt to it to give it a little extra punch (after using low sodium broth in the recipe)

Yum! I used green cabbage because that’s what I had and worked just fine. After measuring the 4 cups for soup, left about 3/4cup for the pesto. Didn’t drain the tomatoes and used a bit more stock instead of water.
Great recipe. We used green cabbage b/c savoy was not available. Turned out great!

I just made this using previously frozen, diced, squeezed extra-firm tofu cut into small dice in place of the pasta and it worked beautifully, soaking up that flavorful broth. 

Don't skip the pesto. It's worth dragging out the food processor.
Ditalini instead of orzo, always. No reason to completely flip the script on classic minestrone. Otherwise, amazing.
This soup is DELICIOUS! Made as written except with plain green cabbage.
Such depth of flavor for a vegetarian soup! Takes a little while but definitely worth it.

I also used fire-roasted diced tomatoes and think they added a lot. The cabbage instructions were confusing, but I still used one-half of a cabbage and eye-balled how much to save for the pesto. That worked out fine. I grated some Parmesan on top of the pesto. Super delicious!
Forgot to add, this was Delicious especially with the dollop of cabbage pesto!

What a soup this is! Thick and rich with flavor and the cabbage pesto is the icing on the cake. I used about 1/2 of a regular green cabbage  (4 cups in the soup and about 1 cup in the pesto--I just kept adding until it felt right). Everyone's version is going to be somewhat different and all should be great. Dried rosemary (or any other herb/herbs) is just as good as fresh. After all this is a winter soup; the fresh rosemary sprigs are not available from outside.
This soup is yummy BUT it has a gratuitous amount of fat and caolories.  Saute the veg in 2 tablesppons of oil, not 4, and replace the pesto entirely with a dollup of lite sour cream.

Loved this, even with my mistake of grabbing the box of barley instead of orzo! Upon realizing my mistake, I at first tried to fish the barley out after I had already stirred it in, and then said, to heck with it, and added the orzo on top of the barley after I had cooked it for a few minutes. Turned out fine and had that hearty barley taste.
This was absolutley delicious

I am also confused about the amount of cabbage to use in the pesto.  I see from the photograph that it looks equal amounts dark and light, so I will use approximately the same amount of cabbage as parsley is called for.

It was delicious BUT--really the amount of cabbage "remaining" is a stumble (as others have noted) because even though you are told to use 1/2 a head of savoy cabbage it depends on the size of the head. I have to assume that IF any is left over 4 cups you may add it to the pesto thus avoiding waste. The recipe really should be reworded.

I made this recipe as written. I loved that all the ingredients are seasonal for winter and the soup was so flavorful. I would add twice the amount of beans next time for heartier fare, and would try white cannellini beans instead of black beans. The added pesto cooled the soup down too much for my taste and i didn't enjoy the contrast between the fresh and cooked savoy cabbage.  HOWEVER, the pesto is a delightful addition to tacos, so it isn't going to waste in my kitchen!
I made this the day one of the groups on the daily Connections puzzle was "stock - pasta - beans - vegetables"!  Having just reviewed the recipe helped me score perfect that day.

This was delicious! I didn't have enough spinach and supplemented with Tuscan Kale. And I used garlic confit instead of raw garlic cloves. Because we have a nut allergy in the house, we used raw sunflower seeds in place of pine nuts. The whole family loved it.

Terrific recipe! I used 1/3 cup oil in the soup which is way more than I usually would and am glad I did, the oil really adds to the dish. I do think it is crucial to the recipe to know how much cabbage to put in the pesto. I had no guidance whatsoever since cabbages vary greatly in size. As a result my pesto was too cabbagey. Will do less next time.
1/2 cup of oil is completely reasonable considering how many servings this is.

Made as written. I thought the orzo got lost in the dish and that overall the flavor was a little flat, even with the pesto. Added a nice squeeze of lemon a few slurps in, which gave it some much-needed zhuzh. Would double the orzo if I made again and would also consider adding a pinch or two of red pepper flakes to the aromatics and perhaps some lemon zest to the pesto. Little meatballs would be a nice addition for the carnivores.

@Erik here again. I’ve gotta say, this soup is much more intensely flavorful after having sat refrigerated for 24 hrs. And, honestly, I take back what I said about doubling the orzo. I’m really noticing it more on day two. For me, this soup just needed a day to achieve full flavor amalgamation. I do maintain that a little squeeze of lemon is a nice touch.
I found it had an oddly sweet taste so I played with it. First a splash of red wine vinegar - not much improvement.  Then some Marsala - slightly better. Finally cumin and found success !

So the NYT cabbage minestrone recipe says 1 medium = 1.5 pounds = 680g. Another cabbage "ragu" says to use 1.5-2 pounds cabbage for reference. Base recipe says to use 340g = 3/4 pound cabbage and the rest for raw pesto (eww). So that's where i would start.
Only cooked once. This dish is ideal for fall, the cabbage pesto is hard to produce without the right tools but can be made in around 15 min overall

Sooooo good. Followed recipe (yes, all of that delicious olive oil and all that time!), subbed lacinato kale for the spinach and used a 28 oz can of whole plum tomatoes instead of diced, plus added the leftover liquid when it called for the water to be added. I am blown away by the depth of flavor, this is a terrific recipe that we have basically eaten twice a day for the last several days. Topped with Parmesan and flaky salt, with a  hunk of crusty bread, this is a wonderful and filling meal.
Made as written. It was good, but it needed more broth. Maybe next time I'll add a 28 ounce can of diced tomatoes and another cup of broth? It was very, very thick.
Made this with left over beef bourgeon I had in the freezer to boost the protein. We are still eating it as it is a great comfort dish for cold weather…
I made it exactly as written, because of my respect for Ottolenghi.  Nothing special.  I will not make it again.

Wow! I was nervous making a vegetable-heavy soup for my 14 and 17 year old, but they loved it. So much flavor, they didn't notice that it's very healthy! The pesto makes it feel special. (I subbed swiss chard for Spinach, cilantro for parsley cause those were on hand). Will definitely make again.

I thought this was excellent.  Only change I made was reduced olive oil to 1/3 c. for the first step.  Made the pesto too, it was a nice addition but thinking any pesto would work if you don't want the extra work.  Looking forward to the leftovers.

OMG!  This is delicious! My savoy cabbage was big, so I put in 2 cups extra, I had a 28 oz can of tomatoes (rather than 14), and used a can of cannellini beans as that’s what I had on hand. Oh, and I only had fresh thyme, not rosemary.  So very slight variation from the recipe and it’s one of the best soups I’ve ever made! You have to make the pesto! It’s amazing in the soup.  I’d say I used about 2 cups of leftover cabbage. So, so yummy!

Great winter soup.   Lots of prep but easy to make once the prep was completed.  I wish my pesto had been successful .   I made it in my Vitamix and it was a flop:-(.  Definitely a stew consistency.  I will definitely try again
Not sure what I'm missing here, but the soup was meh at best. The pesto was super not to my taste and seemed to be totally out of sync with the soup. Not planning to make this ever again.
Don't know WHO took my Black beans, pheeeww, I had a can of kidney beans instead, yummi.

Really loved this! Added a scant 1/2 cup of regular lentils, because who doesn’t love lentils in soup?? Also had to use regular green cabbage, but otherwise made as written. I agree, the full amount of olive oil and the long cooking of the celery base are essential!
I have a family member who cannot abide rosemary. Will another spice work in this dish?
Consider thyme, marjoram, or oregano instead. Rosemary has a 'piney' flavor, and is commonly used in Italy. But as the author said, use the recipe as a guide to create your own soup.

Add basil instead. Always basil. 
Is it just me, or is Basil the best herb ever? Peace.
Would it work to sub ordinary cabbage for the savoy?

I think the reason the recipe calls for Savoy cabbage is that ordinary green cabbage raw can be tough and not suitable for the pesto which needs something that has a more delicate texture.
   If you'd rather not use Savoy cabbage, you could try brussels sprouts, cut larger for the soup and chopped fine for the pesto.

I cut the salt to 1t, and the oil to 1/4 c or so, worked fine. I substituted kale for spinach, red cabbage for savoy, and used fire roasted tomatoes w/ green chilies, since I had those things on hand. Yum! The solid base in this recipe makes substitutions no problem. Served it with a dollop of yogurt sprinkled with parsley and brewer’s yeast. I’ll try the pesto next time. Definitely will make it again. Oh, and I used 4 c of broth and less water to up the protein.

Prepared as written, except fresh tomatoes rather than canned, and found it terribly bland. Missing the traditional Italian oregano and thyme, I added them, along with a good dollop of tomato paste and a few red pepper flakes. The soup should also be salted at subsequent steps in the prep.
Comforting soup, used turnip greens instead of spinach and added fresh lemon juice to brighten it up, just what it needed
I guess this minestrone isn’t traditionally Italian, but the choice of black beans strikes me as odd. I’d use cranberry or cannellini. I like the idea of the cabbage pesto, though.
This is delicious!  Next time I will at least double the pesto.

Used less olive oil sautéing veggies. Used regular green cabbage. Pesto lacked a punch, so I added more "toasted" pine nuts, lemon zest, lemon juice, and parmesan cheese. Delicious! Thank Yotem for the outline here.
Made this tonight. The definition of bland. And pine nuts are $9 for a tiny bottle.
Many Italians use walnuts instead of pine nuts to make Ligurian pesto because they taste great and are much cheaper.

Thank you for this! I’m sorta the soup Nazi and not only love cooking soup (esp. minestrone) but am a bit stuck up on methods to do so. Between using black beans and a non-Basile pesto, I was intrigued. Made it yesterday essentially as instructed. Enjoyed the process and we thoroughly like the soup. Kudos. A nice alteration to a classic. I only changed carrot to a winter squash (same volume) and added a jalapeño and red pepper. Thank you for this recipe - a keeper!

I implicitly trust an ottolenghi recipe. So black beans in minestrone ? Ummmm….Ok! But added the juice from the canned tomatoes and liquid from the canned beans. I did add some oregano and crushed red pepper to the recipe but not all of the water so it was a thicker soup.  Delicious with garlic toast.

Delicious! Had to substitute Nappa cabbage for Savoy as couldn't find that, but it didn't make any difference so far as we could tell. I added 1 pound of oven-cooked Italian sausage (broken into small pieces) - stirred it into the soup with the spinach to make it a more complete meal. Only used 2 cups of spinach to compensate for the sausage. I will probably add more chicken broth during the broth stage -- maybe a cup or so more -- since the addition of the sausage made the broth seem less.
This was a spectacular soup as devised. The Napa cabbage we got at Boggy Creek Farm last week (before the hard freeze) was the celebrated ingredient! YUM!!!
Can this soup be frozen?

I mentioned in an earlier post that we prepared the recipe  as written, but was reminded that, in fact, we used 1/3 cup oil for the vegetables and subbed toasted walnuts in the pesto in lieu of pine nuts. We’ll be divvying out the leftovers carefully today because we woke up thinking about this twist-of-a-minestrone. !
Delicious! Got a sweet cabbage, which made all the difference. Double the recipe and thank yourself when you have it from your freezer in a couple weeks!

This soup was amazing. I made it mostly as the recipe specified, only I used the regular green cabbage I had left in my frig, didn't drain the tomatoes, and used 4 cups of chicken stock and some water. The pesto was fun and added a lot! I'll make this again. The colors were beautiful and it was very satisfying!
Delicious.

Very bland as written. Maybe our cabbage was not as good this time of year. Half a smallish savoy gave slightly more than 4 cups so I added about 1/3-1/2 cup to the pesto which seems to be the right proportion. The black beans were colorful   I don’t think this is a successful variation. The pesto was not good and we would make a more traditional pesto and minestrone. The orzo overcooked while we waited for the spinach to wilt  I love the technique of browning the vegetables first.

Delicious!  I used red and green cabbage instead of savoy.  I also used chicken broth and the leftover canned tomato juice in place of the water. I might try reducing the olive oil next time to cut calories, but it made the soup oh so rich.

Warming soup. I let the onion, carrot and celery sauté for the recommended time which was spot on. I also didn’t use water but instead 4 cups of vegetable broth. I did red kidney beans and chickpeas, along with the cabbage and other shredded veggies — as I don’t like the texture of cooked spinach. I also added a Parmesan rind to thicken and flavor. I made a traditional basil pesto. Definitely don’t skip pesto. It gives the soup a good heap of flavor.
Delicious. I used a bit less olive oil for the vegetables and a bit more broth and less water.

Love Ottolenghi recipes. So much I usually use his name in internet passwords. This was great- lots and lots of veg. Pesto was good and easy, happy I took the two minutes to do it. He really writes great recipes.

This was easy and good - I was able to use several items on hand - the cabbage, home cooked white beans from the freezer, half a large can of crushed tomatoes. Topped with fresh ground parmesan instead of the pesto, it was a satisfying supper.
Made this to the exact recipe. It was oily, bland, and very unappetizing!
This soup is awful. It definitely needs something to give it more flavor and the parsley in it via the peso makes me want to gag.

The list of Ingredients does not list the additional 3-1/4 cups of water that is mentioned in Step 3, and no one else has mentioned it, so I’ll go ahead and add it and see what happens! But I suggest this be corrected so that the first time cook of this recipe isn’t worried!
Has anyone tried to freeze this?  I'm guessing that might not work with the pasta in it...
I'd freeze it without the pasta and just throw that in when you reheat it.

Like most Ottolenghi recipes, this is a project.  Unlike most Ottolenghi recipes, however, this isn't wonderful. I used Israeli cous cous instead of orzo as that's what I had.  Otherwise, followed recipe.   I felt it needed more seasoning; the small amount of rosemary was lost after all that cooking.  The cabbage pesto was a "no" for me.  The taste was fine, but it looked like a bowl of vomit.  Added some lemon to soup which perked it up.  Overall, the soup was fine but not great. Won't repeat.

Soup was delicious but 
Not clear on the cabbage direction for the pesto.  How many cups of cabbage go into the pesto?

Added lots more broth. Otherwise it is more of a stew and not a soup.
The pesto was confusing. It did not seem to add to the dish. What was it supposed to do?

I could only find regular green cabbage at the store, and slicing half of it gave me 4 cups of cabbage. I threw that into the soup and put the other half of the cabbage into the pesto, and literally ended up with a MIXING BOWL of pesto!! Ha! I added some basil and lemon juice to make it yummier.

was just ok but I'm also not a huge fan of minestrone (should've known!) and just used basil pesto I had instead of making the cabbage pesto which seemed too complicated....also the diced tomatoes tasted bitter to me--I don't usually choose to use for that reason.

We felt there wasn’t enough flavor considering all the beautiful veggies. I skipped the pesto and added shredded parm at the end. It was actually a little too sweet for my taste. Probably won’t make again.

IMO, needs some more flavor.  Oregano or basil?  Agree with all about "one-half cabbage".  Give us measurements.  Cabbages come in all sizes, from one to five pounds!  

I omitted the spinach because it just gets limper and limper with reheating.  But if this serves your family with no leftovers, have at it!
@Leora I have metric beta system. It says 340g cabbage in the stew.

I subbed kale for the spinach (added in the cabbage step), and added a preserved lemon - delicious. Next time will add a little red pepper and probably another cup of beans…maybe cannellini. Shaved parm on top with the pesto would also be solid 👍
It was good and veggie forward. Easy to make, tasty, and used up all our CSA veggies at the end of the winter subscription. I'd make it again as it was simple and healthy.

My husband hated this and said it had no flavor. I added to anchovies and the juice and zest of 1 Lemon and tobacco sauce at the end. I disagree that there is no flavor. Where this falls short for me is it isn’t a soup - it s a stew. I add more water when I reheat it but it kind of eats like a pile of mushy vegetables.
The cabbage pesto was particularly tasty, but unfortunately the soup had more olive oil than my digestive system could handle, even without the pesto.

I'm a little curious.  Someone mentioned Marcella Hazan's recipe so I looked it up.  Her's has no garlic at all, and has it simmer for 3 hours !  Quite a different approach.  I wonder if this would benefit from a longer simmer.

Very good as written. I added 1T of Korean fermented black beans and some basil pesto to the cabbage pesto which I thought needed a boost. I do like the parmesan cheese in this type of soup and the basil pest added that along with some lemon.

The instructions are meta lessons for soup making beginning with the patience of carmelizing the vegetables to the withering of the tomatoes. Each step instructs your way to a better soup. Leave it to Ottolenghi to teach while providing an excellent winter minestrone. One thing I'd add, and this is just my personal take, the orzo gets lost in the mix and seems superfluous. IMHO.

Delicious, but I wonder if all that oil was needed?  Will try with less next time. Used the chard from our garden - stems went in with the celery and carrots, etc.   And threw in some leftover penne, which worked fine.

This was bland.  I even gave it a day in fridge before eating, as suggested; but that didn't make much difference.  The dominant flavor profile is garlic.    I am making my way through the copious leftovers with a big dollop of chili crisp atop each serving.   That is a distinct improvement, but I won't make it again.  too much prep time for a so -so meal.

Really tasty!  I made it with large couscous balls as I didn't have any orzo, and I forgot to put in the black beans (total accident -- I love black beans).  Still very tasty tonight and I suspect it will be even better as leftovers tomorrow.

title: Christmas Glazed Ham Recipe
author: Yotam Ottolenghi

comments.commentBody: If this recipe begins with a cooked ham, why does it need to be cooked for 2-3 hours at 325?
Because it takes that long to heat.
to ensure it's heated through and for the flavors to develop

It's fine.  No one at Christmas wept openly in either direction.

Lots of extraneous instructions and temperature changes.  I'm a fat guy in the suburbs, not a NASA Engineer.  Kudos to anyone who can follow all these steps while drinking and dealing with your In-laws.
@Mike V you’ve inspired me to keep it simple. Cheers!
@Mike V gave me a good Sunday morning chuckle. Thank you!
@Mike V I hope your last Christmas wasn’t as bad as what I’m anticipating this Christmas.
What is a city ham?
I must think the eight clementine halves steamed with the ham are peeled before putting in a blender. Correct?
No. The thin peel will add punch to the sauce. Leave it on!

I’m totally guessing here, but I believe since clementine/mandarin orange skin and pith is very thin, the pith won’t cause bitterness once cooked and leaving the peel on would allow both juice and zest into the mixture.

Two helpful rules for reading recipes that save a lot of confusion, and answer recurring clarifying questions on here:

1. Unless it says to do it, don't; e.g., if a recipe calls for a sauce to "simmer for 30 minutes," leave it uncovered; if recipe says "cover pot and simmer." put the cover on.
2.  Read and act from left to right,  e.g. "1 cup walnuts, chopped" means measure whole walnuts, then chop them; "1 cup chopped walnuts." means chop the walnuts then measure the chopped walnuts.
The entire clementine is used. The rind will be very soft after steaming with the ham and adds tons of flavor.

City ham is a term used in some parts of the United States for any lightly cured and/or smoked ham which must be refrigerated to preserve it. It is generally "wet cured", that is injected with or soaked in a brine solution containing high concentrations of salts. Wikipedia
A city ham is that basic ham that you find in a grocery store. Called “city” to differentiate it from country hams.
I think the clementine’s go into the blender skin and all.  The skins should become mellow post-roasting.
No need to peel the clementines. The peel adds a wonderful flavor - I use them whole in my raw cranberry relish.
This ham was delicious.  I substituted 2 oranges (unpeeled), since I didn't have clemintines.  I used Dijon mustard rather than French's.  It worked out great.
This is almost cloyingly sweet, and while the clementine flavor is nice, it's also overpowering, so much so I could hardly taste the ham.

This is the most delicious ham I have ever cooked. My grandson who does not like to try new foods, had a second serving. The clementines are perfect both in the sauce and as an accompaniment and the mayonnaise mixture is perfect.  This is now my favourite ham recipe.
THE most delicious baked ham I have ever cooked.

Our double smoked ham is cooked on the Bone,it’s an annual tradition and is then re heated on Christmas Eve.
Skin removed,scored and a clove in each diamond scored corner.
A mix of whole grain honey mustard and apricot conserve to glaze then baked for 2-3 hrs at 130°C.
The liquid in the bottom of the pan is Orange juice.
This year I think I shall encourage the man of the house to use Oranges halved as well.
New potar out of the garden and salad.
I treat to look foward all year to!!!

I had wondered about adding the mayonnaise to make a sauce - much to my surprise it’s great!! My family loved this ham.  Very delicious, no hint of bitterness.  The subtle flavor of the cloves blended beautifully with the clementines.  Followed the recipe exactly, but did sub light brown sugar for the glaze/puree, and regular white vinegar instead of ww vinegar.  Delish!!
In step 3 - are the clementine rinds included?
If the clementines were to be peeled, the recipe would indicate that.
What adjustments would you make for a pre-sliced smoked ham?
The recipe specifically calls for an "unsliced" ham.
A pre-sliced ham? Forget it, can’t be done. There is no drama in a pile of ham slices. They are good for ham and mustard sandwiches but that’s it.
My guess is that the clementines are not peeled. The skin is used to add body to the sauce like a marmalade which is thick cut.
Could you use an aged country ham instead? Cooking times look similar.

Skin, pith and all but seeds are included. The Claudia Roden cake recipe referenced in the header boils two whole oranges for two hours, and then blends them whole after seeding. A fabulous cake results. I believe an adaptation of her recipe is on this site as well.

I have made this cake many times because it is gluten-free, and my sister-in-law, who is gluten-intolerant, loves it. I think I have written before, but you don't have to boil the oranges. You can do it in a pressure cooker in 15 minutes once you've brought the cooker to full pressure.

At a holiday dinner my niece, a brutally honest Millennial, asked me what were those "awful disgusting dark things on the ham?" I told her they were cloves. She responded by saying, "Please don't ever serve them to her again." It got me to thinking, why can't I use ground cloves and put them directly into my ham glaze? Why indeed. It was a easier than the time-consuming chore of studding a ham with the whole cloves, thereby saving me time--a good thing during a busy holiday cooking season.
Cloves? Disgusting? Oh, my … they look pretty as decoration and ground cloves are dusty in texture unlike the cute little wooden flower buds.
You could remove the cloves before serving, but I'd tell the brutally honest Millennial to "go to the diner" -- as my mother often told me.
Do you think it would be safe to add a cup of water instead of checking it every 30 minutes, which doesn’t work when we are at a Christmas Eve service?
Could I use a bone-in ham for this?
I used a bone in as I love to have the bone for soup. It was just fine.
The glaze is fantastic.  Baking the oranges and blending them into the glaze is a great idea.

Must have done something wrong, but not sure what. The coating was bitter, super runny sauce in the bottom. The crust never hardened nor glazed.  We ended up wiping it all off and just eating the ham ( which was fine). I tried to boil down the sauce after on the stove and got it edible…but not good enough to keep the leftovers.

Made for Christmas dinner and was disappointed.   The glaze fell off in chunks and didn't adher to ham.  Maybe our ham had been injected with more liquid than the one the NY Times staff used, but I ended up with a lot of liquid in the pan.  No way I could mix all that liquid with a cup of mayo and make a glaze.  Ended up using the liquid to make a gravy which was tasty.  Fortunately nothing detracted from the ham which was delicious on its own.  But the recipe didn't enhance the ham at all.

Ham does not need the 2-3 hours cooking time. 
9-10 lb ham needs 1.5 - 2 hours. 
I used 12 clementines/mandarins. 
If roasted veggies are desired, I suggest roasting them separately, perhaps if desired in some bacon or pork fat saved from previous meals.
Ham might not "need" 2-3 hours cooking time, but it "benefits" from that. 20 minutes per pound is a good guide, with tender meat as the result.
I hate to say but this was a huge disappointment on Christmas Day.  Clementine sauce was very overpowering. I am sorry I made this and I seemed to have ruined our Christmas dinner.
A lesson to learn: never make a new (first-time) recipe for a special dinner.
We recently made this for new years, we are from the Netherlands and we were wondering if clementines in the us are maybe different from here? We made it and it was extremely bitter
What do folks think of the addition of mayo  to the clementine/mustard mixture?  I have never added mayo to a mustard sauce and it just sounds wrong to me.

@Liz I was reluctant as well, but gave it a try. It added a layer of flavor and creaminess that enriched the sauce, although I can’t tell you how. I started small, 1/4 cup at a time. That was a good way to find the right balance for your tastes.
Made it tonight for Christmas Eve dinner, will not be making it again.... was too rich and intense.
Table of eight from 6 to 73 found this superb. I had a bone in ham (don’t know why recipe calls for boneless). I can’t wait to make soup with the bone. The flavors are delicious. (Buy a big ham.)

What do you do with the remaining 4 halves of clementine? I'm guessing serve on the platter with the ham as garnish? I "wing it" in the kitchen more often than not, but I guess I'm just one of those people who would prefer, when following a recipe, to have a description of what to do with ALL the ingredients rather than just some of them.
All the halves are mentioned. Reread steps three and four. Step four begins with the ‘remaining’ Clementine halves.
Where can I buy a boneless city ham?
May I use a spiral sliced ham instead
Where can I find a boneless city ham in the NY area?
No explanation of what Demerara sugar is or how to substitute for it (even in substitution guide).
No explanation of what Google is or how to use it!
What brand of ham is the one pictured?
Any suggestions for a smaller hame for less people?
leftovers
Cook time of 20 minutes per pound sounds pretty adaptable to me!
Good flavor but takes too long to cook on a busy day. Would be nice to see tips on adjusting for smaller hams.

There was a point when I wondered what I had gotten myself into, but this turned out beautifully and got lots of compliments. The only step I won't bother repeating is precooking two thirds of the little oranges. Not worth the trouble. The sauce made with the pan juices and mayo was excellent. Never had to add water to the pan as the recipe suggests.

This is the recipe I used for yesterday’s ham, following the recipe carefully. The unanimous opinion (four adults) was that the clementine flavor was overpowering. Thumbs down. Yes, I blended the rinds with the spices. Thinking maybe use a (one) clementine in another recipe at some point. Perhaps a barbeque sauce.
The sauce/glaze was too salty. It was also too thick and not really a glaze.

Many years ago you printed a recipe for Monte's Ham. It was the best ham I ever made but it has been several years since I last made it. Well, this Christmas I decided to pull that recipe out and put it to the test. Unsolicited, several of my guest swore that it was the best ham they ever had.
I have searched every store -- grocery and small market -- in the Asheville area and there is not a single, unsmoked, unsliced ham to be had. Also not finding online. Can anyone help?

I'm in Statesville NC and was having the same problem. - I did end up getting a smoked ham - but I finally managed to find one that wasn't spiral sliced. I just kept googling butchers and grocery stores until I got lucky. We had to drive an hour - located it at The Stock Market - in 
Concord NC. I think it'll be worth the trouble. Good luck!
Bought a bone in ham. Is this going to work out poorly?

@Berndo I made it tonight with a bone in ham. I used navel oranges (instead of clementines) and zested them and then peeled the oranges in the first section of the recipe that you bake before blending so as to have less pith. Also used brown sugar. Turned out fantastic!
Did anyone else get more of a paste than a glaze? I feel like it’s missing some liquid.
Pleasant but not my favorite.  Mustard flavour was very strong
Best ham ever! The Glaze/sauce was exceptional and not too sweet like some can be. My family loved it and this will be our new go-to ham recipe.
I used half oranges half grapefruit which was a happy accident and added a little tartness to match the sweetness of the gammon. And then added some cornstarch to turn the pan sauces into a gravy

title: Spicy Roasted Mushrooms With Polenta Recipe
author: Yotam Ottolenghi

comments.commentBody: I made this tonight. I always follow the recipe the first time to experience what the chef intended. Excellent! I would not change a thing. Fast, easy, and super tasty. Thank you Yotam Ottolenghi
Made this tonight and it was delicious! A keeper, although I added a generous handful of Parmesan to the polenta.
I love how the spice oil includes cinnamon along with the peppers!

I cooked this tonight as a vegan dish -- no added butter or cheese to the polenta. And it was fantastic. The tomatoes (which I will double next time), mushrooms, spicy oil and cilantro add plenty of taste to the polenta. This is a dish greater than the sum of its parts.

I made regular polenta in the microwave. I roasted the tomatoes with scallions in the air fryer, followed by the mixed mushrooms.  Tossed the tomatoes and mushrooms with the cilantro, and then placed them atop the polenta. I put a jar of chile crisp on the table to season to taste. A quick and tasty meal.
Is there any way to get the ingrdient list converted to metric measurements (grams, litres, and kgs)?

@Mark NYT recently added a metric conversion tool. When you open the recipe, scroll down to the ‘Ingredient’ header and there should be a ruler icon to the right. Click this icon to convert the units to metric.

I found this to be an outstanding recipe made just as is.  It comes together in a complex, lovely way -- it's a very unusual flavor combination -- but it's quite easy to make.  I agree with the note to use multiple different kinds of mushrooms, and it was important to roast them a little longer than 15 minutes (in my oven) to make sure they were truly golden.  The spiced oil is fantastic.  A winner!

probably the best recipe i've made so far in 2024. as with all ottolenghi recipes, it makes 4x the amount of oil/sauce you need. i made this recipe while angry and crying; they say don't cook while you're angry so i figured it would turn out bad. i was wrong -- it's amazing. i added mint at the end and roasted the shrooms and tomatoes all at the same time, separately but on the same baking sheet. the cinnamon with the soy sauce... i'm still drooling over this.

My wife made a carrot ginger soup the other day, and asked me to taste it and see what I could do to boost the flavor. I had just finished reading this recipe, and thought that the flavored oil would be just the thing. Indeed it was. I’ll be making that oil to keep around for all sorts of uses, next to the chili flakes, I always have.  I’m sure it will be great with the mushrooms and polenta, too.
450 too hot. Made oil smoke and set off alarms. I lowered it to 400. Requires more time.
Delicious- don’t be shy with the tomatoes!
Agreed, I think you could easily double the tomatoes.

I just made this dish this evening and it’s a winner! I’ll definitely be making this one again. A few things I might change for next time: 1) Add the scallions to the mushrooms rather than the tomatoes as they were a bit over charred at 20 minutes. 2) Add the garlic and ginger a bit after the cumin and pepper flakes are cooked it a bit less. Perhaps doing this step right before plating. I added 1/4 cup Parmigiano Reggiano to the polenta just before the rest and would do that again. This pairs really well with a Sangiovese.

This was so excellent! So many wonderful diverse flavors tempered by a creamy polenta. And it’s vegan! The key is in that hot oil - don’t skimp on the cumin, or other flavors you might love (Szechuan pepper!). It brings everything together.
Beyond delicious! I had to use no cheese, and parmesan would have made it even better. The polenta tasted a little thin without dairy so I added 1.5 tbsp of ghee and it was great.

Delicious. I doubled the polenta as I was bringing it for a large potluck, and added more salt than the recipe called for, and then more soy sauce on top. Be careful if transporting, the loose oil will spill and make a mess.
This was delicious! That blooming spice mixture has changed how I approach plated dishes—I’ve been doing it for most of my recipes now with different combinations of spices and aromatics!
Could I use canned tomatoes?
Rather than polenta, try sourdough toast. Use greens (chard/kale) rather than tomatoes in winter. Pepper sauce and roasted mushrooms are delicious!
Loved it! Just the right amount of everything. I added a bit of nutritional yeast for a bit of a creamier, nutty polenta.

If you simmer the cinnamon in oil for 5 minutes, when you add the garlic and spices, the oil is so hot that it browns the garlic. I would simmer for less time, take off the flame, and then add the ginger, garlic and spices. Also, 450 was too hot for roasting and caused smoking oil. I would roast at a lower speed for slightly more time.

Rating from the boss, my wife, was good/ok/decent. Had a bit of problem with my polenta but we survived. Not sure about the chili oil since I didn’t have Aleppo pepper or Sichuan pepper, but it certainly was different. However the roasted tomatoes and mushrooms were spectacular and together the entire dish turned out great. Just the dinner during thanksgiving week. The monastrell wine helped too..
Forget polenta, this sounds delicious over plain rice!

This was really good. Works for a weeknight dinner, even with real polenta. Go heavy on spices. Maybe add garlic to tomatoes. I used cremini, portobello, and maitake mushrooms. This would also be a great over pasta.

Loved the spicy sauce and the vegetables. 
Will add Parmesan to the polenta next time, since it was a bit bland and didn’t really interact with the rest of the dish.
Incredibly flavorful and easy to make.
Mom, being Italian, started me on polenta early.  This is another “keeper” topping.  Thank you.
When does the ginger go in?
Matchstick sized ginger bits go in to the oil with the rest of the spices!
That was good. Easy. Unique flavor profile (esp. for polenta).
This was oily - I'd back off on the amount of oil in the finishing oil next time (but the spice mix was tasty).  This also felt like it was missing some protein - tofu or shrimp might be good.
Super tasty.  I’ll make this again, with one change.  My scallions almost roasted away to nothing, so I’ll add them later/cook them fewer minutes next time.

Fantastic, and our first attempt at a NYT recipe! Our notes:

* Used green onions since we didn't have scallions
* Used premade polenta block and creamed it with a touch of miso broth
* Grape seed oil - no smoke problems at all
* Roasted the tomatoes and mushrooms together, as others have suggested.

10/10, will make again as a side dish for vegetarian T'giving to add to mashed potatoes in lieu of polenta. Crazy umami richness.
@Alex and Mel green onions and scallions are the same thing

I have made this recipe twice in the past week because my family enjoyed it so much. Aside from roasting the mushrooms a bit longer to help them to turn golden, I followed the recipe to the letter. I loved the spicy, aromatic oil and I absolutely plan to make more to drizzle on other foods. This one is a favorite.

The chilli oil along with all the spices would have been too spicy for my family. I strained the chilli oil and then added in some of the spices back in. Served extra chilli  on the side. Topped the dish with feta cheese. Next time I’ll add pine nuts. Overall it’s a wonderful dish with an excellent flavor combination.

Delicious mushroom recipe.  I skipped the polenta, cooked the mushrooms separately for 8 minutes and then layered the mushrooms and tomatoes in a puff pastry. Amazing!!  If you like spicier foods, I would add some red peppers to the aleppos to give it a little more kick.  Definitely putting this into the rotation.
This recipe is lovely. My only recommendation is to double the chili oil b/c you will want to put it on everything!
Didn’t have cinnamon sticks or fresh ginger, so used 1 tsp ground cinnamon and 1 and 1/4 tsp ground ginger. It turned out delicious.

Interesting flavor combinations, but I am not dying to make it again.  It felt like one of those dishes that wanted to be a main, but it was missing some protein, but it's also too big to be a side.  I struggled with trying to figure out what protein to add since the flavor profile is so unique.
Took to a meeting of NORTH TEXAS MYCOLOGICAL ASSOCIATION potluck. It was a hit!
If I’ve got young kids and I’d love to make everything but the oil, does anyone have a storebought oil to recommend?

This was the vegetarian main dish at our Thanksgiving this year, in addition to the traditional turkey. I left out the Aleppo pepper and Sichuan peppercorns but did the rest of the recipe and served sriracha on the side for those that wanted heat. The kids all loved it.
Confused by the tomato roasting....instructions say 20 minutes, then set aside.  Then, mushrooms go in WITH the already roasted tomatoes?  So tomatoes roast a total of 35 minutes?
I think you roast the tomatoes for five minutes while you prep the mushrooms, then add the sheet pan with mushrooms to the oven for 15 minutes and take everything out at the same time.
Start the tomatoes roasting, then add the mushrooms 5 minutes later so that both are done at the same time. Apparently tomatoes take a little longer to roast than mushrooms.
What kind of vegetable oil is used here?
Delicious! I realized too late that I didn't have any polenta, so I served it over soba noodles, and it was a total hit.

So delicious! Decided to pan fry the mushrooms to get them crispy and I think that was a good a move. Also accidentally used twice the amount of ginger but it added a lot of great ginger flavor throughout.

We've made this twice since it first published, less than two weeks ago!
No negs, just some customizations:
-- worth experimenting with fresh varieties of mushrooms from the market, as in a combination of earthy mushrooms (as opposed to aromatic), e.g., boletes 
-- step 2 (aromatic fried topping) worth doubling the ingredients here and saving half as this is a keeper for adding onto other dishes later e.g., scrambled eggs; rice; noodles
-- step 4 this final oil bath was too much oil for us
I would love suggestions on time for using the air fryer for this.

Very good! Made a half recipe and served with some leftover cheesy mashed potatoes in lieu of the polenta. Roasted the tomatoes/scallions first in the air fryer (~10 mins), then the button mushrooms (~ 7 mins). Mixed with the spiced oil. Next time try the full amount of veggies for 2 servings, with less polenta. Was very flavorful although not spicy-hot, so maybe use more Aleppo pepper too.
How spicy hot is the oil? I can take a little kick but not too much. Should I cut the amount of pepper flakes and peppercorns?
Super yummy! I also added a bit of butter and parmesan to the polenta.
This is just delicious! Spicy but even the 11-year-old loved it.
This was yummy and had quite a bite to it. The polenta helped to cut the spice. As others have said, the polenta amount can probably be cut in half.

I made it even more simply and I think still got all of the flavors and textures. I roasted everything together, including spices, using sliced shitake mushrooms, for 20-25 min. at 350-375. No separate oil infusion step. I also made shoestring parsnips and roasted them at one end of the tray, as a garnish. As others did, l enriched the polenta with cheeses, like cheddar and pecorino romano combo. Truly delicious recipe. Thank you!
Also, I skipped garlic and soy sauce and added sliced red onion to the roasting mix.

I made this tonight —cut it in half since it was just me. Really good. I made regular polenta and added a quarter cup of reggiano. I started the tomatoes first and added the mushrooms to the same sheet pan 5 minutes later.
For Mark, I use Siri to change the measurements from American to UK etc

This is comfort food. Easy and satisfying. Made as directed and served with some crusty sourdough. Definitely will make the chili oil again for other uses! I don’t like using vegetable oil so used extra light olive oil and it worked great.

I made this on vacation, lacking many ingredients (including the tomatoes!). I used chili crisp as the base for the finishing sauce. This was a fabulous dish! I look forward to making it at home and following the recipe with care.
This was delicious. I made it with fresh cherry tomatoes and frozen mushrooms and it was great. Next time I will look for smaller cherry tomatoes and add double.

This was very flavorful! Next time I will use more tomatoes. I will also cook all the veggies on a single sheet pan because the mushrooms could hVe used a couple more minutes. I felt there was too much polenta for the amount of veggies, so next time I will also reduce the polenta. Would make an excellent accompaniment to a simple fish or shrimp!
I made it without polenta and it was great.  I wish I had drained the mushrooms before adding them to the tomatoes

Off the charts delicious.  I followed pretty much to a tea, but instead of creamy polenta, I used the polenta cooked in the oven and grilled squares and served the mushroom and tomatoes over that.  The oil splatters quite a bit when adding the ingredients, but worth the cleanup required.
Grate the ginger.  Add sausage.
Absolutely delicious. Made as per recipe. The second time I didn’t have access to an oven so sautéed vegetables in a cast iron pan, also worked. Agree that one could double the vegetables.
Excellent - I used black pepper instead of Sichuan and ground cumin seeds instead of lightly crushed, and I only had a 'block' of polenta. It was still a winner big-time!!! Will make it again soon.

Made this for a party of 6 last night. I upped the quantity 50% and it was perfect. I made the polenta from cornmeal (not instant), I added 1/4 cup grated Parmesan and 2 TBS butter   1 tsp fresh thyme. I followed others advice and used one large sheet pan for the tomatoes and mushrooms. Delicious! (Don’t skip the oil!) Thanks again to Yotam…
Excellent!
We were really looking forward to this but it was just underwhelming. Too many competing flavours meant it was less than the sum of its parts.
This was good but too many dishes. Add the mushrooms to the tomatoes, and use shallots instead of scallions. Halve the oil in the ginger pan.
Delicious! Added some red onion marinated in apple cider vinegar for a little acidity kick.

I made this last night and it was DELISH! I stuck to the recipe and used portobello and oyster mushrooms and my husband loved it! This is definitely a dish that can stand alone and it’s very flavorful. I did have to roast the tomatoes a bit longer though…but all and all great :)

Very interesting. It's really good, I've never had Polenta before so I was a little worried about it, but it's quite easy to make and is a good base.

The oil is verryyyyy good. The cinnamon hits + spices really make this great. Kudos.
Delicious! Full of flavor, but not mouth-burning spicy. I added a little bit of EVOO to the plain polenta for richness.
