The first time I made this I followed the recipe.  My family loved it.   The next time I prepared it I had to make some changes using what I had on hand.  I subbed shallots for green onions, campari tomatoes for cherry, and parsley for the cilantro.  We still enjoyed it and will definitely make again!  Great to have a meat-free meal!

I tend to follow a recipe on first two „ makes“ and then experiment if necessary but Yotam‘s are already usially perfection! Very easy and satisfying and a nice flavor. When I make polenta though, I generally prepare with a broth instead of water to enhance the flavor! Will share and make again!

This was an amazing recipe! I got a delightful array of mushrooms from our local farmer’s market and followed the directions as written. It resulted in a first bite, “Oh, wow!” from my spouse. Loved the level of flavors and easy by which it all came together. Look forward to making this again for future vegan guests.

This dish, doubled, got rave reviews at our Passover Seder! (Not all Jewish families eat corn products during Passover, but we do). The spiced oil makes it. My cheap apartment oven couldn’t crack 430, so I had to roast the veggies for longer and finish the tomatoes on the broil setting for some nice char. I used packaged polenta, which is all I could find.
Disappointed, the polenta is bland and the dish was greasy.

This was delicious!  Used a mix of blue, yellow and phoenix oyster mushrooms and some Shitake.  Not quite tomato season so used a jar of Trader Joe’s slow roasted tomatoes.  Subbed shallot for scallions.  Served over polenta.  Made the oil earlier in the day so it had time to season - such a surprise how good everything was together.  I am making more of the oil just to have for other things!
Didn’t love it unfortunately. The only flavor comes from the aromatics and spices in the oil mix, so I’d double those if I were to ever make this again. Must eat the dish hot before the polenta sets.

I really enjoyed this and made it as is the first time used a combination of portobello and button mushrooms that I had in the fridge. Next time I think I would make the chili oil with olive oil for better fats. I really enjoyed the pop of acids from the tomatoes and it was just the right amount of spice for my palette. Would make again
I think this would over pasta or farro. Has anyone tried that?
@Lea I was just wondering the same thing as I’m not a huge fan of polenta!  Egg noodles maybe?  I like the idea of farro too.  Have you tried it?
Has anyone made and reheated this for a potluck? Any preparation suggestions for a reheat scenario?
Really good, and quite showy!
Gorgeous to look at, easy to make, and delicious. The complex, marvelous infused oil is inspired. It’s certainly a sum of its parts, especially the cherry tomatoes. Get the best that you can.

Made this tonight. Used a mix of oyster and baby bellas. Mushrooms came out of the oven way too oily and slimy - would use far less oil in the future (we measured everything since this was our first time, but yikes on 1/4 cup!). Our first batch of oil + cinnamon turned into a massive smoky disaster, so please start at a lower heat then warm it up, and carefully monitor. We made the error of straining the cinnamon-oil to serve, since it was  extremely chunky, but next time we would serve it with all the bits and pieces, as the oil itself hadn’t taken on enough flavor to qualify as even remotely spicy - our error there. A lot of work for not a ton of payoff with this recipe, but we will try again and hope for a better turnout with the mushrooms, since we’ve liked other Ottolenghi mushroom recipes with cinnamon.

Really had to trust the process on this one 😅 If your stove or oven cooks hot, TURN DOWN THE TEMPERATURE. Otherwise, the scallions will burn and the oil will get too hot and pop everywhere. Just leave the mushrooms to in a little bit longer. Came out much better than I expected!
This was good and I liked the oil but it was a hassle to make. Next time I would just use a good chili crisp (Fly By Jing) and cut out 80% of the work.

Just a warning:  my garlic and ginger burned instantly when I added them to the hot oil.  

I’ll try again- we loved the mushrooms and tomatoes and can’t wait to enjoy them with the intended sauce.

The only thing I altered was making regular polenta vs quick cooking, since I have a stash of Anson Mills and it is too good to not use every time. Other than that, followed this to a tee, delighted by his chili oil. I made it a good few hours in advance so it could marinate. Deceptively simple, but the flavors dance around forever.

I wanted a lighter dish, and don't love polenta, so I served the roasted mushrooms with spinach (stole some inspiration from Sheet-Pan Roasted Mushrooms and Spinach recipe on this website) and without tomatoes, and served the oil on the side (not all guests like spicy). The result was out of this world. And even the spice-averse guests liked the oil.  I might make it again tonight!

This is great! Don't skip the oil--that's what gives it its flavor. 

Next time I will roast more mushrooms--one pound shrivels up in the oven and ultimately isn't very much for 4 people. 

Served with roasted broccolini.

This dish is absolutely delicious.   Thank you so.much.  my husband asked that it PLEASE go in our regular rotation  (much as we have such a thing!) Lovevthat it's vegan, great dish for nonvegan  company orator take to a potluck!
Is there a way to make this without an oven?

Pretty tasty, good way to use up some basic ingredients that sometimes you just have on hand. Would recommend adding peanuts or some other crunch. had some left over ssam sauce which broke up the oil and gave it an acidic punch. good change of flavours and we both finished our bowls!

I love this!  I avoid heating up the oven during Tucson’s summer, so I use an air fryer to roast the veggies. I pit them in all at once.  If the tomatoes are large, cut them I half. And, my Italian grandmother who occasionally haunts my kitchen suggests adding butter and Parmesan or Romano to the polenta. I use Bob’s Red Mill instant polenta.
Did it again. Delicious

title: Herby Sweet Potato Soup With Peanuts Recipe
author: Yotam Ottolenghi

comments.commentBody: I read these comments as I sit eating a bowl of ghee fried apples, basking in the sweetness of everyone’s experiences and the goodwill it takes to leave a positive comment. These comments are a daily stress reducer.

Incomprehensible to me that a veteran chef calls for bulghar to be ready in texture at the same time as ANYTHING, let alone sweet potato, that may lose flavor fall and fall apart. 

Veteran soup maker here advises: If texture of bulghar (or orzo or….) matters, cook it separately and add it, warm, at the end.

Bulgur is parboiled cracked wheat. For tabouli, you dont boil it, just soak in boiling water and then squeeze out excess water. It takes little time to hydrate/cook, so putting in a few minutes before the sweet potatoes are done makes perfect sense.
PS - for this recipe, coarse rather than fine bulgur will probably work best and won't get too mushy.

Aside from substituting butternut squash for the sweet potato, I followed the recipe to the letter. OMG!! My hubs said it was the “best thing I ever made” and it’s all he ever wants to eat— high praise indeed! I used a pastry cutter to chop up the squash a little, next time (and there WILL be a next time!) I’ll cut cut the squash into smaller pieces. Really amazing—I’m considering making it the first course for our T-Day dinner.
What would you consider a medium sweet potato? The ones in the grocery are enormous. How much do we think a medium sweet potato weighs?

Made a few substitutions just because of what I had on hand (cashews instead of peanuts, omitted 5 spice as I didn’t have it, herbs were reduced to just parsley as it was all that was in the garden) and also substituted quinoa for bulgur to make it gluten free. Very yummy. Will try it without substituting anything except the bulgur next time.

I left the skins on, personal choice. Reduced the liquid by a half cup to get more of a stew result. Absolutely LOVED this dish and so thankful for it being shared. My kitchen smells warm and vibrant at the same time with the blend of all the flavors in this soup. This will be a go to comfort food all winter.

After skiimming the recipe in the morning, this soup recipe dogged me through a busy  afternoon. I arrived home six hours later, cold, wet, and extremely hungry, so I set to work. Completely forgot about the bulgar. Used a dollop of frozen pesto instead of fresh herbs. No lemon or red pepper flakes bc of acid reflex flare up. Ate two bowls alone, listening to the rain pelt the sidewalk. Will make it again this week with the bulgar bc I'm still thinking of it. Delicious.

This was wonderful! I made it with farro, half the salt and no dill; otherwise stuck to the recipe and was very happy with the result.  It has a lovely, complex blend of flavors and the comfort of a good fall stew.  I highly recommend!
Nice soup! We used pistachio, pecan, and almond mix instead of peanuts. This worked great. The greens, red pepper and lemon are absolutely essential

Made this tonight for dinner (which I ate with my husband and adult daughter, so I would argue that it's great to eat with others, not just by oneself!).  Only change I made was to substitute Ras el Hanout for the five spice powder, which I didn't have.  Next time I think I will cut back the olive oil to a total of 4-5 tablespoons because I found it a bit too oily.  Otherwise, it was delicious -- a perfect soup for a cold rainy evening!
If I need to prepare gluten free pasta separately, do I omit the 4 1/2 cups water and use only broth in making the soup?

WPCuoca,  To replace bulgur (wheat so gluten) with GF pasta you may find it useful to know that 1C dry bulgur is about 3C cooked.  Some of the 4 1/2 C water is absorbed by dry bulgur as it cooks.  So I  would  omit water to start and use only the stock thru step 2 since sweet potatoes will cook in it.  When they're done,  I'd add in 3 C of already cooked GF pasta (a little  under done, then water or more stock as needed.  I need to sub GF too and using 3 C cooked navy or flageolet beans.
Made substitutions due to what I had available (pesto oil for herbs, farro for bulgur, chili oil w peanuts for topping) and this was magical. Would definitely try again with or without substitutions.
What is five-spice? What is a good substitute?
I couldn’t find bulgar either. Used red lentils. Turned out scrumptious.

Nothing short of fabulous! Every little part of this was terrific. 

Next time I won’t use Japanese sweet potato because there were too sweet, but that’s what I had on hand. 

I’ll probably cut olive oil a little, and cut sweet potato into 3/4” cubes. Or try with other winter squashes as the recipe suggests. 

And day two! after my bulgur absorbed fluids and became   a hearty stew, I stuffed it in halved roasted honey nut squash. Dang.

I'm on a mission to prove to my meat-loving husband that plant-based dishes can be complex, delicious and satisfying. This proved my point beautifully.  I used pistachios and forgot to add the sesame oil but it was absolutely wonderful!!!!

Excellent flavor combinations. I was concerned that the 5 spice would be overwhelming, but the addition of lemon juice at the end married well. I followed a suggestion to use less liquid for a more stew-like dish. I added some carrots to bulk up my two small sweet potatoes, and I preferred the carrots. Substituted farro for the quinoa.
This was shockingly good! Used a mix of Japanese sweet potato and honeynut squash. Could eat this all of winter/fall and be happy

This soup had a unique, delicious flavor that was perfectly complimented by the lightly sweet-spicy peanut topping. My one comment would be that if you are using orzo or a pasta instead of bulger and you won't be eating all of the soup right away, keep the pasta separate from the soup and add right before serving. I neglected to do so and the pasta soaked up all the broth when it was stored in the refrigerator overnight.

Sublime! Ottolenghi and his team have done it again. Instead of bugler — millet, cooked separately with garlic and butter, folded in at the end (thank you for the recommendation of adding later, demostiX). Perfect salt, perfect lift with the lemon, the dill and cumin pairing — Hail Mary!
What could I sub for peanuts?

martina, I think cashews would work as peanut sub here and they would play well with the chili / garlic oil in the prep.  But if peanuts are needing to be omitted for allergy reasons then it might be that cashews don't work either to be able to eat?  I think you could also consider pumpkin seeds, pepitas, here.
Chopped dried apricots?

Bulgur (especially whole wheat) is so hard to find these days (war in Ukraine?) so I substituted couscous, adding the last five minutes. I would like to try farro, though. Would it take lonher to cook than bulgur?

T Joe's sells quick-cooking farro (10 mins).  Regular farro would take longer than bulgur.  My pkg of "whole grain bulgur wheat" has instructions to cook for 10 mins, then cover and rest 5.  Prof. Google says bulgur has been pre-cooked (parboiled), so perhaps (responding now to DemostiX) the recipe's timing is Not off with respect to bulgur & sweet potatoes.  Maybe you were thinking of cracked wheat, which has not been pre-cooked?  Other notes would indicate the recipe works well as written.
My husband is one of those persons with an aversion to cilantro which family includes parsley.  Any suggestions for what herbs I can substitute for those in this soup?  Thyme? Marjoram?

You want leafy herbs though, right? Thyme, majoram, rosemary are all hard and I think the mouthfeel would be off. Maybe do something like basil, radish tops, beets tops, chives, and of course the recipe-listed dill.

Rosie, Since you need to omit cilantro from this my recommendation is to just leave it out or even add just a bit more parsley rather than introducing the flavor of either of those other two herbs.  In particular, marjoram and five spice do not play well together.
If your husband has an aversion to cilantro, you must know of an alternative that doesn't bother him. Use that. If you don't know what works for him, try thyme. It's always on your side.
Delicious. Subbed 1/2 c. rice to make it GF. Used one box veggie broth plus 1/2 cup water. Subbed cashews for peanuts. Will make again

I doubled the amount of sweet potatoes and added some cooked chick peas.
I think that ras el hanout instead of 5 spice powder would be a better choice.
I am happy to hear that I am not the only one who thought to add chickpeas! Ive got some lovely tiny ones from rancho gordo, and I will be using the ras el hanout from Nyshuk.
I thought this was just okay when I made it. But - when I reheated the leftovers two days later it was excellent. The flavour of the cumin was more evident. The topping is extra special.

We loved this soup and look forward to making and eating it again very soon. Because one of us is gluten sensitive, and to boost protein, we swapped 1.5 C of red lentils for the bulgur (adding the lentils when the bulgur would be added).

Followed to a T except two things - half the amount of salt (my usual for recipes from NYT) and doubled the peanut mixture. My partner said she could have had even more peanut mixture throughout. Absolutely delicious and nourishing. Not skimping on the three herbs and lemon is key.

I substituted short grain brown rice since it was all I had—brought it to a boil with its own water (substracted from what I added to my unpeeled cubed sweet potatoes) then when both boiling added it all. Added a little more broth as it cooked to make it the constituency I wanted. Used persimmon vinegar since had no lemons. No dill so added cut up chard. All raved. Will be cooking it again.

Terrific hearty soup for a chilly day. If you are a fan of peanut butter, I added some natural chunky peanut butter to the leftovers and whisked it into the broth. This made a thicker peanut stew-like dish.
Made it with homegrown kabocha squash and cashews and it was delicious.

I subbed farro for bulgur and added chopped baby spinach at the end. Next time I would do a mix of sweet potato and butternut squash. It’s a little sweet for my liking as is, but still yummy and comforting!
This was a delightful, bright, complex, and flavorful soup. Substituted buckwheat for bulgar based on what I had on hand. Perfect for a cool autumn night!
I followed the recipe to the letter, and it was delicious!!  Will definitely make it again, for company.  Wonderful mix of flavors.
So delicious and more than the sum of its parts! I used a winter squash and orzo as I did not have sweet potatoes or bulgar. But the flavors survived and thrived. Will be a regular.

I am a dedicated carnivore who does not look forward to vegetarian night (3x week).  This blew the family away, goes into regular rotation immediately.  Used 4c chicken stock to empty the box, 2c water, otherwise no changes.  Perfect.
Added preserved lemon. Delicious!

This soup was unusual and nothing like anything I've ever cooked before. I did half bulgher and half lentils, which I cooked and added at the end. Really enjoyed the end result but every component felt necessary to me.
In my experience, farro takes about 25 minutes to cook, as opposed to about 15 for bulgar.

I have never commented before but this soup was simply delicious!! Terrific, comforting flavor and texture. The only thing I changed was to make the amount of red pepper flakes half the recommended amount because I don’t like spicy. Don’t leave out the fresh dill- it really adds something to the flavor. So yummy!
This was great! the peanut topping is a real texture winner and all around great garnish.  Will make more next time to save for other dishes.

Made this tonight with my lovely foodie friend.
I used zatar instead of 5 spice. Herbs from a the garden including parsley, thyme and oregano. I omitted the sugar in the peanut sauce. I contemplated using chickpeas but didn’t bc  I knew the bulgur would be filling. Next time I may swap in chickpeas and not use bulgur. This recipe was delicious and easy! Definitely making again.

I'd take it easy on that two teaspoons of salt! In the end I had to add coconut milk yet it was still on the saltier side. Apart from that it's extremely yummy! Substituted bulgur with orzo, and the five spices with selfmade garam masala (  freshly grinded fennel seeds and star anis) and threw in a few left over cooked potatoes and it was delicious! Perfect for the upcoming cold Berlin winter, will definitely make this again!

Flavors in this are phenomenal! Had no fresh dill so used 1 tsp dried. Worked fine. Used bulgur (Bob’s Red Mill from Whole Foods—yes, it can be hard to find bulgur rn.) It soaked up all the liquid as it cooked! When it was cooked, the sweet potatoes were super soft. As someone mentioned, I will likely pre-cook the bulgur separately next time if I’m using the same brand. You can make your own 5 spice powder—basic 5 are: Cinnamon, star anise, fennel, clove, Szechuan pepper.

Loved this. Added a pound of merguez that I cut up and browned separately while the soup was cooking, and fried the peanuts in the lamb fat. Used fregola instead of bulgur. Used purple sweet potatoes and they over cooked a bit but still tasted great. Doubled the hot pepper and cumin.
Used Garamond masala. Yummy but use a little less hot peppers.
Every bit as good as people say. Used gousangchong instead of red pepper to calm it. Substituted maple syrup for sugar. Still delicious

I made this with Murasaki sweet potatoes and kabocha squash , chickpeas and farro instead of the bulgur . I put spinach in at the end, and more lemon than called for . It was very good . I also used ras al hanout instead of 5 spice , which I think was a good move, there are overlapping flavors between the two blends . A good dollop of yogurt on top brought more acidity to the finished dish as well

I am obsessed. 
I used leeks instead of onion, and carrots and kabocha squash for the sweet potatoes. 
Browned the leeks in the pan, roasted the carrots and squash in the oven and cooked the bulgar separately before combining everything.  
A lot of steps for just soup, but it was great, life-changing with the lemon and peanut topping and even better the next day, when i added a half quart or so of stewed tomatoes.

This is a great soup. I accidentally doubled the oil content bc of the 6 tbsp amount in the beginning. I would halve the bulgur so that it doesn’t sop up all the broth if you have leftovers. Overall nice depth of flavor and a healthy vegan option.

I made this as written.  Agree with the other reviewers that the herbs (especially the dill!) and the peanut mixture are really key.  I reheated leftovers the next day and initially forgot to add the peanuts and it was not nearly as tasty.   It needs the crunch but more importantly the spice.  I will definitely make again.
I made this with butternut squash and orzo.  I wasn’t sure about the outcome, but it was very tasty. Even my meat loving husband enjoyed it!

This is my first note. I had to write it because the soup was so good! I followed the instructions with only 2 substitutes. Quinoa instead of bulgur and almonds instead of peanuts. It was divine! Hearty, full of aroma, perfect for a rainy day in London.

We love this soup. We’ve made it with pumpkin and with sweet potato. I can never find bulgur in Maine, so I make it with farro. It is so good that we made it for Christmas Eve this year. It is somehow light but filling, warming but summery.

Despite having enjoyed tons of NYT recipes, this is the first time I’ve felt compelled to leave a review. I made this recipe as written except for a slightly shorter cook time on the bulgur because I wanted more of a soup than stew, and I am blown away by the depth and complexity of flavors this simple recipe builds. It’s warm, satisfying, and just down right delicious.
used a white Japanese sweet potato which is denser. Hearty but I miss the texture of a yellow sweet potato. Next time! Love this recipe!
What can I sub for sesame oil here? My husband is allergic.
Dean, You can leave out the 1/2 tsp of sesame oil, no need to replace it with anything, with no worries for outcome of the soup.

I was just wondering what to do with the butternut squash I had sitting around for weeks when i saw this lovely recipe. I subbed the squash for sweet potatoes and subbed in barley for the original grain. Both of these subs make this a much lower glycemic index meal and far more diabetic friendly! Also used the pesto as another reader suggested as my herb farm was depleted. Lovely fall dinner!!!

This recipe looks delicious. Does anyone who has made this have thoughts about using chickpeas ( or other beans, lentils, etc.) in place of the bulgur? I would like to up the amount of protein a bit. Thank you.
Use half of the water- only 1 or 2 cups. Forget the bulgar and add more sweet potato. Try adding leftover chicken and a bit of curry powder.

For the person who asked about water quantity if using already cooked gluten free pasta: I weighed a box of jovial malfalda pasta before and after cooking and found that it doubled in weight. So my guess is reduce water by a weight equal to half the weight of your cooked pasta.

Made this tonight—absolutely delicious! Full of complex, warm, autumnal flavors.

I took others' suggestions and halved the amount of water for a thicker texture, but doubled the lemon juice at the end. Also halved the amount of oil and made extra peanut topping cuz why not. Would love to try this with a dollop of creme fraiche next time for an added creamy dimension.

Just made this.  Wow!  I still have fresh herbs in the garden; the only sub I made was whole wheat orzo( about 3/4 cup) for the bulgar.  The topping is amazing-- I do think Aleppo is much better than crushed red pepper for this.

Love this soup! I made my own 5 spice by adapting one from the Woks of Life blog. The blog recipe is actually 6+ spices but the blog author calls it a "5 spice mix". I left out the Aleppo pepper Mr. Ottoleghi calls for because I used Sichuan peppercorns but you could probably use both. Here's the mix I used:
1 tablespoon ground cinnamon 
2 tablespoons fennel seeds 
1 tablespoon Sichuan peppercorns
1 teaspoon cloves 
4 star anise
1 tablespoon ground ginger powder

Amazing, full of complex flavors that the sweet potatoes and bulgur absorb perfectly. 
I did not have cumin seeds so used ground and sautéed it along with the onions. I also used a “course”  larger grain bulgur (would not recommend fine bulgur for soup). Threw in fresh ginger slices which further enhanced the flavor. The peanut sauce is a must and it is perfect.

Does anyone else cook the five spice in the oil until fragrant as a first step? After that, I added the onion and wild rice (no bulgur), the sweet potatoes and then a kitchen sink approach (celery, peppers, spinach), the herbs doubled and simmered in stock until we could not wait any longer! Made a sweet chili sauce for the peanuts. Green onions on top.

Soooo delicious! This soup meets some craving I never knew I had. For other TJ grocery shoppers…they have precut squash (2c exactly) & seasonal frozen bulgar & squash (not a lot of squash..)(they don’t sell dry bulgar). Another tip: I couldn’t find five spice but it’s made of things that you probably already have (fennel, cinnamon etc). I didn’t have the clove, and it tasted great anyway!
Made 2 substitutions: cashews for the peanuts & turbinado sugar for the sugar -- which is not much of a deviation from the recipe & worked really well. Everyone at my table loved it.
Made with chicken sausage and mustard greens that needed to be used. Pepitas instead of peanuts. One qt broth, no water.

I finally made this yesterday.  Yummy, & very cumin-y.  I followed the recipe almost exactly, but halved the bulgur, adding 1 cup water & 4 cups stock instead of 2.  It's acceptably soup-like today.  When I opened my package of cilantro it was a slimy black mess, but dill and parsley alone were quite tasty. I bought some cilantro today for the leftovers.  For me, this recipe will serve more than 4.  The peanut topping is everything, as many have noted.

This is G/D delicious! 

You can sub barley for bulghur – boil for 20 min and the potatoes aren't too soft but the barley is nicely cooked. 

I didn't have five spice so I used a spice blend I had laying around and it's great. Also used a ton of lemon zest and didn't really measure anything in this recipe and it's still an A+. So cozy.
This has become one of my go-to soups, but I never use bulgur or any grain. For me, it's about the sweet potato and peanuts, and whatever of the seasonings are available. Works every time.
This soup is fabulous! 10/5

I thought this was great!  I really liked the peanut topping and the rest of the soup. I might make  it with butternut squash next time, but loved it the sweet potatoes and greens. Made as directed, but didn't have 5 spice so just used  a bit of cinnamon, allspice, nutmeg and graham masala and can imagine you can't really go wrong here (although I'll get some just for this!).  The  bulgar cooked for quite a while. I don't think ruined the texture at all.   Delicious and felt so healthy!
Complex, amazing flavor combination
Made this exactly except used Farro. It was a big hit. Whole family enjoyed it and asked for it again
Made with delicata squash. Otherwise followed closely. Absolutely delicious.

Forgiving recipe - added celery and carrots in at the beginning for extra veg. Had cooked lentils that needed to be used in the fridge so opted for those in place of bulgar and cut the amount of water. Didn't have peanuts but used cashews and almonds, delightful. Could use more salt than the recipe called for and the lemon juice gives a nice brightness at the end.
I made this as written and it is absolutely delicious. Agree with the comment that gave it 10/5 stars. Maybe even 11.

Add carrot. 
Used short grain brown rice instead of barley.  1 c barley to 3 cups water.  So subtract 3 cups water from recipe as brown rice is made separately.

This recipe finally and belatedly decides it for me: Ottolenghi is a genius. His recipes are delicious with surprising ingredients, and doable for us commoners. I am grateful for this chef and recipe writer.

I have made this twice now. I really like it. It's simple and pretty quick. If you find yourself rich in sweet potatoes, it's a nice way to use them up. The bulgar/sweet potato base is mild in flavor and I had to add a bit more salt, and sometimes I riff on the spices (but don't skip the Chinese Five Spice!), but once you add all the herbs and lemon and the garlic chili peanut topping, it brings it home.

I followed the recipe nearly 100% substituted Garam Marsala for the 5 spice as I didn't have it...and because of my sensitivity to spice, i reduced the red pepper flakes by half.  Absolutely a delicious dish! Definitely a make again!

I wanted a thicker stew, vs a soup, so I added four cups of broth and no water. Also added a bit more sugar and peanuts. I used Za’atar instead of five spice, as others did. Delicious!!! Use a pot large, not medium!

This soup really is more than meets the eye. Packed with the warmth of spices, comfort of grains and tubers, freshness of herbs and citrus, it's got all season versatility! Might even taste great at room temperature or as a puree (herbs, grains, topping added separately). I made replacing bulgar with quinoa and without cilantro for a weeknight all in one dinner. It was a hit!

The peanut topping is unusual and in my opinion is the best part. I will double the quantity of the topping next time I make this. My boyfriend who does not like sweet potatoes loved this soup!  I've made it twice - once with orzo and once with bulgar, and we both liked it much more with the orzo.
So good! Made with quinoa instead of bulgar. Lots of varying flavors and it hits all the notes. Will definitely be making it again!
Very flexible recipe. Skipped making the topping and used chili crisp and dry roasted peanuts. It was great!
Best soup I’ve ever made, with a couple of substitutes: almonds instead of peanuts and quinoa instead of bulgur.
Are these sweet potatoes or yams? They look like yams on my monitor

Sweet potatoes. It is unlikely that you're encountering actual yams in a typical American grocery store. In the US you can find yams at African, Caribbean, or Asian markets. This recipe calls for sweet potatoes though.
I really am not a fan of sweet potato or butternut squash. My guess is that regular white potatoes are no substitute, right?
I feel like carrots might be a better substitute than white potatoes, because carrots have a similar sweetness to sweet potatoes. How do you feel about carrots?
What is five spice powder?
Can I put quinoa to make it glutenfree? Or should I look for a gutefree pasta?

Wonderful comments. I wish I were not as restricted, idea-wise, as I wanted to save these comments for possibilities when I begin to make this soup, many times. I am not a great cook. I DO look forward to this soup, though. Cold weather is already sneaking up on us.
I can't stand peanuts. I might substitute toasted pine nuts. YUM.

Do NOT buy pine nuts from Asia, particularly China -- pine nut gatherers / poachers are stripping the forests (as well as trampling them), leaving them barren. The result: wildlife that depend on pine nuts to survive winter are starving and, in some cases, becoming endangered. Buy US or Italian pine nuts. Yes, more expensive, but you'll be doing a good thing for wildlife and maintaining planet health / biodiversity. And pine nuts from Asia have a gross lingering metallic taste.

Excellent flavor, and so easy. We thought it was reminiscent of pho flavors. I used winter squash and followed the recipe almost exactly, because it was the first time I made it.  One exception: I did add some diced tofu for protein. And, I  rarely follow measures for salt. It’s very good without the chile too, for anyone who is sensitive to heat.   Will make again for sure.
Very nice and cozy soup. I sautéed some mushrooms and added them to the mix after the potatoes and I'm glad I did. But why is bulgar so expensive lately? It's a simple grain...

Mods: I didn’t have Chinese five spice so I put anise, cinnamon, cloves, black peppercorn, (about 1/4 tsp each or equivalent for whole spices) and ground cumin (1/2 tsp) in a tea bag and added it to the pot after adding the broth and water.  It was flavorful but none of the spices overwhelmed. I cut the sweet potato into bite-sized pieces and cooked everything for 5 mins in my instant pot. I used farro and cooked it separately as someone else suggested. It turned out very good.

Forgot to buy bulgur, subbed in white quinoa - worked and tasted fine, but I ended up with something way thicker than stew—still delicious—but not at all brothy, more like thick oatmeal consistency (add more water?). Lemon at the end is super important to brighten the flavors—do not skip or skimp. Nut topping was simply outstanding.

Served this to unexpected company and got rave reviews and requests for the recipe. This one is a keeper.
Added some Pepper Plant Sauce to the second bowl, and it really came together. Delicious.
Not sure what happened, but the bulgur soaked up all of the broth and water.  I was left with a tasty porridge/bulgur bowl though.

Hands down the best soup I’ve ever made. Like another reviewer I was also worried about bulgur needing more time than sweet potatoes but they cooked together perfectly in those recommended 15 minutes. I made it a bit thicker to make it more like a stew than soup and made it as the main dish for dinner. Everyone loved it! Well done again, NYT!!!
So this is a soup you make when you want to be alone, but it is enough to serve 2 - 4 people.  Does it keep well refrigerated for a few days, or freeze well?

So yummy, five stars.  Five spice not my favourite so substituted ras al hanout but otherwise made as directed.  Watch the salt.  My chicken stock was no sodium but I forgot I wasn't using Morton salt - so my soup was salty but still ok.  If the stock and peanuts had been salted it would have been too much.  Next time I'll add half the salt and check after I add the stock etc.

Amazing. Used farro, a cup of cilantro, skipped dill and parsley (we love cilantro), did not have enough olive oil so used safflower oil for the peanut topping. Also put all peanut topping ingredients together before warming instead of the two separate steps in the recipe. The topping is a must have. Added a little more broth at the end because it was a little too thick. So delicious!!!!!
I loved this!! I ended up with very little broth at the end, so next time I’d add at least 2 cups more stock and perhaps some more spices.
I found the same thing.  I'll just add some more water when we eat the leftovers.
wonderful depth of flavour and so comforting.  Followed the recipe as written.  A definite keeper of a soup

This was excellent!  Perfect for a dreary fall day.  Did not change a thing.  Flavors are really wonderful. The fresh herbs and the peanut topping add a lot.  Do not leave these out! Next time I am going to try almonds instead of peanuts.
Absolutely delicious! Used whole wheat pearl couscous as I had no bulgur on hand. Flavors spot on as usual for Ottolenghi. Peanut topping elevates this. Going into regular rotation

Soup turned out tasting terrific.  I could not find bulgur anywhere.   I used Organic whole wheat Orzo instead.  
I also did not have vegetable stock, my wife does not eat meat.  I used a can of coconut milk just to try something different (I've been thinking of making Thai soup, that's why I had the coconut milk).  
Used almonds instead of peanuts.  

My wife and daughter, me too, thought the soup was really great.  I'll try it again soon with all of the ingredients listed on original recipe.

Another thing:  I made a doube batch so it took me longer than the 55 minutes total time.  
The Almond topping I made I used brown sugar instead of table sugar.  The contrast of the white soup and the dark topping made the soul take on an entire finished look than the original soup posted with original recipe.
What do you think would make a lovely Gluten Free option for the bulgar? GF pasta doesn’t sound very exciting.
Hi Kat, I used quinoa and it worked great! One thing is that leftovers will continue to absorb the broth, so probably good to keep some extra broth on hand to add a splash upon reheating.

I made almost exactly as the recipe but I left out the sugar in the peanut mix. Couldn't find sesame oil, so I used peanut oil and sauteed with sesame seeds. Also experimented with adding different sweet potatoes - japanese ones were great. Delicious! Can't wait to make again.

It was just that kind of day here today, gray and rainy (well, duh, it's Oregon), AND all I was missing was one sweet potato, an easy pop to the store.  Added a touch more salt and pepper, plus a shake of Aleppo pepper into the brewing soup, and used pistachios instead of peanuts because I had them.  Per one commenter's advice, I made the bulgur separately — just soaked it in boiling stock — and added it when I removed the soup from the heat.  It was perfect.  What a winner!

Delicious!   Wonderful, warm spices.  I added a fistful of baby spinach for some extra green. 
 I was skeptical of  sugar on the nuts (I used cashews instead of peanuts), but it was really nice.  Definitely a keeper!
Is this dish cooked covered or uncovered?  The recipe says "undisturbed".   ???  I cooked it uncovered and lost too much liquid, but it made an absolutely great stew.

It's souper good! Didn't have 5 spice - used quinoa instead of bulgar - dill, cilantro, chives for the herbs. The crunchy peanut topping adds a great texture, just be careful to keep an eye on it as you saute the nuts, I burnt mine on the first try! Looking forward to making this recipe all winter long.
Made this yesterday-followed the recipe exactly and love it!

This was so delicious!  I had some leftover cooked Calabaza. I used parsley, lovage, fennel, and marjoram for the herbs. I used cooked rice in place of the farro. I added a little bit of Szechuan peppercorns to the peanut mixture. So good.

Trying not to be a downer (I love his recipes) but this one really didn't work out well for us taste-wise. Maybe it was the dill? Maybe the size of the potatoes? I don't know. Leftovers (mostly all of it) still in fridge waiting for a miracle.

I don’t know how much 2 medium sweet potatoes is thought to produce, but there was no way I could have cooked this in a medium sauce pan. I used a Dutch oven. I also cut the sweet potatoes into a 1/2” dice and they cooked within the recommended time. This tasted great the next day with shredded chicken added.

Recipe is great as is. I like to add a serrano with the onion for some base heat and the chili freshness.

One thing I’ve found is that I typically have the bulgur go for 20-22 minutes. It has a good toothiness but doesn’t feel underdone.

Quite surprised that the unusual blend of herbs/spices came together so harmoniously. This was tasty, filling, and more subtle in flavor than I anticipated. I used low-salt chicken broth and did not add any salt. I used 4 c broth and 1 cup water and still had liquid after the bulgur had cooked. I followed the recipe otherwise (including cooking in a medium saucepan which was quite full), and I look forward to leftovers later in the workweek.
Thought it needed a deep note - a splash of Worcestershire sauce did it for me.
So delicious, subbed butternut squash for sweet potato and made the bulgur separately.

I followed the recipe with the small modification of adding the peanut topping to the hot peanuts to cook the garlic slightly. The end result is quite thick so I would cut down the bulgur to 3/4 cup next time or keep it separate as another reviewer suggested.

I used Trader Joe’s chili oil instead of making it as the recipe says. I also added Italian sausage for protein. I subbed brown rice for bulgar, both of which take longer than sweet potatoes to cook. I didn’t want the potatoes to be mush, so I cooked the rice in the broth for 30 minutes then added the potatoes for the last 15 minutes. Delicious!

This recipe blew me away with how good it was and the combination of flavors was so uniquely good! The nut topping is an absolute must and smells amazing when the hot oil hits the Aleppo pepper. For a vegetarian meal, this is wonderfully filling and complex in flavors. I will certainly be making this again in the future and has inspired me to use fresh herbs more liberally in my kitchen.
Delicious but the 1 cup of bulgur overwhelmed the soup so that there was almost no broth. Next time I would use ½ cup bulgur.

Made to the recipe- it was hearty but bright with fairly complex flavors and textures. Used a massive sweet potato that weighed a little under 1 1\2 lbs. Regarding cooking time for bulgur and sweet potatoes it would depend on the size of the potatoes  - I boiled both for 17 minutes.

Made this with farro and cut the sugar in half — it’s a really lovely soup to add to the cool-weather rotation. The acid from the lemon is necessary and the blend of herbs was perfect. Next time I’ll omit all the sugar (I didn’t like the hint of added sweetness — the sweet potatoes hit that note for me) and I’ll add the sweet potatoes much later in the cooking process so they don’t soften so much (they were almost mushy-soft and I like them a bit firmer). I’ve recommended this recipe to friends.

Made this with quinoa and topped with pan fried chicken thighs (cooked separately). I did something wrong with the peanuts as they weren’t particularly good but overall, flavorful and would make again. Feels like a dish you can riff on easily, with other veg.
Way too oily for us—cut to 2 Tbs total. Also, just 1/4 tsp 5 spice powder. And chili powder with just a pinch of pepper flakes in the nut topping. Pistachios worked great. Otherwise lovely!

Oh man this is great. Made it for family dinner, had seconds. Ate it for lunch, am sad that it’s gone. Contemplating making it for dinner again tonight. used lentils in place of the bulgar as someone else suggested, one and a half cups. Unbelievably delicious.

This was so lovely -- subbed garam masala for five spice and farro for bulgur, but otherwise stuck faithfully to the recipe. Even my meat-and-potatoes-loving spouse found this good enough to talk about at work and share the recipe with others. Absolutely wonderful for a cold winter evening.

Less oil
Less salt
Can try farro instead of bulgur but then use less water/broth. Could also use rice or orzo

Made with one small butternut squash
No bulgur or orzo
1/2 a large onion 
Scant two tsp kosher salt
3 cups broth
Added all olive oil at beginning
Roasted peanuts at 350 & combined with the other topping ingredients, sans olive oil
Squeezed about half a lemon over it all & dumped in the topping

Used brown lentils instead of bulgur for earthiness and protein. Not a fan of 5-spice so 1/2 tsp of allspice and a pinch of cinnamon did the trick. Couldn’t find Aleppo pepper locally so used a ‘Spicy harissa blend’ from my market. All came out yummy

Great soup! Used combo of sweet potato and delicata squash. Used pearled barley, which took more like 20 mins to cook. Used only parsley. 

Very good result.

Delicious even with a load of substitutions, making do with what I had on hand. I used 2 small sweet potatoes and 1 big carrot; switched barley for the bulgar; added cinnamon, a few whole cloves and a star anise for the 5 spice blend; cashews for the peanuts; ran out of parsley, so just cilantro & dill. Delightful! Will make again with recipe ingredients. Husband loved it.
This is definitely one of those better the next day soups. Finished with a bit of chili oil or chili crisp really amped it up the day it was made though
Ian and Justine's "best soup ever".  And they are critics as well as cooks.
Can I leave the leftover peanut topping out at room temp for a few days? I am not sure how to store the leftover topping!

Wow, great flavors! I made a few subs due to what was on hand: 2 leeks instead of onion, more cilantro, less parsley, 2 tsp dry dill instead of fresh, quinoa (already cooked, put in when potatoes were almost cooked) instead of bulgur. I think I'll cut the sweet potatoes a little smaller next time

I made this last night & it was delicious. On the easier side, as far as Ottolenghi recipes go, and I was delighted when my soup looked exactly like the photo. That never happens! It's a very pretty soup. 

The quantity is pretty good--I doubled the recipe and really didn't need to. 

I didn't have five spice so I substituted with garam masala and a bit of anise; next time I'll prep better. Used farro instead of bulgur, too. Works fine with veggie broth.
One of the most flavorful no meat recipes I've had in a while. Big win for relatively low effort.

Great flavors- lemon juice is a must. If using quinoa instead of bulgur, I recommend reducing the amount of quinoa. I substituted it 1:1 and ended up with a very thick stew. 1/4 to 1/2 cup of quinoa would have worked better.
Delicious. I made it with butternut squash. Will make again!
Turned out more like a rice dish than a soup, it's very filling but no complaints about that or the flavors!
This soup was lovely.  The peanut topping was perfect. Great to have another great vegan recipe to make.
Orzo absorbed all the liquid in leftovers. Yummy though. Double recipe makes an insane amount.

Made this mostly as written maybe with different quantities. I had a tube of cilantro and just squeezed what I thought would be equivalent , a few bits of live parsley and a few shakes of dried dill so maybe not exactly as written. Dumped in 4 cups(32 oz )broth because that was what my container had and so only used 2 cups of water. 

 Loved it. The bulgur expanded so was more of a stew. The peanuts absolutely make the recipe. May leave off the chili flakes for personal preference.
I couldn’t eat this until I added sour cream - it just felt like things didn’t meld together.
Added some chopped kale which added nutrients and interest. We love this dish.

Made this for Poni and Mel.  Omitted onions and garlic but it still turned out well!  Used most/all of the 5-spice called for, maybe a 1/4-1/8 of the Aleppo.  Made 1.5x and I would say it’s right for 4 people.
I subbed 2/3 of the bulgar with quinoa and 1/3 with lentils (gf plus extra protein.  Also threw in some chopped kale leaves.

Possibly the worst thing I have ever made.  The onion, bulghar and sweet potatoes just dissolve into a gray mush.  The herbs are lost in the sludge.  The only flavor or texture comes from the peanuts, which are very good.

This is a great weeknight recipe with less chopping than a typical soup, since the base has no carrots or celery. Rich and complex as written, or a flexible and forgiving base recipe from which to improvise! I’ve omitted the herbs and peanut topping: still good. Subbed honeynut or butternut for sweet potato. Added turkey or various greens. It always works because the five-spice and cumin is such a perfect combo.

Made this with a few subs. Used a garlic sesame spice mix in place of five spice. I just couldn't find it, so I made due. I used black rice in place of the bulgar because I was making it for a gluten free friend. Also, didn't use sugar because same friend can't have sugar. 

My friends LOVED it. The lemon really makes the dish. Brightens all things up. Also, the rice soaked up alllllllll the broth, so it was more of a thick stew than a soup.

I made this for a dinner party and it was a hit. The Recipe came together quicker than I expected. I was really blown away by the delicious flavor. You cannot skip on the peanut topping. It takes this dish to a whole other level.

I made a batch of this "soup," following the recipe exactly, set it aside, went for a walk, and came back to a big pot of....wallpaper paste. Unattractive wallpaper paste. Nothing like the photo accompanying the recipe. It tasted okay and the peanut mix was popping, but I wouldn't serve this to anyone. It's too ugly.

I've made this soup numerous times for various people, and everyone loves it.  I add the cooked bulgur at the end as suggested by other reviewers, and that works very well.  Fresh herbs are delicious in it.

This needs the spices DOUBLED. Also add a stick of cinnamon for 10 minutes. The dish has so much potential but under the amounts of spices prescribed the only think with flavor is the peanut chili mix

So good! I cut the potatoes a tad smaller based on comments, but I probably won't next time. They also didn't have time to caramelize (for me) but soup was delicious nonetheless. And the topping is a must. What a delicious combo of flavors.
Very unusual flavoring in a soup with how sweet it is, but by the end of my bowl I found I really enjoyed it.
My favorite soup. I never have bulgar so I use lentils instead, but otherwise follow the recipe closely. The peanut topping is great with other  soups too.
Used quinoa instead of bulgar since we couldn't find it.  It's so good!

I had high expectations for this soup. I was intrigued by the list of ingredients and the peanut topping.  We loved all the fresh herb flavor, but, I’m not sure it was a huge standout… it was good, I’ll still eat it. lol! I squeezed a lemon over the whole thing at the end of following the recipe which helped lift it.  The smell of the 5 spices may have put me off. I might try again with Baharat, instead. Cardamom might be just the trick.

title: Chilled Soba in Dashi With Tomatoes and Corn Recipe
author: Yotam Ottolenghi

stats.Total Time: About 1 hour 40 minutes, plus at least 1 hour cooling
stats.Cook Time: 1½ hours, plus at least 1 hour cooling

comments.commentBody: Delicious! First time I’ve grated tomatoes! The only adjustment I made was just a few pinches of salt in the dashi in the end instead of the 1 1/2 teaspoons due to low sodium diet. Didn’t miss the salt because the rest was so flavorful.

Just made this tonight. Amazing and a keeper. Taste the dashi and adjust salt accordingly before you assemble! Used a rather large heirloom tomato instead of Romas. Added some furikake to the noodles and sprinkled a little togarashi on the finished bowls. Also added a splash of soy to the sliced mushrooms. Served alongside some teriyaki salmon from the NYtimes.

This was a tremendous amount of work for results. I am an experienced, fast home cook. Yet this recipe was time consuming and necessitated multiple re-checks the recipe. End result was refreshing and… OK, I guess?  Miso tomato pulp element was the best part. But dashi was underwhelming, even though I'd followed recipe precisely. Since it all goes together in a soupy bowl, multiple divisions of the soy sauce seemed overly complicated. Would not make again.
Refreshing and complex tastes and textures.  Definitely will make this again but this time for guests.
Rather than grating the tomatoes, may I just use a food mill?
I wouldn't. You want the skin for the dashi. It's really easy to grate the tomatoes on a regular grater. Use the large holes. Stop when you reach the skin.
The mushrooms: first it says to discard the “mushroom solids” and then it says to slice the mushrooms. I’m confused.
It says "Once the dashi is cold, strain, discarding all the solids apart from the mushrooms. Thinly slice the mushrooms and set aside." Pretty straightforward.
"Season the reserved tomato pulp with the miso and ⅛ teaspoon of salt." Then what...? I assume, mix in with the soba, etc. per the photo. But written instructions don't say.
Step 7: "  scatter the corn, tomato and shiitakes on top"
Been cooking this since i bought "Plenty" years ago. Ottolenghi never disappoints, and so appreciate this homage to the soba!
When does the miso go in?
I have frozen roma tomatos, can I use them? And what about fresh mushrooms?

title: Peach and Chile Galette With Pistachio Frangipane Recipe
author: Yotam Ottolenghi

comments.commentBody:
25 cardamom pods gave more seed than I needed. How I open pods: pulse in the spice grinder, pick out opened pod shells, repeat until you only have seeds.

Making the guajillo syrup (dried chili) was awkward for the small volume, and I didn't get great coverage of the peaches. Next time: make guajillo/cardamom infusion in vodka. Toss peaches, top with turbinado.

If you're out of granulated sugar, this worked with brown sugar. Only needed half the buttermilk to bring the crust together.

This is an intriguing recipe, but I have a few questions. Can I use cardamom powder in place of crushed seeds? White balsamic vinegar in place of Moscatel vinegar? Is the guajillo chile fresh or dried? Thanks!

Oh, the cardamom will be so much better if you prep it fresh, but yeah, that is a lot of work. Guajillo peppers are the dry version of mirasol peppers. I assume the vinegar sub would work.

This recipe looks amazing but oy, assembling the ingredients!

I venture that the title for Diana Henry's book may have been "The Love Song of J. Alfred Prufrock" by T.S. Eliot

Shall I part my hair behind? Do I dare to eat a peach? 
I shall wear white flannel trousers, and walk upon the beach. 
I have heard the mermaids singing, each to each.
I substituted hot honey for the syrup and it was delicious.

I followed the recipe- I didn’t have the vinegar called for but I had a honey ginger white vinegar that was perfect. What an incredible dessert! It takes a little time, but its not complicated. The chili in the glaze adds just a subtle heat and additional depth of flavor! Make it as the recipe dictates and you will not be disappointed!
I will dial down the cardamom, by half, next time. It became the dominant flavor over the peaches.

I made this last night and it was delicious but I will change a few things the next go around as follows.  The frangipane to fruit ratio was off for me- next time I'll put only 1/2 to 2/3 of it in the center.  I had a mix of firmer and softer peaches-definitely preferred the softer ones in the finished product.  I was nervous about the syrup being too spicy so cut the guajillo into bigger strips so I could let them seep in the syrup and fish them out.  The syrup did not take up the heat at all.

Guajillo peppers come from guajillo plants which grow in south Texas and parts of Mexico.  Not for the recipe but if you've ever tasted honey from bees that dwell among guajillos, you've tasted perfection.  At least you have if you have all the extra perception of a super taster.

I could find neither moscatel vinegar nor Cabernet, so I took a leap of faith and got pomegranate vinegar and am hoping for the best. Anyone else in this dilemma? What did you sub and what was the result?

Subs-
Figs for peaches
Combo of pistachio, almond, cashew for frangipane
Syrup- brown sugar, orange blossom water, lemon juice
I love Ottolenghi recipes for their complexities (in flavor AND preparation), but not here thanks. The true beauty of a galette is in its simplicity.

Followed recipe exactly and hated it.  Way too much work for a simple galette.  The dough wasn't wonderful and, no, I didn't overwork it.  I followed the instructions exactly.  The 1 1/4 tsps of lightly crushed" cardamom for the frangipane (and more for the syrup) was overpowering --unpleasantly strong and it overwhelmed the peaches.  Nothing special about the pistachio frangipane - barely tasted the pistachios. The syrup was odd with strips of chile in it so I strained it. We threw this out.

Recently made this peach and chile galette with pistachio frangipane for my sister’s birthday. It is a very forgiving recipe and delivered stunningly as described. The flavors experienced were amazing!!
I agree! I want to freeze a couple and take advantage of the ripe (farmer's market) peaches.

Wow! This is one of my favorite desserts I’ve ever made. After reading other notes, I decreased the cardamom to a teaspoon and I used hot honey instead of driving around to find guajillo chiles. This galette is rich and amazing!
How much hot honey did you use and was it a straight swap with the chiles? I have ancho chiles and wondering if I should use them.
Loved it. Used white balsamic vinegar, mascarpone rather than crème fraiche, and ground cardamom. Didn’t open up in the oven as indicated but tasted great.

Crust and filling were delicious. I skipped the syrup and instead brushed the top with hot honey infused with the cardamom. The whipped creme fraiche was pretty good, but I think a little vanilla ice cream would have been at least as good and perhaps better. This dessert was a lot of work (mostly the frangipane), but I'll make it again. I'll skip the cardamom seeds - this is the second recipe I've made with the whole seeds and although I'm an adventurous eater, that flavor is simply too strong.

I absolutely agree that there is too much cardamom in this recipe. I don’t typically make desserts so adventurous, but certainly appreciate them, and the cardamom in the Frangipane was overwhelming. When it first was out of the oven it was only slightly so but as in sat in the fridge overnight, the remaining galette became almost inedible due to the cardamom ratio. I would recommend cutting it in half.

Absolutely knocked out of the park! I've made this twice and have recieved rave reviews both times.  I do reduce the amount of cardamom by half, about 12 pods, as recommended by others. It's just a subtle hint of flavor without overpowering. The guajillo pepper glaze elevates this galette and isn't too spicy. Just a lovely warmth backing everything. Make this, you will not regret it.
Where does it say what temperature to bake?
Instructions #5: 400 degrees.
@susan:  400F at the end of Step 5. It was hard to find!!!
Would nectarines work better n this recipe? If so, would they need some added sweetening?
Can I substitute another type of nut? My husband has an allergy to pistachios. Thank you
This website has several suggestions, including almonds, cashews and hazelnuts: https://happymuncher.com/pistachios-substitutes/

Can I substiute regular flour for the almond flour?  Trying to avoid almonds & almond flour for environmental reasons.  Almond trees require unbelievable amount of water.  No one in my immediate group has celiac or gluten intolerance.

I made this vegan by using vegan butter instead of regular butter and ground flax seed with water instead of egg. FABULOUS!!! I also substituted some fermented ancho/problano peppers - the peppers really make the dish.
Ditto to AprilSO on the frangipane-fruit ratio. Next time I will follow my instinct to use only half the frangipane and double the number of peaches

A response to Fern: With all due respect, the Guajillo plant (Senegalia berlandieri), fornerly an Acacia, of South Texas is a 15 ft. tall tree, often used as an ornamental and, yes a great bee/honey plant. The guajillo chili is Capsicum annuum, a shrubby herbaceous plant and one of the five broadly cultivated chilis.

Your article just talks about peaches and nuts, which is, of course, a classic combination. But chiles with peaches? That's new to me in a dessert. How much kick do the chiles give this, and could I do without if I'm heat-averse?
You could do without but as applied the little heat is very subtle.
Hubs is allergic to cardamom.  Anybody have a suggestion on an appropriate flavor substitute?  I've heard cinnamon or allspice, but I'm not sure those would match the flavor profile??
It adds depth but you won't miss it.
Re the syrup, photo shows a drizzle effect but step 8 indicates to brush over peaches. Which?
The glaze was a sticky syrup but drizzled well. I would not want to waste what would stick to a brush.

I didn't have access to a lot of the ingredients so I used the following substitutions: cardamom powder, white wine vinegar and chili oil.  All turned out well!  I love the NYTimes recipes but for most of us, these ingredients are not readily available!
It was a lot of work! Over 3 hours. But worth every second. I had to use jalapeño peppers and white wine vinegar reduction. Worked perfectly.

I've gathered the ingredients - except for the Moscatel vinegar (using white wine vinegar).Can anyone comment of whether it really tastes better on the day you make it rather than the next day?My sister is visiting for a few days and I can't imagine making this galette, plus the syrup and cream on the same day that I'm making a dinner (I don't cook much any more but the galette looks fascinating). Advice?  If I make it the day before would it be better to warm it before serving?
I see where freezing it before baking will preserve the fresh, flaky, presentation. Allow plenty of time for baking. We served right from the oven.
Oh- forgot to add that I used white balsamic vinegar which is a perfect substitute for the moscatel vinegar I think. I already had cardamom seeds so that saved time.

We loved it. I will make again. Based on other notes I reduced the cardamom and chile. I won't next time.  I may need a new food processor but I would want the pistachios more finely prepared. I stumbled my way through all the steps and was so pleased with the outcome. After seeding the chile I snipped it with kitchen shears. I will use two next time. I fished them out with a fork before glazing the tart. The crust reminded me a tiny bit of a scone.

I made this pretty much as written, but with already ground cardamom and homemade strawberry vinegar, and without the syrup. It was great. Good flavors, crispy crust. I like the idea of continuously putting the crust back in the fridge so that it’s cold when it goes into the oven and so holds its shape. Anyone know what the vinegar does to the dough? I noticed that it made the butter become tiny droplets, so maybe this contributed to the flaky Ed’s.
Made this for a dinner party! Impressive and tasty. Followed the recipe exactly and it turned out perfectly.

It was seriously delicious.  25 cardamom pods gave much more that 1 1/4 teaspoon - I ground it in a spice blender and used the leftover for something else. I also strained the syrup. My galette did not expand which was of concern in regards to the peaches cooking but it turned out fine. At 400, the bottom caramelized, adding another layer of flavor. Next time, will try making the dough and frangipane the day before.
Fabulous recipe, forgiving and delicious, have made twice in under a week
Delicious crust. Felt the fragipane to fruit ratio was off. In the future would cut back on the fragipane and add more peaches. Really like the cardamom flavor

This was amazingly good. I did cut the cardamom in half as others suggested. For the Moscatel I bought Carandini Bianca sweet white vinegar which is just so delicious. It’s forever a pantry staple now.
I wasn't wild about the crust which didn't hold together well. Next time I'll go with my standard pate sucre.

I'm an Ottolenghi fan.  I made only a few changes... I know, I know--so annoying (!)   I had puff pastry in the freezer that I had made--used it, instead of the dough in the recipe.  Since a number of other reviewers felt that the fruit-to-frangipane ratio was 'off' I added more peaches (final diam. 9").  Served with vanilla bean ice cream (didn't really like the creme fraiche idea).  Rave reviews... wonderful flavors... Though not a 'true' gallette with puff pastry, I would highly recommend it.

I had really high hopes for this dessert but was disappointed.  I liked the bit of spice the syrup brought (the photo looks like they took the pepper rings from the syrup and put them on the tartlet which might have helped) but I dunno.  I was expecting more of a crust than I got - this is a very short crust, almost grainy, perhaps the almond flour. the frangipane was lost.  I don't think I'll make it again.  and for Maggie, I found a vinegar from Modena call Bianca from carandini

This recipe had me from the title. After making it, I know that it’s every bit as good as it sounds. Consider: buttery, flaky crust with the tang of buttermilk and vinegar, roasted salted pistachio (arguably the best nut) frangipane, summer’s best (Palisade) peaches, and creme fraiche buttermilk (why didn’t I think of that!) whipped cream. Oh and cardamom, which is naturally cooling. Get the fancy vinegar. Guajillos are a reason to visit your local Mexican grocery store.

This crust is wonderful. But otherwise, this recipe was a bust for me. I love cardamom, it’s my favorite spice, but this was way too much for me. The frangipane was so over-spiced, it tasted medicinal - I found it inedible. And piling the peaches high on top of the frangipane wasn’t a good instruction - the galette did not spread sufficiently to allow the frangipane to become crispy (and properly cooked) the way you want it to be. The result was kind of soupy. What a waste of a ripe peach.
This dessert hits all the right notes! And mine came out prettier than the picture!! Sorry, it got gobbled up before I could take it's picture.

Delightful. Substituted Brooks and Italian plums due to a peach allergy. Also substituted whole wheat flour, fig-infused balsamic vinegar, and macadamia milk with good results.  Next time I'll infuse the guajillo longer, it got lost. Used cardamom seeds from Penzey's, and ground them with the pistachios.  Definitely a keeper of a recipe, and it's great the next day in spite of what the recipe says.

9/17/23 success! I used approx 1/2tsp of cardamom powder in the frangipane and used 1/4 tsp of slightly crushed cardamom seeds in the guajillo syrup. I was concerned about the cardamom and the warmth of the pepper but it was perfect. I think if the measurement for the cardamom had been in grams instead of spoons, it would have been more successful. My bake took about 45 minutes, but I could’ve pulled sooner even though the frangipane was not yet browned. 
Great crust. Chill it thoroughly.

I loved this recipe. I did everything it said. The only thing I did differently was I made cuts all along the edge of the dough to make folding it easier
I bought the moscatel, that was a new to me ingredient 
I would definitely do this again

If you are considering this recipe, just leave out the frangipane and it will be fine.  Maybe I'm not a frangipane person, but it was too much and the flavor was not pleasing.  Instructions say to make 6" round of it in middle of dough, much too thick.  Plus it is an off-putting green color.  The chile syrup was very good but somewhat lost on the galette.  Just drain off as much of the syrup from the peaches as possible, pile them in the center in maybe a 9" circle, pleat dough over and proceed.

Wow this is great! Crust is beautiful and super easy. Used a sweet lemon white balsamic vinegar as it’s what I had and was a good substitute.  After reading comments, I reduced frangipane by 1/3. I’d do half next time. Don’t skip it- it’s delish- just a lot. I used a mixture of pistachio, cashew, almond as I was short on pistachios. I liked the mix- gave it a nice richness as a foil to the sweet-tart of peaches.  I also reduced the cardamom based on comments. I used 14 rather than 24 but would probably do 10 next time. I love cardamom- just thought it was a touch too much even when reduced.  I guess if you have subpar peaches, maybe the greater amount works but why steal the show from great summer peaches?
No better recipe for great summer peaches.

This was excellent - a visually beautiful way to use perfect peaches, with a sophisticated mix of flavors. I made the recipe is written, except reduced the cardamom seeds to 1 tsp and substituted white balsamic vinegar for the Moscatel, as others suggested. I doubled the syrup and served it on the side as I was concerned that not all of our guests would enjoy the heat of the guajillo peppers, but next time I will just brush it on the tart as everyone loved it. Penzey's sells whole cardamom seeds

title: Zucchini Pasta With Crispy Capers and Pistachios Recipe
author: Yotam Ottolenghi

comments.commentBody: I’m not against fat in cooking but 2/3 c olive oil for 10 oz pasta?  Even with the other ingredients this sounds like it might need another generation or two- either for the recipe to evolve or to digest it. Are you sure 2/3 cup of oil?  I am intrigued by the history and description. I will try it but I will pare down the oil.
You are not consuming the entire 2/3 cup of oil, only using it to fry the zucchini.
@TRF That is not how it reads, though it makes sense to do that, bigly decreasing the oil and adding more if needed, as @Philip proposes.
This is not how it reads, though it makes sense to do that, bigly decreasing the oil and adding more if needed.
This guy is a national treasure for the way that he understands food and culture and  communicates that understanding. That said, add a half teaspoon of red pepper flakes.

What if we roasted the sliced Zucchini with basil, capers, garlic - with barely any olive oil - when roasted golden brown crisp tender - mix roasted zucchini, basil, capers, garlic with red wine vinegar - blend into the ditalini pasta - top with toasted pistachios? This is how I plan on making this dish.
Actually, you are consuming all the oil--see step 3, but that means it's just a little over 2T per serving which is not so different from salad dressing.

My grandmother wasn't Jewish and came from near Naples. She made the very same zucchini marinated alla scapece, hers with mint. Then Stanley Tucci taught the world to make pasta alla nerano. Now Ottolenghi changes up the method and adds capers and pistachios. It just keeps getting better.
I made this tonight and it was delicious! Next time I’ll adjust the recipe for 16 oz of pasta instead of 10 oz because 10 oz is only enough for 2-3 people.

I love Yotam's recipes, but complicated. I break them down into steps as follows:
1.Fry zucchini in olive oil until browned. Drain on paper towels and salt. Place in vinegar.
2. Fry capers in same oil. Remove to paper towels. Same with basil leaves. Same with  pistachios.
3.Transfer oil to another pan. Fry zucchini until lightly browned. Add garlic & pasta. cook 1 min. Add water.
4. Simmer until al dente. Add 1/2 remaining zucchini and pistachios. Plate.
5. Sprinkle all remaining on top
Can the Zucchini be fried and marinated in advance?  I'm thinking there may be some flexibility between your grandmother's "at least 24 hours" and dad's "a few minutes are plenty."
It’s pretty good, and all vegan! My only gripe is that it’s pretty work intensive with a lot of things you have to cook separately.
It's a lot of work for a dish that's pretty bland.
The directions show that this recipe has been perfected over time. I love when simple techniques elevate humble ingredients. Looking forward to trying it out on my forthcoming crop of zucchini!

I am so going to modify this into a risotto. I make zucchini and lemon risotto often, and adding the fried zucchini to that dish is going to be amazing. I turn to risotto instead of pasta since half the household is gluten free and the gluten happy person despises gluten free pasta.

I agree 2/3 cup olive oil seems excessive. I plan to try grilling 1/8 inch strips of zucchini and see how that works. The smoky flavor imparted by grilling couldn’t hurt. Plus, for max nutritional value, I like to try to work in onions and mushrooms into these kinds of pasta dishes.

A short list of ingredients (kind of an anomaly for a YO recipe!) and I had all of them. Did not see the point of using both a small ( to fry) and larger (to blend and finish) saucepan so I used the bigger pot for the entire recipe. I used slightly less than 2/3 c oil. Next time I might use at least 1/2 vegetable broth instead of 2 c of water. Added 1/2 t red pepper flakes. A keeper.
Doesn’t adding hot water to to oil over high heat result in hot oil and hot water spattering every which way and risk burns for the cook? Am I missing something?

I got to this spot in the recipe and I was too afraid to proceed to pour hot water into hot oil.  So I fished out the spaghetti and garlic and cooked it in water in a separate pan.  I really wanted to like this recipe, but it was a lot of prep work, fried basil doesn't really taste very special, even though I had just picked it out of the garden,  and the final product really didn't taste like much.
Sorry- this is not a keeper for me.

Time consuming, too much oil, and not enough flavor (even properly salted!).

All the zucchini slices can be fried in three tablespoons of oil in four batches in a skillet.

Fresh oil for the capers, basil and nuts. Fresh oil for the zucchini and pasta.

For depth of flavor I'll try chicken broth as the liquid and serve with parmesan or pecorino cheese, and maybe some green onion for brightness.

Tried it and it’s easy to make and delicious. Can use one saucepan. So simple.

No, 2/3 a cup of olive oil is not a lot; it’s for 4 people and it both fries and then covers 4 bowls of pasta (up the pasta just a bit). It’s 5.3 fl oz, or 1.3 oz per serving. This is 2.5 tbls for each serving, with some of the oil getting soaked into the ingredients at the earlier frying stages. It’s not greasy, it’s lite and it’s a perfect amount and balance.
Per the recipe you are indeed adding all the oil after frying the chunky zucchini.

it took me more like two hours to make, even though I took a couple shortcuts. My problem was the zucchini wouldn't brown, even when I put it over high heat. The thin slices took about 20 minutes to get any color. The chunks never browned at all. I'm afraid I also managed to light a paper towel on fire when I put it too close to a burner, but that's not on Yotam.

Hello People, we all love this chef...But, this recipe has you standing at the stove nonstop,FRYING, for an hour, in JULY. Yes, I made it. Not one of my favorites of his. He should be able to come up with something better than this for zucchini in JULY!
Really tasty. Reading the comments agree a little crushed red pepper would be good. Or some lemon. Or maybe just a lot more capers- a bite with everything in it was delicious.

Fabulous, truly unique and wonderful.
For Americans who don't want to mess with "10 oz" of pasta, just use 1/2 lb and leave the rest the same, except reduce the pasta water to 2 cups plus 1/4 as needed. Definitely use the ditalini/tubetti, they will cook properly in such a small amount of water; larger pasta may not! Careful not to burn the pistachios, use unroasted if possible, otherwise stop frying when they begin to brown, don't get them too dark or they will be bitter.
Great flavor but there is absolutely no need to fry the first round of zucchini.  You can roast it on parchment paper.
Meh. Too much oil, too many paper towels, and apart from the capers and pistachios, too little flavour- even with additional garlic and lemon juice.

I used 8 oz pasta (2 oz per person,) added red pepper flakes, and simplified the directions. Here goes: Fry zucchini in cast iron pan, marinate in a large bowl. Fry capers, basil, and pistachios in stages, put on a dishtowel (not paper towels.) Transfer remaining oil to a pot and cook the zucchini, garlic, and pasta. There's no splashing when you add hot water because the pot is full of pasta. Then drain the vinegar and add everything else to the bowl. No fussy plating. It was delicious.
Can you air fry the zucchini pasta to reduce the oil?
It's the oil that makes this dish so delicious.
Savta Ottolenghi made this with Israeli kishuim I bet, not American zucchini. They're lighter in color and density.
The fried marinated zucchini is a Venetian Jewish recipe called concha. I add dried mint, instead of basil and would try here as well.

This was pretty good but the directions are terrible. No need to use small and medium saucepans ( f”goes he  mean sauté pan?). Used used a 9- and 10 inch pan when it all could have worked one pan. Too many pans Too many plates to use for draining. But his recipes are too convoluted.  Could be much simplified otherwise flavor profiles are great

This was really tasty (w the addition of some red pepper flakes) but it involves many steps as others noted.  To those who felt the amount of oil was excessive, I'll just say that olive oil is increasingly recognized as a healthy part of people's diets.  Yes it has calories like all oils, but it also confers significant health benefits for a range of endpoints, including CV health and cognitive function. But whether those benefits still accrue after it's been used for frying is not known.

Lots of gripes in comments about amount of oil, and kitchen equipment required, but I followed the recipe and it was quite delicious. Did serve with freshly grated parmesan cheese, as we are addicted to that. The zucchini really broke down and made a delicious sauce. Bit of red wine vinegar was welcome, too.
Fabulous as is!

this was majorly underwhelming. 
none of it worked for me.

My grandmother who was not Jewish, but born in the Abruzzi region of Italy also made zucchini this way.  She also added garlic and lots of fresh mint and served this as part of the antipasta course.  I am delighted to have learned the roots of this delicious dish and will share this history with my family the next time I prepare it.

Beautiful. Simple ingredients turn into a wonderfully nuanced dish. I made exactly per recipe. Next time I’ll fry the zucchini a bit darker and increase the capers, basil, and pistachios just a little bit- they’re delicious! 
Will look forward to making this again.

For 1 lb pasta slice 3 zucchini and cube 3 zucchini.  Also somewhat increase other items.  Cavatappi is good alternative pasta.  Pasta is a little bland…dash of pepper flakes, 1/2 veg stock for water, some cheese mixed in., a little more red vinegar.

Lots of work for the result.  I’m gluten free so I make zoodles with half a zucchini.  Fried some zucchini and left some sliced thin and raw and tossed both in red wine vinegar.  I like the raw better and if I made this again would not bother frying the zucchini. Also would not fry the pistachios or capers as they lost some flavor in the process.  They will heat up when tossed in the zucchini mixture.  Liked the contrast of the sweet pistachios and the vinegar.  I served it with parmesan crisps.
Followed recipe exactly as written. Delicious!

I was worried about this because of the comment section, however, I'm happy to report that all went well! I actually used a lot more zucchini but took my time, frying and drying . . . wonderfully flavorful. I would recommend getting the oil nice and hot; I used a pasta pot so that in the end I did not use another pot for the chopped zucchini and the pasta and water fit well. Perhaps my capers and basil could be a little crispier. I'll work on that!
He sure didn't make his reputation with this recipe. I didn't find the oil excessive but the dish is flavorless.
Many steps as I followed the recipe precisely.  Next time maybe a short cut or two.  But it was delicious.
A lot of work for very bland flavor.  Loved the idea but this needs pecorino or goat cheese at least.

I agree with others who describe this as a lot of work for not a lot of flavor. I added the juice from 1 lemon at the end as the pasta water was reducing, which helped. I’d recommend frying up more capers and maybe more zucchini, as it felt like there was too much pasta for the rest of the ingredients. I’m also not sure what the fried basil added.
This simply wasn’t good. Juice was not with the squeeze.
