Like all his recipes, I think: too many steps. I took the advice of another on this forum and roasted zucchini at 400 F for 20 min. Added capers, garlic and basil for last 5 min. Put zucchini, etc in a bowl, dressed it with red wine vinegar. Let sit. Cooked pistachios in a bit of oil, drained on paper towel. Combined all with cooked pasta. Dressed with a sprinkle of grated Parmesan. Delicious. A keeper.
Great recipe. I cooked the whole thing in the medium saucepan, and it worked out just fine.

Better cold the next day than fresh right off the stove. Will make again as a cold pasta salad with these adjustments: slice and fry all four zucchini, double the amount of capers and omit the pistachios. I love pistachios but felt they didn't add a lot here. Think I might also add some chopped sun-dried tomatoes.

This zucchini, basil, red wine vinegar & garlic combination reminds me of the stellar Canal Hose marinated zucchini dish, one of my all time favourite ways to serve zucchini. This will be amazing I’m sure with pasta and I can’t wait to try it tonight. Love the family history too.
I missed something, is the pasta cooked in oil? Fried raw?

Add the raw pasta & garlic, fry for a minute then add the hot water...
I got it wrong and cooked the pasta separately so I just added it at the end (and of course did not add the water).  It worked fine.

I made as written. Sadly, neither the basil, nor the capers got crispy. But, the flavors were wonderful. it's a lot of work, but good reward. Think I might add some lemon zest at the end next time, and eliminate the fry time on the pistachios. I'll try harder on the basil/capers.

This was good enough to share with company, but for four servings needs to be 1 1/2 x. Two of us finished most of the original recipe. I would fry/ marinate 3/4 of zucchini.  Skip frying the pistachios. This technique is known as pasta e risotto. Season liberally.
When does the marinated zucchini get added to the pasta?
Step 4.
I want to make this tonight (glut of zucchini in the fridge) but I don't have pistachios. What would be a good substitution?

Other nuts that will work include lightly rushed walnuts or peanuts at a pinch, I'd say. 
It's as much the texture as the pistachio taste, IMHO
Utterly delicious.  Just a bit of a pain doing all that frying.  Still...delicious.

This doesn’t take 50 minutes to make, unless your watch stopped somewhere along the way?? It also was really complicated, with many pans and steps. All said, though, it was very tasty. There should be some way to simplify. One quick solution, you don’t need to cut up and sauté the last part of zucchini into squares with the pasta. Just slice all the zucchini up before mixing it into the pasta. Frying the pistachios and basil leaves probably adds more taste, but it too is a lot of work.
Did the notes apply to this recipe? Pistachios, oil???
delish.
The fried zucchini also makes a great topping on bread. Love this recipe we had this many Sundays at my Italian grandparents house.

I did not have some of the ingredients so I inprovised with substituting fresh mint for the basil (mine was not grown mature enough to pick),  walnuts for the pistachios, and summer squash for the zucchini. Still it was awesome and loved the mix of flavors.  

This is why I like Ottoleghi's recipes so much;  one can rid off of them very easily depending on what you have fresh and ready.

I probably know a lot more than Yotam Ottalenghi, so I’m going to complain about his time-tested recipe and talk about all the ways I am going to do things differently.

Oh, wait.  I’m actually not going to do that because I’m not a toolbox.
Bizarre that this chef doesn't add any salt and pepper to the dish. Hence one reader's gripe that the dish was bland, no doubt.
Salt is added in steps 1 (multiple times) and 3. There is vinegar, capers, basil and pistachios. How could this be bland?
I made this recipe. I am a good cook andI don't recommend it. Oily, kind of like Greek stuff leaves. Ok in small amounts, but not worth the time.
Delicious, but that frying---

A lot of work for not great results. I think maybe i will not add pasta and just do zucchini next time. Lots leftover. Thinking of turning leftovers into a frittata. Maybe that will help it. I did like the fried capers and basil leaves.

Excellent, company worthy dish.  Served with grilled pinwheel sausage and a tomato salad.  Fantastic dish for anyone who is dairy-free.  Between the zucchini melting and the generous amount of olive oil, the dish has a very unctuous mouth feel.  Truly a delicious dish!

45 minutes once the zucchini is chopped & garlic is peeled. 3/4 recipe with 8oz ditalini is dinner for two with a few bites left over. Could use _just_ a tid more salt, and like another commenter said, a little bit of red pepper flakes with the chunks of zucchini.

This was a lovely dish, but I made a few modifications to suit my taste.  Added a few squeezes of fresh lemon to the pasta at the end of cooking.  Also garnished with grated Parmesan.  I wish that there was more of a textural contrast between the zucchini that was cooked with the past and that which was fried and then marinated.  I wonder if one could marinate it, drain it very well, and then fry it.  I may try that next time.
Wonderful recipe. Interesting flavors and quite delicious. Will definitely make again.
The large zucchini pieces become over cooked. Next time I'll saute them with the garlic and set aside adding them to the cooked pasta.

A lot of work but worth it. I had small zucchini’s from my garden that I used. Also used a different kind of pasta (anelli). 
And i added some preserved lemon as well for a little bit more flavour!
Why do you need two different pans? Seems like you could do it all in the larger pan.
Hard pass — too complicated. Vinegar was too strong for us
This was way too much work and too much oil for a very ordinary meal. I was trying to find something interesting to do with zucchini but it’s flavor -such as it is- is lost in this. Too much oil!
Divine!  Not really necessary in my opinion to cut the zucchini in different ways. Does add to the look, more interesting, but just another step/complication.  But this dish is a winner.

This was a huge hit. I couldn’t get the zucchini slices to brown, so I ended up air frying them (one layer, several batches) and marinated in the vinegar for some hours. The fried basil leaves are exquisite! They really elevate the presentation, as do the capers. Labor intensive but worth it.
This was a surprisingly pleasant, delicious dish!

Good, but labor intensive. Next time I would roast or grill the zucchini as others have mentioned. I’m not sure what I would do about the basil and pistachios. I did finish with lemon juice and, because I have a lot, parsley

Really good and shockingly easy for an Ottolenghi recipe! The vinegar adds a nice kick. This is a lovely refreshing pasta salad-esque dish. I would serve this with burrata or ricotta in the future to complete the meal.

I agree, frying the zucchini takes a looong time. The olive oil lost its flavor, and most of it ended up on the paper towels anyway. I did like the toasty notes from the zucchini and fried nuts (I used almonds), and the tart notes from the capers. Fried basil had no taste; zucchini chunks didn't "melt into sauce." Someone noted that Israeli summer squash is different – didn't the Times test American ingredients? Next time, I'll pre-salt the zucchini to draw out moisture, and sauté it.

Well, I am persistent, so I tried it again. Pre-salting, drying, and sautéing the squash worked well. I peeled the chunks to help them "melt," and also squished them with a spoon after cooking, and the result tasted like – boiled summer squash. The capers, nuts, and *fresh* basil didn't redeem it. Sorry, Yotam. I don't know what I'm doing wrong here, but this recipe isn't working for me.

Loved the zucchini ‘sauce’ and agrodolce zucchini. And the pistachios and fried basil are to die for (I used purple basil for contrast). This is easy for a weeknight at room temp and stunning enough for a party. Can’t wait to make it again!
Fatal flaw is not checking comments before committing to the recipe. Not work the work for my other half to look at me and say he’s not interested in eating it as leftovers. Bland and too oily :(

Easy to make but time-consuming - I had to do the zucchini in 3 batches. I love vinegar but found this to be too vinegary. There was not a ton of flavor, but I might try again adding some heat - maybe red pepper flakes and something umami. Do not need the second pan - can cook the pasta in the same pan everything was sauteed in.
Too complicated, not enough payoff. Not bad, but disappointing, I had high hopes.

(I'm not a "cook" as evidenced by the comment I'm about to make.) To short cut, I sweat the zucchini then tossed in oil and air-fried. While I was doing that I used about 1/2 c. oil (honestly, still too much) to fry the capers and added the basil and pistachios for the last minute before finishing the recipe as called for in steps 3-4. Depending on your tastes, I'd add black or red pepper and possibly top with Parmesan for serving. Was very flavorful.

I loved this dish and shared it at a company picnic to have something vegan. Will do it again tho I would do 3 zucchinis instead of just 2 for the fried thin rounds. I cooked the noodles in a skillet that had just been used to cook some mushrooms in white wine and I think it really added a nice bit of flavor and color. Will add the mushrooms to this dish next time, too.
This was delicious even with my substitutions. No capers, used anchovies. No pistachio, used peanuts. Also had to use macaroni pasta. I also used less olive oil.

I briefly removed the zucchini chunks and dumped the remaining oil, toasted the garlic and pasta in that oil before adding back the zucchini chunk and water. Added feta at the end for some more flavor but agree with others that it’s overall too bland and the effort to payout ratio pretty high… (and I’m confused about the benefit of frying the zucchini to a perfect crisp only to douse in liquid vinegar! That step made me sad after I completed it)
can I substitute lemon juice for vinegar?
A rather bland preparation.  Needs more  garlic, capers. And basil thats fresh .  The fried basil is a nice garnish but adds little flavor.

So good.   A great way to use a bumper crop of zucchini.   I made it as directed except I added all the fried zucchini, pistachios, etc at once.   Preserved lemon and red pepper flakes may make a good addition

My husband and I loved this, thought it was fabulous. I used avocado oil (neutral flavor) which is a high heat cooking oil, everything I've read about olive oil is that is can't do high heat. Used a skillet for all the frying so the zucchini could spread out, then drained the leftover oil into a Le Creuset to finish. I had the time to do the frying earlier. Next time will do more capers.

We love this dish. I tend to score my dishes by the amount of leftovers it generates. In this case there are no leftovers because everyone goes back for seconds. A delicious way to use up your zucchini.

Really did only take 50 minutes (usually I’m slower than the suggested time). Glad to use ingredients I had on hand. Used less oil. The combo of ingredients did not wow us. Some felt the need to add red pepper flakes for a little zing. Not something I’ll make again.

Wife would’ve given it five stars, I would’ve given it 4.5 if the option existed. Made with gluten free pasta. Usually gf pasta turns to muesli when cooked this way, but Barilla gluten free elbow macaroni held up through the process and was still good the next day.

I added about half a cup of freshly microplaned Parmesan to the sauce while cooking down and a sprinkling on top. Gave extra flavor, a little risotto-like thickness to the remaining liquid, and felt more like a main course meal.
Sub some of the water with broth. Added pecorino, red pepper flakes, and a quick squeeze of lemon.
Full of flavors. I love the idea of cooking pasta with the veggies a bit like risotto. I started with 1/4 cup oil and added when needed. A lot of the oil ends up soaked in the paper towels.

This dish is a great way to get rid of excess zucchini! Only downside is that after over an hour tied to the stove you’re left with something that tastes like oily zucchini. :-/ It'd take more than pistachios to give this zing.
Added ground chicken, roasted first half zucchini instead of frying, chicken broth in place of water

This is one time-consuming, simple pasta dish. That said, I will make it again, even though I hate frying, because of the mess. It's very delicious and I love cooking the pasta in the saute pan. ⅔ cup of olive oil, which you do end up consuming, is really the only fat in it (other than a small amount of pistachios), with no meat or cheese in it.

This disappeared fast. We had a couple of vegans over for dinner and I was searching for nice vegan main recipes and this caught my eye. 

The capers and olives give it a nice kick. I added in some artichoke hearts too cause I had a jar that has been sitting around unused.

I did not think this was coming together well throughout the cooking process, then somehow miraculously it was pretty good at the very end. I only had 2 zucchinis, so it was a bit low on veg- I wished I'd made it up with the handful of mushrooms I have or maybe some frozen spinach or something. I also probably didn't make it to 2/3 cup olive oil, but used that benchmark to at least be liberal-- I also tossed in a tsp or so of chicken bouillon and threw in about 1/4 cup heavy cream at the end.

Honestly, how do you fry so much zucchini in so little oil in an acceptable amount of time, also doesnt oil start breaking down and becoming not so good at some point- it's been 30 minutes of frying zuchinni.
I served it with spelt instead of pasta as I am trying to eat more whole grains and it was fantastic! the capers really bring the flavor.

I would not fry the capers again, and I will roast the pistachios in a pan rather than fry them.
While I am writing this, my first bunch of zucchini are in the air fryer (experiment). But all in all this is a delicious dish and an excellent example of inventive vegetarian cuisine
Nice summer lunch dish. I added some crushed red pepper per the comments and crumbled some feta on top.
I highly recommend to sprinkle a teaspoon of freshly grated lemon zest over the pasta before serving

title: Zaalouk With Tahini Recipe
author: Yotam Ottolenghi

comments.commentBody: I use a time-saving grilled and puréed eggplant product that is preserved in a glass jar.  The brand is called Sera (from Turkey) and it has the natural smokiness you need for this recipe. It is delicious as well for baba ghanoush.

@Mimi you’re right, much of the year jarred eggplant is the way to go! Try Gurme212–another Turkish brand—if you can find it. Their eggplant in glass jars has no citric acid preservative, so tastes even better.
This is very nice, but an extremely high-effort dip. I used tinned tomatoes instead of plum, worked fine.
This takes a heckuva lot longer than the stated 20 minutes to prepare.
Guys, Ottolenghi's recipes always take a bunch of time. But that's kind of the joy. Watch his confit garlic humous recipe on youtube, it's a game changer! Just know you can't rush perfection :)

While the timing is definitely off, I don't think it took that long to make. I had some eggplant that I had roasted + frozen last week and used that instead of roasting from fresh. Benefit of that is that during the thawing process the eggplant releases a lot of the extra liquid naturally. I had this dip on top of baked potatoes with crispy chickpeas for some protein and it was a fantastic (extremely filling) dinner!

I made a half recipe of this and served it over some oven baked cod filets I popped in the same temp oven for about 10 minutes, warming some pita for the last 2 minutes. It was not as complicated as it seems, just takes time for the peppers and eggplant to do their things. While they cook I suggest mise en place in one bowl with the spices, tomato paste and garlic and another bowl with the fresh herbs and salt, sugar etc. that way it’s all set for you to dump in the pan when it is time. 5 stars.

Made this yesterday.  Yes a bit of time and effort but so worth it with some naan bread and cocktails.  Used unlined sheet pans and cleanup still easy.  I’d say not a weekday recipe for working people ( I’m retired) but you could make on the weekend and rewarm.
I used tomatoes in a can. Turned out great.

So good. Quality ingredients and a dark roast is key. Came together really well. Yes, its cooking, couple steps. If you’re into quick&easy might not be your thing, but worth it. Doubled recipe, one portion tonight and for the other I kept the two parts separate in the fridge and reheated and topped next day, even better.

Delicious as described.  May I offer two tips.  

Firstly, this is ‘free time cooking’.   I pop the peppers n eggplant into the oven, and then I leave the kitchen.  Personally I vacuumed the upstairs, but you do you.   If it is a weeknight, roast the P&E the night before for sure.    It need not be a 2-hour slog.

Secondly, if you want to sub in canned/jarred stuff, why not just buy a jar of caponata and forget you ever saw this recipe?   It would be cheaper, and also yummy.

This recipe takes some time, but wow is it delicious. Yotam does not disappoint here! We ate this with focaccia, fresh from the oven, and a chopped salad. Optional merguez for  the meat eaters. I will be making this all summer.

I love using my air/ fryer toaster oven to roast any vegetables- super easy and it doesn’t heat up the whole house.  I also liberally use a mini food processor to minimize chopping if it is all going together in a moosh anyway - I would put both the eggplant and pepper in at the same time - do other work and come back - they way I see it - an hour and a half to do this plus all the other work you get in in the house.  I like the idea of jarred shortcuts but love using up fresh veggies.
It says 2 hrs in the header. 20 min for the eggplant drain

Overall really yummy and while it takes a long time to make it’s not very labor intensive during that time, you can take breaks and do other things while the eggplant is in the oven. Only tip would be the consistency was a bit too watery for us. Next time would either cook down the tomato’s longer or explicitly pre drain some of the tomato juice when pureeing.

Wow, this was one of the best things I’ve made on NYT cooking! Yes, it takes longer than 20 mins, but Ottolenghi’s recipes are works of art that require time. I made this a meal by adding meatballs and pita. Perfectly balanced flavors!
Try adding preserved lemon skin. It adds a salty tangy dimension.  I just made a similar dish from Joyce Goldstein.
One of those dumb questions but I can't stand bell peppers. What can I use instead?
Probably good topped wit feta
This was delicious and worth the time it took

Made this for a family gathering and got rave reviews from everyone.  Toasted the veggies while I prepared other things.  Added a dash of cinnamon that brought it all together.  Left off the tahini.  It was a big hit as a dip and a condiment for the burgers.
This is so easy and delish! I love all the acts necessary to prepare such food especially for others. Thank you Yotam!
I know it’s a dip, but I added chick peas and served it over a bed of wilted greens.  Otherwise made as directed in the recipe.  Delicious.
Excellent!  Had it warm with rice the first day, added a dollop or two of tzatziki at room temperature the next day.
My quick draining method for roasted eggplant: use your salad spinner !
This one is amazing! Me and my husband absolutely loved it! Served with pita chips instead of plain pita. Will be making in future fit sure.

This is probable the best recipe I’ve made from the NYTimes, and I’ve made many!  Easy to make with a blend of spices that are delicious.  I did have a slight problem with the tahini sauce.  Mine was too watery, though I should have stirred the tahini and lemon juice first to check for consistency before adding the water.

Sensational! Eggplant Shakshuka on a spud! Ottolenghi offers recipes made for cooks who like the process of cooking. Procuring, prepping, tending, enjoying. If this isn’t your thing, well, what can one say? My bride gave her top rating, “I could eat this every day for the rest of my life.” (I keep a special folder in my NYT box for these…).  Yes, it took me over two hours, but they were two hours of kitchen joy.

I couldn't pierce the eggplant with a fork... 74 year old hands ;)  so I grabbed my trusty Dexter serrated parring knife and that worked just fine.  I also used a combination of jarred, roasted piquillo peppers and Trader Joe's jarred, roasted red peppers.  I like the piquillo peppers better.  Also I sort of increased the recipe by 30% or more.  Good result.  Hope the Church pitch-in vegetarians like it as well!
I grilled the peppers and the eggplant the night before, so I only had to put it all together the day I served it. Delish!

Delicious. Will make again. Would recommend.
Was time consuming as there are a lot of processes for this dish. Made it on 4th of July.

As others suggested, I roasted my eggplant and peppers the day before. I would also sauté the garlic a bit more before adding the spices and tomato paste next time I make this. I had some leftover tahini sauce from Melissa Clark’s broccoli grain bowl so used that instead. This was fine. Mine certainly doesn’t look as rich or deep red as the one pictured.
Definitely will make again and it really did take 2 hours in prep time. Added extra lemon to the tahini sauce. Ange
Does take a lot of time. Definitely the 2 hours it says and that was with Dad doing the eggplant. Added extra lemon to the tahini sauce. It is worth the time. Glad I started early!

This was insanely good! Charred the peppers and eggplant on grill. Served with grilled country loaf (brushed with olive oil and garlic), bathed feta with herbs and Kalamata olives, pita, hummus and grilled shrimp with green tahini sauce for poolside happy hour. Thank you! So delicious!

The oven char for the peppers steams out more of the red peppers flavors than stove top charring. Leave the red pepper whole to even get more flavor and sweetness. Remove skin, stems and seeds when cool enough to handle, some char left behind is okay too.
I microwave eggplants. Prick several places. Nuke 10 minutes on high. Roast peppers probably don't need to be peeled, especially if they being pureed. I would skip the 1/2 t sugar.
Absolutely gorgeous! Prepped eggplant and peppers and tomatoes the night before - took 10 mins to assemble the next eve. Ate the leftovers eggs and hot sauce - definite yummmm

Really loved this. In fact my generally eggplant disliking spouse thought it was fantastic (we got eggplants in a veg box). 
I ended up serving it over couscous which worked very nicely. 
It takes a while to make, yes, but the labor involved (and mess) is not bad.
Subbed chicken stock for water and added a bundle of thyme. Seriously so good.

This was sooo good! I had leftover eggplant from last summer that I flash froze. I did roast them and the peppers. I guess I forgot to get canned tomatoes and got tomato sauce. I wasn’t sure if it was going to work or not. Fear not, it did. My eggplant was a little chunky too. That really helped. I lightly toasted the pita. Always looking for recipes for meatless Monday.
Add some toasted pine nuts, I promise you won't be disappointed

Very yummy! I did the veggies on the grill to save from turning the oven on in summer, and I just chopped the tomatoes to skip getting out the blender. Served with spiced chickpeas on top of whole steamed sweet potatoes (I steam sweet taters thanks to Samin Nosrat, see her recipe on this site! So good!)

While it is true that this took me about 3 hours to make (I am an objectively slow cook), it is worth the effort. I brought it to a potluck and it was the only dish without leftovers. Luckily, I put some aside for myself!

Can anyone recommend a substitute for tahini?  I've developed a severe allergy to sesame, and, in turn, sesame is now designated by the FDA as a major allergen.  Thanks, in advance, for any suggestions or recommendations.
Hummus

Maybe try cashew cream?  Another possibility would be greek yogurt (plain) with a little olive oil and spices.  It won't taste like tahini, but it will have a similar mouth feel and richness as a counterpoint to the veggies.
Tahini is a major ingredient in hummus, so if you’re trying to avoid sesame I would not recommend eating hummus.

You can’t rush perfection or use inferior ingredients and by that I mean not fresh.  I know I know it’s based on what’s available but if it’s not available then it’s not in season so make something else.

I missed the instruction to peel the bell peppers.  It did no harm.  Like every other one of Chef Ottolenghi’s dishes, we absolutely loved it and will have it again.  Prep does require a little foresight, as you have to roast the vegetables and wait ‘til they’re cooled enough to handle, and get out the food processor to puree the tomatoes.  Then it’s a snap.

Couple of quick questions. I got the jarred roasted eggplant and am wondering how much to use. The jar is 23 ounces and I imagine it could be equal to 2 large eggplants. I also got a 12 oz jar of roasted red peppers and will add them to taste.
I can't wait to make this.  I'm thinking toasted pine nuts might make a nice addition as a garnish.

Wow, this dish was fantastically delicious and not like anything I’ve had before. I
 made a half batch, driven by the medium size of the one eggplant I had.  I added a tablespoon of my in-house harissa to the spice mix and included the zest of the one small lemon that I used.  I stove top charred some flour tortillas on a medium low open flame (1-2 minutes per side) in the place of pita.  

Lesson learned is make a full batch the next time, if the recipe comes from Yotam it will be outstanding.
The pepper roasting technique is genius. I’ll never roast a whole pepper again - this gives a lovely char to the flesh as well as the skin, which transfers to the dish. Perfect.
Loved this as did my guests who ate every last bit!

Just finished making this.  Made the eggplant portion before the rains came 3 days ago.  Just got the roof leak repaired.  Used canned tomatoes as per a few comments.  It is okay but I am not over the moon.

Great recipe.  To cut down on on time roast eggplant and peppers ahead of time. I did it on my gas grill outside since it’s summer.    (Make extra roasted peppers cause you can’t have too many of these in the house!)

Recipe calls for draining the eggplant and discarding the liquid.   Why?  The liquid that comes from this becomes a wonderful sweet umami-rich syrup.  I added it back in to the eggplant and cooked it down.  Delish!

Roast vegetables over gas stove burner.No need for oven. Place in plastic leftover box to "finish" cooking. When cooled down or refrigerated for the next day, the peels slide off easily. No need to puree the tomato. It breaks down during the brief cooking time. Just add a little of the eggplant "water" to the final step, and cook down. Thanks for the recipe. The Zaalouk was absolutely delicious!

Pressed for time, I nuked the eggplant for two bursts of 4 minutes each, then left it in the microwave to soften and drain on the plate while I prepped the other ingredients. Used jarred roasted red peppers, which I will not do again. The flavor was fine, but the texture was strange. We ate this over chicken thighs and brown rice done up with garlic in the Instant Pot. It was beautiful to behold, delicious and filling, with plenty of leftovers! Yum!

I wasn’t sure how deeply to pierce the eggplants before I roasted them. They never completely collapsed so I cooked them longer, which turned out to be too long. It didn’t yield that yummy nutty eggplant flavor as much. I’m guessing I should have  pierced the eggplant more deeply. Anyway it was still pretty delicious.

Delicious! Worth the prep time. Made exactly as instructed in the recipe but roasted the eggplant and red peppers on the grille. Proof: the look of delight on our guests' faces and their sighs of joy as the ate. I put the leftovers (I doubled the recipe) in the fridge and came down for a midnight snack. Had this chilled with a crusty baguette. A very pleasant surprise. Thanks for sharing this recipe.

I made a half recipe, as written.  Very very good.
Wonderful way to use the very fresh produce of late summer. It’s worth the time. The details honor the ingredients.
But…..Ways to save time / simplify on a busy day.
Buy garlic paste
Buy pre-roasted peppers in a jar.
Canned tomatoes
Use bottled lemon juice

Excellent recipe and a great way to use some excess vegetables during harvest season as it seems this recipe is highly adaptable.  We had some extra white eggplant and a large Heirloom tomato that were getting a bit toward the end of storage time and we used those with great success.  We used a little less of the raw garlic in the tahini and cut back on the red pepper for some of the family that are sensitive to those, yet everyone still went back for multiple servings.

Made without a food processor - just dicing and smashing veggies and left the skin on the tomatoes & one of the eggplants. Didn’t drain them either just let them cook down longer. Double or triple spices & double or triple garlic otherwise not flavorful (unless processing - larger volume to season without)
Made exactly as written. Time consuming, but not too difficult. There's a lot of downtime that I used to do dishes, so that was nice. It was so delicious!

You can pace out the prep if you WFH or make over the weekend. I threw the peppers and eggplant in the oven to roast midday, peeled and put aside. Then, cooking at night was fast and easy. Grated the tomatoes. Served on baked potatoes with labneh and chives; added chili oil to the tahini, to put on top.

Absolutely delicious. The tahini sauce, while delicious, is not needed. I served it with and without the tahini sauce and everyone thought it masked the depth of the eggplant, peppers and tomatoes. Double this recipe. It will be devoured.

I am a notoriously slow cook, so the notes on this one scared me. It’s worth it. Wonderful flavor. The good news to my fellow slowpokes is that you’re given plenty of time to clean while the recipe is happening, so at least you can speed it up that way.

Fantastic. I made this as-written for some vegan friends - served as the main course over rice. Everyone really loved it. It was a tad too spicy for one dinner guest, but that’s the risk you take dining in my kitchen.
Made this for my book club to rave reviews. As with all dips that include roasted eggplant, it’s best if the eggplant is burned all over the outside so you get that delicious smoke essence.

Excellent! We made this for Christmas dinner along with Moroccan Lamb Tagine and Couscous. Save yourself the trouble and buy pre-roasted red peppers. We also used canned tomatoes (dumped most of the liquid) and it turned out delicious.

Taking the time to use fresh ingredients was worth the results, the flavor was outstanding and well received even by a few who claimed they disliked eggplant. Normally I'd substitute or make a few changes in a recipe but not this it turned out too good to play with. So in reality I have no notes to follow it's all there.

A”keeper” recipe for my family and worth the prep steps and time. I used Cento brand rustic a passata to sub for the tomatoes,measured 8 oz., and it was great. Served with pita, green salad and very satisfied!

I tried to use some shortcuts (canned tomatoes instead of fresh, jarred peppers) and I’m not sure if it was those substitutions but this was truly gross. Flavorless and just weird. I threw it out after a few bites.

Wonderful recipe, love the tahini-topped tomato-eggplant mixture. I've made with both fresh tomatoes and canned San Marzanos with excellent results. Originally topped a baked potato with it as recommended, which was good, but I like it best with a warmed flour tortilla.

This is worth all of the preparation. Absolutely delicious. I used canned plum tomatoes and it worked well. I will replicate this later in the season when plum tomatoes are in season. This requires homemade pita, if you are up for it!
Grilling the peppers and eggplant on charcoal makes a big difference, just as it does with the eggplant in baba ghanouj.
I must say it is not a true middle eastern traditional dish. Probably should not be called Zaalouk.
Mmm this was so good. A little zatar seasoning on top really makes this great as well. Home run for me :)
This was delicious, though I'm wondering if it's actually necessary to peel the eggplants? I think this would be just as tasty with roasted skin on eggplant.

title: Crispy Coconut, Asparagus and Green Bean Salad Recipe
author: Yotam Ottolenghi

comments.commentBody: I am not one for critiquing recipes, but I feel there is a major adjustment that cannot go unannounced… Double it because it is so dang good you will want seconds. Or thirds. :) Nicely done, Yotam!
Is there a substitute for the coconut, since I hate shredded coconut, and what are "breakfast" radishes?
https://www.thespruceeats.com/what-are-french-breakfast-radishes-5076162#:~:text=The%20French%20breakfast%20radish%20is,peppery%20flavor%20and%20crisp%20texture. I will use regular radishes :)

@Lynne regular red radishes in the US are more tangy tangy than the less available, French breakfast radishes sometimes available.  I like both but usually go with the red radishes due to availability.

Breakfast radishes are long, mild, French radishes.
They are readily available in England, (where the author is from).

Cooked this yesterday and served with lamb chops, and my partner loved it.  It actually took me about 25 minutes.  

Modified the dressing as follows: 
zest and juice from 1/4 lime 
  peanut oil (perhaps a tablespoon)
  a teaspoon of maple syrup 
  a tablespoon of lychee juice (from an Indian store) 
  a teaspoon of mint chutney 
  a teaspoon of capers juice and a tablespoon of finely chopped capers.

Asparagus and green beans are, unfortunately, not available at the same time here.  Asparagus are just appearing now, but fresh beans--not tough supermarket ones--won't be in farmers' markets or my own garden for at least a couple of months. Any suggestions for a substitute?
Asparagus only.
Asparagus and green beans are never available anywhere at the same time--which is what makes recipes like this so weird. I agree with Richard--use just asparagus.

This isn't a recipe for marvelous fresh farmer's market asparagus (or green beans).  This is for shoulder season produce coming from far away.  For the produce from the farmers market or garden, simpler is better
Okay, hear me out. This dressing and coconut crunch, but with avocado and smashed/sliced cucumber instead of asparagus and green bean. So refreshing and so good!

I prepared this dish now twice, the first time for just my partner and I and last night for a guest. He couldn't stop talking how amazing it was. 

I used Ottolenghi branded "Aleppo and Other Chillies Blend" which I bought at Waitrose. My partner is quite sensitive to spicy food and he could handle it

It's the first NYT Cooking recipe I tried and I feel excited to try out more

This was excellent! I used a pound of asparagus only, sliced regular radishes with my mandolin, and chopped whole almonds with my food processor.
I only used about half of the crunchy topping mixture.
Next time I’m thinking of making some poached shrimp to make a delicious spring dinner salad.

Served with salmon and the sweetness was nice with the fish.  I'm not a huge maple syrup fan, and I might substitute more sugar and less maple, or try another sweetener (date syrup?)  This is not a recipe for the best, tastiest spring asparagus, since their essential flavor gets diluted.  But this is a great thing to do with tired grocery store asparagus and bagged, washed green beans

This, from the main article, also sounds really good: “Diana Henry, another British writer, simply mixes butter with harissa and lime, allows it to melt over the warm spears and then sprinkles over chopped lime zest, cilantro and garlic.”

Note to Margaret: 
Asparagus and green beans are always available at the same time here in California. I think it's likely they can be found in cities in the US since California produce is shipped.

I wanted something more like dinner so I marinated cubes of tofu in the dressing (I made extra - one lime supplied more than enough.) Next time I'll use silken tofu but the firm tofu was still a good addition. It was less spicy than I expected (maybe my pepper flakes are getting tired?) I only used green beans - I think they go with the dressing better than asparagus.

Everyone loved this. I followed the recipe and served it with grilled salmon and rice. The dressing and sprinkles were great on the rice and fish too. This will be an easy meal to serve to guests to highlight great PNW fish and produce.

Made this again this past week for a potluck, and it went like hotcakes, but better! It is incredibly flavorful and exciting, with many tastes and textures. The recipe is perfect the way it is. Since I was traveling to the potluck, I artfully arranged the vegetables on a big platter and poured the dressing on top upon arrival, lightly incorporating it in with the veggies, and then sprinkled the topping. I dry the beans and asparagus by leaving them out on top a tea towel on a cooling rack.

This was fantastic!  My 11 year old ate well from the bowl I served, which makes mom happy!  I didn’t have lime so used juice of 1 lemon.  Am not familiar with “breakfast radishes”, so used red radishes that I sliced and then soaked for about 15 minutes in a bowl with salt and water.  Rather than boil the beans and asparagus, I put olive oil in a cast iron pan and sautéed the vegetables for a few minutes with kosher salt, black pepper, and a bit of basil.  Very much a keeper of a recipe!!!!
Shrimp were a delicious addition. Will also add red bell pepper next time for color and taste. Served over jasmine rice cooked in broth.
Does anyone have a suggestion for what to add (if anything) if we leave out the miso? We like all the other ingredients, just don't like the fermented flavor of miso.
Maggi?
asparagus season is feb-thru june, with peak being april/may. green beans are may- october. seems there is some overlap there. i can find both and my farmers market right now.

Where?  Here in Massachusetts, the ground is still frozen in February, and you wouldn't even plant bean seeds before mid-May, except possibly in a greenhouse. Asparagus starts in early May and is gone long before beans are ready. Please be careful about absolute statements.

In my experience boiling asparagus for 4 minutes will ruin them.  One minute tops for small ones and maybe 90 seconds for larger spears.  Put them in an ice bath and once cool pat them dry.  They last in the refrigerator for several days.
Loved the combination of textures and flavors, but I would omit the sugar next time.

Delish. Only suggestion is to shorten cooking times by at least 1 minute, probably 1.5 min. Better when veggies are crisp. If no Aleppo pepper (yup - I don’t have it either) then tsp paprika and scant 1/4 tsp cayenne works well without big pepper chunks.
Have not boiled most vegetables, in years, mostly roast.  Does not lose flavor, only enhances.
Might work better to cook the asparagus and beans separately if the beans are not very fresh. Otherwise the asparagus is too soft
This looks delicious, excited to make it! I’m going to use fiddleheads for the green beans. But I gotta say there’s no way this takes 5 minutes :D
This is really quite delicious. I sliced radishes. And cut veggies on bias. Would be a good summer picnic salad.

It's amazing how a recipe with so few ingredients (well, for Ottolenghi anyway, but maybe not his sister) could taste so marvelous, We were licking the bowls before washing them. I don't know what breakfast radishes are either, so I just used regular American red radishes.

So delicious.  Put a a fried egg for dinner. Perfect.  Planning on making this with whatever vegetables are in season that match the crunch factor — it’s all about the dressing and the topping. I’d order the recipe differently — making topping first, while water boils. Then make dressing while veggies cook.
Girl in her mid 20s here- i tried making this and it didn't really work out - it tasted kind of bitter, but also sugary... not sure what i did wrong, anyone else?

OMG, this was beyond fabulous!!  This has to be one of the best recipes that I have made from the times.  Amazing blend of flavors and textures.  Followed the recipe exactly, we both loved it and devoured all of it.  Will be making this again and again and again.  And its so easy to prepare!  Highly recommended!

I made this again after a sabbatical, having made it often, and it became my contribution to shared dinner events. It is so delicious! In mid-summer, I made it with only fresh green beans. Like others have mentioned, both vegetables aren’t available fresh at the same time of the year. Laying the beans out on a kitchen towel, after the ice-water bath, completely blots any excess water. Otherwise, the excess water dilutes the dressing.

In a word, wow. Given the eclectic list of ingredients, this took a small leap of faith for me, but once again Yotam O. proves himself to be a master of flavor. Keep a close eye on the almonds and coconut while toasting, and make extra of the crunchy topping (it would be delicious on scoop of olive oil ice cream). Phenomenal dish.

@E, the bitterness is from the radishes.  To tame the bite, get yourself a good sharp chef’s knife, slice them as thin as you can, then submerge them in cold water while you prep the rest of the salad.  That will wash out some of the bitter compounds and make them crispy.  If you like fresh veg, this trick works with celery, carrots, etc.  Longer soaking & icy water is best, but a few minutes in cold tap water is fine, too.  I made this recipe without the sugar.  The maple was enough sweetness.
What is a good substitute for breakfast radishes?
This is a really fun way to eat green beans and asparagus! Really springy -- the coconut topping is refreshing and flavorful. Will make again for sure.

My partner is not a huge asparagus fan (and didn't have beans on hand) so I replaced with a good portion of asparagus with snow peas from garden.   Serundeng condiment is fantastic complement to lime dressing....used Chipotle smoked red jalapeno pepper seasoning in place of Aleppo.  Makes ample amount of topping for other uses.  Congrats on awakening the mundane side asparagus/bean dish to a star of the meal status!
As a gardener in NE PA I don't like recipes like this.  When the Asparagus is done for the year it's time to start picking green beans.  Can't make this one....

I used both beans and asparagus (boiled separately for just minutes each and let them dry-nice vibrant green). And as usual, Yotam does it again! so many textures and flavors. Absolutely delicious! He is my favorite chef! (and thanks for the readers that recommeneded doubling it-I agree!)
Nice salad, served with some peel and eat shrimp. No white miso so substitute a good soy. Added a bit of leek and regular American radish.  Excellent flavor and will make again.
Delicious....I used Basil not Cilantro.  One of my guests does not like coconut and did not realise they were eating it. Bought regular beans, not French, and split them.

The dressing and almond-coco topping are divine. I thought it contained too much fibrous veggie so the second time I made it I replaced the asparagus with very crunchy little gems lettuce, and just blanch and seared the greenbeans. Much preferred it with those adjustments, so good!!

Great meal when you need some veggies. I added shredded chicken that was boiled with ginger, garlic and bone broth, a touch of the miso wouldnt have hurt either. Topped with a few green onions also just because
Our curmudgeonly vegetarian grampa commented, "Oh, I see you added every possible food item in here." Clean plate. Win.

I’ve made this so many times! One of the most spectacular dishes. I’ve started making it without the asparagus, radishes or cilantro to simplify and honestly it’s just as fabulous. Can’t recommend highly enough. Make extra topping and keep in a Tupperware for future dishes.

I would make this again, but entirely with asparagus or beans. The overall flavor was amazing, but my guests didn't love the vegetable combo. When asparagus is in season in the Pacific Northwest, it's amazing, but seems like the time of year when it shines, beans are being trucked up the entire west coast and are woody and sad.
excellent. Served with grilled chicken. A winner.

Yet another ridiculous recipe from Ottolenghi. Way too many ingredients. The sugar, maple syrup, coconut, and miso are completely unnecessary. If the asparagus and beans are fresh and organic, they don't need sweetening at all. Just plain old salt, some olive oil or butter, and maybe a bit of feta cheese. That's all. Less is more.
Are we using dried shredded coconut or fresh shredded coconut. I have both but I am unclear on which type is being used here?

Just made this and had to restrain myself from eating the entire batch. I took Katya's suggestion to use peanut oil in the dressing. Used date syrup instead of maple syrup cuz I ran out of the latter one teaspoon in. Also subbed date syrup for granulated sugar cuz why not. 

10/10, will be in heavy rotation this summer.

I am a big fan of this chef, have two of his cookbooks. He is a very creative chef, and I love his varied use of vegetables. 
This was a very nice Springtime salad, and I am glad I had a lot of the ingredients like red miso, Aleppo pepper, grapeseed oil, etc on hand. Tasty and unusual.
What's a "breakfast radish"?
With all of those ingredients, did anyone actually taste the asparagus?

We made this tonight together and it was amazing! Actually let it sit a bit more and the flavors intensified. We had a lovely Argentina Chard. Sad only two of us and we finished it. Will make again. Thank you Yotam!
Boiling asparagus for 4 minutes will yield green shoelaces! Instead pan-fry in ghee for a minute...also go to any good farmers market and buy fiddleheads for the beans.
Substitute sugar snap peas for the green beans, or snow peas.
This was an easy and delicious dish. I will make it again and again.

Made this during the week since I had all the ingredients.  Super easy and quick. I love the balance of tart and spicy. I went with string beans and asparagus. Loved the crunchy almonds and coconut. It's a hit. Will keep making!

So. Good. Perfect flavor balance with the dressing and topping; both would be good on a piece of fish, too.  Served with pork chops and white rice, used asparagus only.  Next time I would serve it warm, but it does work at room temp/cool for a picnic or to make ahead.
The is great as written. Made it for a foursome with grilled pork tenderloin. It can be labor intensive and I prepped everything on the morning to enable fast assembly.

And Yotam knocks yet another one out of the park. Delish! The miso-lime vinaigrette was sublime and provided the perfect zing for the mildy-spiced toasted almond-coconut business that was like a party dress for the asparagus and green beans. Threw some cubed sriracha-baked tempeh on top to make it a meal - and a very good one at that.

The salad is fine, but that spicy, crunchy topping is sublime! I made a double recipe of just that (but with as much shredded coconut as almonds) to stir into cottage cheese, add to oatmeal, pile onto brie…

Another Yotam hit (umami homerun) and it is easy to make! I had almost 2 lbs of asparagus, used most of it without the green beans or radish. The leftover asparagus in the dressing is fine by itself and will go into a salad. I cooked the asparagus at 2 minutes. Thank  you!
This was absolutely delicious. We had it as a main with a side of hummus and naan bread.
Holy cow, this is good! Take it easy on the maple syrup, but overall this is a terrific recipe.
Such a delicious dish - make sure to follow the recipe exactly as is.
Sometimes I think Ottolenghi overcomplicates recipes.  Not here.  This is perfect as it.
Made this last night and it was fabulous!   I followed the recipe as described but swapped radish for radish micro greens.  An incredibly healthy yet indulgent tasting recipe.

I couldn’t find breakfast radishes so I subbed local fresh baby turnips and sliced them very thin. That worked so well. I am not a fan of cilantro so I used my garden parsley which gave a different flavor profile of course. And I totally overcooked the veggies. It was still over the top delicious!

Used white asparagus, which I had to shave, but they are sweeter. Also added shrimp to make it a more complete meal. Doubled it. Will try it next time with green asparagus. Delicious and sophisticated -- will impress your guests.
Truly yummy and even better when broccolini (by itself) is used.
This is great. Instead of coconut flakes (my husband hates the texture but likes flavor) added about 1/2 T  coconut cream to the dressing. More like zest. Liberal w miso too. Yummy —and big hit.
Friend made this and it was delicious. I am now going to add to my list.

Love this recipe! I replaced the red pepper flakes with 1 tsp of Sriracha sauce because I was out of red pepper flakes and it tasted amazing…might actually continue to do that instead of red pepper flakes.
This was a big hit with my foodie friend. Didn't have the breakfast radishes, so just used regular ones.

This was absolutely delicious. I substituted watermelon radish for the breakfast radishes, which added some beautiful color. I also substituted malted rice miso for the white miso, since it was what I had on hand. So good!
Absolutely delicious, exactly as written.
Great recipe!

Made this without with radishes (I’m just not personally a huge fan). And I’m not kidding when I say it’s like crack! Can’t stop eating. As much as I love cilantro, it’s an easy omission for anyone that doesn’t care for it

I love this! And all while I was making the dressing I thought it was so weird it would turn out terrible.  But. Totally amazing!  Used all asparagus, and regular radishes and red? Miso paste sine that’s what I could find.
This was a stunning, bright vegetable dish. My family loved it.
We have beautiful organic green beens in our market as well as spring asparagus. No. California.

This was incredibly good, especially for the small amount of work required.  I roasted the asparagus and didn't use green beans. I'm going to serve this at Easter to offset the richness of the ham and potatoes. Yum!

We made this twice in two days. It’s so good! So well balanced. If anyone is wondering if you can serve this warm/room temperature, the answer is yes. I roasted the vegetables instead of boiling and dressed them from the oven.

I love Ottolenghi, but his recipes are rarely accessible for weeknight family cooking. This is a welcome exception! Made as written and my husband and I couldn’t stop eating it. The dressing alone is just so delicious. Asparagus and green beans have never been so snack-able!

Really, really enjoyed, and the only change I’d make is to cut asparagus and green beans to 2-3” pieces. Our Easter guests struggled with very full plate to add this salad and then deal with it around all the other sides. Probably not an issue if it’s the only (or one of two) sides.

Absolute flavour bomb. Well worth the five stars. 
Added some fresh mint also, recommend.
Phenomenal flavors. The chile coconut almond topping is genius
This was delicious! I added a few chopped makrut lime leaves to the coconut mixture which made it even better!
I brought this to a Seder last weekend and three other people also brought asparagus. But one of the hosts told me my dish was the best one!

I’m addicted to this! I used asparagus (no green beans) with a little baby kale, and used the nut mix from: https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/1021034-chile-oil-fried-eggs-with-greens-and-yogurt?smid=ck-recipe-iOS-share Doubled the sauce, served with a little jasmine rice and ! Ottolenghi always rocks it.

I LOVE this dressing and will use it with other veggies in the future. Broccolini would be great. I was licking the dressing off my hand like a dog with a hot spot. I did not have plain coconut so used the Dang coconut chips and omitted the sugar from the topping. I only had 4 regular radishes and half the almonds so I eyeballed portions to half the recipe. Very forgiving.

Swapped the almonds for hazelnuts and the grape seed oil for hazelnut oil. Was too late to the magnet for the little pink radishes so used mandoline sliced japanese black radish. Had to make if two nights in a row due to popular demand. PS: taste test different miso pastes to find your favourite. They’re all different.

This is so addictively delicious! I couldn’t get breakfast radishes, so I substituted jicama. It was great. My asparagus was definitely overcooked, so next time I’ll boil the veggies one at a time. I’m looking forward to sharing this one at gatherings!

I substituted celery for the beans (including boiling it for a few minutes) and added a great crunch! Was a fantastic recipe. I loved the unusual flavor combinations and so did the others lucky enough to try it!
Asparagus and sugar snap peas. More lime juice, less oil in dressing. Regular radishes. Used sweetened shredded coconut and didn't use the sugar in topping. I would do it again. And again. And again!
Try w broccoli, or sweet potatoes or snap peas instead

Delish! Recipe very adaptable to loved ones’ palettes and/or dietary needs.  Subbed lemon for lime, eliminated miso, and lowered overall sugar content (maple syrup and sugar for coconut).  Steamed the beans too.  Yum!
So easy and delicious! I mandolined the radishes, but should have quartered them as suggested.
This is bright! I knew I'd be eating it all alone, so I kept the parts separate and just mixed as needed for each meal. It works. It is a springtime firework in a bowl

This dressing was absolutely delightful. Served the salad with a homemade butternut squash soup and baguette. I did chop up and add an avocado that needed using. I will make sure next time that miso is a little more on the room temperature side to make mixing dressing  easier. And mind your almonds...on my stove, they nearly burned even with constant attention at 3 minutes on medium low heat.
What protein(s) would people recommend adding to this dish? Unfortunately, I’m not a huge fan of seafood or tofu as options.
I love this dish so much! I've made it fully, and to simplify I've made it without the radishes and asparagus (double the green beans) and no cilantro and it's just as good.
Made this as a bring a plate and got so many compliments. Am currently making it (by request) for my third Chrissy event of the season. Being down under salads are typical Christmas fare.

So here’s the plan: make a big batch of the topping and have it on hand for quickly whipping up this salad at the end of the work day. 

Or, just eat it by the spoonful.

I mixed up another recipe with this one and added a shallot but what a happy accident! Tastes wonderful with the bite from the shallot. I didn’t have coconut so I omitted it but will add it next time!
What is a breakfast radish? I have been cooking since I was 8 years old (over 70 years) and never heard of or saw this in a store. Otherwise, this sounds great!

I didn't have any maple syrup, so used honey instead, and it was super delicious!  I think that topping would be good on a lot a vegetables--I'm thinking even carrots!  Will definitely be making it again.
can I make this without the miso?

Wow - this is fantastic! It does not need so much sugar. I sprinkled a tiny bit on the almonds and only used a teaspoon of maple syrup. I’m not sure it even needs that much. I subbed parsley for cilantro and did not use coconut but we loved the dressing. Interestingly, I was also making black bean burgers to go with the salad. I cannot tell you how delicious this dressing is on a black bean burger!!!

We love this dish. It does not need as much sugar. I didn’t use sugar while toasting the almonds and only used 1 teaspoon of maple syrup. It was still excellent. I happen to be serving black bean burgers and the dressing is also excellent on those.

Did this with just asparagus (no green beans) from the farmer's market. I know a lot of the comments suggest saving this recipe for older, store-bought asparagus, but it was still tasty as all get out. Huge hit for a family dinner with a vegan. (Note: I used the mildest possible red pepper flakes (Flatiron's sweet heat) to make this more approachable.)

This recipe was amazing, and I followed it to the letter, except I blanched the beans first then added the asparagus. That kept the asparagus from mushing out, since all we could find were very thin spears. This dish is going to be in heavy rotation!
This was an absolutely delicious recipe. Served it with king salmon and added the dressing over the salmon - divine! Will be making this again for sure. I love Yotam Ottolenghi’s recipes.

Very easy and spectacular—sophisticated flavor profile.  I cut the red pepper flakes to 1 tsp. and that was plenty spicy to keep from overwhelming the taste of the vegetables.  Only the slightest hint of sweetness.  Brought to a Supper Club and people loved it.  Thank you Ottolenghi!

I made this two times.  First followed directions exactly and the almonds burned within 90 seconds (using a stainless steel pan) and the coconut did not taste very good.  Since so many people rated it with a lot of stars, I decided to try again.  I feel it is much better to brown the almonds in some butter on med low.  Then remove the almonds from the pan and add the coconut mixture with or without butter.  It is easier to get everything nicely browned and consistent.

This was so dang good! I accidentally bought sweetened coconut. To "Un" sweeten it I rinsed it in a colander and dried it. The end result tasted great - but the texture was off as the coconut didn't crisp (my bad).

We liked it so much I shared the recipe with my entire family who love to cook!
This was well balanced and tasty. Will definitely make again. My asparagus lost a fair bit of colour in the boiling process but texture was still good, and the nut and coconut topping is pretty fun!

title: Cheese and Spinach Phyllo Rolls Recipe
author: Yotam Ottolenghi

comments.commentBody:
The spinach could be lightly salted and squeezed for a few minutes to soften and remove extra moisture, like a massaged kale salad, then chopped up. The bright fresh flavor is so good and no need to put in the recipe’s hot water step for preparing the spinach. 
All so delicious!

Lightly salt the raw spinach and squeeze out the extra moisture ( like a massaged kale salad). It would not take long and the flavor of the raw spinach is really good. I was taught this technique by a Syrian cook.
Or put spinach in colander and pour boiling water over to wilt it.
I'm thinking that if you didn't want to mess with phyllo, the spinach ( frozen perhaps?) and onion/cheese mixture would be great over rice or pasta.
You could also use frozen wheat-based spring roll wrappers, which are forgivingly springy and easy to roll, but bake up with a satisfyingly shatter-y crispness.
Put spinach in the microwave for a short time - seconds to a minute depending on you device- and it will wilt.

Recipe doesn't say what size phyllo sheets to use, but if you're using the standard 12"x17" from the grocery, it's better to halve them rather than cut them in thirds. Thirds are 4" wide which doesn't leave enough room for filling and edge-folding. Brush oil or melted ghee/butter up the sides before folding. Also, triple the lemon juice in the filling. It's about right in the topping. This is definitely yummy.

If you’re too lazy to roll the filo, you can bake it in on a sheet pan. Place ten filo rolls horizontally. Then do two vertically, add the filling, and fold them. Then do ten more horizontally. Brush melted butter between each layer of filo and add it on top. Cut it into squares before you bake it. Love from a diaspora cook.

There is a recipe on NYT for spanikopita pie - basically filling baked in a large open “pie”shell of phyllo in a cast iron pan. This recipe would be excellent baked in the same manner for an easy weeknight dinner, without the fussiness of making individual rolls.
Would these freeze well? If so, how best to reheat?

Yes. Freeze rolls before baking. Bake the frozen rolls as directed in step 6 of the recipe. You may need a minute or two longer in the oven.

I make a “spiced lamb” roll up with apricots, cheese and pinenuts much the same way. I make a large batch and freeze whatever I’m not making that day. Easy appetizer when friends are over!

Agree with Terry. The spinach does not need to be blanched. Like many Ottolenghi recipes, this one has too many (unnecessary) ingredients and steps. Works fine with only one type of chess (I used feta). And the last step of drizzling with the onion mixture can be totally skipped.
Is the ‘dried sumac’ ground or something else? It would make a difference in the measurement.

OMG these are so good I wish I had made a double batch. I made a few minor substitutions. I used shredded mozzarella and walnuts . I could eat the entire tray if I allowed myself. 
I am bringing them to a few friends. 
I only used half of the box of phyllo sheets so I made a custard crumple to finish off the meal
These were delicious, I came home from work, started cooking and was ready to eat in 96 minutes.  Maybe a little extra lemon juice with the spinach.

These turned out much better than I expected.  A little time consuming, but pretty fun to roll them up.  Definitely don't let the phyllo dry out cuz it just disintegrates. 
Added some sundried tomatoes because I had a Costco sized jar and used walnuts cuz I didn't know I was out of pine nuts.  The lemon flavor comes through just right.  Didn't bother with the topping.  Everyone loved them!
I’m wondering about a gluten free version of this. Maybe use as a tart filling in a cornmeal crust?
Is the ‘dried sumac’ in the recipe ground and dried or something else?

I brought these to a potluck last night and everyone liked them. I couldn't find halloumi so I substituted mozzarella.  Thank you Terry for the suggestion of massaging the spinach! that worked perfectly.  I used 1/2 cup plus of toasted walnuts for added crunch.
Would it be possible to freeze the rolls, and bake just before serving?
I spead the spinach onion mixture over puff pastry, rolled into logs and sliced to make pinwheels, very successful.  Leftover filling is great with roasted salmon.
I just made all the rolls and realized that I left out the cinamon and mint. Will they still be OK?
A huge hit enjoyed by everyone.

title: Cabbage Rolls With Walnuts and Sour Cream Recipe
author: Yotam Ottolenghi

comments.commentBody: My Aunt Carol makes excellent sarma, a Croatian version of stuffed cabbage. Instead of blanching the cabbage, she put the head in the freezer for 20-30 minutes. That softens up the leaves sufficiently and saves her (and her helpers) from dealing with a boiling hot head of cabbage.

You'd need a chef to prepare this. Why not simply roast slabs of cabbage in olive oil at 400 in the oven and top with walnuts and sautéed onion and spices -- skip the butter. Add sour cream if you want the calories.
Non fat yogurt works for sour cream.
Mary Anderson, you had me until "skip the butter."

Don't people visit this site to learn to be a chef? There's always a "simple" way to cook: the microwave, a frozen meal, delivery. 

I can make 2 pieces of toast and put a slice of cheese between them. Or I can make a lovely grilled cheese sandwich: simpler, but not the same thing.
Lacking an army of babushkas, I’ll appropriate this delicious sounding recipe and convert it to a casserole. Less work but still promising.
please give us details on how you do this!

When I think of cabbage rolls, I envision an Orthodox Church kitchen, filled with babushkas, seriously at work producing brassica rolls for sale. To earn a spot as a roller, one must have been there in that kitchen for forty years. Younger cooks get the tedious or dangerous jobs, such as blanching the cabbage leaves and retrieving them from a steaming pot. The top babushkas roll the bundles as tightly as they’d swaddle a newborn, limbs carefully tucked in the receiving blanket.
Freeze the whole head of cabbage for a few hours or overnight and the leaves will peel off with ease when you're ready to cook. Much easier.

This was so much fun to make. I prepped it today for a twist on the flabby cabbage served with corned beef on St. Paddy’s Day. It was easy to make and it smells scrumptious. Someone mentioned pine nuts being expensive. This recipe only had three tablespoons. I purchase pine nuts from Trader Joe’s, and they are much less expensive there.
Pine nuts, while delicious, are pricey.  I have often substituted hemp hearts with good results.

Re step 4: The stem is very thick at the base of each leaf and then thins out toward the top of the leaf. That thick part at the base is a problem because it's too stiff to roll up the way you need it to (even after blanching). So, you hold a knife parallel to the cutting surface and basically shave down the stem where it protrudes thickly near the base. Then it's easier to fill and roll up the leaf.

Cabbage rolls brings to mind my partner hosting two Ukrainian ladies for an English instructors seminar. They arrived straight from Ukraine, had never seen or used a seat belt before. They wanted to cook dinner. We visited a Safeways to buy supplies. Much discussion with the butcher on the proper grind of beef. Then cabbage selection. We left with four of the largest heads of cabbage for a dinner party of four. Four or five hours later we had a feast. Wonderful food and lifelong friends.
Priceless!  Thanks for sharing this!
Another substitute for pricey pine nuts are cooked/canned garbanzos/chick peas.  I use them all the time for pesto.

This was sublime. I made it for friends, along with a simple fish fillet under broiler. A bit of rice pilaf with lemon zest, parsley and sliced almonds on the side, and people were asking for the recipes.  Spiced walnuts and creamy cabbage stole the show.
A couple days ago right after I rolled up and arranged some cabbage rolls in a plan, my son saw them and said excitedly, "They look like giant larvae!"

I buy pine nuts in the bulk section of my local Winco. About $9 a pound. I find I can buy a serious amount for very cheap. I keep them in a sealed glass pint canning jar in the freezer. Easy peasy and cheap.
This was outstanding! I poured the warm sour cream directly onto the serving plates and placed the cabbage rolls on top. Makes for a delightful sauce.

This was absolutely delicious. I did use a lot less butter and a bit more olive oil. I also substituted raisins for the pine nuts. I didn't find it particularly difficult or time consuming to blanche the cabbage leaves. I will be making this again in the fall for sure!
@SSB Raisins! That’s a great idea!

This sounds delicious  I will definitely try it. My family recipe for stuffed cabbage has never included the sweet and sour red sauce. It's always been with potatoes and Saurkraut nestled among the meat and rice stuffed cabbage. We would add a small amount of liquid to the pot (mixture of water/chicken broth and apple cider vinegar) and toss in  some caraway seeds and chopped onions. I also have baked this with good results. So this recipe is close to my Slovak and Hungarian heart.
Olive oil instead of butter for the vegan folks .

I made this recipe as it is written and it is well worth the hour of prep! Even though the prep time is long it’s very straight forward—not a lot of complex thinking. Once it’s in the oven you can sit back and enjoy a glass or two of wine while you wait for the dinner bell. The leftovers are also delicious. I would not change anything about the recipe.

I made these Sunday, subbing some olive oil for some of the butter but otherwise as directed. My spouse loves cabbage and absolutely hated these. He accused me of forgetting a key ingredient. I was not wowed, either. However, the leftovers, after 2 days in the fridge and slow reheating at 200 degrees in the toaster oven, were significantly better. The strong lemon-dill aspect diminished while the rest of the herbs and spices melded into something much tastier!

Cardboard would taste great with those ingredients. 3/4 of a cup of butter? That mix of tangy and sweet spices? 

Doesn't make a not-a-cabbage-fan into a cabbage fan.

I never thought that I would ever freeze a full (and beautiful) head of Savoy cabbage. I understood the science, but it still seemed somewhat sacrilegious. Could it really be that easy? Having tried, I do recommend the trick. You are robbed of the wonderful smell of blanched fresh cabbage, but it beats dealing with scalding water. The recipe is delicious, too!

It's funny seeing commenters complaining that you need to be a "chef," to make something as basic as cabbage rolls. I can attest that the freezing method is much simpler than blanching though. 

This kind of filling mix is completely alien to me, but I am intrigued and will definitely have to try it.

This is time consuming, but so worth it. The yummiest cabbage rolls I've ever had/made. I froze the cabbage for half a day, as others suggested, and the leaves were NOT easy to peel off. I'll blanch next time. Substituted raisins for pine nuts. Served them with a lemon parsley rice pilaf. So very flavorful and satisfying.
I'm fond of cabbage and always appreciative of new recipes.  Unfortunately, I found the end result of this involved process to be unctuous and underwhelming, in desperate need of acidity.
Acid for it: I've made cabbage rolls dish (from Julie Jordan) with a base of sauerkraut rather than shredded cabbage
Fabulous! I threw some chanterelles in with the onions at about 15 minutes. So good!

Only had green cabbage but made it work even though I only ended up with three larger sized rolls. For shortfall I incorporated some Napa cabbage, green lettuce and even radicchio for a number of smaller rolls.  The filling is delicious, the recipe is easy even with the many steps, making everything in time for tonight’s Mets vs Dodgers game 5. Go Mets!
Has anyone tried adding ground beef to this?
Romanians.  It is called sarmale.  Very good!  I don't think they use nuts, however.

As a fan of cabbage and especially cabbage rolls I’m keen to try this. I remember as a Uni student making a version of cabbage rolls (based on my mother’s recipe) filled with rice and topped with a lemony tomato-based sauce - economical, filling and easy to prepare. I’m looking forward to trying this slightly more elevated version with its walnuts and sour cream, it sounds simple but I’m sure will be delicious as Yottam is a master of flavour.
Sounds delicious but a bit fussy to make, maybe use this recipe for a special occasion.. Also, what do you do with the extra chopped cabbage?
In step 4, the 4 cups of finely sliced cabbage is placed in the baking dish.  The cabbage rolls go onto the bed of sliced cabbage.
coleslaw

I have always cut the core flat, carefully removed the large leaves and put them in the microwave, three at a time, for about 30 seconds. No boiling water. Shave the thick end of rib down with veg peeler.

In order to incorporate meat into the recipe, I browned 3/4 lb ground pork, added 1   cup of leftover cooked rice, and some hot pepper flakes.  I put both the onion mixture and the pork mixture into the cabbage rolls.  The cabbage rolls are stogies rather than cigarillos, but the concept still works.  I apologize if I have disturbed a beloved tradition.  They do taste good.

Personally I’d sauté the cabbage then top with the nut/butter mixture and sour cream. Cabbage tastes better sautéed or roasted than boiled. Also the recipe as written would take hours - sautéed or roasted cabbage with this topping makes this under an hour.
This was outstanding just as is. It’s a great dish to make for guests because it goes in the oven before anyone arrives and there’s very little to do once it comes out. Will definitely make it again!

There is nothing good about this recipe, except maybe the sour cream. I followed the labor intensive instructions to a T. Looked interesting, but no. Has a fishy/paint taste. Yeccch.
Never again.

I am going to try to add a few chopped and sautéed mushrooms to this.  But wanted to add the best cabbage recipe bar none is an old recipe featured in the movie Haute Cousine, savoy cabbage filled with salmon and cooked as a whole.

Armenians make a vegan version (lahana yalanchi sarma) that is served cold/room temp, where uncooked rice and currants are added to the filling mixture which is sauteed in olive oil, not butter, and the rolls are then simmered in water with lemon juice until the rice is cooked thoroughly.  A deconstructed version can also be made by shredding the whole head of cabbage and omitting rice.  Traditional yalanchi are made using grape leaves (in a jar).  A squeeze of lemon is all that's needed.
Made this. Surprisingly bland. Very disappointed.

This was excellent and not at all difficult to prepare, though I do have practice making sarmale.  I followed the prep as written but used pepitas in lieu of pine nuts.  I did not end up with the beautiful silky sauce shown in the photo.  Next time I will use more butter!?  I might also brush the rolls with olive oil after removing the foil to brown them.

This was so delicious and fun to make.  The instructions were very clear and now I'm not afraid to make stuffed cabbage in any form.  I did try to freeze the cabbage for about 45 minutes, but it just wasn't enough to soften the leaves and I still ended up blanching it.
Delicious and fun to make. Add raisins, brings this dish over the top.

PS: Also as a vegan, I of course skipped the butter and just used olive oil. But mostly, I couldn't imagine rubbing the filling through a sieve. I went at it a bit more with a pestle after cooking it and loved the crunchy, chewy result.

We loved these! I made them for lunch with a friend and my husband and did not find the process to be too time consuming or onerous. I was able to assemble the rolls in less than an hour and then they baked for another hour. I did not strain the onions though and used 2 TBS of ghee to cook them. I also added 2 shredded carrots to the stuffing and used about 1.5 TBS of ghee on the bed of shredded cabbage. Do not skip the lemon juice! I used plant based “sour cream” for the sauce. YUM!

Yum! Made it dairy free and bulked it up for a filling main dish: used olive oil instead of butter, added 1/2 a block of crumbled tofu to the onion and nut mixture (subbed sunflower seeds for pine nuts), and added cooked bulgur for heft. Added chicken broth to the pan so they wouldn't dry out during cooking. Topped with almond yogurt. Very filling and spiced.

My ethnic background is Eastern European, so I can make stuffed cabbage rolls in my sleep and I do not need to pare down the stems before rolling the leaves. If you choose rush this recipe and compile all the ingredients into one jumble and bake it, you will not taste the nuances of each individual aroma and flavor. To casual cooks out there, I recommend that you stick to the recipe according to each instruction. Amazing dish!

Delicious filling! I used a large head of "regular" green cabbage, which is what I had on hand, and tossed the chopped-up cabbage with some of the reserved spiced butter when making the bottom-layer bed for the rolls. As cabbage reduces a lot in the baking process, the dish could've used more chopped cabbage. Served with saffron rice, this dish was a big hit. While I can't argue with the flavor of butter, for a healthier dish I'll substitute olive oil the next time I make it.

Delicious.  Made with white cabbage because I prefer the sturdy leaves for rolling and stronger taste.  Added sunflower seeds and golden raisins, as well as the lemon zest and juice to the mixture.  I used 2T to sauté the onions. Served with nonfat quark.  I felt it was rich enough without the butter sauce or sour cream.
Details on how to make this a casserole, please?   I only have half a cabbage so can’t stuff the leaves
Sauté or roast the cabbage then top with the nut mixture.

What a ridiculous recipe. Why not use straw instead of walnuts.
/
While this sounds delicious, I could just hear my coronary arteries clogging from all the butter.

Decades ago, when I was in my 20's, I made my family's sweet and sour stuffed cabbage for an office holiday party. A coworker, decades older than I asked me how I kept the cabbage rolls rolled without using string. I shared the technique of shaving the midrib of each cabbage leaf. She was so grateful to have this simple solution. Now I have to try this delightful sounding recipe.
So the cabbage rolls are just the outer leaf, no cabbage inside?
The remaining cabbage after preparing the leaves is used as a bed in the baking dish. Never seen a cabbage roll with cabbage inside - rice, meat, nuts, etc., no cabbage.
If you make this a day ahead, what is the optimal reheating method?

I'm Slovak/Ukie/Polish from Cleveland, where cabbage rolls are part of our Culinary Pantheon. Most of us freeze the entire head, let it thaw, cut the core, and those leaves peel off beautifully. When making cabbage rolls for myself, I don't even trim the rib off -- freezing makes the rib pliable for rolling. I'll trim only if serving my Polish MIL. Roll the leaf like you're making a burrito, it's the same.  Why drain that tasty butter? I wouldn't brown the leaves at all on this recipe.
Sounds wonderful but I am allergic to walnuts.  Any recommendations for substitutions?
Remind me of Holishkes, a traditional Jewish dish, with the cabbage wrapped round mincemeat.
I am allergic to pine nuts. What would be a good substitute for me please? Thank you for your time.

You may have experienced "pine mouth" rather than a regular allergy. European (mostly Italian) pine nuts seem to all be species that don't cause that. China also exports pine nuts, usually much cheaper, but can include species that do cause some people problems.
Georgians use eggplant for pkhali!

A keeper-  Not difficult to make, if you stage the preparation steps one day and coo construct the next. Fun to put together. I used Napa cabbage 'cause Savoy wasn't available; leaves are thinner, so be careful and unfold the edges before constructing. Volume of filling was 1/3 more than I needed to form 20  rolls. So I will use rest to make a pilaf. I did add 1/2C chopped raisins for some sweetness, next time will use 1C chopped craisins. couls make with ground meat or tofu instead of walnuts.
Might be another perfect for Passover dish.

Maybe I had a super large cabbage but there was not nearly enough to fill 12 cabbage leaves and I even mixed in some finely cut cabbage!  I would actually mix in more cabbage next time for some added texture and flavor.  I would also thin the sour cream sauce some because the dish can be a little dry
