Made with Stan March 2023 - fun to do together.  A bit rich for me!  Served with rice.

This was time-consuming, but not difficult, and the results were well worth it. A rich, perfumed,  and satisfying dish, with a recognizably Ottolenghi flavor profile. Next time I'd add raisins or currants to the stuffing for a little sweetness and additional texture. Will definitely make again.

Delicious meal that makes you feel like you are eating a combination of tastes you have not had before! It’s like something you might get at a very fancy, unique vegetarian restaurant. Pairing with a rice makes for nice meal and complements the strong flavors in this dish. Make sure to read the accompanying article. 

It takes some time, but using a food processor to do some of the prep work helped. 

Definitely worth the effort, it impressed! For leftovers, try serving with cold sour cream.

Neither having nor being an Eastern European mama, I enjoyed making this recipe! I did grow up south of Pittsburgh, where cabbage rolls featured regularly in school lunches and wedding receptions! I tried the freezing trick and it worked really well. I used avocado oil in lieu of most of the butter and cut back on the final bit of dotting butter over the rolls using only about 2 Tbs. The sauteeing procedure in 1/2 cup butter in addition to sour cream sauce seemed rich enough! Delicious.

BIG hit! As a vegan, instead of sour cream I diluted Tofutti cream cheese with some runny but well soured  yogurt (Melissa Clark's cashew milk recipe) and sprinkled my favorite condiment, Nature's Seasons, on it and it was delicious. Before putting the thinly sliced cabbage into the baking dish, I mixed it with olive oil and Nature' Seasons. The filling was just brilliant! I agree with the reader who suggested simplifying this dish by layering cabbage and filling lasagna-style. Next time!
This was so good, but I think next time I will increase the spice level by 1/2 again as much

The filling hits the hallmark flavor profiles of Middle Eastern type of expensive restaurants. Wrapped in cabbage, after an hour in the oven, the dish melds and smells wonderful. On the palate, comes across as a less-sour sauerkraut with a lot of rich complexity and an a warm Greek dolmas swimming in butter.

To Mary Anderson's suggestion—thank you! I'm not adverse to this recipe but am all "cooked out" after Christmas gatherings with fun but significant dinners each night, so my plan for the cabbage rolls was just too muh for New Year's Day. It modified well into roasted cabbage steaks topped with the walnut/onion mixture. Another time for this one.

We found this recipe fun and easy to make, although it does take some time. Good for a rainy day. The cabbage leaves came off easily without coring the cabbage first, and we just blanched them for a couple of minutes. The leaves were very pliable for rolling and didn't break at all. The savoy cabbage with the tasty filling and toppings made for a completely new,  unique, and interesting taste. Great texture with the savoy cabbage filled with walnuts and pine nuts. Had to have seconds. Delicious!
Pleased with myself: I realized that draping the cabbage leaves over the remaining whole cabbage means the central rib sticks up, making it a lot easier to shave down.
Because of Pine Mouth Syndrome I don't use pine nuts but otherwise the recipe looks great.  Has anyone tired just omitting the pine nuts?

title: One-Pan Paprika Chicken With Lentils, Squash and Daqa Recipe
author: Yotam Ottolenghi

comments.commentBody: This is fantastic. Best thing I’ve made in a while. I reduced the heat to 400 rather than 350 (in my experience Ottolenghi recipes tend to need more heat or more time than recommended), and next time I’ll omit the cup of water (lentils were soup rather than side), but the whole family devoured it all.

I concur on omitting the extra cup of water added with the lentils.  Didn't seem like it needed it, it had too much liquid and I had to serve with a slotted spoon.  I'd say either omit the cup of water or add more lentils (+1/2 cup?) since the lentil to onion ratio seemed to be heavy on the onion.

This was delicious! My partner said “amazingly good”! It feels like an underrated recipe. I made with bone-out chicken thighs so the cook time was obviously faster. I also used delicata squash and a sweet potato, which were the root veggies/squash I had on hand and it seemed perfectly delicious.

Do not use Pyrex! My 9x13 pyrex pan exploded into a million pieces when I poured in the chicken stock. Despite this debacle and the resulting clean-up, I managed to salvage about half the onions and finished it in a metal pan. Turned out delicious, lentils and squash were perfectly cooked and chicken skin was browned and crispy. I think I knew 450 was too hot for Pyrex but I forgot - don't make my mistake...
Uh oh.  Mine is cooking in pyrex at this very moment.
@Diana PYREX, (all caps,) is safe. Pyrex is not.

This is absolutely delicious. To avoid the “soupiness” in the lentils I used a combination of split peas and beluga lentils for texture. The peas broke down and absorbed all of the deliciousness into a thick paste which made the lentil part of the dish outstanding. 

Also, come on now NYTimes recipe editors, would it really be so hard to include the measurements in the last step so I don’t have to go back and do subtraction to figure out how much garlic and cumin seeds to put in the daqa?
Haha, agree with the measurements.

All the comments about the daqa remind me how important it is to read a recipe through carefully. I confess that I,all too often, find that I have missed an important ingredient, or step, because of careless reading!
Has anyone tried this with tempeh or tofu?  Love the spices, lentils, squash but we are vegetarian….

In the UK we use a deeper pan than a half sheet for “tray bakes” which can be savory like this or sweet. I use the roasting tray that came with my oven, deeper than a sheet pan so holds the liquid but shallow enough to get a good crispy skin on the chicken

It's useful to note that Kabocha squash is very similar to Buttercup squash (as distinct from Butternut, totally different). Kabocha is like Buttercup without the characteristic cup on the bottom of the Buttercups. In my experience you'd be hard pressed to tell the difference after they are cooked. Totally interchangeable, and in some areas old fashioned Buttercups may be more available.

I made this with tofu instead of chicken, and it turned out fantastic! I marinated the tofu a bit first in a spray of oil, some soy sauce, and some paprika and cumin. I also omitted the seasoned sour cream (though I did sprinkle on some dill at the end). Otherwise I pretty much followed the recipe to the letter, except I only used half of the daqa. The flavors are incredible, thanks for this recipe! With tofu and no sour cream, it's a fabulous, well-rounded vegan meal.
Could this be done with tofu? Or any other plant based protein?
Why not?
The Brits call a sheet pan a baking tray.  We make sheet pan dinners and they make tray bakes. We speak a similar, but different language!
The interesting thing is that the photos appear to show a baking dish, not a sheet pan.  Was something lost in translation?
A sheet pan is much too shallow to hold the liquid for this recipe. A baking tray is more like a (shallow) casserole dish.

I’m in France & not in my own home.  That meant there was no problem getting the squash (farmers market) or the French green lentils. For the daqa I used the balsamic vinegar we had in hand & therefore did not use sugar at all. We dribbled a bit it on the dish and placed the rest on the table for people to take more if they wanted it . I used the full amount of liquid &  most of it was absorbed by the lentils. No slotted spoon needed!  We found the dish flavorful & nuanced —a keeper!

I made this tonight and it was absolutely superb! I read the comments and made the following changes:
- I added Berbere seasoning to the cut up squash and chicken in the bowl
- For the first part, when you roast the onion and garlic I kept the onion slices thick and sliced the garlic instead of mincing it- this was to prevent it from burning
- When adding the broth and lentils I added 2 cups of broth but didn’t add a cup of water- the amount of liquid was perfect
- After turning the temp down to 350 F I cooked the dish for 25-30 min instead of 40 min- if id done the full time it would have been completely overcooked - when I checked the chicken with a meat thermometer it was well over 175 F
- I used Greek yogurt instead of sour cream and it was great- I also added fresh cilantro to the yogurt because I love it (but I know it’s divisive)
- I made a pot of couscous using another cup of broth and a pinch of turmeric and served everything in top of the couscous

End result- heaven in a meal!

Unlike the commenter below I made this in a large oval glass dish with no problems. It did not explode or shatter because I added HOT broth, not cold, to the dish. These are just common sense measures for using glass cookware.

Quick (for a Yotam recipe) and absolutely delicious meal.  Thanks to the previous posters about leaving out the additional water, just using the chicken stock and increasing the temp on the final stage from 350 to 400.  I used black lentils instead of green and red onions instead of yellow.  Turned out lovely...

Delicious flavors, but, as everybody has noted, too soupy. Will make again, except I’ll delete the water, bump up the lentils, maybe use larger lentils. 

BTW I heated the stock to about 175 degrees before pouring it into the hot Pyrex baking dish.

I made a vegetarian version of this last night that was fantastic! Instead of chicken I added a sweet potato (halved and sliced) with three delicata squash, two tablespoons of golden raisins and some left over spiced chick peas I had in the fridge. Used veggie stock instead of chicken stock and threw a bunch of spinach in after taking it out of the oven. This was a delicious and substantial dish for anyone that eats plant based!

What a disappointment this was. Lentils were swimming in soup, chicken was way overcooked (even though I pulled it early). I will choke it down because I hate food waste, but really, really unhappy with this recipe.

Substitutions made as I was too tired to go to store. Potatoes were used instead of squash; smoked paprika was on hand; a lentil trio of red spit, green, and black lentils completed fish.  Dish was delicious! Husband had seconds!
Any ideas about omitting or substituting the lentils? Leave them out and omit the stock and water?
Yes, I’ve done other (sumilar) Ottolenghi sheet pan chicken recipes with other veg & no lentils. The oil & spices should keep it moist esp if you use boneless thighs (less cooking time).
I am confused - I don't think of a baking dish as a baking tray - what do you cook this on/in? Or is it just too early for me to be thinking about dinner../
Use a sheet pan
The recipe calls for a "9x13 baking dish," not a baking tray or sheet pan. There is too much liquid for a sheet pan - you need something with a bit of depth here.
Any tips for using breast meat?  I dont like thighs

quote from top of page: "This oven bake requires minimal effort and is very much a meal in itself. Daqa is a vinegar-based condiment, most commonly used when making the much-loved Egyptian koshari, a hearty dish of rice, pasta and lentils. Daqa is a nifty way to brighten stews, braises and anything that needs an acidic lift. Feel free to swap out the kabocha squash for root vegetables, such as sweet potato or celery root, and serve the whole thing with nothing more than a leafy green salad"

I used bone-in chicken breast and removed early and continued to cook onion/veggie mix as to not overcook the chicken. In addition to a smaller delicata squash, I added a few baby potatoes to the mix. Was a delicious meal and easy to boot!

I should have known better than to listen to comments before cooking from the master himself. I do not agree with omitting the one cup of water. I listened to comments and did omit water originally and found the lentils to be crunchy. I had to take the beautifully cooked chicken and squash out and add the cup of water to then fully cook lentils. 

That being said, this recipe was DELICIOUS! I could eat it weekly. It was very easy to prepare and so flavorful. Highly highly recommend.

How do I prepare a day in advance and serve?
1. Bake for the entire recommended period, refrigerate, bring to room temperature and warm?
2. Bake for less time, refrigerate, and then bake again for the balance time?  (how about salmonella?)
3. Other suggestions?

TIA
I think you could prepare -- but not cook -- absolutely everything.  Then the next day you have 70 minutes of cooking, but no work.

Excellent and easy main dish! 8 thighs, 8 kabocha wedges, 1.5 cups lentils, 3 cup broth (no water), proportionately more onions, garlic and paprika. Fit nicely in enamel cast-iron paella pan. Daqa with roasted garlic and skyr (Icelandic yoghurt) with lots of dill. Marinated the raw chicken in paprika for a few hours and separately (!) from veggies.
Normally I love YO's recipes, but I thought this was good, not great. Certainly easy, but sort of one-note flavors, even with the daqa. Thank goodness for the dill sauce.
What are pickled dill leaves?
"picked" (not pickled) -- maybe they just look prettier than "chopped," for sprinkling on at the end?

The extra water makes more steam to prevent chicken from being dry is my guess. I used 1/4 cup of water here and seared my chicken in cast iron and added to the oven 25 min before done. Worked awesome. I also used my own homemade stock which is like quadruple the chickenieness of store bought stock. Served with Arugula

Ottolenghi recipes always turn out great, but they require a second and third reading beforehand to catch all the hidden ingredients and how the cumulative ingredients list is actually broken down in the application/technique section of the recipe. Beware!

Noted that others recommended foregoing the water, which I did.   Used sweet potatoes in lieu of the squash which I could not find in my local grocery store.  Also used yogurt instead of the sour cream.  Incredibly delicious and will make again.  Thank you, Yotam!

Great recipe. Somewhat laborious but worth it. The lentil and chicken compatibility yields moist chicken and slightly more cooked than al dente, just right lentils. I opted for basil and mint instead of chives and dill, with the sour cream. Highly recommend!
I swapped out the green for red lentils and used three chicken legs. I thought it was absolutely AMAZING. I'm honestly surprised it doesn't have 5 stars, so maybe these changes are needed.

Yes, even though it says tray bake, it's baked in a casserole dish I misread that as well. I concur that the one cup of water is not needed. There  was a bit of liquid left over after cooking and with the addition of the daka before serving the liquid ratio was perfect. We made this with boneless skinless chicken thighs as Ottolenghi loves full fat everything. I could stuff eight thighs in the dish since they're smaller along with the squash with no problem.

Wanted to love this but the kabocha squash came out very dry, there was way too much liquid, and the flavor was a little "one note" - could barely taste the herbs that were mixed in with the sour cream, for example. On the plus side, the leftover lentils were delicious the next day.
This is tasty with complex flavors.  Needs more liquid.  The squash tends to be dry.
I didn't love this -  the squash seemed hard and dry.

Not a fan of this recipe at all. First of all, two cups of broth and a cup of water is easily twice the amount of liquid you need. The end result was a watery, oily mess. Beyond that, the flavors just don't come together - not enough spice, punch or heat in the dish. Adding the daqa just made the dish even more watery without enhancing the flavors. Topping with the sour cream and herb mix meant all you could then taste was sour cream, chives and dill. This recipe gets a definite no from me!

This is a new favorite recipe in my household. We loved it! I followed the recipe as written, except I used a half cup of water instead of a full cup. The lentils were perfect, not soupy at all. Thank you for the great recipe.

This is outstanding! Made exactly as the recipe states except following advice of other cooks, omitted the extra cup of water when adding the stock. I did use ground cumin since I didn’t have cumin seeds. Wonderful! I am so glad I discovered this dish.

Fantastic.  I really loved the daqa, a new experience for me.  Like many other reviewers, this was a soup for us.  Next time, I won't use water, and maybe even cut down on the broth.  Still, fantastic flavor, I'll be making this dish again!
I didn't care for the squash - it was kind of chalky... BUT the flavor of the lentils was amazing.  Also, I omitted the water and it was perfect.
Good but not a repeat.

Excellent meal! I too found it to have too much liquid, so in the future, I would not add the additional cup of water. I used boneless skinless chicken thighs, so I reduced the cooking time to 15 minutes at 450 and 20 minutes at 350 (until the internal temerature of the thights reached 165 degrees F). I accompanied the dish with roasted broccoli which made the perfect compliment (both in color, texture and taste). I can't wait to serve this meal at my next dinner party.
Stir the squash halfway through cooking

Cooked twice already! It is a super delicious meal for all the family ! And love the fact that there is no need to use much cookware except for a baking dish and a chopping board, and of course plates to serve. I followed some tips from the comments section - the need to exclude the additional cup of water which I also suggest. 
I once used boneless chicken thighs and the second time skinless drumsticks - both turned out perfect! Also, I substitute sour cream with low fat Greek yogurt.
This recipe is fantastic! I followed exactly except for sour cream as I was out, so used 2% plain yogurt. Also messed up and used all the ground cumin in the chicken, but it worked great.

Have to disagree about omitting the extra water. I've tried it twice that way and the lentils came out dryer and gritty. It is a little soupy with the extra water but it's easy enough to strain when serving with a slotted spoon. As is, it's one of my favorite recipes on here.

I used air-chilled thighs and cut back the water by half. It was wet, but not soupy, and the skin crisped up nicely. I didn’t have squash, but subbed garnet sweet potatoes cut in 1 1/2 inch chunks. Came out great! I did tend to be heavy-handed with the garlic, paprika, and cumin, adding a bit more than the recipe.  Overall this is a keeper, and so easy.
Two cups of stock is sufficient.
Wow, just delicious. Used boneless, skinless chicken and it was great. Used smoked paprika cuz was what I had. Also great. Will definitely make again. Soon.

Followed what others recommended and this dish was a definite home run.

Omitted the extra cup of water in the lentils.
Cooked everything at 400 instead of 450 until the directions say to lower the heat to 350. Used acorn squash since that’s all I could find in the store.

Upped the spices. Almost didn’t do the sour cream but so glad I did!

Next time, I think I’ll try with beluga lentils to get a little more crunch. This dish is still amazing great even if the lentils are soft.

I was eager to make this dish; it sounded so good. I followed the recipe exactly and the final product looked beautiful. But it tasted just awful. Everyone at the table hated it and I wound up tossing most of it in the trash.
Crazy delicious!  Used only 3\4 cup water and that was perfect.  Used buttercup squash and sweet potato both.  Sweet potato great for bright colour. Liked the combo.  Will make again & again.

Delicious! Made with lots of mods since I didn’t want to go to the store. Used butternut squash, green lentils, za’atar instead of cumin. I used 2.5 cups of liquid rather than 3 and it was still a little soupy but not terrible. I’d do 2 cups if I was starting again. The daqa and sour cream toppings are a must. A little chili flake didn’t hurt either.
One of my family's favorite dinners, the flavors and textures are awesome!

I’ve made this 3 times now for different assortments of family and each time it’s been a great success! I serve the daqa on the side for adding to taste, and I omit the extra cup of water when adding the lentils. This is so delicious; while it is some work up front to get all the various components assembled — and keep track of how much of each ingredient goes where — once it is all put together and in the oven it is effortless. I recommend setting up the sour cream+ herbs and the daqa at the start, so they can steep while you’re preparing everything else.

Delightful!!! The eliminating the 1 cup of water was the right thing to do. I used whole lentils since that’s what I had. Worked out perfectly. Lovely combination of flavors. I will be making again for sure
What is daqa? No reference in recipe or article.
It's something you make, as described toward the end of the directions. Easy.
The daqa is the condiment made in step 5 from the vinegar, water, cumin, garlic, sugar. I toast my cumin seeds and pulverize them in a mortar a pestle. Liquify in blender.
Please reread Step 5 in the directions. It's there for you.
Ok.  Just saw the daqa bit in the recipe.
Same, I was confused at first. This looks like a must make recipe
Takes some work to understand that you are making the daqa from the vinegar.
This looks/sounds so yummy! I would only have to sub something for the lentils. My husband really doesn't like them!! But I'll try this on a cold day down here in Florida!!
I just don’t like squash. Could I substitute, sweet potato or small roasting potatoes?
Yup! From the notes: "Feel free to swap out the kabocha squash for root vegetables, such as sweet potato or celery root"
Yes - I’ve made other Ottolenghi sheet pan chicken recipes with carrots, potatoes, sweet potatoes, many kinds of squash - all work well. Even broccoli & cauliflower florets.
Also mini-peppers and cherry tomatoes.
To make this recipe kosher, I suppose the sour cream can just be omitted. Though I wonder what could replace it?  (I don't think I'd bother with vegan/pareve/fake sour cream.)
Culina plain yogurt might work.
I substitute Greek yogurt for sour cream.
Yogurt might work for function and flavor, but it's still dairy, so won't help with the kosher aspect.
Has anyone used red or yellow lentils?

from the article accompanying this recipe: Although I could have used a wider, flatter variety of lentil (a split red lentil, for example, which would collapse, or a brown or green lentil, which would soften quickly), the choice for a smaller, rounder, firmer French Puy lentil, with its much finer texture, was deliberate. At the same time as absorbing the flavor of the stock and spices they’re steeped in, they’ll still keep and hold their shape when cooked. The result is wonderfully unmushy.

Yeah I did it tonight with red lentils, sweet potato, celery, scallions, and baby carrots instead of onions and squash because it’s what I had. Came out great. Can’t wait to try it with the proper ingredients.
Can you make this in advance and freeze it and make the condiment when serving?
Any tips for substituting the lentils?
I'm assuming the lentils are cooked?

Recipe says:
8 ounces/1 heaping cup "(uncooked)" French green lentils, rinsed

Uncooked lentils are added from their bag to the broth. The lentils cook in the oven with the other ingredients. Please do find/order  the French green lentils (I get mine from Amazon). They hold up well in any baked dish.
Is there a good substitute for kabocha squash? I don't think that we have that available in our region.

Description reads:
"Feel free to swap out the kabocha squash for root vegetables, such as sweet potato or celery root"

All good but I hate hate hate cumin!!! 
My friend who shared the meal hates dill.  So I used parsley instead of dill, and a little coriander and cardamon instead of cumin.
Cool thing is you can save the leftover liquid and stuff and make a variation on eggs in purgatory
Delicious but way too much liquid. I would omit the cup of water next time.

Really really excellent!
As others suggested, reduced the liquid by 1 cup, added an extra 1/2 cup of lentils and took the oven down to 400 instead of 350 for the last 40 mins.
Don't skip the daqa at the end!
It is excellent! I had butternut squash so I added it later.
Really good!  Took the tweaking advice from folks, and wow, is this good! Real good food!

Easy and Delicious! A big hit with my 18 year old. The individual textures and flavors complemented each other beautifully. I used wedges of sweet potato instead of squash. I only had a cup of stock so I used two cups of water. When I make this again I will reduce the liquid to 2 cups of stock or water. The timing and temperature in my oven were perfect. When it finished cooking I lifted the chicken off and stirred in the daqa and put the chicken back on to serve it. Highly recommend.

This was delicious! My tweaks- added 5 mins each to each diff cooking time. Used Acorn squash instead of Kabucha squash (couldn’t find at 3 grocery stores) and used 2 med Vidalia onions. I also omitted the cup of water. It really was so flavorful and beautiful!
So good. Made it exactly as written except added sweet potato instead of squash. The flavors were perfect.
@MS I would just make this without the chicken. The squash and lentils would be more than enough for vegetarian
Has anyone used beluga black lentils? I have them in the cabinet

Just cooked this afternoon/evening today. Needs more time/higher temperature. Followed the recipe mostly but used delicata squash and sweet potato and one leftover zucchini I had on hand. Kept the oven at 400 for the 40 minutes and the sweet potato was still a little too crunchy for our liking. It’s now back in the oven (we just finished dinner) at 375 for another 25 so the leftovers will be better. It’s pretty tasty and the sour cream (we used Greek yogurt) is a must!

Fabulous. The chicken was browning after 20 minutes so I switched to a conversion setting for a few minutes and that did the trick. I kept the convection going and dropped it 325 - it was easily done in another 20 minutes (instead of 40. The lentils and squash were so good - I’d even make it without the chicken.

Yum. Just yum.
Hello - would anyone know the Australian version of squash?  I’m wondering if it’s butternut pumpkin. Many thanks

Kabocha is sometimes called Japanese pumpkin. It's round, green and sweeter than a butternut pumpkin. That being said, a butternut pumpkin would work fine in this recipe. I'm using a delicata squash, because that's what I currently have in my pantry

Wow, Ottolenghi knows what he’s doing! XD 

Used Japanese sweet potato (THE RIGHT CALL) sted squash; scallion sted chives; a drop of honey sted sugar; and Kite Hill sour cream (for lactose intolerance). The last was the only real disappointment, as it melted quickly; might go for a thicker alternative next time. Might also up the daqa or sprinkle lemon juice at the end, as the lentils/squash needed a bit more acid — but this might be the fault of the sour cream alt as well.

Followed the recipe closely but agree that 3 cups of liquid result in soupy results. Maybe to keep the chicken so moist? But very easy and tasty for a week night dinner and you can use a slotted spoon when serving. I used a 9x13" lasagna pan and the contents filled the pan, almost to the brim. Very tasty - do not omit the daqa.
I agree about the liquid.  I just put in the 2 cups of chicken stock and even that was a bit too much. Love the different tastes combined, Very delicious.

Used black beluga lentils and they worked perfectly. I also omitted the extra cup of water. Used boneless thighs but followed everything else to a T. 

I pulverized the garlic and cumin in my mortar and pestle for the daqa and put the whole amount all over the dish. I think it was too much! It was weirdly bitter but I'll make this again play with the amount of sour cream and daqa.

My family ate this up in no time.  Don't be afraid to nestle two more thighs in the lentils.  I would omit the additional cup of water.  There was a lot of extra liquid, which did not deter anyone from devouring this rich dish.  I could only find smoked sweet paprika in bulk, and I think this really added to the depth of the dish.

A delicious, nourishing, and beautiful meal as if right out of a high-end restaurant. Rave reviews from dinner guests. It easy is easy to manage by prepping everything in advance and commencing the actual cooking (step 3) when guests arrived.  Dinner was on the table 1 hour later.  the proportions of water & broth to lentils worked out perfectly. The rich sour cream accompaniment and piquant daqa lifted the dish. Thank you Ottolenghi!
A stunning dish. I will definately make this again and again.  Thanks. Hillary

Everyone enjoyed this and had seconds. From the Times article, I did expect a somewhat more dramatic taste, but it was good. The French lentils were essential or it would have been pea soup because they hold their shape. I used acorn squash and cut off the rinds before cooking. I concur that there was too much liquid. I used a 7x13 pan. I wonder if a larger surface area would have helped more water boil off. Next time I will use only the chicken stock plus 1/3 cup water.

I made this in a pinch, substituting pre-cooked garbanzo beans for the lentils and halved fingerling potatoes for the squash. Worked great. I am now convinced that this method is eminently adaptable to use up fridge parts. One more caveat, I upped the garlic and cumin and didn't bother with water, went broth the whole 3 cups. Delicious and different!
So delicious! We are in an area that doesn't carry anything like the kabocha so I used a sweet potato. I will take the suggestion of omitting the extra cup of water next time - which will be soon!
If one is following the direction to add the cup of water, is there any reason for it to be water instead of another cup of chicken broth?
Any thoughts on using black "beluga" lentils and cooking at high altitude? Longer time? Precook lentils a bit? other?

Although there was a lot of reference to sheet texts and baking pans in the preamble, the actual cooking instructions calm for a 9 X 13 BAKING DISH. I understood that to mean a baking dish, so that’s what I used.
Love the recipe. Made it with butternut squash -- dish size is a bit small. I was left out with 2 tspns of paprika...mystery
? 2 Tablespoons paprika goes on the chicken and 2 teaspoons paprika goes into the pan with the onions and garlic . . .

Made this tonight, will re-make many times.  I will try freezing it- should work.  Only had Butternut, it worked very well.  Green lentels from whole foods- not sure if they were de puy, but they held together.  I concur with the hold out a cup of liquid- we had about that much extra needed slotted spoon.  Check 20 min before done, add more if needed, This is first hearty recipe I can recall with no pepper of any sort.  Added 3/4tsp Kashmiri w/out regret, some black and atsp next time for a hint
This was delicious. Made it exactly as written. No changes needed! The green French lentils seem to be essential to the liquid in the recipe.  Will make on repeat.

Yes, doesn't need the cup of water. 
Sweet potato is a good substitute for the squash
You can easily get away with regular ground cumin and regular green lentils

Had to double the recipe for a crowd.  I omitted the water as advised (thank you gourmands!).  Everything else as directed by YO.  Lentils were the slightest bit al dente which is how I like them. I used kabocha with skin on.  Its dense texture was perfect with the lentils and chicken.  I'd be worried that other root vegetables would get too soft.  This is a keeper, and a breeze.
Article describes it as a tray bake / sheet pan bake. Instructions say to use a 9x13 inch pan. Which works best if using French lentils?
With 2-3c liquid, sheet pan is unlikely to work. Recipe calls for baking dish.   Image shows baking dish.

I generally love any Ottolenghi recipe, but this was a major disappointment. In spite of following the good suggestions from others and eliminating the water, the lentils had to be lifted with a slotted spoon. The chicken tasted good, but the squash was awful. Perhaps we picked a bad squash, but I won't be making this again.

Amazing recipe. I made it mostly as written, except I ran out of paprika, so I subbed some chipotle powder, which I also ran out of, so I added some adobo sauce. It wasn't at all picante, surprisingly, but very rich and delicious. My husband said it's better than beef bourguignon. I also followed others' suggestion and left out the extra cup of water, which worked well.
I loved the dish in general, but found the kabocha to be nearly impossible to slice, and the skin, even after baking, was much too tough. Next time I will go with butternut or maybe delicata.

Beautiful dish. Thanks to others, didn’t add extra water and cooked at slightly higher temp. We thought it would have benefitted from harissa mixed in with the broth and will do that next time. Very easy

My husband and I both said “Ooh, that looks good!” when we saw it in the NYT Magazine, so we made it that night - and it was! I had to make a few substitutions (acorn squash, black lentils instead of French, ground cumin), but it was delicious, and will try it again when I can get the exact ingredients.

This is absolutely delicious. I made it in my air fryer using the roasting function and it turned out great. I also used less water and it took less cooking time in the air fryer than it would have in the oven.
Completely delicious. I dropped temp to 400 instead of 350 and next time I wouldn’t add a cup of water.

Might the letter writers who recommended addition of less water have used conventional brine-chilled chicken, rather than the seemingly more expensive air-chilled bird?  Air-chilled chicken cooks beautifully, like the home grown chicken I grew up with.

The issues: brining results in the meat taking up fluid (a cheap way to increase the sale weight of the bird), in addition, the skin thickens, and the salts used in the brine aren't good for you. The released brine can poach the meat into mush.
Less water, more paprika and cumin
Absolutely delicious!!!

Actually a tray bake - here in Ireland at least - is traditionally a cake cooked in a baking tray (e.g. google Mary Berry lemon drizzle traybake). But we’re learning fast and I love sheet bakes, one pan dishes etc. This one looks amazing and will try it this week. Can’t go wrong with Ottolenghi recipes :)

The flavors were great, but like several others commented, there was too much liquid. The lentil/onion/squash mixture was soupy and overcooked after so much time in the oven. I think I would use less liquid (omit water) and maybe use a larger or firmer lentil or other bean. Maybe a higher heat and shorter overall cooking time.

This was fabulous. I made it with a small whole chicken (flipped the bird twice and took the same amount of time). Also served it with roasted asparagus as a side dish to add a bit of green to the plate. The spices and flavorings were perfect.

Delicious as written. I had no issues with the lentils absorbing all of the liquid, 3C seemed perfect. Overall cook time was spot on, although I let the skins brown a few extra minutes at 450. When I make this again I’ll consider adding some cherry tomatoes (as suggested by another commenter) and a little heat (just because I love it). Loved the simplicity and the ease if clean up. Easy to start toward the end of a WFH day and turn into a weeknight dinner.

This was very good.  It needed to cook longer at 450 to get a nice brown skin, then I lowered to 400 for the remainder as others have suggested.  I made a 1/2 recipe and found that the liquid started to dry up, so I added a bit of additional stock now and then to keep things moist.  (If you have the time to make homemade stock it can really help a dish like this.)  Overall a very flavorful and satisfying meal and pretty easy overall.  I will make it again.
I don't understand the "sheet pan" bit (from the source article).  Seems it cooks in a 9x13 baking dish?
Very glad that I followed advice from the community and omitted the water. Turned out great.

One of the best new recipes I’ve made in ages. Used a large acorn squash, because that is what I had. Flavorful, different, healthy, and delicious. The only thing wrong was we didn’t have leftovers because everyone went back for seconds. Thanks
So good.  Who doesn't love a good tray bake?!  I always make extra sauce so doubled the daqa and toasted a few more cumin seeds.

I completely agree with other recommendations to omit the 1 cup water.  I left out the water and added 1 1/2 cups lentils and thought the results were exactly how we like them.   Not watery, perfectly cooked for sides.  
I substituted plain greek yogurt for my family and it was fine.  Personally, I'd prefer sour cream.

The crispy chicken skin and moist meat on the creamy vegetables is wonderful. I had 12oz of lentils and used them all. There was just the right amount of liquid to cook them and all the veggies through (I also added 2 chunked carrots and a diced celery stalk) Quick and yummy. Easy to double for a crowd.

Made this with cauliflower in place of the squash. Really enjoyed this but I’d definitely try making it with a different vegetable in the future. Think the sweet and sour vibe of squash and the daqa would be great!

I made this with smoked paprika and it was tasty. Like others, I skipped the water but I did add about a half a cup of additional broth. I used yogurt and mint b/c that was what I had on hand. Leftovers were just as tasty! Next time I may add cauliflower and skip the meat.

The lentils were great. I didn’t add the water as suggested, but the chicken was uninteresting.  Perhaps if I’d marinated it longer, it would be better
  
Any suggestions?

This was an excellent recipe, extremely flavourful!  I did sub sweet potatoes for the squash and left out the cup of water (using just the two cups of chicken stock).  There was plenty of liquid with that omission.  Everything cooked beautifully and had a rich, intense flavour.  Will add this to my regular repertoire.

Delicious. Used a combination of delicate and honey nut squash. Omitted the cup of water and was not disappointed. Still very saucy, but not too soupy. Fantastic right out of the oven, but darn good the next day. Only had dill for the yogurt - will make again when chives are back in the garden. Used a baking dish similar to the one pictured. A definite do over!

I made this with 2.5 lb boneless, skinless thighs and had way too much for my 9x13 baking pan!  I put 1/2 the chicken and 1/2 the squash in the pan with the given amounts of onions, lentils, chicken broth, etc.  Then made a whole second baking pan with more onions, lentils, etc.  It was a big hit. I used the given baking time. It came out great!  Good to know from others that it also works with other root vegetables.  If you do all the prep work early in the day, it's a cinch to throw together.

This is fantastic!  I didn’t have lentils, so used a combo of wild rice, quinoa, and split peas.  Amount of liquid was perfect for my 9x13” pan.  The sour cream and accompanying vinegar “sauce” was divine!

Per the recommendations to reduce the water, I added an extra 1/2 cup lentils (finishing off the bag). Worked perfectly with the 2 cups broth 1 cup water.

My husband doesn't like squash, so I used sweet, purple & white potatoes- tasted great!

I also kept it at 450 longer to crisp the chicken - about 10 minutes.

loved the flavor- like with so many of the NYT Food recipes, I upped the spices, though.

Definitely will make it again. Grateful for the other cooks' comments!

This was amazing! The cup of water as other reviews mentioned was no issue for me, it completely evaporated. I would season the chicken a little more next time. This is a perfect dinner party recipe and can’t recommend enough.
As others have said, too much liquid. It tasted delicious but we ate it in bowls with spoons. Sigh.

This was delicious! I took the suggestion of others and omitted the 1 C of water. I used delicata squash, boneless and skinless thighs and added .5 t of ginger and 2 T golden raisins. I stirred in some baby spinach at the end (it ads color and I like the extra greens!) Instead of sour cream I used non-fat greek yogurt and topped with fresh basil instead of using dill. Can omit chicken for vegetarians too! Will put this on our regular rotation!

Unbeknownst to me I had the counter top oven I was using on the Broil setting. Couldn’t understand why everything was cooking so fast . 
Once I realized I quickly changed the dial to Bake …. And I think I salvaged it all. I took the chicken and squash out and continued to cook the lentils …. As they weren’t done enough,  added some more water and 15 mins later … voila! Delicious !
Don’t add any water. Added only 1/4 cup and the dish came out soupy.
Flavor was good but even though I took the chicken out twenty minutes early it was WAY overcooked.

This is by far the worst meal I've ever made. We choked down two pieces of chicken and threw the rest in the garbage. I have never before thrown food away, and it hurt to do so, but this was simply inedible. The amount of liquid -- 2 cups of broth plus one cup of water -- is ridiculously too much. It came out as a soup. The squash was like pabulum, the chicken was tough, with skin that was slightly brown on the outside but rubbery on the inside. It took a steak knife to cut it. Disgraceful.

You likely did not let your chicken come to room temperature before putting in the hot oven, nor did you adequately dry the skin beforehand. The texture you describe is textbook for cold, wet meat in a hot oven. The squash slices would not be overly soft if you cut them to the recommended size (6 wedges per kabocha).

I was not impressed with this recipe. It seemed bland. 
The colors were interesting: the red paprika and the white sour cream topping. The variety of textures was unusual but this did not make up for the bland flavor. 

(I did leave out the extra cup of water and the quantity of fluid seemed to work well.)
Made this with delicata squash and loved it.  Be sure to leave out the cup of water.  I had to pour it all off at the end.

The only way you are going to get crispy chicken skin like what is pictured to sear them skin down in a heavy-bottomed pan, like cast iron, under weight (dutch oven, chef press). Of course this makes it not a one-pan dish, but if you want truly crispy chicken skin, that's what you need to do. It needs more acid or more salt, but I used unsalted broth as I am trying to cut down on my sodium. I'll add more daqa to the next serving.  Definitely don't need that extra cup of water.

I made half this recipe this evening and it was a hit!  Slight substitution of butternut for the kabocha squash since that's the only kind available in our neighbourhood. The water was definitely needed--the sauce cooked into the lentils, which were amazing.
Once again, omit the extra cup of water. But still, this is delicious! My partner who hates lentils had two servings. :)
Has anyone used red or yellow lentils?
I’m about to make this recipe with one exception, we keep kosher so I’m debating using plant based yogurt or sour cream. Any suggestions?

Absolutely loved this recipe! I agree with other commenters that a.) the 2 cups of broth are perfect for lentils, don't add the 1 cup of water and b.) turning the temperature down to 400 for the 40 minutes instead of 350 was a good decision. I also used butternut squash with great success. I was thrilled about the flavor imparted by the daqa, dont skip it!
tasty as it is. added a good bit of salt

I was making this from the actual magazine -- where "daqa" is never explained: glad it is here!  Only checked online once it was in the oven with seemingly way too much liquid: should have read here first!  Thanks for the advice to turn it down only to 400 not 350. The chicken & squash were cooked perfectly!  Lentils were soupy but easy enough to drain off. Wonderful dish & i will def make again. 6 thighs were only 1.75 lbs not 2.25 though.
tasty as is. salted chicken heavily

Great recipe: the flavour hit from the daqa is special. May reduce the water by half, but did need to use baking dish as indicated: my oven pan  was too shallow and crowded, causing steaming rather that crisping.

Haven't made an Ottolenghi recipe for a long time - why?  So delicious.  I omitted the water which was a good call.  I would add another half cup of lentils as well.  Made in a large ceramic baker/roasting dish which left plenty of room.
Absolutely delish! And of course, the next day the leftovers taste even better.

We loved the flavors of this recipe, but you will definitely want to tweak proportions of stock, water and lentils depending on how "thick" you want the dish to be. We omitted the cup of water and used just two cups of homemade stock and it was still more soupy.  Next time we may reduce stock or add more lentils and/or serve over a grain.  We used potatoes and acorn squash for our root vegetables and it tasted great.
my lentils floated above the broth&water and didn’t fully cook. Had to separate the cooked chicken, add broth, cover it all with foil and cook longer. :-/

excellent - but lentils needed a bit more time - I ended up taking out the squash and the chicken and then adding more water to cook the lentils but result was great. Other note - my chicken broth was too salty - this recipe has a lot of flavor in itself...

Very tasty but also had too much liquid and I would skip the water next time as suggested by other several reviewers.  The smallest kabocha squash I could fine was 4 1/2 lbs and the skin was very thick and did not soften.  I will try with delicata rounds next time.

This was outstanding!  Quick and easy and largely hands off once you prepped. I don’t think the extra cup of water was necessary- unless you’re making ahead.  The leftovers did absorb more liquid, but the initial servings were more liquid-y than necessary. Delicious, fantastic weeknight dinner but impressive enough for a dinner party.

Fabulous. My oven is out, so used my trusty creuset cast iron dutch oven and translated to cooktop, for one pot meal. Browned chicken first, then squash, then recipe same. Agree that liquid can be reduced.

Fabulous!   I used a large acorn squash;  could have even been more!  Also 0.7 lbs lentils without increasing the liquid per other comments.  Cooked in a larger roasting pan as my chicken thighs were HUGE.  will make this again!
I didn’t need the water, just broth was enough liquid.

Really delicious and comes together so nicely. Like others, I did not add the 1c. water and the lentil consistency was perfect. Bone-in chicken thighs cooked in about 1/2 the time specified. Used a mix of thighs and legs for variety. The daqa and sour cream were delicious. Will make again.
This is sooooo good!

Super delicious! I couldn’t find kabocha so used delicata which was yummy. Used labne in place of sour cream. Agree that you need a deeper dish because what you put in the oven is quite liquid-y, but mine cooked down really well. I wouldn’t call this an easy recipe but it was simpler than other Ottolenghi recipes I’ve tried.
Definitely do not add additional water. Made with butternut squash
Amazing! Made it second time with boneless skinless thighs and still great. Subbed squash both times: once butternut and second time delicata.

This is a keeper. I had Baharat spice blend to augment the paprika. which worked great. Unfortunately, the kabocha was dry and tasted of cardboard - maybe something wrong with my squash? Next time I'll try to nestle the wedges under the chicken and in the broth, rather than on top. Definitely will be a next time for this one.
PS Also used acorn squash. Excellent.

I followed this recipe exactly as written. The final product was beautiful. But it tasted just awful; everyone at the table hated it. I wound up tossing most of it in the garbage. A huge disappointment.

Agree that 2c stock   1c water is way too much liquid. I omitted the water and it was still rather soupy (even though 2:1 liquid:lentils were the instructions on the lentil package I used.) Still had good flavor, and I will probably make again with some tweaks!
Thank you for the notes. I added 1/2 cup water instead of 1. Other than that I followed the recipe, using sweet potatoes, as that is what I had on hand. Outstanding and a cinch to prepare.

Made this today using sweet potatoes instead of squash. Used 2 cups of stock, no water, and made sure the lentils were submerged in broth under the chicken and sweet potatoes. Halfway through, basted everything because it was looking much drier in the pan than it did in the photos used with the recipe (good move).  Will probably make it again!
Perfect from the start.
For those folks who found this soupy, did you use canned or dried lentils? Thanks.

As mentioned, not the best editing of a recipe. I skipped the extra cup of water and this was still very soupy, needed to be served in a bowl. Also needed my extra deep casserole dish to cook. Used boneless thighs, delicata, sweet potato, and carrots and baked at 425 for 45 minutes. All cooked perfectly except carrots, they still had crunch. Didn't love it, but husband had 3 helpings so someone liked it.
Some very strange flavor combinations in this dish.
This was amazing. I followed the recipe but measured with my heart rather than using exact measurements.
4 star. Solid but not a knockout.

Incredible recipe. This was so delicious!!!

I omitted the extra cup of water, and used sweet potatoes that I had on hand instead. I could cry it was so delicious and the best tasting lentils I've ever had.
This was absolutely divine! Easy to modify however you need, but the kabocha squash is just incredible with the seasoning. I used chicken bone broth for additional richness.

My husband and I agreed this was meh. The lentils and squash were overcooked and the onions were stringy, although I cooked it for 7 minutes less than 40 minutes at the end. Too much water left in the pan, necessitating the slotted spoon. I was testing it for a company dinner and I'm glad I did - I would have been embarrassed to serve it.  The daqa and the sour cream were nice additions, but didn't save the dish.

Loved this! I omitted the cup of water based on other people's notes. The lentils were a little undercooked when I pulled it out of the oven after the recommended cooking time.  I covered it and put it back in for another 15 minutes. Delicious!

Easy & delicious. The dill yogurt is essential, although I didn’t bother with the daqa and just sprinkled apple cider vinegar over the top when out of the oven. Only had 2 chicken thighs, so augmented with a few turnips in wedges and extra squash.
Yum! Followed other recipe notes and used 1 c less liquid. Boosted garlic and cumin a bit in lentils. Daqa was awesome! Used plain whole milk yogurt with lemon vs sour cream, worked great.

Used butternut squash and yams. Used vegetable broth and decreased by 1/2 cup.  Used 1.75 lbs chicken (3 large thighs). After turned down heat, roasted for only 30 minutes in my Breville countertop oven. The chicken was 207 degrees (needed to be only 165 degrees) but was tender.  The lentils were perfect, soft but kept their shape.  It was delicious and I will definitely make it again.
If you live in a food desert like myself, the kabocha isn’t necessary. I think it might be better with butternut or sweet potato honestly.
Awesome! I didn’t add the extra water and used a large shallow braising pan. So many wonderful contrasts— crispy skin and soft squash, creamy herb/sour cream mixture and spiced lentils— so good!

This was a SURPRISE of a delight. The cumin isn’t overpowering, and the paprika makes it just a bit sweet. Loved it! Will make again, this time with thighs instead of tenderloins. Had to make do with what I had.

Used sweet potatoes, and it was great! Definitely agree you don't need the cup of water - may even don't need the full 2 cups of stock. Also agree with turning up the heat - needed more time than listed even at 400.

This was nice and I dug the (almost) one-pot vibe. It definitely needs tweaks, so next time I will try with less water and also blending the daqa, which did nothing for me in it's stirred state. Both were mentioned in other notes, but I didn't incorporate this time. I used sweet potatoes as my shop didn't have squash, but I love kabocha and will make with it next time I see it. Appreciate the warning from Diana about Pyrex!

Used 8 chicken thighs, and in addition to the squash, I also had some rutabaga turnip (great). Had a lot of stuff & used a big sheet pan 15x21, so everything had enough space. As a result, in contrast to others I ended up ADDING 2 cups or so water over the 2 hr baking period (all at 400F). Check every 15 minutes & add water when necessary. I pre-sautéed the onions, garlic, then the toasted and ground cumin seeds, added the lentils & transferred to the sheet pan. Amazing! What a great meal!!
Wonderful dish, it's become a family favorite in our household!

I admit I have a cold but I was pretty disappointed in this dish.  The sour cream was really bland and maybe the vinegar finish might have helped but there just wasn’t enough of it.  I was very generous with the spices, but apparently not generous enough.    I like the combination of the lentils and chicken however, so maybe I’ll try to come up with something that has a lot more zip to it.

Extremely delicious. I was a bit apprehensive with the 4 stars. Made with the following modifications: 1) omit the cup of water, 2) used veg stock because I didn't have chicken stock, and 3) added some maldon salt / red pepper flakes. Heat goes nicely with the sweet/earthy flavors.
This was fantastic, I did not have lentils, and I used my summer squash on hand, still extremely tasty and delicious.  I have shared this with extended family, it’s a hit!

Made exactly to the recipe other than using boneless thighs. Came out perfect and delicious. I will probably make a second round of the sr cream and daqa - next time I make this I’ll double both right out of the gate because I love the acidic tang and bright creaminess they bring.

This was great and both my partner and I truly enjoyed it. I did make the recipe more complicated than the original. 
1. Sear the chicken before mixing with squash.
2. Sauté the onion with spices in a pan, adding salt and pepper. Then transferred to the baking sheet. 
3. Reduced the temperature to 400 F instead of 350 F. 
With the chicken on top of the stew and the skin side up, the seared skin was still crispy at the end of the roast. Truly amazing. The lentil and onion were for me the best part of the dish. We couldn’t stop eating it.

This recipe is a keeper. I’ve made it at least ten times. Agree with others about reducing the amount of liquid. Tonight I used summer’s bounty of zucchini and heirloom tomatoes instead of butternut squash. I reduced the liquid more than usual since the tomatoes yield so much liquid. It was delicious!
I used split urad dal in place of the French green lentils and highly recommend it - they held their shape, but absorbed a ton of flavour as well.

I made this.  It is good.  I thought at first the 3/4 lb squash was a typo as I have only seen these at about 4 lbs (in London you can probably find smaller).  But after slicing the one inch wedges I ended up with about 24 wedges and I only used 6, which was approx. 1 pound (and that worked). I used brown lentils which I prefer over green.  I wish I had not followed others’ advice to omit the cup or water.  They were a bit undercooked and dry.
One of the most delicious things I’ve tasted in a long time. That is all.

This was a great way to cook lentils and the spice mixture and Daqa were really nice. The squash and lentils came out fantastic. The chicken and sour cream mixture were rather one-note and didn’t really add anything. I would definitely make again with more vegetables and omitting the chicken and sour cream.
Loved the flavor combo of paprika and dill, could see this dish working with a variety of veggie combos. Will omit one cup of water next time, lentils were very soupy.

Delicious! I had boneless skinless thighs and will definitely use bone-in skin-on next time so the chicken cooks at the same pace as the lentils. I took others' advice and added only two cups of broth, but needed the third cup anyway in order for the French green lentils to be correctly softened. I wonder if those complaining of soupy lentils used brown lentils which cook quickly with less water?

Delicious! I only had skinless, boneless thighs and butternut squash, so I delayed adding the chicken until after the first 20-minute roast, turned the oven down to 400F and continued for 30 minutes. Perfect. Thank you, Yotam!

Made this last night because it was on the NYTimes 2026 calendar. The flavors were fantastic. I scanned the comments on the app before making—as I always do—and followed all the directions (omitting the extra cup of water which seemed universally recommended).
   I did go to a bigger Pyrex size, because I had 3 extra chicken thighs and my kabocha squash was large (3 lbs). I halved the squash and cut into wedges and added 1/4 cup more French du Puy lentils. This was a perfect fit for a 15 inch Pyrex. 
    As a reader noted, ADD HOT STOCK to the Pyrex so there are no temperature fluctuations (ie. glass explosions). My stock was homemade and unsalted; next time I will salt it before adding—the lentils needed it. 
   The chicken was done 10 min. early so I took them out and placed on a platter, which actually let some fat drain off. At 40 minutes the lentils weren’t tender, so added 10 minutes. (The squash was fine with the extra time.)
   The finished product was gorgeous. I’ll definitely make this again, and I’ll add even more lentils—there was still a fair amount of liquid left in the dish. 
   My only quibble is the “minimal effort” the author promises. A fair bit of shopping and chopping and watching went into the dish. But it was entirely worth every minute.

We really liked this. Changes: 20 oz of squash, whole cut-up chicken (minus the back), 2 cups of broth but no added water, reduced temp to 400 degrees, not 350. Worked very well, though others might find the chicken more cooked than they like.

I took a user suggestion from another recipe and brined the chicken ahead of time. This adds to the prep/cooking time, but turned out delicious. Also, my grocery store did not have kabocha squash, so I opted for spaghetti squash, which works just fine. I also substituted the sour cream with plain nonfat Greek yogurt - that’s our go to, healthier substitute for anything sour cream/mayo. I also found the green lentils needed an additional 10 minutes of cooking time. Very tasty recipe!

Trader Joe sells flavored chickpeas in a can, and I subbed these for the lentils; also subbed a sweet potato for the squash.  Both substitutes were excellent.  This recipe is definitely worth the effort!

if making with sweet potatoes instead of squash, then def want to follow other commenters and have the heat a little higher (reduce to 400 instead of 350) so they actually cook. or parboil. or cut smaller.
I ate it without the daqa, still great.

title: Lamb Meatball and Semolina Dumpling Soup With Collard Greens Recipe
author: Yotam Ottolenghi

comments.commentBody:
This was fantastic, made exactly as written. Complex, comfort-food, delicious. Definitely 5-star, definitely going on the rotation here.

Only nitpick I have is that step 3 should have been broken into multiple steps. I found myself almost missing bits as I moved through it and having to go back and carefully read sentence-by-sentence. Had it been written as 3-4 distinct steps it would have been easier to follow.
Substituted cooked mayocoba beans (Rancho Gordo- like cannellini) for the semolina dumpling part.  That cut off 30 minutes of time, making it a super quick and delicious meal.

This is an amazing recipe — we devoured it. Some suggestions — leave the seeds in the jalapeños, the heat goes well with the heavy lemon addition at the end. Add a splash of fish sauce into the broth with the tomato paste step. Make the dumpling dough before you do anything else. Leave it covered in the refrigerator while you prepare the soup to the step where the dumplings go in. The dumplings are delicate and I think giving them time to set up helps them stick together.

I messed up the dumplings. Used a cookie scoop and I don’t know if it was just because they were too big (I only got around 9) or if they weren’t compacted enough, or possibly it’s because I used multigrain bread, but they mostly fell apart even with extra simmer time. Still incredibly delicious. Whole fam loved it, even the collard averse teen. Will def make again.
Me too!  Dumplings fell apart!  Any idea why?

My partner and I loved this recipe. Perhaps a bit laborious for a busy weeknight but perfect when you have some time to spend in the kitchen. It’s a really simple recipe just with a few parts. I subbed 12 oz of Impossible beef for the ground lamb and it worked out really well. The spices were wonderful in the meatballs. Loved the sour broth. Loved the rich, soft dumplings. No notes! Can’t wait to make it again.
Thanks! I was looking for a cook who used Impossible. It's brilliant in albondigas, so I was pretty sure it'd work well here.

Such bright flavor, and the dumplings and lamb are delicious. I did this over 2 days, mixing meat mixture and jalapeño mixture us prep evening before, then broth and rest of steps the next day. A lot of ingredients but worth it.

Substituted Chard for Collards but Collards would have stood up to the textures and flavors better. Otherwise, I made the soup exactly as written and it was excellent - a serious soup for a cold winter night.
This is a spectacular recipe. Does not disappoint- followed it exactly and really tastes like the middle east.

Made this recipe as written and it was supremely wonderful. Recommend that cooks invest a little time up front prepping (ie measuring spices, slicing collard greens etc) so they won’t be frantically consulting the recipe.

Recipe is delicious but is too time consuming with prep, all those steps, & dirties way too many dishes & pots.  Forget using crustless white bread (any bread is fine), use frozen chopped kale, and I'm going to make as a stew with ground lamb in broth (no lamb balls) but will make those yummy light semolina dumplings.  One pot and all the flavor.

My semolina dumplings were fine--I did give a few of them a couple of extra rolls around in my hand to be sure they were compacted enough before I very genrly placed them in the broth one by one--and covered them right away...10 minutes later they were all puffy, whole, and yummy!
these are restaurant-quality delicious! Absolutely wonderful. Took me a full 2 hours to make and it was worth the time and effort. sooooo good

Followed the recipe almost exactly (used 12 ounces of lamb), it was very delicious and looked just like the picture; fed 4 at my house.  My market only had large jalapenos and I used the 2 called for, which was an error and unbalanced the flavor profile of the dish.  The recipe should say 2 small or medium jalapenos or 1 large.  My group felt the amount of lemon juice added at the end should be cut in half; the called for 1/4 cup was too much citrus brightness for the deeper flavors of the dish.

Delicious. I’ll definitely make again. I followed others’ advice and made the meatballs the night before. I also used a Dutch oven to brown the meatballs and then the same pan to cook the broth. I think it probably deepened all of the flavors. I also prepped all ingredients so when I started cooking, it was stress free.  The only change I will make next time is to make the meatballs truly bite size. Mine were a little too big to be one bite. The dumplings were so good and tender.

First of all, this is 5-star tasty. Saying that, though, I'm not sure it's worth all the effort. The 50 minute preparation time is laughable--I know the first time you make something takes longer, but three separate components with a total of 30 ingredients...it probably takes close to 50 minutes just to get the ingredients gathered and sorted! And you have to clean up as you go because of all the mess from grating, chopping, frying, and meatball- and dumpling-making before you get to the soup.
Love this! I made this last year and again yesterday. It's wonderful but time-consuming (unless you enlist a second pair of hands). Making the meatballs a day or two ahead helps.
Would this recipe work with fresh breadcrumbs made from whole wheat bread?  I don't keep white bread at home.
If you have leftovers, do you need to separate out the dumplings?

Delicious! Made exactly to the recipe. I see BDW's note about step 3 and here's what I'll add- this recipe really benefits from even a cursory mise en place. I was lucky to have time to put the meatballs together and stash them in the fridge. Waited on someone to pick up ingredients I forgot, used the time to pull together everything I could, which made the actual cooking less intimidating than the ingredients list suggests and mostly meant tossing things into a pan. A sneaky impressive meal!

Another note I'll add after running out of characters-it seems like the dumplings are hit or miss for people commenting. Somehow, on my first grab of lamb mixture, I landed on a size that resulted in 18 identically sized meatballs, so I rolled into the same size with the dumpling dough. I would describe the size of them as exactly between a ping-pong ball and a Lindt truffle, and mine puffed up beautifully and had a perfect texture cooked at the temp and for the time called for in the recipe.

Delicious — I did not add the lemon juice at the end as I found it to be sour enough. I served it with a dollop of Greek yogurt.

It took about 2 hours to prepare in a non-rushed way. I made it to bring for lunch the same day, so I made the meatballs and browned them, made the broth, and made the dumplings and formed them.  The dumplings did not puff, but they were a lovely texture. As the dumplings were cold when I put them in, I cooked them for about 12-13 minutes instead of 10.

Didn't get to finish my comment ...  The recipe says "50 Minutes"; not sure what that refers to -- maybe the time it takes to shop for the ingredients ?  Does anyone at the Times test or edit these recipes ?  This took me a bit over 3 hours, non-stop.  It takes a loooong time to pick enough cilantro leaves off the stems to make a finely-chopped 1/2 cup.  And grating an onion to get 1/4 cup of onion slurry takes a while too !  Finally, you must have all the mise en place set up for Step 3 -- thin
One does not need to pick the cilantro leaves off the stems.  The stems are flavorful.  Just chop the leaves on the stems.
Am I surprised? No. It’s like most Ottolenghi recipes. 30 ingredients. I’ll stick to my mother-in-law’s chicken soup. 6 ingredients. Add matzo balls? 3 more ingredients.

Part 3
In step 3, things happen very quickly, so you must have your mise en place all ready, and maybe even a helper to hand you the ingredients as they are required.  But it all works just the way the recipe says it will.

1.5 hours start to finish (unless you have a personal sous chef).
Fried the meatballs in the same pot in which I made the stew.
Cooked the tomato paste for about 5 minutes until brick red.
The stew was incredibly flavorful.  I got dumplings and the man got his meat.  A perfect combination.

I made this as close to the written recipe as possible - EXCEPT - I didn’t make fresh breadcrumbs. I used panko, because it was what I had and it was easier, and it turned out just fine. 

In regard to comment about person using “double” the tomato paste by using the whole 170gm can, I checked the recipe three times while scooping out tablespoon after tablespoon, and it’s basically that whole can, regardless of the 90gm listed in the recipe. 

Oh, and I used a POUND of lamb. No waste. Was good.

I almost did the same but assumed it was less than a lb for a reason; my doggies were happy I did because they feasted on the lamb not used in the one pound package. :)  They won't be so lucky next time tho!!!

This soup comes together in 50 minutes if you have your sous chef take care of all the mise en place. For us understaffed mortals, figure around 90 minutes. That said, this soup is a flavor bomb! So filling and scrumptious, full of warm spices, and the lemon lends a brightness that’s just perfect. We doubled the recipe and will happily freeze a bunch for later.

I don't think this recipe is well written. As someone else pointed out, step 3 is too long. Also wouldn't it make sense to brown the meatballs in the soup pot, remove them and then brown the vegetables etc in the same pot (pouring off oil if necessary?) instead of using a separate skillet? As written it dirties an extra pan and you loose some lamb flavor in the broth. Finally, I followed recipe exactly and dumplings disintegrated creating a porridge. Not attractive but the flavor was great.

Made it tonight for three people & they all loved it.  Followed recipe except local grocery had no collard greens, so used kale, held up very well. I tasted every step-all yummy. Only 1 person was OK with jalepeno peppers--so I chopped and fried them in oil separately and added to my heat-loving friend's portion..but by removing seeds and cores from chili & 6 minutes of frying in olive oil w/ onion and garlic, there was almost no heat left.

This was really good and homey, really hit the spot for me. I would add a bit more water or broth next time. Or simply double the recipe. It made 5 small servings. I wanted leftovers after all the work. And I’d up the lemon juice.

The meatballs were outstanding!  The dumplings not so great, tasted fine but quickly fell apart. I’m going to use the meatballs in a red lentil soup, because they’re too good to not find another place for them.
used 1lb grnd lamb. added extra water to the broth
This takes a lot of pots, pans, prep and clean-up. It's delicious but perhaps best for a night that you have about 2 hours to dedicate.

Sharing my tips for streamlining as this has become a family favorite and a soup I absolutely crave, and we do get it on the table in under an hour  1) prep all the vegetables in two bowls first (meatball and soup base) saving only collards to prep while cooking — saves time and stress as the recipe moves faster than you think and there are lots of overlapping ingredients, 2) brown the meatballs in the soup pot then let the soup base deglaze, 3) skip the dumplings, serve w crusty bread.

Absolutely delicious! Made nearly exactly as instructed, except subbed in 2 thai chilis instead of jalapeños and used only 1/2 the recommended lemon juice at the end. Didn't weigh my collards as we had a beautiful bunch for our CSA and I wanted to use it all up, but extra greens never hurt!
This is recipe to make on a winter weekend or holiday when you are not rushed. It takes time and care, but the flavors and textures are so worth the effort.  Share with friends and family!!
Semolina like Cream of Wheat semolina?

Cream of Wheat isn't semolina.  Semolina's made from durum wheat, a particularly hard type of wheat.  Cream of Wheat is made from other wheats.  You can buy semolina ground to different consistencies, but this recipe seems to want a fine grind, like you would use to make semolina pasta.  It's often available in grocery stores from a number of companies, such as Bob's Red Mill, or you can buy it online.  I get mine from Janie's Mill when I order other flours.
If you have access to an Asian market, semolina is called sooji or rava.
Typically, semolina refers to durham wheat. However, semolina is a course grind of any flour.
Delicious. My stew-wary husband gobbled it up, as did I.  It's a little complicated, but ingredients are ready to hand.
Regarding the use of whole wheat bread instead of white bread: that shouldn't be any problem at all just use bread that is not too dense so that it  makes light crumbs.
I have leftover lamb from a roast. Could the meat be ground and used for the meatballs, just browning quickly or would they be too dry?
Can leftover be frozen?

Haven't tried it, but I'd be concerned the dumplings would turn to mush if frozen. They're really delicate. I'd go with halving the recipe if you felt it made too much to eat in a day or two. 

That said, it doesn't make a ton. I cook for my husband and I, and neither of us are huge eaters. It ended up being one dinner and a lunch for each of us.
This is incredible.
Delicious. Everyone at the table loved it. Even dubious teenage boy was won over.

Wonderful especially for a cold night. Cooked exact, with the following exceptions - used pork in replacement of lamb, added about a 1/2 teaspoon of Harissa to the meat to give some heat and then air fried the meat. The full rest of the recipe was identical and it was delicious!

I totally agree that step 3 should have been broken down into 3-4 segments. There’s a lot going on. One omission was the 1/4 cup lemon juice at the end of the ingredients list for broth. I reread it many times and could not find any mention of when to add the lemon juice. I just added it to the broth last; it tasted nice with it. I haven’t finished preparation yet but think it will be ok. Lots of work!
Last sentence of step 5: "Remove the lid and gently stir in the lemon juice."

Now that I’ve made this from start to finish I’m thoroughly convinced that recipes from restaurant chefs are problematic. I found the use for the lemon juice, which you add as the last ingredient of the entire recipe. The procedure in step three gave me other pauses . After adding the 6 tablespoons of tomato paste it’s exactly the contents of 6 ounce can of paste.  For me it made the soup very dark—brown—instead of the nice reddish hue showing in NYT photo. The dumplings were leaden.
a can of tomato paste is 170g.  read the label.  you added almost 2x the amount required

Delicious and we will make again. The broth is amazing and I think we will be using that as a base for other dinners as well. 
I'm an efficient cook and this still took me 1 hr 45 min and absolutely worth the time.
This was good but I am not sure it was worth the effort.

I couldn’t find semolina and didn’t have time to make full recipe anyway.  I made the meatballs and baked for 14 minutes.  Used can of crushed tomatoes and seasoned it like the soup.  Then made penne pasta and served with meatballs and spinach.  Enjoyed the seasoning and the twist on typical pasta dish.
Excellent.  Prepared as written but 1.5x.   Used prewashed collard greens and wasn’t sure how to convert that weight from the recipe so used less as inclined to anyway.
Can the dumplings be baked ahead of time so they don’t get mushy?

absolutely terrific. i made the soup the day before except for the dumplings, which i added the day I served the soup.  my only substitution was fresh chopped basil in place of the cilantro, only because i forgot to buy the cilantro and had the basil on hand.  i am not sure, when i make this again, if I will use the  cilantro because my version was amazing.

I made this recipe vegetarian by making mushroom meatballs, but they were too delicate after frying them up to put them back in the broth. When I realized this, I kept them on the side and put them in my bowl and ladled the broth and dumplings on top. The flavors were absolutely delicious and I can imagine that with lamb meatballs, the dish would be delectably rich. I can see why this is Mr. Ottolenghi’s comfort food.
I'm going to try it using Impossible Burger meat. Hope that works!
The dumplings did not hold together.  I think that the fresh bread crumbs have too much moisture.  Otherwise, the taste was fabulous.

A fair amount of work, the first time made...and on that basis perhaps a 'company's coming' sort of soup recipe, but definitely a winner.  Due to constraints of our small town, we used a cabbage substitute for the collard greens, and whole wheat flour for the semolina.  Still delicious!
Took me a lot longer than "50 minutes" but came out well. I've never made this style dumplings before and they came out perfectly. Maybe a bit salty.

Absolutely delicious- I doubled this recipe - took all morning- used collard greens already shredded- 
The house smelled heavenly -
Worth the time

very good - about 1 hour 15 min but made the dumplings before starting the broth. Agree it's too much lemon juice - used 2T and honestly liked it even better before that addition. Will make again for sure.

Lemon juice is in the ingredients list.
WHEN  to add it? It is not in the instructions.
"Remove the lid and gently stir in the lemon juice."

I was expecting this to be slightly too spicy for my palate, but I mainly stuck to recipe and it ended up being very well balanced. Will certainly make again. I cooked the meatballs in the oven for 15ish minutes at 425 instead of frying to free me up to make the broth while they were cooking. Subbed curry powder for coriander since I had none on hand and turned out great!

I made this as per Ottolenghi's instructions, using store-bought bread crumbs. The results were deeply satisfying. The broth is reminiscent of chili, but more complex. It's a bit of work, so I made the components one night and brought them together the next. I compacted the dumplings when I made them, then again before adding them to the broth, and they held together well. When I next make this I'll add more spice for deeper flavor, perhaps also to the dumplings.
Can this soup be frozen after cooking?

Made as directed and loved it for something different but satisfying on a cold night.  I lightly floured my hands to aide in a tighter roll of the dumplings and mine stayed perfectly shaped and in tact when cooked.
Absolutely delicious!  I didn’t have fresh cilantro, so left it out and decided that I’ll be making this recipe on repeat.

I forgot to put the egg into the dumpling mixture! ergo the dumplings disintegrated. nevertheless, it is so delicious. a friend showed up by surprise a half an hour after I made it, while eating he exclaimed: "this is like an orgasm in my mouth"

another excellent Ottolenghi recipe.

I faked buttermilk with a mix of Greek yogurt thinned with half and half. Yum.

I had leftovers - had to add water but the dumplings held up great. A little less delicate on day two but they absorbed more flavor! 

Yes, this involves too many dishes and is a little bit of work. But would be fun and not too bad at all with two people - one on dumplings, one on meatballs. I was able to do alone more or less in the specified time, but I was working nonstop.
Worth the extra effort ! Something different.

Here's what Jacque Pepin does with Jalepeños - and it makes a lot of sense. He cuts it open, takes a piece that has the seeds in it, touches the piece and seeds to the tip of his tongue and says in his charming accent "Oooh that's a hot one!"
