Seriously, Jalepeños vary greatly in their heat, so I always follow Jacques advice and taste the pepper before deciding exactly how I want to use it, depending. Salute to Jacque! I still watch his shows in re-runs! Great fun. Like this recipe a lot.

As always with Ottolenghi recipes, the amount of time and ingredients are always greater than expected… with results that are always greater than anticipated. Incredible layered flavor and the semolina dumpling texture was a surprise delight.  That said, I would 100% make the meatballs the day in advance.

I really wanted to like this recipe!  But for me it's just so so.  I had semolina in the pantry and subbed a beautiful bunch of kale for the greens.  I am unable to eat garlic and subbed shallots.  Otherwise followed the recipe and used good quality bread for fresh crumbs.  For me this recipe requires numerous steps and extra dirty dishes and is not worth the extra trouble when there are so many wonderful soup/stew. recipes that provide more flavor and fewer dirty dishes. I am an organized cook and I don't see how one could make this dish in the 50 minutes indicated in the recipe.  "Meh."
This recipe is delicious except my dumplings turned out really dense and did not grow in size at all. I followed the recipe exactly except I used panko bread crumbs. Any ideas what I did wrong?
Only 1/2 tsp allspice, and replaced greens with parsley (what we had in the fridge). Cooked meatballs in air fryer until toasted / browned. Phenomenal recipe - family loved it!

I recently returned from Tel Aviv, where I had a version of this dish at an Iraqi and Persian owned restaurant and was blown away. I wanted to make it myself, but the idea of stuffed dumplings felt overwhelming. This recipe made it doable and the results were fantastic. I replaced the lemon with 1 tspn of citric acid (authentic recipe calls for this), used Swiss chard halved the breadcrumbs and added one egg to the meatballs (so they wouldn't fall apart), and added leeks and carrots. Amazing!

Great recipe! Tasted pretty close to some dishes I’ve had at Mediterranean restaurants. I altered the recipe a bit. I omitted the dumplings and thickened the sauce and served it over couscous with a bit of tzatziki on top. It was delicious! I also substituted the semolina for flour and it was fine. Next time I’d maybe add some diced tomatoes and make a bit more sauce. It’s also important to put a lid over the meatballs when cooked in the sauce. Mine originally came out pretty rare. I’ll definitely be making this again.

title: Butternut Squash, Leek and Za’atar Pie Recipe
author: Yotam Ottolenghi

stats.Total Time: 2 ½ hours, plus chilling and cooling

comments.commentBody: Looks fantastic and it is on the list for this weekend... I have found that Trader Joe's puff pastry is the best... it's only available seasonally... and this is the season to pick it up!

My husband was in Trader Joe's the other day, and they told him they discontinued their puff pastry.  Our Kroger-equivalent hasn't been able to get it for 3 months, so they suggested Crescent Roll pastry.  (This is for a different dish, not this one.)

TJs already closed their beloved puff pastry out for “the season”. For some dumb reason they pull the plug on it right before Christmas … Too bad because @$4.99 for a generously sized 2 rectangle pkg.,  it’s the best deal anywhere on all butter puff pastry.

Really delicious. The only change I made was using a Kale/Baby Spinach blend instead of Swiss Chard. Beautiful presentation and despite a lengthy looking recipe, pretty simple. No need to refrigerate anything between steps. Assembly was very easy. I will make this again, but may consider using goat cheese instead of cream cheese with the feta.
You can make your own za’atar - it’s a blend of spices, lots of recipes online
The essential herb in zatar is sumac, which is also called zatar. No easy substitute for that.
dried 1 thyme, 1 oregano, 2 marjoram, 2 sumac, 1 toasted sesame seeds, and (to taste) salt. Play with this then vary it ....
I'll be using sumac with a bit or oregano and marjoram since I really really dislike thyme.

Made this for xmas lunch and it was the favorite dish served by far.  I did prep the dish the day before and then just assembled and baked in the morning.  Very easy recipe.  I did substitute goat for the feta as my mom dislikes feta.  Mom gets what she wants at this point of her life.  I actually felt the substitution was excellent.  Lastly, i didn’t see it mentioned in the recipe but I poked a few holes into the puff pastry to let steam escape and a crispy crust was had by all.

@Drew
It does mention puncturing the pastry all over.
Step 0 - take the cream cheese out to soften. This will make Step 4 much easier and you won't smash the butternut squash into mush.

Made this last night and it was beautiful and delicious. I roasted the squash, cut in half, skin side down with the maple syrup mixture on it. After it was cooked, I peeled and cubed it- way faster. 
Mixed feta with chèvre and added 2 egg yolks because they were left from another recipe. Used spinach instead of chard and that worked well. Added some chilies. Didn’t cool as long as the recipe said as I was running out of time. Perfectly balanced flavours.

This was delicious, but the photo is misleading. The pie was massive - at least twice a big as the photo depicts. Also, the directions for the pastry are confusing. The bottom layer is 10 x 16 and the top layer is 6 x 16, but the top layer has to cover a mountain of filling and it says to use a fork to seal the edges. I folded the bottom layer with the egg wash over the top layer and then sealed.

The direction could have been clearer, I had to read it though a couple of times to see that the trimmed pastry is the bottom layer:
[Spoon the cooled leek mixture on top of the base of the trimmed puff pastry sheet.....Take your other sheet of pastry, drape it over the pie and trim the excess dough.]
They should have made this into more steps with more white space between the steps and/or put the size of each sheet in the step.
I'm glad you were able to salvage it.
You did it upside down.  The bottom is 6x16 and the top is 10x16 to accommodate the filling.

The za’tar was really over powering. Would use way less next time so I can taste the other flavors. Also the recipe should say to add holes in the top to vent the steam - we followed the recipe and didn’t and ended up with the dreaded soggy bottom :(
What do you think about goat cheese instead of feta? I love feta but have a whole log of goat right now!

Delicious, aromatic and beautiful presentation.  
The bottom crust was over-baked and difficult to cut.  I blame my well- insulated oven which likely didn’t drop to 350 as quickly as an older oven.  Next time, I will place the cold ‘pie’ in a preheated 425 and then immediately lower to 350.
This was worth making and perfect for a holiday meal.  I was wishing I had some whole cranberry-orange relish to accompany.
I added thinly sliced portabella caps ,sauteed in sesame oil.  Layered on top of the squash gave this hearty and well appreciated dish a slightly earthier flavor.

Delicious flavors made for a great dinner on the first try, and friends were impressed with the golden, decorated top crust. But prep is so time-consuming, it is not easy to make except for special occasions, and it is too delicious to leave in that category. Next time, I'll make it like a galette and skip the full crust wrap. The squash, leeks, chard, scallions, cheese and Za'atar are the stars, not pastry dough. I'll also use goat cheese instead of melding Feta and cream cheese--a tiring step.

I ended up with too much of the squash/feta mixture so I warmed up some chicken broth, added the squash and feta and cooked it until the squash was very soft.  Then I pureed it with a stick blender and wow, amazing soup.  The saltiness of the feta against the sweetness of the squash with a hint of cinnamon is just amazing.  I would make this on its own.
A very pretty entree. I found it too sweet though.   Maybe a stronger, sharper cheese instead of the cream cheese/feta blend would work better.

Amazing flavor and presentation but 2 lbs of butternut squash was about 1.8 lbs. too much. I couldn't possibly fit all of it in the puff pastry "package".  Are you sure this wasn't a typo? It was a ridiculous amount for the 6 x 16-inch rectangle. :(
You don't need a "large egg" to make egg wash for any recipe unless you are making many dozens of something. Just sayin.
A trick I learned from my mom when baking chicken pot pie.  Place the sheet pan in oven while preheating that way it will cook the bottom of crust which often gets soggy.

I was thrilled to find geefree (Gluten free) puff pastry.  I wanted to double the recipe, so i bought two boxes.  I followed the instructions for the first one - the clean table, roll out the puff pastry.  It was a disaster.  I couldn't get the puff pastry off of the table.  So for the second one, I used (gluten free) flour on the rolling pin and rolled the puff pastry on parchment paper.  Much better!  Perhaps everyone else knows how to work with puff pastry and knew this trick ....

I made this for Thanksgiving and it was a lovely display (I added some leaf-shaped cutouts and it looked very festive), but I felt like it needed another savory component to balance out the sweetness and richness of the squash mixture, and another sturdier texture in there somewhere. As is, aside from the crispy pastry, the whole thing was very soft (ok I’ll say it: mushy) and quite sweet for my taste. If I made it again, I would probably make these adjustments: 1) leave the maple glaze off the butternut squash (I think the natural sweetness would be enough for me); 2) add some well-seasoned mushrooms as a savory element; 3) add an acid to brighten it up a bit, maybe some lemon zest or vinegar in the leek mixture; 4) add a crunchy(ish) texture, maybe some walnuts or pistachios; 5) leave out the cream cheese and use a mix of goat cheese and feta, or maybe just goat cheese (I used even parts of all three because I happened to have all of them). I appreciated other commenters’ reminders to make slits in the top pastry—mine turned out nice and crisp, and the vents added to the aesthetic appeal. Hope this helps someone!
I am making this right now and was thinking of adding some chopped toasted pecans?
It's delicious, but next time much less cream cheese and more feta and squash.

Really great!! * Ottolenghi's recipe calls for only1.4# of squash - not 2# as listed here
 I swapped the amounts of cream cheese and Feta to have a stronger flavor.  I added between the two layers, a bag of pre-cooked chestnuts that I roughly chopped and tossed with roasted garlic and oil.  Nice touch.  As for the trimmed pastry - cover it with cinnamon sugar - roll it up, slice it, and flatten to make a handful of palmier-type cookies to bake with the dinner.  Will definitely make again

Delicious, excellent vegetarian main dish. Swapped cream cheese for goat cheese and Swiss chard for spinach as I couldn’t find chard. Added roasted mushrooms to the mixture and my family was very happy with the result.
I’m about to make this for the third year in a row, as the center piece of our vegetarian Thanksgiving! It’s fantastic! But I have always subbed goat cheese for the cream cheese and never been sorry!
@Linda did you sub an equal amount of goat cheese, and what kind, e.g. the roll of soft chèvre?

Unfortunately,  I have to make everything gluten-free for my husband.  Instead of using puff pastry could I make a homemade gluten-free pie crust?  It wouldn't be a s beautiful but do you think it could work??
I don’t see any reason it won’t. Go for it!

You may be able to find gluten free puff pastry at your local health food store or Whole Foods. An option might be to make Yotam’s butternut squash fondue pie, which I’ve made, with GF filo dough I purchased at my health food store. Very good!!!
Do you have any ideas about a chutney or sauce that would good to serve with this dish?  Maybe it doesn’t need any sauce.

Time saving advice: use phyllo dough! Made this as specified, except used phyllo instead of puff pastry. Didn't need to chill for an hour or overnight after assembling. Started it at 400, reduced to 375 until phyllo was browned, and then lowered to 350 until we guessed it was hot enough. Fantastic.

I’m not sure this is worth the work. It is certainly okay … but not really special. One thing: it says to serve warm or at room temperature. We discovered when we reheated it that it is a lot better when it is hot, so I wouldn’t let it sit for as much as 20-30 minutes.
Thanks. It was good 5 minutes out of the oven.

I made this for a family dinner 12-26-22 along with the Onion Galette AND the Wild Mushroom tart. For all three, I made the Wild Mushroom puff pastry recipe (so easy). The fillings were made the previous day so flavors could flower and the crust the morning of. What a hit these were! With a green salad. steamed broccoli and a fruit pie for dessert, this was a meal to remember. Thank you NYTimes! Gerry in Oregon

Made this for the first time last night. Big WOW. We made our own za’taar, subbed a pre baked GF pie crust and topped it with an unbaked pie crust (Holes poked in both). And we were beyond thrilled with it. It took us about an hour to prep, an hour to chill (believe that’s essential), and 45 minutes to bake. A great brunch item!

I made this with Whole Wheat Gallette dough, and used the ring of a spring form pan to hold it together for the start of baking, then removed to let it crisp up. Subbed some yogurt and sour cream for the cream cheese.  YUM!

I planned to make one with GF Puff Pastry for New Years Eve. I could only find regular puff pastry so I used one sheet I had in my freezer  to make a small GF for myself (celiac) and made a regular one for everyone else. The regular was far more appealing looking - puffed nicely. GF was also very good but unfortunately GF puff pastry doesn’t “puff” much. It was good but I wish I had read the comments - adding mushrooms and goat cheese instead of cream cheese would make this suburb.

This was delicious! Would be great for a crowd.

Not sure about the squash quantity issues that some folks had, but it all worked out fine for me. It seems like it won't all fit, but if you just pile the squash into an even layer on top of the leeks, it works out.

One big edit - NO ONE had puff pastry in stock, so I made the "all-purpose flaky pastry dough" that Claire Saffitz has over at King Arthur's website and followed the dimensions listed here.

Scrumptious.  Went light on the maple syrup & used goat cheese instead of cream cheese. Looks as good as it tastes.  Remember if using Dufour puff pastry that there is only one sheet per package. Luckily I had bought two. Will make again. Was a big hit

Good to know that goat cheese, which is naturally lower in fat, can sub for the cream cheese with a tasty result.  I can easily imagine how it would change the taste, with the cream cheese lending a sweeter more dessert like flavor and the goat providing some salty tartness, which I imagine would be offset by the maple syrup and sweet squash.
looks great but complicated to make, too rich and not that tasty

This was delicious. I used 2/3 goat cheese and 1/3 cream cheese. I think that the filling might work as a Crostata as well, very yummy. Dufour makes very nice frozen puff pastry, better than Pepperidge Farm. It is somewhat labor intensive but can be done over a few days.
Surprised to not be wowed by this. It was gorgeous, but the cream cheese overpowered in my opinion and I’d use a little less za’atar. Might try again with those alterations.
I used Penzey’s Zatar and it was excellent.

Followed the recipe exactly and it had great potential.  The leek mixture was superb, and the squash was perfectly roasted. When assembled it fit into a puff pastry and looked delicious but after baking it just tasted like a soft, cream cheesy blob of squash with not enough feta taste and not enough leek mixture. Perhaps if I make it again I will use less squash or more leek mix, cut way down on the cream cheese, and stuff it a bit more lightly for a greater pastry to filling ratio.

I didn’t have enough butternut squash, so subbed in some sliced red peppers and roasted them together at step 2. As both are sweet when roasted I cut back on the maple syrup. 
It was made for a dinner party and received lots of compliments. 
Crisp green salad and a bowl of couscous to accompany worked a treat.
I would reduce the cream cheese and/or sub goat cheese. Much too rich. Remember to chill.
This was good I made with sourdough pizza dough (enriched with olive oil recipe from the food geek YouTube). Made it like a giant calzone. It was really good.

The most difficult step for me is rolling out the puff pastry.  Is rolling puff pastry necessary for any reason other than to get a big enough piece?  Could I just use the puff pastry out of the box if I cut down the amount of filling appropriately?  Otherwise, not so hard to make, and I and my guests love this "banquet pie."
Ohhhh... just make this.  You won't regret it.

Delicious! When I make it again I won’t bother weighing out the quarter pound of chard and will simply use the whole bunch. I did use two rather than one leek and I think it benefits from the larger ratio of leek/chard to squash mix.
Real talk this is one of the most delicious things anyone’s ever thought of

I made this as a test for Thanksgiving over the weekend. The worst part was rolling out the pastry as mine was only 12 inches. I ended up making it 7 inches by 15 inches and covering the ends with leftover pastry pieces. It worked fine and was delicious. I'm thinking about making it 8 inches wide by 14 inches and cutting down the middle to get more servings. I really liked the leek zaatar layer. I'm going to double that piece next time. There was lots of filling overall.

We made this to rave reviews for visiting family. It was a lot of work compared to a typical dinner around here, but we took heed of the tip to split up some of the prep the day before, leaving just assembly and baking for the day of. Leftovers were delicious reheated or fridge cold!
This was a fantastic main dish option for vegetarians on thanksgiving! Huge hit! I was generous with the Zaatar and Cream Cheese.
Do you think this would be ok using vegan cream cheese/feta and egg?
Probably not.

I think it would. If you have access to vegan cream cheese and to vegan egg (both sold at Whole Foods), I don't see any reason why that wouldn't work. Cooks Illustrated just did a piece on vegan egg substitutes and found that it works very well.
I used Trader Joe's vegan cream cheese and it was fine -- but I did use regular feta (Trader Joe's Israeli).
Any substitutions for za’atar?

Thyme, sumac
oregano, marjoram, basil,
This looks delicious, though not into the maple syrup and cinnamon, I’m afraid they’re gonna make the squash too sweet. Will I wreck the recipe if I don’t include them?

I also dislike too-sweet entrees. I don't think that small amount of maple syrup would add too much sweetness, it sounds like a "flavor nuance". If you dislike cinnamon, may I suggest a small pinch of mace or a few gratings of fresh nutmeg, which has a bit sharper flavor than cinnamon. 

I'm going to try the recipe as written - almost. I cannot eat feta cheese, so I need to figure out a sub for that.
Any suggestions for a cheese to use to replace the feta? I love it, but it triggers migraines for my partner. Other off limits cheeses are chèvre and blue cheeses. I know—so many of the good ones.
I would use goat cheese as a tangy substitute.
Why do I need to roast the squash? Can I just microwave? Or sauté? BTW TJs and Sprouts sell fresh peeled and cubed Butternut Squash, great time saver

To Cali Oma: The trick with a pie like this is to control moisture so it doesn't end up soggy.  This is probably why he calls for roasting it.  Also, for the nice roasty notes you get in the oven, of course.
does this freeze well?
we veganized this by using vegan cheeses. fantastic!

The minute I saw this recipe I knew I had to try it and tonight I did. A delicious, festive main course for Christmas dinner, who needs Chinese food?? It's a bit labor intensive but worth it. It's creamy, savory, crunchy and so flavorful. A real keeper!
Feta is frowned upon in our house (and it’s not me), so would cotija or parm be a better replacement?
I think either would be delicious.  I’m not a feta fan either, but I did find a French feta at a local specialty cheese shop, and it was pretty good.
goat or gruyere could be a good replacement.
Wondering if I could sub canned butternut squash purée (drained to firm it up)?
I am allergic to dairy. Do you suggest vegan cream cheese for this recipe or how else can I adapt it? Thank you.
Another possibility is silken tofu with a little lemon juice.
TJ vegan cream cheese worked fine
no way all the parts fit into the "pie shell" ..had almost 50% extra ,,,used  exactly a 2# squash and measured others ,,,

I made this for xmas dinner for the vegetarians and served it with roast veggies and a salad. I made as directed, making the fillings the day before, except I halved the salt. It was a great centerpiece and a big hit!
His food is like a warm hug. I'm considering making this into 4 individual pies instead of 1 big one.
If I’m using frozen squash do I still need to roast it?
Made in Melbourne, Australia :) big hit with the family, Ottolenghi always a winner!
This makes a good veggie alternative to Beef Wellington, which is my family's traditional Christmas dinner.  It's very good the first time, even better heated up in the oven the next day!

I made this with my own pie dough instead of puff, but didn't have enough so made it like a galette. Za’atar may exist here but I have never seen it, made it, or eaten it, and subbed red chili paste. No scallions, so subbed red onion and a tragic, left-over half a leek. No chard or greens, subbed small tomatoes left over from the garden, and just skipped cream cheese altogether. Whatever it was, it was excellent, even though it could never be replicated.
Deborah, check out this site for Za'atar: https://shop.themediterraneandish.com/products/zaatar-spice-blend-2
I made this as directed on Christmas Eve. It was dull and disappointing and so not worth the effort in my opinion.
Made vegan with Daiya cream cheese and Trader Joe’s feta. Used slightly less maple syrup. Delicious and impressively beautiful.

It was great and looked lovely; we ate it 5 minutes out of the oven (hot was good).  Has anyone tried convection vs regular bake (I used regular)? Only ingredient changes:  TJ vegan cream cheese and only 1/2 tsp salt in leek mixture.  As advised, I cut slits in the top.  But I  sautéed the diced chard stems with the leeks and scallions (my usual technique).  The filling amount was fine; I piled it up.  I might reduce the maple syrup next time.
Is excellent but we would prefer it without the cream cheese.  Also recommend adding a little more width to the base to make the side easier to form.

Has anyone made this into individual pies?
Also what did people use instead of egg if need to omit egg?
I used half and half instead of egg and it worked great.

Made this twice and love it. Used filo dough as the top crust.
Only disappointment is the grocery store zatar didnt seem to have much taste. Any suggestions?

I agree that za'atar didn't have much taste.  Used a lot, tablespoons, and it was flavorful. I missed instruction #5 to pierce the puff pastry and it was fine.  I don't care for feta either, but I follow directions the first time around, and the cream cheese subdued the feta flavor.  Will use an earlier suggestion to use more greens.  Will try the mushrooms and the goat cheese next time.

If you use Trader Joe's puff pastry it's quite an effort to get the sheets to the correct size because they're only 8.5 oz each.  Brushed with half and half instead of egg.  I was in a hurry so I didn't refrigerate anything, baked 15min less than recommended, and did not let it cool for more than about 5 minutes. It was still really good.
Fabulous recipe! It’s even more tasty the next day.
TJ's is out of puff pastry until next fall.   Has anyone tried this with filo dough or regular pie crust?
Note to Drew and others who may have missed this sentence... -Step 5 tells us to pierce with a fork to make holes in the pastry. This lets the steam escape.
I don't care for feta and don't know its qualities when baked. What other cheese would make a good substitute?
This was excellent. My grocery store was out of chard, so I substituted spinach. Everyone enjoyed it.

I never would have thought of this combo of ingredients but am so glad Yotam did! This was much easier than I expected & the puff pastry hid my many rolling & draping errors. I will definitely make this again for festive events & also just because. I made it as written except  subbed Tuscan kale as I had it on hand. Also poked a few holes in the top as others suggested.

This was a big hit at our family Christmas gathering (including with the non-vegetarians!) I just used one big sheet of puff pastry and folded it over the filling, and the finished product still looked very impressive. As previously noted, puff pastry is very forgiving.
Next time I'll play around with the cheese combination a bit more. I was a bit short on cream cheese so added some goats cheese, which I think provides a nice sharpness.
Definitely one to add to the repeat list.

Yum!  This recipe was easier than I thought it would be.  I liked the breaking it up into stages...as combining it into one go would have a bit overwhelming for this cook.  Accidentally omitted the olive oil and salt on the squash roast, but it turned out fine.  The combination of flavors is just lovely...but I think I would have liked a bit more of the green layer.  Will increase by a half next time.  Thank you for this treat in a crust!
This is incredible. I followed the recipe exactly with the exception of swapping goat cheese for the cream cheese. It turned out exceptional!
Does this recipe require 2 x 14oz puff pastry sheets or 2 sheets weighing 14oz in total?
Two sheets weighing 14 oz.  My two sheet package was 17 oz.  Followed directions and everything worked.

Made this last night for New Years Eve dinner.  Served it with raw Brussels sprout/kale salad.  A perfect winter meal.  Per other reviewers, I used goat cheese instead of cream cheese; it would have been too sweet for me otherwise.  I held back some of the filling, thinking it would be too much or leak out, but there was plenty of drape left for the pastry sheet to cover the whole thing.  Easy steps, make-ahead, gorgeous presentation: it’s a winner.

To wrap this in the puff pastry, use the procedure in the NYT recipe for Mushroom Wellington, and the commenter’s advice to seal two pastry sheets together end to end and cut off one third to get correct size.  Use the cutoff piece to either cut into three strips and braid or cut out shapes to put on top. BUT on my experience of guests feebly appreciating this dish and others previously extolling the mushrooms en croute, make that one instead for a fabulously delicious vegetarian centerpiece.
Truly spectacular! A lovely mix of flavours. Served it for New Year’s lunch and my family raved. The feta and cream cheese combination was delightful. A fair amount of work but well worth the effort.
Really good.  Butternut squash married well with feta and cream cheese.  Used kale because had no chard--just fine.  Makes vegetarian flavorful and easy.
The vegan feta and cream cheese alternatives from Trader Joe’s work beautifully in this recipe. In a place bursting with good cooks, this was the star at a New Year’s potluck.

I prepared the filling as listed except I used baby spinach instead of the chard; I used light cream cheese and feta. For the pastry I used 4 layered phyllo sheets with butter/olive oil to make 4 packets. Baked packet at 400 for 22 minutes. One packet divided between the two of us with a grilled salmon portion each was a perfect meal! The other packets are frozen (uncooked) and ready to thaw and bake another day!

Really enjoyed this (4 stars). Squash was a little sweet, maybe add a little heat for balance next time. Used collard greens because that was what was available, some yellow onion because I forgot  scallions, and made my own puff pastry (Sally’s Baking Addiction Rough Puff Pastry) because our grocery was out. Love that this can be made ahead!!
Delicious!

I made this over the past 2 days.  A lot of work but nice presentation and tasted good. My husband commented that he didn't really taste the za'atar, which amazed me because so much was in there!  I laughed at the end of the instructions where it said to bake another 45 minutes or until cooked through. How are you supposed to determine that?
Oh, I did poke holes in the top before baking, as suggested by others.  The dough was crisp.
I used goat cheese and phyllo sheets. I didn't cool anything. It was terrific.

I made this for New Year’s Eve exactly as the recipe states. It was beautiful and delicious! My friend's husband asked her to get the recipe; she's a major chef so that was a big compliment. Chilling the filling made it so I could build it up into a loaf shape and squeeze all the filling onto the pastry. It was a lot of work but great for a special occasion! All the commenters' suggestions and modifications sound yummy, too. I think you can be creative with this!
Made this for a NYE gathering. I followed the recipe but used vegan cream cheeses as one guest cannot eat cow dairy. It was beautiful, and tasty, but not amazing flavor.
This was good, and I love Zaatar, but I'm not sure it added anything to the dish. It almost seemed out of place.

Bravo! This was fantastic! I didn’t have leeks or scallions, the grocery was out of both, so I used a bunch of shallots and some leftover chives and it worked great. My pie was quite wide because the filling was pretty voluminous but no complaints here. Leftovers heated in oven 350 for 12mins made some delicious lunches.

I only had one sheet of puff pastry and it was absolutely no problem to wrap the filling in one sheet and then flip it to put the seam on the bottom.  For me, it wes plenty of pastry this way since the pastry is so rich.  Also, it was a touch sweet for my taste so I will use olive oil rather than maple syrup in the future.

The store was out of puff pastry, likely due to the holidays, so I made it with regular pie dough. One piece for the bottom of the pie dish and then another used to crosshatch the top. It was delicious! Held together very well for leftovers and was still yummy (and the crust still flaky) eaten cold. I did substitute kale for the Swiss chard, as that was also sold out. Next time I’d cut it down smaller and add it in sooner to cook longer as the ribs were a bit tough.

I didn’t like the puff pastry available at my local supermarket— it said it contains a “bioengineered ingredient” — so I used filo dough instead.  It came out great.  I really like the mix of butternut squash, leek and za’atar in the recipe.

I didn’t find puff pastry I liked at my local supermarket — it said it contains a “bioengineered ingredient “ — so I used filo dough  instead. It came out great.  I really like the combination of butternut squash , leek and za’atar in this recipe.
Delicious and flavorful on a cold, rainy night! Used Dorie Greenspan’s pie dough instead of puff pastry. Have a dark Porter alongside it!

5 out of 5 for me, very delicious and satisfying. I made a double batch of the roasted butternut from Yasmin Fahr‘s Miso-Butter Butternut recipe here on NYT cooking earlier in the week. The miso really helped to balance out the sweetness of the squash and I could use the veggies for two different meals which made the roasting process well worthwhile. Also it paired well with the zatar. The pie was devoured in no time. Thanks for a great recipe.

Made it with homemade puff pastry. The whole assembly was quite a bit of work, but definitely worth it. I brought the cream cheese mixture to room temperature and mixed in spoonfuls with the room temperature squash using my hands. Also built up the squash layer using my hands; I’m not coordinated enough to keep the filling inside the lines with a spoon.
Can you bake the pie after step 6 or does it have to refrigerator first?
Delicious but VERY rich!  The flavors combine sweet and savory...maple syrup, cinnamon, za'atar, leeks and scallions, and cream cheese and feta.  Final presentation is beautiful.

Excellent! I recommend.

I made a few changes (based on necessity) but followed the essence of the recipe. I had my doubts about the flavors about halfway through, but I trusted Yotam and he was, as usual, right. The combo of the sweet/creamy with the sour/vegetal is great!

In case you’re interested in adaptation: I used phyllo instead of puff pastry (made in a square 8x8” tin); subbed green beans for the leafy greens (dietary restriction); and inverted the ratio of feta to cream cheese.

I completely forgot the chard (was going to use spinach), and as a vegan, used a mixture of Tofutti sour cream and Miyoko's cream cheese, which  for other uses I find way too sour, but here it worked and the end result had my dinner guest in ecstasies. Since there were just the 2 of us, I halved the puff pastry but kept the squash and particularly the leeks plentiful. It was wonderful! And the small left-over was delicious this morning. Thanks for a great and adaptable recipe!

Couldn't get my hands on a butternut squash so used a sweet potato instead, was very good. I decided not to squish the cheese mix into the not-squash and instead just layered it on top of the chard and not-squash layers. I did refrigerate overnight, nice to be able to make it ahead. Was excellent!  I had cut the recipe in half to have 4 servings, it was difficult not to just keep eating.
The direction on the squash was wobbly.  It said to cut into one inch pieces.  So I slices it into 1 inch pieces.  But I don't think that was right.  Was it?
One other note.  The olive oil was separated, that is, two different portions at two different times.  I goofed there too.

I made this the other night, got it partially baked, then had to go out. I wrapped it up and finished baking it the next night for dinner. It was very good!

I made changes because of what I had and what I couldn’t find (the great puff pastry shortage). I used acorn squash instead of butternut, spinach rather than Swiss chard (a lot of Spinach), and crescent rolls instead of puff pastry. It still it was very tasty. I would put in something spicy next time, but there will be a next time.

I could not find puff pastry, so used phyllo instead. It took about 1.5 rolls, which I had laid out in layers to make a sheet about 12x18 inches (butter brushed between the sheets). It worked great this way, and with za'atar being a middle eastern spice mix, I convinced myself phyllo was a good fit. 
there was extra filling, which I wrapped in the extra phyllo sheets (like spanikopita) and froze, unbaked.
Wonderful flavors and can easily be adapted to whatever greens/onions/cheese you have on hand. Made as a covered pie with a single top crust and it was delicious!
If baked and frozen what would instructions be to re-heat? Thaw first? Heat in oven from frozen?

Made this today & loved it.It takes awhile to prepare but well worth the time.I did soften the cream cheese per another review and that worked well. Also used a blend of chard, spinach & kale as that’s what I had. I may substitute the feta for goat cheese next time. Was a great meatless meal & I will definitely make again.

My daughter loved this. I swapped out spinach for swiss chard and added mushrooms. Would def. make again. I did have trouble finding puff pastry-for the commentor who mentioned Trader Joe's is is only sold seasonally.
Maybe try phyllo dough
Absolutely delicious!

I made this as written except for the assembly as I wasn’t sure if I had enough puff pastry.  I laid out the pastry I had and placed the ingredients then rolled it.  The result was fine but it did end up with the ‘base’ layer on the side.  If I did the same thing again, I would spend the leek/chard mixture over the whole width of the pastry so that you got some of it in every bite.
Really great. His recipes are a bit complex but great. I need to up my aesthetics on my pastry
This was so good. Somewhat involved, but instructions are clear and easy to follow.

I had only one sheet of puff pastry, so I rolled it thin and laid it in a pie plate, then used the overage to cover the top of the filling. Even had enough for a little cutout as suggested. Did the rest as written, it is very rich and flavorful.

This was so delicious! Started in the AM to have for lunch. Had every intention of following the recipe exactly but had to sub labneh for cream cheese. My leek was small so I added a little red onion. Will make this again and again!
@Barbara Good tip thanks and I use wax paper for pastry roll outs.
@Leslie Doing it now! Tks
Could this be made into a gallete instead?
I found the filling to be too salty. I would cut back on the salt that is added to the chard mixture - the saltiness of the feta more than makes up for it

couldnt be bothered with the puff pastry, so layered the chard mixture and the squash mixture into an 8x8 baking dish, topped with breadcrumbs/evoo/zataar and baked for about 35 minutes.  darned good (though less elegant) with fewer fussy steps.

title: Roasted Brussels Sprouts With Cinnamon-Butter Yogurt and Chestnuts Recipe
author: Yotam Ottolenghi

comments.commentBody: This is a lot of work with many steps and ingredients. Unless you can assign somebody to prepare this single dish, there’s absolutely no way it would make it to a Thanksgiving table since it can’t be prepared in advance, at least as I read the recipe. I find it much simpler to halve and roast the brussels sprouts in olive oil and lightly dress with salt, pepper, hot honey or maple syrup and Aleppo pepper, and a squeeze of lemon or balsamic. Easily prepped and thrown together at the last minute.

Yes, I was thinking the same thing. I'm looking forward to trying this recipe with the leftovers a day or 2 later. And I hope Mr Ottolenghi will try to be a bit more open-minded about our Thanksgiving traditions. The food doesn't have to be bland.
A. Cleary: Yes. I don't recognize the Thanksgiving he describes in the Times Magazine article with which this recipe appears.

We thought this was fabulous!  Used Trader Joe cooked, vacuum packed organic chestnuts.  The dish looked so festive, all red and green.  The pomegranate seeds are a must, as they look like jewels and pop into bursts of sweet juice.  Crispy fried sage leaves are also a great touch--I fried in butter and poured over at the end.  Quite filling, as chestnuts have meaty texture, but not heavy.
Agree with Ellen on the olive oil, salt and pepper. Maybe a splash of balsamic. Honey and maple syrup are too sweet for me. Brussel sprouts are delicious when simple and roasted.

There are a lot of "moving parts" to this recipe but some shortcuts might help. Make the yogurt butter and chestnut topping sans the pomegranate seeds the day before. Similarly prep the sprouts and kale. Cover and refrigerate. The following day bring everything up to room or "recipe temp", & add the seeds before cooking the sprouts and finishing it off.

Roasted Brussels sprouts do not need to be smothered in toppings.  Instead:
Halve the sprouts 
Spread on a baking pan 
Drizzle with olive oil
Salt a bit
Roast about 20 minutes at 400 degrees, or until the outside crisps up and is done to taste. Toss/shake midway through.  (We use parchment paper on top of a baking sheet.)
Test/taste occasionally while the sprouts roast — they will become sweeter and creamier as time passes.
Brussels sprouts are the non-sweet food on the Thanksgiving plate so why squander that by using maple syrup and cinnamon? Better option — flavors like lemon, garlic, mustard, pepper.

Too complicated a recipe for a large Thanksgiving meal. 

Roast with olive oil, garlic and, if desired, pepper and salt. A sprinkling of pomegranate seeds at the end will provide a sweet, tart counterpoint, or substitute balsamic vinegar as others have suggested.

I think there should be a separate section for “yes I cooked this” and either delete or put into a different section all the comments of “I don’t want to cook this.” “I don’t like these ingredients.” “This looks on paper far too complicated, “ etc..
Real talk does anyone actually like eating pomegranate seeds?  They look nice, great flavor, but chewing them up is like eating wood chips
I think they may be an acquired taste. I agree about chewing the woody seed! Beautiful, though. Maybe substitute finely chopped roasted beets?
He warns against overcooking the sprouts, but the photo clearly shows that they have been.

I am sad that Mr. Ottolenghi disparages fresh brussels sprouts roasted in olive oil with salt and pepper as bland.  On the contrary, the fresh, green flavour of the brussels sprouts is allowed to shine and is a good foil for the richness of the stuffing and gravy.

I can’t wait to make this. But it will be a star against a roast chicken or fish. Many Thanksgiving dishes are bland due to the traditional nod to the simplicity of the holiday’s origin, and the limitations of time in the kitchen while putting a feast on the table by 2 pm or so.
This would be a really good recipe for a child who has to be tricked into eating vegetables!
You can only make this at the last minute and it requires both the oven and the cook top, plus it uses several pans. I think I'll make it at a less hectic meal. It looks like something we would love.

This is a complicated and time consuming recipe. Also so special and worth it. Made exactly as written and it is perfect. If someone is willing to execute this as their one contribution to the thanksgiving meal, it will be the star, I assure you.
This recipe is fantastic. Don’t change a thing!

i prepared this complex Brussel Sprout side dish for Christmas dinner along with simpler fare, ham and cornbread. We concluded the day with NY Times's festive, Eton Mess. We thoroughly enjoyed the sprouts and later touted its goodness on Facebook. A big hit with my family. The trickiest part was peeling freshly boiled chestnuts for one of the four layers. (Turned to online advice for help with that.)
I don't know...cinnamon?  What does the hive mind say?
My mind questioned whether cinnamon would work in this dish too.
I love Reem Assil's cookbook "Arabiyya," in which savory dishes often incorporate cinnamon and cardamon, among other spices. Give it a chance!
I never cared for cinnamon - until I started using it in savory dishes.

This was amazing, even served room temperature due to thanksgiving oven space constraints  (meaning it can be made ahead of time).  Would probably 1.5x the garlic yogurt, it was the best part when contrasted with all the mapley warm spices and brown butter. Subbed walnuts for Chestnuts and cardamom for allspice due to shortages at grocery stores,
I realized this was way too much for my Thanksgiving, so made it as a stand alone vegetarian meal. Very good and unusual.

Ottolenghi recipes are usually involved, time-consuming events.  Suggestions for simplifying this recipe are excellent.  Brussels sprouts have many options. Ottolenghi wrote a cookbook called Simple because his sister asked if he could make his recipes simpler.  He can and he can't. Too many ideas in his head. My advice?  Pick and choose which ingredients to use. I am always rewarded, no matter. Ottolenghi is amazing.  Honor his recipes, even if you tweak them to create less time preparing them.

Made this for Thanksgiving and it was amazing!!

I did these steps in the morning; could probably have been done the day before:
- Mixed garlic yogurt 
- made onion topping
- crisped sage leaves 
- cut and tossed sprouts and kale 

In last push, cooked the sprouts (air fryer b/c oven space is premium), browned the butter (delegated to a guest) and assembled. I microwaved the topping briefly to heat. 

Subbed halved pecans for chestnuts b/c I forgot to buy them - was still delicious!
Excellent! We had no kale, so just made it without and halvened the sprouts instead of baking them as whole.

Subbed in delicata squash as the chunky veg and used shaved Brussel sprouts. Made as much as possible in advance and pulled it all together as the roasted veggies came out of the oven- it was a hit at our table!

Was able to make this for Thanksgiving (along with several other dishes) without too much fuss, and it was a favorite at the table! Delicious mix of flavors, that complimented the entire meal so well. 
If you can multi-task we’ll, it’s really not all that complicate, and came together fairly fast. Worth the effort!

So, this might sound like an odd combination. In fact, I plain don’t like pomegranates, and the raw garlic didn’t sound like a good idea. However, this dish is a symphony of flavors and textures. Make it exactly as written and you will be amazed. Served with a whole roasted duck, it made all other side dishes feel superfluous. It’s so delightful and complex, it will be the star of any table.

I am intrigued by this recipe (although a bit too complicated for Thanksgiving, I think), but I don't care for pomegranate seeds or kale.  Any suggestions for substitutions? 

As for the cinnamon, the topping has 1/2 teaspoon and the yogurt 1/8 teaspoon (with 2 lbs of vegetables); such small quantities I think wouldn't give you a pronounced cinnamon flavor, just a little underlying warmth.

My advice is parboil the sprouts; cool and chop;  braise in butter and heavy cream (preferably NOT ultra-pasteurized); salt and pepper to taste -- Julia Child, Vol. 1.  Quick, easy, and you get to enjoy the sprouts.
Any ideas for a nut-free alternative to the chestnuts? Pepitas maybe?
Pepitas would be wonderful. If no one is allergic, sunflower seeds also.
There seems to be a step missing in the cinnamon-butter yogurt sauce, specifically what to do and when to add the butter.
If I accidentally bought raw chestnuts, how must they be cooked for this dish?

I made simple, roast sprouts for early Thanksgiving with my in-laws, who appreciate a simpler preparation, and then with leftovers made this for my family. We loved it! Yes, there are multiple parts and is not traditional, but we enjoy preparing as well as eating together. Will do this for sure for Christmas.

I made it today one day ahead of Thanksgiving and have refrigerated it. It is, indeed, a lot of work with many steps, but it is an interesting dish. I added a little Goya Adobo to the Brussels sprouts.
Arg! Pomegranate seeds not on shopping list

Made this for Thanksgiving. It was delicious, and a unique set of flavors. Everyone loved it. The only drawback is that it is time consuming and has a lot of moving parts, so is difficult to do with trying to get a lot dishes on the table at once.

What a show-stopping Thanksgiving side. We loved it! I will make this again for Christmas. The colors, the flavors, the textures!! I made the recipe exactly as written. I maybe roasted the chestnuts too long (first time I ever did it) but it was great for some of our older people with weak teeth to not have crunchy nuts in the dish.

I dared to make this on thanksgiving and it was barely touched because I had to serve it after everything else, due to all the steps and need for oven + cooktop. (And this is after having prepped the chestnut topping and yogurt sauce, and skipping the browned butter and sage.) There are lots of great ways to avoid a bland thanksgiving without slaving over something this complicated on a busy holiday; love the recipe but save it for another day with a simpler menu.
I did some of the prep for this dish the day before Thanksgiving but still it was the most complicated dish I cooked for the meal and I didn’t love the flavors (esp the cinnamon).
This was delicious

Made this for Thanksgiving. It was delicious and beautiful. Made the yogurt, the chestnut topping, seeded the pomegranate, tore up the kale, and cleaned and trimmed the sprouts ahead. Such a bright addition to the feast!

This was wonderful. Definitely the most complex preparation of all of the dishes I cooked this year, but I was excited for the challenge and knew what I was getting into. I bought whole chestnuts, par-boiled them and peeled them the day before. Pre-peeled chestnuts would make this simpler but peeling them was meditative, ha. This ended up being the favorite dish on the table. Tasty, creative, gorgeous.

I agree with other cooks that this recipe is very involved with number of steps. Unless you’re making this separately, this should not be tried for Thanksgiving dinner. 
I want to mention that I enjoy pomegranate seeds, I have been eating these since I was a kid so may be this is acquired taste. They don’t taste like wood chips as others have mentioned. To me these have sweet nutty taste.
Divine! Well worth the effort.

Concur w/ Ellen.. it was a lot of work, but it was worth it. Complex and complimentary flavors/textures.  Pre-cooked chestnuts can be pricey but worth the time savings.  Now I just need to find some recipes to use the remaining chestnuts.....

Tried this on a less hectic day than Thanksgiving and it is not too sweet and is a nice take on Brussels sprouts. Would use again. I omitted sage and kale, which would have been good but may not be necessary. Also mixed the yogurt butter sauce in instead of plating sprouts on yogurt— an error that made it not very pretty but gave a nice creamy texture.

As mentioned in the description, I did the yogurt and chestnut elements in advance. The rest of the elements came together in about the same time as my usual Brussels sprouts recipes. I had one guest ask for the recipe, the other (who doesn't cook) said they were some of the best Brussels sprouts he had ever had. I made it per the recipe and is going into my keeper file.

I made this because it was my only contribution to Thanksgiving. It was delicious and fancy when it was warm. However, it doesn't have much to do with Brussels sprouts and leftovers have to be cold because of the yogurt. Meh.
We made 2/3 of this and it was a terrific vegetarian meal for two. Only made one change: used hazelnuts because I couldn’t find chestnuts. Also, not really sure the sage added much…
I agree with Ellen. There are so many steps; it takes too long; it is not a good fit with Thanksgiving. It looks very pretty and tastes fine, but you could get the equivalent with less work.
Used salted pecan halves instead- good

I made this as I had chestnuts I wanted to use up.  I thought it was good but likely wouldn’t make again.  Agree with comments that Brussels sprouts are delicious simply roasted and with a lot less work!
Oven 190 c

Agree with all about the overkill of ingredients and time consuming prep.  My go-to Sprout recipe is to toss the halved sprouts with sesame oil, salt & pepper IN A BOWL (why toss on the pan?!) and roast w/whole peeled garlic cloves, onion wedges, peeled fuyu persimmon wedges (here's your sweet) and add a sprinkle of chopped walnuts before serving.  Super delish!!!

I made this for Thanksgiving tonight. The overall effect was really lovely. I agree with others that there are a lot of moving parts to focus on in order to get this on the Thanksgiving table along with all the other dishes. I think I would enjoy making this as more of a vegetarian centerpiece instead of a side in a larger meal. That way I could give it my undivided attention! I also think this would be great with kale and broccoli, lose the Brussels another option.

Forget making this for Thanksgiving if you make a traditional meal... there are already way too many different foods put on that spread. We would never eat like that on a typical day or even for Sunday dinner. If you are like me, I make a prime rib. This would be great with that. This dish could also be a star player on a night with a simple roast chicken or even just with a simple salad for a vegetarian night.

Honestly, love Yotam Ottolenghi and his recipes more than any other current writer.  However.  This is over-done.  There is a great simple roasted brussels sprouts with maple listed here in NYT that retains the vegetable's taste and is far superior.

This was so delicious.  I kinda made up my own recipe after I saw a reel on instagram but they hadn't posted the recipe yet.  It's quite simple if you don't overthink it!  I used walnuts instead of chestnuts and when I didn't have pomegranate seeds I just drizzled pomegranate molasses (which I always have on hand) instead.  I made it for a women's Christmas potluck and they have a contest for best side dish, which I won!  Even in Texas where people don't like Brussels sprouts, it was a winner.

I have so many mixed feelings about this recipe’!  It was really good!  The yogurt layer (which I thought sounded so odd)  really elevates all of the flavors. This dish was also a lot of work. Maybe if you have a roast or ham in the oven and this is your main side, it would be fine. But I found it overwhelming to keep up with while also cooking other dishes. It was very good but, for me personally, I don’t think it was unique enough to go through all of that effort.

title: Butternut Squash Lasagna Pie Recipe
author: Yotam Ottolenghi

comments.commentBody: I'm aware of two kinds of lasagna noodles - the kind that need cooking in advance before making the lasagna, and the kind that will soften and cook in the pan without softening them in boiling water first. Which type does this recipe take?
There isn't anything special about no-boil lasagna. Use either one that you find at a good price.

@MTV
I have not made this yet, but in the video it looks like dry noodles, which if broken into pieces would mix more easily as directed.
A family favorite for us and definitely dry lasagna sheets broken up.👍🏻

Another winner from Ottolenghi!  Veganized beautifully using plain soy milk and olive oil for the béchamel, and "walmesan" for the grated parm (1 heaping cup walnuts, 1/2 tsp sea salt, 1 tsp grated lemon zest, 3 T nutritional yeast, 1 minced small garlic clove, ground to fine crumbs in a mini food processor)

Followed the recipe for the first time and it turned out well. First time making béchamel and needed much more milk to get it right. I do get frustrated when recipes say 2 hours to make and it takes me 3. With bake times and rest time @ an hour and 40 minutes, I’d like to watch any home cook peel, cut squash, measure and combine ingredients in the remaining 20. I rely on NYT recipes for 40 years and times are never near reality. Thanks

This always drove me crazy too until I realized that the published time does not include the time to gather everything that is in the ingredient column. 

So for this recipe, it assumes that you have already peeled and sliced the squash, crushed the seeds, chopped the garlic and measured all the other ingredients. My understanding is that they do this because cooks vary greatly in how long it takes to chop things. Personally, I think they should show the total time, using an average.

It was super helpful when I learned recently that the "rule" for recipe times is to consider it starting from the time the ingredients list is ready. So in this recipe where it says in ingredients, "1 small butternut squash (about 1 ¾ pounds), peeled, halved, deseeded and cut into ⅛-inch-thick slices" , the time it takes to do all that peeling, slicing, seeding isn't counted in the recipe time. Crazy, right? Anyway, helps me dial-up the time estimates with a prep-heavy list.
Instead of feta, use gruyere
I’d like to hear suggestions for those of us who don’t have the requisite springform pan. Thank you!
No reason can't do in a cast iron skillet. Just know that it won't be a 'pretty' presentation when served.  Taste will be the same but a 'not for company' look!

even though i own many springform pans, if i made this i wouldnt bother using one. i'd use a 9" square pan, as it's easier to cut into squares rather than wedges. just scoop out of the pan to serve. round pan works, too, of course.

I agree that a regular baking dish will work just fine.  The lasagna presents beautifully once cut and placed on a plate.  I used thick egg noodles from my pantry instead of lasagna noodles. Once my lasagna was done baking I was dismayed to notice I forgot to add the cumin and coriander at the start of the recipe. I toasted the spices in a small pan, ground them to a finer powder and sprinkled some on top before serving. A very forgiving recipe, all flavor and deliciousness!
It was a bit ambitious of us to make this for a weeknight meal, but it was delicious. We substituted lacinato kale for spinach.

I used fresh (store bought) lasagna noodles and they worked fine.  No baby spinach here so I used “mature” spinach.  Next time I would rip the spinach into even smaller pieces, as hard to cut through.  Otherwise, delicious.
How to you get squash in 1/8th inch sluices?  I’m thinking use a vegetable peeler or mandolin?
@Margaret I’m looking at my peeled butternut squash now and I’m wondering the same thing!  What did you use?

@Amy K. If you have an extra $65, Wirecutter’s mandoline recommendations are
spot on. Less expensive mandoline s aren’t worth the money. I enjoy using my knife skills for most food prep, but I use my Swissmar whenever lots of very thin slices are required. A very useful accessory is cut -resistant gloves, btw!  It’s inconvenient to lose a finger tip during meal prep and the blood loss alarms spectators.

This is the weirdest recipe I have ever made, and it came out perfectly delicious! Halfway through while mixing everything in a big bowl I thought, "all of this will never fit into that springform pan!" but it did and it was wonderful.

@MTV Use the kind that generally requires cooking in advance, but don't bother boiling them since the long bake and moisture content of dish should soften the dried pasta if pan's tightly sealed. Recipes requiring dried, no-bake noodles specify the fact. Ottolenghi contributed a similar recipe to The Guardian, inspiring Deb Perelman to create her own version. Cf. https://smittenkitchen.com/2021/10/winter-squash-and-spinach-pasta-bake/ where directions clearly indicate no need to boil the pasta.

First off, we used regular "cook-first" lasagna noodles and they worked perfectly in this recipe - not crunchy or dry at all!! Second, both local grocery stores were out fo basil so we had to nix it. Otherwise, made the recipe to a T and loved it! Not as fussy as some Ottolenghi recipes can be, which actually mad it perfect for a weeknight. Would easily make again for a dinner party.

Loved this dish even though I forgot to include the basil I'd taken pains to procure. The only deliberate change was to cook it in a baking dish rather than the spring-form cake pan. It looked and cooked up just fine. Two notes: it is important to cut the squash to 1/8th - anything larger and it won't cook in the stated time. Equally important is keeping the moisture in with foil.

I've made this several times and love it.  I've used different types of pans: a 10" square pan, a large Le Creuset dutch oven.  I suspect the springform pan makes the presentation of the dish more spectacular, but since I'm the only one eating it normally, it's not needed.

My one disagreement with the recipe is the bechamel sauce.  Too much flour (3T.butter, 1/3 c. flour, 1 1/2 c. milk).  I looked at my Julia Child recipe and it uses 2 T. butter, 3 T. flour, 2 c. milk

Not intentional, but I prepped this dish two nights before baking, and it worked out fine.  Used a 3-quart oval baker for good results.  Nuked the milk before making the bechamel, which always helps.  The red pepper slather that holds everything together could probably be used to advantage in quite a few other dishes.  Loved the shortcut of starting out with jarred peppers.
Has anyone doubled this recipe?  I'm planning on a crowd of 13.

The first time I made this, there were a few pieces of lasagna that were a little too chewy. Today, I made it again and added two tablespoons of water to the red pepper sauce. All of the lasagna was fully cooked and our friends loved it.
Will try with filo. Butternut börek. Less bechamel. (or none.)

I had a very large bowl but could barely fit all ingredients in let alone mix to coat everything. It was king of a mess. Then into the pan and I did manage to press it down so I’m crossing my fingers that it will cook evenly. I guess a HUGE bowl would be a better description.

I used a delicatta squash this time as the butternut squash at the store were too big,  Also, I soak no-boil lasagne noodles in warm water for 10 mins before tearing into the mixing bowl.  And a 10-12 oz pack of frozen spinach, thawed, drained, and well rung out by hand works, too.  One of my all time fall favorites

@Hamlin O’Kelley
Great ideas!

The ingredients list says: 8
ounces dried lasagna noodles, each roughly broken into 3 or 4 pieces 
so dried would be the most appropriate.

Really great and totally guest-worthy. Followed directions precisely; food processor sliver perfect for the squash. Only change for next time will be to grind the spices a little more finely, since the little pieces of coriander husk were kind of annoying to eat. A winner, baby! 2.5 hours to the table.

I doubled the recipe for a crowd and baked in a large baking pan. Using the food processor’s slicing disc produced thin enough butternut. My noodles were still crisp in parts after over an hour in the oven so I added hot water towards the end to help soften them. Next time I’d try using noodles that don’t need boiling. This is a very labor intensive recipe with rewarding, interesting flavors!

Fantastic recipe!  Given our gluten intolerance, used Barilla gluten free lasagna noodles and found they worked perfectly.  Also made the bechamel with gluten free flour.  The only variation from the recipe is that it took nearly two hours in the oven for the pasta to soften.  This recipe is a keeper

Twice I have followed the recipe exactly.  It was perfect both times.  Don't change anything.  Ottolenghi and his team do a great job.  The sauce you make with roasted red peppers and the garlic mixture would be great to have on hand to put on a sandwich.  Brilliant flavor.

Delicious & recipe worked for me with 2 caveats. 1) I followed this tip: "soak no-boil lasagne noodles in warm water for 10 mins before tearing into the mixing bowl" & noodles were great; 2) I too added more milk to bechamel; 3) for my cooking sanity I mandolined squash & made pepper sauce the night before so prep was simple day of. Question: flavors were even better reheated day after. Does that mean I could assemble a day before I cook it & get richer flavor?  Or is there a better method?

This recipe was a lot of work for not a lot of payoff. I did like the red pepper sauce flavor, but the noodles only cooked if they weren't near the edge of the pan. I'm also not sure in what world this can all fit into a 9" pan - even using less (by weight) than the amounts listed, I had to use a 9x13" pan to fit it all in. If I was to make this again,I think pre-cooking the noodles and making this similar to a traditional lasagna construction would yield a better result.
Agreed!
I cheated and used peeled, sliced squash. I feel terrible but I don't have unpaid interns like Ottolenghi to do grunt work.
@Bob Hoover, don't feel terrible, feel terribly smart. Your suggestion is "nothing but net" to use a basketball analogy.

Fantastic and impressive. The hardest part was mixing it in the bowl. Add the broken noodles last. It did all fit in the 9 inch pan. I had to bake it for an extra 30 mins. Then it was ok to sit and heat up and add béchamel when time to eat. Even then it sat in the turned off oven for 30 mins and was great. Very forgiving with timing.
Any thoughts about making ahead (minus the bechamel) baking, then freezing? I’d add the bechamel after reheating. Really want to make this for a gathering next weekend but must be able to make ahead.
I would just refrigerate after assembling and then bake when ready, adding extra bake time since it's cold.

This was fantastic!  But I am obliged to ask that you NEVER begin a recipe like this with the words "Heat the oven to 400 degrees."  If I had done so, I would have wasted a huge amount of energy in the time it took to get the rest together, even with pre-prepping.  This recipe should state that the oven should be started right before the instruction "Transfer this mixture to your lined cake pan."  That allows plenty of time for the oven to get up to temperature.
Thank you for another winner!

I followed the recipe but my noodles on the bottom ended up charred and hard. I also thought it was a bit too salty. Feta and Parmesan are already salty so I should have followed my gut, but I added the amount called for to try the recipe as written.

This was great. Yes-time intensive, but worth it. I almost went without the bechamel, but went against my urge to cut corners and did make it--and in the end, found it a very important part of the flavors of the dish! 
This is not a budget pie, costing me at least $15 to procure all ingredients, but it has made about 5 servings which does make it a good value. I will make it again!
@so happy. I really liked this dish too

I used regular lasagna noodles, and assembled this in a generously buttered 3 quart Dutch Oven. It took maybe 15 minutes extra baking before the pasta was tender, but I attribute that to the fact that the dutch oven was completely filled (topped with parchment and then foil) before placing the lid on. Delicious, and actually, one of Ottolenghi's easier recipes. This would make a great vegetarian Thanksgiving entree.
Thank you so much for the advice!!! Virginia

Delicious but needs longer in the oven. 

I thought it was delicious and honestly pretty easy. It does take some time but most of that is baking time. I used a 10 inch springform and can’t see how it would have fit in a 9.  I also thought it had to bake a LOT longer than the recipe said to get noodles and squash cooked. Like another 30 minutes!

I followed the recipe, multiplied everything by 1.5 and baked in a 9.5x11.5 (not quite 10x13) pan, buttered, no parchment, covered with regular foil, not heavy duty.  It did not stick and was delicious. Eight people all loved it. But it took me almost two hours of prep to get it in the oven.  I don't know if I'm just getting too old and slow, or if that is typical.  Will make again, but allow time to have a cup of tea and read a few recipe comments on other recipes during the prep process.
To those who don't know of Ottolenghi, yes, his recipes aways take a lot of time. The takeaway? It is always worth it.

Be the sous chef you want to see in the world!  This was delicious and easy to put together by splitting the prep.  Using a mandolin in the morning, the squash sliced up perfectly thin and uniform.  Chop and mince some garlic then, too.  3/4 t of each ground cumin and coriander.  Could not find Aleppo flakes, but 1 1/2 t paprika and 1/2 t cayenne were good subs.  Precook the lasagna noodles at 5 minutes. A bechamel novice, had to guess at the thickness.  This was delicious!

This was absolutely delicious!! Even better on day 2. Shocking to say, I followed the recipe and it was easily done, although more of a weekend meal as it was somewhat time consuming.  Nothing fiddly about it though and totally worth the effort!
I drizzled a 1/2 cup of water over the top to give it a bit of extra moisture while baking. I baked it in an oval casserole dish with tight fitting lid. It was delicious!

I used vegan feta and parmesan in the pie, and made the bechamel with Earth Balance, extra creamy soy milk, and vegan parm. The pie sagged a bit once out of the springform pan. Next time I'll remove the foil and let the lasagna cook down for 10-15 minutes before adding the bechamel. The flavor was excellent. I especially liked the rough ground whole spices. I used a mandoline for the squash, and it self-layered beautifully. My vegan friends were delighted!

So good but needs a lot of adjustments. Used mandolin for butternut squash, soaked no boil noodles in hot water and cut up with scissors. Sauté spinach. Add chicken stock to mixture (way too dry without). 400 oven too hot (burnt edges) 350. Maybe steam butternut squash next  time. Fennel seed not coriander. Perfect for thanksgiving with adjustments. Put in buttered large gratin dish.
To me, this had no flavor and the only change I made to the recipe was crushed red pepper (reduced amount) instead of Aleppo.  Such a disappointment because it does take a lot of time to prepare.

Great flavors but FAR too complicated unless you have a well equipped kitchen and are looking to impress guests. I only tried it because I had a squash and lasagna noodles to use, however some noodles remained crunchy maybe because I did not have a springform pan? I also just used jarred spices not a mortar and pestle. Too messy and time consuming for average cook in tiny apartment kitchen. There are uch easier recipes for squash & pasta. I will be steering away from Yotam's recipes in future.
@DKQ…. No kitchen is too small…. Hug from a tiny kitchen
Is it possible to make this the day before and reheat the next day?

@Ali - my question exactly! It seems like a sturdy pie and I'm thinking of baking it half way, cooling/freezing, then bake the final stretch on Thanksgiving. Anyone out there have any experience with this?
Needs a little bit more salt.  Or, consider using more parm both in the bechamel and in the layers themselves.  Other than that, this is a very good dish and easy to make.  I'll be making it again.

Made this, following the recipe to a tee, for vegetarian main course on Thanksgiving. it was the most complicated and time consuming dish of the night, and so not worth the effort. I will not repeat it in any variation. If you're curious about the combination of flavors, just cube the butternut squash, roast it till done, then toss it with the other ingredients, drizzled with the bechemel. That'll do it. Btw, skip the lasagne noodles - there absolutely no point to them. My 2 cents....
Any thoughts on putting the bechemel sauce on top after forming the "pie" instead of adding it later?

My guess is that, if you put the bechamel on the uncooked pie, it will seep down into it and not form a layer on top. It might also be delicious, but you'd probably end up with a creamier/custardy pie.
Do the lasagne noodles have to be no boil kind, or regular?
Since the recipe has an hour + in the oven, I'm going to guess and say the regular lasagna noodles would work in that amount of time.
Could the Bechamel be made using a non-dairy substitute? My daughter is dairy intolerant.
Of course, Soy or Oatmilk would work fine. There are non- dairy cheeses that would also work in this recipe.

I always make bechamel sauce with soy milk as both my sons are allergic to a protein in cow's milk.  I then generally add grated goats cheese.  However have also replaced the goats cheese with a stongly flavoured vegan cheese on occasion and have found that i also works.  Good luck

I noticed you didn’t ask about substitutions for the cheeses; is your daughter allergic to milk proteins or intolerant of lactose? If it’s a lactose problem you can use a lactose free dairy milk. I prefer an ultrafiltered brand like Fairlife. The filtration removes the extra sugar generated by the breakdown of the lactose so it doesn’t have the sweet taste of most lactose free milk.
I wonder if you could use a wide, flat pasta like reginetti or mafalda? Don't feel like buying lasagna noodles and breaking them up if I could just use another type that I already have. Thoughts?
Just threw it in the oven using 1/2 box of elbows noodles. Fingers crossed it works.
Could this be made ahead and frozen? With or without the bechamel?
this was amazing.  flavor so unique.  worth the effort!

For  more a homey dish- swap out the jar of  red peppers for  homemade roasted peppers.  Easily kept in the fridge a day or 2 they are always a plus,  and my favorite vegetable  side or appetizer. Use here instead of jarred for depth of flavor.

Slice red pourers into 1/2  wide strips. Toss with olive oil & add salt and pepper.  Bake on a sheet pan at 350  for 40 min., or 325 for 50 min., or 315 for closer to an hour. The slower gentler method creates a more slow cooked version.

This was fantastic, the epitome of an autumn comfort dinner!  I was skeptical everything would fit into the pan but lots of smashing did the trick. Be sure that the squash slices are well separated from each other and mixed well with the other ingredients to avoid the occasional piece that is a bit too al dente. And definitely leave it for the full 20 minutes on the final bake. This came out and presented beautifully. A lovely dinner for guests served with just a simple salad.

I put this in a 9-inch square pan to no ill effect save for a less dramatic presentation. Would advise others who do the same to skip the parchment paper; mine got so soggy that it was difficult to extract it from the food. The bechamel on this is a real star!

I doubled the peppers and cooked it in a 9x9 square glass casserole dish (deep). Instead of doing all of the wrapping/unwrapping, I just took the top off. It set up well, came out clean, and the pieces held their shape. All in all, it's a pretty "low stress" lasagna recipe.
Really liked this.  Next time I'll make it in typical lasagna structure and pan instead of the springform and parchment paper.
If you don't like dealing with lasagna noodles you can stuff shells with the mixture...yum..you actually taste the filling better.

The mixing bowl should be humongous, or perhaps a repurposed very large pot. I could not find Aleppo Chile and used paprika and with a little cayenne instead. As the “lasagna” layers are made by the sliced butternut, the 8 ozs. of lasagna noodles could be replaced by any pasta. I used lasagna noodles. I used ground coriander and cumin (and reduced amount). It is fabulous. And time consuming. I think the squash could be cleaned and sliced the day before, and sauce made then as well

Fantastic recipe! I used oven-ready noodles, which worked well. I doubled the recipe and froze one for a future tasty meal. As well, I replaced the brown sugar with honey. All in all, very very good an$ versatile- the heat of the oven melds the various tastes beautifully!

It’s a showpiece dish, fun to make. But I wouldn’t do it again. If I did, I would not use the fresh basil; mine was “off” and gave an acrid flavor to the dish . I would slice my squash thinner. I’d add some tomato paste to the red pepper mixture for a richer flavor. The bechamel was oddly bland. It feeds a crowd, would be impressive for a dinner party. Letting it sit before cutting helps reveal the layers. Regular lasagna noodles cooked fine as long as they were coated with other ingredients.

I am not so crazy about béchamel in general ( and I am originally French !! )  so insteadI I just added a little heavy cream with grated gruyere and let it seep in and get golden on top.  It was sort of lighter  (even  though a little higher in fat ?! ) but really silky and delicious.
I skipped the bechamel sauce top layer and it came it just fine without it. I still cooked it for the entire time, though, an after it came out of the over I sprinkled some parmesan cheese on top.

Easy, so delicious and flavorful, and beautiful to look at. Served it on a cake platter. Reheats well, and is just as tasty cold. Baked it on Monday, ate it Wednesday, Thursday and Friday. Definitely take the time to grind your own spices!

A lovely recipe. It took a while to make but what do you expect from a lasagna. The taste was wonderful and comforting, but at the same time completely new to me. 

Of course we made changes. We used no cook lasagna noodles, they worked fine. Instead of the can peppers we used can tomato. I cut the pepper flakes from 2 tsp to 1 tsp and it was the limit of heat for our kids, but they still ate it. Cooked it in a dutch oven with lid, caramelized like a dream. 10 stars.
This a little fussy and would work better IMO assembled like regular lasagne. Flavor combo very tasty.
Just one word: delicious!!!

Made as written except I used what I had on hand which was ground coriander, regular red pepper flakes, the basil from my garden that I dried yesterday which was probably less than half of what was called for. I was concerned about the heat but with the bechamel to mellow it, it was fine. I also used a square glass pan. This is a keeper and maybe next time I can have the ingredients on hand to try it completely as written.
I forgot to mention  - I roasted two red peppers rather than using peppers from a jar.

I was skeptical, but if you follow this exactly, you will get a perfectly layered, nicely textured, beautiful torte with a caramelized crust and golden creamy top. Even without parchment, it released from my springform pan and held together for cutting. However, the cumin and red pepper flakes obliterated the taste of the squash, spinach and basil. Next time I’ll swap the cumin for sage and use only a smidge of red pepper flakes. I used goat cheese in place of feta and it worked great.
For the amount of time it took to make, not sure it is worth the effort… not very flavorful. Looks good but taste doesn’t measure up.

Cooked as directed. I definitely suggest using "No Boil" lasagna noodles! I cooked on the longer end of the directions and yet found a few noodles that were slightly more al dente than they should be.
Do folks have any ideas as to whether or not you could freeze this?

Based on other suggestions I opted for rectangular Pyrex (8x11). String cumin/coriander flavor and May reduce. Skipped bechamel, and came out fine, but top lasagna noodles were not fully cooked. Next time May throw 1-2C of marinara on top if I skip bechamel again.

Absolutely delicious and well worth the effort. There is a lot of prep, even with a mandoline it took about 40 minutes to get in the oven. Would emphasize the importance of wrapping the entire dish in foil while it bakes the first time. Needs all the steam to stay inside so the squash and noodles fully cook.

Made two necessary changes (didn’t have Aleppo pepper on hand, subbed gochugaru; chopped mature spinach instead of baby) and one voluntary (added sautéed & drained merguez sausage).

This is a magnificent dish.  Due to husband with need for low saturated fat, I substituted low fat Mozzarella for feta ( what a loss!) but everyone loved it.  I would cook the broken lasagna a bit next time before combining everything.  When you are mixing that huge pile of stuff ( I had to use a stew pot for that quantity), those sharp edges hurt your hands.  Also, there were still slightly uncooked pieces on top.  A little more of that red pepper sauce would have been nice too!

This is delicious.  I didn't have enough feta and used extra parm. I also used no boil lasagna noodles. It was also my first bechamel sauce ever. I'm 75! Completely delicious and great for leftovers. Have fun with this one

Made this fabulous dish almost exactly to recipe, subbing 1tsp red pepper flakes for the suggested Aleppo and using semi-dried basil from the store (8tsp semi-dried=1 cup fresh).
