It's a full hour of prep plus cleanup, and then the baking time and resting time..so three hours the true time. We prepped all of the ingredients we could the day before--chopping, peeling, measuring, slicing. Doing so made it easy the following day, although fridge reeked of the pre-prepped garlic despite ziplock!

Super yums! I didn't have red peppers cuz I don't really love them. So instead, I steamed surplus squash with some carrots and then pureed that with a healthy splash of balsamic vinegar. My 9" pan is not that tall and it was clear the ensemble wouldn't fit so I used a casserole pan instead and no regrets. I added ¼ cup water at 45 mins to soften the noodles, next time will add at the start. Definitely a time investment (3 hrs) but I'm looking forward to eating leftovers for the next few days!

Followed the recipe EXACTLY as written and it came out perfectly.  If you follow the directions carefully and weigh and measure the ingredients accurately, the result will look as pictured.  No problem fitting everything in the springform pan and it was easily removed after the specified cooling time.  The taste is as good as others have commented, and the red pepper sauce is spot on!!!

Though it changes the appearance slightly, I used precut butternut “noodles” from the produce dept at Whole Foods-  it saved all the hassle and time  of prepping a whole squash, and that can only be a good thing….
Loved this. The mixture fit fine inside my springform with 3-inch sides, with a little room to spare. In my springform with 2 3/4-inch sides, I think it would have been a very tight fit.
Wear an apron : ) things get messy!

Great but could have been better. I used no-cook noodles and they were the weak link. Next time would use fresh lasagna sheets from the deli. I agree the mixture was pretty dry and could use more of the delicious sauce. OTOH, the finished product, using my springform, looked remarkably like the picture and was perfect for vegetarian Thanksgiving. I felt the recipe was a little unclear on what was intended for lining and wrapping the pan, but the recipe worked regardless.

This turned out amazing. I veganized it: Miyoko’s cashew-baes mozzarella instead of the feta, béchamel with Miyoko’s vegan butter and oat milk. Mandolined the squash. 

I had left my 00 white flour at the family’s house over Thanksgiving, and all I had on hand was stone ground whole wheat! Still, not a problem. The whole wheat vegan béchamel tasted just as creamy and rich as any other kind. 

My pie needed 1.5 hours pre-béchamel. And it takes 3 hours. Totally worth it.
Good as written used clean dishpan for mixing. I would make in square ceramic pans next time. Would also use no cook lasagne just to be sure the noodles will be soft after an hour in the oven.

I made this to serve to guests tonight and before even tasting it, they asked how got so many thin layers. They loved it and I loved how great it looked once plated. I found the directions for lining and wrapping the spring form pan to be a little confusing until I realized I didn’t need to fit the parchment to the pan. Two pieces would make for easier removal when serving. I added a piece of parchment directly on top the lasagna to keep the foil from touching it. Excellent!

I made this last night and it was delicious!  The spice blend was wonderful.  I didn't have Aleppo flakes so I used ground paprika and cayenne.  I used the type of lasagna that requires cooking and, for the most part,  they came out al dente.  A few at the bottom were tough.  I will try no cook lasagna noodles the next time and see if I like it better.  I used a 9x9 square pan instead of the round springform pan.  Worked great.  A keeper!

Ottolenghi recipes always have such a beautiful and complex flavor. This did not disappoint. Used regular Barilla lasagna noodles. I didn’t have the whole spices so subbed ~ 3/4tsp ground of cumin and coriander. For Aleppo, I used a tsp of sweet paprika and half tsp red pepper flake. Wrapped really tight in foil and found the moisture from the spinach, basil, squash and sauce were perfect for cooking the noodles to a gorgeous texture. Baked with no parchment in a greased 9” square. Loved it!

This is delicious; I do agree the tinfoil/parchment paper steps are confusing. To me, "wrap all around" means the outside, but guessed I needed to steam the veg, so I covered the top tightly with foil but without parchment (which didn't seem to matter). I used "oven ready" noodles, and that was the right call. I accidentally used coriander seeds, plus 1 tsp ground coriander in place of the cumin, and still tasted amazing. I also roasted 4 bell peppers because I had them for jar, will make again!
I ended up having to put this in a dutch oven to bake. No way it was all going to fit in my 10x2 inch springform pan. But the dutch oven oven worked ok.

First time I used the Mandolin: squash slices were beautiful even sizes but I doubt as thin as 1/8th So baking a tad longer at lower temp; also accidentally used a sm can of tomatoes paste but that meant all vgiies and lasagna covered a bit; just 1 butternut squash but had to approx add to 2 bowls to mix ingredients so squash and noodles covered; result required 2 9x12 baking pans my small spring form would not have worked. Took a risk and used oven bake lasagna given my worry about low moisture

Great dish, I made it in a  large casserole dish and it worked out well.  I used goat cheese and some cheddar since that's what I had.  My husband is not a fan of feta, so it worked well.  I also just used some campanelle pasta which seemed easier than breaking up lasagna noodles.  Everyone loved it.

After one hour in the oven my squash wasn't cooked. I put it back in the oven without covering with foil for a few minutes, then thought, but what if it will finish cooking under the bechamel during the last 20 minutes? So  I took it out, put the sauce on, put it back at the higher temperature, but no. I ended up nuking our portions in the micro, which kind of worked. It tasted very good, though I do recommend a bit of ground nutmeg in the bechamel, but overall, not worth the time or effort.

Like many others, I was concerned about whether the lasagna would cook. So I soaked the broken pieces in very hot water for about 30 minutes while working on other instructions. Mixed all together in largest bowl I own, then packed into springform pan. It fit! Worked perfectly and was delicious.

use standard noodles.

i prefer a firm, sharp cheese to feta.

if you don't often prep butternut squash, budget another 20 minutes so as not to rush.

OK, nice recipe, good use of butternut squash. Probably better if you have help in the kitchen to chop veggies and clan pans. Precise planning expected if you use the oven to help peel the squash too.

All in all, my fav part of the recipe is the peppers/tomato paste/spices mixture. I think I could definetely adapt this part to a pasta gravy or a dip.

Loved the flavors in this, but I'm wondering where exactly I went wrong, because the majority of my noodles were undercooked. Lots of chewy and lots of uber-crispy; utter minority were perfectly cooked. Saw in notes that someone added water to the pepper mixture, so maybe I'll try that. Maybe wasn't compact enough? Maybe wasn't sealed tight enough? Baffled. But I'll give it a second go, as Ottolenghi recipes are so often a win for me.
I wouldn't add water but maybe cook it longer. Maybe it was not compact enough. When I make it again I am going to cook it at least 20 minutes longer before adding the bechamel.
Make sure you are using enough greens and mix well- they exude water and hydrate the noodles.
Love this recipe, but thought the bechamel was a waste. I usually just skip it, or sprinkle a little cheese on top.
I just made this for the third time. Adding a 1/4 C of water doesn’t hurt. Make sure to check that the squash and pasta are fully cooked. It might take up to one and a half hours. Love this recipe!

I attempted this last night, and it was a disaster. It took something like 2 1/2 hours and the stepwise addition of about 3/4 cup of water before the squash and the noodles softened. I had made two changes from the recipe: I only had a 7 oz of jar of roasted peppers, 5 oz less than called for, and used puréed sun dried tomatoes instead of tomato paste, as at the last minute I found we were all out of I at. Could that have been my problem? So many happy and successful comments here… I’m puzzled.

This was a lot of work, but so delicious.  It is everything wonderful and comforting about lasagna without the heaviness.  Goodbye traditional lasagna! Btw - I cooked it in a 10 inch round silicone pan.  Worked great.
If using GF lasagna noodles should they be precooked?? is there enough liquid for the GF noodles to soften in this dish?
@Ksp I used uncooked gf noodles, and it worked great.

Very tasty. Easier than it looks. I followed the recipe exactly. Definitely let the lasagna cool sufficiently before removing from the springform pan. Be careful in how you’re layering your dried lasagna noodles- in some areas where there were multiple layers they did not cook fully despite over an hour in the oven. However, other parts of the lasagna were fully cooked. Will make again!

Made this as a main dish for the vegetarians (just me) at Thanksgiving. Made with GF noodles, but otherwise as directed.  It definitely took longer than 2 hours from start to finish, but it came out great.  The non-veggies in the household enjoyed it as well.

This was a great Christmas Dinner this year, even though I accidentally used caraway seeds instead of cumin. I think that, as others have already said, this could be made in a casserole instead of a springform pan, and would accept other substitutions gracefully as well.
Followed the recipe exactly and took some advice from the comments. This was soooooo good!!!!

Great recipe!  Prepared this last night exactly as written.  It took an extra half hour of cooking to get the noodles softened enough before I put on the bechamel.  I also needed to add another half cup plus of milk to make the bechamel liquid enough. With those adjustments, it was terrific.  The 9 inch pan was very full, but all the stuff fit in.  Will do again.

I think this needs to describe whether or not you can use lasagne sheets that need to be pre-boiled or not. There’s so little liquid in this I had to add stock and bake an extra 30 minutes to get my lasagne passingly al dente. My own fault for not pre-boiling the sheets I suppose.
@Ryan… I used normal Lasagne noodles and mine wasn’t dry at all.
Sorry.  The layers were not done at one hour, the noodles were hard top inch or so.  I put on more tomatoe sauce and put it in the over for another 1/2 hour on 400.  Hope it works!!
Save a step and a bit of time by using Balkan ajvar, instead of the jar of red peppers.

This is an absolutely stunning recipe. I´ve never followed directions more carefully - but we are talking Ottolenghi here so -(just changed out the spices for fresh sage, thyme and oregano). Everything came together perfectly. The consistency  and random layering was just amazing and the flavour profile was perfect.The béchamel was the best I have ever made and covered the pie perfectly... not too thick, not too thin just beautiful. MAKE THIS! So easy and so good.
Yes! I've made this more than once and it works great for reheating. @Ali
@Lee Thank you! Did you reheat for a specific temp/time? I’m a bit of a novice cook. Thanks!

Stellar recipe, we made to take to a Thanksgiving meal as the vegetarian main it was both delicious and impressive looking. I used plain Greek yogurt mixed w/ an egg and the garlic as a sub for béchamel, which worked but with a longer bake time. Will for sure make again!

Made this yesterday. Its not complicated, but a bit of time, its not fast food. Followed it fairly close except used about third a cup of our homemade pesto instead of finding fresh basil in December.  Cooked it at least 15 minutes more to get noodles soft. Seriously was one of the most delicious vegetarian lasagna I have ever made. I did not want to stop eating it! Side salad of garden greens and sourdough bread, happy eating 😋
winner.  it's easy to make and very tasty. you could add cashews or some other kind of nut like extra to give it a little crunch.
The only item I can't easily get my hands on is the Aleppo Chile Flakes. Can I sub with Red Chile Flakes? Are adjustments needed, if so?
Nikki: Aleppo chili flakes are milder, so I'd start with a smaller amount of red chili flakes and add more if you like more heat.
I bought Aleppo pepper on Amazon. I find that I am using it in a lot of meals.
You can sub in Korean gotchugaru for the Aleppo pepper if you have that...I always have a huge bag of it in my freezer...it's a little bit less spicy and warmer tasting than Aleppo.

Which type of lasagne noodles should be used?
Can this be frozen?  At what step?
What about the bechamel sauce??
Not good. Bummer. The flavors were off, it took longer than an hr to cook the initial step, kind of gross.

Could you make the pie without the Aleppo chile flakes?  I am very sensitive to any food that’s even the slightest bit spicy.  Some foods that I find to be too hot, barely register as even the slightest bit spicy to other people.

I wouldn’t want to make this delicious sounding recipe only to discover that the tiny bit of heat from the Chile flakes has ruined my full enjoyment of what I am sure would be a great culinary experience.
No matter how you slice it, the Aleppo pepper isn't going to work for you. Try the recipe without it. It won't be worse than a bad reaction to the dish with it.
Of course you can omit the chile, it won’t change the cooking process. Use whatever alternatives you prefer. Experiment and enjoy!
Confused about the type of lasagna noodles to use? Please clarify. Do we par boil the noodles first?
Maybe make two pans. One a variation.
To cut down on fat and calories, would this work with less bechamel or none at all?
Way easier to make Giada’s wonderful butternut squash lasagna.
What's not to love here? I never boil lasagna noodles ahead of time, but I thin the sauce a bit (red wine, water, or broth) and the noodles steam quite nicely under foil. I'm lazy.
Can I use acorn squash instead of butternut? We have a huge crop which I need to use.

I made this last night, and I have two important observations to share. First, I would use less basil next time. 1 full cup of basil makes this VERY fragrant. Second, I used the exact measurements of all ingredients, and I needed TWO very large bowls to combine everything.

I love finding a new Yotam Ottlenghi recipe simply because I know I can trust the engineering behind it--and the delight! I made this exactly as written for a small gathering last night. I substituted a few Szechuan peppercorns for the Aleppo chili flakes, baked in a 9x9" square metal pan buttered and lined with parchment to make for easier slicing/serving. It was perfection! A very surprising array of textures from the 'broken' approach, seeds from the drained peppers and toasted spices +flavor
A small food processor? Sorry, I just have a big one. Would an immersion blender work?
Oh. And any tips for slicing the butternut squash?

The instruction to line the springform pan with a piece of parchment paper large enough to cover the bottom and hang over the sides by a couple of inches doesn't make any sense if the pan is round.  Going with a 9" square.

Prepared yesterday, mixed all ingredients together to coat all ended up a large mess. Didn't care for the spice combination plus too much garlic was overpowering. Was a beautiful outcome. I did use a springform pan.

Matters not, really, what size pan you use. I did not use a springform, opting instead to just bake it in my usual small straight-sided oblong casserole dish. It served very easily. I also had 3 bunches of turnip greens, and used them instead of spinach. Very yum all the way through.

I found that the butternut squash needed more time in the oven.  I cooked it one day for 45 minutes and then refrigerated; made the béchamel sauce the next day, reheated for 15 minutes at 400 and then added the sauce for an additional 20 minutes.  The squash was not cooked enough.  Did anyone else have this problem?
I would imagine 45 minutes is not enough--I ended up cooking it (before adding the béchamel) for an hour and 15 minutes.
How do you slice the squash that thin?  I’m thinking use a vegetable peeler or a mandolin.  Advice welcome.

I just used a nice sharp chef's knife, no mandolin necessary.  It really only has to be about as thing as the lasagne noodles, they end up about the same texture.  Just one note--I cooked it for an extra 15 minutes and the noodles were perfect.  They weren't quite there after an hour

Stab it with a fork here and there and nuke it in a damp paper towel for a couple of minutes.  This makes it easier to slice precisely, especially if you take a bit off of one side to make it stable first.  To save more time, you can buy a larger-than-recommended butternut and use only the seedless portion for the recipe, stopping when your slices equal about a pound.  (The remainder of the squash you can seed and chop at your leisure.)
This is amazing.  Just made it tonight, came out perfect, can't stop eating it!  Absolutely delicious.

Vegan version was delicious and presented beautifully!! I used previous commenter Ellen K's walnut-san recipe (best nut-based fake parmesan I've ever made), Violife feta, almond milk for the Bechamel, purple kale and whole grain lasagne noodles. While weighing ingredients learned that 1 whole grain lasagne noodle = 1 ounce.

Just went out and bought a springform cake pan.  Wish I would have read this first.  Read the article in the paper paper.  No comments there.  I have two spring
Form pans already, both with the center in them.  Now I have one plain.

This is a stunning and fabulous recipe! We made it in a parchment lined cake pan - the parchment made it easy to lift out onto a plate - and watching the process was part of the fun! We were pretty true to the recipe—it was simple to make, and the melding of the flavors was delectable. We simply can’t wait to make it again for friends and family! Definitely a favorite and first choice for an impressive meal.
I’m scared of cutting a butternut squash, would substituting sweet potato here work instead?

I wouldn't make this again. It was a lot of work (and many dishes to wash) and didn't come out that well. Some of the lasagna noodles were undercooked, and I didn't think the flavorings blended very well (coriander, cumin, basil, feta ...). It was also very hard to mix. And I don't know where to find a 12 oz jar of red peppers.
Sorry to ask, but spouse can't eat the bechamel. Any ideas for another (non-cheesy) topping?

The Guardian published this recipe several years ago - everything pretty much the same but no bechamel. A quarter of the ricotta and feta is reserved to top the pie, cover with foil to bake and after an hour, remove foil and top with OO til browned and crispy, about 30 minutes. Sprinkle with parsley.
What is "top with OO"?

This is a great recipe, but, it does take a lot of time. I do agree that a springform pan is not necessary, although it does make for a nice presentation. Both the spinach and the fresh basil do turn dark, which might be off-putting to some. But, it is delicious. 

You do need a really, really big bowl or something large.  I used my largest bowl and was still dropping noodles and spinach out and putting them back in.
has anyone tried this recipe with cubed butternut squash?
yes - we used pre-cooked butternut squash from the store (already cut into cubes). Worked just fine (max 1 cm by 1 cm).

I made this with No-boil lasagna from TJ’s. I had kale and ripped it into smallish pieces, as suggested.
It was amazing, and even better the next day. The warmth of the pepper (I subbed Korean pepper) and coriander made it so satisfying.
Cook longer, about 75-90 minutes, for squash to be done.

We loved this and I'll be making it again and again. Sometime I may sub tomatoes for the roasted red pepper or combine the two to make 15 oz. Per someone else's suggestion, I cut up my spinach as it wasn't baby enough which was a great idea. Regular boxed noodles worked just fine.

Delicious! This recipe is a keeper - well worth the preparation time!  We made a few modifications: caraway seeds for the cilantro seeds and a jar of sun-dried tomatoes for the jar of red peppers. Also baked it in a regular pan without any issues. Many thanks to Ottolenghi for the inspiration!
I'm a great fan of Ottolenghi's recipes but this one disappointed me. The combination of sweet squash and the spicy garlic pepper sauce didn't seem to meld. The no-cook lasagna sheets worked fine.
Can this be made a day ahead?
I think it would take even better cooked a day ahead of serving....like a chili or soup.....

Made this with the noodles that are supposed to be boiled. Didn't seem like there was enough liquid in the recipe, so added about one cup. The noodles were still doughy. Also baked it in a rectangular pyrex dish- it was too much to fit in the 9 inch springform, and I was afraid liquid would leak out the bottom of a springform.

I used a glass baking dish and it worked just fine. This was so delicious. I had my doubts about the noodles, but they were perfectly cooked. It was more than well worth the time it took to peel and slice the squash. Next time I'll double it so I have one to freeze.

Question. Does one use no boil or dried lasagna? I would think no boil but do you dip in warm water first because there does mot appear to be a lot of sauce in this recipe. 1/8 inch slices of butternut squash? Hand cut? Other ideas?

This was a fair amount of work for a weeknight dinner and I ended up disappointed with the end result. With all the spices and flavors going in, I expected the taste would be amazing, but I’m afraid it ended up rather bland. Maybe I just needed to significantly increase the salt. Mixing all the ingredients in a large bowl was a big mess, the parchment at the bottom of the pan got very soggy and fell apart as I tried to lift the end result off the pan.  Bechamel was flavorless.
If you go for grounded cumin and koriander instead of seeds, how much should you put in?

I made this for this first time last night and it is a winner. We loved it. I mainly followed the recipe except I used gruyere instead of feta and used less Aleppo chile flakes, since they seemed pretty hot (and sadly hubby doesn't like food that's too spicy). Also, I used a 10x10 baking dish instead of a springform pan and it came out just fine. As others have said, this seems like an adaptable and forgiving recipe, I can envision trying different combinations of greens and cheeses, for example

I find the choice  the  Béchamel layer really uneccesarry and a bit of a downer on this modern take of Lasagne. So, I created Parmesan cheese on the top. I also only put in a teaspoon of Chile flakes. Two teaspoons of chili flakes would burn my face off...but maybe my hot pepper flakes are really hot...Turned out delicious...

Very forgiving and adaptable, delicious recipe! Used a glass baking dish instead - turned out lovely. Tore the spinach leaves and left out long stems. We had some leftover ground beef, so I cooked it in v. small pieces with white onion and kofta spices and mixed it in. Pre-blanched the noodles bc we used gluten free, but needn’t have! Use a GIANT bowl for mixing, I ended up using my hands. First time making béchamel was fine, but next time I might just top it with more feta.
Has anyone frozen and reheated this?
Why grease the pan, when you are lining it with parchment?

Just made this recipe tonight with a few spice adjustments and it was delicious! Saw the previous post about using Gruyère instead of feta and was all in! Not a huge fan of feta and my partner does not really like cumin or coriander. Used Penzey’s Greek blend in place of the two with Gruyère and baked in a Pyrex deep casserole dish. Perfect! Ran out of Parmesan and substituted grated provolone for the bechemel and it was absolutely delicious! Love improvising and solid recipe!

I can’t believe how well this turned out. 
I used a mandolin to cut the squash, so the slices were more 1/4 of the width of the squash than half.

This is really good. Consider making more béchamel than it calls for. And agree with the comments that you will need more milk than this calls for. We added pepper to the béchamel and were happy with that, and also went heavier on the garlic. Our noodles also ended up a little al dente in places - maybe we needed to mix better. But a really good vegetarian main dish. We will make again.

Re recipe times: I alway add AT LEAST 30-45 minutes to any recipe I read. The times are designed for a chef who has a prep team providing all the prepped ingredients.  I think that the recipe writers/editors know that if they were honest about the time the recipes actually take a home cook to produce,  people wouldn't make them. Years ago the Times had a column by Pierre Franey called 60 Minute Gourmet. THOSE really did take only 60 minutes. Nostalgic!
Prep time is never included in a recipe. Write that down and memorize it!
Added mushrooms browned in butter and olive oil.  Roasted the squash beforehand. Delicious.
I never comment on recipes, but this one was delicious! Followed recipe as written, except the aleppo pepper- used my homemade flakes. A bit of work but so worth it!!
I used swiss chard instead of spinach, since the swiss chard went crazy this year in the garden. Also substituted a sheep fresh cheese for the feta, since my family does not like feta. Tasted great.

I ended up using my turkey roasting pan to stir all the ingredients. I was able to get it all into my springform pan. Keep in mind that it will be hot when you unwrap the foil so make it easier by leaving an end on top.

My biggest bowls couldn’t accommodate the ingredients and I ended up using the small bowl of my sink. I couldn’t get it to fit in a 9inch pan and ended up using a 9x13. So I could have used a bit more béchamel. 
It turned out great, I love the flavors.

Also asking about the lasagna noodles so I am just using regular lasagna noodles. My "pie" is in the oven as we speak. Just FYI there is no way mine was going to fit into a spring-form pie pan, I just used a 9x13 baking dish. The presentation will not be there but assuming it will taste delicious.

Notes: I made it and family enjoyed it, BUT I used a jar of Trader Joe's Autumnal Sauce (sorry Kenji) in place of making the sauce and it was still great. Also, skipped bechamel for thick layer of broiled mozzarella, no complaints.

This recipe is beyond stellar in flavor and worthy of all the steps!! I used dried cumin and coriander powder, frozen spinach , cut butternut squash thinly into pieces and didn’t worry about shape, only had 1/2 cup of Feta, so tossed in cheddar to make a cup, added 1 small zucchini cut into slices as well, and used gluten free lasagna noodles broken into big bits ( just half a box). I did have a springform pan and the lasagna came out beautifully when sliced! Superb!

This recipe is beyond superb! Used ground cumin and coriander, frozen spinach,1 small zucchini and 1/2 box gluten free lasagna noodles. Sliced butternut squash thinly and cut into pieces. Baked in springform and voila! Beautiful layered lasagna! This is a keeper!
Use the oven ready lasagna noodles.  Regular noodles were a disaster.  Recipe overall disappointing.
Halved the recipe and cooked it in a loaf pan. Cooked it for the recommended 60min, everything was cooked perfectly. Dont skip the béchamel.

Completely inedible and quite a lot of work.  The butternut squash and the (no boil) noodles were crunchy and raw after an hour and fifteen minutes at 400-450 degrees.  I followed the recipe precisely except I didn’t have Aleppo pepper flakes.

Also my very large bowl and 9” springform dish were nowhere near large enough to accommodate the ingredients.  I followed the recipe carefully and what a headache, what a mess, all for a dinner I dumped straight into the compost bin.  Total fail.

This was a fail as well. Squash was mostly raw. We followed the recipe exactly and our oven is at the correct temperature. A huge disappointment. If I tried this again would cook it at least 90 minutes pre-Bechamel.

This is delicious!  Healthy! Vegetarian!  
It holds up well in the fridge so makes great leftovers.
My only suggestion is to not get worried when you see the huge bowl of ingredients. Trust the process!   It will fit in the springform pan.  Just smash it down!
The dried lasagna noodles soften perfectly, there is no need to pre-cook.

I made this per the recipe and it turned out well. Used regular lasagne. The huge amount of ingredients does fit in the 9" springform. The only change was using delicata squash. My husband loved it, but for me, I was a little surprised that for a Yotam recipe, the flavor was not as bold as I thought it would be, maybe it's just me.
First time making this recipe, loved it, cumin seeds were a new ingredient for me, great discovery, thank you!
Just ok given all the effort.  Wish there was a way to make it a bit more saucy and not so dry.
I made this as written this evening and it was excellent! I was skeptical about the assembly but it worked out so well. I used a serrated knife to slice the squash and it didn't take long at all.

I did not enjoy this very much. The method of assembly (tossing everything together with your hands) is messy and the mixture is hard to distribute evenly. Overall, with that amount of squash it leans too sweet for me and the spices did not add enough oomph. (I did substitute paprika for hot pepper for the sake of certain guests.)

Delicious! I made this gluten free by using 12 no boil gf lasagne noodles (all I could find) and substituting 1:1 gf flour for wheat flour in the béchamel. As others have said, trust the process: it really all does fit in the springform. Other than the gf tweaks, I followed the recipe, and bake times are accurate. I did slice my squash quite thin which I think is key to avoiding raw pieces. All three kids (ages 5, 3, and 15 mos) ate it!

I did the substitutions to make this vegan. It was delicious. But I ended up cooking it in a 9x13 pan to fit it all in.  Should have increased the bechamel to account for the increased surface area.  Perhaps because of that, and I had it sealed but not super tight, it came out dry. Will definitely try this again with some modifications.

I love this! Made adjustments based on what I had on hand - thawed frozen spinach vs fresh; 1 tsp each of ground cumin & coriander vs whole; and it still worked beautifully. Will definitely have it again.

I made this following almost all directions, which i rarely do. But subbed honey for brown sugar. My husband and i every bite we took, “oh this is excellent.”  Will definitely make again. Used regular dry lasagna noodles. Plenty of moisture to cook them over long cook time.

Roughly crushed coriander yielded too many intensely flavored chunks and not enough overall flavor. Sauce overwhelmed other ingredients (like feta) and 8 ounces of pasta proved too be insufficient for the vast quantity of 1/8 inch thick butternut squash slices.

I’m a fast cook, and this took a solid 3 hours, including the prep time, and not including the cooling off time. During the cooking time, I had to clean up the mess created by the prep, which took almost the whole hour. The result was tasty, but not that tasty.

This was delicious! Trust the process. Used a springform pan, subbed spinach for 6 ounces of arugula as that is what I had on hand. Also only had 6 ounces lasagna noodles. Otherwise followed recipe exactly. Fantastic vegetarian main dish! The béchamel is key, rounds out the dish and the extra cooking time ensures the squash is perfect! Will make again.

This was a winner at our place. Best butternut lasagna I've ever made. I used a kabocha squash slightly larger than a typical butternut, so I baked it in a 9x12. Some members of the family don't like feta so I used queso fresco which worked well. Can't wait to try it again with feta. They admitted that they probably would have been fine with it after tasting all the other wonderful flavors. I only used half the pepper flakes and it had nice heat and flavor.
I totally agree with Gary. The recipe took longer than 2 hours! But it is delicious. The perfect meal for a cozy Sunday dinner.

This joins a several other Ottolenghi recipes in the dinner winners folder.
I'm glad I had a food processor with this one. It definitely made prep quick, especially 1/8 in squash slices. I think people had questions about the noodles. I boiled mine according to the package, then got handsy with all the ingredients in the biggest bowl I had. It was too small, so I switched to a large pot. Everything else went by the book and it turned out great.
Great recipe, but leave plenty of time for prep and maybe back off on the pepper flakes a bit. I like heat (and love Aleppo), but this didn’t need as much as the recipe called for.
can this be prepared a day in advance.

I made this with rice lasagna and wheat lasagna noodles and they bother were great.  I used the regular kind, not precooked.  The only thing is, don't let any noodles stick up out of the sauce, or they turn rubbery.

A question:  I like to roast fresh red peppers as opposed to getting them out of a jar, cheaper and fresher.  My question is what cup measurement or gram measurement would be the equivalent of the peppers from a 12 oz. jar?  I've looked at other ingredients I have in the refrigerator that come in jars, i.e. olives, kimchi, relish.  Some jars indicate fluid oz. some dry weight oz. Any help would be appreciated.
Tough one, but I used a 12.5 ounce jar of red peppers, and it turns out the were cut in half. I'm thinking there were maybe 3 or 4 red peppers to fill that jar.

Really, really tasty, with nice textures, but was it worth all the time and effort? Probably. As others have noted, it could be simplified. If I make it again, I’ll just put it in a greased pan and serve it from the pan. I will also trust that the contents will fit in the pan — the height of the pie reduced by at least one third while cooking. And my biggest bowl wasn’t quite big enough for mixing the contents, so I will look for a bigger one.

I followed this recipe, only adding some arugula to augment the spinach.  Based on the baking times, it wasn't enough to fully cook either the butternut squash and definitely not the raw lasagna noodles.  I had to steam it in a skillet with some water to get it done enough to salvage for a dinner.  Glad I didn't have guests!

Followed the recipe exactly, however you need an extremely deep pan to accommodate this recipe. Also next time I would cook the noodles since they came out a bit too chewy.  Not enough cream sauce either—barely covered to top of the pie. Great flavors.

I used the Barilla no-boil lasagne and they worked fine.
We loved this recipe!
Can I build the lasagna in the pan and place it in the refrigerator until the next day?
I should think so though I would bring it to room temp before baking. I made it at 2 in the afternoon and wrapped it and put it in the fridge before moving it to the oven around 5:30.
Love Ottolenghi's recipes, BUT what a mess! Not sure this method was better than just layering. Or shredding the squash.  Also, what does the bechamel sauce really add besides another step?

How much of this, if any, could be prepared in advance? I’d like to serve it as a vegetarian side for Thanksgiving when oven space is a premium (Obviously the bechamel step would not be done in advance)
Lisa,  I assembled everything the night before and refrigerated it, plopped it into the oven late afternoon the next day, and followed the balance of the recipe.  Worked fine.

You can certainly make the red pepper sauce and hold it over until you mix all the ingredients. Also, microwave your whole squash for about 4 minutes to soften it. That will make it easier to slice. You could also pre-grate the cheeses.

I’d like to hear more about the recipe that had ricotta and feta on top. I’m planning on making this low carb and omitting the noodles and adding Italian chicken sausage instead. I think it will be yummy. The feta and ricotta will decrease the carb count

I really wanted to love this, as every ingredient is one I love, but I won't make it again. My husband liked this a lot more than I did, but as far as I'm concerned it's a fair amount of effort for low pay-off.  I couldn't really taste the squash, the noodles cooked unevenly, béchamel was a bit bland, and I felt like the cumin and coriander dominated too much; we ground them in a mortar and pestle; I wonder if their flavors would have been subtler if I'd ground them mechanically.

Very surprisingly good!   The squash lent a nice creaminess, and not an overpowering sweetness, as I feared.
I have picky family, who sneered at the idea. But once they tasted it, a pleasant surprise came to them.  It has a good balance of sweet, salty, and pungent,  The fresh basil is a marvelous taste in this lasagna.
We have a lot of extra swiss chard in our garden (spinach has been done for a good while), so a substitution occurred.  NICE!
Delicious!! I followed the recipe exactly and it was fantastic!
In no works would this fit into a pie pan. I used a big rectangular regular lasagna pan - barely fit. Used a mandolin for the squash.

I don’t like a lot of cumin so next time I make this, I’ll reduce the cumin by half. The noodles were a bit too Al dente. I might try egg noodles next time as someone suggested. Overall a great veggie meal.
Does anyone have any thoughts whether I could freeze this ahead of time and reheat?
I would par cook the lasagna next time. Stayed too hard. Nor would I use a springform pan again. Just a square pan would suffice.
Question for those of you who have made the recipe:  did anyone have an issue with the parchment burning during the final 20 minutes at 450F?
Not a fan of this recipe. Maybe just too many conflicting ingredients?  I really wanted to like it, but it confused my palate. Noodles were to al dent, as others have said.

I wouldn't make this again. For one thing, there was way too much squash. The dish was nothing special and there are lots of much better (and easier) one-pan pasta recipes out there. I did all the prep the day before and I layered all the ingredients in the bowl which made them very easy to combine. I used regular boil-first noodles and they were not cooked when I removed the foil so I added 2 T of water and covered the dish for another 20 minutes then proceed per recipe.
Is it possible to make ahead and freeze this squash lasagna?

Absolutely love this! I didn’t have a 9” square springform pan, so I just used my glass 8x8x2 pan, and it worked out well. After the hour bake, however, the noodles weren’t done because there wasn’t a lot of liquid. I added some chicken broth and then topped it with the béchamel and baked, and it turned out beautifully. Definitely a keeper!

I don’t always appreciate a béchamel but this was so light and fluffy it almost gave the impression of mashed potatoes.  I’m not sure I love the roasted red pepper enough to make this again, but for a cold Sunday night supper this warmed me up.

This is an extremely labor-intensive recipe (read: time-consuming), but I’d probably make it again since I purchased a springform cake pan to do it! (Maybe I’ll press it into service for cakes, although I am not really a baker.) The dried pasta was still a *little* crunchy on the edge of the pie; I’ll try fresh pasta next time. Also I liked the idea of roasting cumin and coriander and sprinkling it on top of the béchamel - those flavors were a little lost mixed in with everything else.

Made this as directed except for using vegan cheeses (yay! for vegan cheese). It was just okay. Somehow it is missing something that gives it more punch. The sauce was so delectable before adding it to the other ingredients, but then it just disappeared. I'll try it again sometime, using porcini mushrooms or chicken or something to give it more oomph. One caution: it says to use a large bowl to mix all the ingredients together. Think: punch bowl.

Delicious! Made it exactly as written, it had a lovely warming spicy flavor and the sweetness of the squash and the chew of the noodles. Yum! Ours needed an additional 15 minutes in the oven before the béchamel because the middle butternut squash still had too much of a bite to it (test with a knife). Of course this recipe could be simplified but then it wouldn’t be an Ottolenghi recipe. This dish was even better the next day for breakfast with a fried egg on top! Truly fabulous the next day!

A delicious entree. The spice mixture was incredible. I cooked it in the suggested 9” springform pan. But I have to say the butternut squash was not cooked enough in the allotted time. Nor were some of the lasagna noodles. I used the regular lasagna noodles. The recipe either needs a longer cooking time or more liquid to get the squash fork tender and all of the lasagna noodles cooked enough. Maybe covering the pan with foil would help during the first hour of baking.
From Step 3: "Using heavy-duty aluminum foil, wrap the cake pan all around until tightly sealed, place on a baking sheet and bake for 1 hour."

Mmmm MMMMM! Made this only due to an acid comment on the infamous Ombré Gratin recipe—I mean, after tackling that I still had an abundance of mandolined squash leftovers ready to go.

Used “no-prep” noodles. Put this into a cold oven and went for a walk for 1.25 hours. When we returned, it was a bit dry on top but roughly cooked. Après-béchamel, any dryness vanished.

It wasn’t simple but it also was far from the most complicated recipe I’ve ever made. And it was utterly delicious!

This is an impressive recipe; it requires little finesse and is delicious.  I can only add that leftover pieces or even a frozen piece, warmed in a microwave, held up perfectly.  This is one sturdy dish.

Great recipe.  Regarding the lasagna noodles, I used “no boil” and added 1/4 c water to the bowl before mixing it all and layering-  cooked up perfectly.  I used a mandolin to assure 1/8” squash slices, it all came out perfectly.  This has great flavor, a perfect vegetarian main.

I made this recipe and it turned out fine but not foolproof. I didn’t have whole spices so used the full measurement of ground instead and it was definitely too much. I also used gluten free lasagne sheets which normally cook well in lasagne and acorn squash sliced thinly but both were undercooked after the full cooking time. The cake tin also seemed a bit unnecessary, I think it would work well baked in a normal lasagne/pie dish to save the extra hassle of lining and taking it out afterwards.
This was SO good! I had to cook it longer for the get the pasta sheets cooked through. It was a good use for my garden grown sweet potatoes.

I had  jarred roasted  tomatoes which I used in lieu of jarred red peppers. No fresh basil to be had but worked out fine with out.  I cut the squash in half and cooked cut side down in microwave to ease in peeling .
Use a food processor with slicing blade to cut squash. Quick, easy and makes uniform slices of hard veg(use it for onions and potatoes in other recipes when lots of slicing is needed (gratins etc).

Left out the cumin&coriander and added some Herbes de Provence to the squash, pesto in place of fresh basil and some fresh thyme & rosemary to the bechamel. A food processor to slice the squash made quick work of it. I was trying to rush, so spread it in a 9x13, increased the bechamel to 2 cups milk (and the rest of the ingredients proportionally) and I used no-boil lasagna noodles.   I was able to get it baked at 425 deg in 45 minutes.  It was delish. I'll do it in the springform next time.
I made this in a 9x13” pan.  Great dish to feed a group.  I think it would still taste great without the bechamel sauce.

This was so so good. It was labour intensive and I was concerned it wouldn't turn out but was pleasantly surprised. I made the bechamel with oat milk and subbed garlic powder and nutritionl yeast in the sauce. Definitely would make again

He is a great chef 
I make all his dishes , this one seemed very interesting, and it sure was 
Just follow exactly and it will be great
Can this be frozen?

Loved this. Made vegan by subbing violife for feta, olive oil for butter, and homemade walnut Parmesan. I also used dry noodles instead of lasagne.  I was worried it was going to be dry so added maybe 1/2 cup water before baking. Everything turned out and I’d definitely do again.

This was good but very rich. It’s kind of a Middle Eastern version of lasagna. A little like a vegetarian moussaka. I made it in an iron skillet and it held together well when cut into pieces for serving. I used no-boil noodles and added a lot of water and still had to turn the top noodles under after an hour baking. I even added more water before adding the béchamel and it was not at all soupy. Not sure it wouldn’t have been just as easy - or easier - to layer as usual.

I use Aleppo all the time and would recommend cutting the quantity in half as two teaspoons is aggressive for most palates. My one springform pan was in use, and I didn't have a nine-inch square pan, so I used a regular 9 X 13 lasagna pan and, while obviously the lasagna didn't stack as high, the essence of the dish was still there and still looked elegant when served to company. Next time I will just roast a whole butternut squash and just scoop out the insides.

This is a great recipe! I thought it would be too complicated but it wasn't- just a few steps really. We ate it all despite the fact that because I did not slice the squash all as thinly as I should have, some parts were not done enough. It was unclear whether one was supposed to use the regular lasagna noodles or the no-boil ones, but it didn't seem to matter- we used regular and it was fine. Wonderful flavors and textures.
I am not sure how anyone fit this in a 9-inch round pan. I barely got this in a 9x13 rectangle!
Make your hand into a fist and layer after layer press,press, press down hard using your knuckles and and folded fingers. As Ottolenghi says, you want to hear the noodles cracking and breaking.
Delicious! I really love that Yotam’s recipes tend to use such a high proportion of vegetables to starch. 6 servings is ambitious for a dish this tasty.
Delicious! Unique! Easy! …the only thing is I have a hard time getting the parchment , under the pie, out of the way to serve. Any tips? Thank you-
This is an absolute winner of a recipe.  It seems like a lot of mix, but will fit in the springform when pressing down. Would work well in other pans as well.

Used no bake noodles and  MOST of it was just perfect -- however wherever 2 noodles stuck together they didn't properly soften.  As nice as it is to just through everything into the sauce and press into the mold, I will make noodle layers next time.   The first time I made it as written, and the 2nd time used ricotta/cottage cheese/egg for the topping and baked covered until the last few minutes. - its a nice shortcut.   The recipe lends itself to any number of variations.

Definitely not a weekday night dish to make. As others have noted, I didn't think the entire mixture would fit into the springform pan, but, it did. I sliced the squash too thickly, I think, so next time will do better with that. I used whole-wheat lasagne noodles; after the hour was up I had to add a little water and bake it for about 15 more minutes. I used ground spices-about 1 tsp. cumin and 1/2 tsp. coriander.

I used fresh pumpkin instead of butternut squash
And replaced spinach with Swiss chard
It was delicious

Used a combo of fresh, cubed butternut squash (later sliced in the food processor) and a package of defrosted frozen butternut spirals (drained). Baby spinach comes in a 5 oz. Package, basil leaves come in a 6 oz. Package, so I used those. No jarred red peppers in my store, so I just cut up a fresh red bell pepper. Used dried lasagna noodles. 
No springform pan, so I used a deep casserole dish with the same volume.
It all worked and tasted good.
Not weeknight meal.
So amazingly delicious.  We added a touch more milk the second time around.  Will make again!
Perfect recipe! I made it for 35 persons and everybody loved it! Thanks

Was never going to fit in my springform tin! Baked it in a Dutch oven instead. Used combo of tinned tomatoes & home roasted capsicums. Needs more moisture to cook all the pasta. Chilli was too mild so I added crushed chilli & used Kashmiri chilli next time around. Recipe needs much more milk than listed to get right consistency.
I've only made this once, but it was a show-stopper!  My guests loved it!  Delicious vegetarian entree!

This was a miss for me.  I thought I would love it based on the ingredients and reviews,  but something just didn't work. I didn't find it hard to assemble and I might do his spring form idea with a more traditional lasagna flavor. I just really disliked the flavor. I didn't have a problem with the bake or anything.  I sliced the squash very thin and it baked as planned.
It’s a super delicious recipe - but noway you can do it in 2 hours…
I’d love a variation without dairy, soy/legumes or walnuts .  Ideas?
Wonderful recipe! lot of work though. I ‚outsourced’ preparing the butternut to my husband. We used our electric ham slicer for slicing. Superfast and
Wonderful recipe! But a lot of work. I ‚outsourced‘ the butternut peeling and slicing to my husband. We used our electric ham slic
I took a less fussy route- shallow covered cast iron casserole, I did out foil on before the lid. I used a 7 oz. box of hopped spinach, thawed and drained instead of fresh.
Add water!
I followed the recipe exactly and we all absolutely loved it! It was savory and delicious!

Honestly, not a winner for me. Not a failure either. The flavours could be duplicated by tossing roasted squash with all the other ingredients, cooking and broiling with bechamel. The fussy presentation adds nothing. The abundance of basil, etc. get fully overpowered by the sweetness of the squash. I’ve been cooking for 30 years and am glad I tested this on my parents before a dinner party as I wouldn’t make it again. Way too much effort for the result.
Am thinking of using this as a veggie main for Thanksgiving. Could you prepare up til the bechamel and refrigerate for a few days and then reheat and top?
Should the jar of red peppers contain vinegar or not?
I use wonton wrappers in place of lasagna noodles in all my lasagna recipes and they work beautifully! They are thin, delicious and do not require any cooking in advance.
Agree w/ Ryan: can anyone clarify if I should use the "no boil" lasagna noodles or the standard boil noodles? I'd love to add this to our holiday table!!!
@Virginiano boil is fine. Mine turned out beautifully
Any suggestions for replacing the béchamel ?

Good flavor, but butternut was not soft enough after cooking the prescribed times, even after putting it back in for an extra 30 minutes. If we make it again (thank you CSA winter squash abundance), we’ll try reducing the amount of squash and slicing even thinner.
This was definitely not my jam. I didn't like the flavors and found it kinda spicy. I won't make it again
When should the feta be added?!??
@Eric, looks like step 3.

So very delicious!  I wasn't sure how all those layers of raw squash would cook but they did, the bechamel browned beautifully, and it was truly scrumptious! Made it for a potluck and it was gone before I could get seconds so I made it again the next day!

Followed recipe and it was delicious and beautiful. No changes suggested. Guests loved it. Very time intensive though. Even with doing the squash prep the day before I still planned my afternoon around cooking this dish. I’ll do it again though!

I loved the sound of this and had some roasted butternut squash on hand. For greens I used kale and Swiss chard and I substituted fresh lasagna noodles for dry.
Instead of the springform pan I used a lasagna baking pan and made layers of the greens/squash mixture alternating with the sauce, feta and lasagna noodles. For more protein I also added a layer of cooked, seasoned ground beef. Baked for 1 hour and did not add béchamel (too rich). A delicious meal on a beautiful autumn day. I will make this again with more yummy sauce!

As others have said, par cooking the noodles is needed.  We used Banza chickpea pasta in case anyone else is interested in that -- and I had to take other commenters' suggestion, and I added 1/2c boiling water at the hour mark for another 15 mins, then an additional 1/4c when the béchamel went on.  Then it was a truly amazing dish, will make again.

Just made this, and its beautiful and it tastes amazing.  It takes a fair amount of work, but its worth it.  I will slice the squash even thinner next time.  I didn't have tomato paste, so I skipped it, and only had 8oz roasted red peppers, not 12oz, but still very delicious. I hope its just as good tomorrow.

I made it for Thanksgiving and served it at room temperature.  Spectacular!  I used dried lasagne noodles, not the oven ready ones.  I noticed that one or two of the noodle pieces hadn’t softened completely after the hour of baking, so I gave it an extra 15 minutes before topping with the bechamel.  Absolutely will make it again.

i baked the dish in a square form, used turkish paprika puree instead of tomato paste and canned tomatoes instead of paprika. Also skipped parmezan in the dish, instead sprinkled mozzarella to the bechamel. Finally, added regular spinach instead of baby and parsley instead of basil. Was still a winner!
@Marion. It seems to me, then, that you didn’t make this recipe at all.
One of my favorite recipes. Not hard, just takes time. Prefer to make this with a cooking buddy to cut down the work, but so worth it.

My first attempt did not turn out well because my ill advised substitutions resulted in an overall lack of moisture. I used drained, frozen spinach instead of fresh spinach and did not have a full container of red pepper in oil so in addition, I roasted another red pepper and puréed that. Following everything else in the recipe to a T, the noodles and Butternut squash were still too hard to eat when I served the dish to my hungry spouse. All was not lost however as overnight everything softened in the fridge and microwaved leftovers with slightly runny fried eggs on top for breakfast have been delicious.

This is a great recipe—tasty,nutritious and makes a lot. However, I found the amount of spice to be excessive, as well as the amount of garlic. I used about 1 tsp each cumin and coriander and 1-2 cloves garlic. The bechamel seemed unnecessary and added nothing. No sugar needed if the squash is ripe and sweet.

2/3 recipe fit into 9” sq pan easily
I broke the lasagna noodles into many small pieces, most of which cooked adequately, the ones on the edge and top dried out a bit (next time will probably pre-boil for a few minutes.  I added a little water and also 1/2 cup of jarred tomato paste as the mix seemed too dry to me, that worked well
I did the pepper mixture in the food processor, put it in a big bowl, then switched the blade to slicer and sliced the squash, which came out perfectly thin and way.
Made it with a soft goat cheese instead of feta and it was very good.

Very tasty! Out of necessity I substituted canned roma tomatoes for the jarred peppers (my family doesn’t care for peppers anyway) and used ancho pepper powder as a sub as well.  I think I would like a bit of nutmeg in the casserole itself or at least in the bechamel sauce. 
Noodles cooked nicely, next time I will make ahead (in 9X9 le creuset baking dish) the day before and let them soften up even more.

I made this for a dinner party and guests were impressed by the presentation.  I followed the recipie as written.  I did not use parchment paper and the pie released easily.  I also used a 10 inch pan vs a 9 inch pan. It was good and the flavor improved with leftovers.  I used a mandoline to slice the butternut squash.  I do keep wondering how to give the recipie a little more "punch".
This is my very favorite dish!  I have been making it just as written for years and it is delicious.

Loved the flavours and texture of this so much. I used ‘oven ready’ lasagna noodles because that’s what I had on hand but for my traditional lasagna recipes, I rarely use those. I would say in this case it is warranted because this isn’t as saucy as some of my marinara based recipes. I didn’t have much basil and using more would have really been great. I substituted crushed red pepper and paprika as others hopefully suggested too. Lastly, I did half feta/ half leftover Boursin: wow! I recommend this big time. 

I may have used too many lasagna noodles and my butternut squash may have been too big because I actually had to make this into two springform pans. Don’t laugh, but when they finished, I carefully put one on top of the other, and it held up so beautifully and look great having the double layers of béchamel!

title: Spicy Coconut Greens With Tomatoes and Shrimp Recipe
author: Yotam Ottolenghi

comments.commentBody: I'm always on the lookout for dairy free mains, but three pans?  Home cooks are typically the dishwasher too.  Maybe put the greens mixture in the serving bowl, keep warm, then use that same pan for the tomato mixture?  Then just use the one extra pan for the shrimp?  Welp, that's what I'm going to do :-)
You can reduce the number of pans by doing the shrimp first, then make the tomatoes in the same pan.

Absolutely delicious.  1.5 lb chard seems like a proper amount for the seasonings and coconut milk, and makes about 4 servings.  The two of us ate most of the tomatoes and shrimp,so I recommend increasing that part.  I’ll make this again.
Or cook the shrimp first, put it in a bowl, then cook the onion and tomatoes in the same pan, and put the shrimp back in at the end to warm.
Ah, that's what I meant to say!

I plan to try this tonight, but question: almost an *hour* simmering the chard? I’m wondering if it’ll be close to algae consistency by then? I stir fry chard a lot, but never that long! Any opinions?
Silkier consistency and more of a flavor meld if you cook the greens down while reducing the coconut milk.

It didn't seem like it would take 50 to 55 minutes to cook the greens, but it took that long to reduce the coconut milk.  Unfortunately, I did not have Swiss Chard, so used a combination of Italian Kale, spinach and a bit of Red-Veined Sorrel to get me to two pounds of greens.  It all worked out well.  Very delicious!

This is fabulous! We are gardeners and don’t grow chard because I’m not a fan so I substituted collards which we have in abundance. Yummy! One note, wear gloves when tossing the spice with the shrimp, my nails are now a lovely yellowish tone.

Agree adding shrimp to tomato pan is easier with good results.

Definitely made a dish of rice to go with it. Having eaten a lot of stewed greens dishes, I do think this would be worth trying with far less cook time on the chard. Maybe even five minutes (with much less coco milk etc).

Topped it this time with shredded basil and mint for an extra kick. The basil was an excellent flavor.
Could this be made with a mix of greens? I have one lonely chard growing, but I also have collards and turnips in my box garden. Can I use a mix for the greens?

Absolutely fabulous! The chard takes a long time and is perfect. The tomatoes, chard, and rice can be made ahead and then you just need to saute the shrimp before the guests sit down. Three pans are no big deal. Perfect for a dinner party as it's a one-bowl event that looks beautiful on the table. Great with a rose!

Any combination of greens is fine but watch for bitterness. Coconut and lime complement the peppers and toasted spices.

This certainly is a novel take on the classic Laing dish from the Philippines. But the addition of blistered tomatoes is a welcome addition. The original dish tended towards spice, with the addition of siling labuyo, a small very spicy red chili pepper. Dried cod was always a staple in our pantry growing up, for this dish and for the Spanish Bacalao.

I didn't follow the recipe 100% - used standard canola oil, turnip greens, habanero peppers, a mix of cherry and standard tomatoes, and increased the tumeric and smoked paprika given my experience and preference. The result was impressively complex, cohesive, and memorable. I also benefited from having some really good gulf shrimp. Still, I bet the flavors would also work with frozen cod cut into shrimp sized morsels and canned tomatoes. The dirty pans are worth it.

Really fun, chefy recipe. I made the greens a day before, per the suggestion. Yes, extra pans, but worth it for me. Followed the instructions to keep the shrimp tender. Love the bursts of flavors.  Delicious.

I substituted tofu for shrimp.  I cooked tofu with soy sauce, honey, and oil till browned and add then added cherry tomatoes.  I used 1 bunch of chard , but probably should have used more. I used 1 can of coconut milk.   I didn’t cook all the coconut milk down.  Very good.
Delicious.  We will definitely make again.

Has anyone cooked this yet?  I have 1.5 lb chard, which produced probably 12-15 cups of shredded leaves.  So there is a mismatch between the 2 lb / 7 cups called for in the recipe.  I'll assume my chard is more or less the right amount and go ahead with the recipe.
Great recipe. Used one habanero Chile instead. Just the right amount of heat. Tossed out the Chile after simmering the tomatoes

As one very familiar with Hawaiian cuisine, spinach is the closest thing to taro leaves if the real thing isn't available.  The texture is very similar, and since the stems are much more tender than chard, you don't have to cook the stems separate from the leaves.  Love the idea of this recipe and will definitely prepare it!

This was a disappointment- followed precisely. After 50 minutes The greens  became soggy overcooked and unpleasant. I regretted using my beautiful homegrown rainbow chard. The spicy shrimp were nice. I will make the shrimp again, and reuse the sauté pan for  lightly braised greens; using the ginger and spices  from the recipe. I will use the coconut milk to make the rice, and I will hope to have the meal on the table within 30 minutes, with half the number of pots and pans!
This was a lot of work and used 4 pans (one for the rice) but it was really delicious.  I used 1.5 lbs of chard and 1 lb of shrimp.  I'll make it again when I have lots of time.
So delicious. I reduced the heat a bit by using smoked paprika and not hot smoked paprika.

I have never been disappointed in an Ottolenghi recipe, always delicious. I only had 1 lb. of chard so I added about 8 ounces of spinach which seemed to work out fine. Also, I used 1 lb of shrimp rather than the 1/2 lb called for because I adore shrimp and it seemed like the wise choice.  I used a large skillet for the chard and I don't think it took the entire 50 minutes for the coconut milk to reduce, more like 30 minutes.

This is the usual Ottolenghi magic where you’re cursing the amount of ingredients until you taste it and realize each plays its part. For those reading before shopping, 2 lbs of Swiss chard is a lot, like 3 large bunches so buy accordingly. Like others, I only had 1 bunch (11 oz) so supplemented with a lot of spinach. I think you could use 1 lb of shrimp as 1/2 lb seemed a bit skimpy.

This was amazing & memorable. Really a lot of flavors going on and it was all marvelous. 
I also increased the shrimp and tomato count, as others mentioned. 
Didn’t have the chilis so I just used jalapeño and poblano and it hit the spot. Would like to try with the recommended chilis next round.

The flavors are outstanding, this is an excellent recipe although didn’t have the ingredients to follow exactly. Used collard greens and they were fabulous. Cooked the shrimp first and then did the tomatoes to cut down on pans. Will double the tomatoes and shrimp next time. This is a keeper!

I tossed the tomatoes with the spices as instructed and then roasted them on a sheet pan for ten minutes at 400F, then added the shrimp to the sheet pan and cooked for another ten.  Love the sheet pan!
I have made this numerous times and always do the greens last. All in the same pan and just setting aside the previous cook. This makes it a one pan meal…

This is an amazing recipe! I didn't have Swiss chard so replaced it with broccoli rabe (or rapini) and some fresh spinach. I also replaced the medium shrimps with baby shrimp. My husband had a second serving which usually is a big no-no for him. The recipe says four servings..three would be more accurate unless you have a side dish. So no left-overs. I'm gifting this recipe !
The magazine introduction says this recipe can be made with cod. For those of us who do not eat shellfish, could NYTimes please provide that original cod recipe?

Use all of the ingredients — toss together— add more lime. Red pepper flak /coconut milk/ cherry tomatoes 
Cook rice in lime.  Coconut oil and scallions 
Serve over yummy rice.  
Love his ingredients and do not worry about the details.  And. Yes have cocktails,wine,beer
I used kale and cooked closer to 25 minutes. This part alone makes a great side
Day 1 this was ‘meh.’ Day 2: Wow, delicious!  It’s not pretty to look at but man it’s tasty. (I didn’t make the shrimp tomato part, just the greens and tossed in some tofu as a sub for shrimp.)
We followed this recipe exactly and thought it was absolutely delicious! A definite do-over.

This was so, so delicious. I had seen this recipe and happened to cross a gigantic beautiful bunch of chard at our market and that was all the impetus I needed. I used only 2 pans, cooked the shrimp first as suggested but don’t think it’s necessary if you time it right. It’s worth how many pans you use. We upped the shrimp and no chilies. It was perfectly warm with the flakes.Lovely.
This dish was a flavorful hit! Will definitely be making it again. Substituted 1 habanero pepper for the birds eyes and only added 1 tsp of fish sauce. Served it over brown rice. YUM
I read the original recipe and made this with cod instead of shrimp. It was great! Except… the greens were just bad. Don’t know why. Followed recipe. I will make this again but skip the greens!

I found this very difficult and recipe instructions were not adequate.   It took too many pots and pans.  It is very easy to overcook the greens, 30 minutes might be better.  Very easy to overcook the tomatoes, mine became tomato sauce. The shrimp were very good. I feel there was too much coconut oil. Not something I would try again. No suggestions on substitute for swiss chard, I could not find in multiple grocery stores, so I used bok choy. Now extra stems of no use to me.
You could cut the bok choy stems on a thin diagonal, then stirfry with maybe fermented black beans and garlic. Top with sesame oil. Or stirfry and add oyster sauce and sesame oil Yum.
I only had a small amount of chard at this time of year so I substituted 2/3 kale leaves. It was delicious.
Cooked the shrimp in the tomato sauce at the end, great way to reduce the dishes and add a blast of favor to the shrimp

This was outstanding! The chard takes on a creamed  spinach quality, and is lovely. I added more chilis to the tomatoes, and then cooked the shrimp and tomatoes in the same pot sequentially.  Topped it all with cilantro and crispy onions. Fabulous!
Substituted cod for shrimp, kale for chard. Oddly, the coconut flavor didn’t come through.

Saw this and signed up for a NYT cooking app subscription to get the full recipe. Yottam did not disappoint! (Aussie Chatters - the boyfriend does it again!). I had to swap salmon for the prawns (allergies of a family member) and it was delicious. Will definitely make this again.

Used fresh salmon I had in the fridge.  Hot coconut oil made wonderful crispy salmon skin.   Loved the flavor of adding Thai basil, fresh limes and serrano chilis from the garden.  

A new recipe always takes me longer than when I make it a second time.  Adapt my shortcuts, heat level, etc.  Will definitely make this again - and again!
Delicious! We licked our plates clean afterwards. Reminiscent of an Indian Saag sauce.

This was a big win with our guests Friday night. It was worth cleaning up the mess I made! I reserved a little coconut milk but couldn't figure out where to add it later. I didn't tell my eaters and they didn't miss it.
Made this for dinner with a friend who is on a no/low carb diet (re: the cocoanut milk). Substituted cocoanut/almond "milk" and it was delicious.
I skipped the red birds eye chilis and dint miss it at all! What a wonderful dish full of so many complex flavors. A new favorite for the family.
I made this almost exactly as written but doubled the topping. Fed four to five. Delicious! I’ll make it again.

Question: would any of you serve this over a grain such as rice? The photo in the paper version of article shows two bowls, the smaller one on the right appearing to have rice underneath the shrimp/tomato/greens. Thoughts please and thank you
Big hit with both my husband and two year old. The two year old even had a second helping of greens! Made this without the chiles to suit a toddler’s palate, but it was still very delicious.
It would be most helpful if the author listed the kitchen tools, gadgets, pots and pans required just before the list of ingredients. Thank you.

This is a great recipe for early fall when you can buy local chard and cherry tomatoes (or pick if you have a garden). I made for 7 and used two large bunches of chard, stuck to the 8 cloves of garlic, 1 1/2 pint containers of tomatoes, 1 1/2 lbs. of shrimp, and doubled everything else. As for cooking chard for so long, I cooked the coconut greens for about 35 minutes: the sauce cooked down and the greens were well wilted. The dish was a big hit. Question- fresh bird’s eye chiles or dried?
@Sarah, once the coconut greens were cooked, I transferred them to a serving bowl, and then used the large saucepan to cook the shrimp, so two pans rather than three.

This is so good! To spread out the work I made the chard the day before, cooked it about 1/2 hr on med. Next day sautéed the shrimp, put them in a bowl, and made the tomato sauce.  Used 2 Serrano chiles in the sauce, and made double the recipe with same amt of chard.  Yummy!
Added okra to the greens (I used collards) because I had it, lovely.

I found this to be really tasty and I needed a whole can of coconut milk, while only simmering for about 25 minutes. Maybe I had a few oz more greens - I chucked in some kohlrabi greens in addition to the chard. The only flavour change I made was to add a little more fish sauce and a small splash of soy sauce to the greens. I felt that they definitely needed more salt and a little umami never hurts.
I typically love Ottolenghi recipes - but for some reason this one just didn't sit well with me. It tasted good, but turned out very soupy.
Made it with half turnip greens and half mustard greens instead of chard, and I'm pretty sure it was a big improvement in the flavor profile. Next time I'll add a bit more lime juice.

Delicious, if quite spicy treatment of shrimp, braised tomatoes, and chard.  Spice level can be controlled by using smaller quantities of red pepper flakes, fresh ginger, and bird's eye chiles than the recipe calls for, but for my husband and me, it was perfect.

Quite stunning! A real winner.  Going on the rotation for nights when I have more time. I'll substitute grilled or roasted eggplant for the shrimp as a change-up. For me it is worth the effort of it's multiple pans...
The swiss chard part is the correct amount but the tomatoes and shrimp both need to be doubled, especially the shrimp. 2 oz person is not enough. But, the taste is wonderful!

title: Sunset Pavlova With Sweet Vinegar and Rosemary Recipe
author: Yotam Ottolenghi

comments.commentBody: What a wonderful article. I found, when I worked making pastry for Whole Foods, that a big jar of meringues on the counter drew customers to it like seagulls at the dump.
"...drew customers to it like seagulls at the dump." made me laugh.
Do they have good meringues?  Could I use theirs instead of making my own?

For Joan B prepared meringues will not work for this I prepared meringue is cooked for at least an hour at 200° in order to make it dry and crisp all the way through a Pavlova is crisp on the outside and still chewy on the inside if you make this with store-bought meringues you might have a nice dessert but you won’t have this dessert
Maggie Ruggier gets huge props for styling that pavlova.
I always spread a layer of pastry cream on the bottom of the meringue, then top with the whipped cream and fruit. Am I gilding the lily?
I do the same thing! A good way to use up some of the leftover egg yolks.
I love pavlova with very ripe strawberries mixed with a spoon of strawberry or raspberry jam topped by fresh mint.

We like to make single serving size pavlovas--then you don't have to slice to serve. Leftover yolks can be made into lemon curd, a delicious filling on its own or topped with the fruit and/or whipped cream.

Outside of New York City, where apparently one can buy any ingredient from any culture on the planet, where/how/why would one obtain cardamom PODS?! Can you just leave a note of how to substitute ground cardamom from a jar, please? Usually NYTimes Cooking is so good at that!
I order mine from Amazon
Nuts.com or any place that sells bulk spices.
Penzey’s or Kalustyan’s via mail order
Made this yesterday with homegrown peaches and figs in honey, orange blossom water syrup. It was heavenly and light as a feather for a summer night dinner party with best friends. Perfect!

I have no words.  Well, that is never true, but I will tell you what is.  This recipe is the best thing I have made in a very, very long time.  Every bite is a burst of layered flavors and textures, and it’s gorgeous. The kumquats! The passion fruit! The nectarines! Oh my! I made the meringue and the sauce the night before, whipped up the cardamom cream and packed it all up with my mandolin and the fruit to bring to a party the next day.  Now I have one word left to say.  Sublime….
8 cardamom pods = 1 1/4 tsp ground cardamom

This was spectacular! Made it for New Year’s Eve because I had a lot of frozen leftover egg whites I wanted to use up. Everyone was blown away and lots of seconds were had. Nectarines and plums are not in season then, so swapped that out for kiwi fruit, which is traditional in a pavlova anyway. Couldn’t get fresh passion fruit, but got a bag of frozen passion fruit puree cubes instead. Also sprinkled pomegranate seeds on top because I had them. Sounds like a lot of steps, but it’s actually quite easy, especially if you make the syrup and prep some of the ingredients a few days ahead. Make it!

This was INCREDIBLE. Totally worth the hour spent slicing up grapes et al. I used regular granulated sugar and swapped the nectarines for dragon fruit, but otherwise didn't change a thing. I will say, compared to the beautiful photo above, mine looked more like a sheet pizza

You can make caster sugar by whizzing regular white sugar in your food processor until fine. King Arthur also sells it. Cardamom pods are available at Asian markets, but ground cardamom is usually in grocery spices, especially at holiday time.
To Ellen: yes you can make your own superfine sugar by whiling it in the Osterizer. It has to be very dry, though.
For four egg whites.  Forty minutes not enough, try fifty.  Sub crème fraiche for mascarpone

Quite the showstopper!  Used locally available summer fruit (nectarines, peaches, plumcots). Couldn't eat it all in one sitting, so nibbled at it the next day, and the pavlova was still crisp and chewy.  (Not soggy.). Just a note about the cardamom: I don't think shelling and grinding the seeds was necessary; next time I'd just add around 1/4 teaspoon of ground cardamom.  Overall: Highly recommended.

This is genuinely one of the best desserts ever. You can switch the fruit up depending on the season. Using whole cherries with stems is gorgeous and gives the dish some height. I usually need to cook the syrup longer to get the consistency I like. I also totally totally recommend using the passion fruit like the recipe says, it really adds beautiful color and texture (and obviously flavor) to the pavlova. 
The syrup is incredible, don’t be put off by the vinegar and rosemary.

Gorgeous dessert!!!  I skipped the superfine for white sugar in the syrup....it's going to dissolve anyway....next time I'll try demerits. I ran the cardomom pods through my mini coffee bean grinder WITH the superfine sugar before adding it to the cream...boosts the fragrance and no grit.....
Whole fresh Passionfruit is super expensive here, but I found frozen seedless chunks in the frozen fruit section of my grocery. I followed this food styling from right to left, and am very pleased with the result. My guests licked their plates.

10/10: I’m an Aussie and this is the best pav we (and friends I made it for) have ever had. The meringue was amazingly soft and fluffy and crispy. the rosemary in the syrup was chefs (づ￣ ³￣)づ 

Cooked without kumquats and subbed Cardamum cream for whipped cream with nothing added because I ran out of time.
Does it have to be apple cider vinegar - can I use white distilled?
No.  Different taste.  apple cider vinegar is readily available in virtually every supermarket.  If you youse white distilled it will taste like your floor mop.
If you don't have apple cider, use another "sweet" or fruity vinegar. I've used red wine vinegar, balsamic and sherry vinegar in my pavs before, with great success. Avoid white vinegar though.

I often make Nigella's strawberry pavlova on this site (with whatever fruit we have on hand), that recommends white wine vinegar, but I've also used white distilled and even once used balsamic. I think it's there to help stabilize the meringue vs for taste; honestly, the amount is so small I'd bet that no one can taste the difference between one type of vinegar and another.
Summer berries Maseratis in ameretto  toasted almonds and ameretto chantilly cream , yum !!

Anyone know where I can get kumquats and ripe 
stone fruits at the same time in the U.S., particularly in... August?
You can use any kind of fruit with this. Kiwi fruit is a pavlova classic, as are strawberries. But any combination of your favourite fruits will work
@Rebecca: I'd say you can't. I have a kumquat tree, its fruit ripens in winter. You could make this in the winter with imported plums which are usually no good. IMO.
True kumquats are not available in August even in the San Francisoc Bay Area, they are out of season.

--I love pavlova with very ripe strawberries mixed with a spoon of strawberry or raspberry jam topped by fresh mint.
--I always spread a layer of pastry cream on the bottom of the meringue, then top with the whipped cream and fruit. Am I gilding the lily?
--What a wonderful article. I found, when I worked making pastry for Whole Foods, that a big jar of meringues on the counter drew customers to it like seagulls at the dump. :)
