Cardamom pods are more widely available than you think. I’ve found them in the South Asian section of Wegmans, but most often I get them at my local India Bazaar.

Found cardamom pods at Whole Foods, and passion fruit in the frozen aisle as cubes. Prepared everything in advance, and assembled right before serving, using a mandoline to slice up fruit last minute. Stunning dessert, can’t wait to experiment with different herbs and fruit.
I buy cardamom pods (and many other fine botanicals) at mountainroseherbs.com.

Wonderful. A perfect end to a summer supper. The syrup is what truly makes this and don't be intimidated by all of the steps - it goes very quickly. Made while my kids were napping.  Went with what I could snap up at the farmer's market so used a peach, a nectarine, some prune-plums, and strawberries.
TinaS - cardamon pods are super common & I'm not in NYC, Amazon sells them with next-day prime too.

While I was quite sure I had "ruined" the recipe at every turn (the meringue seemed too liquidy, and despite my repeated attempts, my rosemary orange syrup never developed into a "maple syrup consistency"), the result was nearly as photogenic as the photo. "Have you tried this yet?" my guests asked after I had served them, and I was sure because I had made it inedible, but I was delighted to learn that it was a delicious crowd-pleaser. I'll be sure to make this again!
This is the most amazing desert! Complex, yet subtle flavors; gorgeous no matter which fruits you use; can use whipped coconut cream for dairy-free guests.
Made this pretty much as written with just nectarines, passionfruit, and red currants instead of grapes, and it’s one of the best things I’ve ever eaten. Sublime
five stars!
This is simply the very best merengue recipe ever !
What a gorgeous dessert! But will likely be not be finding nectarines in February. Not even  certain I will fine kumquats now. But will be looking!!!

Finding kumquats in June…? Or finding passion fruit anywhere? I live in a large city with some luxo stores but no. 
So passion fruit nectar was a good idea and luckily I had some. Subbed thinly sliced key limes for the kumquats, but allowed them to marinate a bit in the sugary passion fruit nectar while preparing the meringue. 
Gorgeous! So impressive and equally delicious.

This dessert is not only a delicious crowd pleaser, the colors of the fruit, whipped cream and pavlova make for a beautiful presentation. Everyone who has tasted it feels it is one of the best things they have tasted. The juxtaposition of flavors makes it a culinary experience. I will be adding this to my list of go to desserts. Even going to make it for my birthday party this weekend.

Used sherry vinegar instead of apple cider, cooked for 50 mins instead of an hour to get that marshmallow texture instead of a hard meringue all the way through. The glaze didn’t thicken properly for me.

This was the best Pavlova I have ever had. I don’t actually love Pavlova. I find it too sweet and a bit lacking in depth. Not this recipe.
Gorgeous, creative, adult and not sickly sweet.

Couldn’t get nectarines, kumquats or plums (out of season) so I subbed with kiwi, mango and starfruit. It was good but I look forward to trying this recipe again in the summer when the fruits are in season. Agree with other commenters, the vinegar syrup is an excellent addition.

The flavors are ASTOUNDING together! A fairly low effort/skill level dessert despite having multiple steps. The fruits suggestions sound delightful but are weirdly out of sync with each other seasonally. You really can use whatever fruit you like as long as you follow the syrup recipe and have the passion fruit. Mandolined fruit slices are so visually appealing, though, and make for an easy time composing the final dish. I made one pavlova as written and one with aquafaba and they both turned out great (the aquafaba pavlova needed more time in the oven).
Can I ask if there is a substitute for the orange juice?  I'm allergic.
Orange juice pairs well with rosemary. I can't think of another fruit that would work with rosemary: pear juice maybe.

Looks like a great desert.  I'm assuming any combination of fruit could be used - mandolin sliced peaches, blueberries, kiwi, etc.  

The problem is that it has to be served within an hour of finishing which suggests that the main meal would have to be something already prepared.  

And how does one cut and serve it? - it looks like it would all fall apart especially cut into 8-10 servings.

You can make the meringue ahead, as mentioned in the header notes. Everything else can also be prepared ahead so you just assemble before serving. Very easy. When serving, expect it to fall apart and be a mess, that's part of the joy of a pavlova (and note that an Eaton Mess is basically a pavlova that has fallen apart ;-)

@Golem18
I like it cut unevenly. If you want it tidier make a tighter rectangle or circle.
any thoughts on using ground cardamom vs the whole pods/grinding process? how much/little to use?
I'd recommend starting with 1/2 t. It's strong stuff. Taste it and add more if you like, but I bet that will be plenty.
Can I make the meringue in advance and store it in an air-tight container overnight, then add the fruit and cream before serving? Or will is soften since the inside is chewy rather than crispy?
@Leftie The recipe says you can, but I wouldn't do it, not here in the humidity of the south. I suppose it depends where you live and what season of the year it is.
Leftie:  I have made the meringue in advance many times.  Just be sure to keep it dry.  I usually leave it in the oven, but an airtight container would work well.  Good luck.

TinaS, 
order them from Amazon
Amazon: the death of small business
We like to make single serving pavlovas instead of one big one--no cutting is needed to serve. Yolks make lemon curd, which is a delicious filling on its own or topped with fruit.
My dilemma is where to find passion fruit?? That's the base for the original Australian version, I believe, but unable to find, even canned.
Passion fruit puree is readily available on Amazon

Tina S: agreed that many ingredients are "not available" in places like Ohio. I found cardamom pods (which I use for making my own chai latte) on Amazon. What I can't find in my grocery stores is superfine sugar! Maybe that's on Amazon too? Also, kumquats and passion fruit are nowhere to be found in my local Kroger. Guess I'll have to improvise. I make a double cream chocolate meringue that I love, and I think I'll love this one too.
You can make your own superfine sugar. Just whir in a spice grinder.
Or a blender or food processor
You can make superfine sugar from granulated sugar in a blender.

I found kumquats at an Asian market. They seem to be currently in season. The pavlova served up beautifully in tidy squares. We gobbled it down and the next day i'd have killed for another piece. I will say, the fresh passionfruit was a bit hard to find. There's a frozen puree available inexpensively by La Fe ... in a pinch you could use this and any extra would be beautiful on yogurt or in a cocktail. This was worth the effort 100 percent
Best pavlova I’ve ever made. Followed recipe as is with addition of mango. The cardamom cream and rosemary vinegar sauce elevate the composition immensely. Can’t wait to do again.

So many Pavlova recipes are so fussy. I've had my share of flops but I keep on trying. It's hard to make enough for 12-15 people. I'm going to try this cavalier recipe that makes it sound do easy! I loved living in NZ where they pile the pavlovas next to the berries in the grocery store!

Made this for guests and it was big hit. Left out the rosemary. Couldn’t taste the cardamom. Will add more next time. Substituted oranges for kumquats and mangoes for peaches. The syrup and the passion fruit kicked the flavor up. Made all components ahead of time and they kept nicely.
I made this Pavlova this weekend and it was out of this world. My only change was the fruits I had on hand. A recipe to repeat soon!
Made this for my mother's birthday. A huge success, as almost always with NYT Cooking receipts! and it adds a bit of novelty to all this daily cooking business.
Is there a way to make a savory meringue and substitute tomatoes and mozzarella for the stone fruit?  I know it would no longer be a "pavlova" in the strictest sense of the name.

Maybe sundried tomatoes? Fresh ones will sog the meringue. As to savory: you need sugar in a meringue to give it structure. If you don't mind a collapsed meringue you could try cornstarch, but I'd guess it wouldn't be too successful. The acid in the meringue (can use cream or tartar instead of vinegar, same chemistry) strengthens it so that you can use less sugar, but still it's going to be sweet. But then, so is teryaki.
@Paul Banas pastry chef here. In a word: no.
JA - I was also thinking of peaches. Did the orange honey blossom syrup work well with the cardamom cream?
Does the recipe (cook time/temp) change if you’re making individual size merengues?

Made exactly to instruction except increased the fruit. I wasn’t sure as I was making it. It is stupendous. The flavours really work together and this will be a keeper. Perhaps with the rosemary vinegar syrup on stone fruit to cut the calories.
TinaS:  Look for cardamom pods in South Asian food stores.  Online orders from the big biz companies would be my second choice.
Question: green cardamom pods or black cardamom pods?
@Theresa  Green cardamom pods, which are often yellow/tan, only faintly green, and about half an inch long.  Black cardamom is very different and quite big, black and a bit hairy.
Can the syrup be prepped day ahead?
Does the syrup need to be refrigerated,  if made ahead?

The sugar and acid content is high enough it should be okay left at room temp for a few hours, but I refrigerated mine and the chilliimade the syrup a bit kore viscous (which better clung to the fruit and pavlova)
Astounding adult dessert with delightful textural and flavor complexity.  Earned me the moniker "Goddess of Flavors" from my book club
Can somebody tell me what you do with the 7 egg yolks? it feels wasteful and disrespectful of the chicken (remember the poor chicken?)
@Sunset Stella actually I think best option is to make a lemon curd which I frequently use on my pavlova or on baked goods or just plain toast!

@Sunset Stella My chocolate mousse recipe calls for 4 yolks. I double the recipe and put it in individual cups that my husband eats once a week as a treat. Or, if when I want a more decadent Pavlova, I can spread the chocolate mousse in the pavlova shells and accent with unsweetened whipped cream and bittersweet chocolate curls. Lovely in winter.
Incredible recipe.
I’m a there a sub for corn starch (corn allergy)? Perhaps arrowroot?
@Deane Try potato starch.
Can I leave out the Marcap one or replace with something else?
I don’t like cardamom, any suggestions for a replacement?
@Andrea Burdick it’s pretty subtle. I feel like you could maybe just leave it out?
The rosemary is very jarring.
Can the meringue be made ahead?

I buy cardamom pods online from Diaspora spices, a woman-owned smallish business. They are the freshest,most sustainably and equitably farmed spices available anywhere. The owner buys the spices direct from farmers in India, mostly, cutting out the middlemen and other players that add to the cost and delay the transit of the spices to you.  The cardamom is divine.

title: Grilled Cucumbers With Tomato-Cardamom Dressing and Mozzarella Recipe
author: Yotam Ottolenghi

comments.commentBody: I get so mad at recipes that call for something like 7 tbs of olive oil. That gives you 7 chances to make a mistake. Why not just 3.5 (liquid) ounces? Measure once. This recipe calls for another tbs oil later. So measure 1/2 cup  and use a little for coating the cucks when grilling.
Totally agree. Better yet, give weights for everything and move into modern times already.
Does cooking allow one to eat the cardamom shells?
Excellent question.  I never buy the shelled cardamom as I cant quite understand this too!
I was looking for the step that strains them out, but it was not there.
He says to reserve shells, but he doesn't use them again, I think it was meant to read 'discard'. You can eat shells if ground into oblivion, but they don't taste like much
Dept. of Stupid Questions: would this work with a zucchini as well?
Yes.  It's wonderful.  I am Head of the Dept. of Stupid Questions.
I grill zuc & yellow squash all the time!  They absorb smoke nicely & over a super hot flame - they’ll get all charred up!
Wonderfull flavor combination.  Didn't have Persian cucumber, but English cucumber worked well.  Used burrata - VG.  I am saving the leftovers to go on pasta.
Could one use powdered cardamom, if so how much?
I was wondering the same thing...

Does the dressing really require that much oil? Looking at other ingredients, max I would use to cook would be 2 -3.  Has anyone already tried reducing? I am all for evoo unctuousness but this seems excessive …

Most of the oil is absorbed by the miso, tomatoes, etc., and so I would not change it. The sautéing and cooking takes a little while. I found the oil portion to be perfect in the end result. Nothing liquid at the bottom of my serving bowl -- just enough.

A number of commented on the cardamom. I believe adding the shells to the oil is a mistake. I put the pods in the pestle, cracked them gently, then separated the seeds from the shells. Seeds were coarsely ground and used in the oil and the shells discarded. For the miso, I simmered it as a blob, then wisked it in the liquid at the end before the cukes were added. I also used larger farmer's market cukes which needed to be cut smaller after grilling. I still retained the skin.
There are 16 tbsp in one cup, you don’t have to use your table spoon you can use a half cup and don’t fill it to about 7/8 full

The irony is that in Ottolenghi's country of residence, a cup is 10 oz not 8 oz. So 7 tbls is not, for him, 7/8 of half a cup. It is, I think, 7/20 of a British cup. Thank God he isn't Australian or we would never figure this out!

This recipe was surprisingly delicious. The cooked cucumbers were still crisp and fresh and the tomato dressing was jammy and delicious!

I didn’t use as much oil as was suggested (maybe half) and it still turned out great and could even be less oily. I didn’t have cardamom so used cinnamon and ginger as a sub (per googling suggestion)

I think I might prefer a slightly softer cheese like burrata next time.
Is this green cardamom or black cardamom?  I know black is pretty intense, but just checking.

Or it could be white cardamom! Who knows! The shells of all three are pretty unpleasant to chomp on, black most of all, but the flavor of black cardamom seems most felicitous here, so I'm stumped too.

Cardamom shells are tough and typically not eaten. They have a lot of flavor, though, when used either whole or powdered seeds. But it is a delicate flavor and, in my opinion, lost in a dish such as this.

Further to what to do with the cardamom pods, please look at step one.  They are mixed in with the rest of the ingredients used for the dressing, although I plan on removing them once everything is cooked.

Nine cardamom pods sounds like a lot. 
Is this correct? 
Also I have access to very good ground cardamom seeds. What would be an equal measure to 9 pods. 1tsp?1Tbl? Do the pod hulls add something because I hate counting & fishing them out?

Not sure what I did wrong but the miso charred and clumped into a mess in the pan. Whenever I cook with miso it’s usually dissolved first in some type of liquid. I probably won’t make this again unless anyone has  any ideas for the miso.
I skipped the miso and it was perfect.
Mine took a while and a lot of stirring/breaking up in the pan, but didn't burn. You might try a T of hot water, mix w miso first, then add to sauté.
Absolutely delicious even the kids went for seconds!

I found the 1/2 cup of olive oil to be too much.  Will pare it down next time.  And the cucumber tasted a lot like zucchini, only less dense. Will try zuke next time, but highly recommend the cuke. I know, use both!  There's plenty of oil for both. Didn't have Miso, did I miss anything besides salt?  Sublime recipe.
Why add cardamon shells? 9 pods mean 18 (at least) bits of shell to be spat out. Also, isn’t nearly all the flavour in the seeds?

The Tomato-Cardamom dressing is vintage Ottolenghi—outstanding.  I would use just that as a bed for roasting some gorgeous halibut filet with a side of perfectly baked Basmati rice (maybe with a few more Cardamom pods/seeds).  Didn’t love this dish as is.  It was pretty to look at, but the cucumbers didn’t do it for this lover of cucumbers any which-way.  The “dressing” is a definite keeper.

This was interesting, but I'm not sure it was a) worth the effort or b) well represented the variety of ingredients. I followed the instructions and  expected something more complex. It was fun to use cardamom pods, but, in the end, it was just ok.
…and what - if anything- would be a substitute for cardamom shells/pods?
I just looked, 1 cardamom shell is equal to 1/6 of a teaspoon of ground. So, that would mean 1 1/2 teaspoons is equal to 9 yes? I'm bad at math so I'd double check that amount hahaha

After laboriously shelling a bag to get 2T of seeds for another recipe I found a great source for shelled cardamom. Inexpensively at an Indian food market. I’ll lightly crush them before adding. 

Unless your aversion is to cardamom in any form. Then I might suggest a pinch of cinnamon and a tiny bit of ground clove.
Any suggestions for a substitute for the miso? I've got everything else, and I'm unlikely to buy white miso for this.

I know that this isn't very helpful, but miso (& other fermented soybean pastes) aren't easily replaced, save with a different fermented soybean paste. They're also incredibly versatile and delicious, white miso most of all, so it's well worth having in your fridge. That said, you can omit it and add a little extra salt when it's used in such a small quantity as it is here.
When does one one remove the cardamom pods and the bay leaf?

Has anyone experimented with different types of cardamom for comparisons?

Also, how do the grilled cucumbers compare with grilled zucchini?
What about trying the cucumbers in an air fryer?

I imagine that because Chef Ottolenghi is writing for an American food column - in the newspaper of record for this country - that he is using US standard measures. Whether you feel it is too much oil is a matter of preference & open to adaptation.

Went to bed wondering, "Why did the recipe say to reserve the cardamom shells?" I'd tossed them, having missed the part in Step 1 to add them into the sauté. Be that as it may, this is a DELICIOUS and easy dish. We had it with Korean BBQ  chicken quarters (made up a marinade/baste/sauce w/what I had on hand: bottled Teriyaki & KC BBQ sauce, Gochujang, and a little each of lemon juice, sesame oil, & soy sauce -- turned and rebated 3 times: YUM) and corn on the cob. Very nice summertime dinner!

Holy moly! This was next level delicious. I used regular cucumbers, half the tomatoes because that’s what I had, and no cheese because I’m vegan. Would make again and double the sauce. I only cook with really quality olive oil from Italy, so the amount was not an issue at all for me. In fact I thought the sauce needed it.

I don’t understand this recipe. The article talks about standing around a grill, yet he wants us to cook them on a grill pan in the kitchen. The article even talks about putting them on a “griddle“. Which is it? Are we standing around outdoors, or are we praying the smoke alarm does not go off in the kitchen?
I can't wait to try this. But when it comes to not wanting cooked cucumbers to be limp, try Julia Child's braised cucumbers with cream. And you don't need the grill.

Use less oil
Any kind of
I halved the oil for the sauce and had delicious results.

I gave this a shot tonight. The flavor was interesting, maybe a bit too heavy on olive oil. After I made it, it just felt lacking a bit, so I ended up adding some farro to the salad. Overall, it was good, not great. Probably wouldn't make it again.

Years ago..in the 50s..when zucchini was a new thing in our little prairie town, a neighbor gave my mom some.  She sliced them crosswise and cooked them.  When we were eating, my father was having none of it.  My mother asked my dad, who always wanted to know what everything was, if he had a question?  His reply:  I know what it is but I don’t know WHY you cooked the cucumbers.  Not sure how my mother handled this, but I am still laughing 60 years later!!
I bet you could combine the grilled cuc from this recipe to another recent NYT recipe "Marinated Cherry tomatoes" (which is delish). Seems more adaptable.
You shouldn’t boil miso because it kills all the healthy enzymes in it. It would be better to add it at the end with the lime juice.
Don’t use shells, 1 1/2 t. Ground cardamom

I found this disappointing, but I did make a couple of mistakes. First, I used Armenian cucumbers which are a bit larger and probably less flavorful. The grilling didn't add much. Second, I tried to make it as a light main course; however, despite the rich colors of the dressing it is actually quite mildl and didn't stand up alone.

For those wondering about the oddly-specific 7 Tbs of olive oil, that's what you get when an intern converts 100ml. It's a dressing, just read that as ~1/2 C.
This was unlike any cucumber salad I’ve made before and is definitely worth a try! Made as written.

Made this recipe tonight using regular cucumbers, ground cardamom and Caribbean ground bay leaf.  It was wonderful!  Grilled a couple of lamb chops to go along with it. My husband and I thought we were out to dinner at a five star restaurant!  Excellent!

Loved this! I never thought of grilling cukes but this was sublime -- I should have doubled the dressing recipe because we have so many cukes ready to pick in the garden & we’ll eat this over & over!!!

Definitely only 9 cardamom pods.
I grill the cucumbers first, so I can put them in the sauce when it is finishing.  Easy to overcook the sauce.
The “excess” oil soaks up the subtle flavours and is marvellous. Use some fresh bread to sop it up!

So good. I made the cukes on my stovetop. As long as you keep oil light and the heat high (and fan ON), it works. Also skipped miso but added one sweet and one hot pepper, chopped, when cooking the garlic/tomato mixture. And used 1.5 tsp of ground cardamom vs 9 pods. I don’t think there’s a good sub for cardamom! Served on a bed of whole grains which sopped up the delicious dressing.
The sauce is out of this world!
